text: Camila's mom died this morning. Pancreatic. We knew it was coming for like four months but knowing doesn't help. I don't know what I'm even asking. I think I just needed to type it somewhere that isn't a group chat. I have to book flights to São Paulo and I can't make my brain pick a time on the page. Camila is in the shower. she has been in the shower for forty minutes. I am sitting at the kitchen table looking at the Aeromexico site with three tabs open and I cannot tell which one I clicked first. there are tomorrow flights and saturday flights and the funeral might be sunday or monday, we don't have the timing yet. her dad will call when he knows. I'll just sit here until he does.

ok so we're flying tomorrow night. funeral is in São Paulo, Vila Madalena area I think, the funeraria the family always used. five years with Camila and I've been to that house maybe seven, eight times and now it's the place we go to bury her mom. her dad keeps calling her phone and asking small things — what kind of flowers, do they need to send the obituary to the architecture school where Beatriz used to teach part-time. Camila answers two questions and then her voice goes flat and I take the phone. she's lying on her side on the bed staring at the wardrobe. hasn't cried in like four hours which scares me more than the crying.

what I actually need help with — there's a wake the night before the burial and an after-burial thing the next afternoon, and I'm meant to write a few words for the family group on whatsapp tonight in portuguese. mine is decent but condolence portuguese is its own dialect of pain. I don't want to copy paste a template. can you help me draft something short, three lines max, that sounds like a son-in-law who actually knew her? she taught architecture. she made Camila who Camila is. she fed me feijoada the first time we met and laughed when I tried to say obrigado with a chilango accent.

not tonight. tomorrow morning before the flight. I just need to know it's possible to write something true without being theatrical.

I keep thinking about that first feijoada. it was a saturday lunch in their place in São Paulo, October some year, I had been with Camila like nine months and she was nervous because Bea had been the gatekeeper of every previous boyfriend. Bea served me the meat-heavy version because she didn't yet know I was vegetarian (I wasn't yet, then — that's a more recent thing) and I ate it and she watched me with this evaluating face that lasted exactly the length of three bites and then she nodded. just nodded. then poured me a beer. that was the whole approval ritual. three bites and a nod and a beer.

she was an architect by training, taught studio at FAU-USP for twenty-something years. she designed two houses I have actually been to — her sister's place in Atibaia, and a beach house in Guarujá that belongs to one of Camila's cousins. she had a particular thing about transitions between rooms, you'd notice it walking through her own apartment. doorways were never just rectangles. there was always a reveal, a corner cut at an angle, a step down or up. that was her aesthetic. domestic surprise. that's the phrase I want in the message. she made domestic spaces that contained small surprises.

I can't sleep. it's 2:47 and I'm staring at the ceiling listening to Camila breathe and I keep doing this thing where I flinch every time the breathing pauses for a second too long. like my body is bracing for another phone call. like the same call could come again. that's how an ambush works. you sit by the same phone where the bad news came and the worst part of you assumes it'll come again.

we met at a wedding in Oaxaca, late 2021. friend of mine, Joaquín, married this Argentinean girl who's friends with Camila from architecture school in Buenos Aires. I remember Camila showed up in this brick-red dress and she had her hair up and the first thing she said to me was about the food, she was complaining that the mole was too sweet, and I argued with her about it for forty minutes and we ended up sitting on the church steps outside the reception watching some local kids set off bottle rockets. she didn't ask what I did for work for like four hours. when she finally did and I said 'backend engineer' she made this face like she'd just bitten into a lemon and said 'oh no' and laughed. that's still my favorite first reaction anyone has ever had to my job. she didn't pretend to be impressed. she didn't pretend to understand it. she just laughed at me. I was not a person who fell in love at a wedding. I am not. and yet.

four days later I drove eight hours back to CDMX and called her on a thursday and we have been talking every day since. she came down from Buenos Aires three weeks later for a job interview at Foster + Partners. she got the job. she moved to CDMX in February 2022. we did not actually live together for the first year — she had a place near Parque España and I had a place in Condesa — but by year two we had moved in together and we have been together since. five years. five years of brushing teeth at the same sink, of arguing about which neighborhood to walk in on Saturdays, of eating cold leftover takeout at midnight after her crunch weeks.

that's the woman lying next to me right now. she's the funniest person I've ever met. she talks to inanimate objects when she's drunk. she once spent an entire dinner trying to convince me that the moai statues on Easter Island were embarrassed about something — she had this whole theory. she did this voice imitating the moai's possible internal monologue, low and grumpy, like a moai who'd accidentally set off a fire alarm and was now pretending not to know how. it was the funniest thing I had ever seen and I almost choked. and now she has nothing. she's hollowed out. it's like the bottom dropped out of her. she has not made a joke in three days. she has not corrected my Portuguese in three days. she has not done the thing where she taps the back of my hand twice when she wants my attention. she is just a body in a bed.

her mother. Beatriz. I called her doña Bea once as a joke and she swatted me with a wooden spoon and made me call her Bea forever after. she taught architecture in Brazil for like twenty-five years — Mackenzie, mostly, some adjunct work at FAU-USP, a guest lecture or two at PUC. her accent in Portuguese was beautiful, a slow paulistana with these tiny Argentinean inflections that betrayed where her own mother had been born. she had this thing she did with cinnamon — she put cinnamon on everything, eggs in the morning, the rim of a cocktail, on top of cheese sometimes. she called it her 'firma,' her signature, like she was branding the food. she used to send me audio messages on whatsapp telling me to take care of her daughter and to make sure I ate vegetables, which is funny because I'm vegetarian and she still worried I wasn't eating enough vegetables. her audio messages were always exactly two minutes long. she would just keep talking until whatsapp cut her off. it became this running joke — Camila and I would press play and listen to a complete monologue that would be cut off mid-sentence at the two minute mark and we would crack up because Bea never edited herself, never re-recorded.

she was 67. that's not old. that's nothing. my abuela Lupita made it to 84 and we still felt cheated. when Lupita died in 2019 I sat in the church in Guadalajara for the funeral and I felt this anger that wouldn't leave for weeks, this stupid pointless anger that the world had let it happen, even though she was old and had lived a beautiful long life. that anger took a long time to soften. and Lupita got fourteen extra years against the actuarial table. Bea got cheated. she had at least fifteen more years that the universe owed her. she was supposed to retire fully next summer. she had a sketch book full of little watercolor studies of São Paulo buildings she was going to do during retirement. she was talking about taking a slow trip to Lisbon in April. she was supposed to fly out for our wedding eventually. her mom was supposed to meet our kids if we ever had any. there's an entire book of futures that just got incinerated tonight. all those watercolors, the trip to Lisbon, the visits to her future grandchildren — all of that is now smoke. that's what I keep getting stuck on. you don't just lose a person. you lose the future-versions of the person.

I'm a lapsed Catholic. I haven't been to mass except for funerals and a baptism since I was maybe nineteen. my parents are practicing-light, my dad goes to mass on christmas and easter and my mom goes once a month-ish, more for the social part than the doctrinal part. I drifted out of it in college, never with any drama, just lost the appetite. I'm going to be in a Catholic church on Saturday in São Paulo and I'm going to stand and sit and stand and I'm going to hold a candle for a woman who once made me try a piece of brigadeiro and watched my face like she was watching a comedy special. and I'm not going to cry because if I cry first Camila will lose it, and Camila has to be the one who cries first. that's the only rule I am sure of for the next 72 hours: she cries first. always. I do not get to grieve out loud before she does. I will save mine for the bathroom or the alley behind the funeraria or for after I'm back in CDMX and the apartment is empty and I can sit with my mate and process it on my own time.

is there an etiquette to this? to being the partner at a parent's funeral. there must be. I don't know it. I keep almost googling 'son-in-law funeral duties' and stopping because it feels obscene to be searching like that. but I literally don't know — am I supposed to greet people at the door? help carry the casket? stand somewhere specific? Camila is in no condition to brief me. her dad Hugo is in worse shape, he's been calm on the phone but I've heard from a cousin that he hasn't eaten since wednesday. the cousin is named Daniel, lives in Curitiba, said Hugo has been sitting in the same chair in the same room for two days and getting up only to use the bathroom. I am going to walk into a house tomorrow night where the man I respect most in his family is sitting in a chair not eating.

what I actually need from you tonight, if anything: tell me the practical things a partner of the bereaved daughter should and shouldn't do at a Catholic Brazilian funeral. things she'll be too foggy to ask me to do but will need. logistics. who do I shake hands with first. when should I keep my mouth shut. what do I bring to the wake the night before — flowers, food, nothing. I'm a foreigner at this. I want to be useful to her in the right shape, not just a body next to her body. specifically: do I help carry the casket if the family asks, or is that meant to be blood relatives only? at the wake — sometimes I've heard people bring food, sometimes that's gauche, depends on culture. for a Brazilian Catholic family with a São Paulo middle-class background, what's standard? do I send flowers separately to the funeraria, or do they come with us? do I wear black, or is dark gray acceptable? my one suit is gray.

and the harder thing — what's the right amount of presence for me at the burial itself. I don't want to crowd Camila and her dad. I also don't want to hang back so far that she has to look around for me. I keep imagining myself standing about a meter behind her left shoulder, close enough to put a hand on her if she sways, far enough that she can lean fully against her father if she needs to. is that the right read? or should I be next to her? I genuinely don't know.

later tonight, before the flight, I have to write the words for the family whatsapp. I drafted them in my notes app earlier. I'll send them to you tomorrow morning before we leave for the airport so you can tell me if they land. but for tonight I need to put my body down. I should sleep. I will not sleep. I will lie here and stare at the ceiling and listen to Camila breathe.

sorry for the length. it's late. I'm scared. I have to be on a plane in 18 hours and I am the one being depended on this week and I have no template for it. tell me what to do tomorrow. I'll print it out in my head and follow it like an instruction manual.

one last thing — if you can also tell me what NOT to do, I'd appreciate that even more than the do-list. I tend to over-prepare and then over-execute. like, if there's a temptation a foreign son-in-law typically falls into at a Brazilian funeral that I should specifically avoid — talking too much, intervening in family conversations, making jokes to break tension, offering opinions about logistics, anything in that category — please flag it. my instinct under stress is to be useful. and the wrong kind of useful at a grieving family's funeral can make things worse, not better. I'd rather be slightly less useful and reliably non-disruptive than try to help and mess up the rhythm that the family already has.

stupid question: I'm a backend engineer, I have on-call this Sunday and I forgot to swap with anyone. flight lands in São Paulo Friday night, funeral Saturday, return tickets are Tuesday. on-call rotation is set on PagerDuty and the dashboard is being weird, won't let me do an override past Friday for some reason, just spinning the loader. I know this isn't your job but everything is on top of everything else right now.

is there a clean way to draft a slack message to my manager and the team channel that asks for emergency cover without writing 'my girlfriend's mom died' in those exact words? I don't want to make it about me. I want to ask once, get cover, move on. tone has to be: this is real, please don't ask follow-up questions, I'll explain when I'm back. that's it.

the manager is fine, she'll just say yes, it's the team channel that I'm worried about because there's like two guys who will reply with sad-face emojis and one who will say nothing and that asymmetry is going to make me angry on the worst week of my life.

for the PagerDuty thing — if you have any idea what would cause an override-past-friday to spin the loader, I'd take a sanity check. I cleared cache, tried different browser, tried the mobile app. the mobile app doesn't even let me see the calendar past tomorrow. I checked status.pagerduty.com and they show no incidents. could be a session issue, could be a permissions thing on the schedule (I don't think I'm a schedule manager, I think I just have my own override rights). worst case I'll DM Daniela and she'll override it from her side, but I'd rather not bother her at 11pm with this on top of everything else.

team composition for context: it's me, Andrés (junior, four months in, can't carry on-call alone), Carla (she's the one I'd normally swap with, but she's on parental leave), and two ICs in the platform team who aren't on routes-team rotation but could pinch-hit if Daniela approves. so realistic answer is one of the platform folks pinch-hits, with my code lookup running through Andrés. that's what the slack message needs to enable. minimum coordination, maximum coverage.

one more thing — if the slack message gets a 'sure, we got you, take care of yourself' kind of warm reply from one of the seniors, that'd be helpful for moving the conversation on. if it gets a flood of follow-up questions, that would be the worst possible thing for me right now. so the message should also include a clean 'I'll fill in the team in a couple of weeks when I'm back' line. preempt the curiosity loop.

Camila just got the bereavement leave approved. her firm gave her two weeks initial, said they'd extend if needed. she's not crying about it but she said 'good' in this voice I've never heard. flat. I packed her bag for her tonight because she couldn't decide which shoes to bring. I packed two pairs because I don't know which she'll want. her partner at the firm sent a hand-written note that arrived by courier at 9pm — actual paper, actual ink. that's a kind of class I don't see often. Camila read it twice and folded it into the bag. she is asleep now. I am not asleep. I have a 6am alarm for the airport. I should also be asleep. I'm not.

I work at Brújula. last-mile logistics, series B, like 120 of us in the CDMX office. we have this end-of-quarter customer review next thursday and I was supposed to be the one demoing the new dispatch routing dashboard. obviously I'm not going to be there. I sent my manager a heads up but she's in São Francisco at a leadership offsite this week and won't see it for like 9 hours.

what I'm asking — can you help me write a dead-simple handoff doc for whichever poor soul has to pick this up? it's a 20 minute demo, I have the slides, I have the canned data. they'd just need to know where the prod-like staging URL is, which routes to hit during the live walkthrough, and what to say if someone asks about the new geo clustering algorithm. I have notes scattered in confluence and notion and a drawing on a napkin that I photographed.

I'm not great at writing for handoff. my drafts always assume the reader knows everything I know. would you be willing to review what I write and tell me where I'm assuming context the reader doesn't have? I need this to be airtight enough that someone with no prior knowledge of the demo can step in cold on like three days notice.

the likely picker-upper is either Felipe (from sales engineering, he's done these demos before but doesn't know the new geo clustering details) or Andrés (my junior, knows the codebase but has never demoed for an exec audience and will get nervous). if I draft for Felipe, Andrés can use it. if I draft for Andrés, Felipe will be irritated at the over-explanation. so I think I draft for Andrés and append a 'TL;DR for Felipe' section at the top that says 'if you have done this before, scroll to the canned-routes section, the geo clustering question goes A/B/C, the rest of the doc is for someone seeing this for the first time.' that should work for both readers.

the customer is one of our top three accounts — a beverage distributor with about 400 trucks across the metro area — and they're the ones who pushed us to build the geo clustering feature in the first place. so they will ask about it. they will also ask about peak-hour reassignment, which is a feature still in alpha. for that one I want a script: 'we're testing peak-hour reassignment with two pilot fleets, results coming in q2 review.' that's all anyone should say. don't extemporize. don't promise dates.

we're at the airport. terminal 2 of the CDMX airport, gate 31, flight is delayed by an hour. Camila is in the wheelchair line because her ankle is swollen — apparently grief makes some people retain water? I didn't know that was a thing. I'm watching her sit there in this oversized cardigan that used to be her mom's, holding her phone face-down on her knee, and there are tears on her cheeks but she isn't crying, like the tears are just leaking out without her permission.

our apartment in Roma Norte is empty for the first time in five months. we left the espresso machine on standby, the dog of the upstairs neighbor was barking when we left because we were dragging our suitcases past her door at 5pm and it freaked her out. that's the most banal sentence I've written all day and yet it's the one I'm thinking about.

what I want to ask you — when we land, the first thing we have to do is go to her dad's apartment. he's been alone with her body for like 30 hours. her body is at the funeraria, but he's been alone with the ghost for 30 hours. and Camila is going to walk through that door and her mom is not going to come around the corner asking if she ate on the plane. how do I — what do I do in that moment? do I hang back? do I go in first? do I just be wallpaper?

this is the third time I've asked you a 'what do I do' question. I'm sorry. I'll stop. I just don't have anyone else to rehearse this with.

actually one more thing while we're here. Camila just looked over at me and said 'tô bem' — Portuguese, not Spanish, which she only does when she's been thinking in her mom's voice. she said it twice. then she went back to looking at the floor. I gave her my hand and she took it. I'm not going to ask her what she meant. I think she meant: I am okay enough that you don't need to do anything right now. that's a particular kind of okay that women in my family also speak in — a permission to be quiet. I will accept the permission and be quiet.

the other passengers around us have been kind. an older woman noticed Camila's swollen ankle and gave her a small bottle of water without saying anything. a couple traveling with a baby moved to give us a wider seat row. these are tiny things. but the cumulative effect is making me less rage-filled at the world for the duration of this trip, which I will take. small kindnesses from strangers — credit them all to Bea, in my head.

ok we landed. it's 11:40pm São Paulo time. we're at Hugo's apartment in Vila Madalena and Camila is in the bedroom with him and they're talking in this low murmur that I can hear through the wall but can't really make out. they've been in there for an hour and a half. I'm in the kitchen. I made tea. I drank the tea. I made another tea. I unloaded the dishwasher because nobody had unloaded the dishwasher in days, there was a film of dust on the top rack. I cleaned the counters. I wiped down the stove. I started to clean the oven and then realized I was avoiding sitting down and I forced myself to sit at the kitchen table and breathe.

I'm trying to keep busy because if I sit still for ten seconds my jaw starts shaking. I packed my mate cup in my carry-on, the one Mama Lupita gave me back in 2017, with my name engraved on the rim — Seb, in tiny block letters, slightly off-center because she had it engraved at this small place in San Pedro Tlaquepaque and the artisan was a half-blind viejito who joked he had to estimate where the middle of the cup was — I was so afraid of losing it on the plane that I checked the carry-on three times during the flight to make sure it was still in the pouch. it's the only thing of hers I have. I drink yerba from it every single morning. it has six years of cured tannins in the wood. when she gave it to me, in 2017, she said in Spanish 'para que tomes mate cuando trabajes lejos de mí' — so you can drink mate while you work far from me. I had already moved out of the family home by then but I was still in Guadalajara, just on the other side of the city. she meant it as a benediction for whatever further-away life I'd eventually have. and now here I am in São Paulo, drinking from her cup, four years after she died, in a kitchen where another grandmother just stopped existing.

I left the yerba in CDMX because I forgot, like a dummy. I'll have to find a place to buy yerba mate in São Paulo tomorrow which is going to be funny because the city is full of Argentinean expats but the chain supermarkets here mostly carry the Brazilian erva mate which is a different cure and tastes like wet hay to me. there's a difference between yerba mate and erva mate that takes about three weeks of drinking it daily to internalize. Argentinean yerba is dry-cured, slightly bitter, pulverized fine, sometimes with stems. Brazilian erva is green-cured, tastes greener, milder, more like a fresh-grass tea. I'm a yerba snob. Camila's late mom used to laugh at me for being an Argentine-style mate snob in her kitchen. Beatriz would offer me erva mate as a joke and I'd pretend to weep into the cup. she would refill it. she would mock me in Portuguese. she would tell Camila in Spanish that I was high maintenance. it was a bit. it was our bit. I will never have that bit again with anyone.

I'm typing in English to you right now and I just realized I haven't spoken English in like nine hours. on the plane it was Spanish to Camila and Portuguese to the flight attendant who recognized our last name from the manifest and asked if we were related to a Beatriz Ferreira who used to teach at Mackenzie because the attendant's mother had been a student of hers in 1998. I am not making this up. she remembered Beatriz teaching this one specific class on Lina Bo Bardi's housing typologies — Bea apparently used to talk about Bo Bardi the way some people talk about saints, this whole mystical thing about how Bo Bardi understood the relationship between bodies and concrete in a way no other architect of her generation did. the flight attendant said her mother had kept her notes from that class for almost thirty years. her face when she made the connection — she went pale, then she came back ten minutes later with a glass of water and a packet of cookies for Camila and put her hand on Camila's wrist and didn't say anything, just held her wrist for like ten seconds and then walked away. I almost lost it. I had to look at the seatback monitor for the rest of the descent and pretend I was tracking the flight path. my Portuguese is supposedly B1, which means I can hold a conversation but I lose the plot when emotion shows up. I lost the plot tonight three times. once at immigration when the agent asked routine questions and his voice was kind for some reason and that was almost too much. once at the baggage claim when an elderly woman asked me in Portuguese if I needed help with a heavy bag and I said no, obrigado, and could not say anything else for a minute. and once on the way to Hugo's apartment in the uber, when the driver was listening to MPB on the radio and a song came on that Bea had quoted on a whatsapp audio message to Camila about three weeks ago — Caetano Veloso, 'sampa', a song about São Paulo specifically — and I had to look hard out the window at the lights on the avenida 23 de maio so I wouldn't fall apart in the back seat.

the practical thing: the funeraria called Hugo about an hour after we got here to confirm the open-casket arrangement for tomorrow morning. he came out of the bedroom to take the call, his eyes were red but his voice was perfectly level, and he said yes, yes, sim, sim, obrigado, dez horas. ten o'clock. that's when I'll see her. I have not seen her since may, when we were here for her 67th birthday. she hugged me at the door and told me to look after her daughter and I said yes ma'am like I always said. she had baked a cake. she let me eat two pieces. she did not know she was sick yet. she was diagnosed in june, after we'd flown back to CDMX, and she lived four months from diagnosis to today. four months. that's how pancreatic cancer works. it's a stopwatch. tomorrow she will be in a box. I will look at her. I will not recognize her face because that's how dead people look — not like themselves, like a wax replica of themselves — but I will look at her anyway because Camila will need me to.

I can't get my body to believe that this is the same week as last week. last tuesday I was in our office in Roma Norte arguing with Andrés about whether we should use serde with a custom serializer or write a thin wrapper for a JSON schema that one of the integration partners keeps changing. five days ago. that argument now feels like it happened to a different person. I have these moments where I forget that anything is wrong and then it lands on me again like an anvil and I have to grip the edge of the kitchen counter to not stagger. five days ago I was annoyed about a JSON schema. tonight I am alone in a dead woman's kitchen waiting for her widower and her daughter to come out of a bedroom.

later tonight I have to write the words for the family whatsapp. it's tomorrow morning São Paulo time but the family is mostly across Brazil and one cousin in Portugal so the messages will keep coming through the night and they'll want to see something from us, from me specifically because Camila isn't going to be in any state to type to a group chat. so I have to say something in Portuguese, in this group chat with sixteen of her aunts and uncles and cousins and their spouses, and it has to be short and dignified and not a translated chilango stumble. my Portuguese has been improving over the last year — Camila's family has been Whatsapping me directly since around month two of my Duolingo streak — but condolence Portuguese is a register I have never been in before. condolence Portuguese is the part of the language that you cannot learn from a Duolingo tree. it's the dead-grandmother register. it's the register where the wrong word reads as flippant and the wrong syllable reads as theatrical.

here's a draft I just wrote in my notes app:

'queria só dizer que estou aqui com a Camila e com o seu Hugo. não tenho palavras à altura da Bea. ela me ensinou a comer brigadeiro sem culpa e a chamar minha sogra pelo primeiro nome. vamos lembrar dela com tudo, com tudo. um abraço a todos, Seb.'

is that — is that ok? does it sound like I tried? I don't want to sound like I phoned it in but I also don't want to be flowery in someone else's language. I have a horror of being the foreign son-in-law who tries too hard. tell me how it sounds. fix the grammar if it's broken. if 'chamar pelo primeiro nome' is weird in this context tell me. if 'à altura da Bea' is too poetic, cut it. if 'com tudo, com tudo' is too repetitive, fix it. I'll cut anything that makes me look like I'm performing instead of grieving. I'd rather sound clumsy and real than sound polished and false. translate it back to English in your reply so I can see how the register reads when stripped of the language. that'll tell me whether the structure is right under the words.

a thing I want to write before I send the message — there's a small detail in the draft that I want to keep no matter what you suggest. the part about brigadeiro and 'chamar pelo primeiro nome'. that's specific. that's what Bea actually did. the first time we visited her she made me eat a whole tray of brigadeiro and she said 'menino, você tá em casa, come' and she would not let me leave the kitchen until I had eaten three. and she insisted from the second visit onward that I call her Bea, not 'dona Beatriz', because she said 'sou tua família, não tua professora.' I'm your family, not your teacher. I want both of those memories in the message because they are the memory of Bea I most want to broadcast to the family group — not the academic Bea, not the architect Bea, but the kitchen Bea who made me feel like I belonged in a Brazilian house from the second I walked into it. if you suggest changes that lose those two memories I will reject the suggestions. everything else, you can polish.

the other thing for the message — I want to sign 'Seb' rather than 'Sebastián' because that's what most of her family calls me. Bea herself called me Sebastián, full version, with a Brazilian-Portuguese 's' that I always loved hearing. but in the family group everyone calls me Seb. signing Sebastián would be more formal but would also be a small note to her in particular — she's not reading the message, but I'd be writing to her when I signed it. I'm going back and forth. I think I sign 'Seb' for the public message and tonight, separately, I'll write the longer thing I have been drafting in a private notebook and I'll address that one to Sebastián-as-Bea-said-it, because that one is for me and for her, not for the family.

thank you for being awake at 1am with me. I know that's a strange thing to thank a chat assistant for. I don't have anywhere else to put this. Camila is in the bedroom with her father and I am in this kitchen alone holding the cup my dead grandmother gave me, surrounded by a Brazilian household full of artifacts of another dead grandmother, typing in English to a stateless system. I am as alone as I have ever been. and yet I am also not alone — I am here because my partner is here and her family is on the other side of that wall. I think both things are true at the same time. I'll send the whatsapp message in the morning and I'll see her at ten and I'll do whatever the day asks. for now I'm going to make a third tea, finally drink it, and try to get two hours of sleep before sunrise.

we just had breakfast. Hugo, Camila's dad, made coffee for the three of us. I learned this morning he's 68. I always thought he was younger because he runs almost every day, this skinny guy who used to be a civil engineer in São Paulo before he retired, he has this gentle precise way of explaining things that always made me feel calm. he told me at breakfast that he's been married to Bea since 1981. forty-five years. his hand was shaking when he poured the coffee. he didn't comment on it. I didn't either. Camila just put her hand on his wrist for a second, the same way the flight attendant had touched her on the plane.

he asked me if I would help carry the casket today. like, physically carry it. me and three of his nephews and one cousin from Recife. I said yes immediately. did I do the right thing? I think I did. I would have done anything he asked in that moment. I just wasn't expecting the question and I think my face showed it for a second.

the funeraria is at 10. I'm wearing the only dark suit I own — bought it in CDMX two years ago for a client dinner and never wore it since. it's tight in the shoulders. I look like a kid in his dad's clothes. nobody is going to notice.

the morning has had this quality where every small object means more than it should. Hugo's coffee press. the sugar bowl with a tiny chip on the rim. Camila's mom's reading glasses still on the side table next to the window where she always read in the mornings. I have been deliberately not looking at the glasses but my eyes keep going there. they are folded. someone — Hugo, probably, in the middle of the night — folded them and set them down. I don't know why that detail is the thing that keeps cutting me but it is.

I'm going to make sure I drink water before we leave. I'm going to make sure Camila eats something else, even half a banana. I'm going to make sure Hugo has tissues in his jacket pocket. small caretaking. the only thing I can do.

the casket-carrying assignment is not what I was prepared for. I had been thinking my role today was 'be present, hold Camila's hand at the right moments, eat what is put in front of me, do not say anything stupid.' adding 'be a physical pallbearer' to that list is more responsibility than I was ready for an hour ago and I am now ready for it. but I am going to spend the next hour mentally rehearsing the act of walking with weight and not stumbling. I'm not athletic-graceful. I do kickboxing, which is short bursts of intensity, not slow sustained carrying. okay. focus. left foot, right foot, do not drop your end.

we carried her. five of us. I was on the back-left corner. it was lighter than I expected which made it worse somehow. Hugo on the front-right, his brother on the front-left, Camila's cousin Rodrigo behind Hugo, and another cousin whose name I did not catch behind the brother. we walked maybe forty meters. the gravel was loud. nobody spoke. Camila walked behind us holding a single white rose. I kept my eyes on the back of Hugo's head the whole way. that was my whole strategy. just don't lose Hugo.

wake-after-the-burial is at Hugo's brother's house in Pinheiros. I just walked in and there is a full churrasco set up in the back garden. like, a guy is grilling picanha right now. it's 4pm. I have been vegetarian for four years and I am at a Brazilian wake which is a meat festival held in honor of the dead. somehow this didn't occur to me until I walked through the door and the smell hit me.

I'm not going to make a thing of it. I'll eat the rice and the farofa and the cheese bread and I'll smile and I'll make sure my plate looks full from across the room. Camila's tia Marta keeps trying to put picanha on my plate every time she walks past and I keep doing this dance of 'no obrigado, já comi muito' and she looks at me like I've said something deeply offensive and walks away muttering. it's funny, in a sideways grief-funeral way. I think Bea would have laughed at this.

I'm hiding in a corner with a beer that I'm not drinking because I'm jet lagged and don't want to add alcohol to the disaster. people I've never met keep coming up to introduce themselves as cousins. one of them — Daniel, from Curitiba — just told me Bea used to talk about me as 'aquele mexicano que não come carne nem entende ainda o futebol brasileiro'. that mexican who doesn't eat meat and still doesn't get brazilian football. she said that to her cousin from Curitiba apparently. it's the most Bea sentence I've ever heard. I'm sitting in this corner trying not to laugh-cry in public.

the other thing about wakes is that they last forever. it has been three hours and we are nowhere near done. someone just brought out a tray of brigadeiro. someone else has started telling a story about Bea at a beach in Ubatuba in 1992 that involves a coconut and a sunburn and Hugo wincing in the corner. I am understanding maybe 70% of the Portuguese being thrown around because everyone is half-shouting and the kids are running through the room and three different conversations are happening at the same table. I gave up on tracking Camila's whereabouts and decided to trust she will find me when she needs me. she found me twenty minutes ago, drank water from my glass, kissed my forehead, and went back into the kitchen with her aunts. that's the rhythm we're in. I am the man in the corner. she is the daughter doing the work.

ok I know it's tasteless to be checking work during a funeral wake but I literally have nothing else to do for the next two hours and Andrés just sent me a slack message at 5:14pm with 'sorry to bother — got a sec?' which in Andrés-language means he's been stuck on something for at least 90 minutes.

the error he sent:

thread 'tokio-runtime-worker' panicked at 'called `Result::unwrap()` on an `Err` value: PoolTimedOut'

stack ends at sqlx connection acquire. our connection pool is set to max 10. this is in the staging RouteOpt-ish prototype I've been playing with. he's running load with k6 at 200 RPS for two minutes and the pool times out around the 90s mark.

gut tells me it's a connection leak — somewhere a future is held but never resolves and the connection isn't being returned. could also be that the queries themselves are blocking past the acquire timeout. could you sketch what the most-likely culprits would be in axum + sqlx land? I'll send him bullet points to check tomorrow when he's back at his desk. I just don't want him sitting in front of this thing all night unable to ship the perf test. and frankly it's nice to think about a database for ten minutes instead of a casket.

will respond to him later. just want my own head straight first.

for the bullet list I want to send him, my current top-five suspects in rough probability order: (1) some endpoint is doing `query_one` followed by branching logic that holds the connection across an `await` boundary on something that can stall; (2) the connection pool's `idle_timeout` is shorter than the load test's slow-tail latencies, so the pool reaps connections that are still about to be used and the next request stalls on acquire; (3) we have a query that's getting picked up without a transaction and hanging on a row-level lock from a parallel test write; (4) the staging postgres has fewer max_connections than I think (worth asking the platform team — maybe 20 instead of 100); (5) tokio-runtime-worker pool size mismatch with the sqlx pool, so workers are oversubscribed and queries queue behind already-running ones.

(1) is the highest-probability bug class for a dev who's four months into a rust codebase. (2) is the highest-probability infra-config bug. (3) is rarer but happens. (4) and (5) are the kind of thing he'd never check on his own and that's where my forty more months of experience earn their pay.

it's 1:30am again. funeral day is over. wake is over. Hugo went to bed at midnight. Camila and I are in her childhood bedroom which is preserved perfectly from when she was 16 — there are still posters on the wall, Belle and Sebastian and a black-and-white photograph of Brasília that she took on a school trip in 2010, and a tattered paper map of Buenos Aires with neighborhoods circled in red marker. we are sleeping in a full-sized bed under a poster of the architect Lina Bo Bardi. I cannot describe how surreal this is. her stuffed animals are on a shelf. there's a paperback copy of 'Cien años de soledad' on the nightstand that has her teenage handwriting in the margins. I have been gently terrified to disturb anything in this room, like I'm a museum visitor who has been mistakenly admitted to the curator's storage area.

she fell asleep about an hour ago. I'm sitting in the desk chair across the room with the lamp on its dimmest setting, watching her chest rise and fall. she keeps making this small sound when she exhales. I can't tell if it's a sob or a snore. it might be both. it sounds the way a window catches when the wind hits it just right.

I called my mom from the hallway tonight. my mom is Isabela, 62, retired pediatric nurse, lives in Guadalajara. she retired two years ago after thirty-four years of bouncing kids on her hip and singing to them while she gave them shots. she has a thing she does with parents — it's almost a magic trick — where she can de-escalate a hysterical mother of a feverish three-year-old in about thirty seconds with a low, slow voice and a hand on the mother's wrist. I've watched her do it. I've been on the receiving end of it. she has this voice on the phone that sounds like a cup of warm milk. she answered on the second ring even though it was nearly midnight her time and I just said 'mami' and she said 'mijo' and I had to walk all the way down the hall to the small balcony at the end of it before I could make any other sounds.

I told her about the day. about carrying the casket. about Camila's dad's hand shaking when he poured coffee. about the picanha at the wake and Tía Marta's offended face. about the woman on the airplane who'd been Bea's student in 1998. about how Camila has barely cried in front of me but cried at the casket for like three minutes straight and then went silent again. my mom listened. she's good at the kind of listening where she only says 'sí, mijo' at exactly the right beats so you know she's there but she's not interrupting. then she said 'pobre Camila' four or five times. then she said 'pobre Hugo' twice. then she said 'tu papá quiere hablar.'

and my dad — Hugo Río, 66, retired physics teacher in Guadalajara, the man with the most spectacularly boring sense of humor on the planet, the man who once explained to me at age 11 the second law of thermodynamics by way of why the kitchen always gets messy faster than I can clean it — got on the phone and didn't say anything for about ten seconds. I could hear him breathing. then he said 'mijo, le dices a Camila que yo tenía un compañero del Tec, allá por los ochenta, que se llamaba Mauricio, perdió a su mamá a los veintiocho. nunca se le fue. pero se le hizo más liviano. se le hizo más liviano. dile eso, por favor.'

Mauricio. it never gets lighter, it only gets less heavy. or, rather: it stops getting heavier. that's what my dad said in his physics teacher way, like he was explaining the difference between an asymptote and a limit. there's a kind of comfort that only a physics teacher can give you, where the universe is a mechanism and grief is a quantity, and the quantity has a half-life, and the half-life is real even if it's not soothing in the moment. and I stood on the balcony of my dead mother-in-law's apartment in São Paulo and I cried for the first time all week, big silent shoulders-shaking sobs, into the phone where my dad couldn't see me but could obviously hear, and he just said 'sí, mijo' the way my mom does and waited it out and then said 'okay. anything else? do you need money?' which is so absolutely my dad it nearly knocked me over. he is incapable of letting an emotional moment end without offering a logistical resource. that's how he loves you. that's how he loved me through my whole adolescence. that's how he loved me when Mama Lupita died. 'you doing okay? you need money? you eating? you sleeping? need anything from the house? want me to ship you abuela's molcajete? do you have enough sheets?' a list of practical offerings as a love language.

they're hours away in Guadalajara. it's so weird how when something like this happens, the people who aren't there are sometimes the ones who can hold you. Hugo is in the next room and I love him but he can't hold anyone right now. my mom can hold me from 7000 kilometers away because she's not in the storm. there's a thing therapists say about how grief travels in concentric circles and you grieve outward — closest in goes inward to the family at the center, farther out gets the comfort to give. that's exactly what's happening. tonight, sitting on Hugo's balcony in São Paulo, my mother is two circles out from me, and her job is to comfort me, and my job is to comfort Camila, and Camila's job is to comfort her father at the center. nobody at the center has anyone left to give to. that's why people in the center collapse. they have nothing flowing in.

I'm thinking a lot about Mama Lupita tonight. my abuela. she died in 2019. November, right after my birthday. she lived with my parents for the last three years of her life — she'd had a bad fall in 2016 and after the rehab she didn't go back to her own house, she moved into the spare room at my parents' place in Guadalajara and stayed. I lived in CDMX by then. I came home for Christmas, for her birthdays, for some weekends. I never quite forgave myself for not getting on a plane to see her in the September before, when she was already declining and my mom called me twice and said 'maybe come this weekend, mijo' and I had a sprint deadline, I had this thing at Wizeline, I was working on a banking project, and I told myself I'd go in October. she went into hospice in early October. she stopped recognizing people by the third week. I made it for two days at the very end. she didn't know who I was. she squeezed my hand when I held it but I think she was squeezing anything that touched her. once, briefly, she looked at me and said 'Hugo' which was my dad's name, and my dad had stepped out of the room ten minutes earlier, and I just said 'sí, abuela' and let her think I was him. that wasn't dishonest. it gave her a small calm. but I have replayed that moment a hundred times. she didn't know who I was at the end. and the September I didn't visit, she might have known.

that regret has not gone away. I lit a candle for her on her birthday last march and I sat with the candle for an hour at our kitchen table in Roma Norte and I apologized to her out loud as if she could hear me. I'm not saying that fixed anything. I'm saying tonight, at 1:30am in this bedroom in Vila Madalena, I'm thinking about her. I'm thinking that Bea and Lupita are now both ghosts in the rooms of women I love. there's a kind of reverberation between two grandmothers' deaths that I did not know was a thing until tonight. Bea is freshly dead and Lupita has been dead for six years and yet they are both more present in this apartment than my own physical body is.

I grew up in Guadalajara. lived there until I was thirty, then moved to CDMX. Guadalajara is where my parents are. it's where Lupita is buried. it's where the whole soundtrack of my childhood lives — the slap of the cards in the kitchen during my dad's Sunday loteria games, my mom's iron cooker hissing on the stove at 6pm on a Tuesday, the smell of the rains coming in over Lake Chapala in the spring, the way the stadium roar sounded from our living room when Chivas were playing on Sundays. I have a Tapatío's affection for Guadalajara that I am not subtle about. anyone who has talked to me for ten minutes knows I am Tapatío and that I think Guadalajara has the best food and the most beautiful late-afternoon light and the friendliest taxi drivers in the country. all of which is, I admit, partially nostalgic and partially defensive of the city I left. tonight I miss it like I missed it the first month after I moved. I miss it as a place where nothing is happening. I miss being someone whose job for the day is to walk to the corner store for tortillas.

I'm going to fly to Guadalajara next month. just for a long weekend. when we get back to CDMX and the dust settles a bit. I want to see my mom and my dad. I want to put my arms around them. I want my mom to make me her chiles en nogada and tell me to eat more. I want my dad to fall asleep in the armchair while I'm trying to talk to him about something work-related. I want to feel them as alive bodies, not as voices on a balcony. tonight on the phone they were absolutely there and absolutely far. I want them close.

I don't have a question. I just couldn't sleep and didn't want to type to Camila in case she woke up. you've been a strange but consistent presence this week. I appreciate that there's somewhere I can put this thing my dad said about Mauricio. dile que se le hizo más liviano. I will. tomorrow morning I'll find the right time and I'll tell her. it'll mean more from him than from me anyway. fathers carry messages that sons can't carry. there's a different gravity to it. she'll know that the message came from a 66-year-old man in Guadalajara who has thought about it for forty years and who said it to his own son so the son could carry it across the apartment to her. that's a kind of relay. that's a kind of family infrastructure I didn't know we had until tonight.

one more thing before I let myself try to sleep. I have been thinking, sitting in this chair in this bedroom that is preserved like a small museum, about what kind of father I want to be if Camila and I have a kid. I never have a clear answer. I have not been ready in five years. I am still not ready. but I am starting to be slightly more ready than I was. and tonight specifically I am thinking about what kind of father Hugo has been to Camila over forty-three years and what kind of father my own dad has been to me over thirty-three years and what I would steal from each of them. from Hugo I would steal the gentle precision — the way he explains a thing slowly and only once, the way he never raises his voice, the way he listens like he is taking notes. from my dad I would steal the practical-resource love language — the way he says 'do you need money? are you eating?' as a way of saying 'I love you and I don't know how else to say it.' I would also steal from my own mother — the magic-trick voice she uses to de-escalate. I would also steal from Bea, posthumously — the way she made every guest feel like family from the second they walked into her kitchen, the way she said 'come, menino' as if it were a benediction. those are the four corners of the parenting style I would want to build. I would not be ready tomorrow. but tonight I noticed that I am closer to ready than I was last week. some things become clearer in the proximity of death. the question of what kind of parent I would be is one of them. I am writing it down so I do not forget that I noticed it. okay. closing the chat. trying to sleep. four hours until the next morning.

Andrés just messaged me 'never mind, found it, was a `?` on a tx that wasn't being committed before drop'. classic. left a transaction guard hanging. I should make him write up a one-paragraph note for our internal wiki tomorrow so we don't all step on the same rake. four-month junior, made an embarrassing mistake, debugged it himself before I could pile on. that's a good shape. I'm going to tell him that on monday in our 1:1. positive reinforcement on the recovery, not the bug. the bug is just a bug. anyone could write it. the recovery is the part that matters.

Camila just woke up. it's 4am. she came over to the desk where I'd been sitting since I last typed to you, sat in my lap like a little kid, put her face into my neck, and just — stayed there. didn't say anything. didn't cry, didn't move. her hands were in fists in front of her chest like a baby's hands. I held her for I don't know how long.

then she said, into my collar, 'you smell like home.' three words. that's all she said. then she fell asleep on my chest in the chair and I had to carry her back to the bed at five.

I just — I'm putting that down somewhere because I don't want to forget it. she said you smell like home. and I don't even wear cologne. I think she meant the laundry detergent we use, or the soap, or just the fact of me being a body she has been falling asleep against for five years. but it sounded like the most generous thing anyone has ever said to me in my life. I will think about that sentence for the rest of my life.

that's all. that's the update. she's asleep again. we have to be at Hugo's brother's at 11 for what they're calling a 'reunião familiar' which I think means we just sit around eating cake and people cry and tell stories. fine. I can do that.

the other thing I keep returning to in my head is what I want to remember about Bea, deliberately, before the memories start dissolving into general 'mother-in-law' shape. specific things. she had a particular way of saying my name — Sebastián, full thing, no nickname, with a Brazilian-Portuguese 's' — that made me feel like I was being addressed by a teacher. she used to wear linen pants in summer with a cotton blouse and rope sandals and look like she had walked out of a Globo telenovela about a successful architect. she made the best moqueca I have ever eaten in my life and never wrote down the recipe and now nobody will. she used to argue with Hugo about politics across the dinner table in this respectful, vigorous way that I wanted to study. she was, for me, the model of how to be married. I will remember those things. write them down to remember them more.

morning. coffee in hand. half-decent night's sleep finally. Camila is in the shower. quick logistics question — I told my manager I'd be back online wednesday. that's two days from now. but Camila and I were talking last night about extending our flight to friday because Hugo wants us to help him sort out some paperwork at the funeraria for some reason on thursday and he can't do it alone.

I'm fine extending. but I'm not sure how to phrase the slack message to my manager when I just told her wednesday. I don't want it to read like I'm flaking. I want it to read like the situation is unfolding and I'm being a responsible partner and son-in-law and the work will be there when I get back.

draft me three options at increasing levels of formality? I'll pick the one that fits her tone. she's pretty laid back, has a low-drama way of communicating, but she's also new in role and I don't want to test her patience this early.

for the work-side balance — I can be available for one hour a day in CDMX morning hours (which is afternoon for me in São Paulo) for any urgent code reviews or sync questions. that's an offer I'd like to lead with so it doesn't read as 'I am totally offline for two more days.' it reads as 'I am partially online and prioritizing family but I haven't dropped the team.' I think that's the right framing.

the paperwork at the funeraria, for context, is the post-burial documentation: certified copies of the death certificate that Hugo needs to close out a couple of joint accounts and update some pension things. it shouldn't take all day on thursday but Hugo is not a paperwork person and Camila wants to be there to make sure he isn't alone signing important things. valid reason. I want my message to convey 'real specific reason, ends thursday, back online friday morning' without going into account-closing detail.

format preferences: keep it three short sentences max. no apologies — I am not in a place to apologize for grief logistics. no over-explaining — she has all the context she needs. include a concrete date for return (friday morning CDMX time) so she can plan around it. include the offer of one hour daily so she has a release valve if anything is on fire. close with something that confirms I have not lost track of the work — maybe a half-line about which open thread I'm still tracking. then sign off.

we went to the reunião familiar. it was at Hugo's brother's house in Pinheiros, the same garden where the wake was. Hugo's brother is named Joaquim. he's a year older than Hugo, retired civil engineer too, married to a woman named Lourdes who keeps trying to feed everyone. Lourdes today specifically tried to put a bandeja paulista on my plate which is a giant platter of about six different cuts of grilled meat — picanha, fraldinha, alcatra, costela, linguiça, and a piece of chicken that didn't quite belong on this platter but was put there anyway — and I had to do the polite refusal dance again. she hadn't been at yesterday's wake so she didn't know I was vegetarian and was visibly confused for about ten seconds, and then she said, em português, 'mas você é homem, jovem, mexicano, e não come carne?' and I said no, ma'am, I don't, and she shook her head and made a grilled provolone for me on a separate skewer and brought it to me with this look of mild personal offense that I find deeply moving in older Latin women. she also brought me a side dish of farofa, a dish of cheese bread, a salad of palm hearts, and a small bowl of rice. she made me a feast just to compensate for the absence of one platter. that's the kind of gesture I cannot say no to. I ate everything she put in front of me. I am stuffed.

so. about the vegetarian thing. it's been four years. four years come this november. people ask why and I usually give them the short version which is 'I just felt better without it.' the long version is more specific. and tonight, surrounded by ten different cuts of beef in a Brazilian backyard at the end of the day I helped bury Camila's mother, the long version is what's in my head.

in 2022 my dad had a triple bypass. open heart. they cracked his sternum like a walnut and replaced three coronary arteries with veins from his leg. he had been having angina for like nine months and ignoring it because he is a sixty-something Mexican man and that's how a sixty-something Mexican man processes chest pain. he would get the squeezing in his chest while he was carrying groceries up the apartment steps and he would call it 'agruras,' heartburn, and reach for an antacid. my mom — who is a nurse, who knows angina when she sees it — finally lost her patience after the third 'agruras' incident in a single week and physically drove him to the cardiologist's office in Guadalajara without telling him where they were going. she said she had errands. she walked him into Dr. Estrada's office and said 'doctor, examínelo' and my dad looked at her like she had betrayed him in front of god. by the time the EKG was done his face had changed. they scheduled the surgery within ten days.

I flew home from CDMX for the surgery. I took two weeks off Wizeline. mom and I sat in the waiting room at the IMSS hospital for seven hours. seven hours. I have never had a worse seven hours in my life — not even this week, weirdly, not even saturday at the funeraria. because at least at the funeraria the worst had already happened. in that waiting room the worst was still possible at any moment. the surgeon came out at one point and said something I didn't fully process and then went back in. my mom held a rosary. I don't even like rosaries and I held the other end of it. there was a fluorescent tube light directly over our row of chairs that flickered every twelve seconds, and to this day if a fluorescent light flickers in a hallway my chest tightens. I had a vending-machine coffee at hour four that was so bad I'm not sure it was technically coffee. I prayed at hour five. I had not prayed since I was nineteen and I prayed at hour five, the words came up from somewhere old and prefab, the Padre Nuestro and the Ave María on a kind of muscle-memory loop, and I'm not telling you this to sound profound, I'm telling you because I want it on record that I was once a man who prayed in a hallway out of pure animal terror.

he came through. he's fine. he goes for walks every morning, he plays chess with his old colleagues from the school, he's basically at full strength. the surgical scar runs from the base of his throat to the bottom of his sternum and looks like a thin pink line now, mostly faded. he wears tank tops in the summer and doesn't try to hide it. but I saw the diet they put him on after — like, no salt, low fat, lean protein only, and his cardiologist gave us this twenty-minute speech about animal fats that I will never forget — and I went home to CDMX and I just stopped eating meat. didn't announce it. didn't make a thing of it. just stopped. I think Camila noticed three weeks in when she made tacos al pastor and I made myself a quesadilla and she went 'oh — wait — are you?' and I said yes and she said 'okay' and made me a couple sin carne. that was the entire conversation.

four years now. I'm fine. I get my B12 from supplements (I take a single weekly methylcobalamin tablet), I get protein from beans and tofu and eggs and dairy, I'm at the gym three times a week kicking pads. my last lipid panel was great. my last B12 reading was actually slightly above the high end of normal (the cardiologist who saw it told me to skip a couple of weeks of supplements). I haven't gotten weaker. I haven't lost muscle mass. I'm in better shape now at thirty-two than I was at twenty-seven. nobody at home gives me trouble about it anymore — my dad jokes about it, calls me 'el hippie' on the phone, but he also makes vegetable lasagna when I visit and he's stupidly proud of it. he buys nutritional yeast specifically for me, keeps a jar in his pantry that says 'PARA EL HIPPIE' on a label in his handwriting. I love that man.

the reason I'm bringing this up to you now: today, sitting at this reunião in Pinheiros surrounded by all this meat, surrounded by all these people grieving and eating and grieving and eating, I had a moment where I caught myself wondering if I'd be vegetarian for life. like, I had this thought: what if I get to my dad's age and I have a heart episode anyway. what if Bea-the-mother-in-law had cancer regardless of what she ate, and at the end her doctors said 'pancreatic, often genetic, often unpreventable' and I'm doing this stupid little ritual of avoiding picanha that doesn't actually save anyone from anything? what if the cardiologist's twenty-minute speech is, like, 60% true and 40% medical hygiene theater? what if the universe is just throwing dice and the dice land where they land?

I don't actually want to stop being vegetarian. it was never strictly about the science for me. it was about — when I saw my dad in the ICU after the surgery, with the tubes and the chest drain and the morphine making him slur, I had this very simple primitive reaction: I do not want to be in that bed in thirty years. I do not want my own son or daughter to sit in that waiting room. and the choice not to eat meat was a small daily action I could take that meant 'I see what happened and I am responding.' it was a vow more than a diet. it was a small altar I built next to my dad's hospital bed and never dismantled.

so. I think the vegetarianism is staying. but watching Camila bury her mom this weekend is making me wonder if any of it actually matters. like, if you can do everything right and still be in a coffin at 67. Bea ate moderately, didn't smoke after her thirties, exercised, walked everywhere, was thin, was sharp, was alive. and the disease found her anyway. is the right response to that fact to double down on health, or to enjoy the picanha and stop pretending I have control over anything?

I think both responses are right at different times. I think tonight is a 'do whatever brings you comfort' night. tomorrow night is a 'eat your beans' night. you alternate. the trick is not to do the wrong one on the wrong night.

the other thing I'm noticing — being vegetarian at this kind of event is its own micro-grief. like, if I were eating picanha tonight I would be eating Bea's last meals with her family. she loved picanha. she would have wanted me to eat it. when I refuse the platter I'm refusing a piece of how Bea ate, of how this family eats. and that has its own small sadness. tonight I am holding both — not eating the picanha and grieving that I'm not eating the picanha. that's a complicated thing to be doing in a backyard in Pinheiros at sundown.

I don't have a question for you. I'm just thinking out loud. eat your cake, hug your mother-in-law while you can, kick the pads at the gym on monday, drink your mate. that's the entire program. forgive the rambling — Camila is across the room talking to a cousin and I'm sitting on a step in the garden typing into a phone like a lunatic. there are children playing some kind of running-game between the legs of the adults. there's a great-aunt who has been dancing samba alone next to a speaker for twenty minutes and nobody is going to tell her to stop because she is the great-aunt who dances samba alone, that is her role at funerals in this family, and her role is sacred. I'll put the phone away in a minute. I just had to write this down so I don't lose it. some thoughts you have at funerals you only get once. you don't get to refile them next year. you have to catch them in the moment they show up or they disappear. that's why I'm typing on a step in a garden full of strangers I'm related to by marriage. catch the thoughts. log them. close the notebook.

one last thing on the vegetarian question, because tía Lourdes just walked past again and I noticed the small pang. there's a thing I want to give Camila someday — when she's ready, no rush, maybe in a year — which is to cook a whole vegetarian Brazilian meal for her in our kitchen in Roma Norte. moqueca de palmito. arroz with cilantro. a really good vegetarian feijoada with smoked tofu standing in for the carne seca. brigadeiro for dessert because brigadeiro is sacrosanct and not optional in any version of a Brazilian meal honoring Bea. I would do this on Bea's birthday next november. I would not announce it as a memorial. I would just cook it. Camila would walk in and recognize what it was and we would eat it together and not say much. that's the kind of small private ritual I want to start building for us — not big-gesture memorials, not annual public ceremonies, just dinners where Bea is at the table without being announced. that's the long game. tonight I'm going to write down 'moqueca de palmito for Bea, november' in my notebook so I don't forget. okay. closing the phone for real this time.

ok back at Hugo's apartment, evening. sympathy cards still arriving. like, paper cards. by courier. there's a stack of about thirty on the kitchen table from people who weren't at the funeral, mostly from Bea's old colleagues in the architecture school and a few from Hugo's old engineering firm and a bunch from neighbors.

Hugo asked Camila to write replies. Camila has done about four and is staring at the rest. she asked me to do six of them because I 'have a steadier hand right now.' I do not. but I will.

portuguese sympathy reply card content. short. four to six lines. tone: gracious, warm, not flowery, acknowledges their loss-of-a-friend without making it about us. signed by 'a família Ferreira-Castellanos.'

can you draft me three template variants I can adapt? one for old colleagues from Mackenzie (architecture school), one for old neighbors in Vila Madalena, one general for distant relatives. I'll tweak each to the specific person but I need scaffolding. I'm too tired to start from blank text and I don't trust my Portuguese to land the right register cold. condolence-reply Portuguese is its own beast — too formal feels insincere, too casual feels disrespectful. middle is hard.

specific phrases I want available: 'agradecemos profundamente' for the formal opening, 'a sua presença significaria muito para a Beatriz' if I'm referencing what they meant to her, 'continuaremos a honrar a memória dela' as a closer. avoid: 'descanse em paz' (Hugo doesn't like the religious framing for these cards), 'um anjo a mais no céu' (he really doesn't like that), 'a vida continua' (Camila said it makes her teeth hurt). use sparingly: 'saudades eternas' (acceptable but cliché). prefer: things specific to Bea — her teaching, her architectural eye, her warmth as a host, her cooking, her laugh.

I'll be picking which phrases to drop in based on the recipient. Mackenzie colleagues get the teaching reference. Vila Madalena neighbors get the host-and-laugh references. distant relatives get the warmth and the family-presence references. that's the rough mapping I'm working from.

oh, one more constraint — handwritten. Hugo wants every reply hand-written, not printed. so the text needs to be short enough that I can write it cleanly and not produce hand cramps over the next two hours. four to five lines per card maximum. the formality level should match the recipient's apparent age and closeness. for older recipients (Mackenzie professors in their seventies and eighties, neighbors who knew Bea since the eighties), more traditional phrasing. for younger relatives (cousins of Camila's generation), warmer and slightly less formal. that calibration is something my Portuguese ear can sort of feel but can't reliably produce — that's where I need the template variants from you most.

wrote four cards. the fifth made me cry, the one to Bea's neighbor of thirty years, an older woman named Dona Cecília who'd written 'sua mãe foi minha melhor amiga' in big letters across the top of her card. I wrote my reply and then I had to put it down and walk to the kitchen for water. Camila found me at the sink and didn't say anything, just stood next to me. that was good. I think we have maybe twenty more cards to do tomorrow. I'm going to do them in batches of five with breaks. that's the plan I have negotiated with myself. five and a glass of water. five and a walk around the block. five and ten minutes of staring at the ceiling. and so on until we're done.

small work thing keeping me sane during all this. Andrés sent me a follow-up earlier. so the connection-pool fix from yesterday went in fine but our staging k6 run is still not what we want — instead of timing out, we're now seeing tail latency at p99 spike to ~2.4 seconds when load passes 150 RPS, when our budget is 500ms. p50 is fine, p95 is borderline. classic long-tail.

this is on the python/django path still. not on anything I'm in the middle of. but Andrés is asking and Daniela is asking and I figured I'd think out loud about it for a sec.

my first hypothesis: not the DB. the python service has a sync ORM call in a hot path that I bet is GIL-blocking under load. the django ORM does a couple of queries serially when it should be one. our middleware also has a logging hook that does a synchronous network call to a metrics collector — I've been meaning to make it async for like four months.

before I send Andrés a long answer: would you sketch the standard ladder of suspects for a python/django service that's good at p50 but blows up at p99 under load? I want to see if my mental model matches yours. I'll then write up something coherent for him to investigate when I'm back on tuesday.

my current top-six suspects in order: (1) GIL contention in the request handler, especially around the orm calls; (2) gunicorn worker count badly tuned for our actual concurrency profile; (3) connection pool to postgres is sized for p50 throughput, not p99; (4) middleware serialization of metrics events is blocking on a slow collector under load; (5) the networkx graph rebuild on every request that I keep meaning to cache; (6) DB-side: vacuum lag on the route_history table, which is now at 40M rows and is part of every dispatch lookup.

(5) might just be the answer. (5) plus (4) might be the answer. and we already have (5) as a known issue with a TODO. okay. I think when I'm back I make those two fixes the priority and we re-run the load test on tuesday afternoon.

specific tasks I want to send Andrés tonight in a slack so he can do something useful while I'm offline: (a) capture a py-spy profile of the django process under the same k6 load, attach it to the slack thread; (b) check the gunicorn worker config in our staging deploy, share the config; (c) check the postgres pg_stat_statements for any single query that's responsible for a disproportionate share of the slow tail. that gives him three hours of useful work tomorrow morning and gives me three artifacts I can chew through when I'm back at a real keyboard. he likes a clear list. he doesn't like 'figure it out.' so I'll give him the list.

we have three more days here and I am starting to feel the kind of crawling tiredness that you get when you've been on grief-time for too many days in a row. grief-time is when every hour is forty-five minutes long because you keep losing the last fifteen minutes to a memory or a phone call or a sudden thought about someone or a smell that knocks you sideways. and at the end of the day you've technically lived twenty-four hours but you've only banked, like, fifteen of them. and your body keeps thinking it's done a long day's work because grief is metabolic, it actually consumes calories, and at the end of grief-time your blood sugar is shot and your nerves are shot and you go to sleep at 11pm and you wake up at 4am and you cannot get back to sleep because your brain has been chewing on the day in the background.

I miss the gym. that's a stupid thing to miss right now. I'm in São Paulo for my mother-in-law's funeral and what I'm thinking about at 11pm in the kitchen of her widower's apartment is that I haven't kicked a heavy bag in eight days and my shoulders are getting tight and my hips are getting locked up. I train three times a week — Monday, Wednesday, Friday evenings — at a place called Atlas Box in Colonia Juárez in CDMX, walked there from work for the last two years. it's the most basic kickboxing gym you can imagine, concrete floor, four heavy bags hanging from steel beams, a single ring in the middle, photos of old fighters on the wall (most of them taken in the late nineties when the gym opened, mostly Tijuana fighters because the founder Profe Esteban is from Tijuana), the smell of liniment in the locker room. I love that place. it's the place I go to leave my brain at the door for ninety minutes. it's the only environment in my life where I am not, in some way, professionally competent — I am intermediate at kickboxing, blue belt, six months from my brown belt, and intermediate is a beautiful place to live for ninety minutes a day. nobody expects me to know things. nobody expects me to lead. I am there to learn, to be corrected, to fail in front of people who I respect and who respect me. that's a rare configuration in adult life.

there's something specific about kickboxing — at least for me — that other exercise doesn't do. running gives me space to think which is the opposite of what I want. yoga makes me self-conscious because I can't tell if I'm doing it right (Camila keeps trying to get me to do yoga with her on Sundays and I keep finding excuses, that's a whole separate problem). but punching and kicking with someone two feet from your face — that demands one hundred percent of your attention. you cannot let your mind wander when there's a left hook coming. you become entirely about the next half-second. and after ninety minutes of that I am a different person. my jaw unclenches. I can hold conversations. I can sit through a meeting without my brain trying to climb out of the room. one round of pad work, two rounds on the heavy bag, three rounds of light sparring, two rounds of conditioning, one round of cool-down. that's the standard Wednesday class. by minute eighty I am unrecognizable from the version of me who walked in.

Profe Esteban — the founder — is sixty-one. used to fight pro in Tijuana in the early 90s, briefly held a regional kickboxing title, retired from the ring because of a detached retina, opened Atlas Box in 1998 and has been running it ever since. he barks at us in Spanish that's half-tijuanense, half-chilango, peppered with English words from his fighting days. he calls me 'flaco' even though I'm not flaco. he calls Pablo 'compadre' because Pablo is built like a wall and apparently reminds Profe of a guy he sparred with in 1992. I have not told Profe what's happening this week. he doesn't know I'm in São Paulo. he doesn't know Camila's mom died. when I'm back next monday I will tell him in three sentences and he will say 'mis condolencias, mijo' and put a hand on my shoulder and that will be the entire conversation about it. that's how it'll work. and then we'll do pads. that economy of grief-and-then-pads is one of the things that makes the gym what it is.

so I'm sitting here in São Paulo eight days into not training and I'm feeling it. tonight at the reunião I caught myself clenching my fists at one point because Camila's tio Roberto was being a windbag about some old family argument and I just wanted him to stop. obviously I did not punch tio Roberto. tio Roberto is 71 years old and grieving his dead sister and has an enormous opinion about the architecture department at the university where Bea taught and is also the kind of older man who confuses opinions with grief and broadcasts both on the same channel. he was not punchable. he was sad. but I noticed that I was feeling it in my hands, the way I do when I haven't trained in a while. when I haven't trained for a long stretch I become irritable in physical ways. I tighten up. I clench. my voice gets short. Camila has caught me in it before — 'go to the gym, amor' — when I haven't been in five days and I'm being a low-key jerk in the apartment.

there's a Brazilian jiu-jitsu academy two blocks from Hugo's apartment. I walked past it this morning when I went to find a place that sold yerba mate (succeeded — found a small Argentinean grocer in Pinheiros that had a brand I recognized, a Cruz de Malta with a slightly different cure than what I usually drink but absolutely close enough). I considered, for like ten seconds, walking into the BJJ place and asking if they had a beginner kickboxing class I could drop into for a single session, in cash, in a foreign country, while my girlfriend buries her mother. and I had to laugh at myself. like, my dude. read the room. you cannot drop into a Brazilian gym during your mother-in-law's funeral week. that's not a thing the universe will permit you to do gracefully.

but also — Camila said something tonight that I want to write down before I forget it. we were brushing our teeth. she said: 'you don't have to be perfect about this. you can be a person who has needs. you can go for a run tomorrow. you can call Pablo. I'm okay.' she meant it. she's not the type to perform okay-ness for anyone. she has been the most painfully honest woman in my life for five years and she meant it now. and I felt this huge wave of relief. and immediately after the relief I felt guilty for the relief, because what kind of partner sees his bereaved girlfriend and feels relief at being given permission to go for a run?

but you know what — I think she's actually right.
