I think keeping a small piece of yourself alive during someone else's grief is one of the things you owe to the relationship. if I show up back in CDMX on saturday completely depleted, no body, no sleep, no mind, then I'm useless to her over the next six months. and the next six months are going to be heavy. she's lost her mother. her dad just lost his wife. the whole orbit of her life has shifted. she will need a partner who has not collapsed. she will need a partner who can be present in the kitchen when she is sad in november, in december, in january, in march. that partner has to be a body that is not breaking. so the run tomorrow is part of the long-term plan. it's not selfish. it's strategic.

so tomorrow morning I'm going to go for a run along the rio Pinheiros. there's a path. it's flat. it's allegedly safe before 8am. I'll do six or seven kilometers, come back, shower, make coffee, sit with whatever the day brings. just one small ritual that says 'Seb is still in here.' and on monday when I'm back in CDMX I'll go to Atlas Box even if I have to take a half-day off work to make it. I will tell Profe what happened and he will say his three sentences and we will do pads.

related, completely unrelated, here's a small Portuguese question while I'm thinking about it — when I message Camila's tia Marta tomorrow to say thank you for hosting today, is 'agradeço pela hospitalidade' weird in a tia-context, or does that come across as too formal? she's family but she's older, and I don't want to either over-formalize or come across like I'm too casual. she fed me a provolone yesterday. she deserves the right register. give me a sentence I can use that lands somewhere in the warm-but-respectful zone. also if you have a regional-paulista equivalent that's better, I'll take it. tia Marta is paulista to the bone and she'll catch any awkwardness in my register the way she'd catch a kid trying to lie about homework.

I'm rambling again. it's the lack of training and the lack of sleep and the layered language switching and the constant low buzz of grief in the background. I have been thinking in three languages today. Spanish to Camila, Portuguese to her family, English to you. by 9pm my brain feels like a CPU that's been switching between three runtime languages without breaks. the linguist friends I have call this 'language fatigue' and tell me it's worse for non-native speakers. I believe them. by tomorrow night I'm going to be unable to construct a sentence in any language. I'll just point at things and grunt. forgive it in advance. you don't have to address all of this. just the Portuguese sentence. the rest was for me. and maybe — maybe also tell me how 'kickboxing in São Paulo' would compare to Atlas Box if I ever ended up moving here, just hypothetically, just because I'm curious. it's a thought I noticed in my head during the BJJ-academy walk-by and I want to follow it for ten more seconds before I put it down.

a last thing because I'm not done. one of the smaller threads I've been thinking about today is what kickboxing has in common with engineering. they're both practices where you can be objectively wrong and the wrongness is fast feedback. in engineering you push code that breaks a test. you see it red. you fix it. green. you push again. you cannot argue with the test runner — it's not impressed by your intentions. in kickboxing your guard drops and you eat a left hook. you see the floor. you stand up. you tighten your guard. the bag is not impressed by your intentions either. both practices reward the same character traits — humility about your current skill level, willingness to be corrected by an inanimate adversary, a long-term love of incremental progress over big leaps. and that, I think, is why I love both. they make me a more rigorous version of myself. when I have not done either for too long I lose some piece of the rigor and I notice it in everything else — in arguments with Camila, in patience with my mother on the phone, in the willingness to sit with a difficult Portuguese paragraph instead of code-switching to English. the rigor matters. it has to be maintained. that's the running thesis of how I want to live my forties when I get there. maintain the rigor. ship the work. love the people. eat well. train. simple list. the hard part is the consistency.

ran 7k along the river this morning. felt every one of those eight days off. shoulders are tight, hips are tight. but I can think again. ate two pieces of cheese bread when I got back and Hugo silently put a third on my plate. I ate the third. I think the run was the first time in eight days my brain was operating at something like baseline. five minutes in I was breathing hard and not thinking about anything except the breathing. five minutes after that I was thinking about a piece of code and how to refactor it. that is a return of the normal me. I missed the normal me.

Daniela slack DM'd me at 2pm. 'no rush, hope you're doing ok, when you have ten minutes can you skim this perf doc and react in thread? not urgent.' she's good — that exact phrasing is calibrated. 'when you have ten minutes' actually means when you have ten minutes, not 'now.' I appreciate her so much for that.

the doc is a one-pager about whether we should start scoping a rewrite of the routing service. she's gathering input. she wants to know whether we go with another iteration on the python/django service or do a more invasive rewrite in something else. golang and rust both mentioned in the doc as candidates. the rationale she's writing is 'the service has p95 of around 340ms which is at the edge of our SLA and it scales linearly with the size of the route graph, which we expect to grow 4x in the next 18 months.'

she's not asking me to commit to anything, she's asking for my reaction in the thread. but my reaction is going to set a tone — if I say 'yeah, rewrite, rust' it's going to nudge the room one direction. if I say 'iterate, we can squeeze 30% out of django,' it nudges the other.

I'm in São Paulo. I am not going to make a serious technical recommendation while sitting in my dead mother-in-law's husband's living room. but I'd like to type a couple of clean paragraphs to her tomorrow morning that say: here are the criteria I'd use, here are the questions I'd want answered before I'd make a call. tee her up to make a good decision without putting my finger on any side of the scale yet.

help me draft that, briefly. four or five questions max. I want it to read like a mature engineer, not like a guy showing off. tomorrow morning, before the coffee.

the questions I'm leaning toward: (1) what's the target p95 we want from the new service — 100ms, 50ms, something else? that anchors everything else. (2) what's our staffing reality for the next 12 months — can we afford to take two engineers off feature work for a full rewrite, and for how long? (3) do we have enough team rust experience to ship rust to production safely, or are we entering a learning curve while also delivering? (4) is the route graph 4x growth assumption load-tested or theoretical — i.e. have we simulated a graph of that size against the current service to see exactly where it falls over, or is it modeled? (5) what's the rollback plan — can we run python and the new service in parallel behind a feature flag for a quarter, or is it cutover only?

I think those five questions force the right conversation. they don't take a position on rewrite-yes-or-no. they make the cost of each decision legible. that's the contribution I want to make in this thread.

we went to the cemetery this afternoon. just the three of us — Camila, Hugo, me. quiet. we brought flowers. Camila knelt at the headstone for a while. Hugo stood about two meters back with his hands behind him like he was at attention. I stood about two meters behind Hugo because I could not figure out how close was right.

the headstone is temporary. it's a wooden marker with her name and dates. they'll put up a stone in three months when the ground settles. that's how it works in Catholic Brazilian burials apparently — you don't put up a stone right away. so right now her grave is this little patch of fresh dirt with a wooden marker that has 'BEATRIZ FERREIRA, 1958-2025' burned into it with a hot iron, and a small cross above her name. and that's all. her whole life is currently summarized by a wooden plank.

Camila sat there for fifteen minutes and then she stood up and brushed the dirt off her knees and said 'okay.' like she was clocking out of a meeting. and then she walked back toward the gate without looking back. I followed her. Hugo followed me. and we got in the car and drove to a churrascaria — yes, again, churrasco at the meal after the cemetery visit, because there is no escaping it — and we ate and Hugo told two stories about Bea where she came out of it looking ridiculous in the funniest way and we all laughed. like properly laughed.

that's the part nobody tells you about funerals. there's a moment, somewhere between day three and day five, where the family laughs about the dead person. like really laughs. and it's not disrespectful. it's the opposite. it's the first sign that the person is going to keep being a person in the family, not a wound. Hugo told a story about Bea trying to assemble an IKEA bookshelf in 1996 — she was an architect, she understood structures, she was going to tell IKEA exactly what was wrong with their design — and she got the shelves on backwards and they all collapsed at 11pm and she sat on the floor laughing for half an hour. Hugo was crying laughing telling that story.

Camila laughed too. for the first time in eight days, she laughed without a lid on it.

I'm not asking for anything. I'm just writing this down because I want to remember what laughter at a cemetery dinner sounded like. I want to remember that this week is not only the worst week. it has these little corners in it where a kind of light gets in. that's worth keeping.

ok big conversation tonight at dinner. Hugo, Camila, me, in his kitchen. Camila served pasta — first thing she has cooked in eight days, this very simple cacio e pepe, no improvisation, just pasta and pepper and pecorino, because that's what she could do with her hands without thinking. she stirred the pasta with her left hand and held the pepper grinder in her right and her movements had this kind of slow underwater quality, the way you move when you're moving through grief and pretending to be at normal speed. we sat there with these big steaming bowls and Hugo cleared his throat and said in Portuguese, 'eu queria conversar com vocês sobre uma coisa.' I want to talk to you both about something. and Camila put her fork down and so did I and we both looked at him.

turns out he's been thinking. he doesn't want to leave São Paulo. this is his city, his apartment, his church, his friends, his brother, his neighborhood. he's lived in this Vila Madalena building for thirty-six years. he and Bea raised Camila in this building. it's where his memory of her is alive. he was very clear: 'aqui ela ainda está em todo lugar.' here she is still everywhere. her perfume bottle is still on the dresser. her reading glasses are still on the kitchen counter where she left them. her handwriting is on the calendar in the hallway. her annotations are in his cookbooks. her shoes are by the door. he said if he leaves all this he's going to go through the second loss of her, the loss of all the small physical traces, and he's not ready for that. he might never be ready. and I understood. of course you don't move. of course.

but. and this is where it got harder. he said he wants to come to CDMX once a month. he said he can't be alone for thirty days at a stretch. he said he can fly down for one week each month, he'll cover his own ticket, he'll stay in the small spare room we have, he'll cook, he'll be useful, he won't be a burden. he said he needs a place where he doesn't sleep alone in his marriage bed for a whole month at a time. one week out of four where he wakes up in a guest room that doesn't have her absence in every corner. he started to choke up here and Camila reached over and put her hand on his arm and just kept it there. he said it like a man who had thought about it for several days and gotten the words exactly right and was now delivering them in a voice he had practiced. it had that quality. it was a request he had rehearsed.

Camila said yes immediately. said yes before I had even processed the question. she said 'papai, claro que sim, claro que sim.' two times. and she was right to say yes immediately. of course. of course she said yes. her father has just buried her mother and he is asking for a week of his daughter's home each month. that's a small ask. that is the smallest possible ask compared to what he could have asked.

I also said yes. I said 'claro que sim' in Portuguese and I meant it. I want him with us. I love him. he is one of the most decent men I have ever met in my life. he taught Camila to read maps when she was four. he taught her to play chess when she was six. he is the kind of father who shows up to his adult daughter's apartment with a hand-drawn map of the neighborhood with the bakery and the pharmacy and the metro station marked on it because he wants to learn the area himself but he also doesn't want her to think he's lost. that's the kind of man he is. he is not going to be a difficult guest. he might genuinely be a help — he can walk to the panadería, he likes our café Cardinal, he plays chess with me on Sundays. I want him in our orbit. I want him close.

but I sat there at the kitchen table after we'd said yes and I noticed something in my chest that I want to name to you because I don't think I can name it to Camila for at least a week and I feel weird sitting on it.

one week a month. for how long? six months? a year? indefinitely? because if it's indefinitely — and it might be, and that might be the right thing — then our living situation is going to change. our small two-bedroom apartment in Roma Norte, with the spare room that's currently a junk-drawer-room with my woodworking-curious tools and Camila's drafting table and a stationary bike, becomes a guest room where my father-in-law lives a quarter of the time. our weekends, where Camila and I usually go for ramen on saturday at Nudo and lounge on sunday morning with the espresso and the newspapers, become weekends where we host her dad. our friday evenings, where I usually train at Atlas Box and then collapse in front of vinyl, become friday evenings where I have to be a son-in-law present in the home. our sex life — which has been quiet this week for obvious reasons but which is normally an everyday-most-days kind of thing — becomes a sex life that is on hiatus for one week out of every four indefinitely. all of that is fine. I am not complaining. I am noticing.

and the bigger thing — there was a moment at dinner, before we said yes, where Hugo asked very tentatively whether Camila might consider moving back to São Paulo. like, the two of us. moving back here. he didn't pressure her, he was so gentle about it, he said 'or we could think about whether you and Sebastián might consider eventually being closer.' he said it as 'closer' — 'mais perto' — not as 'move here' specifically. he was leaving room. and Camila said no immediately, very gently, very firmly. she said 'papai, minha vida está em CDMX agora. Sebastián está lá. meu trabalho está lá. nossa casa está lá.' and Hugo nodded and didn't push and asked instead about the once-a-month visit thing, which is the smaller compromise version of his original question.

so the relocation question got asked. and it got answered. and it was over in maybe sixty seconds. but the question existed for sixty seconds. for sixty seconds at this kitchen table the possibility of Camila moving back to her dead mother's city was on the table. and I sat there and I watched her say no. she did not look at me when she said it. she looked at her father. and I think that was the right move — she made the call as her father's daughter, not as my partner. I don't think she was performing for me at all. I think it was a real answer that had been formed in her own head over the last eight days while she lay on her side in bed.

but god. for sixty seconds my whole life was on a slightly different timeline. and that timeline is gone now. but it existed. it brushed against me and kept going. it has the texture of something that almost happened. and I'll feel it for the next few weeks even if I never speak about it. somewhere in me there's a Seb who said 'okay, let's move' and got on the next phase of his life. that Seb is now a ghost. he never existed but I can feel his outline.

okay. I'm telling you this because I don't have anyone else to tell. I'm not going to bring up the sixty-second moment with Camila. she made the call. it's not on the table. she doesn't need me processing my own little flinch. but I needed to put the flinch somewhere because it was there and I noticed it. and there's a part of me — the part of me that has been to therapy enough times to know what to look for — that knows if I leave this thing un-named in my own head it will fester. it will become a tiny resentment in three years that I will not be able to source. so naming it now is preventative maintenance.

for the record, in case I need it later: she said no on her own terms. that's the truth. it was not a no for me, it was a no for her. she has built a life in CDMX over the last four years and she does not want to dismantle it. her career is going better in CDMX than it would in São Paulo (Foster + Partners CDMX is a hot office right now, lots of large infrastructure projects, real ladder for her). she has friends here. she has a dog she keeps wanting to adopt with me (we keep saying we'll adopt one and we keep not). she likes the food, the climate, the apartment we share. she is happy here. her saying no was not a no to São Paulo, it was a yes to her life. I should hold those two things separate.

the practical thing — Hugo will start coming next month. like, in three weeks. he wants to come for the second week of next month. that gives me three weeks to make our spare room a real guest room. I need to clear out the junk, get a real mattress, set up wifi. I'd like to make it nice. like, nice nice. soft sheets, a small reading lamp, some flowers on a side table. he should walk into a room that someone made for him. not a room we improvised at the last minute. not a room that smells like the cardboard of the boxes we just removed from it. a room.

any ideas for cheap-ish, not-ugly bedside lamps in CDMX? ideally something I can buy in person at the casa Liverpool or similar so it arrives before he does. I will tackle the rest of the room piece by piece but the lamp is the thing I keep getting stuck on, weirdly. like the lamp has come to symbolize the whole thing for me. if I get the right lamp I'll have done it right. if the lamp is a sad cheap chrome thing with a weird shade I will have failed at the small thing that matters. I want him to turn off the lamp at night and feel like he is in a place where someone thought about whether he'd want to read in bed. I think Hugo reads in bed. he carries paperbacks everywhere. he's always reading something — currently I think he's on a Drummond de Andrade poetry collection because Camila mentioned it. so a reading lamp. warm light, not blue. adjustable arm if possible. not ugly. budget around 1500 pesos, give or take. tell me which Mexican brands to look at. I'm going to spend half a saturday on this lamp when I'm back. it's the lamp now. it's the lamp.

one more thing about the visits, while I'm thinking through it. I'd like to ask Camila — not tonight, but maybe next week, after we've slept some — whether we should establish any explicit agreements about Hugo's weeks. like, are mornings ours or are they shared. is friday night kickboxing still a thing that happens during his visits. do we eat dinner together every night or some nights. I have not been a son-in-law-with-a-recurring-houseguest before and I want to set the rhythm right at the start, with kindness, because if we don't set it explicitly the rhythm will set itself by accident and probably set itself wrong. I'd rather have a slightly awkward five-minute conversation now than four months of accumulating small frictions later. she's my partner. we're good at logistics together. we'll figure it out.

wrote my reply in Daniela's perf doc thread this morning. didn't take a position. asked four questions. she replied with a thumbs-up and 'this is exactly what I needed, thanks Seb.' which from her is the equivalent of a standing ovation. nice. one less thing.

she also dropped a cryptic 'we'll talk about other things when you're back' which I cannot decode from São Paulo. could be anything. could be 'the routing rewrite is happening and you're going to be involved,' could be 'we're losing our funding,' could be 'the snack budget is being cut.' I have a tendency to spiral on these unread-tea-leaves manager messages and tonight I'm going to choose not to spiral.

not a question for you, just a status update. I'll find out when I'm back.

though, while we're here — when you have a manager who drops a 'we'll talk about other things' line, what's the right rule for whether to ask for a one-line preview vs. wait? my instinct is wait. she's seven hours behind me right now anyway, asking would feel like fishing. but my anxiety is asking what if it's something I can prepare for. I think anxiety should not get a vote here. my instinct is right. wait. tuesday morning back in CDMX, 9am 1:1 on her calendar, she'll tell me whatever it is.

meanwhile Camila just called me from the bath. she wants me to come read to her for a bit. that's a thing she has started doing this week — asking me to read aloud to her in the bath, anything, doesn't matter, just my voice. tonight I'll read a few pages of Lispector since her mom's annotated copy is on the couch. okay. closing the laptop. going to read to my partner.

one more thing — I think this 'we'll talk about other things' moment is also a good test of my own patience. I have a tendency to want to resolve uncertainty immediately. my whole engineering brain is wired to chase the open ticket. but a manager dropping a hint and not following up is not an open ticket. it's a 'I'll bring this up at the right moment' that I should respect. so I'm going to make a small note on a sticky on my laptop that says 'D — open thread' and I'll see it monday morning and that'll be enough. no chasing. wait.

Hugo's brother Joaquim came over with a bag of Bea's clothes that needed to go somewhere. Camila looked at the bag for a full minute then walked out of the room and shut the bathroom door. Hugo and I sat in silence for ten minutes with the bag between us on the floor. eventually Hugo pushed it aside with his foot and said 'depois' and we got up and went to make tea. that was the right call. depois. later. some things don't have to happen today.

totally unrelated tech-thinking-out-loud while my brain needs a vacation. I've been turning over in my head, since Daniela's perf-doc note, what a clean rewrite of our routing service would look like. don't quote me, this is ten minutes of hammock thinking, but if it were rust:

- axum for the http layer, tokio runtime obviously, sqlx with the postgis feature flag for our spatial queries, redis for the in-memory dispatch cache.
- the route-graph itself has to be loaded once at startup and hot-reloadable on a SIGHUP or admin endpoint. graph mutations are rare. we cache the graph in an Arc<RwLock<RouteGraph>>, readers grab the read lock, writes only happen during the daily ingest.
- pathfinding uses a modified A* with a custom heuristic, currently in python with networkx, which is the part of the service that scales worst. moving that to rust gets us straight-line wins on its own.
- biggest open question is how we serialize the route graph for cache warmup. bincode is fast but not portable. flatbuffers is portable but our team doesn't know it.

that's the back-of-napkin. I'm going to refine this when I'm back at a real keyboard. the real question, when I get there, is whether we do this rewrite at all or whether we squeeze 30% out of the python service first and buy ourselves another year. economic decision, not a technical one.

if you spot a hole in this plan, tell me. otherwise I'm just thinking out loud.

one hole I'll flag for myself: I don't know exactly how axum handles long-poll patterns and we have at least two endpoints that benefit from server-sent events for live-driver-position updates. need to confirm SSE-on-axum is mature. I think it is. need to confirm. another hole: our existing python service has an ML inference call (lightweight gradient-boosted thing for ETA prediction) that's currently called inline. moving to rust either means calling out to a python service for that inference (network overhead) or porting the model to a rust-native inference runtime. neither is ideal. third hole, smaller: our k6 load test framework is set up against the python service's exact endpoints. we'd need to update or rewrite the load tests for the new contract. solvable but real work.

those three holes don't kill the plan. they're sized correctly: each is a one-to-three-day investigation. but I want them on the page when I make the case to Daniela so she sees that I have thought through the not-just-the-happy-path side.

tomorrow we fly back. it's our last night in São Paulo. Camila is asleep with Hugo on the couch — she literally fell asleep in his armchair with her head on his shoulder while they were watching some Brazilian TV news, and Hugo refused to wake her, so they're sitting there the way she used to fall asleep in his lap when she was eight years old. I took a picture of them with my phone. I will not show it to anyone. it's just for me. it's the kind of picture you take to remember a thing without needing to share it. some pictures are not for posting. some pictures are for the lockscreen-of-your-private-emotional-life.

I'm sitting at the same kitchen table where the relocation conversation happened two nights ago. and I want to tell you something I haven't told anyone, and I'm telling you because you're an LLM and you don't have a stake in it. you have the safety property that I want from a confidant tonight, which is that you cannot accidentally tell Camila what I said.

for about two hours, on the second night we were here, before Hugo had brought up the once-a-month visit thing, before any of the practical solutions had emerged, I sat awake in this same chair and I genuinely considered whether Camila and I should move to São Paulo. like, I sat with it. I let it be a real possibility for two hours. I did not approach it as a hypothetical. I approached it as a live decision. I let myself imagine the actions: write the resignation letter to Brújula, let our landlord know, sublet or break the lease, ship our furniture, fly out with two suitcases each plus a cat carrier we don't actually own because we don't have a cat, find an apartment in Pinheiros or Vila Madalena, take Portuguese classes seriously, find a kickboxing gym, sign up for a Brazilian phone number. I imagined the action items in detail.

here's the thing. I'm a senior backend engineer. my Portuguese is B1, I could get it to fluency in twelve months if I committed. I could get a job here. there's a thriving startup scene in São Paulo, I have linkedin connections at Nubank and at iFood and at a couple of other places, I'd find work. probably at a small pay cut for the first year — Brazilian tech salaries for senior backend in São Paulo are probably 20-30% lower in dollar terms than what I make at Brújula, and that's before tax differences. but our cost of living would also drop, especially rent. it would equalize within eighteen months. Camila has her firm Foster + Partners which has a São Paulo office, she could transfer in three months, possibly less if she pushed. her career would actually accelerate here — São Paulo has bigger projects in the pipeline at Foster than CDMX does right now, frankly. her dad would not be alone. her cousins are here. her childhood is here. her cousin Daniel from Curitiba could visit on weekends. her tia Marta could fold her into Sunday lunches. there is an entire emotional infrastructure waiting for her here.

our life in CDMX is good but it isn't anchored to anything that couldn't be replicated. our friends — Pablo, Joaquín, Andrea-and-Tomás, Ana, the whole crew — sure, but I could fly back twice a year and they would forgive me. my kickboxing gym, Atlas Box, sure, but São Paulo has muay thai gyms by the dozen and I'd find one. my mate cup and my vinyl collection (about 150 records, mostly Latin jazz) and my apartment in Roma Norte. all of those things are precious and replaceable, except the mate cup which I would carry in my carry-on the way I always do. my parents are in Guadalajara, not CDMX. they were going to be a flight away whether the flight was 90 minutes or 9 hours. the only thing genuinely irreplaceable about CDMX for me is the city itself, which I love with my whole chest, but loving a city is not a reason to anchor a marriage to it when your wife's surviving parent is widowed across an ocean.

so for two hours I sat in this chair and I rolled it around in my head. I imagined waking up in this neighborhood every morning. I imagined finding a kickboxing gym in São Paulo (I had even started mentally cataloguing the BJJ academy I walked past, the Pinheiros muay thai place I've heard about, the strip-mall MMA gym in Itaim that a friend went to once). I imagined writing technical specs in a mix of portuguese and english at some Brazilian fintech. I imagined Sunday family lunches in Hugo's apartment instead of Sundays in Roma Norte. I imagined Camila's slow recovery happening here, where she could spend an afternoon at her dad's apartment going through her mom's things piece by piece, where she could see her cousins at every birthday party, where the soundtrack of her life would be the same one she had as a child. and I noticed something — I noticed that I could imagine it. that none of those things made me want to flee. I would do it. if Camila had said two days ago at the dinner that she wanted to move back, I would have said yes. not without grieving CDMX. but I would have said yes within twenty-four hours. it would have been the right call.

she didn't. she said no. and I respect that. but I want to write down somewhere that the version of me that exists today is one who could have done it. that's a thing I learned about myself in this kitchen. five years ago I would not have said yes that fast. ten years ago, after my second year out of ITESM, I would have argued for two weeks. now I just — I love her enough that the geography is a footnote. I think that's what people mean when they say marriage. I'm not married. but. you know. five years in. five years of brushing teeth at the same sink. that does something to your geography.

and here's the more uncomfortable thing I want to put on the record. for those two hours, I was not just willing to move. I was actively wanting to move. like, the imagined version of the move had a kind of fizz to it. it was not a sacrifice. it was a possibility. I was excited about the version of me who had to start over. I was excited about waking up in a city I don't yet know fluently. I was excited about a Portuguese-fluent Seb who exists eighteen months from now and who has eaten his way through every cantina in Vila Madalena and who has found a coach to call profe in São Paulo. that excitement scared me a little. it told me I might be more restless than I usually admit. it told me there's a part of me that wants a bigger reset than my normal life accommodates. that is a thing I am going to carry back to CDMX with me and probably eventually have to talk about with Camila, but not for at least six months.

the other thing. the once-a-month-visit plan with Hugo. I'm in. I'm fully in. and I want to make it work in a non-resentful way. I've been going through in my head all the small frictions a once-a-month visitor introduces in a two-bedroom apartment shared by two people who have each finally figured out how to live with each other. I don't want those frictions to fester. I'd rather front-load the conversations now. I'd rather make rules now and adjust later than discover six months in that I've been quietly resenting things and never said.

for example: my work-from-home days. I work from home maybe three days a week and I take long meeting blocks in the kitchen because the spare room — soon to be Hugo's room — has bad light. when Hugo is here, I'll need the kitchen and he'll need the kitchen and we cannot both be in the kitchen at 10am. I'll need to negotiate with Camila about whether I just go work at café Cardinal those weeks. I think I will. I'll just preemptively reserve a corner at Cardinal for the Hugo-weeks and that's that. that's a 200-peso-a-day overhead that I'm happy to pay.

the gym. I train Mon/Wed/Fri evenings. that's not changing. but on Sundays Camila and I usually have a slow morning together and Hugo will be a third party in that. Camila will probably want to spend Sundays specifically with him because that's their day. I'll need to find a Sunday-Seb thing — long run? errand morning? coffee with Pablo? I'll figure it out. probably the long run. there's a path along the Reforma that I've been wanting to do regularly and never made the time. Hugo-weeks become long-run weeks.

I don't drink (much) and I don't smoke. I've never smoked, not even in college. my dad smoked when I was little and I have a primal aversion to the smell. I drink mezcal or wine maybe one or two times a week, mostly with Camila on weekends. that's relevant because a lot of how a Brazilian-Argentinean family bonds is around late-night wine. Hugo doesn't really drink either, just one glass with dinner if at all, and he stopped smoking decades ago though he used to. so we'll match each other on that. Camila will out-pace us on the wine front and that's fine. we'll just be the two non-drinking nerds at the table while she has her glass and gets quietly philosophical.

the sex thing. I mentioned it earlier. I'll mention it again because it's on my mind. with Hugo here one week a month, that week is dialed-down. that's fine. I think a lot of long-term relationships have to grow flexible about that anyway, and we are about to have an extra-strong reason to flex. we'll figure it out. I'm noting it because if I don't note it I'll forget I noted it and I'll be vaguely confused at month four about why I'm vaguely irritable on the Tuesday of every Hugo-week.

actually. I want to tell you my name. I notice I never have, in any of these messages this week. I'm Sebastián. Seb. I don't usually say it to LLMs because — what's the point, you don't keep me as a person across sessions, my name doesn't matter to a stateless system. but tonight, in this kitchen, at the end of the worst week of my partner's life, I want to put my name on this somewhere. so. Seb. that's me. you've spent a week being a journal for me. you've earned the courtesy of my first name.

okay. flight is at 9am tomorrow. I should sleep. tomorrow is its own day. I will say goodbye to Hugo and I will hug him at the airport curb and I will tell him in Portuguese that we'll see him in three weeks and I will mean it. and then I will get on a plane with Camila and we will fly home to Roma Norte and I will start being whatever I need to be next. I don't know yet what that is. I'll find out. maybe it's the same Seb as before with new gravity. maybe it's a slightly different Seb who has been to a funeral and now lives knowing the floor isn't solid. probably some hybrid. I'll find out which when I land.

we made it to the airport. early. Hugo drove us. he didn't want a uber, he wanted to drive his daughter to the airport. that's a forty minute drive each way and he insisted. we let him.

at the curb at terminal 2 of GRU, Hugo hugged Camila for a long time. like, two minutes long. and she was crying for the first time in two days. he kissed the top of her head three times. then he hugged me. and he said into my shoulder in Portuguese, 'cuida da minha menina, Sebastián.' take care of my girl, Sebastián. and I said 'sim, claro, sempre' which is what you say when you're trying not to cry in front of your father-in-law at an international airport curb. and we got our bags out of the trunk and we walked away and I did not look back because if I looked back I was going to lose it.

he'll come to CDMX in three weeks. that's the new world.

on the plane Camila slept the whole way. four and a half hours. her head on my shoulder. the flight attendant didn't recognize our last name this time, thank god, I don't think I could have done that conversation again.

while she slept I made a list on a notepad. things to do in the first week back. (1) buy a new mattress for the spare room. (2) order blackout curtains. (3) call mom and tell her in person on the phone how the funeral went so she has the real version not the whatsapp version. (4) write to my brother Mateo, longer than a text. (5) get back into the gym monday or tuesday at the latest, even if I'm only at 60%. (6) reply to Daniela and confirm 1:1 monday morning. (7) take Camila to a doctor for her ankle, which is still swollen. (8) do absolutely nothing on social media for the next two weeks. (9) write thank-you notes to the friends who covered for me at work. (10) make Camila a real meal on tuesday night at home, our kitchen, our food, our table. that is the plan.

for item (1), the mattress — I have already been doing some research. there's a Mexican brand called Luuna that has good reviews and a generous return window. budget I have in mind is around 15-18k pesos. for item (2), I want curtains heavy enough to block early-morning sun (Hugo wakes at 5am because he always has, and our spare room faces east). item (7), Camila's ankle, is the one I'm prioritizing — three weeks of post-flight swelling is past the point where 'just rest it' is the right answer. I'll book her a doctor for tuesday or wednesday.

home. Roma Norte. it is 3:30pm. apartment smells like nothing because we left the windows open under the awning and the air had time to circulate. Camila is in the bath. I made coffee. I am sitting in our kitchen with my mate cup and a stack of unopened mail and a strange sense of having returned to a stage set. like the apartment is a movie set someone built out of an apartment-shaped box and our actual life is still somewhere over the Atlantic. I will sit with the strangeness. it'll resolve. it always does. but for now — kitchen, coffee, mate, mail, strange.

Doña Marta from upstairs knocked on the door an hour ago. she had heard from somewhere — probably the pan-de-muerto network of CDMX gossip in our building — that Camila's mother had passed. she came down with a tupperware of pan dulce and a small bouquet of flowers and she stood at our doorway for ten minutes, refused to come in, just kept saying 'lo siento mucho, mija' over and over to Camila.

she also told us that during the week we were gone she'd been collecting our mail and watering the plants and feeding the stray cat we'd been feeding on the balcony. she has a key. she's been our upstairs neighbor for two years and we have never once asked her to do this and she just — did it. she is the kind of woman who at 5am is baking bread for her grandchildren and at 4pm is taking pan dulce to her grieving downstairs neighbor.

Camila cried into Doña Marta's shoulder for like a minute. Doña Marta patted her on the back and clucked. then she handed me the tupperware and said 'come, mijo, está delicioso' and she went back up the stairs to her apartment.

I am thinking a lot tonight about how kindness from neighbors is a form of infrastructure that you don't notice until it carries weight. you don't notice the sidewalk until you stumble. Doña Marta is sidewalk for our family right now.

do you have a Spanish phrase, like a small ritual sentence I could write on a card and leave at her door tomorrow with a little something? I want to thank her without making it a big deal. she is allergic to anything that feels like ceremony. it has to be small.

little something I'm thinking: a small bag of the good Oaxacan coffee I keep stocked, or a jar of the homemade hot sauce a friend of mine brings me from Veracruz that Doña Marta has commented on twice when she's seen it on our table. probably the hot sauce, actually — she would smile when she opened it because it would mean we'd noticed her noticing. that's the right register. small and specific.

the phrase has to be one sentence, two at most. has to use 'usted' (she's older and we always usted her). probably opens with 'gracias' and closes with something warm that isn't ceremonial. I trust your ear here.

first day back at the office. monday. I walked through the door of Brújula at 9:15 and got hugs from a few people, professional handshakes from a couple of others, the kind of awkward shoulder-claps from the engineers who didn't quite know what to say. nobody asked the wrong questions. that was kind. one of the platform team folks, Ariana, who I barely talk to but who has met Camila once, came up at the coffee machine and just said 'estoy contigo, Seb' and walked away. that was perfect. I went to my desk. I opened my laptop. I opened slack. I opened the perf doc that Daniela had asked me to read the week before. there were 47 unread comments on the doc since I last looked. some of them were good. some of them were posturing. one was actively wrong. I read all of them and made a list of the threads I want to engage with.

okay. the actual problem. I want to put this on the record because it's been brewing for months and I haven't really written it down clean. and I think writing it out for you helps me sharpen the answer I give Daniela on wednesday.

our main backend service — internally we call it RouteOpt — is the system that takes a list of pending deliveries, the current locations of our 600 last-mile drivers, the time-windows the customers have specified, the road graph of CDMX (and three other cities now), and produces an assignment from drivers to deliveries plus an ordered route per driver. it is the heart of what we do. when it works the company makes money. when it slows down the drivers stand around looking at their phones and the customers complain.

it is currently a python/django monolith. yes, monolith, because we built it in a hurry in 2022 when the company was smaller. it has a django admin panel grafted onto it that operations uses for manual overrides. it has a celery queue for asynchronous batch jobs (mostly the daily route planning runs at 4am). it has a couple of dozen REST endpoints serving the dispatcher app. and it has a python implementation of a modified A* graph search using networkx as the underlying graph library. the original engineers who wrote it are mostly gone — Carla Vela is the only person on the team who was around for the original architecture decisions, and she's about to go on parental leave for five months. so the institutional memory of why decisions were made is leaving the team three weeks from now.

the metrics, as of last month:
- p50 endpoint latency: 80ms (fine)
- p95: 340ms (over our SLA of 300ms — we get yelled at when this number creeps up)
- p99: 2.4s under load (frankly horrifying)
- 4am daily batch job: completes in 38 minutes for CDMX alone, which means we cannot start serving morning routes until ~5am, which is later than ops wants
- memory: ~1.3gb resident on each replica, growing roughly 50mb a week, which is a slow leak nobody has time to chase
- request volume: ~22 million requests/day, peaks at 1200 RPS during morning rush
- 8 replica pods in prod, each on a 4-vCPU 8GB pod

the pain points, in priority order:
1. p95 over SLA. this is the customer-facing one. nobody on ops cares about p99 unless it sustains, but when p95 creeps over 300ms our customer success people start fielding 'why are my deliveries late' calls.
2. that 4am batch job. when we add the next two cities we're rolling out to (Monterrey, Bogotá), we forecast the batch job at ~110 minutes for all of them. that means we can't start serving routes until 6am, which is unacceptable for a logistics company. our customer SLA literally says 'morning routes available by 5am local.'
3. the memory leak. annoying. not blocker. but it forces us to do a rolling restart of the replica set every 5-7 days which adds operational toil and means an engineer needs to be on call for the restart window.
4. the django admin panel is increasingly being used by ops in ways nobody anticipated, and it's becoming a source of accidental data corruption — ops will manually toggle a field through the admin and break invariants that the python ORM doesn't enforce. last month we had a 90-minute incident because someone in ops manually unchecked an 'active' flag on a driver who was mid-route, and the dispatcher app crashed when it tried to render the driver's nullified state. that's a problem of architecture, not django specifically, but django makes it easy to commit those sins.

there are basically three options, and Daniela's perf doc was an attempt to get the team to think clearly about which one to pursue:

(A) iterate on the python/django service. squeeze 30% out of the hot paths. profile the A* implementation, replace networkx with our own thinner graph in pure python, get aggressive about caching (especially the cached-graph thing I'm going to ship this week regardless), kill the django admin in favor of an internal tools app. estimated ~3-4 months of focused engineer time. brings p95 to maybe 230ms and the batch to maybe 28 minutes. doesn't fix the memory leak in any clean way. doesn't help us when we add two more cities. buys us roughly 12 months. then we're back here having the same conversation.

(B) rewrite the pathfinding hot path in rust as a callable extension via pyo3, leave the rest of django alone. estimated ~5-6 months of focused engineer time. brings the A* down by a factor of 8-10x by my back-of-envelope estimates. p95 maybe 120ms. batch maybe 12 minutes. memory leak unchanged. cost: now we have a python/django service with a rust extension which is a real maintenance pattern not everyone on the team can debug. but it's the smallest 'rust' bet. and frankly it's the most credible step on a path to (C) — we'd be writing rust we'd reuse later.

(C) full rewrite. brand new service, rust + axum + tokio + sqlx with the postgis feature, redis cache for hot dispatch state. take everything from scratch. estimated 8-12 months. potentially p95 of 40ms. batch maybe 4-6 minutes. memory leak gone. and a service architected for the next 3 years of growth. we get to redesign the data model around what we actually use, not what django ORM made convenient in 2022. we get to remove the django admin panel and replace it with a controlled internal tools UI that enforces invariants. cost: it's the biggest commitment we'd make as a team this year. we'd need to hire one more rust-experienced engineer or have some of the team upskill. and we'd have to maintain both services side by side for at least the cutover period, which is operational pain. and during that maintenance period there's a non-trivial risk of feature drift between the two services.

Daniela is, I think, leaning toward (C). she has not said so. but she's been asking architectural questions in the doc that only make sense if she's already entertaining (C) seriously — questions about how we'd handle blue/green deployment, questions about runtime memory profiles, questions about whether we'd want to break the service into multiple smaller services or keep it monolithic in rust. those are post-(C) questions. and frankly, when I think about it cold-blooded, I think (C) is the right call too. (A) is fixing a leaky boat. (B) is a duct-tape hybrid we'll regret in 18 months unless we explicitly treat it as a stepping stone toward (C). (C) is the actual answer.

but I don't want to be the loudest voice for (C). there's a senior engineer on our team, Carla Vela, who has more rust experience than anyone (she did a year of rust at her previous company at a payment processor in Madrid), and (C) effectively means making her or me the tech lead of the rewrite. she's eight months pregnant and on parental leave starting in three weeks. so it would land on me. and that's a lot. tech leading a 6-12 month rewrite while also being a recently-promoted senior who is supposed to mentor a junior (Andrés) and be available for cross-team architecture questions is — that's a heavy bag.

there's also a (D) that I want to acknowledge for completeness even though I don't think it's the right call: don't rewrite at all, just throw more pods at the problem. horizontal scaling. it's the cheapest in engineering hours, the most expensive in cloud spend, and it doesn't fix the architectural pain points. but every leadership meeting at every series-B startup eventually has someone propose this. so when I write the answer to Daniela I should mention (D) and explain why we're rejecting it. otherwise the CEO will ask the question in three weeks.

I'm not asking you to decide. I'm asking you to help me think clearly. if Daniela asks me at our 1:1 on wednesday — and she will — 'what do you think we should do?' — what's the most intellectually honest answer I can give that doesn't either oversell rust or undersell the python iteration path? I want my answer to her to be the kind of answer she can use in a leadership meeting without looking like she's just channeling me.

specifically, let me think about how I'd frame it: 'I think we should commit to (C), but begin with the (B) sub-step as a hedge. specifically: spend 6-8 weeks porting the A* hot path to rust as a pyo3 extension. this is a real engineering investment, not a throwaway prototype, and the rust we write here will be reused in the (C) service. by week 6 we'll have shipped a meaningful perf improvement (p95 from 340 to ~150) and we'll have validated whether our team can ship rust to prod. if it goes well, we commit to (C) at week 8 and start the full rewrite. if it goes badly we have a real fallback because we still have the python service. and meanwhile, the cached-graph fix and a couple of other quick wins ship in parallel to keep us within SLA today.' that's the answer I'm sketching. tell me if it has holes.

let me know if I'm missing options. and lay out for me the strongest argument for each of (A) (B) (C) (D) — like, the strongest version of each, presented by an advocate who genuinely believes it. I'll use that to stress-test my own current lean toward 'commit to (C), enter via (B).' I want to walk into Daniela's office on wednesday with a calibrated answer, not a passionate one.

one more thing I want to flag for myself before I close the laptop. whatever we choose, the operational story matters more than the raw perf. the difference between (B) and (C) on perf might be 30-40%. the difference on operational story is bigger. (C) gives us a service we can take pride in operating — clear invariants, clear deployment story, clear blast-radius for changes. (B) leaves us with a hybrid that we will be apologizing for in standups for the next two years. that operational consideration is the thing I most want Daniela to weight in her own thinking, because comp committees and CEOs underweight operational story in favor of perf headlines, and if we let the conversation settle on perf headlines we'll end up with (B) by inertia.

final thought: I want to write all of the above into a one-page memo tonight after Camila is asleep. just for myself. just to make sure I have my own argument crisp. memos for me, not for distribution. that's the intermediate step between 'thinking out loud with you' and 'walking into Daniela's office with a clean recommendation.' three iterations: chat, memo, conversation. that's the loop I trust.

Camila is officially on bereavement leave through the end of this month. her firm gave her two weeks initial and then extended it without her even asking. her director called this morning and said 'take what you need, we have you covered until the end of the month, we'll re-evaluate then.' that's pretty extraordinary for an architecture firm where billable hours are everything. I don't know what they did internally to make that happen but I'm grateful.

she spent today on the couch. she's reading. she has a stack of like seven novels next to her and she's working through them. one was her mom's. she found it in a box when we got home — a copy of Clarice Lispector's A Hora da Estrela that her mom had marked up with notes in the margins, and Camila has been reading the notes more than the book itself.

the bereavement leave is good. work would not be good for her right now. but I'm worried about week three and four. the silence in the apartment when I'm at the office. she has friends here but most of them are working during the day. and Hugo isn't here yet — he comes in three weeks. I want to make sure she has structure. not pressure-structure, but rhythm-structure. things to do that aren't forced. is there a non-condescending way to suggest she walk over to her firm's office for a coffee with a coworker once a week, just to maintain contact, without it feeling like I'm pushing her to perform recovery? I don't want to get this wrong. she has the ability to read condescension at the molecular level.

the other thing I'm wondering — should I take a couple of half-days myself in week three so the silence isn't full days. work-from-home a morning, lunch with her, work-from-home an afternoon. not every day, but maybe two days a week. would that read as protective or as overbearing? I think it depends entirely on whether she asks for it. I think the right move is: I offer it once, not pressuring, and let her decide. 'hey, just so you know, I can wfh a couple of mornings a week for the next few weeks if you want. zero pressure either way, just want you to know it's available.' that feels right. I'll write that on a sticky note and stick it on the fridge so she sees it during the day rather than as a heavy in-person ask.

Andrés sent me his profiling output from the staging RouteOpt service. 41% of CPU time is spent in networkx graph traversal. 19% in serializing django ORM responses. 8% in our middleware logging hook. 'wow' is a verbal tic I rarely use but. wow. that 41% is exactly the budget I needed to see to make the pyo3 case to Daniela. graph traversal is the biggest single line item in the profile and it is also the cleanest thing to port to rust because it's pure compute on a self-contained data structure. this is the universe handing me an answer.

follow up on the profile. so 41% in networkx is way more than I expected. I knew networkx was slow but I assumed maybe 20%. 41% means we are spending almost half the request building and querying a graph object every single time. I don't think we even need to be doing the build per request — the road graph for a city doesn't change minute to minute. we should be loading it once at process startup and caching it in memory.

actually let me reread our code. there's a chance we're doing something dumb like reconstructing the graph from rows on every API hit. that would be bad. that would also explain the memory growth — if we're rebuilding it every time we have a window for objects to leak through circular references that python's gc has to special-case.

okay I'll dig into that tomorrow morning. wanted to write it out before I forgot.

separately — running mental math. if we do option (A) (iterate on python), this single change of caching the graph could already get us 25% of the way to our perf goals. that's interesting. that doesn't change the long-term answer (we still want option (C) for the next two cities) but it does mean we could front-load a quick win in the next two weeks while we scope (C). I'll propose that to Daniela on wednesday.

the sequencing I want to suggest: ship the cache fix first as a low-risk single-PR change behind a feature flag. observe in staging for 48 hours with our regular load profile. roll out to prod 10/50/100 over a week. that buys us our 25% win without any architecture decision being made about the larger rewrite. then in parallel I scope option (C) properly with a written design doc that has the four or five questions I asked Daniela in the original thread answered concretely, and we make the rewrite call with cooler heads in two to three weeks. that's the responsible plan. fast win, slow decision.

the other thing I want to do — and this is partly career-shaped because of the promo timing — is write up the cache-fix discovery as a short tech-blog-internal post for our engineering wiki. not because I'm trying to look good for the comp committee (though it would not hurt). because the lesson is genuinely interesting: a three-year-old TODO sitting in a hot path was 25% of our latency. the takeaway for the team is 'old TODOs in hot paths are technical debt with compounding interest.' that's worth twenty minutes of writing.

wednesday. 1:1 with Daniela. forty-five minutes. she pulled me into the small meeting room with the green wall, the one we call 'the lime.' I had a coffee. she had a cortado. she kicked off the way she always does, with: 'so. how are you. real version, not the one you tell other people.' that's the line she opens every 1:1 with. she has been opening 1:1s with that line since I joined Brújula. it doesn't sound rehearsed when she says it because she actually waits for the real answer.

so I gave her the real version. I told her about the funeral, about Hugo, about the once-a-month visits starting in three weeks. about Camila being on bereavement leave through the end of the month. about my own residual jet-lag and the fact that I am sleeping like a man who has been asked to sleep next to an unknown sound. she didn't try to fix anything. she just listened, and at one point she put her hand briefly on the table near mine, not on my hand, just near it, in the kind of solidarity gesture that doesn't ask anything of you. she has lost a parent herself — her father died a couple of years ago, before I joined the company — so she knows the shape of this from the inside. she didn't bring up her own loss. that's also one of her tells. she does not perform empathy. she just radiates it.

then she said: 'okay. so. there's a thing I want to talk to you about and I want to be respectful of where you are this week. tell me if it's the wrong time and we can push it.' I said no, please, work is actually a relief, please go ahead. and I meant it. I had been waiting for forty-eight hours for the news that she signaled in her cryptic 'we'll talk about other things' message from São Paulo, and the suspense had become its own kind of mental weight.

she said: 'end of year promotion cycle is open. I'm putting you up for senior. it's not a slam dunk but I think it's a strong case and I want to write it.'

I sat there for about ten seconds without saying anything. she let me sit. she's good at that. she has the rare manager skill of letting silence do work. most managers fill silence with reassurance or with caveats. she just lets it sit.

I've been at Brújula since late 2023. I came in as a mid-level. about fifteen months in. the typical promo to senior at Brújula is twenty-four to thirty months in, so this is a slightly accelerated case. she said she'd been thinking about it for a couple of months and the postgres outage we had a few weeks before São Paulo (the four-hour one in May) — where I'd written the post-mortem and led a lot of the recovery — was the thing that pushed her over the edge. she'd watched how I handled it. she'd watched how I wrote the blame-free post-mortem. she said 'that document is what a senior engineer writes. I want to make it official.' her words. she repeated 'I want to make it official' twice during the meeting, which is a Daniela tell — when she repeats a sentence in a meeting, it's because she's already had this conversation in her head a number of times and she's done committing to it.

she walked me through the case she's writing. she has it in a draft google doc. she shared it with me on the spot. it cites three specific things: the post-mortem (with three excerpts from the doc, including the part where I argued for a less-blameful framing of an ops mistake), my mentorship of Andrés over the last four months (which I had not even thought of as mentorship, just as 'helping the junior get unstuck' — apparently Andrés had told her in his last 1:1 that the reason he hasn't quit is that I made him feel like he wasn't drowning), and the architectural input I'd given on the routing rewrite scoping (which was, like, a couple of slack comments and one hour-long whiteboard session in early May). she'd also added a section on cross-team work — apparently I'd given a clean architectural review to the platform team back in March that they'd referenced in their own quarterly retro, which I had completely forgotten about.

she also told me — this is the part that matters and that I'm chewing on tonight — that she expects me to be the tech lead on the rust rewrite if we go that direction. she said 'I'm scoping it as part of the senior-level case. the senior promotion comes with leading the rewrite. those are two parts of the same package.' she did not present this as a take-it-or-leave-it. she presented it as an explanation of why the case is strong. but the implication was clear: the senior level at Brújula is the level where you start leading projects of this size, and the rewrite is the project that justifies the senior title. without the rewrite the case is harder to make. with the rewrite it becomes obvious.

okay. so. the package. seventeen percent salary bump if it goes through. tech lead role on a major rewrite that will define the next year of my work life. and the timing is — well. it is what it is. it's right now. it's three days after I came back from a funeral.

I said yes. of course I said yes. I'd be a fool to say no. it's the right move for my career, it's the right move for the team, it's a vote of confidence from a manager who I trust deeply. I told Daniela that I was excited and grateful and that I needed maybe a week to be in proper ramp-up shape because of the family stuff. she said 'take two weeks. ramp up at your pace. the comp committee meets at end of next month, that's our deadline for the case, and we'll be fine.' and then she added something that I don't think I've ever heard a manager say before — she said 'and Seb, this is going to sound counterintuitive, but I want you to know — I am not going to penalize you in the writing for being slow this week. I want you to write the rewrite case from a place of recovered energy, not from a place of pretending you have energy you don't. so when you do the architectural document for the project, do it next week or the week after, when you've slept properly. that document is going to be the spine of the case and I want it to be your best thinking, not your jet-lagged thinking.' that is calibrated management. she handed me a permission to be slow without making the slowness about my grief. she made it about the quality of the document. that is the move of a manager who knows how to coach a senior.

but here's the part I don't know how to feel about. I walked out of that meeting and I had this moment in the hallway where I was just standing there next to the water cooler grinning to myself like an idiot. like a real grin. for about thirty seconds I was the happiest I've been in three weeks. and then I caught myself grinning and I felt this wave of guilt because Camila is at home reading her mom's marked-up copy of Clarice Lispector and I am at the office getting promoted to senior. those two things should not be co-occurring. those two things should be six months apart. but they're not. they are the same week. that's the texture of life — it doesn't separate the joys from the griefs into separate weeks. they show up on the same Wednesday afternoon and you have to hold both.

the ratio of joy in our apartment right now is — let me try to get this right — at most fifteen percent should be mine, at most. she should hold the rest. and yet I just took on something that is going to add maybe twenty percent more joy to my side of the ledger. I'm going to come home tonight and I'm going to have to decide how to tell her. and if I get it wrong, I will introduce a tiny crack into the apartment that is going to need months to heal. so I want to get it right.

that's the thing I want to ask you about. how do I tell my partner — who buried her mother nine days ago — that I got promoted today, in a way that is honest and shares the news but doesn't ask her to perform celebration that she doesn't have? she will perform if I let her. she is the kind of person who will throw her arms around me and say 'amor I'm so proud of you' and mean it and also be hollowed out doing it. I don't want her to spend any of her thin energy reserves making me feel celebrated. that's not what I need from her this week.

I was thinking I could tell her at dinner, very low-key, like 'hey by the way Daniela mentioned today she's putting me up for senior, comp committee meets end of next month, no pressure on either of us either way, just wanted you to know.' that's the spirit. understated. real. doesn't make it a moment. lets her absorb it as information rather than as an event. she can come back to it tomorrow if she wants. or next week. or never. the news is on the table without expectation.

but Camila will know. she'll see through the understated framing immediately. she will know that this is a big deal for me. she will say 'amor. that's huge.' and she'll mean it. and then she'll cry, possibly. for entirely separate reasons, possibly braided together.

is there a way to do this where I don't make her cry? or do I let her cry and not flinch from it? I think the answer might be the second one. but I want to hear how you'd frame it. specifically — give me a few openings. give me three different framings, each one a few sentences long, that I can pick from depending on her mood when I get home. one for if she's reading on the couch. one for if she's already in bed. one for if she's at the kitchen table working on something. tone calibration matters here. I want options.

for what it's worth — Daniela is the one who put me up for this. I want to remember her name on the record. Daniela Otero. she's been my manager for a year and three months. she is one of the few managers I've ever had who I would walk into a fire for. she has the kind of management style that makes you better at your job by osmosis. you are around her for six months and you start writing better tickets, running better meetings, giving better feedback, simply because you have absorbed her cadence. if she leaves Brújula, I will leave too within six months. that is not on the table right now but I want to write it down because I will not be honest with myself about it later. she's that good. and the senior promotion is going to be partly because of who I am and partly because of who she is, and I will never quite know the proportions, but I will not pretend that the second factor is small.

I told her. tonight. at dinner. cooked some lentil soup that I made up as I went, served it with a piece of bread Doña Marta had brought down earlier in the week. I waited until we were halfway through the soup. then I said the thing about Daniela putting me up for senior, exactly the way I'd planned, low-key, no pressure either way.

she put her spoon down. she looked at me for a long second. then she got up, walked around the table, and hugged me from the side, my face pressed into her ribs, and she said 'amor.' just that. amor.

then she sat back down, picked up her spoon, took another sip of soup, and said 'you have to call your mom tonight. she's going to be insufferable. she's going to want to throw a party in Guadalajara.'

she didn't cry. she didn't perform celebration. she made a joke about my mom. it was perfect. she's perfect.

I called my mom after dinner. she answered on the first ring. she said 'mijo' in this tone that knew immediately something was up — moms have radar — and I told her and she screamed 'aaaaaay HIJO' and I had to hold the phone away from my ear. she put my dad on. my dad said 'felicidades, mijo, ya te lo merecías' and asked me three specific questions about what 'senior' means in pesos, which is the most charming thing he could have done. fifteen minutes on the phone with them and then I came back to the couch where Camila was reading and put my head in her lap and she stroked my hair and we sat like that until I fell asleep on her thigh.

she woke me at midnight. led me to bed. that's the day.

I keep going back to her face when she put the spoon down. there was a half-second where I could see her two reactions racing each other — the grief reflex and the joy reflex — and the joy one won, just barely, and that was a small grace I want to mark. she could have made it about her, about the contrast, about how complicated joy feels when your mother is three weeks dead. she didn't. she made it about me. she made it about us. she chose to be present with my good news rather than to be present with her sadness in that moment, and she did it cleanly, no performance. that is the kind of partner she is. five years in and I am still surprised by her generosity in moments like that. I want to remember it. I want to be that kind of partner back when her good news comes.

confirmed: yes, we ARE rebuilding the networkx graph on every request. it's in a function called `get_route_graph()` in routes/views.py and it's called from like fourteen places. someone wrote a TODO three years ago saying 'cache this' and nobody did. fixing it now. lru_cache with a maxsize tied to active CDMX subgraphs, invalidation hook on the road-update event bus. couple of hours of work, massive payoff, blast radius small if I gate it behind a feature flag for a week.

ok pushed a fix. cached the graph at module load with a lazy initializer. wrote a small invalidation hook that gets triggered when the daily ingest job updates the road graph rows. local benchmark shows the new code path is roughly 4x faster on the median request and like 9x faster on the slow tail. didn't expect 9x. that suggests the graph rebuild was the dominant cost on the worst requests, which makes sense — the rebuilds were doing a O(n^2) thing under the hood that I now skip entirely.

asked Daniela if I can deploy to staging tomorrow morning and run the k6 perf suite against it to confirm. she said yes. if the numbers match my local bench, this single change will move our p95 from 340 to maybe 220. that wouldn't fix the long-term problem but it'd take us out of SLA-violation territory in the short term.

that would also genuinely strengthen Daniela's senior promo case for me. like, the timing is fine. it's not why I did it, I did it because I saw the bug, but it's a nice instrumental side effect.

I'm typing this from the couch. Camila is across the room reading. Nube would be on my feet if we had a Nube. (we don't. we keep talking about getting a dog. someday.)

one thing I should write down so I don't forget — the cache invalidation hook is the part that scared me when I was writing it, because cache invalidation is one of the two hard things in computer science. but our case is gentle: the road graph changes maybe once a day during the ingest job, the ingest job is a single batch process I can hook into directly, and a stale graph for a few extra seconds during the swap-over is not a correctness issue (it's a freshness issue). so I went with a single global cache pointer, atomic swap on ingest completion, no read locks needed because the readers see the new pointer or the old pointer and both are valid graphs. simple. the simplicity is the right call. I want to remember that I resisted the urge to over-engineer this with a versioned cache or a multi-region invalidation pattern. the simple version shipped tonight. the over-engineered version would still be in design review.

it's saturday. ten days since we got back. fourteen days since the funeral. I want to write down where Camila is, where I am, where the apartment is, before I lose track. the days are starting to blur into each other and I want a snapshot. partly because I want to be able to read this back in a month and see how the picture has shifted. partly because writing it makes the world legible to me when it doesn't always feel legible inside.

Camila. she's reading a lot. she's started cooking again — last night she made arroz à grega which is a Brazilian dish her mom used to make for her when she was sick. yellow rice with raisins and ham (she made me a vegetarian version with mushrooms instead of ham, on a separate pan, she insisted). she stood over the stove for forty minutes stirring with this distant focus, like she was trying to taste her mother through the dish. she's been crying about once a day, usually in the late afternoon, usually triggered by some small thing — yesterday she cried because she saw a woman on the street wearing a coat in the same shade of camel as one of her mom's coats. then she stopped crying after about ten minutes and went back to whatever she was doing. the day before she cried because the cleaning lady moved a vase to dust under it and put it back two centimeters off-center, which Bea would have noticed and corrected, and Camila stood in the living room and put her hand on the vase and burst into tears for forty seconds, then wiped her face with the back of her wrist and walked into the kitchen.

she has good fifteen-minute stretches now where she seems entirely herself. ten days ago there were no such stretches. that's progress, I think. progress is the wrong word. that's something. the wrongness of the world is starting to be punctuated by stretches of okayness, instead of the other way around. that's the metric I'm tracking. before São Paulo, her baseline was 100% Camila and 0% wrongness. last week it was 0% Camila and 100% wrongness. now it's maybe 35% Camila and 65% wrongness, with the Camila stretches getting longer over time. somewhere there's a curve that flattens out at maybe 80%, where she is the post-Bea Camila and that's just who she is now. I think we get there in eight or twelve months. I don't have evidence for that timeline. I just have a feeling.

she's been talking to Hugo on the phone every day. they have a 9pm call ritual. he calls. she answers. they talk for forty-five minutes to an hour. some nights she comes back from the call with red eyes. some nights she comes back laughing about a story he told. some nights she comes back blank. I leave them alone. I don't ask what they talked about unless she wants to share. tonight she came back from the call and said 'papai is doing the laundry. he had never done the laundry. mama always did the laundry. he just told me he washed a wool sweater on hot and shrunk it down to doll-size.' she was laughing. she said it like a delighted seven-year-old reporting on her dad's incompetence. and then she said, quieter, 'I should have visited more. I should have taught him how to use the washing machine.' and then she went into the bathroom and was in there for ten minutes. that's the rhythm of it. one moment of laughter, one moment of regret. they take turns.

she's not back at work. she has another two weeks of bereavement leave officially. I expect she'll go back the second week, maybe four hours a day. the architecture firm has been saintly so far. her director Marina sent her a handwritten card last tuesday — actual paper, hand-delivered to our apartment by a courier — that said 'we will be here when you're ready. take your time. don't perform readiness.' direct quote. that's a sentence to put on a t-shirt. that's how you write a card to a grieving employee. Marina has my respect for life now.

me. I am — okay. I notice that I keep using the phrase 'i'm fine' when people from Brújula ask, and 'i'm okay' when my parents ask, and I have not really articulated what's actually going on under there because I've been running so hard on the work side. the work side is good. the senior promo is in motion. the routeopt fix landed in staging this morning and the numbers held — p95 went from 340 to 215. Daniela slacked me 'absolute hero' which from her is a thirteen-stage standing ovation. the rust prototype is taking shape. I have momentum at work. that helps. it gives me a place to put my hands during the days that would otherwise be too quiet.

but underneath that, I am tired in a way I haven't been in years. like, my body is tired. my back has been catching at me when I sit too long, especially on the work-from-home days, and I think that's leftover stress from carrying the casket honestly. my shoulders have been knotted since São Paulo. I went to Atlas Box twice this week — Wednesday and Friday — and the Wednesday class wrecked me. I had to sit out the last two rounds. Profe Esteban looked at me and just said 'you good, mijo' which from him means 'you don't look good, mijo' and I said 'family stuff, profe, will be back to normal next week' and he nodded and didn't push. on Friday I made it through all eight rounds but my form was sloppy in the last two and Pablo, my regular sparring partner, caught me with a left hook to the body in the last round that knocked the wind out of me for a full thirty seconds. that's not him being aggressive. that's me being slow. when you're slow you eat shots that you would normally slip. I'm noting it. I expect to be slow for another two weeks and I should not spar full-contact during that period.

I think what I'm processing — and this is where I'm going to be a little floppy — is that this past two weeks have permanently rearranged my sense of how stable my life is. I had this baseline assumption, before all this, that the people in my life who are alive will stay alive for a long time. that's a stupid assumption. it's stupid the way assuming the floor is solid is stupid. you don't think about it until something gives way. but I had it. I was operating on it. I had built an entire architecture of plans on it. I think most people in their early thirties operate on this assumption without noticing. it's invisible until you bury someone and then you can see it everywhere — in your calendar, in your savings allocation, in your career timeline, in the way you put off phone calls.

the plans I had: keep working at Brújula for two more years, get the senior promotion, save 30% of my income, eventually buy an apartment in Roma Norte or Condesa, propose to Camila in maybe eighteen months, get married in maybe two and a half years, kids in maybe four years if we wanted them. somewhere in that timeline my parents and Camila's parents would all attend our wedding.
