one more piece — there's a thing i'm worried about for the rfc-level work going forward at staff. the routeopt rewrite case is the obvious next big one, and i want to do it, but the way these things usually go at brújula is: an engineer pitches a rewrite, leadership gives a soft yes, six weeks of design happen, and then the rewrite gets de-scoped to 'incremental hardening' because feature velocity panic kicks in. i've seen it twice. i don't want to be a third casualty of that pattern. so i'm going to need to design the proposal differently — probably in a way that ships value at week 6 and 12 not just at week 24. but that's a different conversation.

so. case looks how to you?

oh and — i realized i haven't told mom and dad. i texted dad yesterday and he replied with 'que orgulloso estoy m'hijo' which made me tear up at my desk. he asked how much it pays which is the question dad always asks because he equates pesos with safety. i told him a number lower than the actual because if he heard the real number he'd think i'm flexing or in trouble. that's a small ongoing dishonesty i should think about. mom called separately and just cried for like a minute and said 'tu abuela estaría feliz' — abuela was the first person in the family to graduate high school and she'd have understood the staff promotion as a continuation of that line. i'm going to drive down to oaxaca in a few weeks once cami's stable and bring photos of the ceremony i never had at brújula because of the soft announcement. they'll like that better anyway.

separate observation about how i'm processing this. i noticed i went straight from 'i got the news' to 'who do i need to thank' to 'am i ready' to 'what's next'. like the celebration window was about ninety seconds. i don't think that's healthy. i think it's the engineer-anxiety pattern of treating a milestone as the start of the next stretch goal instead of as a thing to sit in. cami went through grief group and one of the things the facilitator said that stuck with her — and that she repeated to me — is 'culture conditions us to skip the marker and rush to the next thing'. she said it about wakes and funerals but it applies to wins too. so. i'm going to try to actually sit in this for a week. i'm not going to start drafting the routeopt rewrite rfc for at least seven days. i'm going to let the staff-engineer thing settle as a thing before i make it a platform for the next thing. write that down in your memory of me, please. seb made staff and tried to actually feel it before sprinting to the next milestone.

one more loop on the 'am i ready' question. the framing of 'cycle dynamics' worries me. brújula has had bad calibration cycles in the past — the autumn-2 cycle famously promoted three people who got pip'd within twelve months. so 'they promoted me' is not the same as 'i'm staff-grade'. the way i'd self-test is: would oscar (the principal who came from uber dispatch) hire me as a staff engineer at his last company. he'd say yes for the architecture work, probably yes for the operational work, probably no for the cross-functional influence work because he saw me get steamrolled in the q4 vendor review meeting before i wrote the build-vs-buy memo. so the honest grade is staff-with-a-development-edge. that's the real shape. i'm going to keep that frame in mind and not let the title harden into 'i've arrived'.

update for the record — comp negotiation step is closed. i didn't push back on the 18%. i could have probably gotten 22 if i'd grinded but the timing wasn't right and 18 is solid. people ops said 'happy to revisit at year-end' which is the corporate way of saying 'we have room' but i'm fine. taking the win and moving on. equity refresh hits the next quarterly grant. i'll log the doc in my notes folder under 2026-comp-and-promo so future-me knows what year it landed when i look back.

okay so on the staff role question. daniela and i had a 30-minute sync today. she walked me through the plate. summary: i'm now 'platform' coverage which means i lead architecture review across three services — routeopt (the one i hate), dispatch-core (well-loved, well-maintained), and the new geofencing service that the ops team is spinning up in q2. i'll have a dotted-line relationship with two other senior engineers — amir on dispatch-core, who's solid and i've known him since he joined in 2023, and lupita who's the geofencing lead, who i've worked with on cross-team things but never directly. not their manager, but their architecture sounding board. daniela was clear that 'sounding board' means i can push back on architectural choices but i don't write performance reviews and i don't sign off on time off. that's important. she said it twice.

she also said — and this is the part i've been turning over — that 'whatever you've been wanting to do with routeopt, the door is open. if you want to make the case for a rewrite, this is your cycle to do it.' so. that's the green light i was looking for and didn't expect to get this fast. now the pressure is on me to make a real proposal not a vibes-based 'rust go brrr' rant.

i'm going to put together a six-week assessment plan. weeks 1-2: instrument routeopt properly with otel traces, capture p50/p95/p99 and tail behavior under three load profiles (nominal weekday, monday morning batch, end-of-month broker rush). week 3: run a controlled benchmark of a small rust prototype doing just the cost-matrix computation in axum + tokio against the current django version. specifically, i want the prototype to handle a single endpoint — POST /benchmark/cost-matrix — with the same input shape django gets, no orm, postgres direct via sqlx, and measure p95 on identical hardware. weeks 4-5: write the rfc with three options costed: (a) keep django, harden it, target 200ms p95; (b) gradual rewrite, route by route, behind a flag; (c) full rewrite, parallel deploy, cutover. week 6: present to platform leadership.

does that pacing sound right or am i giving myself too much rope? part of me wants to ship the rust prototype next week to feel something. but that's not the disciplined version of this. and i know from past experience that prototypes shipped fast become 'why don't we just productionize this' before anybody's done the comparative work, and that's how you get a half-finished service that nobody owns.

second question: the rfc audience is platform leadership which is daniela, her boss raul, and two principal engineers — natalia who's been around since brújula's series-b and is conservative, and oscar who came from uber's dispatch team and probably has feelings about route optimization having seen it done at scale. natalia is going to ask 'why now' and 'what does this cost us in feature velocity for the next six months'. oscar is going to ask 'why rust and not go' because he watched go scale at uber. i need solid answers to both. give me a strawman for each.

cami asked tonight, after dinner, what i'm going to do with the promo bump. like — not 'we' going to do, but 'you'. she's still in this thing where she's trying to give me space to enjoy the win without making it feel like our money. i told her it's our money, obviously, but i also wanted to give her a real answer. so here's what i told her, and you can sanity check:

option 1: bump emergency fund from six months household burn to nine months. we're at 6 right now and i've been wanting 9 for a while because the chalie startup memory is still a thing — that was 2020-2021 when the seed-stage place i was at couldn't make payroll for two months and we burned through our buffer. and we both have that healthy cdmx anxiety about random shit happening. nine months gives us a runway through, say, a layoff plus a slow job search plus a moving-related hiccup.

option 2: send more home — about $400/month bump to my parents in oaxaca. dad's working less and pretending he's not. mom's still doing tortilla orders for the neighborhood but it's not steady cash. i was going to wait until father's day to bump it but with the new comp i can do it now and frame it as 'promo present to the family' which dad would take more easily than 'we're worried about you'. dad's a proud guy. the angina scare two years ago made him quieter about his health and prouder about his earning. the framing matters.

option 3: the espresso machine. i've been trying to convince myself i don't deserve it for like a year. lelit mara x. but i don't want to make a big toy purchase the same week i told cami 'we're upping the emergency fund'. she'd see right through it. (she'd also approve. she's not stingy. but i know me.)

option 4: do nothing for 60 days. let the first paycheck land, see what the equity refresh looks like at the quarterly grant, and revisit then. this is the disciplined version. it's also the boring version.

i think i want to do 1 + 2 immediately and defer 3 by 60 days. cami said 'do whatever feels right' which is what she says when she trusts me but also doesn't have the bandwidth to argue. so i shouldn't take that as full endorsement. what's your read? and — if i defer the espresso machine 60 days, am i actually disciplined or am i just performing discipline so i can feel virtuous about a purchase i'm definitely going to make? be honest, i'm not asking you to flatter me here.

okay. i'm doing it. lelit mara x. i ordered it from cafetera-shop tonight at 11:42pm because i finally let myself. let me explain because i want to be honest about why now and not later, and i want this conversation in my notes for future-me to look back at.

three reasons. first — i've been talking myself out of this espresso machine since 2023. that's two years. the lelit mara x is not exotic. it's a heat-exchange machine with an e61 group head and a built-in pid that hits temperature stability that my current breville barista pro can't approach. i've been pulling double shots on the breville for four years, and the breville is fine, but it has temperature surfing issues on the second shot of a session that i can taste, especially with the lighter andean roasts cami likes. i've quantified this. i ran a temp probe on the breville last summer — third shot in a session was 91.2c, which is meaningfully under spec for the kind of light roasts we drink. the mara x with its hx and pid sits at 92.5-93.5 across a session. that's the whole game. for darker roasts the breville is fine. for the lighter buena vista or finca chelín we get from un café tinto in roma norte, the breville pulls them sour. unacceptable.

second — money-wise, i can do it without being stupid. the mara x is about 38k pesos shipped from cafetera-shop's roma norte warehouse. i did the math. with the promo bump going into effect on the next paycheck and the equity refresh being on top of that, my net monthly increase is about 9k pesos. so this is roughly four months of the increase. and i'm doing options 1 and 2 from my last message — bumping emergency fund to 9 months, bumping the parents' allowance — those are first. this is the third thing in line and i'm letting myself do it. i also have an unused chunk in the 'fun money' bucket from skipping eats and ubers during the funeral month. it's not a stretch buy.

third — and this is the soft reason — i need a thing in this house that is just mine. cami has her plants. she's got the monstera and the four pothos and she does the pruning and the misting and that's hers. i don't have a thing here. the kickboxing gym is mine but it's not in the house. i want a corner of the kitchen — the espresso corner — to be a place where i make a thing on a weekend morning that is my hands, my tamping, my milk steaming, my texturing, my latte art that's mostly bad but improving. cami already loves the cortados i pull on the breville. on the mara x it'll be better. that's it.

i am not going to make this sound like therapy. but cami's mom passing has reset my sense of 'wait until the right time'. there's no right time. there's a time when you can afford it and the time is now. she would have approved actually — cami's mom was the one who taught me how to make café de olla properly, with the piloncillo and canela together not stacked, and she always said 'no escatimes en café' — don't skimp on coffee. so. there.

logistics: it ships in 5-7 business days. i'm getting the eureka mignon specialita grinder also, used, from a guy in polanco who's selling because he's moving to madrid. 6500 pesos for a barely-used unit. i'm picking that up saturday. meanwhile i'll keep pulling on the breville. when the mara x lands i'll set it up on the left side of the kitchen counter, displace the toaster oven to the cabinet, and run an extension cord behind the spice rack until i get a proper outlet installed.

what i want from you — i want you to help me think about water. interlomas tap water is hard. like — i did a tds reading at the kitchen sink last summer and it was 380 ppm. that will scale the boiler in the mara x within months. i need a plan. options i know about: bottled bonafont (cheap, plastic-heavy, hassle), an under-sink ro filter (expensive, big install but gives you a remineralization stage you can dial in), brita-style pour-through (probably underpowered for hard cdmx water), or a third-party lelit-recommended water with mineralization tabs. what's the right move here for an interlomas apartment with average usage of, say, four shots a day plus two milk drinks on weekends? rank them on cost over five years, taste, hassle, and risk-of-scale.

bonus question: how often should i back-flush and descale on this machine to keep it healthy. i know e61 groups have specific maintenance rhythms. give me the actual schedule, not vague advice — back-flush with plain water how often, back-flush with detergent (cafiza) how often, descale how often, group head gasket replacement interval, shower screen interval, and pump pressure check interval. with cdmx water hardness factored in.

also — what's the right tamper for a 58mm portafilter and is a calibrated one actually worth it or is it espresso-snob theater. i know people argue about this on home barista. i want your honest take based on whether the consistency improvement is real or just measurable-but-imperceptible.

quick taxonomy of what i actually need with the machine on day one. the mara x ships with a single spout and a double spout portafilter, two baskets (single 7g and double 14-18g), a plastic tamper that everybody on r/espresso says is garbage, and a blind disk for back-flushing. so the 'must buy' list is: a real tamper (58mm or 58.4mm depending on basket choice), a wdt tool for distribution, a leveler (optional but useful for beginners), a knock box, a milk pitcher (i have one), and a bottomless portafilter if i decide to go that route. the 'nice to have' list is: a precision basket (vst 18g ridgeless), a puck screen (helps with channeling and group head cleanliness), a digital scale that fits under the portafilter for shot weight (acaia lunar is the gold standard but 4500 pesos used; the timemore basic at 800 is fine for me starting out), and a bean cellar for resting beans 5-10 days post-roast. total damage on the must-haves: about 3500 pesos for the lot if i shop carefully. nice-to-haves: another 3000-5000.

oh — and the kitchen counter prep. the counter where the mara x is going has a slight dip toward the sink, like maybe a 2mm slope across the machine's footprint. i need to shim the back legs of the machine or the e61 group will run downhill on the gauge. the lelit forum has a thread about this. shim with rubber furniture pads or a cut sheet of cork backing. doable.

and for the breville — i'm going to keep it as a backup machine and as the 'guest' machine for when cami's sister marta comes over and wants a quick latte. marta's not picky, the breville pulls fine for milk drinks, and it lets me leave the mara x as the 'serious shot' machine without having to switch beans every time someone wants a cortado. dual-machine setup at home feels excessive but i know me — the moment i sell the breville i'll wish i had a backup the morning the mara x needs a service.

last thing for tonight: i want to set up a shot-tracking habit from day one with the mara x. on the breville i never tracked anything and i think i plateaued because of it. options for tracking — a notebook by the machine, a notes app, an actual barista app like beanconqueror. i've seen beanconqueror on android and it's overengineered but it's good. tell me what to use and what i should be tracking per shot. dose, yield, time, grind setting, water temp if i can read it, taste 1-10? ratio? i don't want to optimize for measurement, i want to optimize for a small set of variables that actually drive shot quality so i can see patterns over weeks. and if the answer is 'just learn by taste like the italians do' i'll accept that, but my hunch is that for a guy training his palate from scratch, tracking accelerates the learning curve substantially.

also — beans. cami's preference lately is the colombian washed processes from un café tinto. mine is more anaerobic naturals from chiapas. on the breville i've been compromising. on the mara x with grinder consistency i should be able to dial in two profiles — one for cami's preferred medium-light and one for the funky naturals — and switch between them in like ninety seconds with single-dosing. is that realistic with a mignon specialita or do i need a stepless adjustment that i'd have to upgrade to a niche zero for. niche zero is 16k pesos used in cdmx. not in my budget this round.

separate logistical thing — the mara x has a vibe pump not a rotary. i know rotary pumps are quieter and theoretically more pressure-stable, but the mara x is the only hx in this price band with a built-in pid and the vibe pump is fine for home use. i'm telling you this because i went down a six-hour rabbit hole comparing it to the bezzera unica pid which is a similar price tier with a rotary. the bezzera has the rotary advantage but a less responsive pid and weaker community parts availability in mexico. the mara x has stronger community support. also, my friend ricardo who's an actual barista at máquina café in condesa told me 'the mara x is the most repairable e61 in this band, parts are everywhere, lelit in europe stocks fast.' that tipped me. so the rotary thing was a temptation i resisted.

one more — should i get a bottomless portafilter or stick with the spouted one that ships with the mara x. bottomless lets you visually diagnose channeling, which would help my puck prep. but it also splatters everywhere if your distribution is bad and i'm a beginner relative to where this machine deserves. lean which way? if bottomless, what dose range — ridgeless basket, 18g vst seems standard. if i go ridgeless 18g vst i'll need a 58.4mm tamper not 58mm because of the wider basket bore. small detail but i'd rather get it right on day one than wait for the imported tamper to arrive a week later.

i also want to be clear-eyed about the time cost of this hobby. each shot takes about 4-5 minutes from grinder on to first sip when you're dialing in (more if you weigh the grounds, time the shot, taste, adjust grind for the next one). a fully dialed shot takes 90 seconds. the dialing-in phase per new bean is 2-4 shots, about 12-15 minutes. so a new bag of beans (say 250g) lasts me 2 weeks at 4-5 shots a day, with the first 15 minutes being dial-in. that's a sustainable rhythm. it's not a 'spend an hour every morning playing barista' habit. it's a 'three shots in twelve minutes, then the rest of the morning' habit. cami's not going to feel like espresso took her husband. that matters.

last thing on the lelit purchase logistics. i talked to ricardo (the máquina café barista) about the warranty. lelit's mexico distributor is in monterrey and they handle warranty claims through cafetera-shop where i bought it. so if the pump fails or the boiler springs a leak in year 1, i ship it to cafetera-shop, they ship it to monterrey, lelit pays. typical turnaround 2-3 weeks during which i'd be back on the breville. that's why keeping the breville is the right call. backup machine is not optional in a one-machine household where coffee is non-negotiable for both of us. and ricardo also gave me a list of three cdmx-based espresso techs who do paid out-of-warranty repairs once the lelit warranty expires — one in roma norte, one in coyoacán, one in tlalpan. wrote them down in the notes folder. future-seb will need them eventually.

small thing — the eureka mignon specialita pickup happened today. drove to polanco at lunch, met the guy at a starbucks because he didn't want to give me his apartment address (fair). machine looks clean, hopper has minor abrasion ring from a chemex i guess he was doing rotary on, burrs look fresh, no detectable wobble in the burr carrier when i did the manual spin test. paid 6500 pesos cash. drove back. it's sitting on the kitchen counter next to the breville now. mara x ships in 4 days according to cafetera-shop's tracking. cami saw the grinder and said 'oh no, here we go.' fond, not annoyed.

on the kickboxing return — went to martín's friday session. did exactly what i told you i'd do. drills only, no full contact. 90 minutes total. 30 minutes of footwork, 30 minutes of pad work with another regular named diego (good guy, same age, also a programmer of all things, works on payments at one of the banks), and 30 minutes of light technical sparring with martín himself which he kept at maybe 40% intensity, no head shots, just body and movement.

felt — not great, not terrible. my conditioning is not gone but my reactions are slower. i ate a pad-work shovel hook from diego that i'd have slipped two months ago. nothing serious, i was wearing the gear, but i felt the difference. specifically the lateral slip is gone — i was moving back instead of off-line, and you can't out-back-pedal a hook from a faster opponent, you need to step off-axis. that's the rep i need to put back in. martín pulled me aside after and said 'sebastián, no te castigues, regresar es regresar, no es competir.' which is the kind of thing he says. don't punish yourself, returning is returning, not competing.

after the session, took a long shower and felt — clear. not happy clear. clear as in 'the static dropped 30%'. so the answer to my earlier question about exercise and decision-making is — for me, post-session i can think more linearly. i drafted half of the rust prototype's data layer in my head walking from the gym to the metro. didn't write it down yet because i was metro-ing, but the shape is there. axum with a single POST endpoint, sqlx for postgres, uuid keys, time_window joined eagerly, cost matrix computed in a tokio task pool of n=cpu_count, return as a flatbuffer rather than json to skip the serialization tax. that last bit is probably overkill for a benchmark but i want to see it.

i also realized — and i don't know if this is post-workout brain or actually correct — that the structural advantage of rust here is not raw speed of computation, it's predictable latency. python's gc is unpredictable on tail latency. our p99 problem at 290ms is partly memory pressure during peak batches because the orm builds a lot of garbage. rust would let me allocate the cost matrix on the stack or in a single arena, no gc pauses, and the p99 would collapse to something close to p95. that's the actual pitch — not 'rust is faster' but 'rust eliminates this specific pathology that's causing tail latency issues.' that's a stronger story for a rewrite rfc than 'we'd save some milliseconds.' write that down for me.

also — the espresso machine is officially out for delivery. tracking says wednesday. i'm probably going to be useless at work that morning. you have my permission to remind me to actually code that day instead of staring at the box. specifically i have a code review to land on the prefetch_related deploy and amir is going to ask why i haven't responded to his thread. i'll set my morning calendar block to 'mara x setup, no meetings, async pr review only after 11am.' that's the disciplined version of unboxing-day.

on routeopt — i did the prefetch_related work today. one line in OptimizeView.get_queryset, .prefetch_related('time_windows') on the dispatch queryset. ran the test suite, all green. deployed to staging behind a flag. ran the synthetic load test — 100 batches at the nominal weekday profile (averaging 60 deliveries per batch) — and p95 dropped from 340ms to 218ms. that's 36% just from the n+1 fix. obviously this means the previous engineer was doing something close to malpractice but i'm not going to put that in writing. the previous engineer is mateo (different mateo from my ex-manager, this one is the original routeopt author who left brújula in 2023 to do a startup that pivoted twice and now does b2b sales tooling). i don't want to retroactively shit on him because he was solo on routeopt for nine months and his job was 'ship features' not 'engineer for scale we didn't have yet'.

the postgis ST_Intersects work is harder than i thought. the issue is our service area polygons are stored as geojson in a json column, not as a postgis geometry. so i need to do a backfill migration to convert them. there's about 14000 polygons across all brokers. the migration itself is fine, but the tricky part is keeping the geojson column in sync until all the readers are updated, and one of the readers is in another service (dispatch-core, of all things, amir's domain) so this becomes a cross-service deprecation. i'm going to write the backfill plan tonight and ping amir tomorrow.

question — am i overthinking the dual-write phase? options i've considered: a) backfill migration, write to both columns for two weeks, swap readers, drop old column. b) compute postgis geometry on the fly via ST_GeomFromGeoJSON each query (avoids migration but pays per-query cost). c) materialized view that computes the geometry from the json column refreshed nightly. i lean (a) but it's more operational lift. (b) is tempting because it's less work but ST_GeomFromGeoJSON in the hot path adds maybe 4-8ms per query depending on polygon complexity, and that compounds when we're doing tens of intersections per request. (c) is the worst of both — staleness of nightly refresh plus storage cost plus refresh-time spike. so (a) it is, but i want a sanity check on the deprecation timeline. is two weeks of dual-write the right window or am i being aggressive?

okay routeopt update from a longer week of work. did 1 (prefetch) and started 2 (postgis migration) and i'm now in the weeds on dual-write. let me dump it to think clearly and ask a few questions.

current state: prefetch dropped p95 from 340 → 218ms. nice. postgis migration is in progress — i wrote the alembic migration that adds a geometry(MultiPolygon, 4326) column called service_area_geom alongside the existing service_area_json column. backfill script processes 14k rows in batches of 500 with ON CONFLICT DO NOTHING idempotency in case of mid-run crashes. ran the backfill on staging in 4 minutes. fine. did the spatial index — gist on service_area_geom. EXPLAIN ANALYZE on a representative ST_Intersects query against the new index shows 12ms vs the previous python-side shapely intersection which was averaging 38ms per request. so the 60ms shapely number was actually 60ms across multiple intersections per request — i underestimated.

now the dual-write. wrote a django signal on the Polygon model's pre_save that recomputes service_area_geom from service_area_json on any update. tested it. one bug — when the json is malformed (shouldn't happen but does, occasionally, when brokers paste from gpx exports) the pre_save raises and i was killing the save. fixed: wrap in try/except, log to sentry, save the json column anyway with service_area_geom set to NULL, alert on the sentry log. better.

reader migration — there's three call sites for service_area in routeopt: the OptimizeView intersection (the hot path), an admin endpoint for visualizing service areas on a map (cold), and a daily report that flags overlapping service areas across brokers (cold but cron-d). i did all three this afternoon. the OptimizeView is the one that matters and it now does ST_Intersects directly via a custom queryset method i added to the Polygon model. the admin endpoint i pointed at the new column for consistency. the daily report i left on the json reader for now because it has business logic that depends on the json structure (doesn't need geometric ops, just attribute reads from properties). will migrate that one in a follow-up.

cross-service piece — dispatch-core. amir's service has one reader that pulls service_area_json via the internal api and runs its own shapely intersect. i pinged amir on slack and he was great about it — said 'sí güey, dame una semana, tengo el sprint en la cabeza pero después.' which means: not this sprint, next sprint. that's fine because the dispatch-core call site isn't on the hot path, it's a nightly job. i added it to the platform tickets backlog with a 2-week sla. amir will own the change.

now the harder question. with both 1 and 2 done, p95 is at — drumroll — 188ms. that's a 45% improvement from the baseline. before i ran the haversine vectorization (#3 from my earlier plan), i wanted to see what we got from 1+2 alone. answer: pretty good. p95 is below the 200ms internal target. p99 is at 290ms which is still bad but explainable by the long tail of mega-batches.

so the question is — does this change my rfc strategy? earlier i told you i was going to do the harden-first work and then make the rust case. but the harden work has already taken us from 340ms to 188ms p95. the routeopt rewrite case is meaningfully weaker than it was a week ago. honest gut: rust would still be 5-10x faster than where we are now, but 'better than 188ms p95' is a much weaker pitch than 'better than 340ms p95'. and the engineering effort for the rewrite is something like 16-20 weeks of one engineer plus migration cost. we're talking about spending a quarter to drop maybe 100ms of p95. that's not nothing — at our broker volume, that's a real ux win — but it's no longer a 'we're on fire' kind of pitch.

what would be? if the haversine vectorization (#3) lands and we're still over 150ms p95 on the morning batch profile, the rewrite case stays alive but it's 'incremental optimization for a 30% gain'. if the haversine lands and we're at 110ms p95, the rewrite case basically dies on roi grounds and we should keep django and shore up tail latency by adding a circuit breaker and a queueing layer for batches over 200 deliveries. that would be the disciplined recommendation.

(also, side note: cami noticed me staring at the screen unfocused on saturday morning when she got up and asked what i was thinking about. i said routeopt. she said 'sebastián your job is making your eyes go like this' and pulled my face into a grimace. she's not wrong. the truth is i was thinking partly about the rfc framing and partly about whether dad's going to mention the angina episode at sunday's call or whether he's still pretending. but i didn't tell her that part. anyway.)

help me think about the rfc framing under the assumption that the harden work has worked and the rewrite case is roi-marginal. how do you present a 'we don't need to rewrite, here's the alternative roadmap' rfc without it sounding like you backed down? because the truth is i did the honest analysis and the answer changed and i should be okay with that, but professionally i don't want to look like i pitched a rewrite and walked it back. the framing matters.

specifically the audience problem: oscar (principal, ex-uber) will read this as 'sebastián chickened out'. natalia (principal, conservative) will read this as 'good, you saved us a quarter.' daniela will read it as 'sebastián did the actual analysis and reached a calibrated conclusion which is exactly what staff is supposed to do.' raul will read it as 'do whatever daniela signs off on.' so the rfc has to land for oscar without being defensive about it. one approach: lead with 'i was originally skeptical that incremental hardening could close the gap, here's the data that changed my view, here's where rust still wins (tail latency on memory pressure), here's the conservative recommendation (harden now, prototype rust as a follow-up if traffic patterns shift).' that frames the pivot as evidence-driven instead of cold-feet.

oscar specifically — i should also pre-empt his skepticism with a side-by-side data table. column 1: 'rust prototype best-case p95'. column 2: 'django hardened p95'. column 3: delta. for our actual workload at current scale, the delta is probably 60-90ms. that's not nothing but it's not the order-of-magnitude gap a rewrite usually justifies. when oscar sees a 60-90ms delta against a 16-20 week effort, he'll do the same math i did and arrive at the same conclusion. the chart is just to make him do that math instead of having a vibes-reaction.

second framing concern continued — the trojan horse path. i think the honest version isn't 'sneak in rust as a side investigation', it's 'build a rust prototype as the ultimate tail-latency probe so we have data for the next decision'. that frames the prototype as evidence-gathering rather than as a foothold for a future rewrite. if it turns out rust is dramatically faster at our scale, we know. if it's marginal, we know. either way the 6 weeks of prototyping reduce uncertainty about the next call. that's a defensible reason for principals to bless. it's not sneaky. it's just being honest about what we don't know yet.

second framing concern — there's a 'trojan horse' version where i write the rfc as 'harden django now and write a rust prototype as a 6-week side investigation to inform a future decision'. that lets me keep the rust work alive while not betting the company on it. i'm tempted by this because it's intellectually honest and it gives me the project i secretly want. but it might also read as 'sebastián is doing political horse-trading to get his pet project blessed' which is exactly the kind of optics i want to avoid in my first cycle as staff. tell me whether you think that frame is honest or sneaky.

also — what postgres parameters should i look at if p99 is still 290ms? i'm guessing some of that tail is checkpoint i/o or vacuum-related but i haven't looked. specifically pg_stat_statements would tell me but i've never set it up properly on the routeopt cluster. and i wonder if it's worth me also setting up pg_buffercache to look at hit ratio on the gist index — if the index is cold-paging on the bigger broker queries, that alone explains 50ms of tail. the routeopt cluster is on aurora postgres r6g.xlarge so memory isn't the constraint, but the buffer cache might be tuned suboptimally.

separately — i want to be honest about scope. when i started this 'harden routeopt' arc i was expecting it to be a 4-6 week effort that would cleanly justify the rewrite as a follow-up. but week 1 already produced a 45% improvement and week 2 will probably produce another 20%. by week 3 i'll be at the point where 'rewrite' becomes a luxury, not a need. that changes the rfc shape from 'why we should rewrite' to 'how we incrementally evolve toward a service that can sustain 10x volume.' completely different doc. completely different leadership conversation. i need to think about whether i want to be the one to drive that pivot in framing or whether i'm putting too much of my staff identity into 'i shipped the rust rewrite' as the marquee project of year one.

cami caught me staring at the kitchen counter at 11pm last night with a cup of tea going cold and asked what i was thinking about. i said 'whether i'm going to rewrite a service or just gradually fix it.' she said 'you sound disappointed about the second option.' she's right. that's something for me to sit with.

one more thing on the ego front. i think part of the rust attraction is that 'staff engineers in mexico ship rust services' is a thing in my head, mostly absorbed from reading too many threads on bsky and lobsters. it's not a real signal. it's status. and 'staff engineer who hardened a django service from 340ms to 110ms p95 by removing n+1s and using postgis correctly' is actually a much better staff-level move because it shows surgical thinking. a rewrite shows ambition; a calibrated hardening shows judgment. brújula doesn't need ambition right now, it needs judgment. so the more i type this out the more i think the harden-and-mature path is the right call.

side note on tail latency — i set up pg_stat_statements this morning and the top-by-mean-time queries show a few unexpected things. one is a NOT EXISTS query in the exclusion logic that's doing a sequential scan on a big table because the query planner is choosing wrong. probably needs an index hint or a query rewrite. that alone might be 30ms of p99. and there's an autovacuum interaction with the gist index where index bloat under heavy write load is dragging intersection cost. autovacuum is on default settings which means it's running too lazy. i'll bump autovacuum_vacuum_scale_factor to 0.05 from the default 0.2 on the polygon table specifically and re-measure tomorrow.

so for the rfc — i think the structure is: 1) the problem (340ms p95, 800ms p99 on big batches). 2) what i did this week (4 specific fixes). 3) the data (188ms p95, p99 still 290ms). 4) what's left to do under harden-and-mature (haversine vectorization, autovacuum tuning, NOT EXISTS rewrite, batching for >200-delivery requests). 5) projected end-state under harden-and-mature (110ms p95, 180ms p99, lasts us through 3x current traffic). 6) where rust still wins (eliminates gc tail, supports 10x traffic, but requires 16-20 weeks). 7) recommendation: harden through q2, revisit rust in q3 if traffic projections require it. 8) decision asked of leadership: bless the harden plan, set a traffic threshold that triggers re-evaluation. that last bit — making the leadership decision crisp and actionable — is the thing my old rfcs missed.

okay i want to start a side project and i'd like your help scoping it. background first. i've been kickboxing at lobo gym for almost four years and last weekend after the friday session diego (the bank dev guy) was complaining that he can never tell if his form is right when he shadowboxes at home because there's no coach. martín (head coach) said something interesting — 'you know who would benefit from a camera that watches your form and tells you when you're dropping your guard or rotating wrong? everybody who can't afford private coaching, which is most of us.' and that thought has been in my head for a week.

so the side project: a pose estimation tool that watches a kickboxer drilling at home — recorded from a phone or a laptop webcam, no special hardware — and gives feedback on a small set of high-value form errors. specifically:

1. guard drop — when the rear hand drops below chin height during a jab or cross. this is the #1 amateur mistake and it's why everyone gets hit with hooks they should have seen coming.
2. weight transfer — on a cross, are you driving from the rear leg or arming it out from the shoulder. you can detect this from hip rotation timing relative to shoulder rotation timing.
3. chin position — chin tucked vs chin sticking out. simple landmark check.
4. footwork — are you crossing your feet during forward movement, are you stepping too wide on the lead. this is harder because it requires temporal analysis of foot landmarks.

mvp would do guard drop and chin only. those are the most common errors and the easiest to detect from a single camera angle.

i'm thinking the architecture is:

— frontend: react native app (because i want it to work on the gym floor with phones, not just a laptop). stretch goal is also a web version using webrtc to stream from laptop webcam.
— backend: python service that takes a video clip (5-30 seconds), runs pose estimation per frame, returns a structured feedback object with timestamps and error types.
— pose model: this is where i need help. options i know about — mediapipe pose (google, mature, runs on-device, good benchmarks). tensorflow movenet (fast, less accurate on occluded poses but boxers don't really occlude). openpose (research-grade, slow, gpl issues for any commercial path). yolov8-pose (newer, hot in the community, fast). and rolling-your-own with a fine-tuned hrnet which is overkill for a side project.
— scoring rules: deterministic post-processing on the keypoint sequence. so if the model gives me 17 keypoints per frame for 30 fps over 10 seconds, i have 5100 keypoint vectors and i write rules like 'wrist_y > chin_y for >300ms during a jab' = guard drop. that's the part i actually find fun.

i want to do this for a few reasons. one — i need a non-work project that's mine. routeopt and the staff role are big and i love them but i need a thing that doesn't have a kpi. two — diego and i could test it on each other and it'd give us a way to be friends outside the gym. three — there's an actual user need that martín surfaced. four — pose estimation is something i've done zero of professionally and it's a chance to learn a new domain end-to-end.

scope discipline: this is a side project. not a startup. i'm capping it at 8 weeks of evenings. i'm going to write a one-page mini-spec this weekend with explicit non-goals (not building a community, not selling subscriptions, not training a custom model, not supporting more than one camera angle). i'm going to use whatever pose model gives me the fastest path to a working prototype because i want to spend the time on the rules engine and the ux, not on training pipelines.

there's also a question of how i think about the latency budget. for a 30-second video clip processed asynchronously, total time-to-feedback can be 5-15 seconds and it's fine. the user already knows what 'submitted' feels like, they wait for the result. but if i ever go to 'live coaching' mode where the camera is on while you spar and feedback is real-time, the budget collapses to <100ms per frame, ideally <50ms. that's a different model size and a different deployment architecture. for the mvp i'm doing the async batch model. the live coaching mode is a 'maybe in 6 months' stretch.

the user flow i'm imagining for the mvp: open app, hit record, do 30 seconds of shadowboxing, hit stop, app uploads the clip, server processes in 5-10 seconds, app shows the playback with annotations on the flagged moments. tap a flagged moment, see what specifically went wrong (text label + the keypoint overlay). that's the experience. very low-friction. lower than 'film yourself, send to coach, wait a day for feedback.'

questions for you:

1. for an mvp where i need fast iteration and decent accuracy on standing-up moving humans in normal indoor lighting, what should i pick — mediapipe pose, tensorflow movenet, or yolov8-pose? rank them and tell me why.
2. for the rules engine, what's the right way to model 'a sequence of keypoints' with attached temporal logic? i'm imagining a small dsl where i can write things like 'detect guard_drop when wrist_y > chin_y AND wrist_x within 0.3m of chin_x sustained for >0.3s during punch_window'. is there an off-the-shelf library for this kind of temporal event detection on keypoint streams or am i rolling it myself?
3. for the frontend, react native with a python backend means i need a way to upload a video and stream feedback. is this a websocket thing (stream feedback as the video processes) or a request-response thing (upload, wait, get full report)? i lean request-response for the mvp because it's simpler and the videos are short.
4. for the deployment — i'm planning to run this on a $20/month vps with a small gpu, not because i need scale (i don't) but because i want to learn how to deploy a python ml-ish service properly. is that overkill for a side project? should i just run inference on the phone via mediapipe and skip the backend entirely?

last thing — i want to name it. working title is 'sparring partner' which is on the nose. or 'guardia' which is shorter. or 'cornerman' which is the boxing role i'm most directly imitating. preferences? this is the kind of decision that's irreversible if you build any momentum so i want to land it before i write any code. cornerman is my current favorite because it's specific to boxing/kickboxing (vs the generic 'sparring partner' which could be anything), it has a clear human role analogue (the corner of a fighter's coach in between rounds is exactly what an automated form-check would feel like), and 'cornerman.app' is available as a domain. if you have a better idea throw it out, but otherwise i'm going with cornerman as the working name and i'll revisit if it doesn't feel right after a month of typing it. spanish-language bonus — 'tu cornerman' is also natural in español as a borrowed term, since spanish-speaking boxing has been using 'cornerman' for decades. that gives me bilingual marketability if this ever goes anywhere, which it probably won't, but the optionality is free.

let me also be specific about what i'm not building, because the failure mode for side projects in my brain is that they grow tentacles. non-goals for cornerman v0:

— no community features. no leaderboards, no shared sessions, no gamification, no public videos. one user, one local store, no social.
— no monetization path. not even a stripe integration as a 'when i'm ready' placeholder. the moment i wire payments i've changed the project's identity.
— no model training. i am not fine-tuning anything. i am not collecting a dataset for future training. i'm using off-the-shelf pose estimation and writing rules on top.
— no multi-camera support. one camera, one angle, one fighter. if a user has two cameras i'm not stitching, i'm asking them to pick one.
— no live coaching. async only. record, upload, wait, get feedback. live mode is a v2 problem.
— no ai voice or chat. the feedback is text + visual playback annotations, not a chatbot saying 'good job champ'. cringe.
— no support for grappling, mma, bjj, krav maga, or any martial art that's not stand-up striking. i don't know those well enough to design useful rules. boxing/kickboxing only.

writing those down now because every one of them is a thing i could be tempted into in week 4 when the prototype is exciting and i feel like adding 'just one more thing'. anchor list.

architecture decisions i'm pre-committing to: python for the backend (familiar, ml ecosystem), fastapi (because i want to learn it and it's simple), opencv for video decoding, postgres for any persistence (probably overkill — sqlite would be enough — but i know postgres), and react native for the eventual mobile app. desktop-first cli prototype before any mobile work. tests in pytest.

let me also write down what i'd consider an unsuccessful exit. if at week 8 the prototype is half-working with no clear path to a v1 that diego or i would actually use weekly, that's a fail. if at week 8 it works but i can't articulate one form-error pattern it has measurably helped me fix in my own training, that's also a fail. if at week 4 i find myself building unrelated features (auth, payments, social) that's a fail-in-progress and i should pull back. the success criteria is narrow: by week 8, diego and i are both using the tool weekly, both seeing measurable improvement on at least one form metric, and the codebase is shippable to a public github repo with a 5-minute install. that's the definition. i'm holding myself to it.

actually one more category of failure i want to flag. if cami's grief gets worse and the side project becomes a place i hide from her instead of being present, that's the worst kind of fail. i don't think it'll happen — she's been the one nudging me toward joyful pursuits — but i want to write it down so future-seb has the trip-wire visible. if i ever notice myself reaching for the laptop because i don't want to deal with what's happening in the living room, i stop. close the laptop, go to the living room, sit. the rule is simple. and the side project being optional makes it easy to follow: anything from cornerman that doesn't get done is fine.

oh and — more honest motivation. i mentioned the 'thing that's mine' thing about the espresso machine. this project is the same family of need but applied to my brain. routeopt is a thing i do for daniela and brújula. cami's wellbeing is a thing i do for cami. my parents' allowance is a thing i do for them. this pose estimation tool is a thing i'm doing because i can. no kpi. no audience. just me, diego, maybe martín if i show him later, and a problem i find interesting. cami already noticed me being more energized about evenings since the idea took root. she said 'oh good, you're doing that nerd thing again.' which is her being kind. i needed permission to want a side project this fast after the funeral month and i think hearing her say that gave me the green light without me asking for it.

so. start small, ship the mvp, don't let it grow tentacles. tell me if my choice of mediapipe-vs-movenet-vs-yolov8 sounds right or if i'm missing a better option.

okay started the pose estimation project. i'm picking tensorflow movenet for now. let me tell you why and you tell me if i'm wrong.

constraints i'm optimizing for: fast inference (i want sub-100ms per frame on cpu so i can run real-time on a laptop without a gpu), small model size (i want to be able to run it on-device on a phone via tflite eventually), and reasonable accuracy on standing humans in motion in indoor lighting at moderate distance from the camera.

movenet hits this profile. the lightning variant is 7mb, runs at 50fps on a modern cpu, returns 17 keypoints per frame, and the accuracy on coco benchmarks for the people-class is competitive enough for amateur form feedback. mediapipe is also good but the python bindings have been a pain in the past for me — i remember struggling with the pip install on an arm mac last year — and the model is more focused on full-body 33-keypoint output which is more than i need. yolov8-pose is hot but the licensing situation makes me nervous for any future-proofing and i don't want to discover six months in that i can't use it.

so movenet thunder for higher accuracy when needed, movenet lightning for the real-time path. i'll start with lightning, see if accuracy is sufficient for guard drop and chin position detection, and switch to thunder only if i need to.

infrastructure: i'm running the prototype as a python fastapi service on my dev machine. video comes in as multipart upload, gets decoded with opencv, frames pushed through movenet via tensorflow, keypoints stored in a list, ruleset evaluated, response returned as json. no persistence yet. no auth. hardcoded to one user (me, with diego as test user once i show him).

next two evenings: get a 'detect guard drop' rule working end-to-end on a 10-second clip of me shadowboxing in the home gym tomorrow morning. if i can get one working detection by sunday night that's a huge first milestone.

quick gut check — i think i'm picking movenet for the right reasons (speed, on-device path, license clarity) and the wrong reason (familiarity bias because i've used tensorflow before for some toy stuff). is that wrong-reason actually a problem or just normal human bias? in startups you optimize for time-to-running-prototype and going with what you know is rational. but i want to be honest with myself if a better tool gets ignored because i don't know it yet.

quick log — recorded the first test video this morning. 12 seconds of me throwing 8 jabs and 6 crosses in front of the home gym mirror. 1080p 30fps. movenet lightning ran the full clip in 4.2 seconds on my mac mini. all 17 keypoints look reasonable on the visualizer. one keypoint flicker on the right ankle around 3.2s, probably because my robe drape was occluding it briefly. should be fine for the rules layer. now i write rules. saved the clip to ~/code/sparring/data/seb_001.mp4 and the keypoint json next to it for repeatability.

big day for the side project. let me document everything because future-me is going to want to remember the moment things started clicking.

so — i wrote the first rule today. detect_guard_drop. simple version: for each frame, check the y-coordinate of the rear-hand wrist (left wrist for orthodox, right wrist for southpaw — i defaulted to orthodox for now). if wrist_y > chin_y for more than 9 consecutive frames (~300ms at 30fps), flag a guard drop event with the timestamp range. that's it. 30 lines of python including the smoothing pass.

ran it on the 12-second video from yesterday. detected 4 guard drops. went back to the video and watched at 0.25x. all 4 were real — i'm dropping the rear hand on the recovery from the jab, classic amateur mistake. one was a false positive in the sense that the drop was barely below chin, like 5cm, which is fine in real life but the rule fired. i'll add a hysteresis margin tomorrow — drop must be more than 8cm below chin to count. easy fix.

then i did the 'show diego' thing. drove to his place in la condesa around 8pm. he records about 30 seconds of himself doing a 1-2-3 combination. we run it through. detects 7 guard drops in 30 seconds, more than half on the third punch in the combo where his rear hand is way late getting back. diego's reaction was — and i quote — 'puta madre, sí, estoy haciendo eso, lo siento al pegar.' which is the exact reaction i wanted. he said he never realized he was doing it that consistently and that he could feel it on the gym floor but never had a way to confirm. that was the moment i knew this thing has actual user value, even at this dumb prototype stage. diego is officially user #2. user #1 is me.

so i'm calling it: pablo (diego's nickname for me when we're sparring is 'pablo el rítmico' from my footwork, and he's pablo el contundente from his power, so we trade — fine, doesn't matter, the name's stuck) and diego are testing this thing. early test users. they'll give me real feedback because they're actual kickboxers, not strangers.

next week's plan:
1. add the chin-position rule. simple keypoint distance check.
2. add hysteresis on guard drop to drop the false positive rate.

things i'm deliberately not doing yet:
— no react native frontend. cli is fine.

questions for you:
1. on smoothing — i'm currently doing a moving average over 3 frames on the keypoint coordinates before applying rules. should i be doing something more sophisticated like a kalman filter or savitzky-golay? for our use case (guard drop detection where the signal we care about is sustained position over hundreds of milliseconds, not high-frequency motion), is the cheap moving average actually fine?
2. on rule architecture — right now my detect_guard_drop is a hardcoded function. i can already see the next rules (chin out, foot crossing) will share boilerplate (load keypoints, smooth, iterate frames, emit events). should i abstract a 'rule' interface now or wait until i have three rules and then do the refactor? i'm wary of premature abstraction.
3. on diego's data — is there an ethics thing about storing video of someone training even with consent? he said yes verbally. i didn't make him sign anything. is a one-line 'i consent to my training videos being used for prototype development' email sufficient or am i being naive about a future where this becomes anything more public?
4. recording angles — diego records from straight ahead. i record from the mirror reflection (so it's effectively straight on but mirrored). do i need to handle the mirroring in software or is it cleaner to just always record from a consistent angle? both work, just a question of where to put the complexity.

there's also the social-proof loop to think about. if diego sticks with this and shows it to two other guys at lobo gym, and i let them try it, am i suddenly running a tiny user trial? at what point does it become unethical to keep collecting their videos without telling them what i'm doing with the footage? i don't think this turns into anything bigger than 'four guys at a kickboxing gym in interlomas' but i should set the boundary now before it surprises me. i'm thinking — anyone who tries it gets a one-paragraph note that says 'this is a hobby project, your videos stay on my mac mini, deleted after you ask, never used for training a model, never shared.' that feels right. but is it enough?

cami asked me at dinner what i was so excited about. i said 'i wrote a thing that watches video of me boxing and tells me when i drop my guard.' she said 'that's so weird and good.' which is what she says when she's pleased that i'm building something fun for myself but doesn't fully understand it. it's been a while since she's said anything close to 'good' about a thing i'm doing for myself. small win on the home front.

last thought tonight — diego's reaction is changing how i think about this project. before today, i was building it because i thought i would use it on myself. but seeing diego react in real time to seeing his own form objectively measured for the first time made me realize the value isn't in the data. the value is in the moment of recognition where someone sees their habit on screen and says 'oh shit yes, i do that.' that's the product. the rules engine, the keypoints, the rest is plumbing. that reframes how i should design the ux. the punch isn't the report. the punch is the playback with the flagged moments highlighted. i'm going to design the ux around playback annotations first, report second. that's a different architecture decision than i was about to make. write it down: ux is annotated playback, not a list of errors. annotated playback. mira tu propio jab y date cuenta.

also — i want to make a tiny commitment in writing here so future-me holds present-me to it. the side project will get a maximum of 5 hours per week on weeknights and one weekend morning. it does not bleed into routeopt time. it does not bleed into cami time. if i find myself wanting to spend a saturday afternoon on it, i pay myself in advance by doing something with cami in the morning. if i find the project pulling on me during work hours, i close the tab. those are the rules. write them down for me so i can copy them into my notes app. i've blown up too many side projects by letting them grow tentacles into the rest of my life and i'm not doing it again, especially not in the year cami's mom died and i became a staff engineer at the same time. the discipline matters more than the project.

last thing — i need to set up a private git repo for this. github private. no readme yet. licensed mit if i ever open source, but that's a 'future seb' decision. just want to start committing. naming the repo 'cornerman' tonight unless you have a better idea. cornerman.app would be a decent domain if this ever goes public but i'm getting ahead of myself. and i'm using github actions for ci even at this prototype stage because i want to enforce that any rule i add has at least one test against a known clip. if the test suite stops being green i don't merge. that's the discipline i want to bake in from day one. i've watched too many side projects rot because the author let test coverage drop in week 3 and never recovered.

one more thing on the diego reaction. he asked if he could show this to his cousin who fights amateur in puebla and is preparing for a regional tournament next month. i said no, not yet, because the false-positive rate at this stage would actively mislead a competitive fighter. that's the thing — for me and diego doing recreational shadowboxing, an extra two false guard drops per minute is a useful signal in aggregate. for someone preparing for competition, a false alarm could push them to overcorrect and develop a worse habit. so the product has different fitness levels for different users and i should be intentional about who tries it. for now: lobo gym recreational guys only. diego is the canonical test user precisely because he's at my level — recreational, technically literate, willing to give honest feedback, not preparing for any kind of competition where my false positives could mess him up.

side note on the model choice. i was reading the movenet paper tonight and the architecture is a single-stage detector that does both person detection and pose estimation in one forward pass. that's why it's fast. but it's also why it struggles in occluded scenes — when the person partially leaves the frame or another body occludes them, the keypoint confidence collapses to near-zero and the smoothing has nothing to work with. for boxing where the camera is fixed and one person is in frame, this isn't a problem. but if i ever extend to two-person sparring footage, movenet will break and i'll need to swap to a top-down model that does detection first and pose second. probably yolov8-pose or a fine-tuned hrnet. but that's a 'maybe in 6 months' problem. i'm flagging it for memory.

and the keypoint json. i'm storing it as a list of {frame_index, timestamp_ms, keypoints: [{name, x, y, confidence}, ...]} per frame. that's verbose but readable. binary formats like protobuf would be more compact but i don't care yet. for the volumes i'm dealing with — a 30-second clip is 900 frames is maybe 200kb of json — readability wins. i'll revisit if the side project ever has volume that justifies a binary format which it probably never will.

last 'principle' i want to write down for the project. no premature deployment. no premature scale. no premature architecture. the next 8 weeks are about getting one rule working really well and then a second rule and then the ux for the playback annotations. nothing else matters. if i catch myself thinking about kubernetes or auth or stripe integrations, i'm pulling myself back to the core loop. period.

quick win on cornerman tonight. added the chin position rule. it's stupider than guard drop. just compute the angle from the spine vector (hip midpoint to shoulder midpoint) to the chin-to-skull-base vector (chin keypoint to ear midpoint). when the chin sticks out (head pushed forward, classic 'chasing the punch' habit), the angle exceeds about 18 degrees. when the chin is tucked, it's under 8. set the threshold at 14 with hysteresis to avoid jitter.

ran it on the same seb_001.mp4. detected 11 chin-out events in 12 seconds, mostly on the cross. that's brutal but accurate. i watched myself in playback and yeah, every time i throw a hard cross my chin is dangling out there like an invitation. martín has been telling me this for two years and i've been mostly fixing it but the rule made it visceral in a way verbal coaching didn't. there's something about seeing the keypoint overlay drift forward in slow motion while a 'chin out' label flashes on the corner of the screen that engages a different part of the brain than 'oye seb, mete la barbilla.' i'm going to think about why that's true — maybe it's the absence of social judgment in the feedback loop, you can take a critique from a machine without flinching that you can't take from a coach.

so two rules, two real detections. project's basically working.

started a 'session' table in postgres. local docker compose, single container, schema is just sessions(id, user_id, created_at, video_path, duration_s) and events(id, session_id, rule_name, t_start, t_end, severity, metadata_json). normalized. boring. correct.

questions: 1) the metadata_json column on events — i'm using it for rule-specific info like 'wrist_y peak value during this guard drop' so each rule can store its specific signal strength without me having to add new columns per rule. is that overuse of jsonb or is it the right escape hatch for an extensible rule set? 2) should the events table have a 'severity' column or should severity be a function applied at query time? i went with a column for now because i wanted to be able to sort by severity in the ui. tell me if i'll regret it. 3) for video_path — is storing the filesystem path in postgres acceptable for now or should i be storing in s3 from day one and just put the s3 url in the column? i lean filesystem because the videos never leave my mac, but if i ever go multi-machine the s3 path is the right answer. tell me lazy.

big cornerman update. spent the saturday morning on it. four hours, more than my self-imposed 5-hours-per-week budget allows me, but i'm front-loading and i'll skip a midweek session to compensate. let me catch you up.

refactored the rule architecture finally. each rule is now a class implementing a Rule protocol with three methods: setup(self, video_meta) — runs once per video, useful for any rule-level state like 'what fps are we at'; evaluate(self, frame_keypoints, frame_index) — runs per frame, returns either None or a partial event; finalize(self) — runs once after all frames are evaluated, can emit deferred events that need full-video context (like 'chin out 80% of the session, flag a session-level warning').

guard drop and chin position both fit this interface cleanly. the test for whether the abstraction was right: i added the third rule, foot crossing, in 90 minutes including writing the test. that's a good signal. premature abstraction is when you spend three hours on the abstraction and then the new use case doesn't fit it. mine fit it. so the abstraction was timed correctly.

foot crossing rule: detect when, during forward movement, the lead foot's x-coordinate crosses behind the rear foot's x-coordinate, holding for >150ms. that's the 'crossing the feet' habit that loses lateral mobility and makes you trip over yourself when you have to evade. i tested it on a 25-second video of me doing 'in-out' footwork drills. detected 3 crossing events. went back to the playback and one was a real cross-over (probably a moment of laziness on the third rep), one was a false positive caused by the foot landmark jittering during a step, and one was ambiguous. so 1/3 false positive rate which is too high.

the false positive root-causing — the foot keypoints jitter when the foot is in motion mid-step. movenet is fundamentally a per-frame model. it has no temporal coherence. so during a fast lateral step, the keypoint can momentarily land on the wrong foot or wobble. options to fix: a) smooth more aggressively (current is moving avg over 3 frames, could go 5-7), b) use a tracker on top of movenet to maintain identity across frames, c) require the cross-over to be sustained for 200ms instead of 150ms (less responsive but less false positive). i lean (c) for the mvp because it's a one-line change. (b) is the right long-term answer but it's another component to maintain.

separately — i started thinking about packaging. the current setup is python fastapi + opencv + tensorflow + my rules + a sqlalchemy/postgres layer. that's a lot of dependencies. installing tensorflow + cuda on a vps is a pain. i'm wondering if for v0 i should just bundle this as a docker image with the model weights baked in, deploy on a simple t3.small ec2 (no gpu — movenet runs fine on cpu), and call it done. the alternative is fly.io or railway but i don't want to learn a new pass system right now. ec2 is the boring choice and i'm fine being boring on infra.

cami's mood today: better. she went out with marta for brunch and came back with flowers from a market stall. ferro slept on her lap for two hours. she said 'i think i had a good day' before bed which is not a thing she's said in five weeks. i didn't make a big deal about it because i'm worried that pointing out a good day will make tomorrow feel worse if it isn't. but i logged it in the not-quite-spreadsheet i'm keeping (you'll be pleased to know i went with the notes-app option, single line per day, not the structured doc).

questions:
1. for foot keypoint jitter mitigation, should i go with the easy 200ms threshold or invest in a proper tracker (deep_sort, byte_tracker)? for an mvp where i have one user (me) and one test user (diego), is identity tracking even necessary? we're alone in the videos.
2. on packaging for v0, is t3.small ec2 with docker the right path or am i over-thinking and i should just keep this on my mac mini and not deploy at all until i have at least 5 users? lean lazy here, please tell me lazy.
3. on the rule abstraction — the finalize() hook for session-level patterns — am i overdesigning or is it actually useful? i can imagine a rule like 'if more than 60% of crosses have chin-out, escalate to a session-level coaching note' but that might be premature.

last thing — diego texted me asking when round 2 is. he wants to try a longer recording, like a full 3-minute round of shadowboxing, to see if the system holds up at length. that's a real user testing prompt and i should accommodate it. setting up a session for tuesday evening at his place.

deeper questions creeping into my head:
4. as i build out more rules, am i building a generic 'fight form analyzer' or a kickboxing-specific tool? if i ever extend it to bjj or muay thai or even yoga, the rule abstraction will get strained because those sports have totally different temporal patterns. should i name and structure the project explicitly as 'striking sports' (boxing/kickboxing/muay thai) and accept that other striking adjacent sports might fit, but ground sports are out of scope? i think yes. setting that boundary early prevents scope creep.
5. on rules-as-code vs rules-as-config — right now my rules are python classes. that's fine for me as the only author. but if i ever wanted other coaches to author rules, i'd want a yaml or json dsl. should i be designing toward that or am i premature-optimizing for users who don't exist? lean premature.
6. on the sqlalchemy layer — i'm using sqlalchemy core with fastapi for the persistence layer. it's fine for now. but the per-frame keypoint storage is straining the relational model — a 3-minute video with 5400 frames * 17 keypoints means i'd insert ~92k rows for a single session if i normalized keypoints. obviously i'm storing the full keypoint array as a parquet file alongside the video. is that the right pattern (relational metadata in postgres, keypoint data as columnar files on disk) or should i be looking at duckdb for the keypoint analytics layer? duckdb seems like a better fit for the 'aggregate keypoint stats over a session' query pattern. tell me if duckdb is overkill or actually simplifying.

closing thought — when i started this project two weeks ago i told you i'd cap it at 8 weeks of evenings. i'm 3 weeks in. on track. cami is doing better. routeopt is going better than i expected. side project is producing real wins on a 5-hour-week budget. i've been sleeping 7 hours which for me is good. i'm going to log this so future-me has a snapshot — this is what 'sustainable side project velocity' actually looks like in real life when other things are going well.

a small but important productivity observation. the side project on a 5-hour-week budget produces more weekly progress than my early-career attempts at 20-hour-week budgets. i used to think more hours = more progress, linearly. it's actually more like — focused hours within a sustainable rhythm produce more useful output than burnt-out hours within an unsustainable one. specifically the 5 hours i spend on cornerman are cognitively top-shelf because they happen after a kickboxing session or after dinner with cami when my prefrontal cortex is happy. the 20-hour-week version was 2am attempts at staying awake on adderall i didn't have a prescription for. terrible by every measure. logged so future-seb who sometimes feels lazy for not 'going harder' remembers that going-hard is a long-run negative signal, not a virtue.

oh and — i did the saturday morning commitment thing. cami and i went to a market in coyoacán in the morning, walked around for two hours, ate elotes and tacos al pastor at a stall she liked, came home, she napped, and then i did the cornerman work. that's the prepayment model i committed to and it works. i'm going to keep it.

one last thought experiment for you. say cornerman works. say in 8 weeks i have a working mvp that diego and i are using daily and that 4 other guys at lobo gym want access to. what's the right call: open source it as mit and let the community pull it forward, freeze it as a private 'just for friends' tool, or commercialize it lightly with a 5-bucks-a-month patreon vibe? my gut says open source because i don't want this to become a thing i resent. but a small subscription would justify keeping it polished. tell me how you'd think about that fork in the road, even though i'm 8 weeks early to be deciding it. there's also the option of letting this stay private indefinitely and never sharing it — just a tool for me and diego forever. i would rather not — the world has enough people sitting on useful private tools, the cost of opening this up later is low, the downside of not opening it up is i never know if it would have helped anyone else.

other questions creeping in for the longer arc.

7. the rules library shape. right now i have three rules and they're all 'detect a bad habit'. but actual coaching is also 'reinforce good habits' (the equivalent of a positive feedback signal — 'good chin tucks: 14 in this session'). should the rule taxonomy include detect-bad and detect-good, or am i overthinking? from a ux perspective, getting only negative feedback is going to demoralize users. positive reinforcement is part of any decent coaching loop. i think v1 should include at least one positive rule (like 'good shoulder rotation on cross') alongside the bad-habit detectors. balanced feedback.
8. on the 'session goals' concept — diego's reaction at his place last week ('this changes my training plan') suggests there's a workflow where after a playback you set 1-2 specific goals for the next session, and the next session's playback shows whether you improved on those goals specifically. that's a real coaching loop. should i be designing the data model toward that from the start, or is that v2? lean toward designing the data model that way now (it's just a 'goals' table tied to a session) but not building the ui until v2.
9. on offline-first vs cloud-first — kickboxers may train in gyms with bad wifi. the desktop cli prototype handles this fine because everything is local. but the eventual mobile app needs to handle 'record offline, upload when wifi returns'. that means client-side queueing, retry logic, and conflict resolution if the server's already processed an older upload. it's a real piece of work for the mobile version. flagging now so when i get to mobile in 6 weeks i don't pretend it's simple.
10. and the privacy backbone. video of someone training is sensitive — it shows their body, their gym, sometimes other people in the background. v0 stores everything on my mac mini, easy. v1 with diego and 4 lobo gym buddies, also easy. v2 if it ever opens to strangers, i need a real privacy posture: encryption at rest, deletion on demand, no third-party model training, no analytics on video content. designing for that from day one is cheap. retrofitting it after the fact is brutal. i'm going to write a 'privacy promises' document this weekend and pin it in the repo readme so it's a constraint i can't quietly break.

okay i'm going to stop typing and go pull a shot on the mara x. weekend morning, cami still asleep, ferro already complaining for breakfast. that's the rhythm i want to preserve through this whole side-project arc — building cornerman is sustainable as long as it doesn't crowd out the things that make me a person worth being. coffee, kickboxing, cami, family. side project lives in the spaces, not on top of them.

okay actually one more architectural thought before i go. i've been thinking about whether the rule engine should be event-driven or stream-driven. event-driven means: each frame produces a 'frame_processed' event, rules subscribe to events, an event bus dispatches. stream-driven means: each rule consumes the keypoint stream directly via a generator/coroutine pattern. for python, the stream-driven version with async generators is simpler and i don't need the indirection of an event bus until i'm running multiple subscribers per event type. so stream-driven for v0. event-driven if and only if i ever need plugin-style rule extensibility from third parties, which probably never. write it down.

routeopt update — the haversine vectorization landed today. moved from the per-row python loop to a single numpy call computing the full 200x200 distance matrix in vectorized form using broadcasting. the math: convert lat/lon to radians, broadcast pairwise differences, apply the haversine formula in vectorized form, return as float32 ndarray. about 35 lines of code. ran the synthetic load test. p95 dropped from 188ms to 142ms on the nominal profile, and from 290ms to 198ms p99. that's the win i was hoping for.

cumulative impact of the harden work this past two weeks: 340ms → 142ms p95, 800ms → 198ms p99. that's 58% off p95 and 75% off p99. for the rfc i'll round the p99 to 'roughly 4x improvement' because that's the headline that lands. p95 gain is also good but the p99 gain is the actual reliability win since that's the one that breaks slas.

at this point the rust rewrite case is officially dead on roi. or rather, it's not dead, but it's a 12-month problem not a now problem. so i'm pivoting the rfc. new framing: 'routeopt operational maturity playbook' with a section called 'when do we revisit a rewrite'. the trigger conditions for revisiting are: (a) p95 climbs back above 200ms under nominal load despite tuning, (b) traffic doubles, or (c) we add a feature that fundamentally requires sub-50ms response (e.g., live broker dashboard with real-time route updates). without one of those, we're better served by continuing to tune django.

that's an honest framing, it positions the rust work as a future option rather than a defeat, and it gives leadership a concrete trigger to watch. cami pointed out at dinner that 'you sound proud of the not-rewriting' which — yeah, i kind of am. the rewrite would have been a vanity project at this point.

specific work left to do this week: 1) write the 'how the harden plan is structured' section of the rfc with the four fixes documented. 2) write the 'what the trigger conditions for revisiting are' section. 3) put together the headline numbers in a small chart for the principals (oscar especially, he loves data). 4) get amir and lupita to read the draft before i submit. amir because dispatch-core is downstream and lupita because geofencing might want to absorb some of these patterns. 5) book the raul coffee.
