cornerman tuesday session at diego's place. set up the laptop on his coffee table, faced toward his living room (which is small but has good lighting). he did a 3-minute round of shadowboxing — combos, footwork, the works. recorded at 1080p 30fps. file size 540mb because i wasn't compressing on capture. need to fix that.

ran it through the pipeline. movenet processed in 38 seconds for the 5400-frame video. all three rules evaluated. detection counts: 18 guard drops (mostly on punch returns), 22 chin-out events (mostly on power crosses), 4 foot crossings (with the 200ms threshold, so the false positive rate dropped from 33% to 0% on this video — sanity check passed).

diego watched the playback with annotations and his reaction was — 'okay esto es brutal pero útil.' brutal but useful. he said the chin-out detection was the biggest revelation. he hadn't realized he was committing his face every time he threw a power shot. he said 'martín ha estado diciéndome esto y no le creía hasta que lo vi en pantalla.' that's the user moment again. seeing your form objectively measured is fundamentally different from being told.

he also asked a question i wasn't ready for: 'puedes detectar mi pivote en el cruce?' — can you detect rear-foot pivot on the cross? that's actually a hard one because it requires three-axis foot landmark accuracy and movenet on a single camera view doesn't reliably give me foot rotation. i told him 'eventualmente sí, pero no con esta cámara' and noted it for the post-mvp roadmap.

other note from the session — 3 minutes is a real test. the file size, the processing time, the keypoint storage all need to scale. for a 3-minute clip at 30fps that's 5400 frames at 17 keypoints each, so 91800 keypoint vectors. fits in ram easily but i should think about disk format. currently storing as a list-of-dict json which is wasteful. switching to parquet or a structured numpy save will probably 5x compress.

also at diego's i noticed something about the user moment that's worth recording. before he watched the playback he was relaxed. while he watched the playback with annotations he was tense — leaning forward, hand on chin, occasionally muttering 'no mames'. after the playback he went quiet for like a minute and then said 'okay esto cambia mi plan de entrenamiento esta semana.' — this is going to change my training plan for this week. that's the conversion moment. i should be designing the post-playback flow around that — capture the 'training plan' resolution while the energy is high. like a one-tap 'add this to my session goals for next week' button. that turns a single playback into a behavioral nudge with continuity across sessions. logged in the post-mvp roadmap.

what i didn't expect from this session — diego started teaching me. specifically he said 'oye seb, fíjate, la chin-out la veo en mi power cross pero también en mis hooks que no estás detectando. tu sistema solo mira jab y cross.' he's right. my rules engine doesn't have a hook detector. so we sat for an hour and he showed me the keypoint pattern of a hook (lateral hand motion with hip rotation, no forward extension), and i sketched the rule on his coffee table napkin. that's the kind of design conversation i can't have with myself. user-as-collaborator. logged: build the relationship with diego beyond 'he tests, i build' — he's a domain expert i should be co-designing with. he's not paid, he's not formally part of the project, but he's adding more value than most paid contributors do.

on routeopt postgis migration — amir came through. he migrated dispatch-core's reader to the new geometry column today, ahead of the 2-week sla i'd set. nice. service_area_json column is now read-only legacy and i can drop it in a follow-up cleanup ticket.

incidentally, while reviewing his pr, i caught a subtle bug — he was still using ST_Intersects in 4326 (lat/lon degrees) which is wrong for distance-sensitive queries because intersection in 4326 doesn't account for projection distortion at high latitudes. for cdmx, monterrey, guadalajara that's basically fine because we're at low latitudes. but if we ever onboard a broker in tijuana or further north, the error compounds. i suggested he project to a local utm zone or use ST_Intersects with ::geography casting. he chose the geography cast which is the right call for our use case since it's an internal correctness fix not a performance fix. small architecture review moment that landed cleanly. this is what the staff role is supposed to be.

cami had what she called 'a setback wednesday' — woke up at 4am crying and couldn't get back to sleep, lay in bed staring at the ceiling until 7am, and called in sick to foster which she's only done one other time in her two years there. when i checked on her at 6am she said 'i don't know why today is bad. nothing happened. i just feel her absence very loud today.' she didn't want to talk about it more than that. spent the day at home with ferro, didn't shower until 4pm, ate two pieces of toast and half a bowl of oatmeal and that's it.

i was at the office. came home at 6pm. she was in the back garden with a blanket and ferro. the air was getting cold. i didn't know whether to bring her in or sit with her. i sat with her. brought her a sweater. didn't talk for 40 minutes. eventually she said 'i was thinking about whether mom would have liked the apartment in interlomas' — we moved here after her mom got sick, and her mom only ever visited the san ángel place where we lived before. and now her mom won't ever see this kitchen, won't ever sit at the table, won't ever make café de olla in the moka pot for cami again. that landed like a brick. she was crying quietly the whole time.

what i did: i didn't try to fix it. i didn't say 'she would have loved it' or any of the platitudes. i said 'tell me what mom liked about the san ángel place' and cami talked for an hour about her mom and the san ángel apartment, the way she always wanted to sit on the kitchen floor and hand-press tortillas because the counter height was wrong for her, the time her mom rearranged our spice cabinet because she couldn't bear how disorganized it was, the time her mom met ferro for the first time and ferro hissed at her and she said 'mira, ya sabes que soy una persona difícil, gato' and laughed. cami was laughing and crying at the same time. it was the most she'd talked about her mom since the funeral.

i know there's no manual for this but i think i did it right tonight. i didn't fix, i listened, i asked one open question, i let her go. now she's asleep. ferro's on her chest. i'm in the kitchen with the moka pot and i don't think i'll go to bed for a while.

related — the promo announcement went out on slack monday. amir, lupita, isa, mateo, renata all sent congrats. the staff cohort sent dms. carla the sre lead sent me a meme of a server on fire with a 'congratulations on your new responsibilities' caption. it was a low-key but warm response. one thing i didn't expect — daniela's boss raul sent me a personal email saying 'sebastián, glad to see you at staff. when you're ready, i'd like to grab coffee and hear how you're thinking about the routeopt question.' that's a meaningful nudge. raul hardly does coffee.

so — there's a 'work seb' and a 'home seb' and they were both processing a lot today. work seb is at the cusp of a real rfc that will land. home seb is sitting in a kitchen at 11pm thinking about how grief moves in waves and how there's no schedule.

questions that aren't really questions:
— is the rfc revision i described last weekend solid, or am i missing something important about what raul will care about? his email said 'how you're thinking about the routeopt question' which i read as 'are you going to recommend the rewrite or not'. he probably wants a steer before the rfc lands.
— should i preemptively schedule the raul coffee for next week, or wait for him to follow up? i lean schedule, because he initiated and not responding is rude.
— for cami, what's the right rhythm for these setback days? is there an evidence-based answer about whether grief setbacks come in patterns or whether they're truly random? i don't want to surveil her but i'd like to be able to predict bad days so i can be home for them.

going to bed eventually. mara x sat unused on the counter today. for the first time since it arrived. i'll pull a shot tomorrow morning.

one more thing — i sat down at 10pm tonight and wrote a 'past seb to future seb' note, like the one i described to you weeks ago for the promo decision. tonight's note was about the cami garden conversation. wanted to remember the specifics: the sweater i brought (the navy zip-up i got in oaxaca two christmases ago that she's been wearing since the funeral), the time (45 minutes of silence before she spoke), the things she said about her mom (the spice cabinet story, the ferro story, the san ángel kitchen floor story), my move (one open question, no fixing). i want to remember that this is what showing up looks like in this phase. and i want to remember that 'showing up' meant 'shutting up' for the first 40 minutes. that's not a thing my engineer brain wants to learn but it's apparently the lesson.

i also texted marta tonight, which i told you weeks ago i should do for the bad days. i kept it minimal. 'cami had a hard one today. she's okay now but if you can call her tomorrow that would help.' marta replied within 4 minutes — 'gracias seb, voy a llamarla mañana en la mañana, se nota cuando es uno de esos días pero no quería invadir.' so she'd been wanting to call but holding off out of respect. that's information i needed and didn't have. now we have a soft signal protocol. when i say 'hard one', marta calls. when i don't, marta gives space. clean.

related — i think i need to get cami a therapist. not because tonight was a crisis (it wasn't) but because the setbacks are pattern, not random. and the grief group didn't work for her because the format was 'share with strangers' and her temperament is 'process privately with one trusted person.' a 1:1 therapist who specializes in bereavement is the right fit. i don't want to pitch this tomorrow because i don't want her to feel like the garden conversation was 'evidence' i collected to make a case. i'll let it sit a week and bring it up on a non-bad day when she's in a problem-solving headspace.

also — for me. i think i need to talk to someone too. not because i'm cracking (i'm not) but because the 'home seb' is doing a lot of unpaid emotional labor and 'work seb' is at a level where the stakes don't go down. martín at the gym is a good outlet but he's not a confidant for the deeper stuff. an actual therapist for me, even just 6 sessions to talk through the year so far, would probably keep me from blowing something up. cami going first is the natural order. if she sees the value, i'll follow. if she doesn't, i go regardless.

going to sleep. there's a lot of next-monday to get to. and a hard pivot to make in the rfc framing that needs to land clean.

p.s. i looked at the cornerman repo before bed and there's a subtle bug. on the chin-out rule, when the camera angle is even slightly off-axis (which diego's was, his living room camera was tilted maybe 8 degrees), the spine vector is biased and the chin angle reads ~3 degrees higher than it should. so some of his 22 chin-out detections might be borderline false positives that would clear under a true frontal camera. i need to either calibrate the camera angle from the keypoints (the shoulder-line angle is a decent proxy for camera tilt) or normalize the spine vector to a frontal projection. this is a real thing for v1 because the whole point is users can record from any angle in any gym. logged. but my night-tired brain isn't fixing it now. tomorrow morning i'll write the camera-angle correction as a preprocessing pass before the rules layer runs.

last 'principle' on this for tonight. if the rule is firing on a borderline case, the right product behavior is to label it as 'borderline' rather than as either 'fired' or 'not fired'. binary classification on a continuous signal is one of the cardinal product mistakes in detection systems. cornerman should have three states per detection: confirmed, borderline, suppressed. confirmed means high confidence the bad form happened. borderline means medium confidence (signal in the gray zone, user can choose to attend or ignore). suppressed means we caught it but discarded it because of context (e.g., a guard drop while the user was visibly resting between combos). the borderline category is the trust-building one — it tells the user 'i saw something, you decide if it counts' rather than 'i'm sure'. that's a more honest product. logging it for v1 implementation.

actually one more thing on the camera-angle correction. the elegant solution is to fit a homography from a calibration video — user records 5 seconds of standing still in a 'neutral pose' (arms at sides, feet shoulder-width apart, looking at camera), and we use the keypoints from that to derive the camera's rotation and translation relative to the user. then every subsequent video gets warped into the canonical frame before the rules apply. that's clean, generalizable, and worth maybe two days of work. but it adds a 'calibrate once' onboarding step which is friction. for v0 i'll do the simpler heuristic — derive camera tilt from the shoulder-line angle in the first second of motion, apply a per-frame rotation to the keypoint coordinates, no calibration step. if accuracy isn't enough i'll add the homography path in v1. logging this trade-off so future-me knows why the easy path was chosen.

also tonight cami told me, after the garden conversation, that she wants me to take a saturday in two weekends to drive down to oaxaca and see my parents. she said 'they're worried about you and you keep promising them you'll come and not coming. just go. i'll be fine for two days.' and i pushed back — 'no, i'm not going to leave you alone for two days right now' — and she said 'sebastián, i love you, but you are not my whole grief. you don't have to babysit me. and your dad's heart is not getting younger. go.' which — that's a thing. i'm going to go in two weekends. drive down friday night, stay with my parents through sunday lunch, drive back sunday afternoon. that's the right call and i should have made it weeks ago. cami pushing me to make it is, in some weird way, a sign that she's recovering enough to think about other people's needs again. small data point.

okay actually going to bed.

cornerman tweak today — fixed the camera angle bug. wrote a preprocessing pass that estimates camera tilt from the line connecting the two shoulder keypoints averaged over the first 30 frames where the subject is standing relatively still. compute the angle of that line from horizontal, treat that as 'camera roll', and apply an inverse rotation to all subsequent keypoints before passing them to the rules. ran it on diego's clip from tuesday — 8 of his 22 chin-out detections dropped out as borderline false positives under the corrected angle. that's a 36% reduction. still detecting the real ones, including all 7 of his power-cross chin-outs which were the ones he most needed to know about.

caveat — this assumes the camera roll is the only off-axis component. yaw (camera tilted left/right) and pitch (camera tilted up/down) are not corrected. for a phone propped on a gym mat with the lens pointed roughly at the subject, roll is the dominant source of error and pitch/yaw are small. for camera-on-tripod-pointed-at-someone-far-away, those components matter more. logging it as a known limitation. the right v2 fix is to estimate full 6dof camera pose from a multi-frame structure-from-motion approach but that's massive overengineering for a side project. accepted limitation: 'level your phone before you record.'

separately — wrote a 'session report' generator. simple text summary, deterministic, generated server-side. for diego's 3-minute clip it reads:

'session: 3:00 of shadowboxing. 14 chin-out events detected (7 confirmed power-cross, 7 mixed). 18 guard-drop events detected (mostly on jab return). 4 foot-crossing events detected during forward movement. session-level note: chin-out frequency is high enough to be a session-level emphasis area for next training session.'

that's the kind of thing diego can screenshot and send to martín if he wants to. low-friction. doesn't require him to load a tool. martín told diego at thursday's session that 'whatever you're doing this week is working, your jab return is much cleaner.' diego smiled and didn't tell martín about cornerman. fair enough. martín will hear about it eventually.

next eve: integrate the report generator into the playback ui as a download button. and start sketching the trends layer — diego's session 2 should show that his chin-out rate went from 22 to 16 (per 3 minutes) which is a 27% improvement after one week of conscious work. that's a real signal and being able to surface it longitudinally is the unlock that turns this from a one-off check into a coaching tool.

morning espresso log — first 'good' shot on the mara x today. 18g in, 36g out, 28 seconds. the colombian washed cami likes pulled clean, no sour notes. she said 'oh' when she tasted it. that's high praise from cami. mara x is real. seasoning shots from the previous three days are now done and i'm ready to start dialing in for real. notebook by the machine, dose-yield-time tracked. eight shots in already and the variance is shrinking. learning curve is real but tractable.

okay another cornerman session. extended the rules with a fourth one — shoulder rotation timing on the cross. the rule: when a power cross is detected, the rear shoulder should be reaching peak rotation 80-120ms before peak hand extension. if the shoulder rotates after the hand reaches peak, that's an 'arm punch' and you're losing power. the detection is timing-based on keypoints, no force measurement.

implementing this required adding 'punch event detection' which i hadn't done yet. currently rules just iterate frame-by-frame. now i need to first detect punch events as a higher-level abstraction, and then run the timing rule on each detected punch. so the rules architecture is becoming a two-layer system: layer 1 is event detection (jab, cross, hook, kick, footwork-step), layer 2 is form analysis on detected events.

felt good to refactor. the new abstraction is cleaner. i went back and rewrote guard-drop in the new architecture — it's now a 'rule that listens to punch events and checks that the rear hand stayed up during the event window'. instead of looking at every frame for guard drop, it only checks during punch events, which removes a class of false positive (rear hand naturally lower when not punching).

ran the new pipeline on seb_001 and diego's clip. detection rates are more consistent. felt the architecture pivot was worth the time even though it slowed me down a day.

questions: 1) for the punch event detector — i'm currently using a heuristic where a punch is 'rear hand x-coordinate moves forward by >X% of body width within 100ms and returns within 400ms'. that's gross but it's working. should i think about training a small classifier on labeled examples (say, 200 hand-labeled punch events) or is the heuristic enough for the mvp? lean: heuristic, ship, iterate. 2) hooks and uppercuts are different motion patterns from jab/cross. should the punch detector handle them now or in v2? current intuition: ignore them in v1, the rules are jab/cross specific anyway. 3) for the hook, the form errors are different — overrotation, dropping the off-hand. needs different rules entirely. that's a v2 problem.

rfc submitted. let me document the moment.

draft was ready friday eod as planned. i circulated to amir, lupita, and daniela monday morning. amir came back tuesday with a substantive review — he caught two factual errors in the historical traffic data (i had cited q3 numbers that were actually q4 and the implication of the trend looked different) and pushed back on the 'p99 collapses with rust' argument by pointing out that the gc tail isn't the only contributor to p99 — there's also lock contention in our connection pool which a rewrite wouldn't trivially fix. fair point. i incorporated both. lupita came back with a smaller comment about the geofencing service's interaction model and i added a 'forward-compatibility' section that addressed her concern. daniela left a single comment that said 'love it, ship it after amir's edits' which is daniela for 'this is good, you don't need me to fix it.'

submitted thursday. raul read it before our friday coffee and came in with a sharper question than i expected. he asked: 'if traffic doubles in 18 months and we're back at 200ms p95, do we have the same engineering capacity to do the rust rewrite then or are we deferring an inevitable lift?' that's a real question and i didn't have a great answer in the moment. i said something like 'i'd rather defer the lift until the data demands it because the cost of an unnecessary rewrite is higher than the cost of a deferred one in our context.' he nodded but didn't respond directly. i could tell he was thinking about it.

we then talked about the staff role more broadly. he said 'one piece of advice — staff is when you stop being the smartest person in the room about your service and start being the most useful person about other people's services. you'll feel like you know less every quarter. that's the job.' that landed. i'm going to write that down and stick it on my desk.

he also asked about cami. (apparently daniela mentioned it.) said 'i lost my dad two years ago, the first six months feel like swimming through paste, but you do come out of it.' that was unexpected and kind. i didn't know what to say so i said 'thanks raul, that helps.' he nodded again and we moved on.

after the coffee, walking back to my desk, i realized — the rfc thing is over. it landed. people will comment on it next week. it'll get incorporated into the q2 plan. and that's it. the thing i'd been spinning on for two months is now a doc that exists and is being read.

oscar dropped his comment on the doc late friday. it was three lines: 'good analysis. agree with the harden-first call. flagged a small concern about the autovacuum tuning approach being too aggressive on the polygon table — see comment on §4.2. otherwise lgtm.' i was bracing for a defensive 'why not rust' interrogation. didn't happen. natalia approved without comment. raul replied 'thanks, this is the kind of doc i want to see from staff this year.' that's the line i'm holding on to.

what's left for the harden plan: 1) NOT EXISTS rewrite, 2) autovacuum tuning, 3) batching for >200-delivery requests. those are q2 work, dotted-line owned by me. amir is going to lead the dispatch-core compatibility cleanup. lupita is taking the geofencing forward-compat work. that's the staff distribution starting to happen — i'm not the executor on every piece anymore, i'm the architect setting the trajectory and letting other senior engineers own pieces.

we also talked about the therapist thing on saturday afternoon. i did the 'wait for a non-bad day' approach i told you about. we were sitting at the kitchen table with morning coffee and she said something offhand like 'i don't know how i'd describe what i'm feeling now to anyone who hasn't lost a parent.' and i said 'have you thought about a therapist who specializes in bereavement? not group, individual, someone who's seen this many times.' she didn't react defensively which i'd been worried about. she said 'i've been thinking about it. i was waiting for someone to suggest it because i didn't want to be the one to say i needed help.' which is the most honest she's been about it. so that was the unlock. she's going to ask marta for a recommendation (marta knows everyone in cdmx mental-health-wise, foster's hr lead recommended someone to her three years ago when she was dealing with her divorce). first appointment likely next week.

that's a big shift. i wrote a note in my evening journal that said 'cami opened the door on therapy on her own terms today, partial credit to me for asking but mostly her, save this for the bad-week version of myself who thinks i'm not helping.' put it on the desk note rotation.

amir's lunch this week was great. he picked a guisados place in roma sur i'd never been to. we talked routeopt for 20 minutes and life for an hour. he's expecting his second kid in summer. he asked me how cami was. i told him honestly. he said 'i was at my dad's funeral when my first was born, the same week. so i know that one.' i didn't know that about him. that's why these lunches matter — there's a whole layer of human context that doesn't surface in slack. logged: do amir lunches at least quarterly going forward.

i also reached out to martín after thursday's session and we grabbed a beer. he's the only person in my life who knows how to talk about pressure without making it dramatic. he said something like 'sebastián, en el ring lo que matas es la duda. fuera del ring también.' — what you kill in the ring is doubt. outside the ring too. that's the kind of frame that hits harder than it should. i went home and slept seven straight hours which i haven't done in weeks.

separately — i set up the year's first 1:1 with my new dotted-line peers. amir on dispatch-core, lupita on geofencing. recurring biweekly. agenda is loose: 'what's coming up in your service, what's blocking you that i can help unblock, what cross-team architecture decisions are you nervous about.' that's the staff cadence. i don't want to drift into manager-of-people territory but i need a recurring touchpoint with each of them to stay current on what's happening. lupita asked me on the first one whether the geofencing service should adopt the same postgis pattern routeopt is moving to. i said yes but with a caveat — geofencing has a different access pattern (read-mostly with infrequent updates) so the dual-write phase doesn't matter as much; she could just do the migration in a single deploy with a small downtime window. she liked that. that's the kind of conversation that wouldn't have happened in a slack thread.

a thought on the staff cadence overall. i used to evaluate my work week by lines of code shipped. now i'm evaluating it by 'how many decisions did i help others make better than they would have made alone'. that's a fundamentally different metric and it's the one raul implicitly mentioned at coffee. it also means a 'productive week' looks different — fewer commits in my own name, more comments on other people's prs, more architecture conversations in 1:1s, more time spent asking questions like 'have you thought about what happens when traffic spikes 5x' rather than writing the code that handles the spike. that's hard to feel good about when your engineer brain is wired for output measurement. i'm trying to hold the new shape. the test for whether i'm doing it right is whether amir and lupita are getting better at architecture, and whether the services they own are aging well. those are 6-12 month signals not weekly ones. i need to be patient.

one more 1:1 note — at last week's lupita session she asked me a question i wasn't ready for: 'sebastián, ¿estás disfrutando el rol nuevo, o solo estás haciendo el trabajo?' — are you enjoying the new role or just doing the work. i said i was enjoying it but i could feel myself defaulting to the polite answer. so i caught myself, paused, and said 'lupita, déjame pensar y te contesto bien.' and the next week i came back with: 'i'm enjoying the parts where i'm helping others get unstuck. i'm not enjoying the parts where i feel like i'm performing the role rather than living it. i don't know yet which parts are which but i'm watching.' she said that was the most useful answer she'd ever heard from someone in their first month at a new level. i don't think she was flattering me. i think she was telling me that asking the question more honestly is itself the staff move.

okay. cornerman pivot. real one. let me explain.

so this past week i hit a wall with movenet that i couldn't engineer my way out of in a clean way. i'll write it down because future-me needs to remember why we pivoted.

the failure mode is rotation accuracy. specifically: shoulder rotation timing relative to hand extension on power crosses. movenet's 17-keypoint output is good for position but loses fidelity when the body is rotating and the angles between keypoints become small (shoulders rolling forward toward the camera mean the shoulder-to-shoulder distance compresses in 2d projection, and movenet doesn't recover the 3d rotation properly). i tested this on a sequence of 30 power crosses i recorded, frame-by-frame, with a stopwatch, and the timing of 'rear shoulder reaching peak rotation' that movenet detected was off by 50-150ms compared to ground truth (which i annotated by hand using the visible bicep ridge as a proxy). that's enough error to make my arm-punch detection rule essentially noise.

i looked at three options. first, train a domain-specific model on hand-labeled data. that's a 6-month project i'm not doing on a side budget. second, use a multi-camera setup. doable but defeats the 'works with one phone' constraint that's the whole point. third, swap to a different pose model that handles 3d better.

so i tried mediapipe pose. and let me tell you — it actually does the right thing here. mediapipe pose 33-keypoint output includes derived 3d coordinates (z-axis estimated from the model rather than just 2d image-plane), and the rotation estimation through the body is way more stable on rotating subjects. ran the same 30-cross sequence through mediapipe. timing accuracy was within 30-50ms of ground truth, comparable to coach-grade observation. that's the difference between 'i can detect arm punches reliably' and 'i can't'.

so i'm pivoting from tensorflow movenet to mediapipe. it's a real bummer because i'd told you i preferred movenet for license clarity and python install pain and familiarity. it turns out that for the detection problems i actually care about (rotation timing, hip-shoulder coordination, weight transfer), mediapipe's 3d-aware output is straight-up better. the install issues i'd remembered from a year ago are mostly resolved on python 3.11 — cami helped me through a pip session this evening (she does not get the why but she likes solving fiddly install problems, it's like crossword puzzle for her). got mediapipe pose running in a fastapi handler in about 2 hours. ported the rules layer to consume mediapipe's 33-keypoint format instead of movenet's 17. mostly mechanical translation — landmark name mapping is the only real work.

the move costs me about 2 days of porting, plus i need to retest all the existing rules to make sure they still work as cleanly. but the unblocked progress is much bigger than the cost. and the lesson — 'pick the tool that solves your hard problem, not the tool you're comfortable with' — is one i should have learned faster. i was treating the choice between movenet and mediapipe as a tribal preference when it should have been a head-to-head benchmark on the specific signal i need.

side note: this pivot also makes me think about how i made the original decision. i picked movenet partly on speed (movenet lightning is 7mb, mediapipe pose is heavier), partly on familiarity, partly on community signal. but i never benchmarked the two on the actual question that mattered to my domain. i benchmarked on coco accuracy which is bench-press for pose models, not on the kickboxing-specific signal i care about. that's a methodology miss. logged for the next time i pick a model: define the rubric in domain terms first, benchmark against the rubric, not against generic.

what's good about the pivot: most of the architecture (rules layer, sessions table, ui flow) is unchanged. i was right to invest in the rule abstraction early because it absorbed this pivot cleanly. the rules don't care which model produced the keypoints — they consume keypoint vectors regardless.

what's bad about the pivot: my mental investment in 'we'll use tensorflow' is gone. i'd been thinking about tflite deployment to phones. mediapipe has its own deployment path through mediapipe solutions (they have a js runtime, an android sdk, an ios sdk) which is actually arguably nicer for the on-device path but it's a different mental model and i need to rebuild that part of the plan.

questions:
1. for the on-device path eventually — mediapipe solutions has a js runtime that runs in the browser. is that a viable mvp deployment (just a webpage where you upload a video, mediapipe runs in the browser via wasm, no backend) instead of a fastapi service? that would simplify hosting drastically. but the rules engine would need to also run in js or in wasm, which is a port. for the python prototype i'm doing the server path; for the public mvp the browser path might be cleaner.
2. the 33 vs 17 keypoint difference — does any of my existing logic break? specifically the chin-out rule used the spine-vector calculation between shoulder midpoint and hip midpoint, and mediapipe has more granular hip and shoulder keypoints. is there a 'right' way to use them or am i overthinking and should just take the same midpoints?
3. do i tell diego that we pivoted models? he's a programmer, he'd appreciate the engineering story, but he'd also remember 'i had to redownload the app because seb pivoted' if i ever package this for installation. i think yes, tell him, with the framing 'i learned something and pivoted to do better detection on power crosses, you'll see more accurate flags now'. honest.

cami asked tonight 'is your robot coach project doing well' and i said yeah, just had to switch the underlying model. she said 'oh you do that with everything' which made me laugh because she's right. when i find a better tool, i pivot, no sentimentality. that pattern shows up at work too. my dad calls it 'soltar lo viejo cuando lo nuevo es mejor' — let go of the old when the new is better. always thought that was a virtue. now i'm realizing it's also a thing that can be done badly if you pivot because of restlessness rather than evidence. this pivot was evidence. i tested 30 crosses against ground truth. that's not restlessness.

reflecting more — there's a pattern in my engineering life of 'pick a tool because of community signal or familiarity, hit a wall on the actual problem, pivot to what works, regret not benchmarking earlier.' i did this with rust at chalie in 2021 (picked it for hype, ended up hitting interop walls with the python ml stack we needed and had to bridge with pyo3 awkwardly), did this with kafka at brújula in 2022 (picked it for community, didn't actually need the persistence semantics, sqs would've been simpler), and now i did it again with movenet here. the meta-lesson is: build a small benchmarking step into every tool selection. for my future self, the rule is — never pick a tool without writing down 'here are the 3 specific signals i need it to handle, here's the test that would distinguish good from bad.' that takes 30 minutes upfront and saves 2-week pivots later. logged for the future-seb file.

i've been thinking about why this pattern keeps recurring. i think it's that at each tool-selection moment, i have a familiar cohort of options i'm already biased toward (postgres, python, rust, tensorflow) and an unfamiliar cohort (duckdb, go, mediapipe, polars) that requires me to do learning work to evaluate. the rational move is to evaluate both cohorts on the same domain-specific rubric. but the easy move is to pick from the familiar cohort because i know how to reason about them. so i pick familiar, hit the domain wall, and then get pushed into the unfamiliar cohort by necessity. the cost of this pattern is two weeks of learning under pressure rather than two days of learning during selection. across a career that's a lot of weeks. and the way to break the pattern is to budget 30-60 minutes specifically for 'evaluate the unfamiliar option on the same rubric as the familiar one' before any tool selection that has more than two weeks of downstream commitment. that's the rule. write it down on the desk note rotation alongside raul's 'feel like you know less every quarter.'

going to bed. tomorrow morning i'm porting the trends layer. and reflecting that the project keeps surprising me — every week i learn something concrete about my own form, about diego's form, about pose estimation, about how to scope a side project. that's the difference between a thing you build because you're bored and a thing you build because you actually care about the problem. cornerman is in the second category. so was rust at chalie even though it didn't pan out. so will the next thing. the test for whether to keep going is whether i learn something every week. so far yes. eight weeks budget means i have five weeks left to ship a clean v0. realistic? yes if i don't add scope. that's the discipline. and if i finish early, i ship and rest, i don't add features. shipping early would be unusual for me but i want to model that for myself. cami would notice. she always notices when i finish a thing instead of just adding to it. that approval matters.

one more thing on the pivot specifically. i'm sitting with the discomfort that i wasted ~3 weeks on movenet-specific code that i'm now throwing away. tempting to feel like time wasted. but actually most of what got built — the rules abstraction, the session/event schema, the camera-tilt correction, the visualizer, the smoothing logic, the tests against ground-truth clips — all transferred to mediapipe with zero changes. the only thing thrown away is the keypoint-name mapping and a thin adapter layer. so the 'wasted time' is probably 4 days of coding. the value of the rules abstraction and the test discipline is what made the pivot survivable. that's the architecture lesson i want to carry forward — invest in the thing that's downstream of any tool choice, treat the tool itself as swappable. for routeopt the analog would be: invest in the cost-matrix abstraction, the request/response shape, the test fixtures; treat django-vs-rust as the swappable layer. that's actually how i should have framed the harden-vs-rewrite analysis from the start. the rfc is mostly fine but the framing could have been sharper if i'd used the cornerman pivot as the lived analogy. i'll mention it in the next 1:1 with daniela because it's the kind of meta-lesson worth surfacing. she'll appreciate it.

going to bed for real now. i can hear cami making tea downstairs. ferro just yelled at the wall again.

separately on the rules layer — i want to write down the keypoint-name mapping i did during the port, because future-me will absolutely forget. movenet's 17 keypoints are: nose, left_eye, right_eye, left_ear, right_ear, left_shoulder, right_shoulder, left_elbow, right_elbow, left_wrist, right_wrist, left_hip, right_hip, left_knee, right_knee, left_ankle, right_ankle. mediapipe's 33 keypoints add: mouth_left, mouth_right (skip), eye_inner/outer pairs (skip, use the same 'eye' generic), pinky_left/right, index_left/right, thumb_left/right (could use index for wrist position refinement but for now just use wrist), heel_left/right and foot_index_left/right (better than ankle alone for footwork), and the chest/sternum derivation from shoulder midpoints which i was already computing manually. so the port is mostly: replace movenet[5..16] with mediapipe[11..22] for upper body, and use mediapipe[27..32] for the foot rules where i was using ankle in movenet. wrote a small adapter function that maps movenet-style names onto mediapipe indices so the rules code reads identically. that adapter is the only thing future-me has to maintain when (if) mediapipe deprecates a keypoint or i swap to another model. clean separation of concerns, exactly what the rules abstraction was supposed to give me.

quick log — cornerman ported to mediapipe successfully. all four rules retested on three videos. detections holding up. arm-punch detection now functional. moving on. one minor regression: foot-crossing detection is slightly less precise on mediapipe because the foot landmarks (heel, foot index) carry more noise than movenet's ankle keypoints. acceptable trade-off for the rotation accuracy gain. logged. running 1.6x slower per frame on cpu vs movenet but still real-time on m1 hardware so fine for now. shipping the chin-out improvement notes to diego tonight.

cornerman with diego — second user testing session. recorded another 3-minute round. ran through the new mediapipe pipeline. detection results: 16 chin-out (down from 22 with the camera-angle correction, holding now in the new model), 14 guard-drop, 5 foot-crossing, and 9 newly-detected arm-punches. that's the new rule firing, and it's catching what i expected — diego's third punch in his combos is consistently arm-y, exactly what we'd expect to see when fatigue starts setting in.

his reaction this round: 'cabrón ahora ves cuando estoy lanzando fuerza vs cuando solo estoy moviendo el brazo.' yes, that's exactly what the new rule does. and his question was good: 'puedes diferenciar entre un cross verdadero y un jab fuerte?' — can you tell a true cross from a hard jab? that's a great question because the rule fires on rear-hand punches but it could be confused if someone throws a 1-1 and the second 1 comes harder than usual.

told him: rear-hand-only is the current heuristic, and the spine-rotation signature is the second filter. but he's right that there's edge cases. need to think about it.

i'm starting to think this side project is going to need a bigger user base eventually to find these edge cases organically. four guys at lobo gym is a start. martín agreed yesterday to let me show it to two other regulars next weekend if they're interested. one is a woman named ana who's been training at lobo for six months and who diego thinks would be a strong tester because she's deliberate and detail-oriented, exactly the personality that gives good feedback. the other is sergio who's older (50ish), more experienced (started boxing in his 20s), and has the 'old school' counter-perspective on form that would either validate the rules or surface where they're wrong. between them and diego, i'd have a small mixed cohort — one beginner (ana), two intermediate (diego, me), one experienced (sergio). that's enough variety to start finding the rule-edge cases that don't show up in just-me-and-diego data.

i also want to think about how i present cornerman to ana and sergio. they're not programmers. the technical narrative isn't useful to them. the right pitch is probably 'i built a thing that watches video of you training and tells you when you drop your guard or commit your face. want to try it?' — that's the entire pitch. anything more is over-explaining. i'll bring my mac mini to martín's saturday session and run it on a quick clip of each of them with martín there. that's both a test and a low-pressure introduction.

okay. dad called tonight. let me get this down.

he had another chest pain episode this morning. real this time, not the 'just heartburn' version he likes to do. mom said it lasted about 8 minutes, he was sweating, his left arm felt heavy. she made him sit down, gave him the nitroglycerin tablet from the bottle his cardiologist gave him after the angina diagnosis two years ago, and within 4 minutes the pain receded. she called the cardiologist's office and got him in this afternoon. cardiologist did an ekg in clinic, said the readout was 'consistent with stable angina, not an mi event', wants him to come in next monday for a stress test and possibly a coronary angiography depending on what the stress test shows.

dad's framing of the call to me was 'no es nada, m'hijo, tu mamá se preocupa por todo' — your mom worries about everything, this is nothing. but he agreed to the stress test which means it's not nothing. dad doesn't agree to stress tests for nothing.

so. we're now in the situation where my dad's heart is actively misbehaving and the cardiologist is doing the workup. i'm going to drive down to oaxaca this weekend. tomorrow i'll book the flight — actually i'll drive, it's 6 hours and i need the time alone in the car to think — and stay through monday. i need to be in the room for the stress test. dad will say not to come. mom will be relieved.

what i need from you tonight is help thinking through:

1. what should i be reading about angina before monday? i remember the basics from when this happened two years ago — coronary artery disease, plaque, sometimes vasospasm, treatment ranges from medical management to angioplasty to bypass depending on severity. but i want to be smarter about what the stress test could show, what coronary angiography would tell us, and what the decision tree looks like after each result. i don't want to be the panicking son in the cardiologist's office. i want to be the son who can hear 'we recommend a stent in the lad' and ask 'why a drug-eluting vs bare-metal' and be useful.

2. what's the rfc-strategy equivalent for medical decisions? like — i'm good at decomposing technical decisions into 'options, costs, decision points'. is there a corresponding framework for 'we're sitting in a doctor's office, here are three treatment paths, pick one'? i want to bring some of my engineering brain to it without being annoying about it.

3. how do i tell cami without making her grief situation worse? she's 7 weeks past her mom's death. her mom died of pancreatic cancer, fast and aggressive. my dad's heart issue is chronic, manageable, treatable. these are very different categories of illness. but cami's wiring will hear 'parent health crisis' as 'familiar pain'. i can't avoid telling her — she's my partner — but i need to frame it carefully. and i need to tell her tonight before i drive down because she'll be hurt if she finds out from a text from oaxaca. so it has to happen in the next 90 minutes.

4. work — what do i tell daniela? i'm in the middle of the q2 routeopt rollout work. NOT EXISTS rewrite isn't done. autovacuum tuning is partial. amir's compatibility cleanup is about 60% in. me being out for 4-5 days isn't a crisis but it's not nothing. i lean — be direct with daniela, say 'family medical situation, i'm in oaxaca thursday-monday, will be on slack for emergencies but otherwise off, amir will lead routeopt during this'. she's reasonable. she'll back me up.

5. cornerman — going to be off for the trip. i was supposed to do another diego session this saturday. i'll text him to reschedule. is there value in pushing through the cornerman work in the hotel evening hours in oaxaca, or is the right move to let it sit for a week. lean: let it sit. i'll be present for dad and won't have the headspace anyway.

let me also just say what i'm feeling — not for advice, just for the record. i'm scared. i love my dad. he's not old (he's 64) but he's been my person for 33 years and the angina thing in 2024 made me realize for the first time that this is something that ends. cami's loss is in the foreground of our life. now there's a possible loss creeping up in the background of mine. i don't know how to hold both. i'm not asking you to fix that. i'm just noting it for future seb so he remembers what tonight felt like.

i'm going to call cami down to the kitchen and tell her now. she's reading on the couch. ferro is on the rug. it's 9:47pm. wish me clarity.

before i go down to her — let me think about how i frame it. options i'm considering for the opening line. (a) 'cami, dad had a chest pain episode this morning' — direct, factual, no buildup, gives her the news fast. (b) 'cami, i need to talk to you about my dad' — heavier preamble, sounds ominous, might trigger her grief brain. (c) 'cami, dad's heart did the thing again, the one from 2024, but he's stable.' — references the past episode which she knows about, anchors the new event in a known frame, leads with stability. i think (c) is best because it gives her a known reference point ('the angina from 2024') and immediately includes 'stable' which reduces her threat response. then i can fill in the details. she's smart, she'll ask the right follow-ups.

what i'm going to NOT do is pretend it's smaller than it is, or perform calm i don't feel. my dad has unstable enough symptoms that the cardiologist booked a stress test for monday. that's serious. cami has earned, in this last 9 weeks, the right to my real face about this. so i'll be calm but i won't be dishonestly calm.

last logistical thing — the routeopt q2 work. i'd planned this week to do the NOT EXISTS rewrite. amir's already 60% through the dispatch-core compatibility cleanup. i'm going to send daniela a slack tonight letting her know i'll be out thursday-monday for a family medical thing. amir will lead routeopt during this. lupita can cover any cross-team architecture review questions that come up because she's been shadowing me on those for two weeks anyway. it's actually fine. the staff distribution work this past month means me being out for 5 days isn't catastrophic. that's one thing the promotion materially gave me — slack in my own absence.

cornerman — i'm letting it sit. won't open the laptop in oaxaca for it. diego knows. martín is good. ana and sergio testing pushed to next next weekend. that's fine.

the dad thing — i need to remember this. i need to be useful in the cardiologist's office on monday. but i also need to be useful as a son today, this week, sitting at his kitchen table eating tlayudas mom made and asking him about the cab business and listening to the answers without rushing them. that's the part that doesn't fit in an rfc framework. it's just presence.

what i'm packing for the trip: laptop with charger, three days of clothes, the dress shirt for the cardiologist appointment because dad will care that i look presentable in front of his doctor (it matters to him in ways i don't fully understand but i'm not going to fight it), my notes folder with the printed history of his cardiac stuff from 2024 in case i need to reference dates, and a small bag of un café tinto beans for mom because she's been wanting to try the colombian washed i've been talking about. for cami — a small care package with the navy zip-up sweater she's been wearing since the funeral, the book she's been reading slowly, and the moka pot from the kitchen because we'll be at my parents' place and mom doesn't have a moka pot, only a drip filter machine, and cami needs her café de olla in the morning to feel like herself.

route — i'm taking 150d down to puebla, then 135 down through tehuacán to oaxaca city. about 6.5 hours with one rest stop. cami will read or sleep. i'll think.

what i'm scared of, separate from dad's heart: that this trip becomes a turning point in cami's grief in a bad way. her mom died of pancreatic cancer 9 weeks ago. now she's flying to oaxaca for my dad's heart workup. that's a lot of medical-system context for someone in week 9. i've thought about whether i should suggest she stay in cdmx and i go alone. but she's already said she wants to come, and overriding that would be its own kind of disrespect. so we go together. i'll watch for signs that she's overwhelming herself and i'll cut my own work short if i need to be present for her. that's the dual-attention thing. i can do it.

what i'm scared of about dad — that the stress test reveals significant disease and we're talking angioplasty or bypass within months. dad's a guy who's told me eight different times in his life that he wants to die behind the wheel of his cab on a routine job, not in a hospital bed. that's a real fear for him. medical procedures threaten that frame. i need to be ready to hear him talk about that and not redirect to 'but procedure x has 95% success rates' which is what my engineer brain wants to do. just listen. like with cami in the garden. the same lesson, different parent.

going to pack now. i'll log from oaxaca.

one more thing for the record. when dad called tonight, mom was on speaker. she sounded scared but she was being mom — that means she was already planning the kitchen for the days after the procedure, what soup to make, when to call my abuela on dad's side. mom's grief response is logistical. dad's grief response is fatalist. cami's grief response is interior. mine is task-organizational. four different ways the same family feels the same thing. that's something therapists probably have a name for. it's also why we work as a unit — the responses don't overlap, each of us takes a different lane and between us all the lanes are covered. mom does food. dad does fatalism. cami does interior. i do logistics. luis does presence. that's the family's stress operating system and it's actually pretty good. logged.

actually one more practical thing before i pack. the cardiologist questions list. i'm going to write them down now while my brain is sharp because monday morning i won't be. (1) what does the stress test look for specifically and what would 'positive' or 'negative' mean for next steps. (2) if the test is positive, what's the typical decision tree — straight to angiography, or further imaging first. (3) what's the risk profile of doing nothing versus medical management versus stenting. (4) for medical management, what's the regimen (statins, beta blockers, antiplatelet, lifestyle) and what's the realistic compliance rate at dad's age. (5) for stenting, drug-eluting vs bare-metal and why. (6) what's the recovery timeline for a stent — when can he drive a cab again. that last one matters because driving the cab is dad's identity, not just his income. (7) what's the warning signs to watch for after — when do we call you, when do we call 911. (8) the angina diagnosis from 2024 — does this represent progression or does it indicate the original diagnosis was incomplete. (9) any meds currently on the list that interact poorly with the new regimen. dad's been on lisinopril for hypertension for years and i want to make sure that's fine. (10) lifestyle — specifically, can he keep the kickboxing-style stretching cami's mom taught him before her death (yes my dad does kickboxing-stretching, it's a story).

that's the prep list. i'll bring printed copies of dad's 2024 angina workup for the doctor to reference.

update — told cami. she handled it like a pro. she said 'sebastián go, we're going, when do we leave.' she insisted on coming with me to oaxaca. i didn't expect that and i'm not sure if i'm relieved or worried. she said 'if it were my dad you'd come with me.' which is true. driving down thursday morning, both of us. ferro going to her sister marta. daniela informed. cami told foster she'd be remote from oaxaca through monday and they were fine with it. i'm so glad she's coming. honestly. i was bracing to do this alone.

quick log from oaxaca. arrived monday around 4pm. cami napped most of the drive which i was glad about — she needed the rest. mom met us at the door and hugged cami for a long time which made cami cry which made mom cry. dad was watching tv inside, came out, hugged me, said 'no era necesario que vinieras, m'hijo' which is the dad version of 'thank you for coming.' he looks tired but not pale. that was my first read.

stress test is at 9am tomorrow. cardiologist is the same one as 2024 — dr. herrera, in the medical complex on calzada porfirio díaz. dad has been seeing him for two years and trusts him. mom says dr. herrera was direct on the phone today and said 'we'll know more after the test, no need to spiral until then.'

cami is doing okay. she's a little quiet but she's eating, she's sitting with mom in the kitchen helping with dinner, she said hello to dad and asked about the cab. that's all i was hoping for. mom keeps looking at her with that 'i remember being you' expression but she's giving cami space. mom lost her mom 12 years ago and she's been in cami's exact place. she knows the rhythm. that's helpful in a way i couldn't have predicted.

dinner was tlayudas. mom made them from scratch. dad ate carefully because he's been on a low-sodium diet for the past 3 weeks since the chest pain episode and he's missing salt visibly. cami ate a full one and complimented mom's salsa, which was the right move. afterwards we sat in the back patio with horchata and dad told the same story about the time he drove a politician from oaxaca to the airport in a thunderstorm and the politician left him a 2000-peso tip and a folded thank-you note. i've heard the story 40 times and it's still funny.

i'll log after the test tomorrow. also — i set up a small workspace in the back room for the days i need to slack with daniela or check on amir's prs. dad's wifi is fine for that. cami is going to work from the kitchen counter where the light is good. mom keeps offering us food and we keep accepting because saying no to mom's food is futile.

post stress test — dr herrera says 'mild ischemia visible in the lateral wall under stress, recommending a coronary angiography next week to characterize.' that's neither great nor terrible. it's the next-step result, not the result. dad is taking it well. cami held my hand. mom is making chocolate caliente in the kitchen because that's what she does when she needs to feel useful. luis called from puebla. he's coming down for the procedure. dad said 'no es necesario que vengan todos' which is also dad-speak for 'thanks for coming.'

on cornerman during the trip — i said i wouldn't open the laptop and i mostly didn't. but tuesday afternoon while dad was napping i did 90 minutes of catching up on slack and i looked at diego's text from sunday. he'd recorded another solo session and uploaded it (i'd given him a script that ssh-uploads to my mac mini) and ran it through the pipeline himself. his message: 'cabrón, mi tasa de chin-out bajó a 9 por 3 minutos, semana pasada eran 16, esta tecnología funciona.'

so the trends layer is working without me even being there. and diego is showing measurable form improvement after a week of using cornerman. that's a real signal. one user, one week, but a real signal. and the thing i didn't expect — diego sent the screenshot to martín. martín then texted me — yes, martín himself, who refuses to use most apps — and said 'sebastián, está padre lo que estás haciendo, quiero verlo cuando regreses.' that's the coach asking to see the tool. that's a tier of validation i hadn't planned for. when the head coach wants to use the tool you built, you've made something real.

i'm logging it. and i'm going to text him 'orgulloso, cabrón' and leave it there until i'm back. plus a line to martín: 'sí coach, te lo enseño cuando vuelva. saludos a la familia.'

also — i'm noting the timing. i was 5 weeks into the cornerman project when i got the user-improvement signal. one week of dedicated use produced a measurable form change. that's the loop closing — build → use → improve → measure → confirm the build. i didn't expect it this fast. and the fact that diego ran the upload and the analysis on his own without me being available means the tool is no longer dependent on my presence to be useful. that's a maturity threshold. it transitions from 'a thing seb made for himself' to 'a thing that exists in the world'.

logged: cornerman crossed the 'works without me' threshold during my dad's stress test. that's the kind of inflection point i want to remember the date of. small moments of stuff working that you wouldn't think to celebrate. but exactly the kind of thing future-seb will want to know about. project autonomy is a milestone you don't get a notification for.

dad and i went for a walk around the neighborhood thursday morning before the angiography appointment. he wanted to show me the new tile work he did on the front patio. then we sat in the back garden and he talked about the cab business and the regular passengers he's had for 20 years. he said something i'm going to remember — 'm'hijo, los carros me dan dinero pero la gente que monta me da vida.' the cars give me money, the people who ride give me life.

i asked him if he's scared. he said yes but he also said he's lived a fuller life than he expected and if it ends sooner than he wanted, the math is still positive. that wasn't a thing i was ready to hear and it took me a few minutes to process. i didn't try to argue him into more optimism. i just sat with him.

related: i went for a 30-minute jog around the neighborhood after he and mom went to bed. shorter loop than i'd do at home but the elevation in oaxaca is harder than interlomas. cleared my head. mostly thought about how dad's frame on his own life is healthier than mine on a bad day. i should learn from that.

cornerman is dormant. cami is sleeping next to me. we go to the cardiologist again tomorrow.

cami helped mom in the kitchen tonight making mole negro from a 30-year-old recipe. she said making something that takes 4 hours and many ingredients was meditative. mom watched her do the chocolate-tempering step which is the hardest part and didn't correct her once. that was mom's gift to her.

dad and i also talked about money briefly. he asked about the promo and i told him a more accurate number than i'd told him on the phone. he didn't react in the way i'd feared. he said 'm'hijo, está bien que ganes bien, eso significa que mamá y yo no tenemos que preocuparnos cuando llegue el momento.' it means he doesn't have to worry. i wanted to say 'you should never have had to worry' but that's not the conversation he was having. he was telling me that knowing his son can take care of him gives him peace. so i let him have that, and i told him i love him. he said 'yo también te quiero, cabrón.' which is dad-vocabulary for the same thing.

and one logistical note — i'm going to talk to mom tomorrow about whether dad needs anything in the house i can buy for them this weekend. ramps near the front step (he doesn't need them now but in case the procedure recovery is harder than expected), a softer chair for the patio, a better bedside light so he doesn't strain getting up at night. small things. dad won't ask but mom will appreciate the asking.

angiography results — dad has 70% blockage in the left anterior descending artery. dr herrera is recommending a stent placement, scheduled for two weeks from today. drug-eluting stent, single placement, outpatient procedure. recovery time about a week. dr herrera's expected outcome is 'returns to normal cab-driving activity within two weeks of the procedure.'

dad accepted on the spot. i was actually surprised by how quickly he accepted. mom squeezed his hand. cami squeezed mine. i asked dr herrera the questions i'd prepared (drug-eluting vs bare-metal — DES is the standard of care, restenosis rate <5%, requires 6-12 months of dual antiplatelet therapy; what physical activity is permissible after — walking immediately, jogging at 4 weeks, no heavy lifting for 6 weeks; what's the warning signs that we should call him about — chest pain not relieved by nitroglycerin in 5 minutes, shortness of breath at rest, irregular heartbeat). he answered each one thoroughly. i felt useful in a way i was hoping i would feel.

separately — i'd been thinking about whether the promo bump means we have buffer for any catastrophic medical scenario. answer: yes, we do. mexican private healthcare for a stent is around 200-280k pesos cash, and dad has insurance through his cab cooperative that covers most of it, so out-of-pocket exposure is maybe 50k. our emergency fund absorbs that without breaking. good. that's the real value of the buffer i've been building.

driving back to cdmx tomorrow. cami doing better than expected.

i had to ask dr herrera for the discharge instructions in writing because dad's a 'i'll remember' kind of guy who absolutely won't remember. herrera printed them. dad rolled his eyes but didn't argue. i taped them to the fridge before we left and told mom to leave them up.

cami told me on the drive home that she felt useful in a way she hadn't felt in months. i said 'you held my hand at the cardiologist's office, that's not nothing.' she said 'no, it's actually big.' grief made her feel like a passenger in our shared life for a while. helping me through a different version of medical fear gave her a chair at the table. i hadn't thought about it that way. it's an unexpected silver in a hard week.

also — luis (my hermano) is driving down from puebla for the procedure on his own initiative. he texted me 'voy a estar ahí, no te preocupes seb.' that's the brother thing happening as i hoped. i'm going to tell him when i call later that i'm grateful. luis and i don't talk often but the moments that matter, he shows up. logged.

back in cdmx. monday morning. caught up on slack from oaxaca week, did the routeopt sync with amir, looked at the rfc comments. nothing exploded. amir's NOT EXISTS rewrite is at 80%. autovacuum tuning has been live for a week and the p99 is now at 165ms, down from 198ms. that's the next 30ms i was hoping for. the harden plan is working as designed.

cornerman — back into the rotation tonight. diego's solo session data is good. i'll integrate it into the trends layer.

mara x — i'd been worried that being away for 5 days would mess up the dial. came back, pulled the same recipe (18g/36g/28s on the colombian washed), came out exactly as i'd left it. that's the temperature stability the breville never gave me. quantitative win. i'm calling the espresso project a success and starting to tinker with the chiapas natural beans.

the staff role rhythm — i'm starting to find it. mornings are 'maker time' for routeopt and rfc work. afternoons are amir/lupita 1:1s, architecture review of cross-team prs, daniela sync. evenings are home with cami, dinner, sometimes 1-2 hours of cornerman, in bed by 11. that's a sustainable shape and i think i can hold it.

one thing i'm noticing — the staff role costs me about 15% of my old 'deep work' time on routeopt itself. it's been a real adjustment. i used to spend 4-5 hours a day actually writing code. now i spend 2-3, with the rest going to documents, reviews, conversations. raul's line about 'staff is the most useful person about other people's services' is starting to make sense as i live it. the lupita biweekly is the highest-leverage hour i have most weeks because she's the lead on the geofencing service which is the riskiest q2 deliverable. me being available to her cuts her decision latency and that compounds across the team.

isa, the new grad i mentored, is going to be on her first calibration cycle this round. i wrote her a peer feedback note for it — partly returning the favor she did me last cycle but mostly because she earned it. she's grown a lot.

raul also asked me to draft a 'platform engineering operating principles' doc for the q3 plan. that's me being asked to articulate what we believe about engineering at the architectural level. unprecedented for a 4-week-old staff. i'm honored and a little intimidated. drafting starts this weekend.

small log — pulled the chiapas anaerobic natural this morning. 18g/40g/32s. the funky tropical thing came through clean. cami refused to taste it because she 'doesn't trust naturals' which made me laugh. ferro tried to drink from my cup. mara x continues to deliver. dose-yield-time tracking notebook has 47 entries. variance dropped substantially from week 1. learning curve real and tractable. i told dad on the phone about the morning shot and he said 'me parece que estás haciendo café como si fuera trabajo.' as if it were work. yes, dad. exactly.

water plan landed too — i went with bonafont jugs for now. one 20l garrafón lasts me about 6 weeks of espresso. minimal scale risk. brita-style filter on tap is the secondary fallback. i'll revisit ro filter under-sink in a year if i'm still pulling daily.

i also did the first descale today. 6 weeks in, the e61 group head shows trace mineral on the dispersion screen, not bad given cdmx water. ran a cycle of cafiza through the back-flush procedure, then a citric acid descale through the hx, then 3 cycles of pure water flush. machine sounds slightly quieter on pump cycle which probably means the descale loosened a small mineral buildup in the pump path. quantitative confirmation of the maintenance rhythm being right. logged for the next cycle in 6 weeks. and the espresso corner of the kitchen, with cups visible on the open shelf and the moka pot retired to the cabinet, has become the place i start the day from. cami started joining me for the morning shot routine on weekends. she likes watching the pre-infusion bloom, the way the puck swells before the extraction starts. small ritual.

i'm tracking a small puck-prep improvement project — adding a wdt (weiss distribution technique) tool to the workflow. amazon mexico has a brushed-needle wdt for 250 pesos that does the job. should arrive next week. the bottomless portafilter showed me i'm getting a slight uneven extraction on the right side of the puck which is classic distribution-problem signature. the wdt should fix it. small iterative improvement. that's the espresso project mode — small, measurable, never finished, always satisfying. cami once said the espresso corner is the place i'm most calm in the whole apartment. she's right. it's a place where the loop is short and the feedback is honest.

okay — long week and i want to write it all down because there's stuff to remember.

dad's stent procedure happened wednesday. successful. dr herrera reported 'textbook placement, no complications, patient stable.' dad was discharged thursday morning with the dual antiplatelet therapy script (clopidogrel + low-dose aspirin for 12 months) and a follow-up echo in 6 weeks. he's home with mom, walking 20 minutes twice a day, eating mom's bone broth and lots of vegetables, and he sounded good on the phone tonight. mom said he insisted on going up to the roof to look at his cab — to make sure it was 'okay' — which is exactly the kind of thing dad does. 'are you okay' 'tu papá necesitaba ver que el carro estaba sano antes de creer que él estaba sano.' your dad needed to see the car was healthy before he could believe he was. i love that man.

i'd been planning to drive down again for the procedure but dad called me sunday and said 'm'hijo, este es un procedimiento de un día, no necesito que dejes el trabajo otra vez. mamá está aquí, marta de la esquina viene a comer con nosotros, tu hermano viene desde puebla.' which is true — my hermano luis, who lives in puebla, drove down for the procedure. he sent me photos from the hospital that morning. dad looked grumpy but okay in his hospital gown. mom looked tired. luis took good care of them. i'm grateful.

routeopt update — the harden plan continues to deliver. p95 is now stable at 138ms, p99 at 162ms. the NOT EXISTS rewrite landed last tuesday and it gave us another 25ms on p99 alone. autovacuum tuning compounds. amir's compatibility cleanup landed wednesday. we are now solidly under our internal slas across all profiles. for the first time since i joined brújula i feel like routeopt is a service i'm not embarrassed to operate.

raul sent a follow-up note in slack thursday. 'sebastián, the harden plan is paying off. nice execution. one thought — the q2 plan has open headcount for one more senior backend ic. you'd own the hire. interested?' so that's the next staff-level move — start hiring. i hadn't expected hiring this soon but it's a healthy sign that the platform engineering scope is real. i told daniela i was interested. we're going to scope the role this week.

cornerman update — diego, ana, and sergio are all using it now. the saturday demo at lobo went better than i expected. ana asked smart questions about the chin-out detection threshold (she has a wider stance than the average orthodox boxer because she's a former muay thai practitioner and the foot-placement tendency carries over, so the rule was firing more on her stance even though it's not the same fault pattern in her style). that's a real edge case and it tells me i need to think about per-user calibration. sergio has been around the sport for 25 years and he watched the chin-out playback on his own clip and said 'okay, lo sospechaba pero no lo sabía.' that's the validation moment from someone who has earned the right to be skeptical.

so cornerman now has 4 active users. i have requests for 3 more (martín himself wants in, and two of his other regulars who heard about it from diego). that's 7 within 4 weeks of the first user moment. i need to think about how this scales.

specifically — running everything on my mac mini was fine for me and diego. for 7 users it becomes an upload latency problem because they're all sending videos through the network at random times and my mini is also my dev machine. i need to deploy somewhere. options:

1. small ec2 with docker, t3.medium, ~$30/month. the boring choice. no gpu, mediapipe runs fine on cpu.
2. fly.io, with mediapipe in a python image, $5-10/month at this scale, good developer experience. but i don't know fly's gpu story which i don't need now but might in 6 months.
3. railway, similar to fly but i've used it more.
4. just keep it on my mini and add a queue (redis) so uploads don't all hit at once. cheap and dumb. probably fine for 7 users.

i lean (4) for now and revisit when we hit 15 users. minimum effort. and if cornerman never grows past 7, i never paid a cent for hosting which is the right outcome.

cami had a hard week mid-week — wednesday and thursday were rough, related to a foster client matter that triggered something her mom used to comment about. she didn't tell me what specifically until friday and i didn't push. she went to the bereavement therapist (marta's recommendation, took 2 weeks to get the appointment but it happened) on thursday and came home different. quieter, but in a good way. like she'd been able to put something down for an hour. she's going weekly for a while. that's the right move. i told her i was proud of her and she rolled her eyes which is cami for 'thank you, that helped.'

questions:
1. on the hire — what should i be looking for in this senior ic role? brújula's bar is high but i'm new to running a hiring loop. i'd want strong systems thinking, postgres-savvy, comfortable with cross-team architecture conversations. what am i forgetting?
2. on cornerman scaling — am i right that mac mini + redis queue gets me to 15 users? or is there something i'm missing? bandwidth-wise our internet is 200 mbps fiber which should handle the upload volumes fine. compute is the question — mediapipe takes about 30 seconds for a 3-minute video on the mini's m1 cpu, so 7 users uploading once a week each is ~3.5 minutes of compute per week, totally fine.
3. on dad's recovery — what should i be paying attention to in the first 6 weeks post-stent? i know about the dual antiplatelet therapy and the activity ramp. i don't know about diet, sleep, mood — does post-cardiac-procedure mood matter? am i missing a category?

going to bed. cami's already asleep. ferro's on her side again. i'm going to pull a shot in the morning, do my routeopt amir sync at 9am, and start drafting the role description for the new hire.

a small practical note for the morning amir sync. amir mentioned in passing on slack last week that he's been feeling stretched on the dispatch-core compatibility cleanup. it's the kind of small signal that, if i ignore it, becomes a problem in three weeks. so for tomorrow's sync i'm going to lead with 'amir, i want to make sure you're not eating it alone on the cleanup. what's the realistic deadline if i give you another week and what would it cost the team' rather than 'how's the cleanup'. that reframes the conversation from check-in to support. small but real difference at staff level. write it down.

related — i want to start tracking the 'small signals' i pick up from amir, lupita, and clara as a deliberate practice. one-line entries in the notes app each week. e.g., 'amir feeling stretched on cleanup, ask about deadline pressure', 'lupita asked twice about geofencing migration sequencing, follow up with explicit recommendation', 'clara seems unsure about postgres tuning ownership, pair on a query plan reading session'. that's the staff anticipation muscle. logged.

one more thing on the hire. brújula has a 'company-wide bar' for senior engineers which is implicitly the bar i'm hiring against. but daniela said in passing that for routeopt specifically i should weigh certain skills heavier than others. specifically, postgres-internals literacy (because so much of routeopt's hot path is postgres-tuning), python dynamic-typing comfort (because we don't have type hints everywhere and we won't anytime soon), and ability to read other people's slow services and not panic (because routeopt was a hot mess for years and the new hire needs to be okay walking into it). the third one is hard to interview for. maybe a take-home where they're given a slow service to diagnose with limited context? but take-homes are 50/50 — top candidates won't do them. hmm. i'll think on it more this week. probably a paired diagnosis exercise live in the loop is better than a take-home. sit with the candidate, give them production-ish slow query traces, ask them to diagnose. that's harder to fake and it tests the real skill.

also — i want to be intentional about who's on the loop. four panelists ideally. me, daniela (hiring manager), one peer (probably amir), one cross-functional (probably renata or carla — sre lead is a strong cross-functional choice for a backend role). i'll talk to amir tomorrow about whether he wants to be on the panel. he probably will. he likes interviewing.

one final thing — i'm processing how the past 90 days look in retrospect. funeral month, partial-return phase, promo week, lelit week, cornerman started, side project mvp shipped, dad's chest pain, oaxaca trip, stent procedure, return to cdmx. that's a lot. and somehow at the end of it all, cami is doing better, dad is recovering, work is at the strongest steady-state i've ever managed at brújula, and cornerman has 4 users with measurable form improvement. i don't want to over-credit myself for outcomes that depended on luck (dad's stent could have been a worse blockage; cami's grief recovery could have stalled), but i do want to credit myself for showing up across all these vectors without dropping any of them. i'm going to write a longer 'past seb to future seb' note this weekend covering the whole arc. the 90-day note. so in 12 months when i'm looking back from a different vantage point, i can read the tone of how i was metabolizing it in the moment. that's the discipline of writing things down. logged-state beats remembered-state. always.

separately — clara, the new hire from monterrey, accepted the offer this week. she starts in two weeks. she'll be on routeopt with me. her interview pipeline was clean, all four panelists rated her strong-hire, the technical loop she did was a refactoring exercise on a python service with sqlalchemy queries that need optimizing and she absolutely nailed the n+1 detection without prompting. she's going to be great. i'm hiring my first ic ever. and from the people-ops side, that's a weight on my plate i hadn't fully metabolized — i'm now responsible for someone else's onboarding success at brújula. that's a different kind of staff work than rfc-writing. i need to think about what good onboarding looks like.
