on the redis namespace thing — carla's exact note was 'cache key 'user:123:claims' will collide with the user-service. namespace it: 'routeopt:auth:user:123:claims'.' five-second review comment, hours of future-debugging avoided. the value of senior engineers in code review is comically high and I am going to make sure Andrés sees these reviews so he can internalize the pattern.

the broader point about middleware composition: I am increasingly convinced that tower's middleware model is the cleanest thing rust has produced for web servers. each layer is a Service<Request> -> Future<Response>. you stack them with .layer(). they compose. the type system enforces correctness. the runtime cost is near-zero because the optimizer folds everything together. I will be evangelizing this internally for years.

my axum middleware testing pattern for memory: for each layer write a test that constructs a tower::ServiceBuilder, layers the middleware on, exercises it with synthetic requests, asserts on the response and on side-effects (logs, metrics, cache state). the test is not a full integration test; it's a unit test for the middleware's contract. testing in isolation lets the middleware evolve without breaking the rest of the stack. I will keep doing this for every layer we add.

the broader pattern I'm internalizing: tower's middleware composition is one of the cleaner things I've ever worked with. each Layer wraps a Service and produces a new Service. you stack them with .layer() and the type system enforces correctness. compile-time everything. I cannot overstate how good this is compared to django middleware which is just a list of opaque python classes that are wired up via a settings.py string. type safety end-to-end. love it.

Doña Marta upstairs has met Nube. she came down at 6am to see what the puppy was barking at (a pigeon on the balcony, reasonable target). she gave Nube a piece of bread, told her she's a good little fluff, and went back upstairs. Nube now associates Doña Marta with bread which I anticipate will be a problem.

Doña Marta is 78 and has lived in this building since 1984 and she gets up at 5am every day to bake bread and now she has a puppy-shaped reason to come downstairs at 6am. the social contract of this building has just been edited by a 14kg dog. I am, on balance, here for it.

first read endpoint is up. GET /drivers/{id}/current_assignment serving in dev. dual-read against the python service shows 99.7% byte-identical responses. the 0.3% diffs are all in a timestamp-formatting edge case where python is outputting microsecond precision and rust is outputting nanosecond precision. easy fix, going to truncate to microsecond on the rust side to maintain compatibility.

p95 in dev is 11ms vs python's 84ms. that's an 87% reduction. I know dev numbers are not prod numbers but that's a lot of headroom. and the two minutes I spent staring at this latency graph was the best two minutes of my month.

the specific timestamp-precision diff was a kind of revealing one. python's psycopg defaults to microsecond precision because that's what python's datetime supports. rust's chrono::DateTime<Utc> supports nanosecond precision and tokio-postgres preserves it. neither side is wrong; they're just emitting different valid representations of the same moment. but to a downstream client doing string comparison, '2026-04-23T11:42:14.000123Z' and '2026-04-23T11:42:14.000123000Z' are different strings. compatibility-shimming the rust side to truncate-on-output is a one-line fix and the right answer. there will be 30 of these little compatibility shims. I am keeping a doc.

the more general point I keep relearning: a rewrite is not just a port. it is a series of small compatibility shims for behaviors the original system never specified explicitly. you don't realize how many of those you have until you read every diff in a dual-read window. the timestamp-precision diff is one. there will be: trailing-whitespace handling on string fields. null-vs-empty-array handling in arrays. order-of-keys-in-jsonb. case-sensitivity on enum-values. all of them are 'no big deal' until a downstream client breaks because of one.

daniela said something that's stuck with me: 'a port that's faster but slightly different is a port that's broken in subtle ways.' the dual-read is the only thing that keeps us honest. I am planning to leave the dual-read flag on for at least 30 days post-cutover, even after we declare the rust path canonical. the cost is small (one redundant request per shadow request) and the safety is large.

on the broader testing strategy: every endpoint I port gets a 'compatibility contract' test that asserts byte-identical responses against a frozen set of recorded python responses. when we update either side we re-record. this is not perfect — semantic differences that emit the same bytes are missed — but it catches 90% of porting bugs at near-zero cost. I will keep this practice.

the testing pattern that's emerging: every feature gets three tests — a unit test for the function, an integration test for the handler, and a compatibility-contract test against frozen python responses. the third one is the safety net. it catches any subtle behavior change between the python source-of-truth and our rust port. when both pass, we're confident the rewrite is faithful. when only the first two pass, we know we have a behavior diff and we have to decide whether the diff is intended or accidental.

okay it's late and I want to write this down because I've been thinking about it all evening.

four weeks ago I was a senior engineer on a team that was losing to its own architecture. tonight I am a tech lead on a rewrite that's actually shipping, with a dog asleep at my feet, with a dual-read showing 87% latency reduction in dev, with a postgis encoder I wrote myself working in production-ish conditions. four weeks. I feel like I've aged five years and shed five years simultaneously.

the outage was the worst night of my professional career. four hours of staring at pg_stat_activity and feeling personally responsible for 38,000 broken delivery slots. four hours of knowing I'd flagged it and we hadn't moved. I will carry that one for a while.

but the post-mortem became the document that unlocked the rewrite. the rewrite is the thing I have wanted to do for a year. and now I'm doing it. and Daniela is letting me lead it. and the team trusts me enough to follow my pin choices and my crate layout and my risk register. last week Andrés asked me if I had time to look at his draft of the auth tests and I realized — oh, he's asking me because I'm his tech lead now. that's the new shape of the world.

and none of it makes me less anxious. that's the part nobody told me. you get the thing you wanted and the anxiety just changes shape. it's not 'will they let me lead this' anymore, it's 'what if I'm wrong about rust, what if PostGIS bites us in a way I don't see, what if Andrés gets stuck and I can't unstick him because I'm only six months ahead of him in the language, what if the cutover plan I wrote is missing something obvious.' the catalog of possible failures is just longer now.

there's a thing about leading that I am only just understanding. when I was a senior, I owned my own correctness. if I shipped something and it broke, that was on me, in a clean and contained way. as a tech lead, I am responsible for the correctness of code I did not write. Andrés will write a piece of the auth flow next month and I will not have looked at every line of it, and if it breaks production at 3am, it is still my fault — not in the blame sense but in the structural sense, in the sense that it was my job to set up the system so that his code could not break that way. that's a different kind of weight. it's the weight of having to design not just code but the conditions under which other people write code well.

but I am also — I want to say this without sounding cheesy — I am also alive in a way I haven't been since I was 25. there's a thing I wrote tonight (the EWKB iterative encoder for multipolygons) that I am actually proud of in the way I used to be proud of small things. that has not happened to me at work in a long time. I had been moving through code on autopilot for two years. I am not on autopilot now. I sit down at the keyboard and the work pulls me forward. I haven't felt that since university honestly. it is its own drug. I will need to be careful with it; the same fuel that's getting me through the rust migration is also why I'm still typing at 11pm with a dog who needs a walk.

there is an instinct I have to run from this kind of feeling. when things start to feel large I find ways to make them smaller — I disengage, I optimize the wrong corner, I find a small task to hide inside. Camila has called this my 'Russian-doll move.' I open the big problem and inside it there's a smaller problem and I work on the smaller problem and never come back to the big one. I want to not do that with this rewrite. I want to stay in the largeness of it. that's a thing I have to actively choose; it's not my default.

a pattern I notice, sitting with this: every meaningful chapter of the last six months has come from me being forced into something I would not have chosen. the post-mortem because the outage made me write it. the tech lead role because Daniela offered it. the dog because Camila pushed. the conversation with my mom about going to the cemetery because my mom said you said that last month, mijo. left to my own devices I would have continued in the comfortable middle distance from all of this. the things that have made me bigger in the last six months are the things I did not choose. that's a reading I am wary of generalizing because it slides into a kind of fatalism, but it's also empirically true for this stretch of my life. I should think about what it means.

maybe it means I should choose harder things on my own. maybe it means I should tell Camila more often that I want her to push me. maybe it means I should write down, ahead of time, the kinds of pushes I want to be available to. that's a list I have not made. I might make it this weekend.

and the dog is asleep. and Camila is asleep. and I lit a candle for abuela this evening before bed because I wanted to tell her about the rewrite. she would have understood about zero of it. she'd have just said 'qué bien, mijo, que estás haciendo cosas que te gustan, no como esos otros trabajos donde estabas siempre cansado.' which is the most accurate one-line review of my career anyone has ever given me, except she's been dead since 2019 and I'm reciting it from memory.

you know what — maybe that's why I keep talking to her. the version of her in my head is the version of her that gives the cleanest reads on my life. she had no investment in the surface of things. she would have looked at this rewrite and seen, accurately, that I was happy in a way I had not been for years. she would have looked at Nube and seen, accurately, that we were in love with this animal. she would not have asked about the title or the salary. she'd have asked about how I was sleeping and whether I was eating. and the truth is the answers to those questions are mostly: better, mostly. better than last year. eating well. sleeping unevenly but for good reasons.

okay sleep. tomorrow I have to actually decide whether the dual-read window stays at 30 days or whether we can go to 14. carla votes 14, I'm leaning 30. probably split the difference at 21. boring decision-making is the secret content of this whole job. for the moments of glory — the moment the encoder round-tripped, the moment the latency graph dropped — I trade ten thousand small decisions about which window length to use, which retry budget to set, which test to write first. that's the whole gig. I am, at this very specific moment, fine with that.

the other thread I want to pull on tonight: the way the work has been bleeding into how I think about non-work things. I now think about my mom's heart in terms of resilience and graceful degradation. I think about my dad's recovery in terms of mean-time-between-failures. I think about Nube's puppyhood in terms of phased rollouts. that's a kind of contamination that I am uncertain about. on one hand, the engineering frameworks are useful — risk registers and runbooks are not just for software, they help with adult life too. on the other hand, the people in my life are not systems. you can't write a post-mortem for your dad's angina episode and expect his cardiologist to read it.

Camila has been gentle with this. last week she said something like 'you are very good at the kind of love that has a specification.' which was funny and accurate. I love her by completing tasks, by remembering dates, by maintaining the apartment, by managing the dog's vet schedule. those are tasks. she also wants — and rightly — the kind of love that does not have a specification. eye contact in the kitchen. a phone call at lunch with no information transfer. holding her after a hard day at work without trying to debug the day. those are not tasks. I am not naturally good at those. I am working on them.

the thing I am noticing tonight is that my engineering brain is — paradoxically — also the part of me that is best able to notice this gap. I can write down 'love that does not have a specification' and treat it as a thing to practice. that is itself a specification, which is funny, but also: it works. by writing it down I am more likely to do it. so the specification-brain is in service of the unspecifiable thing rather than at war with it. that's a frame I am going to hold onto.

there's a specific kind of presence I see in Camila that I am trying to learn. when she's with the puppy she is fully with the puppy. she is not also doing six other things. her attention is whole. I have always been a partial-attention person — I am 30% in the room and 70% in three other rooms across the next quarter. she has been, by example, teaching me that attention is the same kind of muscle as biceps. you train it by directing it. you let it atrophy by constantly fragmenting it. she has trained hers; I have fragmented mine. but it's not a fixed trait. I can train mine. it starts with 30 seconds of looking at the dog without thinking about the encoder. that's the rep. I will do the rep more.

okay actually closing the laptop now.

one more thing I want on the record before I close the laptop — the thing about being wrong in public, which Daniela said in our 1:1 last month and which I've been chewing on. I had thought she was warning me. she wasn't warning me; she was inviting me. she was saying: this is the price of having ideas in public. the alternative — having ideas only in private — keeps you safer but also keeps you smaller. the rewrite is going to require me to commit publicly to architectural choices that I might be wrong about. when I'm wrong, the public correction is part of the work, not a failure of the work.

I have spent twelve years in software being conservatively right. I have not been wrong in public very often because I have not committed in public very often. that's a strategy that works for IC engineers and fails for tech leads. the rewrite is the first project where I have to commit-in-public on the regular. I will be wrong about some of these decisions. I am, this week, deciding to be okay with that. it is going to feel bad in the moment when it happens. I am going to remember that the badness-in-the-moment is the system working correctly, not breaking.

finally — I want to note something about my dad. my mom said he asked about me last week. specifically he asked 'is he sleeping enough.' that's what he asks when he means 'I love him and I worry about him.' direct emotional speech is not in his vocabulary. he expresses care via concern about basic biological functions. it has taken me 33 years to translate this dialect. now that I can translate it, I can love him back through it. I can ask him 'are you sleeping enough' and that means 'I love you, dad.' I am going to call him this weekend. I will ask him about his sleep. that will be the message. I think he will hear it correctly because he speaks the same language.

Nube has started doing the heeler thing where she stares at me intensely until I stand up, then walks me to a thing she wants me to do. this morning she walked me to the door for a walk. yesterday she walked me to the kibble bag. she is herding me. I am the cattle now.

she also stares at me until I take her out for the second walk of the morning. she has learned which jacket means walk and which jacket means I'm just going to the kitchen. she is smarter than at least three of my coworkers. I will not name them.

spent the day on the spatial query port and I want to capture the whole journey because future me is going to want it.

the target query, in the python service:

SELECT r.id, r.driver_id, r.status, ST_AsGeoJSON(r.path) FROM deliveries_route r INNER JOIN deliveries_stop s ON s.route_id = r.id WHERE r.status IN ('scheduled', 'in_progress') AND r.created_at >= $1 AND ST_DWithin(s.location::geography, $2::geography, 1500) ORDER BY r.created_at DESC LIMIT 100;

in rust the magic part is `center_point as Geography` — that's my custom type going through the encoder I wrote, into EWKB on the wire. `RouteStatus` is a postgres enum that I derived sqlx::Type for. enum-as-text-array binding took me 90 minutes to get right because I had to explicitly cast in the query (`$1::route_status[]`) since sqlx couldn't infer the array type with custom enum elements.

the LATERAL/EXISTS pattern from the python query carries over directly. and crucially the query plan in postgres is the same — index scan with the GIST index on s.location, the EXISTS short-circuits per route. p95 in dev: 23ms vs python's 287ms (that was the post-LATERAL python number). 92% reduction. against the original (pre-LATERAL) python it's a 96% reduction.

two unexpected things: sqlx caches prepared statements per pool by default and our pool was small enough that statement-cache thrash was costing us 1-2ms per request, bumped to 100; and tokio-postgres uses a single multiplexed connection per pool member, so long-running spatial queries can hold a connection blocking shorter queries — bumped pool max to 32 in dev and we'll see.

risk: this query depends on the GIST index being chosen. in production the same planner regression that caused the outage could happen here. mitigation: I'm adding a pg_hint_plan comment as a belt-and-suspenders. I know I griped about hints earlier but in a freshly-rewritten service I want the safety wheels on while we build out the load-test harness. tomorrow: wire up tracing spans so we can see query latencies broken down by handler in datadog.

breaking down where the 23ms goes: 8ms in the spatial index lookup, 11ms in the rest of the planner work and tuple fetch, 3ms in our rust decode + serialize, 1ms in middleware. on the python side the same query was: 240ms in the ORM round-trip (yes, ORM was the dominant cost), 30ms in the index lookup, 17ms in serialize. python wasn't slow because postgres was slow. python was slow because django's ORM did a tremendous amount of work per query. removing django removed the bottleneck.

that finding alone justifies a chunk of this rewrite. the planner regression that caused our outage was real, but the underlying issue was that our application layer was paying enormous overhead per query that masked our actual database utilization. now that the application layer is essentially zero-cost, the database has more headroom to absorb load. the rewrite will probably let us scale on smaller postgres instances than we're currently running, which is a cost win nobody costed into the project.

one more thing I want to write down: I tried the LATERAL+EXISTS pattern again in rust, and the planner picked the same plan it would have picked from python. so the win is not from rewriting the SQL — the win is from removing the python overhead. that's a useful clarification. people often confuse 'rewrite for latency' with 'rewrite the SQL.' in this case the SQL is identical; the application layer is what changed. I'll be careful to communicate that distinction.

Nube's first puppy class. she got the gold star (literally, a sticker). best in class on recall. attempted to herd the labradoodle next to her. instructor was charmed. heeler problems are a feature.

the instructor said heelers are notorious for being either great or impossible at puppy class and Nube apparently picked great. she also said 'watch out for adolescence around 8 months' which I am now bracing for. I have been warned. she ignored a treat on the floor when I told her to wait. I almost cried.

tracing layer is up. structured json logs with trace_id propagating from the inbound http request through every db query, with spans for handler-total, db-query, db-encode, db-decode. datadog ingesting cleanly. 

grabbing a sample request: handler-total 24.3ms = auth-middleware 0.6ms + db-query 19.8ms + serialize-response 1.2ms + middleware-other 2.7ms. the dominant cost is the db query, which is what I want — means we're not spending time in the language runtime.

decode time on EWKB linestrings with ~50 points: 380ns per row. negligible. the encoder is paying for itself in spades. and the trace spans line up nicely in the datadog flame graph — every time I see one of these graphs I get a small dopamine hit because we never had this kind of visibility on the python side.

on the python side, datadog spans were anemic — handler-level only, no per-query breakdown without manual instrumentation, and the per-query traces missed the encode/decode time entirely because everything was inside opaque ORM calls. the rust trace_layer gives me span hierarchies for free. the cost was: 60 lines of trace-span setup in main.rs and a few `#[instrument]` attributes on hot functions. the value is: I can see exactly where every microsecond goes, in production, on every request. this is the kind of observability story that pays for itself within the first incident.

the other observability win: error chains. rust's anyhow + thiserror combination produces error chains that are like reading a dependency graph of what went wrong. our 5xx logs in datadog now look like 'auth failed -> redis connection refused -> dns resolution failed -> upstream consul-agent returned NXDOMAIN.' four levels of cause, in one line. on the python side that same error would have been one stack trace and a generic 500. the error-chain quality alone is going to halve our incident debugging time over the next year.

I'm increasingly convinced that observability is not a feature you bolt on; it's a property that emerges from the language and framework choices you make. tower + tracing + thiserror + anyhow is, by accident, a pretty good observability system. you don't have to think about it on the way in. the spans appear, the errors chain, the logs structure. it's the right tax to pay.

the span hierarchy in datadog is so good that I caught my first 'unexpected' performance issue today within 30 seconds of looking. one of our middleware layers (the rate limiter) was doing a redis call on every request, even though most requests don't need rate limiting. moved the check to a path-prefix filter so only protected routes hit redis. saved 0.6ms per request and approximately one whole pod's worth of redis throughput. would not have caught this without the span tree. fast feedback wins.

the trace_id propagation also lets us correlate logs across services. when an alert fires we can grab the trace_id from the alert payload and pull every related span across rust + python + redis + postgres in one query. that's the kind of cross-service correlation that took us a week of debugging to do manually during the outage. now it's a 30-second datadog query. the time-to-diagnosis on the next outage is going to drop by orders of magnitude. that alone justifies a chunk of the migration cost.

Camila said something at dinner that's been turning over in my head. she said the dog has changed how I am at home. that I'm physically more present. that I put my phone down more. she didn't say it as a complaint, she said it as a thank-you almost — like, the dog has done what she's been quietly hoping for the past year.

and I — I can feel it too. picking Nube up out of her bed in the morning, the whole-body relaxation she does when I scratch her chest, that's a thing my prefrontal cortex cannot multitask through. you can't be doom-scrolling while a dog is being soft on your chest. it's like a circuit breaker for screen time.

I don't know what I expected from getting a dog. some chaos. some chewed shoes. I did not expect this — that there'd be a small living thing in our apartment that gives Camila and me a third pole, an outside-of-us point of attention, a thing we both love that is not the relationship itself. I think we needed that and I didn't know.

there's a relationship-research idea that long-term couples need 'shared attention to a third object' to stay healthy — kids, pets, projects, gardens, anything that isn't just the two of you looking at each other. without it the relationship tries to be its own object of attention and that gets exhausting. with it the love becomes parallel: we're both looking at the same thing and feeling the same thing and that creates a kind of low-bandwidth connection that doesn't require words. nube is, I'm realizing, our shared object of attention. she's the third pole. she's the gravitational center we're both orbiting.

that's a heavy way to talk about an animal that just chewed the corner of the couch. but the heaviness is real. some relationships need a baby to find their third pole. we found ours in a 14kg heeler with eyebrows. I'll take it.

later that night Camila asked if she can come with me to Guadalajara when I go to visit abuela's grave. I said yes immediately and was a little surprised at how immediately. I've been treating that visit as a thing I have to do alone. but Camila has been waiting to meet abuela's grave for years — she's heard the stories, she's read the bombilla engraving, she's lit the candle next to me on May 12. she has earned the right to be there. and I have, finally, earned the trust to bring her.

nube comes too I think. she'll do fine in la torta for a 6-hour drive if we stop every 90 minutes. the cemetery doesn't allow dogs but we can leave her with my mom for that hour. abuela would have liked Nube. I keep coming back to that. that woman would have looked at this dog and said 'parece una nube de tormenta' and then fed her a piece of bolillo and called her a luchadora and laughed. the dog she would have approved of. that's a useful little compass — would abuela have approved of this. she'd have approved of the dog. she'd have had questions about the rewrite. she'd have approved of the partner.

she also said something subtle and I want to write it down — she said 'you've been showing up differently for the last two weeks.' two weeks is exactly when nube arrived. she did not say 'because of the dog.' she let me draw the connection. she is, by far, the better psychologist between us. her habit of letting me draw the connection myself is the difference between her being a partner and her being a corrective influence. the difference matters. correction puts you on the defensive. drawing-your-own-connection puts you in agency.

first staging deploy of the rust service today. let me walk through what happened because it's a useful diary entry and I want to be able to look at it again the next time I do this and feel a little less alone.

9am: cargo build --release in CI, took 4m20s. rust compile times remain a thing. binary is 21MB. published to ECR. CI pipeline is green for the first time in 9 commits because I had to thrash on the docker image pin three times to get the base GLIBC right against our ECS Fargate runtime. I am keeping the working dockerfile pinned to the wall above my desk because I never want to relearn the specific trick of using debian:bookworm-slim with a manual libssl3 install over the rust:1.78-bookworm builder.

the specific dockerfile, for posterity:

```
FROM rust:1.78-bookworm AS builder
WORKDIR /build
COPY . .
RUN cargo build --release --bin routeopt-api

FROM debian:bookworm-slim
RUN apt-get update && apt-get install -y libssl3 ca-certificates && rm -rf /var/lib/apt/lists/*
COPY --from=builder /build/target/release/routeopt-api /usr/local/bin/routeopt-api
USER 1000:1000
CMD ["/usr/local/bin/routeopt-api"]
```

the libssl3 thing is because rustls (which I'm using for TLS in tokio-postgres) doesn't actually need libssl, but our postgres driver pulls in some openssl-sys for the SCRAM auth flow due to a transitive dep, and the bookworm-slim base image doesn't ship libssl3. spent 90 minutes on this before I realized. now it's pinned.

2nd attempt at deploy: ECS task started, then crashed in 8 seconds. logs showed `failed to connect to postgres: SCRAM authentication failed`. we use SCRAM-SHA-256 in staging and tokio-postgres needed a feature flag for it that I'd missed locally because dev was using md5. added `runtime-tokio-rustls` features properly, rebuilt, redeployed.

the specific stack trace was helpful in retrospect:

```
Mar 28 14:22:41 routeopt-rs-staging[task-9281] ERROR sqlx::pool: failed to acquire connection from pool: error returned from database: SCRAM-SHA-256 authentication failed
  caused by: SCRAM-SHA-256 authentication is not enabled for this connection
  caused by: feature `tokio-rustls` not enabled in tokio-postgres
Mar 28 14:22:41 routeopt-rs-staging[task-9281] ERROR axum: handler returned error: pool unavailable
```

the error chain pointed straight at the missing feature. love a good rust error chain. would not have been so easy to debug in the python world where the analogous error would have been a generic 'authentication failed' with no hint about which crate or which feature.

3rd attempt: task started, health check passed, traffic-flagged at 0%. dual-read flag flipped on. requests started flowing.

4th attempt: 30 minutes in, datadog alerted on a 1% error rate. errors were a single endpoint returning 500s for routes with empty stop lists. python service handled empty lists by returning an empty array; my rust port treated empty lists as malformed input. quick fix, redeployed. wrote a regression test that explicitly asserts empty-list behavior for both endpoints. that test is going on every code review checklist from now on.

the specific bug in code:

```
// before:
if stops.is_empty() {
    return Err(AppError::BadRequest("empty stops".into()));
}

// after:
// empty stops are valid; return empty result
let candidates = if stops.is_empty() {
    vec![]
} else {
    fetch_candidates(&db, &stops).await?
};
```

that is, in code, the entire fix. five lines. the kind of mistake that you make when you're porting a service and the python implementation made the empty-list case implicit and you didn't catch it because you didn't actively look for it. there will be more of these. dual-read is the only thing that catches them before users do.

5th hour of the day: stable. dual-read showing 99.4% byte-identical responses, the rest are timestamp-precision diffs we already knew about. p95 is 41ms in staging which is honestly higher than I wanted (dev was 23ms) — my hypothesis is that staging postgres is on cheaper hardware and the spatial index lookups are slower. need to test against prod hardware before I commit to a number. I'll know more next week when we get a perf-test slot on the prod-shaped staging cluster.

what I learned today, in no particular order:
- feature flags in cargo are the kind of thing that is silently wrong in dev and loudly wrong in staging. always grep the cargo.toml against the deployed env's expectations before you trust dev numbers.
- empty-list edge cases are the cheapest possible test to write and the most expensive possible bug to fix in production. write the test before you write the handler.
- 41ms p95 in staging means we should aim for 50ms in prod with a generous buffer. that still lands inside our sub-50ms target. cautiously optimistic.
- ECS task definitions need a longer initial health check grace period for rust services because the binary starts in 120ms but the SCRAM handshake adds another ~80ms and the default 5s grace is fine, but if the dns resolver in our VPC is slow to warm (which it sometimes is) the first task can flap. bumped grace to 15s.
- the dual-read middleware found the timestamp-precision issue within 60 seconds of being turned on. it would have taken me hours to find that manually. designing for verification was worth every line of code.
- rust deployment pipelines, once they work, are wonderfully boring. the binary is small, starts fast, has predictable memory, doesn't leak file descriptors, doesn't require a runtime install. compared to the python deploy story this is a different category of operation.
- Andrés had the idea to add a 'rust-build' label to PRs that touch the rust service so the CI runs the rust path; we used to share a single CI config that ran everything on every push. small dev-velocity win.

feelings: enormously relieved. I had a small panic at the SCRAM error because for ten minutes I thought the whole thing wasn't going to work in staging at all. I went and walked Nube around the block. came back, saw the obvious feature flag fix, fixed it. dog walks remain my best debugging tool.

Daniela popped into the team channel: 'congrats on the deploy, this is a real milestone.' it is. it is a real milestone. one read endpoint serving 0% prod traffic via a feature flag is a small thing on the surface and a huge thing as a proof point. it's the difference between a rewrite that exists in slides and a rewrite that exists in production. those are different categories of object.

the team is hyped. Andrés started a doc for next week's planning. Carla bought celebratory tacos for everyone (she's still on light parental leave duty but stopped by the office for an hour). I went home at 7pm which is the earliest I've left in two weeks. Nube was overjoyed. she did the full-body wiggle when I came through the door, the kind that's about 40% wag-radius on the spinal axis, and Camila laughed and said 'is this the earliest you've been home in a month?' and the answer was: yes, it was.

so a rust service is now in our staging environment, accepting traffic via a feature flag, and a dual-read middleware is comparing its responses to the python service in real time. that is, as of today, a fact about the world that was not true yesterday. small steps. but the right steps.

and a closing thought: a year ago this service was an idea that lived in a memo nobody read. six months ago it was a vague intention. four weeks ago it was a slack thread. tonight it is bytes on a disk in an AWS region somewhere, serving a feature-flagged percentage of traffic. things become real when you ship them. that's a thing I keep relearning. the talking and the writing are good and necessary, but until something is on disk in production it is not a thing in the world. tonight a thing entered the world. small, partial, behind a feature flag, but it entered. that's the joy of the job.

the morning-after addition: I slept eight hours, which I have not done in weeks. I woke up to nube curled against my calf. Camila brought me coffee in bed without asking. there are days where the work and the personal align in a small luminous moment and yesterday/this morning was one of them. I want to write that down so I have it on file for the next bad week.

the broader pattern I am internalizing about deploys: every deploy is a small stress event that I can choose to make catastrophic or routine. yesterday's deploy was, ultimately, routine — three small fixes, each caught by the dual-read or by quick monitoring. nothing about it required heroics. what made it feel high-stakes was the lack of past-tense. it was the first staging deploy of the rust service. there will be many more deploys before this is done. each one will feel less special. that's the goal. boring deploys are the goal. exciting deploys are the failure mode. yesterday was exciting, and that's because it was new; in six months I want it to be boring, and that will be because we've shipped a hundred of them.

actionable: I am going to write a 'rust deploy runbook' that captures every step we did yesterday plus the gotchas (libssl3, SCRAM, grace period, dual-read enable). next deploy follows the runbook. third deploy modifies the runbook based on what changed. by month 3 we have a stable runbook and deploys are boring. that's the maturation curve I want. I have been on too many teams where deploys never matured because nobody captured the institutional knowledge between deploys. that's a failure mode I am refusing to repeat.

Nube is 17 weeks. she is bigger every time I look at her. she has discovered that if she presses her cold nose to my calf during dinner I will give her a bite of bread. I am being trained at speed.

the specific bread bite was a corner of pan dulce that fell off Camila's plate. Nube intercepted it in midair like a wide receiver. heeler reflexes plus opportunism. I am keeping a running tally of foods she has stolen and I will be reading it at her wedding.

second read endpoint live in dev. POST /routes/batch (sans optimizer kernel — that's still calling out to python). dual-read working. byte-identical response shapes after the timestamp-precision normalization. p95 in dev is 18ms which is wild given the original was 612ms.

still need to test under load. Andrés is writing a load-test harness this week, k6 with realistic traffic patterns lifted from a dump of last month's prod logs (anonymized). target: 2000 rps sustained, 5000 rps burst, no error budget consumption.

nervous about the prod deploy step. that's still 3 weeks out but I'm already running the rollback plan in my head.

rollback plan, written out so I can reason about it: we have the feature flag at 0% in prod. flip to 1%. monitor error rate, latency, dual-read divergence for 24h. if any signal degrades by more than 2x baseline, flip back to 0%. the python service is still serving the other 99% so a flip-back is a one-keypress change with zero downtime. if the rust service is healthy at 1% for 24h, we ramp to 5%. if 5% is healthy for 48h, we ramp to 25%. if 25% is healthy for 72h, we ramp to 100% and let it bake for two weeks before we declare victory. total ramp: ~10 days. total observation window: ~14 days. only after that do we even consider deprecating the python path.

the load-test plan, in slightly more detail: replay anonymized prod logs at 1x, 2x, 5x, and 10x speed. measure error rate, p50, p95, p99, p99.9. compare against python service running same load (we'll spin up a parallel staging instance). target: rust service handles 5x current load with p95 under 50ms and zero errors. python service breaks somewhere between 2x and 3x with elevated error rates and p95 above 800ms.

if rust handles 10x cleanly we'll know we have headroom for two years of growth. if it breaks at 5x we know we have phase-2 work ahead. either way we get a real number, not a vibe. real numbers are how you survive in production. vibes are how you have outages.

the rollback plan also has a 'panic button' option: a single feature-flag toggle that flips the entire rust path off and routes all traffic back to python. tested in dev. tested in staging. exists. nobody hopes to use it. everybody is glad it's there. that's the deal with rollback plans.

the load-test harness Andrés is writing is also being scoped to run in CI on demand, not just before-prod-rollout. so we'll be able to load-test any branch against synthetic traffic. that's a discipline I have not had before. previous places I've worked at, load testing was a once-a-quarter ceremony. with cheap on-demand load tests in CI we can shift load testing left — every meaningful change gets a baseline check before merge. that's a different category of confidence.

end-of-month retro for myself, since I keep these notes for the future and this month was a lot.

Month 5 calendar:
- April 23: 4-hour postgres outage. nightmare.
- April 24-25: post-mortem draft and review cycles.
- April 28: post-mortem published, rewrite approved by Daniela.
- May 1: rust scaffolding starts. tech lead role real.
- May 5-12: postgis encoder for points and linestrings.
- May 15-21: polygons and multipolygons in encoder, auth middleware, first read endpoint.
- May 25: adopted Nube. life changed.
- May 27: second puppy class, Nube heroes again.
- June 2: spatial query port working, dev p95 23ms.
- June 5: staging deploy, first prod-like proof of life.
- today (June 8): two read endpoints live in dev, dual-read showing 99%+ identical, postgis encoder stable.

lessons:
1. the post-mortem was the most consequential document I have written at this company. four pages, no ego, all facts and action items. it changed the shape of the next 18 months. if you ever want to make change happen at an org, write a really good post-mortem about a real incident; nothing else moves leadership the same way.
2. being tech lead is mostly writing things down. the actual code I wrote this month is maybe 30% of my time. the rest was: the rewrite memo, the post-mortem, the rfc for the rust stack, the kickoff email, the risk register, the phase plan, the auth-middleware design doc, the encoder design doc. seven major documents in six weeks. nobody told me leadership was a writing job, but every senior I've worked under is, in retrospect, a person who wrote a lot.
3. PostGIS in tokio-postgres is a real gap and now I have a small crate that fixes it for our use case. I'm proud of this in a way I haven't been proud of code in years. it's not glamorous. it's an EWKB encoder. but it's something I made from nothing because the ecosystem didn't have it, and it's running in our staging right now serving real bytes. I will keep this kind of work close — the kind where the gap is real and the fix is small and the satisfaction is durable.
4. you can adopt a puppy in the middle of the most stressful month of your professional life and the puppy will, paradoxically, make the month easier. she has reset my evenings. she has forced me out of the apartment for walks even when I would rather be debugging. she has made me put my phone down. she has, through pure mammalian presence, lowered my heart rate.
5. Camila and I are better with the dog. that's a bigger sentence than it looks like. we had been functioning fine on the surface but I think we'd been not-quite-aligned for a while since her mom died, kind of moving in parallel rather than together. the dog is a project we are both invested in equally. we trade walks. we discuss whether she's pooping enough. we send each other photos throughout the day. it has been, weirdly, a kind of relationship therapy.
6. I have not visited abuela's grave yet. I said I would. I will. I'm flying to Guadalajara mid-month for Mateo's birthday and I will go. writing it here so I have to do it. that's the function of writing things down — the next-self can hold the past-self accountable.

things I want to remember:
- Profe Esteban watching the pose-est screen and saying 'sí, exactamente lo que le digo desde hace un año.'
- Nube falling asleep mid-play on our first day
- Daniela saying 'this is annoyingly compelling' about my memo
- the moment the EWKB hex round-tripped for the first time and the test passed
- Andrés' fire emoji in slack at the kickoff
- Camila on the rescue floor with a puppy's chin on her knee
- Andrés saying jefe at 3:30am during the outage
- Doña Marta giving Nube a piece of bread at 6am like they were old friends
- the moment the dual-read middleware caught the timestamp-precision diff at minute 60 of staging deploy
- the moment I read Daniela's first round of post-mortem edits and realized she'd kept the Camila-tacos line

things I am avoiding looking at:
- the kid-timing conversation Camila and I keep almost having. she's been increasingly direct about wanting to talk through it. I keep deflecting. I know I am deflecting. she knows I am deflecting. we both know we both know. that's the next conversation.
- my dad's heart. the angina episode was 'minor' but my mom is scared. I should call him directly, not through her. I have not.
- the MIT thing. it's been in the back of my head since the pose-est project started landing. I don't have the bandwidth to seriously work it up while the rust migration is in early phase but I keep reading research papers from the Affective Computing group at like midnight. it's a future-shaped thing I am keeping under wraps from myself.

meta-reflection on my own journaling habit: I write these end-of-month retros because I read mine from a year ago last week and it was illuminating in a way I had not expected. month-old me thought he was failing at things he was actually about to succeed at. month-old me had concerns that have evaporated. month-old me also had concerns I have ignored that are now larger. the journaling buys me a small mirror that the present-tense me cannot hold. the cost is low (an hour at the end of the month) and the value compounds.

okay. that's the month. on to the next one. there's a kid-timing conversation Camila and I keep almost having and I think it's coming this week. heads-up to you so you're not surprised when I'm a wreck about it.

but for now, sleep. Nube is on the rug. she has one paw on my foot. life is, against all odds and all expectations, kind of good. not in spite of the chaos but because of how the chaos is shaped — outage to post-mortem to rewrite to dog to staging deploy. that is, looked at sideways, a series of doors I walked through. each one closed behind me. each one opened the next. I did not pick the order. I would not have picked this order. but here I am, on the other side of a month I would not have predicted, kind of glad to be here. and that's enough for tonight.

one more thing I want to capture before I sleep. I read back through my notes from January and the contrast is brutal. january-me had no project, no clear path to the rewrite, no dog, no plan for visiting abuela. january-me was professionally stuck and personally avoidant. four months later: a real project, a clear path, a dog, a plane ticket. the rate of change is, in retrospect, not because of anything I did especially well. the changes happened to me. the post-mortem happened because of the outage. the rewrite happened because of the post-mortem. the dog happened because of Camila. the cemetery visit will happen because my mom said 'mijo, you said that last month.'

the causal arrow points outward. circumstances act on me, and I respond. I'm trying to figure out whether that's a story about being responsive (good) or being passive (bad). I think there's a middle reading: I am responsive to things I don't have to choose, and resistant to things I do have to choose. the rewrite was 'easy' to commit to because daniela handed it to me. the dog was 'easy' because Camila pushed. the cemetery is 'easy' because my mom called it out. the things that have stayed undone — like therapy, or talking to my dad, or starting a serious application to grad school — are the things where there is no external pressure forcing me. I will not make them happen unless I impose external pressure on myself.

maybe that's the lesson of the month: I need to manufacture external pressure on the things I want to do but won't initiate. that means: schedule the therapist. block out time for grad-school research. tell Camila I want to be held accountable for calling my dad weekly. make the things visible to other people so the social cost of not doing them is real.

I know how to do this at work. I do it for the team all the time. weekly check-ins, public commitments, written goals. the same machinery is available for personal life and I have not deployed it. that's the actionable reading.

starting tomorrow: I am going to send Camila a list of three things I want to commit to in the next 90 days. then I'm going to ask her to hold me to them. then she's going to laugh because this is, again, the spec-brain in service of the unspecifiable. and that's fine. let it be funny. let it work.

on the kid-timing thing — I want to commit to a piece of process before I close. I have been deflecting this conversation for two years. specifically: when Camila brings up timing, I say 'soon,' I list the work obstacles, and we move on. that is, in retrospect, a textbook avoidance pattern. the work obstacles are real but they are also infinitely renewable; there will always be a project, a rewrite, a sprint, a launch. if I let work obstacles dictate, we never have the conversation. we have to have the conversation against the backdrop of work obstacles, because the work obstacles never go away, they just change shape.

so my commitment is: in the next 14 days I will sit down with Camila for a real conversation about timing, with a real timeline, with a real number of years, with a real plan for what conditions need to be true (financial stability check, parent-illness contingency, my own readiness work which means therapy first). the number does not need to be 'this year' or 'next year' — it needs to be 'in N years, here is what we do, here are the off-ramps if we need them.' that is a contract, not a commitment to a specific outcome. but it is a contract, and contracts are what adults have, and we are adults.

I am writing this here so future me reads it in 14 days and is forced to either have done it or to admit I did not. accountability through journaling. weak form, but real form.

Nube turned 18 weeks. measured at 14kg. heeler size charts say she'll cap around 18-20kg. she has finally figured out the leash. not biting it counts as figuring it out for a heeler. progress.

14kg at 18 weeks puts her on track for the larger end of the heeler range, around 20kg. fine by us. she is a small dog by some standards and a medium by others and she is exactly the right size for our apartment. she ate one of my running socks this morning. mostly figured out the leash.

found a small bug in the encoder while doing extra paranoia testing this week. when a polygon has a hole that touches the outer ring at a single point (rare but legal under the OGC spec), my writer was emitting the rings in the wrong order — I had assumed the first ring is always the exterior, but proper spec says the exterior is the largest by signed-area. fixed by computing signed area at encode time and ensuring largest-first.

added a test case for this. zero of our actual prod polygons have this shape, but I want to be right not just lucky.

the bug surfaced because I'd been reading the OGC simple-features spec on my commute (yes, this is what I do for fun now) and noticed the line about signed-area determining ring orientation. I went back to my encoder and confirmed: my writer was passing the geo crate's ring order through unchanged. the geo crate does not guarantee exterior-first. so on inputs where the geo construction happened to put a hole first, my writer would emit a polygon that was technically backwards under the spec. postgis was tolerant of it on round-trip but the spec says it's wrong. we are now spec-compliant.

lesson: read the goddamn spec. specs are boring and they are also the only place where the answers are.

the other small spec correction I made while I was in there: the EWKB extension flag bits are SRID-bit (0x20000000), Z-bit (0x80000000), M-bit (0x40000000), and I had documented Z and M backwards in my own internal notes. I never actually emitted Z or M in production code (we don't have 3D geometries) but if a future colleague had read my notes and trusted them they would have wired Z to the M-bit and gotten very confused. fixed the doc.

specs are dry. specs are also the reference text. when there's a question about behavior, the spec wins over my intuition every time. my intuition was wrong about ring orientation. my intuition was wrong about the Z-vs-M flag bits. my intuition is wrong more often than I'd like. this is why we read the spec. lesson keeps reasserting itself; lesson keeps not staying internalized; I am going to print the OGC simple-features spec and put it next to my desk.

the other thing I want to write down: in the process of going through the OGC spec I noticed that we don't strictly conform to the WKT-output side either, in our public API. we emit a slight dialect that postgis-tools handle but other GIS tools might not. that's a separate issue but I'm filing it. once we own the encoder fully we should also own the WKT serialization end. consistency across the boundary is the difference between a tool that interoperates and a tool that has 'works with our stack' caveats.

follow-up note on the OGC spec reading: the spec is dense but well-organized. section 6.1 covers WKB. section 6.2 covers EWKB extensions. there's a worked example in appendix A that shows the byte layout for every geometry type. I have folded the appendix and stuck it on my desk. when in doubt, byte-level doubt, go to appendix A. I have had to do this three times in the last week.

ran with Nube for the first time today. easy 2km along Avenida Amsterdam. she trotted next to me like she'd been doing it for years. heeler genes. Pablo saw us on his bike and yelled 'qué onda, jefa' at the dog. she ignored him with dignity.

she trotted next to me at heel-position the whole 2km. zero pulling. zero distractions. one disdainful glance at a chihuahua. heelers are, when properly fueled with affection and exercise, the most professional dogs alive. pablo on his bike was charmed and a little scared.

Camila and I had the conversation. the kid-timing one. it didn't go badly, exactly, but it didn't go well either, and I want to write it down because I keep replaying it in my head and I'd rather have it on the page than in my chest.

she asked me at dinner — calmly, after we'd had wine — when I thought we should start trying for a kid. she said she'd been thinking about it since her mom died, that watching Beatriz go made her feel time differently, that she didn't want to be 38 trying to start. she said she's 30, she wants two years of more-or-less normal couple life with us, and then she wants to start. so two years. roughly.

I said okay, I hear you. I asked if I could think out loud and she said yes. I said two things — first, that I love her and I want kids with her, and that hasn't changed and won't. second, that the timing scares me. specifically, I'm 6 months into being a tech lead on a major rewrite, I just adopted a dog who's still in puppy class, I have not been to my grandmother's grave in five years, my dad's heart is precarious, and I am quietly considering applying to a graduate program that would require us to either move or be apart. layering a baby on top of all that — I don't know what kind of partner I'd be in that scenario. I might be a worse one. I think I'd want to be a better me than I am right now before I become a parent.

she took that in. she said she understood the words. she said she also understood that the words could be a way of saying never. she said she'd rather I tell her never than slow-walk her through a decade of not-yet. that landed like a kick to the sternum because she's not wrong. I have a documented history of slow-walking. I slow-walked visits to abuela. I am, structurally, a slow-walker. I built a career on slow-walking — on saying 'next quarter, next quarter' until the quarter never comes. and Camila has watched me do it and she has been, in her quiet way, modeling for me how a person who actually moves through life acts. she has not been waiting to ask me this; she has been giving me the chance to figure it out without her asking. she finally asked because the chance had run out.

I told her it's not never. I told her I think it's two-and-a-half to three. she said three is okay. she said two-and-a-half is okay. she said five is not okay. we landed on: revisit in 18 months, real conversation with a real timeline. couples therapy from someone competent if we can't get there ourselves.

then she cried. then I cried. then Nube put her head on Camila's knee like she had at the rescue and we both laughed because of course she did, that's her move. the dog as a small bridge between two adults who could not, for those particular three minutes, look at each other directly. I am very glad we got the dog. I am also keenly aware that the dog is not going to solve this conversation; the dog can only put her chin on a knee.

I don't know how to feel. I feel like we landed somewhere honest, which is more than we'd done in a year. I also feel like I just signed a contract with a hard deadline and I'm scared of the deadline. she's not wrong that I am 33 and that her body has its own clock and that the conversation can't be postponed indefinitely.

the other thing — and I want to say this because I've been sitting with it — is that part of what scares me is becoming my dad. closed off, workaholic, present-in-room-absent-in-spirit. I watched my mom carry the weight of his presence for forty years. I have the same stiffness in me when I'm stressed. I have the same trick of escaping into work when feelings get loud. if I become a parent and I don't fix that first, I will pass it down. I don't want to pass it down. and I don't have a plan for how to fix it; I have a vague intention to fix it, which is not the same thing as fixing it. you don't fix a generational pattern by intending to. you fix it by sitting in the rooms where it shows up and not running. I have not been good at that.

so the contract has a clause attached, and the clause is: I have to do the work. therapy. less screens. fewer 11pm rust sessions. the kind of presence Camila has been quietly modeling for me. Mama Lupita would have said: hijo, ya es hora de crecer un poco. it is time, indeed, to grow up a little. I have been working very hard at being a good engineer. I have been working much less hard at being a good person.

there's also a thing I want to acknowledge to myself that I haven't said in this thread before. the rewrite, the postgis encoder, the tech lead role — those things have made me feel competent in a way I've been hungry to feel for two years. and that hunger has been, in part, why I am still avoiding the parent question. it is so much easier to be the person who solved a complicated tokio-postgres problem than to be the person who has to commit to a timeline for becoming a father. one of those problems has a clear interface and a test suite. the other has neither. I have been, structurally, choosing the problem with the test suite. I see myself doing it.

the thing I keep returning to, in the middle of this anxiety, is that Camila is not actually asking me to fix everything. she's asking me to commit to a direction. that's a much smaller ask than the way I've been hearing it. 'two-and-a-half to three years' is a heading, not a destination. you can change a heading without sinking the ship. I have been treating her as if she were demanding a destination — a fixed date, a fixed plan — when she has been asking for a heading. she will let the heading evolve as we evolve. she is not the kind of partner who books non-refundable life decisions; she's the kind who points at the horizon and says 'roughly there, yes?' and waits for you to nod. all I have to do tonight is nod. I nodded. tomorrow I have to keep nodding by acting like a person who is heading there. that's the work. small course corrections in the right direction.

okay. the encoder is shipped. the tech-lead role is real. the dog is asleep on the rug. the partner is asleep in the bed. and there is, ahead of me, a longer set of harder problems than any I've ever solved at work. let's see how I do with those. let's see if I am the kind of person who can stay in the room when the problem is shaped like a person and not a query plan.

I'm going to go to sleep. tomorrow we have a kickoff meeting for phase 2 of the rewrite — porting the optimizer kernel — and I want to be sharp for it. but tonight I'll just sit with this for a minute. just sit with it. that's apparently a skill I'm supposed to learn.

also: I am going to call my dad tomorrow. directly, not through my mom. I have not called him since the angina episode. he will say everything is fine and I will say I love you, dad, and he will be embarrassed and pleased, and we'll talk about Sergio Pérez or chess or whatever, and that will be the small step. small steps in the right direction beat large steps in the wrong direction. that's the working theory tonight.

and I'm going to email the therapist Camila found a card for last month. her name is Dr. Carmen Muñoz, in Condesa. Camila has been carrying that card in her wallet for four weeks and not pushed me on it. I am going to take the card off her hands. that's a small concrete thing I can do tomorrow morning that will, I hope, change the shape of this conversation by the time we revisit it. starting things is hard for me. continuing them is easier. so just start. send the email. let future Seb continue from there.

one more thing — and this is probably the last thing tonight, I'm tired and Nube is tired and Camila is asleep — I want to say something to my future self in case I look back at this and need a reminder.

future seb: if you are reading this and you have followed through on the small things — the therapist, the dad call, the cemetery, the 18-month timeline check-in — then it worked. write it down. journal it. let your past-self know.

if you are reading this and you have not followed through on the small things, then: stop. read this paragraph again. you are repeating the pattern. the pattern is: avoid the unspecifiable thing, throw yourself at the specifiable thing, feel productive, feel hollow. you have done it your whole adult life. you do not get to do it during your forties. your forties have to be the decade where you do the unspecifiable work. your thirties will not have been wasted but they will have been necessary preparation. now is the work.

and future seb: if there's a child in your life when you read this, look at them. look at them for a minute without thinking about anything else. that is the rep. it never gets less important. you will be tempted to be 30% in the room. resist. be 100% in the room. the child will absorb either version of you and the version that lasts is the version that was 100% present.

okay. closing the laptop. tomorrow I email the therapist. tomorrow I call my dad. tomorrow I commit to the 18-month conversation. small steps. small steps.

nube has rolled onto her back in her bed with her paws in the air, which is the position she sleeps in when she is most relaxed. that's a good sign. she trusts the apartment. she trusts us. it took her three weeks to do the belly-up sleep. now it's just how she sleeps.

I am going to take that as the headline image of this month. the dog who, at week three, decided she was safe enough to sleep belly-up. that is, I think, what we are all trying to do — get safe enough with a place and a person to sleep belly-up. Camila got there with me a long time ago. I am, slowly, getting there with her. Nube got there with both of us in three weeks because dogs are smarter than people about this. I am going to take notes.

final note to the journal: I sent Camila a text just now from the bathroom (don't tell her). it said 'thank you for asking the hard question tonight. I love you. tomorrow I will email Dr. Muñoz.' she will see it in the morning. she will not respond directly. she will just put her hand on my back when she walks past me in the kitchen and that will be the response. that is how she does this. that is one of the languages of this relationship.

things I am refusing to do tonight: open my work laptop. check slack. check the rust crate's CI status. those are tomorrow problems. tonight is a tonight problem and the tonight problem is: sit with the conversation, sleep next to my partner, let the dog sleep next to my partner too. that's the whole evening. nothing else. let the evening be only what it is. resist the engineering urge to optimize the evening. some evenings are not optimization problems. some evenings are sit-still problems.

ok rubber-duck mode. I want to walk you through the full state of the postgis encoder problem because I keep going in circles and writing it out helps. you remember the rust migration kickoff back in month 6 — when we decided to move routeopt off python/django and onto rust because the postgres outage made the case for us, and because the django ORM was choking on the geography joins so badly we were eating 800ms per dispatch query at peak — okay so the first major rock in the road, the one I told you about then, is that tokio-postgres does not have a native type mapping for postgis's `geography` type. there's a half-dead crate called `postgis` that wraps geometry but it doesn't compose well with sqlx, and we are doing this on sqlx because the team needs the macros for the rest of the schema. so I had to write a custom encoder. that part has gone okay-ish but it has substructure I haven't told you about, and I'm three weeks into it now and there are decisions worth recording so I don't keep making them. you know my pattern: I oscillate at 11pm and forget by 9am.

the encoder takes a wkb byte slice (well-known binary, the standard binary format for geometries) and returns my own internal `Geometry` enum — Point, LineString, Polygon, MultiPolygon, GeometryCollection — that's all we use for routing. inbound is fine, that's just byte-fiddling. outbound is the hard part. when I want to insert a route polyline back into postgis from rust, I'm building a Postgres-flavored EWKB blob — the EWKB variant has the SRID prefix bit set, srid=4326 for us — and sqlx's `query!` macro is choking because it sees the column type as `geography(LineString,4326)` and there's no native type mapping for that. so I'm having to use `query_as` and bind via `Encode<Postgres>` manually, which means the compile-time SQL checking I love about sqlx — the killer feature, the whole reason we picked sqlx over diesel, the only reason andrés agreed to use sqlx — just does not apply to those specific queries. that hurts because we picked sqlx over diesel SPECIFICALLY for the macros. losing them on the geometry-touching subset of queries (which is, frankly, most of them) means we're using sqlx at like 60% of its value. carla is going to ask me about this when she's back from leave and I'd better have a story.

let me lay out the options I see, because I keep oscillating between them at 11pm and never deciding. five branches, with the costs and benefits I've enumerated for each:

1) keep the manual encode + lose compile-time checking on those specific queries. accept it. compensate with extensive integration tests against a real postgis container in CI. cost: I lose the type safety on maybe 20% of our queries — the geometry-touching ones, but disproportionately the load-bearing ones since geometry is in the hot path. benefit: zero infrastructure cost. ship today. risk: a refactor in 6 months breaks one of those queries silently and we don't catch it until staging, which is exactly the failure mode the postgres outage taught us about — silent breakages in dispatch are worse than loud ones.

2) write a wrapper type `PgGeography<T>` that implements `Type<Postgres>` with `type_info()` returning a custom OID I look up at runtime. but here's the snag: the OID for postgis types is dynamic per database — it's not a fixed pg_type entry, it's installed with the extension, so the OID is database-specific. I'd have to query pg_type at startup, cache the OID, and then sqlx could use it. but this fights sqlx's macro infrastructure because the macro wants types known at compile time. there's a workaround using `sqlx::Decode` and `sqlx::Type` impls with `#[sqlx(type_name = "geography")]`, and I tested that on a small repro yesterday and it MOSTLY works. the tricky bit: the macro reads the type_name and matches against the connection's type info during the prepared-statement phase, and if the OID lookup hasn't happened yet on a fresh pool acquire, it fails with an inscrutable error: `column "" of unknown type` (yes, empty string column name, sqlx's internal logging swallows the actual column).

3) drop sqlx entirely for this one repo, go pure tokio-postgres + bb8 pool. lose the macros. but gain full control. but lose every other dev's understanding of the codebase, because everyone else at brújula who touches rust is using sqlx in the geocoding service. consistency penalty: high. carla will hate this when she's back from leave — she championed sqlx in the rust working group three quarters ago and there'd be a lot of 'I told you so' to navigate. organizational cost outweighs technical cost here.

4) write a stored proc that accepts text wkt and converts internally. then I bind a String. easiest path. but that's a layer of indirection I'd hate, and we'd be doing wkt parsing inside postgres on every insert, which is slow — wkt parsing is 10-100x slower than wkb on the postgis side, I checked. plus we'd have an out-of-band sql artifact that is not version-controlled with the rust code, which violates everything I believe about migrations and reproducibility. and stored procs have versioning problems — when you upgrade them, who runs the upgrade, and how do they sync with the rust deploy? this is a path that creates more problems than it solves.

5) — the option I have not been able to dismiss — write the encoder, extract it as its own crate, eventually open-source it. solve it once, solve it for everyone in the rust+postgis world. there is genuinely nothing on crates.io that handles this cleanly. but the maintenance burden of 'a crate strangers depend on' is real. I have approximately zero free hours. and yet — and yet — every time I write the workaround code I think 'someone is going to want this'. the venn diagram of 'people building rust services that talk to postgis' is small but it is also intense — those people will use the crate hard and will have opinions and will file issues at midnight my time. I am not in a life stage to take on that audience.

leaning toward 1 + 2 hybrid for now: manual encode for inserts, wrapper type with `#[sqlx(type_name)]` for reads, which is most of our query volume anyway. extensive integration tests on the inserts — round-trip a real geometry through a docker postgis instance in CI, verify the output matches the input byte-for-byte. push the open-source extraction to after migration ships. if I survive that, the crate is a nice resume thing — and on that note, more on resume things another day, that's a separate spiral I'm not letting myself open tonight, but the masters-application stuff is starting to creep in around the edges of my late-night thinking and I'm going to have to deal with it eventually.

the open question that keeps me up: how do I structure the OID lookup so it's robust across pool refills? right now I have it gated by a `OnceCell::get_or_init` on the pool's first connection acquire. but if the pool gets reset (which it does on extended idle plus a postgres restart), the OnceCell holds a stale OID. tested with a manual `pg_ctl reload` in dev and it does fail. fix: instead of `OnceCell`, do the lookup once per connection on acquire, cached on a per-connection lifetime via a custom `before_acquire` hook in the sqlx pool config. expensive in latency by like 1ms but bulletproof. that's probably the right call. the alternative — refresh the OnceCell on connection error — is conceptually reactive and feels wrong; you should not learn about extension OIDs by failing first.

also side note: the load test I want to run later this week is going to expose whatever lifetime bugs I have in the JoinHandle handling on the fan-out scoring path. I have a feeling about that one. spawning N tokio tasks per request and awaiting them serially is dumb, I knew it when I wrote it, I'm going to fix it before we even ship to staging. but first the encoder. one rock at a time. the temptation to nibble at multiple problems is the same temptation that gets engineers stuck — you are partially solving 5 things and shipping zero of them. ship the encoder, then the JoinHandle fix, then the metrics, in that order, no peeking ahead.

and a meta thing: I am noticing that 'one rock at a time' is the discipline I have to actively practice on this migration, because there are like 14 rocks visible from here and the urge is to triage by trying to nibble at all of them. the discipline is to put 13 of them in a backlog file (which I have been doing — `MIGRATION.md` at the repo root, currently 412 lines) and only work the top one. daniela taught me this. she says 'the engineers I trust most are the ones who can put a 12-rock backlog down on the table without sweating'. I'm trying. I have not yet succeeded. I keep glancing at rocks 2 through 14 to make sure they haven't grown teeth in the night. they haven't. they will not. they are rocks. they will be there tomorrow.

okay. encoder option 1+2 hybrid, OID lookup per-connection via before_acquire hook, proceeding. tell me if you see option 6. and remind me that backlog discipline is the actual technical skill, not the encoder fluency. encoder fluency is rented. backlog discipline is owned.

third mate of the morning and I haven't even logged into stand-up yet. the bombilla is shedding a tiny bit on the rim, where 'Seb' is engraved. seven years it's been mine, longer than abuela had it before she gave it to me. I should ask my mom what she did to keep it from oxidizing — she watched abuela maintain it for decades. there's some ritual involving lemon I half-remember but don't want to get wrong by guessing. and that's the kind of thing I notice now in a way I didn't five years ago. small inheritances become heavier as you age, not lighter. I keep noticing this pattern.

lunchtime ramen at nudo. the kitsune udon special is back — they only do it when the cook from osaka is in. third bowl this month. tofu instead of pork. cardiologist would not approve but he's in guadalajara and besides, the tofu is the substitution he'd actually recommend. when my dad got the bypass I didn't think 'I'm going to give up meat for life' — that crystallized later, in the hospital reading, when I had a paperback about cardiac diet open on my lap and a vending-machine sandwich (turkey, ironically) untouched next to me. but the upstream cause is still that surgery. four years in October and I haven't touched red meat. fish a few times a year when camila's family is hosting. otherwise vegetarian. the funny thing is how much my palate has rewired itself — I cannot smell beef now without my body reading it as 'too much'. weight of it, density of it, the after-feeling of it. I crave plain rice and beans more than I ever craved a steak.

nudo's owner — yamila — saw me staring at the menu the first time I came in and asked 'vegetariano?' before I'd opened my mouth. apparently it shows. she puts an extra dollop of yuzu kosho on the kitsune udon for me now and never charges me for it. the regular treatment in cdmx is one of the reasons I have not seriously considered moving anywhere else, even when bogotá was on the table last year. you don't get this from a job. you get it from showing up at the same noodle shop forty times. that's the math of belonging. forty bowls of ramen. that's the price of being recognized.

I notice I have been reflecting more this year than in any previous year of my life, and I think that is because I am 32 and on the cusp of 33, and 33 is one of those numbers that lands. 30 didn't land — 30 felt like an extension of 29. 33 feels like a verdict. it's the age my dad died. that's part of it but not all of it. some of it is the migration, some of it is muñoz, some of it is the prospect of having a kid in two years. some of it is realizing how much of the last decade I sleepwalked through.

the value of writing this out, I think, is that I get to see my own assumptions on the page and notice when they're wrong. there are things I would never say out loud because I'd flinch at hearing them, but I'll type them in a chat at midnight because the chat doesn't flinch back. that's the deal I've made with this medium. it's a kind of half-confession to no one.

I'm going to come back to this in six months and either nod or wince, and I want both versions of me to know I tried to be honest. that is the goal of journaling for me right now. not to predict, not to plan. just to leave an honest record so that future-me cannot lie about who past-me was. that is the bargain.

so the dog. so. nube. she's been with us a month now and she's amazing but she has SEPARATION ANXIETY, capital letters, all caps. yesterday I went to atlas box for a 90-minute class and came back to the upstairs neighbor's note slid under the door — doña marta in her perfect old-lady cursive — saying nube cried for the entire 90 minutes. not barked. CRIED. like a baby. camila got home before me and found her panting with her water bowl knocked over and a chewed-up corner of the rug. blue heelers are working dogs, the rescue lady warned us, they need a job. the job apparently is following us from room to room and watching us pee.

she's getting trazodone for the storm-panic season but the vet was clear: meds for storms, not for baseline. baseline gets behavioral training. so we're starting desensitization stuff this weekend — leave for 30 sec, come back, leave for 1 min, come back. classic protocol. but I'm tired, and camila is tired, and the only one who isn't tired is the dog herself. she's vibrating with energy. she pushes her nose under my elbow when I type and just stays there. I don't have the heart to push her off, which is exactly the kind of softness that probably perpetuates the anxiety. but. anyway. tomorrow: phase one, boring everything.

doña marta has been a saint about it. she has not actually complained, even though her note said nube was crying. she signed the note 'con cariño' and slipped a pan dulce under the door with it. the cariño was load-bearing. she could have called the building manager. she did not call the building manager. instead she fed our dog a piece of bread when she heard her crying. cdmx neighbor energy. I owe her a roscón at three kings, no question. I owe her two roscones. she will accept one and force me to take the second back. that's how this works.

and I want to be the partner who notices these things without being told, but I am also the partner who is in the rust migration tunnel and will go for days without lifting his head. the gap between intention and behaviour is the place where relationships get worn down, slowly. muñoz keeps saying that. I keep nodding. then I keep failing it. then I nod some more. it is not a logic problem. it is a body problem.

Camila says I am 'present-adjacent' — that's her phrase, she made it up two months ago and now it's a household word. it means I am physically there but my attention is in 30 other places. she is right and naming it has helped, slightly, because now I can hear the word and pull myself back. but only slightly. the deeper work is making the word unnecessary, and that's a year-long project, not a week-long one.
