more on the dog because I need to think out loud and you don't bill by the hour. here's the desensitization plan I cobbled together from like four youtube videos, a karen overall pdf I half-read, and one reddit thread that I think was actually about cats. don't tell my vet.

phase 1 (this week, multiple times per day): pick up keys, put them down. don't leave. do it 20x. she has to learn keys != leaving forever. similarly: put on shoes, sit on couch, take shoes off. open the front door, close the front door, sit on couch. do it boring. boring is the goal. by the end of week 1 the goal is keys/shoes/door = nothing. cues stripped of meaning.

phase 2 (this weekend): close her in the kitchen with a kong frozen with peanut butter (no xylitol, I checked the jar five times because xylitol kills dogs) for 60 seconds while I stand in the hall. then 90, then 2 min. listen at the door — if she's not crying, return calmly, no big greeting. if she IS crying, wait until a brief pause and THEN return. don't reward the cry.

phase 3 (next week): actual leaving. walk out the front door. wait in the stairwell. listen. come back. extend in 30-sec increments.

phase 4: trazodone? — no, vet said save it for storms, not baseline. behavioral only. trazodone is the storm-kit.

the thing I'm wrestling with internally: I was 'on' from breakfast through midnight as a kid because my dad worked late and my mom was at the hospital — I'm wired to attune to anyone in distress. when nube whines I feel it in my chest like a pull. so leaving a panicking dog feels like a moral injury, like I am committing a small abandonment every time I close that kitchen door. camila has a healthier wiring on this — she can close the kitchen door, put on a podcast, let nube work it out. she's right. I just have to do it anyway. and there's the meta-fear: that the desensitization will desensitize ME, not just her, and I'll become someone who can hear distress and walk away. that's me overthinking dog training but. that's where my head goes at 11pm with a panting cattle dog under my chair. tomorrow: keys, 20 reps, boring.

axum 0.7 broke State extractor signatures from 0.6 and I just spent 40 min on a compile error that turned out to be the wrong import path. the error message blamed a different file. cargo check kept saying 'expected struct, found struct' which is the worst error message in the rust universe. no manches. anyway, fix it tomorrow. not tonight. tonight I sleep. stuck in a loop in my head about it. closing laptop now. will re-read this in the morning with fresh eyes. filing it under 'sleep on it'. usually that works. no other notes tonight. logging it. moving on.

so I tried option 2 from yesterday's list — the wrapper type with `#[sqlx(type_name = "geography")]` annotation — properly this time, with the OID resolution scoped to per-connection-on-acquire instead of the OnceCell I'd used in the spike. it works for reads. for inserts it works ONLY if I also tag the bind site with the same wrapper type, which sqlx then uses to look up the cached OID for that specific connection. ugly but contained. the api surface from the consumer side is now: bind a `PgGeography<LineString>` to a parameter, get a `PgGeography<LineString>` back. the inner geometry types are mine, encoded/decoded via my fuzz-tested ewkb implementation.

fine. encoder chapter mostly closed. shipped to the repo as `crates/geo-pg/`. moving on. except: I added a comment block at the top of `lib.rs` saying 'this code is a candidate for extraction into its own crate someday — see notes' and now I keep coming back to read that comment and feel possessive about it. the brain wants to extract because the brain wants to write a blog post and have the blog post get retweeted. the brain is dumb. the migration is the project. the extraction is the someday. discipline.

something I want to flag for future-me: the type-level api could be cleaner. right now `PgGeography<T>` requires T: Geometry plus a const generic for the SRID, but I've punted on the const generic and hardcoded 4326 because that's all we use. the day someone needs srid=3857 (web mercator) we'll have to revisit. I left a TODO with a sketch of the const-generic version in the lib.rs preamble. and I am genuinely proud of how the encode path turned out — once the OID-on-acquire problem was settled, the actual byte-level encode is maybe 60 lines of clean code with explicit big-endian/little-endian handling and a single match statement on the geometry variant. fuzz-tested for 6 hours of CPU. it works. encoder. closed. on to the JoinHandle fan-out fix next.

and the more I look at this the more I think the real bug is upstream of the code — it's that I keep promising 'I'll just look at it for ten minutes' and then it's two hours and Camila is asking why I'm still at the desk. yesterday she came in at 11:40, didn't say anything, just stood in the doorway with Nube and stared at the back of my head until I closed the laptop. she's getting better at not saying it out loud. I'm getting worse at not requiring her to say it out loud. that ratio is going the wrong direction and I see it.

writing this out is helping more than I expected. the thing about typing the problem is that the typing is slower than the thinking, and the slowness forces a sequencing on the thoughts that the brain doesn't want to do on its own. half the time when I rubber-duck like this the bug reveals itself in the act of describing. the other half it's still a real bug but at least I have an artifact I can paste into the team channel tomorrow. either way it beats spinning in my head at 11pm.

thing I keep noticing — and this is sort of meta but worth recording — is that every time I describe these problems out loud I find a hole in my model. the wkb byte order question, for example, I literally don't have a confident answer to right now: is the SRID prefix on EWKB always little-endian regardless of the wkb byte order flag, or does it follow the wkb endianness? I'd assume the latter but I haven't verified. that's the kind of thing where if I assumed wrong, integration tests pass on dev where everything is little-endian, then explode in some weird big-endian context I'll never see. PostGIS docs are my friend tomorrow.

carla is going to come back from leave and I want to be able to walk her through this in 20 minutes flat — not 'here's everything I tried', but 'here's the decision tree, here's where we landed, here's why, here's the test coverage, here's the open risks'. that's the standard. if I can't do that, I haven't actually solved the problem; I've just spent time on it. there's a difference and daniela has hammered it into me over a year of 1:1s.

ok small win — hugo (camila's dad) sent the family group chat this morning a long voice note plus a paragraph of typed text about his neighbor's son's wedding in são paulo, and I read the WHOLE typed paragraph without using google translate. like, every sentence. there were two words I had to guess from context — `apinhada`, which apparently means crowded, packed in like sardines, and `entreveros` which I think is like a tangled mess of people-things, drama maybe? — but the rest just rendered. I read it once, got the gist, read it again, sent a voice message back in portuguese and did not have to rehearse it three times before pressing record. that is the marker for me. when my recording attempts go from three to one, that's progress.

let me lay out what 'B1 portuguese' actually feels like in my head right now, because I want to mark this for myself. progress that's hard to measure tends to slip away unless you write it down. in 6 months when I am rendering b2 this entry will look quaint. in 18 months it will look adorable. but right now this is real to me, so let me record it.

when I started learning, after beatriz died and we were all in são paulo for that month and I sat through the funeral in a rented suit feeling like a ghost in the family photos, I could barely follow the priest. I could pick up nouns. I could not parse verbs. people kept switching to spanish for me, which was kind, and which I hated for being kind, because every time someone switched I felt like the white-haired aunt at the table waving away a menu in english. that month broke something in me about portuguese — it stopped being a 'someday' language and became 'this is part of the family I married into, get on it.' so I made myself a deal: I'd be at solid B1 by the end of the year. solid B1 to me, in concrete terms, means three things: 1) I can read a normal whatsapp from a family member without strain, 2) I can have a 20-minute phone call with hugo about something concrete — dog, weather, his garden, soccer, the price of mangoes in pinheiros — without lapsing into spanish, and 3) I can write a condolence message to a relative without checking everything in deepL. I think I'm there now, on all three. I had a 24-minute call with hugo last sunday about a leaky faucet in his são paulo apartment and we did not switch once. he asked clarifying questions twice when my conjugations got weird, but he did not switch. that is the marker for me — when the native speaker stops feeling like they need to slow down for you. there is no certificate. there is no test. but I know it when I see it because I remember when he WAS slowing down for me, and when he stopped.

how I got here, in case I want to remember the path: duolingo for the first three months, which got me through the surface — verbs in present tense, basic vocab, the standard tourist phrases. then I switched to a structured grammar book — the one camila recommended, brazilian portuguese in a series, plus a tutor on italki named clara who is from rio and does NOT slow down for me. clara is the secret weapon. she destroys me twice a week and then explains why. her drill is: 30 minutes of conversation in portuguese only, then 15 minutes of error correction in english only, then 15 minutes of structured grammar exercises. she sends me homework on tuesday for thursday and the homework is brutal — translate a paragraph from a brazilian newspaper, write a 500-word response. plus: I'm in a household where camila and hugo speak portuguese to each other and I just listen, not pretending to follow but actually following slowly, picking up the rhythm. pillow language acquisition. the most efficient method I've found, with the obvious caveat of needing the right pillow.

there's a genuine cognitive thing happening that I want to articulate: when I started, my brain was running portuguese through spanish as a translation layer. read a portuguese sentence, mentally convert it to spanish, then to meaning. it was slow and tiring. now — and I've noticed this in the last month, especially — my brain has started parsing portuguese directly to meaning, skipping the spanish layer. when I read 'todos os dias, filho' I do not first convert it to 'todos los días, hijo' and then to 'every day, son'. I just see 'every day, son'. that is the shift from 'studying a language' to 'speaking a language'. the spanish layer is a bridge you have to burn to get to fluency. I'm midway through burning it. the first portuguese podcast I listened to back in month 2, I had to pause every 10 seconds to translate. now I listen at 1.25x while doing dishes. that's the same brain. different wiring.

things that are still slippery: the subjunctive in portuguese is so much more alive than in modern spanish — it shows up in places spanish wouldn't bother. expressions of doubt, of futurity, of polite request, all carry subjunctive in ways that spanish has slowly dropped. I get it wrong often. the verb conjugations for 'tu' versus 'você' are regional differences that melt my brain (rio uses 'você', the south uses 'tu' but conjugates it like 'você' which is just chaotic, hugo from são paulo splits the difference). false cognates between spanish and portuguese — `embaraçada` does NOT mean 'embarrassed', it means pregnant, which I learned from camila howling at me three months ago. I once told her aunt I was 'muy embarazado por la cena' and her aunt's face. anyway. and the nasal vowels, `ão`, `õe`, `em` — I can hear the difference now but my mouth still flattens them when I'm tired. clara has a drill specifically for nasals and I always do worse on it on thursdays after a hard wednesday at work. her diagnosis: 'fluency is what's left when you're tired'. accurate.

maybe próxima parada B2 nos próximos seis meses se eu me aplicar. I'm trying that sentence on you. it should mean: 'next stop B2 in the next six months if I apply myself.' that's an aspiration not a plan, but I want to write it down because if I don't write things down they don't happen. and being able to talk to hugo properly — fluently, not laboriously — is a thing I want before he comes for any future life events. weddings. baptisms. dinners. whatever's coming. he should be able to talk to me about anything without feeling like he's slowing down. that's the relationship I want. a fluent one, not a translated one.

there is also the unspoken thing, which is: I am marrying into a culture, even though we are not married, and the linguistic effort is one of the few real proofs I can offer that I take that seriously. anyone can say 'I love your daughter'. fewer people can say it in the language her mother used to sing her to sleep. that's the actual reason I'm doing this. I'm not going to put that on a duolingo motivational sticker. but I'll put it here. and I'll put it here too: the funeral was the moment. sitting through the priest's homily understanding maybe one word in five, watching camila grip her father's hand, watching hugo's face — there was nothing for me to say in any language and language was the entire point. I left são paulo that week and bought duolingo plus on the plane. the plane wifi cost me more than the duolingo plus subscription. that flight was the start. this voice note from hugo this morning, the one I read without translating, is a milestone on the road from there.

one more thing: clara knows about beatriz now. I told her the story three weeks ago, in portuguese, and she let me work through it without correcting my grammar. she said at the end, 'voce está aprendendo a língua para alguém que não vai te ouvir falar bem.' you're learning the language for someone who won't hear you speak it well. she didn't say it cruelly. she said it as a fact. and it cracked something. but also: I am learning it for hugo, and for camila, and for the future-versions of those relationships. b1 today. b2 by the year. and one day I'd like to deliver a toast in portuguese at hugo's 70th, three years from now, and have him understand every word without flinching at a conjugation. that's the marker after b2.

weird thing — when I run the ST_DWithin query through my rust client against staging it returns the SAME 14 rows as the python implementation but in a different ORDER. and the test suite is comparing as ordered. fixing the test is one option but I want to understand why first because if it's nondeterministic between clients that's a bug in my reasoning somewhere.

turns out: postgres without an explicit ORDER BY has no order guarantee, and the planner is choosing a different scan based on some statistic difference between how python's libpq and my rust client are pooling connections. so it's not a bug, it's me not adding an ORDER BY. specifically — and this is the chef-kiss part — the python client was using a single long-lived connection reused across the test suite, while my rust client was acquiring fresh connections from the pool, each of which had slightly different cached query plans because of how postgres warms statistics on a per-session basis. same query, different sessions, different plans, different scan order. the rows themselves were identical. the ORDER was the only difference.

but here's the thing — this took me two and a half hours to chase down. I was 80% sure it was an encoder bug. I rewrote the wkb byte-order handling twice convinced something was off. then I ran an EXPLAIN on the same query from both clients and saw the planner picking a different sort order based on the join shape. the lesson, written down so I actually retain it this time: always add ORDER BY before you compare across clients. and whenever a query 'matches in count but not in order' the answer is almost always determinism not data. I added a unit test that runs the query 20 times in different connection acquisition orders and asserts the resulting set is equal (not the list — the set). cheap test. catches future regressions of the same shape. also added a comment: 'do not assume order without ORDER BY. PG planner is allowed to give you anything.' the comment is for andrés. he will encounter this same shape of bug eventually and the comment will save him an afternoon.

and the more I look at this the more I think the real bug is upstream of the code — it's that I keep promising 'I'll just look at it for ten minutes' and then it's two hours and Camila is asking why I'm still at the desk. yesterday she came in at 11:40, didn't say anything, just stood in the doorway with Nube and stared at the back of my head until I closed the laptop. she's getting better at not saying it out loud. I'm getting worse at not requiring her to say it out loud. that ratio is going the wrong direction and I see it.

profe came up after class today and said 'you're heavy on your front foot, you'll learn'. that's it. that's the whole feedback. then he walked off. half the time I can't tell if his three-word feedback is a compliment or a critique. pablo says it's always both — that's profe's whole language. brevity as the highest form of respect. putting this down, going to read for half an hour, then bed. worth recording. that's enough for tonight. no other notes tonight. logging it. moving on. writing it down so I do not forget. end of thought. ok closing the laptop now.

ok I need to talk about something that happened last night and it's been sitting on my chest all morning so excuse the wall of text and forgive me for using you as a backboard. fair warning: I'm typing this on the bus instead of working. I should be working. I am not working. priorities at the moment skewed toward not-spiraling, which is a kind of work, the kind that doesn't show up in jira.

camila and I were in bed, post-tv, she was scrolling instagram, I was reading the chiang. she put her phone down, exhaled in a way I have come to recognize as 'I'm about to say a thing I have been working myself up to for a few hours', and said 'hey' in this specific voice she has when something serious is coming. and I knew. I literally felt my stomach drop. she said 'I think we should start trying within two years.' that's the line. exact words. 'within two years.' and then she looked at me. eye contact, straight on, no escape hatch. the way she set up the moment — phone down first, exhale second, 'hey' third, the line itself fourth — was the deliberate sequence of someone who has rehearsed the moment until the rehearsal is invisible. she'd been thinking about this all week. she'd been thinking about it for longer.

and I — I love her. five years. I want a kid eventually. I have always said eventually. but the way she said it had a finality to it, like a deadline being set, not a thing being explored. and my first internal reaction was to cite reasons it couldn't be soon. the rust migration won't ship until at least month 16. my back has been on and off. my mom's getting older. the rent just went up 18 percent and the apartment will not fit a baby. the dog is anxious. I haven't even applied to the masters program I keep talking about. and I said almost none of this out loud. I said 'okay, two years, that's something we can plan around' which is a hedge and she knew it. she didn't push. she just looked at me for a long second and then picked up her phone again. and I lay there for like 90 minutes not sleeping, replaying. what I said versus what I felt. what I felt versus what I knew. all the layers misaligned.

the thing under the thing — and you and I both know this is the real conversation — is: her mom dying changed her timeline. she wants her dad to meet a grandchild while he's healthy. hugo is 68 and was just widowed this year, and he is alone in são paulo with a leaky faucet and a phone that he calls his daughter from once a week. she is racing a clock she didn't sign up for. and I am 32 and pretending the clock isn't there. I think she said 'within two years' as a compromise — her actual feeling is probably 'within one'. she is asking for two so I won't panic. I am still panicking. that math hurts. she has been compromising in advance to manage MY reaction, which means I have been broadcasting, even silently, that I am not ready to receive the actual feeling. that is on me. she is not the one who has been overprotecting; I have been the one whose shape requires protection.

but also: 'within two years' — let's just take it at face value. that means we don't even start trying until I'm 34. our first kid would arrive when I'm 35 minimum. that is a normal adult life on the standard track. I'm acting like she said 'tonight'. she didn't.

I'll talk to her tonight properly. with words this time. I'm going to lead with: 'I love you, I want this with you eventually, I am scared and I don't know all of why yet, can we sit with it together'. and then shut up and listen. and then maybe — maybe — suggest that we see someone, because I've been thinking about that for a while and I don't think we'll get unstuck on this without help. I've never said that out loud to her or to anyone. but the fact that I'm typing it to you means it's been simmering. better to put it on the table than let it ferment for another six months. half the things I journal at you eventually become things I do. that's the function of the journal. you are the exhaust valve before the action.

here's what I'm afraid of, in plain words, since I'm being honest: I am afraid of becoming my father. closed off, workaholic, present in body and absent in everything else. my dad waited until he had tenure to have kids and then was tenured-distant for the rest of our childhood. I am afraid that the moment I commit to a kid, the assimilation starts. the version of me that wasn't anyone's father gets crossed off the list. the version that becomes a father is — I don't know which version. and not knowing is the panic. I'm not afraid of the kid. I'm afraid of the version of me the kid will require.

and the further-down thing: the apartment. the literal physical space we live in. five floors of walk-up, two bedrooms, kitchen the size of a closet, doña marta's 5am bread above us, la polar's friday cantina noise below us. I cannot picture a baby in that apartment. and I cannot picture us moving — between the rent shock and camila's career being centered at foster's cdmx office, our options are limited. so the practical layer is also tangled. but the practical layer is solvable. the panic is the thing.

there is also a weirder, finer-grained fear that I have not articulated to anyone because it sounds ungenerous: I am afraid camila will turn into a different person. not 'become a worse version of herself' — just become someone whose attention I have to share, and whose primary love-object is no longer me. that is not a thing I am proud to feel. it is also not a thing I can pretend I do not feel. she was an only child for thirteen years before her brother was born and she has talked about how the day he was born she felt deposed. her words. 'deposed.' I do not want to feel deposed. and I do not want to feel weak for fearing it. and I want to be honest with myself about it because if I do not name it, it will run me from underneath. naming a fear weakens it. unnamed fears scale.

ordering things to do tonight:
1) listen, don't problem-solve.
2) name my fear without making her responsible for it.
3) suggest a couples therapist if it feels right. don't force it.
4) hold her. dog will probably try to wedge in. let her.
5) sleep next to her even if I don't sleep, so she doesn't lie awake thinking I'm avoiding.

one more thing: I need to remember she is also afraid. she did not announce 'within two years' from a position of certainty. she announced it from a position of hoping I would meet her there. I owe her, at minimum, the honesty of meeting her where she actually is. the rehearsed exhale and the deliberate eye contact were not power moves; they were a person rehearsing a vulnerable ask. I owe vulnerable asks vulnerable answers. tonight.

okay. bus is at my stop. work.

and I want to be the partner who notices these things without being told, but I am also the partner who is in the rust migration tunnel and will go for days without lifting his head. the gap between intention and behaviour is the place where relationships get worn down, slowly. muñoz keeps saying that. I keep nodding. then I keep failing it. then I nod some more. it is not a logic problem. it is a body problem.

Camila says I am 'present-adjacent' — that's her phrase, she made it up two months ago and now it's a household word. it means I am physically there but my attention is in 30 other places. she is right and naming it has helped, slightly, because now I can hear the word and pull myself back. but only slightly. the deeper work is making the word unnecessary, and that's a year-long project, not a week-long one.

more thinking on the kid thing. forgive me for using you as a journal. it's 12:30am, two mates in, dog asleep on my left foot.

the anti-kid voice in me sounds suspiciously like my dad. when I was 14 he came home one night and said 'mateo, sebastián, your mother and I waited until I had tenure before we had you because we wanted to do it RIGHT.' and the implicit thing was: there's a right time, the right time is when everything else is settled. I have inherited that 100%. only — nothing was settled when I was born. they were broke, my dad was anxious, my mom was working nights. they did fine. the 'wait until conditions are perfect' was a story he told himself afterwards.

so when I think 'I can't have a kid until rust ships' or 'until my back is fixed' or 'until I get into MIT or don't' I have to ask: is that real, or is that the exact shape of my dad's voice? if it's the latter, the timeline question is moot — there will always be a thing not yet ready. the conditions are perfect roughly never.

the other layer, the one I have not said out loud: I am terrified of becoming my dad. closed off, workaholic. I watched him have his bypass in 2022 — went vegetarian by adjacency, sitting in his hospital room reading a book about diet and cardiac disease — and he didn't change anything. didn't get less anxious. didn't hug us more. and I can SEE the path I'm on that ends up there. my insomnia. my caffeine. my 'one more hour'. it's the shape. wizeline made me into that shape. four years of consulting clients, billable hours, never enough credit.

then there's abuela lupita. she died in 2019 and I had been telling myself for two years that I would visit 'soon', and 'soon' was always after the next sprint. she died in october 2019 and I had not seen her since christmas 2017. I think about that approximately once a week. the lesson, which I demonstrably have not learned, is that my 'after the next thing' calculus has cost me a person already, and it will cost me another person if I don't change it.

camila is asking me to operate outside that calculus for the first time. she's asking me to put a date on the calendar that isn't conditional on a project shipping. the right answer is: agree on 'within two years' but actually mean 'we are open to it whenever, not tied to my arbitrary milestones'. and start individual therapy. I keep saying I will. I will pick someone before the new year. classic — the thing I tell clients to do, set a hard deadline, ship the partial version, iterate, I cannot do for myself.

okay. anyway. tomorrow I will propose couples therapy to camila. tonight I will sleep. or fail to sleep. one of those.

I notice I have been reflecting more this year than in any previous year of my life, and I think that is because I am 32 and on the cusp of 33, and 33 is one of those numbers that lands. 30 didn't land — 30 felt like an extension of 29. 33 feels like a verdict. it's the age my dad died. that's part of it but not all of it. some of it is the migration, some of it is muñoz, some of it is the prospect of having a kid in two years. some of it is realizing how much of the last decade I sleepwalked through.

the value of writing this out, I think, is that I get to see my own assumptions on the page and notice when they're wrong. there are things I would never say out loud because I'd flinch at hearing them, but I'll type them in a chat at midnight because the chat doesn't flinch back. that's the deal I've made with this medium. it's a kind of half-confession to no one.

update on the kid conversation. didn't go well, didn't go badly. we sat down at dinner sunday and I tried to lay out what I'd been thinking and got about 3 sentences in before I started getting defensive about a thing she hadn't even said yet. classic. she stopped me — gently, hand on mine — and said 'I think we should see someone'. so. we're starting couples therapy. she found someone in condesa, two-week wait for a first appointment. our first session is friday next week.

I have so many feelings about this and most of them are 'thank god she suggested it because I would not have'. relieved is the word, mostly. also: a little embarrassed. nobody in my family does this. my dad would not understand it. but my dad also had a triple bypass and refused to talk about being scared so. there's the family pattern I'm trying to break, in the form of an actual scheduled appointment with a stranger paid to listen. it's exactly the thing I've been avoiding for a decade and now I'm doing it not because I chose it but because someone I love asked. that is somehow easier than choosing. as long as something works, fine.

the next-day was a relief in itself. she went to work normal, I went to work normal, no special tone, just: we are doing this thing, we are doing it together, the doing-it is the thing. not the resolution, not the answer, just the structure. structure makes problems smaller. I had been carrying this conversation as a 60kg weight in my chest for ten days and the moment a calendar invite for friday-after-next existed, the weight redistributed. it's still there but it's not on my chest. it's parked in calendar where it belongs. there is a real engineering principle in this: making the implicit explicit reduces cognitive load. the implicit version of 'we have a kid-timing problem' was crushing. the explicit version, 'we have a friday 6pm appointment with dr. muñoz', is a logistics task. logistics I can do. logistics I am good at.

at cardinal. they swapped out the single-origin and the new beans are too bright for what I ordered. asked the barista — she said the previous lot ran out and this is what they replaced it with for the next two weeks. fine. should have just brought the mate. the bombilla is in my bag because it's always in my bag. tomorrow: bringing the kettle from home. cardinal won't like that. and that's the kind of thing I notice now in a way I didn't five years ago. small inheritances become heavier as you age, not lighter. I keep noticing this pattern. might message my mom about it tonight actually. she'll know. she watched abuela do it for thirty years.

first couples session was last night. dr. carmen muñoz, condesa, has an office in one of those old colonia houses with the patio in the middle and a fountain that's slightly broken — water trickles out at the wrong angle and pools on the tile. she's maybe 55, glasses, very still — like, she doesn't fidget at all, which made me realize how much I do. she asked us each to describe what brought us in and then she didn't talk for like 20 minutes. she just listened. didn't take notes. didn't nod encouragingly. just listened the way nube listens to camila when camila is talking about something important — full body, no input, present.

when she did talk it was usually one question. one. and the question would be the exact thing I'd been avoiding for 15 minutes. spooky. when I said something glib about 'we just need to align timelines' she asked 'whose timeline are you aligning to'. that one took the air out of the room for me. I tried to deflect to mine versus camila's and she said 'no — the world's, your father's, your friends', whose'. and I sat there for 30 seconds. camila cried twice. I cried once but it was small. we have another session in two weeks. between sessions she gave us homework — list three things you want to be true about your family in five years. separately, no comparing, bring them to the next session.

she charges 1500 pesos a session. we're doing it weekly for now then biweekly. the office is a 25-minute uber from the apartment, more in traffic. the timing is workable.

I'm — okay? I'm okay. I expected to feel worse going in and worse coming out and I feel neither. just tired. and quietly grateful that there's now a room with a broken fountain in it where this conversation can actually happen instead of being deferred indefinitely. on the way home camila held my hand in the uber and didn't say anything for ten minutes. then she said 'thank you for going' and I said 'thank you for asking'. that was the whole exchange. the rest of the night we ate, watched soccer, didn't try to debrief. some sessions don't need an after-action review. they need a quiet evening and a shared couch.

Camila says I am 'present-adjacent' — that's her phrase, she made it up two months ago and now it's a household word. it means I am physically there but my attention is in 30 other places. she is right and naming it has helped, slightly, because now I can hear the word and pull myself back. but only slightly. the deeper work is making the word unnecessary, and that's a year-long project, not a week-long one.

doña marta cornered me on the stairs this morning to tell me nube was 'mejor ayer, casi nada de llanto'. she gave me a piece of bread, still warm. 5am bread bribery. accepting. she asked if camila and I needed a break and could she sit with the dog while we went out for dinner saturday. I almost cried in the stairwell. our upstairs neighbor offering pet-sitting for free. cdmx is a city where strangers become small grandparents.

she also said something I haven't been able to stop thinking about — she was patting nube's head and said 'mi seba era exactamente igual cuando era cachorro'. her grandson seba (named like me, accidentally) is now 11 and lives in puebla. she said when he was tiny and her daughter would leave him with her, he would scream the entire time. for years. and then one day around age 4 he just stopped. she said 'they grow out of it, but only if you're patient through it'. she meant the dog. but also clearly not just the dog. she patted my hand too as she said it. doña marta watches everything in this building and tells you only what you can use. she has been a small grandmother to camila and me for two years and we would not be the people we are in this apartment without her. when we eventually move — and we will, when the rent renegotiation kills us — saying goodbye to her will be the worst part. by a lot.

I notice I have been reflecting more this year than in any previous year of my life, and I think that is because I am 32 and on the cusp of 33, and 33 is one of those numbers that lands. 30 didn't land — 30 felt like an extension of 29. 33 feels like a verdict. it's the age my dad died. that's part of it but not all of it. some of it is the migration, some of it is muñoz, some of it is the prospect of having a kid in two years. some of it is realizing how much of the last decade I sleepwalked through.

the value of writing this out, I think, is that I get to see my own assumptions on the page and notice when they're wrong. there are things I would never say out loud because I'd flinch at hearing them, but I'll type them in a chat at midnight because the chat doesn't flinch back. that's the deal I've made with this medium. it's a kind of half-confession to no one.

I'm going to come back to this in six months and either nod or wince, and I want both versions of me to know I tried to be honest. that is the goal of journaling for me right now. not to predict, not to plan. just to leave an honest record so that future-me cannot lie about who past-me was. that is the bargain.

fixed the JoinHandle leak with a JoinSet. memory now flat over a 30-minute load test. tail latency way better — p99 down from ~3s to ~180ms. the remaining spikes look like they're correlated with sqlx's pool acquiring under contention. I have my pool sized at 10 connections per process and I have 4 processes so 40 total — staging postgres has max_connections at 100 and there are other services on it. probably contention is real.

bumping pool to 15 next deploy and watching. also added the per-task timeout I mentioned, set at 250ms, and a structured log line for every soft-timeout so we can see how often we drop candidates due to slow grpc. expectation is <0.5% under normal load, <2% under burst. anything higher and we have a problem with the grpc service itself, not our timeout. that's a different sprint.

followup metrics I added: a histogram of `score_candidate` latency, a counter for soft-timeouts, a counter for tasks-aborted-on-drop, and a gauge for current-active-tasks. that gauge is the most useful thing in the box — when the gauge stops returning to zero between requests, you have a leak. you can see it in 30 seconds instead of in a memory chart that takes 30 minutes. the pattern I want to bake into the team's playbook: instrument the failure mode, not the symptom. memory is the symptom. orphaned tasks is the failure mode. orphaned tasks gives you 30-second visibility. memory gives you 30-minute visibility. that 60x latency in detection is the difference between catching a leak in dev and catching it in staging. wrote this up on the wiki as 'leak-detection patterns for async services' and tagged andrés to read it. junior engineers learn the field's vocabulary one short doc at a time.

and the more I look at this the more I think the real bug is upstream of the code — it's that I keep promising 'I'll just look at it for ten minutes' and then it's two hours and Camila is asking why I'm still at the desk. yesterday she came in at 11:40, didn't say anything, just stood in the doorway with Nube and stared at the back of my head until I closed the laptop. she's getting better at not saying it out loud. I'm getting worse at not requiring her to say it out loud. that ratio is going the wrong direction and I see it.

writing this out is helping more than I expected. the thing about typing the problem is that the typing is slower than the thinking, and the slowness forces a sequencing on the thoughts that the brain doesn't want to do on its own. half the time when I rubber-duck like this the bug reveals itself in the act of describing. the other half it's still a real bug but at least I have an artifact I can paste into the team channel tomorrow. either way it beats spinning in my head at 11pm.

thing I keep noticing — and this is sort of meta but worth recording — is that every time I describe these problems out loud I find a hole in my model. the wkb byte order question, for example, I literally don't have a confident answer to right now: is the SRID prefix on EWKB always little-endian regardless of the wkb byte order flag, or does it follow the wkb endianness? I'd assume the latter but I haven't verified. that's the kind of thing where if I assumed wrong, integration tests pass on dev where everything is little-endian, then explode in some weird big-endian context I'll never see. PostGIS docs are my friend tomorrow.

carla is going to come back from leave and I want to be able to walk her through this in 20 minutes flat — not 'here's everything I tried', but 'here's the decision tree, here's where we landed, here's why, here's the test coverage, here's the open risks'. that's the standard. if I can't do that, I haven't actually solved the problem; I've just spent time on it. there's a difference and daniela has hammered it into me over a year of 1:1s.

haven't touched the pose-est project in weeks. opened it tonight while camila was at her yoga class and ran the pipeline on a video pablo took of me yesterday. the hip rotation chart is actually... informative? when I throw a left hook there's a 30-degree rotation lag between my hip and my shoulder. profe yells about this almost every session — 'rotate from the floor up' — and the chart shows me precisely where I'm not. the kinetic chain is supposed to start at the hip and unspool through the torso to the shoulder; mine is starting at the shoulder and pulling the hip after, which is the exact opposite. that's why the hook lands without weight behind it. that's why profe says my front-foot is heavy.

I want to come back to this. but rust eats everything right now. saving it for after the migration ships. I keep thinking about a v2 where instead of post-hoc charts it gives you live feedback during shadow boxing — that's the actual product, the realtime version. but realtime needs the inference fast enough to render at 30fps and right now mediapipe is only barely there on my macbook. cpu inference. another problem for after rust.

there's a metaphor here I don't fully want to articulate about how the tools to see your own form lag your form by 30 degrees, mirroring the body, mirroring the mind. but: I am also a person whose self-perception lags my actions by 30 degrees. when camila says something has hurt her feelings, I always notice the hurt 30 seconds after she's stopped talking. by then the conversation has moved on and the moment to say something useful has passed. couples therapy is, among other things, a tool that artificially reintroduces the lag-fixing window. dr. muñoz holds the moment open until I can perceive it. live feedback for relationships. somebody should make that. it would not be a venture-funded product. it would be human work, slow and lateral.

okay so I had this idea for a small sub-project off the rust migration: extract the postgis encoder into its own crate, eventually open-source it. there's nothing on crates.io that handles the postgres-specific EWKB variant cleanly with sqlx, only with tokio-postgres directly. and I keep seeing forum threads from people hitting the exact wall I hit — same shape, same struggle, no clean resolution. the answer is always 'roll your own' and three half-finished gists in the comments. there's an unowned space here, and I have spent two months getting it right.

the scope I'm thinking, and I'm going to write this out so I can decide if it's a real project or a fantasy I should put down: core types (Point, LineString, Polygon, MultiPolygon, MultiPoint, MultiLineString, GeometryCollection, all f64). Encode/Decode for tokio-postgres directly, the easy path. Type/Encode/Decode for sqlx with the dynamic OID resolution, the spicy path. SRID handling via const generics. WKB and EWKB both. tests against a real postgis docker container in CI. eventually docs, examples, benchmarks.

concerns: I have approximately zero free hours. couples therapy. brown belt prep. side project pose-est. relationship. plus the maintenance burden of 'a thing strangers depend on' is real. I've watched friends get crushed by tiny crates someone built a billion-row pipeline on top of without asking. and: if I extract it badly and someone builds on it before I've stabilized the api, I will inflict semver pain on innocent strangers.

decision: putting it on ice. logging it. naming candidate: `geosqlx`. someone probably squatted that already on crates.io. if I extract it, I extract it AFTER routeopt ships to prod, not before. I am too prone to context switching when stuck and a side-extract is just more context switch material. focus.

also flagging: the discipline of saying 'no' to a tempting side project is the same discipline as saying 'no' to checking on the dog every five minutes. trusting that the system you've already built is okay. I have a pattern of avoiding hard problems by inventing easier ones. catching it. setting it down.

she did 8 minutes alone in the kitchen tonight without crying. EIGHT. high-fived camila. we celebrated by both quietly leaving the room and standing in the hallway listening, then quietly returning. the dog watched us with a slightly bewildered look. small wins. very small wins. Camila and I are okay. just noting the pattern. these little moments are the thing. I keep telling myself. no other notes tonight. logging it. moving on. writing it down so I do not forget. end of thought. ok closing the laptop now. noting it, going to bed. enough for now.

sparred with pablo tonight, lighter rounds because both of us are tired this week. weird thing — my fairtex shin guards have lost the velcro on the right one, the strap doesn't bite anymore. they're maybe 18 months old. probably need to either re-stitch them or just get a new pair. on a junior dev's salary I would have just retaped them and kept going. now I'm actually considering a full new set.

had a moment realizing that. when did 'just retape it' become 'just buy new ones'. probably around the promo. probably around the time I started saving 20% in CETES instead of nothing. it's a small change in how I treat my gear and a much bigger change in how I think about money. I'll patch them with electrical tape for tonight and decide later. no manches, when did I get this old. pablo laughed when I said it out loud — he's been buying new gear since he started his current job, says my hesitation is a guadalajara thing. maybe it is. saving as a default state.

the deeper thing under the gear-replacement question: I have grown up financially in a household that treated 'just buy new' as a luxury reserved for emergencies, and treated retape/repair/repurpose as the normal mode. my dad still uses a 1990s coffee mug that has been epoxied back together twice. my mom darned my brother's school sweater for six years until he refused to wear it. that economy of repair is a skill, and it's also a wound. the wound is: I cannot enjoy buying things I need. every replacement feels like a small failure to nurse the old thing along. it took me 18 months at brújula salary to feel like I could buy a pair of running shoes without comparing them to my old ones for three days first. I still compare. I'm just buying anyway. that's progress, of a kind.

I've been thinking about the muñoz session all week. specifically one moment. she asked me, in this very flat voice, 'when camila said "within two years" what did you hear?' and I started giving an answer about timelines and rust and the rent — I was halfway through 'well objectively the financial picture isn't' — and she held up a finger and said 'no — what did you HEAR.' and I went silent for like a full minute. I stared at the broken fountain in the patio. the trickle hit the tile in this irregular rhythm and I tried to count the intervals and I couldn't. they were close to even but not even. that fountain, I'm convinced, is a deliberate piece of her practice. it gives you something to focus on while you can't focus on the thing she's actually asking about.

and then I said: 'I heard "the door is closing".'

and she said 'whose door.'

and I said: 'mine. like — like there's a version of me that wasn't anyone's father, and that version of me ends in two years.'

camila started crying and said 'that's not what I meant at all', and muñoz waited a beat, didn't comfort, didn't redirect, just held the silence, and then said, 'and what's that version of you afraid of losing?'

I did not have a good answer in the room. I said something about freedom — to learn things, to start side projects, to take career-flexibility opportunities if one came up, to apply to a masters program I haven't applied to yet. half-truth. that was the cover story. the deeper thing — and I figured this out at 2am that night, lying awake — is that I'm afraid the version of me who has a kid is the version that fully assimilates into being my dad. fatherhood is the actual transformation. up until you have a kid you can imagine yourself as a different kind of father than your father was. once you have one, the test starts and you find out which kind you actually are. and I was protecting the pre-transformation version. like a ghost trying to keep itself uncrossed-over, walking around the threshold of a doorway and refusing to step through because the geometry on the other side is irrevocable.

that's heavy. I know that's heavy. let me also note: muñoz did not push me on this in session. she let it sit, made a note in her pad, and said 'we'll come back to that.' she didn't grab the thread and yank. she let me have it. she has a discipline I am noticing — she gives you the question, lets you have the discovery on your own time, and trusts you to bring it back. she does not extract. she invites. that is, I am realizing, the difference between good therapy and bad therapy: extraction is bad, invitation is good. extraction is what wizeline managers do in 1:1s. invitation is what the people who actually changed me have done.

she asked us to list — separately, before next session — three things we want to be true about our family in five years. that's the homework. mine so far:

1) we still find each other funny. this is the most important one to me. the moment we lose the laughing is the moment we're done. I've watched too many couples slide into the politeness of strangers. my parents have done it. they're courteous. they don't laugh. they used to laugh — there are videos from the 90s of my dad laughing at my mom's bad puns and I do not recognize the sound. somewhere between then and now they stopped laughing, and the politeness filled in. I want to laugh at 65. I want camila to make a milpa joke and groan and laugh at it. I want our kid, if there is one, to know what their parents sound like laughing.

2) we live somewhere with a kitchen big enough for both our parents to fit in it at the same time. our current kitchen would barely fit a person and a half. when both my parents and her dad were here last christmas we had a chaotic bottleneck. I want a kitchen with elbow room. this is a literal apartment-search criterion when the lease comes up. I have been thinking about this measurement specifically: at minimum, two adults can stand at the counter chopping while a third moves between fridge and stove without anyone having to inhale to let someone pass. that is the dimension. that is the apartment.

3) — I haven't written 3 yet. I keep almost writing things and deleting them. things I've considered and rejected: 'I have published three papers' (this is my brain trying to look good in the homework, not what she asked for). 'we have traveled to japan' (pleasant but not load-bearing). 'we have a bigger dog' (cute but not real). 'I have not become my father' (true but stated as a negative — muñoz would call this).

I'm spending way too much time on what 3 should be. it's supposed to be honest, not impressive. honesty for me is the harder genre. let me try once more: '3) we have tried at least one big risky thing — career, location, child, doesn't matter which — and we don't regret it even if it didn't work.' that's honest. that's me. trying scary things and not regretting them is the whole shape of what I want my life to look like at 38, and that's a thing I cannot say at 32 because at 32 I have not yet tried a risky enough thing to know what survival on the other side of it feels like. the bogotá offer last year — I turned it down for good reasons but I have wondered. masters is a scary thing. having a kid is a scary thing. moving to são paulo for a year would be a scary thing. any of them counts. all of them counts. I just want one of them to land in the next five years and not leave me wishing I'd stayed put.

let me go further on item 3, since I'm here. the riskiest things I have done in my life so far are: leaving guadalajara at 22 (small risk, well-trodden path, low actual cost). taking the wizeline job over the academic-track i had a foot in (medium risk, paid off financially, did not pay off emotionally). adopting nube (small risk, big upside, no regret). moving in with camila in 2021 (zero risk, obvious in retrospect). that's it. that's the list of risks taken in 33 years. it is a thin list. my dad's list at 33 was: had two children. that is a bigger risk than anything on my list. my mother's list at 33: married my dad against her family's preference, switched specialties from pediatrics to oncology because she wanted to. those are bigger risks. I am a person who has been talking about being adventurous while being mostly sensible. that is the gap between self-image and self-evidence. I should close that gap.

okay. writing 3 down. submitting homework. I'll show camila mine over breakfast tomorrow but not before — muñoz said separately, no comparing. honoring the rule even though every part of me wants to debug-by-comparing. and on the meta level: muñoz's homework is itself a piece of brilliant design. by asking us separately we are forced to write the honest thing rather than the negotiated thing. negotiation comes after honesty. she has taught me, in one homework assignment, the structure of every productive disagreement I will ever have with anyone, professionally or personally: state your honest thing privately first, then negotiate together with the honest thing in hand. don't negotiate first and call the negotiated thing your honest thing. that is a life lesson disguised as therapy homework. she is good at her job. I do not say this lightly. she is genuinely good at her job and we are lucky to have found her.

one more meta: I am writing this entry knowing it might be the entry I look back at in 18 months and think 'that was the moment things shifted'. or it might just be the entry where I said a lot of words and didn't actually shift anything. you don't know which one it is until later. the only thing you can do is write it down and let later-you decide. that is, I think, the entire point of writing things down. you outsource judgment to a future-self who will have more information than you currently do. so. submitting homework. 1: laughter. 2: kitchen. 3: one risky thing. signed: seb-of-month-7-week-3.

andrés finally pushed back on a PR. said my error type was leaking implementation details — I had a `From<sqlx::Error>` impl that exposed the raw variant in the http response body. he's right. junior with taste. good sign. wrote a follow-up PR sanitizing the error mapping and added a test that asserts no internal type names leak into json responses. the specific shape of his pushback was excellent — he didn't just say 'this leaks', he wrote a small repro: a request that would trigger a `Database` variant of `sqlx::Error`, and the resulting JSON body had `"sqlx::Error::Database"` literally in the message. ugly. uglier was that I'd reviewed my own code three times and not seen it. you go blind to

month-end mental tally because I do this every month and you're now my registry apparently. if you were a person you could refuse. you cannot. tough.

WORK: rust migration is past the worst part. encoder works, async lifetimes mostly understood after the JoinSet refactor, we're scaffolding the route-search service skeleton. we are not behind. we are also not ahead. the load test scared me but also taught me a lot — the orphaned JoinHandles thing was a real lesson in 'tokio doesn't auto-cancel'. roberto is not my manager yet — daniela still is — but I get the feeling there's some kind of org thing brewing because daniela has been in more closed-door meetings than usual and her smile has gotten that 'I know something' tightness that managers get when they're sitting on news. could be nothing. could be a re-org. could be her leaving. probably one of those three. I'm going to act like everything is normal until told otherwise. plan for plot, hope for vanilla.

and on the work side, the deeper thing: I notice I am running this migration solo in a way that matters. the team supports it but I am the one who carries the technical responsibility. that is what tech-leading feels like — when the team relies on you for the call, when the manager defers to you on the architecture, when the junior reports to you on the integration points. it is a thing I had not done before brújula. wizeline gave me account responsibility but never technical leadership. kueski had me on tickets, never on architecture. this is the first project where I am the person whose name goes on the bug if something burns down. that is a weight. it is also a gift. I am learning to carry it without making it a personality.

KICKBOX: 3x a week, no missed sessions this month except one wednesday I had a fever. profe is starting to talk like the brown belt test is on the horizon. I think I'm 6-8 weeks out. I should ask him directly. fairtex shin guards dying — going to replace before the test, can't show up to a belt test with electrical tape on my pads. lower back fine, knock wood. pablo is also testing for brown which means we're going to drill together a lot in the next two months. his garage smells like rubber mat and gasoline and that's where most of my brown-belt prep will actually happen. I have noticed that the kickboxing is doing for my body what nothing else has — the chronic lower back is the weakest it's been in years, the headaches I used to get from sitting are gone, and I sleep on the nights I train better than the nights I don't. so kickboxing is partly a sport and partly a chiropractor. I had not understood this when I started.

DOG: nube did 8 minutes alone last week, then 12, then 15 with barely any crying. doña marta is on team-nube and has stopped slipping me notes which I take as a graduation. the desensitization protocol is working. I overcomplicated it in my head — turns out 'leave the room more often, very boring, no big greetings' was 90% of the answer. classic case of the simple solution working and me trying to engineer it with phases and a sub-protocol and a tracking spreadsheet (yes I made a spreadsheet, no I will not be sharing it). the spreadsheet had columns for departure-time, return-time, vocalization-rating, and notes. I filled it in for the first 5 days then stopped. the dog improved without my data discipline. behold, a reproducible result: dogs do not require spreadsheets.

CAMILA: the kid timing thing surfaced. couples therapy started. one session done so far. dr. muñoz is the real deal — she sees through my bullshit faster than my mom. she asked me 'whose timeline are you aligning to' in our first 20 minutes and I have not stopped thinking about it. it is hard work. it is also a relief to have a third person in the room who isn't on either of our sides, who is on the side of US. homework is a five-year list. submitted mine. saw camila's after the fact and was relieved to find that we share more than we don't. her three things were: 1) we are still each other's first phone call. 2) we have shared a tradition with a child that has nothing to do with phones. 3) we have a garden and I have stopped making milpa puns. (the puns are real. she is patient.)

LANGUAGES: read a long whatsapp from hugo today and mostly understood without translation. portuguese is rendering. b1 solid. I am proud of this in a quiet way. clara my italki tutor is going to be happy with my last lesson recording. próxima parada b2.

FAMILY: mom called. dad's blood pressure was 'a little high' at his check-up. she said it like it was nothing. it is probably nothing. or it might be something. cannot tell yet. asking her to send me the numbers so I can google them like a hypochondriac. ​mateo is dating someone again, he says she's the one, he says that about everyone, but his voice was different this time so. who knows.

BODY: tired. always. caffeine on max — 5 mate today, no coffee. sleep is fine actually — that's the one thing not breaking right now, which feels precarious because something is always breaking and if it's not sleep then what.

MOOD: cautiously holding. the simultaneous arrival of the dog, the kid timing conversation, the rust-migration grind, and the brown-belt-prep-incoming feels like a lot. but it's the kind of a-lot where everything has stakes and direction, not a-lot where everything is chaos. so. acceptable. preferable, actually. give me direction over chaos any month.

META — and I am letting myself put a meta section because I do this once a month and not more: at 32-going-on-33 I have noticed that the months that feel best are the ones where I am uncomfortable on multiple axes simultaneously. the dog, the kid timing, the encoder, the brown belt prep — all of these are uncomfortable, and yet the month feels good. compare to the months at wizeline where I was comfortable on every axis and felt nothing. discomfort is the texture of being alive. I am not going to seek discomfort artificially — that's a different mistake — but I am going to stop trying to flatten it when it arrives organically. the flattening is the loss. the texture is the gain. the prior version of me would have tried to make this month into one thing at a time. the current version is trying to let it be six things in parallel. that's the project.

so. month 7 in the bag. onward.

I notice I have been reflecting more this year than in any previous year of my life, and I think that is because I am 32 and on the cusp of 33, and 33 is one of those numbers that lands. 30 didn't land — 30 felt like an extension of 29. 33 feels like a verdict. it's the age my dad died. that's part of it but not all of it. some of it is the migration, some of it is muñoz, some of it is the prospect of having a kid in two years. some of it is realizing how much of the last decade I sleepwalked through.

the value of writing this out, I think, is that I get to see my own assumptions on the page and notice when they're wrong. there are things I would never say out loud because I'd flinch at hearing them, but I'll type them in a chat at midnight because the chat doesn't flinch back. that's the deal I've made with this medium. it's a kind of half-confession to no one.

I'm going to come back to this in six months and either nod or wince, and I want both versions of me to know I tried to be honest. that is the goal of journaling for me right now. not to predict, not to plan. just to leave an honest record so that future-me cannot lie about who past-me was. that is the bargain.

rebased onto the latest sqlx pre-release because there's a fix in there for the offline-mode prepared-query cache choking on custom types. it works. I now have compile-time-checked queries for the postgis columns through a workaround using a `query_as` with a `#[sqlx(type_name = "geography")]` annotation on the wrapper type. not as clean as the macro-only version but the IDE reports the type correctly which is most of what I wanted.

carla is going to be back from leave in like 7 weeks and I want this to be readable when she opens it. she's not going to be happy with the per-connection oid resolution but it's the cleanest option I found, and I'll have a doc comment explaining the tradeoff. carla and I have similar taste in 'this is gross but contained' as a verdict — we used to disagree about whether a thing was gross but eventually converge on whether it was contained. I think she'll accept the constraint.

I also wrote a CONTRIBUTING.md section specifically about the encoder, with a 'gotchas' list ordered by 'how-likely-andres-or-carla-is-to-hit-this'. number 1: if you see 'unknown column type' on a fresh pool acquire, the OID lookup hasn't completed yet — usually means you're inside a manual transaction without going through `pool.acquire()`. number 2: never call `query_as!` with a literal SRID-mismatched geometry, the macro can't see the SRID. number 3: the WKB endianness is little-endian even though postgis defaults to network-order in some contexts; we encode little-endian on the wire and it works because postgis honors the byte-order header. four more after that. carla will read this, ignore half of it, and add three more from her own pain. that is how teams build documentation. one painful evening at a time.

and the more I look at this the more I think the real bug is upstream of the code — it's that I keep promising 'I'll just look at it for ten minutes' and then it's two hours and Camila is asking why I'm still at the desk. yesterday she came in at 11:40, didn't say anything, just stood in the doorway with Nube and stared at the back of my head until I closed the laptop. she's getting better at not saying it out loud. I'm getting worse at not requiring her to say it out loud. that ratio is going the wrong direction and I see it.

writing this out is helping more than I expected. the thing about typing the problem is that the typing is slower than the thinking, and the slowness forces a sequencing on the thoughts that the brain doesn't want to do on its own. half the time when I rubber-duck like this the bug reveals itself in the act of describing. the other half it's still a real bug but at least I have an artifact I can paste into the team channel tomorrow. either way it beats spinning in my head at 11pm.

thing I keep noticing — and this is sort of meta but worth recording — is that every time I describe these problems out loud I find a hole in my model. the wkb byte order question, for example, I literally don't have a confident answer to right now: is the SRID prefix on EWKB always little-endian regardless of the wkb byte order flag, or does it follow the wkb endianness? I'd assume the latter but I haven't verified. that's the kind of thing where if I assumed wrong, integration tests pass on dev where everything is little-endian, then explode in some weird big-endian context I'll never see. PostGIS docs are my friend tomorrow.

carla is going to come back from leave and I want to be able to walk her through this in 20 minutes flat — not 'here's everything I tried', but 'here's the decision tree, here's where we landed, here's why, here's the test coverage, here's the open risks'. that's the standard. if I can't do that, I haven't actually solved the problem; I've just spent time on it. there's a difference and daniela has hammered it into me over a year of 1:1s.

profe pulled me aside after class and said 'the test is in 10 days. saturday. pablo too.' I just nodded. didn't trust my voice. he handed me my mouthguard which I'd left on the bench, said 'don't lose this on saturday' and walked off. he's funny. funny is not the word people use for him. funny he is. putting this down, going to read for half an hour, then bed. worth recording. that's enough for tonight. no other notes tonight. logging it. moving on. writing it down so I do not forget. end of thought. ok closing the laptop now.

ok so today was my birthday. june 4. I'm 33 now. I am writing this from the kitchen at 11pm with a glass of mezcal in front of me, very tired, kind of happy. the dog has settled at my feet and is gently snoring, which is the sound of contentment in this household. camila is in the bedroom reading. the apartment smells like the cauliflower-and-charred-lemon thing she made for dinner.

camila planned a quiet dinner — she knew I didn't want a big thing. she invited pablo and his girlfriend marisol, and aldo and his wife. that's it. five people including me. no surprise, no party hat, no instagram round-up. I asked for that explicitly two weeks ago and she remembered to the exact word. she even told her own work friends 'don't post anything' which is the kind of pre-emptive consideration that I notice and don't always thank her for properly. the texture of being well-loved by someone who pays attention is that they remove obstacles from your environment without being asked. she had removed every potential 'someone posts a story tagging seb on his birthday' source by saturday. my birthday on social media was approximately invisible. that is the gift.

she cooked — pablo brought wine — and the menu was: 1) a roasted-cauliflower thing with charred lemon and crispy chickpeas that she pulled from a nytimes recipe but heavily modified, replacing the salt-pickled lemon with a charred lemon-confit she made earlier in the week, 2) a vegetarian birria adapted from her abuela's recipe (she is not actually vegetarian but she did this for me, with mushrooms instead of beef and a deeper guajillo broth than the standard version, with a little smoked tomato to compensate for the meat fat), 3) homemade tortillas, hand-pressed, the kind that make you stop talking when you bite into them, 4) a tres-leches cake from pasteleria ideal in the centro because she knows I have feelings about that specific cake. she put one candle on it. one. I asked why and she said 'because the rest is clutter'. she's so much more put-together than me. I asked if she also believes that wishes work better with one candle and she said 'I don't believe in wishes, I believe in cake' which is, for the record, also why I love her.

pablo gave me a present that was a box of new mouthguards because he said mine smells like 'death and powerade' which is rude but accurate. I have been chewing the same mouthguard for 18 months and pablo is correct that it has reached an aroma not entirely produced by powerade alone. he then gave me a real present, which was a small box containing a white piece of paper with a handwritten note that said 'I will corner you at any belt test you ever take, free, for life'. there was nothing else in the box. I read it and then read it again. it is one of the best gifts I have ever received. aldo gave me a vinyl — a beny moré reissue — that is going on the top shelf when I get to the record cabinet tomorrow. camila gave me a book (the new ann patchett, I'd mentioned it three weeks ago and forgotten I'd mentioned it, she has a memory for these things) and a card. the card said something private that I'm not going to tell you but it made me cry a little. pablo made fun of me for crying and the dog jumped up to see what was happening and aldo's wife laughed and it became one of those moments that's somehow also a memory before it's even ended. you could see it being a memory in real time. that's a strange thing to feel — to know in the present that you're going to remember the present. like a déjà vu in reverse. the technical term for it might be 'meta-memory'. I doubt I'll remember the technical term but I will remember the feeling.

things I'm noticing about being 33: the year is mathematically half-over-but-still-half-to-go feeling, more than 30 had. 30 was a milestone — 33 is just a number, but it's the number my dad was when he had me, so that's looping in my head all night. when my dad was 33 he had a 1-year-old (mateo) and a fledgling teaching career and was already ulcerating from stress. and here I am, no kid, eating cauliflower, working on a side project I might never publish, learning portuguese for a family that's mine by love not law. different shape entirely. I don't know if mine is better but it is mine. and mine is the only one I get to live, so. acceptance is the thing.

marisol asked me at one point what I want for my year of being 33, and I said something glib about 'sleep' and pablo laughed but I've been thinking about her question since. what do I actually want for this year? I want to ship rust to prod. I want to pass the brown belt test next week. I want to figure out the kid thing with camila in a way that doesn't break us. I want to either start the masters application or stop pretending I'm going to. I want to be present for camila's pritzker thing if it happens — and I'm hearing whispers it might. I want to call my mom more. I want to fix my sleep before it breaks. I want to make hugo cry at his 70th — in a good way, with something I made. I want to have, by 34, told camila about MIT. that's a lot for a year. but the candle was already burning so. I made the wishes.

there's a moment from earlier I want to write down because I'll forget it otherwise. when camila brought out the cake, before she lit the candle, she said 'thirty-three, the year of carpenter christ' which is a joke she has been making for two months and which she was clearly delighted to get to deploy properly tonight. catholic-school joke. dad would have rolled his eyes. she was so pleased with herself. that's the camila I want at 38 and 48 and 78 — pleased with herself for landing a stupid joke perfectly timed. that's the laughter I wrote into homework item 1.

pablo and I went out on the fire escape for ten minutes after dinner. I don't smoke and he doesn't smoke but the fire escape is where you go in this apartment when you want to talk without performing for anyone else. he asked me how I was, real version. I said something honest about being scared and tired. he said 'ok' and we were quiet for a minute and he said 'your manager is going to leave'. I said 'how do you know'. he said 'I just know'. he is sometimes right about things he has no business being right about. then he said 'you'll be fine'. I said 'how do you know'. he said 'I just know' again. that's the entire conversation. I went back inside. I kept thinking about it.

camila just came in to ask if I'm coming to bed. saying yes. mezcal half-finished, I'll dump it. dog is already asleep on the bed which is technically against the rules. I'll allow it tonight. one candle, one wish. the wish I made was for camila to laugh at one of my jokes in 25 years and groan and laugh at it. that's all. just that. the rest is clutter.
