kid-timing conversation is no longer a closed door. couples therapy with dr. muñoz: two sessions done, one to go before pause. she made us write 'three things true in five years' lists. we read them out. we discovered we'd been hearing each other wrong — she heard 'I want to settle' from me and I heard 'I want a baby' from her and neither of us was actually saying what the other heard. we will probably keep doing this kind of work without her, with check-ins as needed. camila is — she's good. she's in motion. she's not braced for impact the way she was three months ago after her mom died. she has been laughing more this month than she had in the previous six combined. she made a joke at my brown belt celebration on monday that referenced hugo's 'filho' message AND profe's 'la siguiente es la larga' AND a yoga instructor we both can't stand, in a single sentence, perfect timing. it killed. that joke is going to stay with me. it was a measure of how much she has been listening to my parallel lives. she is fluent in the multiple languages I move through. fluency in the partner's contexts is the work of long love.

LANGUAGE:

b1 portuguese is real. hugo wrote me 'filho' last week and I felt it land. I'm going to try b2 by the new year, casually. clara my italki tutor is going to be told this monday so she has time to mock me about it. duolingo is too slow at this stage; I'll switch to a structured thing. native-speaker conversations 2x a week + one writing exercise per week is probably the rough plan. plus continued passive exposure from camila and hugo whatsapping each other in the family group chat.

FAMILY:

dad's blood pressure check came back fine after the recheck. mom's voice less worried. brother mateo is dating someone new — sofía, dental hygienist in tijuana — who he says is 'the one' which he says about everyone. cautiously optimistic. he's 28, time will tell. mom asked when camila and I are visiting guadalajara next; I said before the end of the year and meant it. october probably. november if october is too packed. I want to introduce camila to mom's empanada-making routine, partly because mom has wanted to teach her since they met, partly because of muñoz and the tradition-with-a-child thing — that frame has my brain rewired and now everything kind of looks like 'a tradition that gets passed' to me. it's a useful filter. I am going to call my mom more this month. I have been saying that for a year and I have not done it. this month I commit to once a week minimum, sundays. she will not know that I have committed. she does not need to. I will have committed and she will benefit from the consequences.

BODY:

back fine. sleep fine. caffeine moderate (4 mate, no coffee — coffee triggers something in my chest, mate doesn't, weird). eating okay. weight stable. brown patch nailed me into doing the recovery work properly — stretching after every class, foam rolling 3x a week, drinking water like it's my job. I will not stay this disciplined. I will try.

MOOD:

this is the best month-end report I've written in a year. I'm aware of that. I'm also aware month 9 starts with rent renegotiation, a new manager, and probably some new shoe dropping I can't see yet. but I'm going to take the win for tonight.

brown belt. couples therapy started. portuguese landed. dog calm. rust marching. mom okay. camila in better shape than three months ago. abuela lupita's mate cup right here on the desk where it always is. I light a candle for her on her birthday in november — that's coming up. I should plan ahead this year. one of those quiet rituals I have not let lapse, even though I've lapsed on basically every other piece of catholic habit. abuela was the family's saint, secularly speaking. lighting a candle for her is the religion I retain. I will buy good candles this year. real ones. and I will say her name out loud when I light them, because I have been doing it silently and I think she would have wanted to hear it. my abuela lupita. that's her name. she made the bombilla for me. she taught my mom the lemon trick I still need to learn. she fed half the block. she died alone-ish, not because she had to but because I did not visit. november 8 is her birthday. I am marking it now in the calendar, for the candle, for the name out loud, for the small repair of the years.

signing off month 8.

ok so the brown belt thing is still settling in weirdly. like my brain hasn't caught up. i tested two weeks ago, Profe handed me the belt, half the gym clapped, and ever since i keep catching myself looking at it on the hook by the door and thinking it's not mine. like someone else's belt got into my bag. sounds dumb writing it out. anyway my left low kick is faster now, i don't know why, maybe the test forced me to commit harder. there's a guy at the gym who's been brown for two years and he was watching me drill yesterday and gave me this nod that i'm choosing to interpret as respect and not pity. either way i'll take it. mind you my hip flexors are screaming today, i ate a whole bowl of pasta last night thinking carbs would fix it, did not work, hurts to put on socks this morning. i think the belt grade was harder than i admitted out loud — six rounds, three of them with Profe himself, who at 61 still moves like a ghost. i'm tired in a way that has nothing to do with sleep.

the other thing — and i don't talk about this on the mats because nobody wants to hear it — is the imposter feeling. blue belt felt earned. blue belt felt like 'ok i can throw competent combos, my defense is real, i don't gas in round two anymore'. brown belt feels like a promise i haven't grown into yet. like i was promoted to a job they think i can do but i'm not 100% sure i can do. there are days i drill and feel sharp, and days i drill and feel like a 21-year-old white belt with a fancier scarf. i talked to pablo about this on the way home from monday's class and he said 'wey, ese sentimiento no se va, sólo cambia de color, espera al negro'. which is the most pablo possible response — slightly philosophical, wraps a real piece of advice in a joke about belt colors.

Profe pulled me aside after thursday's class and said 'tu defensa contra patadas medias todavía está blanda, vamos a trabajar eso esta semana'. i nodded. then i went home and watched my own sparring video from the test, the one pablo recorded, and Profe is right — i'm catching everything in the gloves but my hips are square, which means a body kick from the right angle is going to fold me. i'm going to drill that this week. brown belt energy: drill the thing the coach said, don't drill the thing your ego wants.

i also went and re-read my training journal entries from a year ago, when i'd just gotten blue. eleven months ago me wrote 'feeling like the brown belts are on a different plane'. i had to put the notebook down and laugh. brown is just blue with one more year of bruises. there is no plane. there is only more accumulated reps. that's the whole game. note for future seb if i'm ever stupid enough to second-guess this: read the entries from twelve months back. the cabrón you used to be thought brown was unreachable.

logged tonight's drilling: 6 rounds bag work, 4 rounds shadowboxing focused specifically on hip rotation during slip-and-counter sequences, 100 jumping rope skips at the end as cooldown. ate a bowl of arroz con frijoles after, with the new habanero sauce. heavy bag is the cheapest therapist in cdmx. and unlike dr muñoz it doesn't ask me to feel my feelings — it asks me to express them at 80% power across six rounds and then go home tired enough to sleep, which is the other kind of therapy.

a thing i didn't put in the email but i'm logging here: the couples' counselor pause is the second pause we've taken. first one was in march, three sessions and we paused because we were 'ready to apply tools'. lol. we did not apply tools. we just stopped going. i'm aware of the pattern. i don't think we're doing it on purpose. but pause-1 (march) plus pause-2 (now) plus the rent stuff plus the mom stuff plus the la polar stuff is going to compound. i'm flagging it to myself.

roberto sent a 'looking forward to working with all of you' email. zero personality. used the phrase 'aligned execution' twice. daniela's last day is friday. team is doing tacos at el califa. ugh. i'm going. carla can't make it because parental leave but she sent the group chat a voice note saying 'cabrones, llórenle por mí, me debe una michelada cada uno cuando regrese'. that note was the warmest thing in the channel all week. roberto did not reply to it. andrés replied with three crying emojis. i replied with 'hecho'. small data point about who's going to fit and who isn't.

ok i need to write this out somewhere because i can't talk to pablo about it and obviously can't talk to camila about it more than we already have, so you get it. we did the third session with dr muñoz last tuesday and at the end camila said, like really softly, 'i think we should pause for a bit', and dr muñoz did this thing where she didn't react, just nodded, and said 'pausing is also a form of work', which i'm sure is a thing she says to everyone but it landed. so we paused. three sessions and out. i don't even know if 'paused' is a real word for what it is or if it's just the polite version of 'this isn't helping' which i was starting to think too if i'm honest.

the sessions themselves were — i don't know how to describe them. session one was logistics, where we live in our heads, what we want, blah blah, the sort of thing you do at the start so the therapist can map you. dr muñoz spent most of session one asking us how we communicate when we're stressed, and the answer turned out to be 'badly, in different ways' — i go quiet and start fixing things in the apartment, camila gets verbose and asks rhetorical questions. she pointed that out and we both just sort of stared. and then she asked us each to describe a time the other person handled stress 'in a way you admired', and we both went quiet for like 30 full seconds, and i could see camila wanting to say something and not finding it, and i had the same problem, and dr muñoz let the silence run, which was the kind of move that makes you understand why people pay 1500 mxn for an hour with this woman. eventually i said something about how camila handled the funeral, the practical part of it — booking flights, talking to hugo's family, picking the music — with this kind of brutal grace, and i could see camila's face change a little when i said it. and then she said something about how i held her at night during the first week back from são paulo without saying anything because she didn't need words, just a body that didn't move. and that was session one. dr muñoz sent us home. we held hands on the walk to the metro. didn't talk about it.

session two was the kid thing. she wants to start trying within two years. i said i wasn't ready and dr muñoz tried to get me to articulate why and i mostly fumbled it because the real reason is that i'm scared of becoming my dad — workaholic, closed off, a person who shows love by paying for things — and i couldn't say that out loud yet. i said something about timelines and finances and dr muñoz did this thing where she just waited for me to keep going, and the silence got longer and longer until i finally said the dad thing, and camila reached over and squeezed my knee and i thought i was going to cry but i didn't. dr muñoz then asked camila to tell me what she heard, and camila said 'i heard that he's afraid of becoming his father, and that has been on his mind for longer than this conversation has existed', and dr muñoz nodded and said to me 'is that accurate?' and i said yes. and that was the entire content of session two. half an hour to get to one sentence and twenty minutes to digest it.

session three was actually about her mom. about beatriz. about the funeral, the months after, how she felt like she was performing being okay so i wouldn't worry, and how that performance ate her, and how the kid thing is partly that her mom died without seeing her have kids and the math of that is now broken. i sat there and held her hand and felt like an idiot for ever framing the kid conversation as a logistics question. she said something like 'i can't tell you when, but i can tell you i don't want to wait until i'm her age and have the same regret', and the room was very quiet for a while. dr muñoz asked me what i heard and i said 'i heard that the timeline isn't a deadline, it's a fear of running out'. she said 'that's a good summary, sebastián'. i don't think anyone has called me sebastián in a context like that since my mom when i was twelve.

then dr muñoz did the thing she does — she pivoted from the content to the meta. she said 'how does it feel to have heard each other this clearly?' and we both did the awkward laugh you do when a therapist asks you a question that's too true to answer cleanly. camila said 'tired'. i said 'relieved and also unsteady, like i've been told a true thing about myself'. dr muñoz wrote that down, which was unsettling.

so then she said pause, and i agreed too quickly, and i'm worried that 'too quickly' is going to come back as a thing in some future fight. like 'you couldn't even sit through four sessions'. i should have said 'are you sure'. or asked dr muñoz what she recommended. instead i was relieved. that's the part i'm trying to be honest with myself about. i was relieved because the sessions were three hours of being seen and it was exhausting, and i was already in the middle of belt prep and the daniela thing at work and i just wanted to go home and not be a project that needed working on. there's a worldview where therapy is a thing you do for a season and then you graduate. there's another worldview where you're always in it and the only question is whether you've got a current therapist. i was raised in the first worldview. camila was raised somewhere closer to the second. neither of us said that out loud either.

dr muñoz did say something at the end that i keep replaying. she said something like, the goal of pausing isn't to stop, it's to give the question time to ask itself differently. which sounds like one of those phrases you put on instagram with a sunset behind it, but i think i get what she meant. like maybe the question isn't 'do we want kids in two years', maybe it's 'who do we want to be to each other in two years'. those are not the same question and i don't know how to answer the second one cleanly either. she also said — and this one i'm still chewing on — that the 'pause' was a chance for us to practice the things we'd been working on without the safety net of her in the room. that the real test of therapy isn't what happens in the office, it's what happens in the kitchen at 9pm on a wednesday when one of you is tired and the other has had a bad day at work. and i thought, ok i can absolutely apply that frame to debugging too — the test of an engineer isn't what they do in front of the senior reviewer, it's what they do in the diff at 11pm alone. but i'm not going to tell camila i thought that during couples therapy because the metaphor would not land.

camila and i went to the bookstore in condesa after session three, didn't talk about the session at all, bought books, ate ramen at nudo, came home, slept. that night was the first time in a month she fell asleep on me on the couch. and i lay there with nube on my chest and camila's head on my shoulder thinking, ok this is fine, this is more than fine, why does it feel like a question i'm not answering. i kept thinking about her mom. about beatriz. about a wake i barely remember because i was so jet-lagged and so out of my depth. about the way camila looked the morning of the funeral, like she'd shrunk inside her own skin. there's a version of me that handled that month well and a version that didn't, and i still can't tell which one i was.

the other thing dr muñoz said in session three that i didn't write down at the time but is sticking with me a week later: 'sometimes when one partner has experienced a recent loss, the other partner experiences a delayed mourning of their own — for the version of the relationship that existed before the loss'. and i nodded like i understood and now i think i actually do. because there is a before-beatriz camila and an after-beatriz camila and they are not the same person and i miss the before person sometimes and that feels like a betrayal to even think. before-beatriz camila laughed easier. after-beatriz camila laughs but pauses first, like she's checking if she's allowed. before-beatriz camila would dance in the kitchen while we cooked. after-beatriz camila puts on music and stands at the counter and chops vegetables and looks at the wall. she's coming back. it's been eight months. she's mostly back. but there are evenings where she isn't and i'm sitting across the table with a person who is grieving in a register i can't reach, and the only thing i can do is be there and let it not be performative on either side.

the paused-therapy version of this question — 'how do we be a couple when one of us is grieving' — was supposed to keep going. it stopped. i don't know how to feel about that. dr muñoz said the door is open. she said 'whenever you're ready, both of you'. she said it warmly. she meant it. i still feel like we left class early.

anyway. paused. dr muñoz left it open — 'whenever you're ready, both of you, the door is open'. which i appreciated. i don't know when ready is. brown belt was last week and i thought my mind would be clear after but mostly it's just adding to the pile. i'm going to do the thing where i don't decide anything for a few weeks and just see what happens in the kitchen at 9pm on a wednesday when one of us is tired. that's the homework, even if it wasn't assigned. and probably in three months one of us will say 'hey, should we go back?' and the other will say 'yeah' and we'll go. or we won't, and that will mean something else, and we'll deal with it then. i don't have the bandwidth to grieve in advance the version of us that doesn't return to therapy. that's a future-seb problem.

birthday was last week and i forgot to mention. quiet dinner at nudo with camila, pablo dropped by for one beer, then we went home. i'm 33 now. my dad turned 33 the year my brother was born, which is a stupid math i keep doing. camila got me one of those overpriced japanese knives, the kind with the hammered finish, with a little engraving on the handle that says 's.r.' which i'd been low-key wanting since i saw one at a friend's house a year ago. i don't know how she remembered. she always remembers. the card said 'te amo, viejo' which made me laugh because viejo is exactly what 33 feels like in mexico. pablo gave me a bottle of mezcal del maguey vida and a kickboxing patch that says 'old man strength' which he ordered from some guy on instagram. i wore the new brown belt to dinner, which is gym-bro behavior, but camila let me have it. nobody from work texted except daniela who sent a voice note at 11pm that i could tell she'd been drinking, which was the most honest happy birthday i got — she said 'felicidades cabrón, te quiero, te voy a extrañar mucho cuando me vaya'. i listened to it three times. i didn't reply tonight. i'll reply tomorrow. some things you let sit overnight. mom called sunday morning, sang the version of las mañanitas she's been singing me on every birthday since 1993, dad was in the background humming the bass line because he's tone deaf in the most charming possible way. they're driving up for christmas, mom already started the menu. 33. ok.

the other unexpected thing was the day-of, in the morning, before camila woke up, i sat on the balcony with yerba mate watching the light hit the building across the street and felt the particular cold-wind-of-mortality you feel on round-number-adjacent birthdays. 33 isn't round but it's the age my brother was born and the age my mom had her first miscarriage and the age — i'm pretty sure — that my dad started his department-chair gig at the prepa where he eventually retired. those are weights from the family timeline. and i thought about hugo ferreira sr in são paulo, 70 in a few months, widowed. and beatriz dying at 65. and abuela lupita at 86 in 2019. you can stack the family ages and stare at them and basically dr muñoz would say you're avoiding your feelings by doing math. she'd be right. but the math is also real.

and then camila woke up and shuffled into the kitchen and said 'feliz cumpleaños, viejo' with her hair sticking up in the way that, after five years, still makes me forget the math entirely. so. 33 it is. dinner at nudo. truffle ramen for me, miso for her, the chashu pork bun for pablo because pablo always orders the pork bun. one beer each. quiet. came home, watched two episodes of a brazilian show camila is into where the dialogue is too fast for my portuguese and she translates jokes one beat too late, which is itself a small comedy. fell asleep with nube on my chest. that was 33. the math can wait.

side note on roberto: he opened the standup with 'a little reminder that 11:00 means 11:00 and not 11:02' which was directed at no one and at everyone. mateo (the new dev, not my brother) was the obvious target — he joined at 11:01 yesterday. nobody said anything. we all just stared at our screens. the chill of that single sentence rearranged the meeting's whole energy. roberto does not understand that 11:01 vs 11:00 is not the variable that determines whether a 5-person team ships rust migration on time. or he understands and chooses to optimize for compliance theater instead. either way, bad.

logged in my own private brain-dump file at end of day: roberto did three things in standup that none of the rest of us did before he joined. (1) read the screen for thirty seconds at a stretch, in silence, while we waited. (2) used the word 'cadence' twice. (3) said 'i'll noodle on that' once. each of these is a tell that he's role-playing the manager he saw in the saastr keynote videos he watches at 5am.

drilled spinning back kicks for an hour. brown belt energy is do drills you skipped at blue. shins are bricks. ate ramen at nudo after, the truffle one that camila says is gimmick but i love. asleep by 11. Profe stopped me at the door and said 'mañana cardio, te falta gas en el quinto round'. five rounds in. fair. i used to gas at three. progress is non-monotonic, etc. heavy bag tomorrow, hill sprints saturday. shins iced for twenty minutes tonight. low kicks tomorrow are going to hurt to watch.

ok no manches. landlord just sent a whatsapp. five thousand pesos a month. five. as in our rent is going from 28k to 33k starting next month. and the way he wrote it was the worst part — and i'm pasting it because i need to vent it twice — 'estimado seb, después de evaluar la situación inmobiliaria de la zona y el incremento de costos de mantenimiento del edificio, le informo que el nuevo monto de renta a partir del 1 del mes entrante será de $33,000 mxn mensuales. confío en su comprensión.' confío en su comprensión. like we're in a regency novel. like he's not the same guy who took six weeks to fix the boiler last february. i did the math and 5k a month is 60k a year and that's almost what i pay in cetes interest, like the entire passive income line on my spreadsheet just got eaten by this cabrón.

for context for whoever you are: we live on the fifth floor of a 1940s building in roma norte, two bedrooms, no elevator (which i know is unhinged but the apartment has its own laundry and a small balcony with afternoon light and we love it). we've been here almost three years. at 28k it was a stretch but doable, especially after the promo bump put us at a place where we were saving 20% and putting the rest of the cushion in usd index. at 33k the math gets ugly. it's not catastrophic — between camila and me we make plenty — but the principle of it is what's making me grind my teeth. like, you don't get to invoke 'inmobiliaria de la zona' when the inmobiliaria de la zona is full of two-month-old roma norte one-bedrooms going for what we pay for two. and the maintenance line is rich considering the maintenance is doña marta upstairs starting her panaderia at 5am and the cantina downstairs being a war zone every friday and our lock has been wonky since may.

let me actually do the math because the math is the math:

old rent: 28,000 mxn/month → 336,000 mxn/year
new rent: 33,000 mxn/month → 396,000 mxn/year
delta: 60,000 mxn/year

sixty thousand pesos a year is — let me convert in my head — roughly 3,000 usd. so it's 3 grand a year extra. that's one fancy weekend trip. that's one full kickboxing seminar fee. that's two festool routers. that's about 25% of our annual savings rate at the previous numbers. it's not catastrophic. it is also definitely not nothing. and the kicker is that this is a permanent recurring outflow, not a one-time hit. so the npv of this rent increase, at let's say a 5% discount rate over a 5-year horizon, is something like 270k mxn — basically a year and a half of savings vaporized into the wall i'm sitting against right now. fifteen years of compound, at a 7% real return, would have been more like 1.5m mxn of forgone wealth. obviously we're not staying in this apartment for fifteen years and obviously the comparison is artificial. but the brain does the math regardless and the brain doesn't like the math.

then there's the comparable analysis. i actually pulled idealista this morning, while my coffee was still hot, like a true mexican millennial in distress. comparable 2br apartments in roma norte right now, on idealista, that have the same general vibe (high-floor, light, walking distance to nudo and cardinal):

- 32k mxn: 1br with den. so 'comparable' is generous.
- 36k mxn: legitimate 2br but ground floor with metal-grilled windows facing tonalá.
- 38k mxn: gorgeous 2br, balcony, but on calle medellín which is louder than ours.
- 42k mxn: nice 2br, good street, but they want a 'fianza' on top of deposit which is another month upfront.
- 28k mxn: too good to be true, listing has been up for 3 weeks, no photos of the bathroom. i don't trust it.
- 34k mxn: corner apartment colima/orizaba, north-facing windows so light is bad, but otherwise solid. probably the best comp for 'identical to ours minus the light'.
- 31k mxn: on the actual ground floor at insurgentes, no joke they listed the metro stop as a feature. nope.

so 33k for our place is — annoyingly — at market rate or slightly below. which is the part i didn't want to admit. the cabrón is not actually wrong about the inmobiliaria de la zona. roma norte has run away from itself in the last three years. condesa is the same. i could move us to la juárez or to escandón and save 5k easy, but then camila has a 45-minute commute and i lose nudo and cardinal and the whole reason i moved here. i could move us to coyoacán and save 8k but the commute is 90 minutes each way which is a no from camila. i could move us further out to tlalpan or xochimilco and save 12k but at that point we're talking about a different city.

i haven't told camila yet. she's at the foster + partners office until 8 tonight, she had a call with the são paulo office this afternoon (her dad called yesterday, hugo is doing okay i think, but the calls are heavier now), and i don't want to be the person who hits her with rent news on top of that. i'll tell her over dinner. i already know what she's going to say. she's going to say 'we should look at other places' and i'm going to say 'but the light' and we'll spiral into a 90-minute apartment-zillow doomscroll where we look at idealista and inmuebles24 and discover that everything in roma or condesa under 30k is either a closet or facing a freeway, and then we'll close the laptops and order tacos and accept the new rent because the cost of moving with nube and 150 records and a 60kg espresso machine is itself like 40k mxn and a week of our lives.

the other thing — and this is the part i'm trying not to spiral on — is that this is the second time in eighteen months that an external thing has made our lives objectively worse without us getting any input on it. first beatriz dying, which is not the landlord's fault obviously, but it changed our financial picture because hugo visits monthly now and we eat that travel cost. now this. and there's roberto starting next week which is going to make work suck. things are stacking. i can feel them stacking. there's a phrase i learned from one of the chiang stories — 'the difference between knowing and feeling' — and right now i'm in the gap between them, where i know objectively that the rent increase is small as a fraction of household income and i feel like the city is grinding me into mortar.

i'm going to push back on the landlord. i'll write him a polite reply asking for a breakdown of the maintenance increases. i won't get one. but i want it on record that i asked. i also want him to fix the hot water in the master bathroom which has been cycling cold for like ten weeks now and i've been politely ignoring because moving is worse than cold showers, but if he wants 33k he's going to fix the goddamn boiler. and i'll redline the new lease. and i'll keep a paper trail. that's the strategy. paper trail. fight on the boring axis where the cabrón is not strong.

órale. i'm going to make a hot sauce. that's my coping. i bought habaneros at mercado medellín on saturday and they need to be processed today anyway. at least the habaneros don't raise their rent. though if they did i wouldn't put it past them at this point. nothing in this city is fixed-price anymore. the carnitas at my favorite stand by mercado medellín went from 90 to 120 in eighteen months. the ramen at nudo is up 30%. the metro went from 5 to 6 pesos. wages went up like 8%. it's the standard mexican story but the gap between cost-of-living and wage-growth is biting harder this year than last. i'm not the only one feeling it. the difference is most of my friends in cdmx don't have a 33k roma norte rent, so the felt-difference is bigger for me. that's the cost of choosing the lifestyle apartment. i chose it. i'm going to keep paying for it. but i'm allowed to be annoyed about it for a single afternoon.

told camila. she said 'we should look at other places'. called it. ordered tacos from el huequito because that's our 'we're stressed' tacos place. ate them on the floor with nube. she did not get any. i did not budge. camila pulled idealista on her phone while we ate, said 'mira, este en escandón' and i said 'no'. she sighed. she knows. neither of us is moving. but she's allowed to dream-shop for ten minutes before bed, that's the deal. nube ate a stray piece of cilantro that fell on the floor and made a face like betrayal.

roberto's first standup as manager today. it was — fine, on the surface. he asked everyone to share blockers in a particular order, by 'cross-functional dependency severity', a phrase he used twice, and then asked andrés to share his screen and walk through his ticket. andrés was visibly clenched. daniela never made anyone share their screen at standup. daniela barely ran standups, honestly — we'd just talk for ten minutes and dip. roberto then asked me, after standup, to send him 'a quick async write-up' on routeopt timeline so he could 'get up to speed'. quick async write-up = take an hour out of my day. i'll do it. but the vibe is set. it's micromanagement with a polite voice. carla isn't back from leave for another two months, andrés has been here three months, i'm the senior on the team and he's going to lean on me for the political stuff. great.

the thing that made me clench specifically was when he asked andrés about the postgis encoder ticket — and he didn't know what postgis was. he asked 'is that a 3rd-party library?' and i had to step in and explain. and then he asked 'why are we using it?' which is a fair question for a new manager but the way he asked it was 'why are we using it' as in 'should we be using it', not 'help me understand'. there's a difference. one is curiosity. the other is the opening of a redirect. i don't know yet which one he meant. by the end of the week i'll know. i'll write the timeline document and watch what he does with it.

post-standup andrés pinged me on slack and said 'eso fue raro, no?' and i said 'sí pero esperemos a ver'. the fact that the junior on the team noticed this in week one tells me something about how visible the friction was. i don't want to be the senior who poisons the well in week one. but i also don't want to be the senior who told the junior 'no fue nada' and gaslit him about something he correctly perceived. middle path: validate that he saw something real, advise patience. 'esperemos a ver' is the maximum-restraint response.

i drafted the timeline doc tonight. eight pages. covers: (1) the python/django legacy stack and why it's slow, (2) the rust kickoff and its rationale, (3) the postgis encoder problem and how we solved it, (4) the current task model and the memory-shape concern i'm chasing, (5) the rollout plan i'm projecting (staging this quarter, prod next), (6) team capacity (carla on leave, andrés ramping). i tried to keep it factual and load-bearing but i also salted in two paragraphs of 'why this matters for brújula's customer SLAs' so that when roberto reads it he can't reduce it to a tech-pet-project framing. paper trail. if he wants to redirect rust, he's going to have to do it against a doc that pre-empts the redirect.

fwiw i talked to camila about it tonight. she asked 'what's your worst case' and i said 'roberto installs a 9am daily checkin and a thursday demo and starts asking me to update the jira ticket every time i push code'. she said 'and what would you do'. i said 'find another job, probably'. she said 'you've been at brújula three months'. i said 'i know'. she said 'don't quit yet, give him a quarter to settle in'. she's right. i'll give him a quarter. if a quarter from now we still have a 9am checkin and a thursday demo and ticket-by-push i'm leaving anyway.

a thing camila said while we were doing dishes: 'sebastián, vos siempre crees que el problema es la persona, pero a veces el problema es la estructura'. fair point. roberto could be a fine human in a different role. it's the structure brújula gave him — a head of platform with no platform background — that's producing the friction. but the structure is fixed for now. so i'm dealing with the person.

other thing: i started keeping a 'roberto-says' file in my notes app. just verbatim quotes from him with timestamps. day 1: 'cadence' (3x). day 2: 'noodle' (1x). day 3: 'circle back' (2x), 'sync up' (2x), 'unblock you' (4x). corporate buzzword bingo. weekly trend: each week he adds a framework. learning curve, we're the practice.

so i wrote the landlord back. spent an hour on it because i wanted it to be the kind of letter he can't ignore but also can't escalate, you know? respectful. but cold. with receipts. i'm pasting the draft because i need someone to tell me i'm not being a jerk:

'estimado señor pacheco,

gracias por su mensaje del lunes informándonos del ajuste de renta para el periodo entrante. comprendo que el contexto inmobiliario de la zona ha cambiado y aprecio que nos haya dado aviso con anticipación.

antes de proceder con la firma del nuevo contrato, quería retomar con usted dos pendientes que han estado abiertos durante varios meses y que considero deben ser resueltos como parte del nuevo periodo:

1. el sistema de agua caliente del baño principal ha presentado fallas intermitentes desde febrero. en múltiples ocasiones — incluyendo los reportes del 14 de marzo y del 22 de mayo — el agua sale fría durante 5 a 10 minutos antes de calentar, lo cual es particularmente complicado en las mañanas de invierno. entiendo que se realizó una revisión parcial, pero el problema persiste.

2. la cerradura de la puerta principal ha estado floja desde mayo. envié foto del herraje el 18 de mayo. no se ha realizado mantenimiento.

estos puntos forman parte del compromiso de mantenimiento del inmueble que usted menciona en su mensaje, y considero razonable que sean atendidos antes de aceptar el nuevo monto de renta. mi propuesta es la siguiente: aceptamos el ajuste a 33,000 mxn mensuales si: (a) el sistema de agua caliente es revisado y reparado por un técnico certificado en las próximas dos semanas, y (b) la cerradura es reemplazada en el mismo plazo.

quedo atento a su respuesta y le agradezco de antemano la atención.

saludos cordiales,
sebastián río'

is this too much? camila read it and said 'es perfecto, mándalo'. i sent it. and then forty-five minutes later he called me. CALLED me, didn't whatsapp. i picked up because curiosity. and what followed was twenty minutes of him being escalated about the hot water specifically — like the rent he barely defended, the hot water he was personally offended about, kept saying 'pero el calentador es nuevo, lo cambié hace dos años' and i said 'ok pero el agua sale fría', and he said 'pero el calentador es nuevo' on a loop. at one point i actually said 'señor pacheco, el calentador puede ser nuevo y aun así no funcionar'. very calm. i was proud of that line. then he said he'd send a técnico 'pronto' and that the rent ajuste was firm. i said i'd wait until the técnico came before signing. there was a pause. then he said 'está bien'. i said 'gracias' and hung up.

let me reconstruct the call as a transcript because the cadence of it was instructive and i want to remember it for the next time i argue with a 60-year-old chilango about a household appliance:

pacheco: '¿sebastián? soy el señor pacheco.'
me: 'sí, señor pacheco, buenas tardes.'
p: 'recibí tu mensaje. mira, el calentador es nuevo. lo cambié hace dos años.'
me: 'sí, lo recuerdo. pero el agua sale fría en las mañanas, durante varios minutos.'
p: 'pero el calentador es nuevo.'
me: 'sí. pero el resultado — el agua que sale por la regadera — está fría.'
p: 'a lo mejor es la presión.'
me: 'a lo mejor. por eso pido un técnico.'
p: 'mira, voy a mandar a un técnico, pero el calentador no es el problema.'
me: 'el técnico va a evaluar.'
p: 'el técnico va a confirmar lo que te estoy diciendo.'
me: 'está bien, que confirme. y la cerradura?'
p: '... la cerradura, sí, también, también la voy a revisar.'
me: 'gracias. el ajuste de renta — lo firmamos cuando el agua caliente esté funcionando.'
p: 'pero el ajuste —'
me: 'cuando el agua caliente esté funcionando, señor pacheco. con gusto firmo.'
p: '...está bien.'
me: 'gracias. quedo atento.'

i notice three things rereading this. first, he repeated 'el calentador es nuevo' four times. that's a tell. when someone repeats a defense that doesn't address the complaint, they know it doesn't address the complaint. he was trying to wear me down by repetition, which is a thing my dad does when he wants to win an argument by saying his position so many times that you stop wanting to argue. it works on my dad's argument partner (my mom, sometimes me) because we love him. it does not work on me right now because i don't love señor pacheco. second, the moment i said 'el calentador puede ser nuevo y aun así no funcionar' was the moment the conversation pivoted. that's when he switched from defending to pseudo-cooperating. small but useful: when someone is repeating a position, find the framing that lets the position be technically true while still losing. third, i didn't escalate, didn't raise my voice, didn't bring up the rent increase as a moral grievance. i kept it on infrastructure. that's the right axis to fight him on.

the negotiation theory of this is — and i'm mostly stealing from a chris voss book i half-read on a flight last year — that you never argue with the position, you just keep restating the constraint that makes their position untenable. his position: 'rent goes up'. my constraint: 'i don't sign for an apartment with broken hot water'. i didn't argue about the rent. i kept restating the constraint. eventually his position has to bend around it because i'm not signing the contract. the negotiation isn't 'you against me'. it's 'his desire to close the deal vs my willingness to walk'. i was not actually willing to walk. but he didn't know that and i made my voice sound like i was. that was the whole game.

camila clapped. literally clapped. she said 'you negotiated with a 60-year-old mexican landlord and didn't lose, that's a personal best'. nube barked because camila clapped. it was a moment.

but. i feel like i won the fight on the wrong axis. the rent is still going up. i just made him fix the boiler he was supposed to fix anyway. and now he's mad at me, which means the next time something breaks he's going to take three weeks instead of two. there's no winning these things, just rebalancing the loss. i think the real game here is that pacheco is going to fix this one thing now, slow-walk everything else for the next twelve months, and at the next renewal try to push the rent again because he'll feel he's owed for having dealt with us. that's the trap. the only counter is to be such a low-touch tenant for the rest of the lease that he can't justify it. low touch except for the things i actually need fixed, which i'll document obsessively. paper trail, paper trail, paper trail. anyway. técnico is supposed to come thursday. we'll see if 'thursday' means 'thursday' or 'eventually'.

side note: camila pointed out i'm doing the same negotiation pattern at work with roberto. like, i don't argue with his standup norms 2.0 directly, i just keep doing my work and noting where his structure produces friction, and at some point i'm going to bring the friction notes back to him with a 'maybe we should adjust' framing. she said 'tu pinche estilo de pelear todo es exactly the same, te lo veo en la cara cuando estás haciéndolo'. she's right. i have one negotiating algorithm and i run it on all the cabrones in my life. it works enough that i haven't bothered to learn another one.

extra thought before bed: there's a reason this works on landlords AND middle managers AND postgres deadlocks. all three are systems that respond to consistent constraint pressure. you don't argue with them. you make their preferred outcome impossible until they shift. you do it patiently. you don't gloat. you accept the small losses (the rent goes up, the standup norms stay, the deadlock recurs once before you patch it for real) so you can win the bigger thing (the boiler is fixed, the standup norms get walked back over a quarter, the underlying schema gets refactored). camila called this the 'engineer's negotiation' once. i think she's right. it works because it treats people as systems with state, which is rude as a worldview but accurate as a tactic. i'm trying to limit it to landlords and managers and not apply it to camila, with mixed success.

técnico didn't show. of course. pacheco's daughter messaged 'mañana sin falta'. i've heard 'sin falta' enough times in my life to know what it means. low expectations, recharged. screenshotted the message and added it to the 'edificio' folder camila started. timestamps matter for these things. paper trail rule one: if it's not in writing it didn't happen. she also said 'va a llegar entre 9 y 11', which in mexican-time-conversion is roughly 'between 11 and 4'. i'll be at brújula. camila will be home. pre-warned her. she sighed.

rust migration update for the sake of writing it out. the postgis encoder is in production-ready shape, ran it through the integration suite for two days and it survived. axum routes are clean — basically a thin layer over the optimization core. the tokio task model is what i'm worrying about now, not the encoding. spawning a task per request was clever in june, in october it feels like a smell. i should benchmark it before it bites me. carla would have caught this in design review but carla is on parental leave and andrés is too new to say no to my ideas. note to self: write a 'why i did this' doc this week so that when she's back she can rip it apart and i'll be ready.

actually the deeper concern is that i did this thing engineers do when they're slightly too senior to be challenged: i made an architectural choice early without writing it up, and now nobody on the team has a frame to question it. carla, when she's back, will. but until then it's just me looking at my own code at midnight going 'is this fine'. the answer to 'is this fine' from your own author-bias is always 'sure'. i need the doc, badly, even if no one reads it for two months. writing it will force me to spot the seams. let me draft a section header list right now: (1) why spawn-per-request, (2) alternatives considered, (3) failure modes if traffic doubles, (4) failure modes if request size doubles, (5) observability hooks i should have added but didn't. just listing those headers tells me i haven't thought carefully enough about (4).

the alternatives i should have considered properly: a worker pool with a bounded mpsc channel for incoming work, where the runtime is shaped at startup; a per-customer dedicated executor (overkill for our scale but sometimes good for noisy-neighbor isolation); a pure-future model with no spawn at all where the optimizer runs inline on the request task. the spawn-per-request choice was made because i was nervous about long-tail requests blocking the response future, and spawn lets me put a deadline on it. but spawn has its own costs and i was treating it as zero-cost in my head when it isn't. classic engineer trap.

writeup target this week: 1500 words, max, internal-only doc. carla will read it when she's back. andrés will read it now and ask three questions. i need andrés to ask three questions. the team has been too quiet about my decisions and that's because they're juniors or absent. quiet around senior decisions is a smell. i need to break it deliberately. i'll send the draft to andrés with a 'tear this apart' note. lower the cost of disagreement. then maybe i'll start finding the leaks before prod does.

extra context: i looked at the standup norms 2.0 doc one more time before bed and it has a section called 'meeting hygiene' that includes the phrase 'cameras on as default for psychological safety'. this is the kind of phrase you write when you have read about psychological safety in a manager training course but have not understood it. cameras-on does not produce psychological safety. it produces the *appearance* of psychological safety from a manager's office. these are different things. roberto wrote a sentence that is 180 degrees inverted from its source meaning and feels good about it.

footnote: i drafted a reply to roberto in markdown explaining why 'cameras-on as default' is bad psychological-safety practice and then deleted it. i don't have the political capital to teach roberto about psychological safety three weeks into his tenure. that conversation happens at month four if i'm still here. or it never happens because he's gone. the reply stays unsent.

addendum: at end of day i sent daniela a private slack 'hope you're doing ok with the standup norms thing'. she replied 'i'm fine but jorge messaged me too, you should check on him'. so jorge is rattled. jorge is the most experienced engineer on our team — 8 years at brújula, was here before camila and morales-ramos and roberto. if jorge is rattled, that's a signal. i'm going to grab coffee with him tomorrow. i'll listen, mostly. jorge is not a complainer; if he says something it'll be pre-cooked.

ok so la polar last night was a level i did not know existed. i need to log this because i'm going to file a noise complaint with the alcaldía and i need a paper trail. it's a friday, sure, fridays at la polar are always loud, that's the deal of living above a cantina. fine. but last night was special. i'm going to walk you through it because i need it on the record:

21:30 — start of the live banda. trumpets. fine, audible, normal. measured 64db on the phone in the living room.

22:15 — second band starts, this one is a tribuna-style mariachi but louder, and someone has clearly upgraded their PA system since last time i was annoyed enough to check. 71db in the living room. windows closed.

23:00 — they start doing mariachi karaoke. yes. mariachi karaoke. some señor is on the mic doing 'el rey' and he is missing every other note and the crowd is cheering him on because he's clearly someone's tío. 73db. nube starts pacing.

23:40 — second drunk señor takes 'el rey'. apparently 'el rey' is the song of the night. this one is worse. nube is now under the bed.

00:15 — fight breaks out. i don't know about what, but there's shouting and a glass breaks and someone yells 'PINCHE WEY' loud enough that i hear it through the floor over the band. 78db. eighty-eight if you count the human shouting. camila is officially awake. camila does not get loudly awake, she gets quietly murderous awake, which is much worse.

00:50 — band is replaced by reggaeton through the same PA system, presumably to calm everyone down (it does not), and we are now hearing bad vibrations through the wood floor. the bookshelf is buzzing. i can feel it through my feet.

01:20 — i finally call la polar. someone answers, i can hear bachata in the background, i say 'buenas noches, vivo arriba, podrían bajarle al sonido por favor' and the guy says 'sí, claro joven, ahorita le bajamos' and absolutely does not lower it. i waited fifteen minutes. nothing. i called again. nobody picked up the second time. so the 'sí, claro joven' is just the cantina equivalent of 'lo vemos y te aviso' which everyone in mexico knows means we will not be looking at it and we will not be telling you anything. the cultural training i should have had at age 8 is letting me down at age 33.

02:00 — it stops. i don't know why. either the fight escalated and someone called the cops or they ran out of mariachi or someone unplugged the PA. doesn't matter. silence.

02:00-02:40 — me lying in bed, awake, reading reddit, planning the noise complaint i am about to file. camila on her phone next to me, sending a voice note to her sister in são paulo about how she might literally move to são paulo just to escape la polar. nube under the bed.

05:00 — doña marta. of course. the universe coordinates these things. i could hear her oven door at 4:55 because i was still half awake. doña marta is not the villain here, doña marta is a 73-year-old widow who runs a one-woman panadería out of her apartment and her bread sells out by 8am because it's that good, but the timing of doña marta's oven door 3 hours after la polar finally stops is the kind of thing that makes you wonder if you're being personally targeted by the architectural acoustics of a 1940s building.

the noise complaint i'm going to file with the alcaldía cuauhtémoc is real. there's a process, you submit photos, decibel readings (i'll re-measure tonight), a written description of the events. la polar has a license that supposedly limits live music after midnight. i looked it up. the license says 'hasta las 24:00 horas'. last night they were live until 00:50 and PA-loud until 02:00. they are violating their license. for the record:

- license type: 'establecimiento mercantil con giro de cantina con venta de alimentos'
- music allowance: 'música en vivo permitida hasta las 24:00 horas, música ambiental hasta las 02:00 horas'
- decibel limit per the alcaldía guidelines (which is a separate document i had to dig for): max 65db measured at the boundary of the property between 22:00 and 06:00.

so last night at the boundary of MY apartment (which is roughly the same as their property boundary, since the building is a single structure and our floor is directly above their bar) we hit 78db at peak with live amplification past midnight. that's two violations stacked. live music after the cutoff AND amplitude over the limit. i could submit this with the readings and the screenshots and dr beltrán-style cite-the-clause precision, and the alcaldía would technically have grounds to open an inspection.

the paranoid part of me thinks la polar pays someone to overlook this. this is mexico city. of course they do. the licenses get reviewed at the delegación by people who have known the cantina's owner for forty years. there's not going to be a sudden shutdown. but i'm filing it anyway. if nothing happens i'll file it again next month. and the month after. and the month after that. the rent went up 5k, i'm getting my 5k of malicious compliance back somehow.

i googled 'cómo poner una denuncia de ruido en cdmx' tonight and the answer is the alcaldía's portal, with a written description, and ideally decibel evidence. there's also a separate path through procuraduría ambiental but that's for industrial-scale stuff. for a cantina the alcaldía is the right lane. and there's a specific phrase you're supposed to use in the complaint — 'incumplimiento del horario de operación y exceso de niveles de ruido permitidos por la normativa nadf-005-ambt-2013' — and once i learned that phrase i started seeing it everywhere. that's the cite. nadf-005. mexican environmental noise standard. i'm going to put it in every complaint i file this year. there is something deeply satisfying about reducing my landlord-and-cantina rage to a citation number.

anyway. tired. nube finally came out from under the bed at 03:15 and slept on my chest, which was the only nice thing about last night. and there's something kind of horrible-funny about being woken up at 5am by panadería bread smell after being kept up til 2am by mariachi karaoke. like, this is the full roma norte experience condensed into one acoustic envelope. people pay extra to live here for this. people specifically choose this. i need to remember that next time someone at brújula asks why i don't move to coyoacán.

actually the most embarrassing part of all this is that i walked past la polar saturday morning around 11am to grab cardinal coffee and there were three guys outside, in their 60s, sweeping up the previous night's mess, looking like the most hungover men you've ever seen in your life, one of them said 'buen día joven' and tipped his hat and i didn't have it in me to be mad at them in person. it's so much easier to be mad at the institution than the people. the people are just trying to keep their abuelo's bar open in a roma norte that's gentrifying around them. the institution is what's broken — the licenses, the enforcement, the acoustic isolation that doesn't exist. so i'm filing the complaint against the institution. but i'm not going to scowl at the abuelos sweeping at 11am. that's the line.

extra detail i want to capture for the alcaldía submission: the building is concrete-and-wood, 1940s construction, with terrazo floors throughout. the wood subfloor of our apartment is directly above the cantina's tin-tile ceiling, with maybe 30cm of cavity between, including a layer of 1940s construction debris that conducts sound rather than absorbing it. there is no acoustic foam, no resilient mounting, no decoupling of the cantina's ceiling joists from our floor joists. the building was designed for residential-over-residential, not residential-over-cantina, and the cantina is a 1980s addition to the ground floor, retrofitted into a space that was originally a haberdasher. so the acoustic transmission is not just bad luck — it's architectural negligence on the cantina retrofit. i could include this in the complaint as 'el establecimiento opera en una estructura no diseñada para mitigar transmisión acústica vertical, agravando el incumplimiento de los límites permisibles'. that sentence took me twenty minutes to compose. i'm putting it in. paper trail with technical specifics is a stronger paper trail.

filed the alcaldía complaint. there's a portal, ventanilla virtual or something, i uploaded the decibel screenshots and a written description in formal spanish that took me 40 minutes to write. confirmation number, all that. they say up to 30 days for response. i'll bet you a coffee at cardinal that the response is 'gracias por su denuncia, se procederá a revisión' and then nothing. but it's filed. it exists. if i ever go to small claims about the rent or the boiler or anything else, the noise file is in the record. building a case, in a sense. very un-mexican of me to be this paper-trail oriented, my dad would think i've turned into an accountant.

the form had a section called 'medidas previas tomadas por el ciudadano' — 'prior actions taken by the citizen' — which i appreciated because it forced me to actually list my failed attempts: (1) called la polar 1:20am, asked them to lower the volume, ignored. (2) called again at 1:35am, no answer. (3) considered going down in person but did not because it's 1am at a fight-prone bar and i have no death wish. (4) waited until silence, slept four hours, filed complaint via portal. listing it like that made it feel real. it's a paper trail that says 'i tried the soft path first, you forced me to the formal one'. that's exactly the framing i want.

other sections: i filled in the decibel readings as a table with timestamps and locations within the apartment (kitchen counter, bedroom, living room near the floor). i attached the screenshots from the decibel x pro app — 23 of them, one per peak event. i transcribed in spanish the cantina's response when i called, including the phrase 'sí, claro joven, ahorita le bajamos' followed by zero action, because the alcaldía form has a section for 'respuesta del establecimiento' and 'sí pero no' is itself useful evidence about the cantina's posture toward complaints.

i also added a 'historial' section where i documented prior fridays — 11 weekends i can recall in the last 3 months where the noise exceeded the limit, with dates, even if i didn't have decibel data for those nights. this is a thing my dad taught me without ever stating it as a rule: when you escalate, never let it look like an isolated event. always paint the pattern. el patrón es el argumento. one isolated incident is bad luck. eleven incidents in twelve weeks is a pattern that requires regulatory response. that's the framing.

camila read the submission and her only edit was to change one verb from indicative to subjunctive in a clause that said 'solicito que las autoridades inspeccionen el establecimiento' — she pointed out 'inspeccionen' was already correct (i'd typed 'inspeccionarán' first, which would have been future indicative and weakened the request). thank god for a girlfriend who writes legal-tone spanish for a living. submitted at 11:43pm. confirmation # CDMX-CUAUH-DENUN-2026-0837214. saving that number forever.

last bit on this: i told camila over dinner. she asked 'why are they raising 14% when CPI is what, 4%' and i said 'because they can'. she said 'you negotiate with him?' i said 'i'm going to try'. she said 'you've never won a negotiation in your life sebastián'. i objected. she said 'name one'. i couldn't name one on the spot. i'm going to send her the negotiation outcome regardless of how it lands because she's curious and also because she's right that i'm bad at this and i should learn.

later, i ran the renewal numbers through my budget spreadsheet. 28k → 33k is +5k/month, +60k/year. that's 18 months of yerba mate, or ~6 months of camila's gym + my gym, or one really good vacation, or about half of the cdmx-to-tokyo flight i've been pricing for our anniversary. it's not abstract. that 5k has shape.

— ran the math one more time at 11pm: the 33k rent for 12 months = 396k. plus utilities ~48k, plus internet 7k, plus building maintenance fees 18k = 469k/year just to be in this apartment. that's $24k usd. for a 2-bedroom in roma sur. cdmx is supposed to be cheap and at this rent it's edging into 'not cheap anymore' territory. especially with the cantina underneath.

working from cardinal today. flat white, focus playlist, two hours of writeup. small win. the barista who has my drink memorized asked if i wanted to try the new oaxacan single-origin and i said yes and it was great. tiny dopamine. wrote 1100 words of the rust task-model design doc. saved a draft. closed the laptop before doom-scrolling. cardinal is the only place in cdmx where i can write. la polar can't reach me here. mañana more.

ok new contract draft just landed in my email from señor pacheco's daughter, who apparently 'le ayuda a papá con la renta', and i need to walk through it before signing because there are at least three things in it that are weird and i want to make sure i'm not missing a fourth. pasting the relevant clauses (translated from the spanish for my own clarity, original on file):

clause 4 (renta y depósito): 'el monto mensual de renta a partir de la fecha del presente será de $33,000.00 mxn (treinta y tres mil pesos 00/100 mn). el inquilino renueva su depósito en garantía equivalente a un mes de renta, ajustándose la diferencia respecto al depósito previo dentro de los primeros 30 días.' — translation: i pay him 5k more in deposit. on top of the rent. this is normal but i had forgotten about it and it's another bite. so the actual short-term outflow next month is 33k rent + 5k deposit difference = 38k. plus the lease signing fee (notario light, 1500 mxn) which they bury at the end. so call it 39.5k for the first month. i need to make sure the savings buffer covers this without panic.

clause 7 (mantenimiento): 'el inquilino se compromete a reportar oportunamente cualquier desperfecto. los desperfectos derivados del uso normal serán atendidos por el arrendador en plazo razonable, mientras que aquellos derivados de mal uso serán a cargo del inquilino.' — fine on its face but 'plazo razonable' is doing a lot of work in that sentence and i want to add a clarifying clause that defines 'razonable' as 'no más de 7 días para fallas críticas (agua caliente, gas, eléctrico, cerraduras) y 30 días para fallas no críticas'. i'm also going to add language that says 'mal uso' must be demonstrated with evidence — otherwise pacheco can declare anything he doesn't want to fix as 'mal uso' and the burden is on me to prove otherwise. that's how informal mexican rental disputes work and i don't want to be in that position. a friend of mine in la condesa got blamed for a leak in the unit below his that was actually structural; took him three months and 18k mxn in attorney fees to prove it wasn't his fault. i'm not living that.

clause 9 (terminación anticipada): 'en caso de terminación anticipada del contrato por parte del inquilino, este se compromete a dar aviso por escrito con 60 días de antelación, además de pagar el equivalente a un mes de renta como pena convencional.' — sixty days plus a one-month penalty? this is harder than the previous contract which was 30 days notice and no penalty. unacceptable. i want to push back to 30 days no penalty, will accept 60 days no penalty as a compromise. the penalty clause i'm not signing. for context: the difference between 30-day no-penalty and 60-day-plus-penalty over a 12-month lease, in expected-cost terms if we have a 20% probability of breaking the lease early (which is realistic given the rent volatility, the noise situation, and the possibility i get into mit or camila has to move to são paulo for her dad), is roughly 0.20 × (30k extra rent + 33k penalty) = ~12.6k expected. the new clause adds twelve grand of expected cost. i'm not paying that.

clause 12 (huéspedes): 'el inquilino no podrá alojar a personas distintas a las firmantes del presente contrato por periodos mayores a 15 días sin previo aviso por escrito.' — this is new. previous contract had no equivalent. why is it here. is this about hugo when he visits? hugo visits monthly for 4-7 days post-widowhood, that's well under 15, so technically fine, but the chilling effect is bad. i want this removed or softened. also: what is 'previo aviso' supposed to look like? a whatsapp? a notarized letter? if i don't pin down the form of the notice, pacheco can claim i didn't give him one for any visitor he wants to make trouble about. i want this changed to: 'visitors of any duration permitted; tenant will inform landlord in writing for stays exceeding 30 consecutive days, by whatsapp or email.'

clause 14 (mascotas): 'se autoriza un (1) animal de compañía siendo este: nube, perra raza ganadero australiano, hembra, esterilizada.' — fine but the 'siendo este' wording locks us to nube specifically. if anything happens to nube and we get another dog we have to redo the contract. i'd like 'un animal de compañía hasta 25 kg' instead. nube is going to live forever obviously but the contract should be robust to the universe being unkind.

clause 18 (ajuste anual): 'al término del periodo anual, el monto de renta podrá ser ajustado en base al índice nacional de precios al consumidor (inpc) o por acuerdo entre las partes, lo que resulte mayor.' — wait. wait. 'lo que resulte mayor'. so the rent goes up by inpc OR by negotiation, whichever is higher. that means there is no scenario in which the next renewal is below inflation. i'm not signing that. i want it changed to 'lo que resulte menor' or, more reasonably, 'inpc + 0% negociado, ambas partes pueden proponer otro índice mediante acuerdo escrito'. this is the kind of clause that looks innocuous and bites you 12 months later. good catch on my own part. would have missed it on a fast read.

clause 22 (garantía / fiador): 'el inquilino presentará un fiador con propiedad libre de gravamen en la ciudad de méxico o en su defecto póliza jurídica.' — the previous contract didn't require this because i had a long good-payment record. why is it suddenly here? if pacheco wants me to either get a fiador (i don't have one in cdmx; my parents are in gdl and cdmx fiadores are notoriously hard) or pay for a póliza jurídica (which is 4-7% of annual rent, so call it ~25k mxn), that's a hidden cost equivalent to almost a month of rent. unacceptable. i'm going to push back hard with the argument that we have 33 months of perfect payment history with him.

so my plan is: send back a redline to clauses 7, 9, 12, 14, 18, 22. accept clause 4 because that fight is lost. send it back politely but firmly. i'll have it ready tonight. camila will read it. she's good at the wording for this stuff because she does it all day at foster + partners with construction subcontractors — she literally has a 'standard pushback language' email template she reuses, which she sent me earlier today and which i'm now adapting for the rental context.

i'm going to title my email 'comentarios al borrador del contrato' and frame the redlines as 'sugerencias de claridad' rather than 'pushback' because in mexican contract negotiation you never want the other side to feel like they're being told no. you want them to feel like they're being helped to improve the document. it's a face-saving thing. it works. it took me a year of doing it the gringo way and getting nowhere before i learned this. ironic that a coursera distributed-systems class made me a better mexican.

let me draft the opener: 'estimada srta. pacheco, gracias por enviarme el borrador del contrato. lo he revisado con detenimiento y le incluyo a continuación algunas sugerencias de claridad y precisión que considero pueden hacer el documento más sólido para ambas partes. quedo atento a sus comentarios.' — see how that reads vs 'i'm pushing back on six clauses, here's why'. exact same content. completely different reception. there is a craft to this and it took me too long to learn.

second meta-thought: the daughter handling the lease is itself useful. the dad handles negotiations by phone (escalation, repetition, instinctive defense). the daughter handles negotiations by email (logical, can be argued in writing, leaves a record). i'm shifting my counterparty from the dad to the daughter on purpose by sending the email to her directly with him cc'd. she'll process the redlines first and present them to him as 'papá, el inquilino propone esto', which lowers his emotional temperature. small but real. this is how you fight institutions made of two people: pick which person is the rational layer.

órale. adulthood is so much paperwork. and i just did 90 minutes of clause analysis instead of writing rust code. roberto would have a field day with my time tracking right now.

roberto added a new ritual. weekly 1:1, 30 minutes, structured agenda. structured agenda. he sent us a notion template titled 'manager 1:1 — 1.0' that has sections for 'wins', 'blockers', 'feedback for manager', 'longer-term aspirations', and at the bottom 'coaching topics'. one of the coaching topics he pre-filled for me is 'communicating technical decisions to non-technical stakeholders'. i did not ask for coaching on this. i have not given him reason to think i need it. carla is going to lose her mind when she comes back from leave.

the notion template has version history visible because roberto didn't lock it down, and i can see he's been iterating on it for at least three weeks. he was building this template before he was even our official manager. the 'wins' section was originally called 'accomplishments' and got renamed. the 'longer-term aspirations' section was added in revision 4. there's a comment thread between roberto and someone named 'mark t.' (presumably his old manager wherever he came from) where mark suggested adding 'pulse check' as a section header and roberto said 'i think pulse check feels intrusive, going to leave this one'. so he has SOME judgment. small consolation. the template is also identical in structure to one that's been floating around tech-management twitter for like two years — i recognize the shape. so this isn't bespoke thinking, this is following a playbook. that's fine for a new manager but it tells me he doesn't yet have his own opinions about how 1:1s should run, which means the early months are going to be him running an algorithm and waiting for it to fail.

the 'longer-term aspirations' section is going to be a battleground. he's going to want me to write 'leadership track' or 'staff engineer' or some legible-to-management thing. i'm going to write something boring and accurate like 'finish the rust migration to production successfully and get carla back from leave smoothly' and refuse to expand. i'm not telling roberto about mit. mit is not for the manager-1:1 doc. mit is for me and camila and pablo and daniela and that's it for now. if i write 'considering masters program' he will (a) ask for the school name, (b) ask for the timeline, (c) start treating me as a flight risk and either over-invest in retention or quietly route work away from me. neither is what i want during application season. so i'll be boring on paper. small acts of strategic banality.

the other thing i'm noting about the template — and this is a thing i learned at wizeline in 2018 from a manager i actually liked — is that the structure of the doc telegraphs the manager's anxieties. wins/blockers/feedback/aspirations is the structure of a manager who is anxious about reporting upward. it gives him five things to extract from each engineer that he can then aggregate into the kind of update his own manager wants. the engineer-centric structure would be different — it would lead with 'what do you want to talk about today' and let the conversation shape itself. roberto's template has zero open space. every minute is pre-allocated to something he can carry upward. that's the read. and once you see it you can't unsee it.

later that night: pablo from the 7th came up to chat in the elevator. he asked 'cuánto te subió el morales-ramos?' i said '14%' and he said 'a mí también, cabrón, también 14%'. so it's not me-specific, it's a building-wide 14%. that's both worse (he's normalizing it across all units) and slightly better (it's not retaliation for any tenant being annoying). so it's a market move. fine. i'm still going to push on the lease language separately because that's actually defective regardless of the rent number.

later: walked nube around the block at 11pm to clear my head. the cantina was loud as always. a saturday so the noise floor was already at 65db at street level. i thought 'i pay 28k to live above this and they want 33k'. and then i thought 'wait — maybe the cantina is the leverage'. if the building has known nuisance issues then 14% increase is even more egregious. there's a posture here. i need to think about how to frame it.

took nube to chapultepec. she chased a pigeon for thirty seconds, then sat in the grass and looked at me. valid. came home, she fell asleep across the door because she still does that when i'm out of sight. she's also started doing the herding thing again where she circles us in the kitchen if both of us are standing — heeler genetics confirming themselves on schedule. we googled how to redirect it. answer: more exercise, more chew toys, do not yell. did not yell. gave bone.

ok i need to vent and i need to do it surgically because i don't trust myself to be fair to roberto in conversation right now. so. roberto sent the team an email titled 'standup norms — 2.0' last night at 22:47. yes, the version number is in the subject line. yes, this is the standup that until two weeks ago was 'show up and talk for ten minutes'. i am pasting the bullet points verbatim from the email and adding my own annotations because i need to process this somehow:

— 'standup will start at 09:30 sharp, no later than 09:31. cameras on for the first 5 minutes (introductions / vibe-check), optional after.' — vibe-check. he wrote vibe-check in a work email. and the camera-on-for-five-minutes thing is a level of micromanagement i have not encountered since my first job at kueski in 2014 when the founders made us do 'energy stretches' before standup. i am 33 years old. i did not become a senior backend engineer to do a corporate vibe-check at 09:30. i became a senior backend engineer because i'm very good at making postgres do things it does not want to do, and that skill set, last i checked, does not improve with morning vibe-checks.

— 'each engineer shares: (1) what they shipped yesterday, (2) what they will ship today, (3) blockers ranked by cross-functional dependency severity. format: bullet, no narrative. target time per person: 90 seconds.' — '90 seconds' for six engineers is 9 minutes. then he wants 'cross-functional summary' for 5 minutes. then 'leadership comments' (his) for 5 minutes. that's a 19-minute meeting which 30 seconds ago was a 10-minute meeting. and 'no narrative' means i can't say 'i shipped the postgis encoder but it's gnarly because tokio-postgres doesn't speak postgis'. i have to say 'shipped postgis encoder, blockers: none'. he is removing the actual content of standup, which is the off-script grumbling that helps you spot trouble. the way real teams work, the value of standup is not the status update — github tells you status — the value is the hallway-conversation-but-scheduled. it's the 'oh wait, you're touching that?' and the 'i thought we were doing it the other way?' and the 'has anyone seen this weird error?'. you cannot get those moments out of a 90-second bulleted update. they emerge from narrative. roberto is killing the narrative because narrative is illegible to managers who are trying to look organized.

— 'after standup, please update your jira tickets to reflect the day's plan. tickets without estimates will be flagged for grooming.' — flagged. by whom. by him. so he's reading our jira every morning and checking estimates. carla is going to come back from parental leave and quit. and the 'estimates' framing — anyone who has worked on a real engineering project knows that estimates on rust migration tickets are fiction. you cannot estimate 'fix tokio runtime starvation' in story points. it's done when it's done. forcing estimates on it just creates the appearance of predictability for someone above roberto who needs the appearance of predictability. it's not for our benefit. it's for the deck.

— 'blockers ranked 'high' or 'critical' will trigger a follow-up in slack channel #logistics-platform-blockers within 30 minutes. please use this channel only for blockers.' — slack channel just for blockers. so any productive conversation about a blocker — like how to debug it, or workarounds we tried — goes in a different channel and gets fragmented. brilliant. and 'within 30 minutes' is the kind of SLA you put on a customer-facing tool, not on engineers debugging an internal tool. the implicit message is 'i will be watching that channel and timing the response'. ok cool, no pressure.

— 'fridays we will do a retrospective bullet at standup: one thing the team did well, one thing to improve. these will be aggregated monthly into a manager report shared with engineering leadership.' — 'aggregated monthly into a manager report'. so anything we say at retro goes up the chain unless we coordinate not to say things. i'm sure he'll say it's anonymized. it won't be. there are six of us. and any 'one thing to improve' that names a person will, in a small team, be impossible to anonymize. so people are going to learn very quickly to retrofit the 'thing to improve' to be technical and impersonal. which means we'll never get the actual signal. which is the whole point of retros.

— 'questions, ping me directly. open door (slack).' — open door slack. ok señor.

let me also note what's NOT in the email:
- nothing about 1:1 cadence (which he is going to introduce separately, i can already smell it)
- nothing about how he'll be running planning vs daniela's old way (she did planning every two weeks, light touch, 90 minutes)
- nothing about how he'll handle on-call rotation (we have a real rotation; will he disrupt it?)
- nothing about routeopt rust migration specifically. zero. this is the project we're shipping. it gets no attention in this email. he's talking about meeting structure instead of the actual work.

the right move here is to not respond at all today. tomorrow we have standup with the new format. i'll show up, do my 90 seconds clean, watch andrés stumble, raise an eyebrow at carla in spirit, and then in the 1:1 next week i'll bring up two things: (1) the 90-second-no-narrative rule is going to mean we miss real signal, and (2) the jira-estimate-policing is going to make people pad estimates. i'll frame it as 'i've seen this before, here's what happens'. he'll say 'good feedback, let's revisit in a month'. nothing will change. but it will be on record that i raised it. and at performance review time, when it has all gone wrong and he's looking for someone to blame, my paper trail will be cleaner than his.

there's a particular flavor of management failure i've seen before, at kueski in 2015 with a director who came in from a bigger fintech and tried to install all of his old company's processes in three weeks. you can see it coming. the symptoms are: (a) version-numbered process docs, (b) channel proliferation, (c) explicit manager-tracking of engineer outputs, (d) ritual creation faster than ritual evaluation, (e) anti-narrative formats that prefer status over signal. roberto is hitting four of five in week one. the kueski director got pushed out at month five after two senior engineers quit. roberto isn't going to get pushed out at brújula because brújula is a smaller team and there's nobody senior enough above him to push him out. he's going to grind us until something gives. i need to figure out my own posture vs that grind. options as i see them: (1) absorb it, ride it out, focus on shipping rust to staging which is where the actual value is.
