(2) push back hard early, be the senior who pushes back, set norms so he doesn't get away with it. (3) start interviewing elsewhere quietly. (4) some combo of 1 and 3.

the broader meta-question — am i going to last under this? — is the one i'm trying not to think about right now because i don't have a clean answer. brújula was built on daniela's culture, not on management infrastructure, and the difference between those two things is exactly what's getting renegotiated this month. i could weather six months of roberto. i could weather twelve. i don't know if i could weather twelve months of roberto AND la polar AND the rent AND the kid-timing question all at once. that's the actual question. let it sit.

daniela ran six engineers for two years on vibes and we shipped three full launches. roberto is going to run the same team into burnout in six months by trying to look like a manager.

órale. i'm going to drink yerba mate and not respond. drafting nothing tonight. let it sit. and tomorrow at standup i'm going to be the most polished, on-time, 90-seconds-exactly engineer he has ever seen, because the way to disarm a process-fetishist manager is to be conspicuously good at his processes while subtly demonstrating the friction they cause. play the long game. paper trail. paper trail. paper trail.

the thing about daniela that i didn't appreciate while she was here is how much she shielded us from this stuff. like, brújula has had political brain-rot at the executive layer the whole time i've been there, and i didn't really notice because daniela was filtering it. she took the meetings. she translated the corp-speak into actual engineering decisions. she said 'no' to executives so we didn't have to. roberto, i'm watching, is going to do the opposite — he's going to amplify the corp-speak because it makes him look serious. there's a worldview difference. daniela was a senior engineer who became a manager. roberto is, i'm pretty sure, a manager who has theories about engineering. these are different jobs. anyway. waiting it out.

i texted daniela today. just said 'hey, hope the new gig is going well, miss having you around'. she replied within ten minutes — 'cabrón, lo sé, lo siento, te debo una cerveza'. didn't ask about roberto. she knew. that's the kind of manager she was. she could read a one-line text and know exactly what was happening behind it. roberto could not read a one-line text from his team if you printed it on a billboard.

the specific examples of daniela-shielding i'm now noticing in absence: (1) when the cfo wanted weekly burn-rate reports from each team and daniela said 'we'll give you monthly summary and if anything spikes we'll flag it' — that one took her a 45-minute meeting to argue and we never knew about the meeting. (2) when product wanted us to add a 'try it free for 30 days' banner to our route-planning api docs and daniela said 'no, the api isn't a marketing channel' and got the head of marketing to back down. (3) when the vp of eng tried to mandate that all teams use the same observability stack and daniela said 'fine but my team picks the dashboards' and that compromise let us keep our grafana setup. those were three separate political battles she fought and won and the only reason i know about them is because she mentioned them in passing at her farewell dinner. she was running interference on three executive friction points i'd never have to handle. roberto is not going to fight those battles. he's going to comply, then push the compliance friction down to us.

reading 'a manager's path' last weekend (yes i'm reading management books, i know, shut up) and one line stuck with me: 'the role of an engineering manager is to absorb chaos so engineers can produce signal'. daniela did exactly that. roberto is going to broadcast the chaos because he thinks broadcasting it is what makes him look on-top-of-it. those are different theories of management. i think both can work in different contexts. i don't think roberto's works for our context. small team, complex migration, two years of relationship trust under daniela. roberto's playbook is built for a 50-person org with low trust. he's running the wrong playbook in the wrong shape of room. and i don't have the standing to tell him that without it sounding like 'i miss my old manager'. so i'm noting it here and waiting.

camila's suggestion at dinner — 'just sign it and stop spending two evenings on this' — is also the conventional wisdom. but the conventional wisdom is what landlords count on. if 95% of tenants sign without reading and 5% pushback gets concessions, then the 5% pay less in expected value. the math says push back. plus the lease language stuff is a real defect, not a matter of taste. a tenant doesn't have an obligation to inspect the property quarterly because the landlord wants to skirt the warranty rules. that's just illegal. so i'm going to push.

side calculation: the pena convencional clause says i'd owe 3 months' rent if i break the lease early. that's 99k mxn at the new rate. that's a meaningful number. but it's also the kind of clause that's almost never enforced — landlords use it as a deterrent, not as a revenue line. still, having it sit at 99k is an information asymmetry that benefits him. i'm going to push for 1 month's pena. that's the standard for a 12-month lease in cdmx.

ran a quick benchmark on the task-per-request model. no leak yet but i can see memory growing under sustained load — about 40mb over 30 minutes of constant traffic, which is too much. it's not catastrophic and the gc-equivalent might just be slow, but it's a smell. ran the same scenario in release mode to rule out debug allocator weirdness — same growth. i have a theory it's the spawn pattern interacting with a long-lived hyper connection pool somewhere, going to instrument tonight. i don't want to write this up to roberto because if i do, he's going to ask me for an estimate, and i can't estimate a problem i haven't found yet. so it stays in my notebook for now.

the load profile i'm running is about 50 rps with realistic stop counts (10-150 stops per request). it's not crushing the service — cpu sits at 12-15%, latency p95 is 38ms which is the headline number we want anyway. the problem is purely the memory shape. if it kept growing forever the kernel oomkiller would eventually slay us, and if i shipped this to prod we'd be slaying ourselves at 3am two weeks in. classic slow-bleed regression. the kind that doesn't show up in your demo because the demo is 30 seconds long and the bleed is 30 minutes long. i'll find it tonight.

methodology for the benchmark, since i'll need to redo it once i have a hypothesis: locust generating traffic against a local-binary instance with prod-shape postgres (postgis, 12gb dataset cloned from a recent backup), simulating 50 rps with 10-150 stops per request distributed log-normal. instrumentation: heaptrack on the rust process, prometheus scraping resident-set-size and tokio-task-count every 5 seconds, jaeger collecting traces. run for 30 minutes minimum, ideally 2 hours, and record the curves. then differential — same load profile but with task count fixed at one (i.e. running optimization inline on the request). if the inline version doesn't grow memory, the spawn pattern is implicated.

second thing i need to do is sample the task-spawn closures with `tokio-console` and see if any of them are stuck in 'idle (waiting)' state under load. tokio-console is the right tool for this and i should have been using it months ago. i bookmarked the docs in june and never read them. classic. tonight is the night the bookmark gets paid off.

if the result of the experiment is 'the spawn pattern is leaking', then the fix is one of: (a) bound the task pool to a fixed size with mpsc backpressure, (b) inline the optimization onto the request task and use cancellation tokens for the timeout, (c) use rayon for the parallel-search portion and let tokio handle only the i/o. option (b) is simplest. option (c) is fastest but adds rayon to the runtime. i'll know which once i see the data. tomorrow morning is the writeup.

andrés just pinged asking if i want him to run the scenario on his machine in parallel. yes. two boxes, two runs, easier to spot environmental noise. answered him, sent him the locustfile. small moments where the team functions like a team are still happening, even with roberto's standup theater. that's a real thing to notice. don't lose track of those signals.

the email to morales-ramos was 11 paragraphs long and i'm worried it's too long. but if i shorten it i lose the citations to the civil code articles, and the citations are what give it teeth. without them it's just 'i don't want to pay more'. with them it's 'these clauses violate articles X, Y, Z and i can document this'. the length is the value. i'll see what comes back.

addendum: i logged the email to my own backup folder with the subject 'morales_ramos_renewal_negotiation_2026' and tagged it 'lease/2026'. paper trail. if this ever ends up in a tribunal de arrendamiento i want everything saved with timestamps. and the language i used is unambiguously professional and citation-heavy. no insults, no pleading, no emotional appeals. just civil code articles and proposed alternatives. that's how you negotiate with a landlord who's been doing this for thirty years — you don't try to befriend him, you just speak landlord-language better than the other tenants.

técnico finally came. 'fixed' the boiler in 20 minutes. left without testing it with hot water actually running. we'll see at 6am tomorrow. odds: 30%. he wrote a one-line report on a folded piece of paper saying 'revisión efectuada, sin observaciones'. i took a photo for the file. asked him to sign and date it. he hesitated. signed it. that signed one-liner is now legal document number seven in the edificio folder. paper trail rule one in action. seven.

second friday at la polar with decibel readings, because i told myself i'd build a case and i meant it. tonight's data, with my phone running a calibrated decibel app on the kitchen counter (3 meters from the floor where the noise comes through, all windows closed, AC off):

20:00 — 48db. ambient. talkable. nube on the couch.

20:30 — 53db. the cantina is opening up for the night. low chatter, jukebox at a polite level. cool, fine.

21:14 — 61db. first banda starts. trumpets. they're playing 'la negra tomasa' which is correct for that hour.

21:45 — 67db. crowd has filled in. clapping. shouts.

22:30 — 70db. some idiot is doing 'el rey' on the karaoke. AGAIN. i think 'el rey' might be a la polar friday tradition and i need to make peace with the fact that i live above its temple.

23:00 — 72db. second band starts. mariachi-style with horns. they are louder than the first band by 3-4db, possibly because the PA was reset hotter.

23:45 — 76db. someone has gotten on the mic to do toasts. 'a la salud de mi compadre raúl' echoed through our floor like a deity addressing his subjects. raúl, congrats man, but please.

00:00 — license cutoff time. they keep playing.

00:30 — 78db. another fight breaks out, this one i could hear words from. someone called someone a 'pendejo agachón' which is so specifically rude that i had to write it down. nube under the bed.

01:00 — 81db peak. amplifier feedback. screaming feedback. someone in the crowd is yelling for it to stop, the band is still playing. the apartment vibrates.

01:25 — abruptly cuts to silence. unclear why.

01:25-01:50 — silence. eerie. i lie in bed listening to my own heart and processing the data. camila is on the couch with nube watching reels because the bedroom is unlivable.

01:51 — they start back up. reggaeton. 76db. why.

02:30 — finally over. 73db dropping to 60. i fall asleep around 03:00.

05:00 — doña marta's oven. like clockwork. you can set a watch by it.

so. data point two for the alcaldía complaint. i'm going to send the readings as an addendum next week, with a note that this is the second consecutive friday i've measured violations. i am turning into the noise-complaint guy. i hate the noise-complaint guy. but the rent went up and the principle is the principle and someone has to be the noise-complaint guy.

let me also document my methodology because if i'm going to do this every friday i want it to be defensible:

- decibel app: 'decibel x pro' on iphone, calibrated against a reference SPL meter at the gym (max divergence 1.5db across tested ranges).
- measurement location: kitchen counter, 3m from the floor where the cantina noise transmits.
- environment: all windows closed, AC off, no music or tv playing in apartment, no human conversation in apartment during readings.
- sampling: i note the highest value over a rolling 5-minute window, not instantaneous spikes.
- timestamping: phone clock, synced to network time.
- annotations: written in the same notes file, in spanish for the eventual alcaldía submission.

that's roughly the protocol the alcaldía guidelines describe. i'm overengineering it on purpose. if pacheco-energy ever escalates and someone tries to dismiss my complaints, i want them to know they're dealing with someone who has actual data. and the chain of custody is clean because everything is timestamped and saved to icloud and i can produce screenshots of the source.

i'm also keeping a parallel log of the calls i make to la polar. so far: tonight i did not call them — the first friday i learned that calling does nothing, so i'm preserving evidence that i tried the soft path once and it failed, rather than calling repeatedly and creating a 'you only escalated' counter-narrative for the alcaldía. the alcaldía form has a section for 'medidas tomadas por el ciudadano antes de presentar la denuncia' and 'una llamada al establecimiento que fue ignorada' is exactly the right entry. anything more would weaken the formal complaint by suggesting i didn't pursue formal channels promptly.

camila, who is normally the one who tells me to chill out, was the one who said 'send the addendum' tonight. she said 'estamos pagando 33k y no podemos dormir, eso no es vivir, eso es estar pagando'. which is a sentence so accurate i'm putting it on a t-shirt.

the weirdest part — and i'll stop in a sec — is that i actually like la polar. in the daytime. the food is good. the abuelos who play dominos at 4pm are great. the place has soul. it's a cdmx institution. but the cdmx institution should not be running my apartment building's friday vibe. there's a way for it to exist without us being acoustically waterboarded every weekend. the alcaldía could enforce decibel limits with a single inspection. the cantina could install acoustic foam on the ceiling — there's a roma sur cantina that did exactly this two years ago after a similar complaint and the upstairs tenants stopped filing — i know about this because pablo's wife josefina is friends with one of the upstairs tenants. so it's not unprecedented. it's just lazy ownership and lazy enforcement and i'm the one stuck above it.

the other thing i'm going to do, separately from the alcaldía, is research what tenant rights i have under cdmx noise abatement law specifically. there's a thing called 'derecho a la habitación pacífica' that's part of the civil code and that, in theory, would let me sue the landlord (not the cantina, the landlord) for failure to ensure peaceful enjuyment of the leased property. i don't actually want to sue him. but i want to know that i could, because that's what changes the negotiating posture at next year's rent renewal.

extra evidence i'll add to the addendum next week, things i didn't think to capture friday-of: a video from the kitchen pointed at the floor (you can see the bookshelf vibrating, audio was distorted by my phone's mic but the visual is conclusive), a screenshot of the la polar instagram from saturday morning where they posted a video from inside the cantina at 01:14am with live amplification audible (their own social media is now my evidence — they advertised the violation), and a screenshot of the building's whatsapp group where doña marta's son (who lives one floor above me, on the 6th) complained at 02:13am that he could 'no se puede dormir, otra vez la polar'. so it's not just me. there's a pattern across multiple units in the building. that's the kind of detail that, if the alcaldía actually inspects, will let them survey other tenants for corroboration.

i'm also going to draft a one-page document titled 'cronología de molestias acústicas - calle orizaba [redacted] - junio a octubre 2026' with all eleven incidents tabulated, dates, times, decibel data where available, weather conditions (because outdoor amplification is partly weather-dependent), and any cantina events i could verify from their social media. that document i'll attach to every future complaint as appendix-A. consistency across submissions matters. each new complaint that references the same chronological exhibit reinforces the pattern in the alcaldía's record.

ok done. going to sleep. nube has joined me on the bed and she's snoring louder than la polar at this point.

footnote on the methodology: i should also document AC-off as a deliberate choice. running AC creates a 4-5db floor of fan noise and would mask the true ambient. friday nights cdmx in november do not require AC; the apartment is at 19c without it. so AC-off is honest and would also be the typical state of the apartment when residents are trying to sleep. that's the right baseline. i'm noting it because if pacheco-energy ever reads my submission and tries to claim 'these readings exclude HVAC noise' i can point to the ambient values being entirely consistent with a quiet residential interior.

second footnote: doña marta's son's whatsapp complaint at 02:13am, which i screenshotted, also has location metadata in the image (he photographed his bedroom clock). that's corroborating geo-evidence from a second unit. cdmx alcaldía cases that go to enforcement usually have at least 2-3 unit complaints; mine is now solo with one corroborating screenshot, which is decent. if i can get one more apartment to file in parallel — pablo on the 7th has hinted he's annoyed too — that's a stronger pattern.

found the memory thing — almost. instrumented the spawn paths and the growth correlates with route-planning requests that include more than ~80 stops, which i didn't have realistic test data for. the growth is bounded per-request but not freed until the executor is dropped. i have a working hypothesis: a vec is being moved into a closure that the spawn captures, and rust is conservatively keeping the original allocation alive longer than i expected. need to verify. classic case of 'the benchmark is right, the load profile is wrong'. carla is going to laugh at me when she's back. i'd rather catch this now than in prod.

the annoying meta-lesson here is that i wrote synthetic load tests that hit the request rate i'd expect at 2x prod traffic, but the request shape was uniform — 25 stops per request — because that's the median. i didn't model the long tail. real prod traffic has a fat tail, with 5% of requests over 80 stops (from the bigger b2b clients with full-truckload routes), and the bug only triggers in that tail. so my synthetic test was correct on volume and wrong on shape. shape matters as much as volume in load testing and i forgot that. i will not forget that again. mental note: when modeling load, always grab a real distribution from prod, not your guess at a distribution.

second meta-lesson: i ran the original benchmark before the postgis encoder was wired in, so the latency curve i was looking at was incomplete — postgis adds ~3-4ms p95 because of the geometry parsing, which doesn't matter for correctness but matters for sizing the runtime budget. should have rerun the bench after every meaningful integration step. i didn't, because each rerun takes ~2 hours and i had a deadline-shaped feeling. the deadline-shaped feeling lied to me. you don't save time by skipping the rerun. you just discover it later under worse conditions.

i pulled production traffic samples from last week — anonymized but preserving stop-count distribution — and re-shaped my locustfile. now my synthetic load looks like prod load. ran it for 90 minutes. the memory growth pattern reproduced cleanly: ~40mb over 30min, ~120mb over 90min, no plateau. so the growth is monotonic in the long tail of large requests. that confirms the hypothesis. now i need to actually find the line of code. that's tomorrow.

andrés's parallel run gave me data point two: same growth pattern on his box. so it's not environmental. it's the code. wrote that into the channel, put a 'memory regression confirmed, root cause hunt continues' bullet in the standup notes for tomorrow. roberto will read that and probably ask for an estimate. i'm going to write 'unknown — bug bounty open, will update when found' and let him do whatever he does with that. estimates on root-cause hunts are not estimates. they are guesses dressed in suit jackets.

family update: mom called from gdl today. she asked about camila and the kid question. i deflected. she asked about work. i deflected. she asked when i was coming to visit. i said 'december, navidades'. she said 'estás bien, mijo?' i said 'sí, ma'. i was not bien. but mom's role is not to absorb my bad weeks; she has her own life. or so i thought, until friday. anyway, today she sounded fine on the phone. dad was in the background asking what i wanted for christmas dinner. they're being parents. it's nice.

later: roberto sent a slack DM saying 'hey seb, i'd love to see your priorities for the quarter — could you put together a 1-pager?'. i started writing one and then realized this is exactly the kind of thing i should NOT spend a friday on, because it's the manager-comfort doc, not the work doc. so i wrote a thirty-line bullet list with two diagrams and sent it. roberto said 'great, this is perfect, thanks!' which means it was. ugh.

— end of day note: i closed the laptop at 7:30, walked nube around the block, and made sure to NOT think about roberto for an hour. limited success. but the discipline is the thing. without the discipline you become someone who is roberto in your head 24/7. i'm not going to be that. brown belt me has gym tomorrow at 7am sparring round.

camila got an email from the firm partner that the pritzker mentor-prize jury wants her to come to a panel in barcelona in march. all expenses paid. she came home and showed it to me on her phone like she was trying to play it cool, and i could see her hand shaking a tiny bit. i grabbed her face and said 'is this a yes?' and she said 'i think this is a yes', and we sat on the kitchen floor for ten minutes laughing while nube tried to climb on us. the rent shock and roberto and la polar all faded for one whole evening. she's going to barcelona. she's going to be on a pritzker panel. mi novia.

she wants me to come with her. four days, gaudí, sagrada familia, the panel is one afternoon and the rest is hers (ours). i'll move heaven and earth at brújula to be there. roberto can have his standup-2.0 without me for a week. camila has spent eighteen months grieving and showing up for everyone else and tonight is the first time in a long time that something has happened TO her that is unambiguously good, and i want to be in the room when she says her thing in barcelona. flights are cheaper than i expected if we book now. i'm doing it tonight.

i booked. cdmx-mad-bcn on aeroméxico via madrid, depart march 14, return march 21. seven days total. she's on the panel march 17. that gives us three days before and three days after. hotel near the panel venue, also booked — small place in eixample her firm recommended. all booked under camila's name as primary because i want the pritzker hotel reservation to have her name on it. small thing, real thing.

other thing i did tonight: pulled out our wedding-of-her-mother letter from the funeral last march. the letter beatriz wrote camila for her 28th birthday two years before she died. i hadn't read it in months. there's a line in it where beatriz says 'siempre supe que ibas a ser arquitecta, desde que con cinco años empezaste a dibujar planos de casas en mis cuadernos'. i showed it to camila tonight after we booked. she cried for ten minutes. i held her. then she said 'mi mamá no va a verme en el panel'. and i said 'ya sé, amor'. and that was the right answer. there isn't a better one. then she said 'pero si va a estar ahí en la maleta', and went and put the letter, folded, into her carry-on bag for march. we still have five months. it's going in early. that's how camila does grief — by giving it a seat at the table early and letting it stay through the meal.

i also messaged hugo sr in são paulo to tell him about the panel. he responded within ten minutes — 'qué orgullo, mi camilita, beatriz estaría tan feliz'. then he sent a follow-up: 'voy a barcelona también'. so hugo is flying in. so it's a family event. it just got bigger. camila doesn't know yet. i'm going to let her sleep on the news of the panel itself before i tell her hugo's coming. one celebration per night, that's the household rule i just invented.

the kicker: roberto put the new standup on my calendar with title '9:00 daily standup (new)' and a description that says 'cameras on, please be prompt, agenda below'. there is no agenda below. it's a calendar invite for a meeting with no agenda where the only rule is 'cameras on'. that's the entire pitch. i accepted it because not accepting would be a flag. but i accepted it with notes-to-self that i need a strategy for surviving 9am cameras-on five days a week.

— i told camila about the 9am over dinner. she said 'sebastián, tenés que aprender a no llevarte el laburo a la noche'. i said 'es que el laburo es la noche'. she said 'tu laburo no es la noche, es que vos hacés que sea la noche'. fair. she's not wrong. i schedule the discomfort. i could schedule it differently. roberto is real but my response to roberto is also a choice. i'll think about it.

made a habanero-mango hot sauce. too much vinegar. needs more roast on the habaneros. v2 next week. labeled the bottle 'la polar v1' as a private joke. not entirely a joke. recipe notes for v2: roast habaneros 8 min instead of 5, drop vinegar by 30%, add a pinch of cumin to deepen the back-end. mango ratio is right. lime juice can stay. small batch of 200ml. shared with pablo. he said 'es bueno pero le falta huevo'. fair.

lease signed. with my pushback edits — they accepted the maintenance-window definition (7 days for critical, 30 for non-critical), they pushed back on the termination clause and we landed on 30 days notice / no penalty as a compromise. they removed the 15-day-guest clause. they kept the pet clause locked to nube specifically, which i let go because at this point i wanted it done. on the inpc clause i got them to change 'mayor' to 'menor', which was the win i'm most proud of even though they'll probably try to renegotiate it next year. on the fiador clause they accepted 'continúa el régimen anterior basado en historial de pago' which is real-estate spanish for 'we trust you for one more year because you've paid on time for 33 months'. i am now legally on the hook for 33k mxn a month for 12 months. the boiler is still cycling cold every other morning despite the técnico visit. but i have a paper trail. and a contract clause. and a 30-day exit option. mi cabrón.

the signing itself was at a notario in colonia roma, 2pm tuesday. señor pacheco didn't come — sent his daughter, who was actually nice, asked about nube, said her dad 'es un poco difícil pero buena gente'. the notario was efficient, ran us through the document in 25 minutes, made me read clause 18 out loud to confirm the 'menor' edit, signed, stamped, done. signing fee 1500 mxn split between us. i ate a quesadilla at a stand around the corner after, alone, with the contract folded in my back pocket like a passport. it felt strangely formal — like i'd just done a small adult thing right.

the clause-by-clause win/loss tally: pushed back on six clauses, got concessions on four (clauses 7, 9, 12, 18). kept pet clause as-is (loss). kept fiador-equivalent (loss but acceptable). kept rent at 33k (lost the macro fight as expected). on a 6-clause negotiation, 4-2 is a respectable score for the inquilino side. and the inpc-mayor-to-menor change is the kind of clause that compounds. if cdmx inflation runs at 5% next year and pacheco wants to raise rent 10% by negotiation, the new clause forces him to accept the lower number. that's structurally better than what i had before, even though the headline number went up.

the other small but psychological win: the lease is now in a single signed pdf saved to dropbox and printed twice — one copy for the edificio folder, one for camila's filing cabinet at the foster + partners office because she said she wanted a backup not in our apartment. that's the level of paranoia we're now operating at. earned, i think. paranoia is a coping mechanism that becomes infrastructure once you do it long enough. roberto would call it 'risk hygiene'. i call it not getting hosed by my landlord twice. either way it's the same act.

sidebar: i drafted a private google doc called 'roberto management strategies' which is a list of how i'm going to manage him without him noticing. this is the doc you write when your manager is not actually managing you, you're managing him. items so far: 1. always have status updates pre-baked for the 9am, even if they're nothing-burgers. 2. cc daniela on PRs that need review so roberto sees daniela's signoff and doesn't need to chime in. 3. answer slack within 30 minutes during work hours so he never gets to write 'just checking in'. 4. prepare 1:1 agendas in advance so they don't become 'roberto rambles about his MBA capstone'. it's a lot of work. it's also necessary work.

— i also logged a personal kpi: 'days until i seriously consider leaving brújula'. i'm at 47. if it gets to 90 i'll start talking to recruiters. if 120 and nothing has changed (or has gotten worse), i'll start interviewing. that's the timeline. mit applications are in december. between now and then i have a parallel optionality track. that's how you survive a roberto without doing anything stupid.

— the parallel optionality track also includes: dust off the linkedin (last update 2024), update the personal site, push two more PRs to the open-source rust crates i contribute to, draft the mit application essay one more time. five lines on a notes-app list, but each one is a card i'd want in hand if leaving brújula becomes the move.

pablo and i sparred three rounds. i ate a head kick in round two. ego bruised. ribs fine. pablo apologized like four times even though it was clean. brown belt humbling at scale. he caught me telegraphing the level change i was setting up — i drop my hand half a second before i shoot the cross. he saw it. capitalized on it. went home and re-watched the round on the gym camera and yeah. fix the tell. or lean into it as a feint. either is fine. the current version isn't.

weird month. like every domain of life decided to renegotiate its terms in the same four weeks. rent went up. boss changed and started running standup like a battle drill. cantina downstairs decided it was a war zone. couples therapy paused. brown belt earned and barely processed. and the rust thing finally stopped fighting me. i'm tired in that very specific way where everything is technically fine and you're technically ok and the cumulative weight of 'fine' is what's taking you down. camila said tonight 'maybe we should take a weekend somewhere'. she's right. we should. valle de bravo, oaxaca, anywhere that doesn't have a la polar under it.

the pattern i'm noticing — and this is the part i can write down here but couldn't say to camila — is that none of these things alone would be a problem. each is an annoyance, an adjustment, a new piece of admin in a city that runs on admin. but the simultaneity is what's hitting me. like four medium-tempo songs playing at once, none of them a banger, but the polyphony is exhausting. the right move is probably to dial something down on purpose — say no to a thing — instead of trying to handle them all on the same volume.

things i could dial down: (1) stop filing the noise complaints monthly and let it go to quarterly, (2) skip a kickbox class per week through the holidays, (3) stop tracking roberto's micro-aggressions in the doc i started, (4) tell camila i'm taking a week off the kid conversation, (5) ignore the inbox on saturdays. each of those is a small constraint relax. each of them frees a finite amount of attention. but each of them also has a cost — the noise pattern needs sustained pressure to become enforceable, the kickbox cadence is tied to body recovery from the back, the roberto doc is paper trail for review season, the kid conversation can't be paused without making camila feel evaded, and the inbox-saturday rule i've never managed to keep anyway. so each relax has a cost.

the version of this i'm actually going to do: skip kickbox tuesday this week (sub yoga at home), don't measure decibels next friday (let one weekend lapse), don't preview the roberto 1:1 agenda on sunday (let me show up cold). three small relaxes. see how the week feels. if it feels lighter i do it again. if it feels worse i revert. small experiments, not big pivots. that's the rule when everything is tilted.

the bigger meta-thought, the one i haven't written down yet: nothing about my situation is exceptional. people in cdmx live with 4-5 stresses simultaneously all the time. middle-class life is built out of compounding admin. the difference between 'fine' and 'not fine' is not the count of stresses, it's whether you have ten minutes a day where nothing is asking anything of you. i used to have those minutes on the porch with yerba mate. they got eaten by slack and routeopt. i need to claw them back. the experiment starts tomorrow.

the morales-ramos response came back today. he agreed to drop the increase from 14% to 9% (28k → 30.5k) and to remove the quarterly inspection clause. he did not budge on the maintenance-burden language. i agreed to 9% with the inspection removed and pushed back one more time on maintenance, this time citing article 2412. he replied 'señor castellanos, le aseguro que estamos siendo razonables, mucho más que otros propietarios en la zona. firme o no firme'. so. 9% it is. final final.

— filing the signed lease on monday. doing it in person at morales-ramos's office on calle puebla because i want to physically hand it to him and shake his hand and look him in the eye. that's also how you negotiate the next lease — by being someone he remembers. i'll take a copy of the signed contract for myself and one for camila. i'm scanning it to icloud the same day.

— camila's reaction at the door when i told her it was 9% not 14%: she said 'ok'. that's it. i was hoping for more. she did say 'good job, sebastián' later, while we were brushing teeth, after she'd thought about it. delayed praise. it counts.

second alcaldía complaint addendum filed with the new decibel readings. confirmation says they have a backlog. the backlog is six months. so realistically nothing happens until april and by then it'll be a different season's problem. but it's filed. one day if la polar tries to renew their license i want this dossier to be heavy. ten complaints heavy. i'm going to keep doing this every month even if nothing happens with each one individually. it's the only revenge that doesn't cost me anything but time, and i have time at 2am every other friday.

camila printed copies of both filings (yes, paper, like an old man) and put them in a folder labeled 'edificio'. the folder is filling up. the lease, the addenda, the alcaldía confirmations, the screenshot of the boiler whatsapp chain. she said 'cuando renegociemos en un año, este folder va a ser nuestro mejor argumento'. she's right. she's always right. i love this woman.

inventory of the edificio folder as of tonight: (1) signed lease, all pages, both copies. (2) original whatsapp from pacheco announcing the rent increase. (3) my response email to him. (4) the call transcript i wrote up after our boiler call. (5) the new contract draft with my redline. (6) the final signed contract. (7) the boiler técnico's signed one-liner. (8) screenshots of the messages with pacheco's daughter scheduling and rescheduling the visit. (9) alcaldía complaint #1, the friday-night decibel readings. (10) alcaldía complaint #2, the second friday's data. (11) the cronología appendix with all eleven historical incidents. (12) printed copies of the la polar instagram posts that are evidence of the live amplification. (13) doña marta's son's whatsapp screenshot from 02:13am. (14) the building whatsapp group screenshot showing other tenants complaining. that's fourteen documents. four months ago there was nothing in this folder. today there is fourteen.

camila said tonight 'sabes que esto es una hobby a estas alturas, verdad?' and i said 'sí pero un hobby útil' and she said 'todos los hobbies se justifican como útiles, mi vida'. fair. i'm still doing it.

the meta-thing — and i wrote this in my notebook because i don't want to forget — is that the folder is also a way of converting helplessness into agency. i can't make pacheco fix the boiler faster. i can't make la polar quieter. i can't make roberto a better manager. but i can document. and the act of documenting changes the felt experience of being in the situation, even when the situation itself doesn't change. the documents create a feeling of progress where the underlying reality has none. i don't know if that's healthy or just a coping mechanism dressed in legal pages. probably both. either way the folder is going to keep growing. ten months from now, when it's renewal time, it will be the heaviest thing in the apartment.

camila put a single page on top of the folder labeled 'objetivos para la próxima negociación' with three bullets: (1) renta congelada o aumento por debajo de inpc, (2) cláusula de mantenimiento con plazos definidos, (3) opción de salida en seis meses sin penalización. her handwriting. her three bullets. she's already strategizing for october 2027. that's how this woman operates. i'm just along for the ride.

camila and i celebrated by ordering pizza from el primer al pastor — vegetarian, with mushrooms and rajas, the way she likes it — and watching a movie called 'la civil' that we'd had on the queue for a year. it was a hard film but a good one. we did not talk about the lease for two hours, which i'm counting as a win.

— and on the way home from picking up the pizza camila asked 'cuándo es la próxima sesión con la doctora ramírez' (the couples' counselor). i said 'todavía estamos pausados'. she said 'sé, pero cuándo'. i said 'enero, después de las fiestas'. she said 'ok'. that's pause-2 ending in january, in theory. let's see if we actually go.

— other tiny win this week: nube has finally accepted the new cat scratcher. it took her three weeks. she now uses it twice a day. cat-training-with-camila has a 100% success rate over zero years of marriage. i'm putting that in the relationship-positives column.

ok i need to write this down because it just happened and my heart is somewhere around my throat. mom called twenty minutes ago. she never calls in the afternoon, she calls sundays at 11am like a metronome, so when i saw 'mamá' at 3:47pm on a tuesday i picked up before the second ring.

here's the call as best i can transcribe it. i'm doing this partly because i need it on the record and partly because writing it out is the only way i can stop my brain from looping it.

me: 'hola ma, todo bien?'
ma: 'hijo. estoy bien. pero tengo que decirte algo y quiero que estés tranquilo.'
me: '...'
ma: 'me toqué algo en el cuello. una bolita. del lado izquierdo, debajo de la oreja, más o menos del tamaño de una canica. la doctora dice que probablemente es un lipoma o un ganglio, pero quiere hacer una tomografía para estar seguros.'
me: 'cuándo te la encontraste?'
ma: 'la semana pasada. no quería preocuparte sin saber qué era. tu papá me dijo que te llamara hoy sin esperar más.'

(i had to mute and tell camila who walked into the kitchen. she sat down on the floor next to nube. unmute.)

me: 'ma. cuándo es la tomografía?'
ma: 'el viernes. en el hospital méxico americano. la doctora velasco la pidió.'
me: 'voy a guadalajara. compro vuelo hoy.'
ma: 'no, hijo, no exageres, espera al resultado.'
me: 'no es exagerar. voy.'
ma: 'tu papá te va a decir que no exageres.'
me: 'mi papá puede decirme lo que quiera, voy.'
ma: 'está bien. te quiero, hijo.'
me: 'te quiero, ma.'

she hung up and i sat at the kitchen table for like fifteen minutes just looking at the wall. camila brought me yerba mate and didn't say anything for a while. then she said 'la mayoría de los lipomas y los ganglios son benignos, te lo digo como hija de doctora'. which is true. her mom was an internist. her mom also died of pancreatic cancer at 65. she didn't say that part. neither did i.

let me unpack the conversation because i keep replaying it and the replays are noisier than the original:

the pause. when i said '...' it's because i was processing the word 'bolita' in spanish, which technically just means 'little ball', but in mexican medical context bolita is what you call a thing on your body that you don't yet have a word for. you don't say bolita for a known thing. you say lipoma or quiste or ganglio. you say bolita when the diagnosis is open. mom is a retired pediatric nurse. she knows the medical vocabulary. she chose 'bolita'. that's a tell.

'estoy bien. pero tengo que decirte algo'. the structure of this sentence is mom's whole personality. she leads with the reassurance because she's reassuring herself as much as me. she puts the bad news in the second clause to soften it. dad does the same thing — 'todo está bien. pero el doctor dice que necesito ajustar la dosis del statin'. it's a family vocal tic that is also a family coping mechanism.

'la semana pasada' for when she found it — she's been sitting with this for at least seven days alone. she went to dr velasco probably 4-5 days ago. the CT was scheduled for friday, today is tuesday, so the scan is in 3 days. the protective layer of secrecy was a full week, plus however long she'd been touching the lump and not telling anyone before going to dr velasco. i don't get to know that part. she's the only person who knows that part and she will never tell me.

'tu papá me dijo que te llamara hoy sin esperar más'. so dad knew. dad has been holding this with her, alone, for at least several days, and dad is the one who finally said 'tell seb'. dad is 66 and has been managing his own angina for two years and now he's also been managing mom's not-quite-named lump for a week. i have to call dad next.

'no exageres' is mom's instinctive response to me saying i'd fly out. she always says that. she said it in 2019 when abuela lupita was dying and i was deciding whether to fly back from cdmx and she said 'no exageres, está estable'. abuela died ten days later. i was at work. i did not see her again. there is a thing in mexican families where the parent will tell you not to come because they don't want to be the reason you take time off, and you have to know to ignore them. i ignored her in 2019 because she said 'estable' and i believed her. abuela is dead. i'm not making that mistake again.

so. let's stay rational. mom is 62, retired pediatric nurse, has been a nurse her entire adult life and she is the kind of person who doesn't go to a doctor unless it's bad, but she's also the kind of person who knows what nodes feel like because she's palpated them on a thousand kids. so the fact that she went to dr velasco and the fact that dr velasco ordered a CT and not a watch-and-wait is meaningful. it could be meaningful in the boring direction (a CT is just standard for any neck mass over a certain size, even probable benign ones) or meaningful in the not-boring direction.

i called dad five minutes after mom hung up. he picked up immediately, which is itself unusual, dad usually lets the phone ring. he said 'mijo'. i said 'pa'. he said 'gracias por hablar'. i said 'cuándo te enteraste'. he said 'el lunes pasado, ella me lo dijo el lunes y yo le dije que te llamara'. so a full week. he sat with it for a full week with her and convinced her to call me. then he said 'no le digas a tu mamá que estás preocupado, finge que estás tranquilo, ella necesita que tú estés tranquilo'. i said 'sí, pa'. then i said 'tú estás bien?' and he was quiet for a few seconds and said 'estoy bien, mijo. ven'. that 'ven' was the most direct request my dad has made of me in maybe a decade. he doesn't ask. he hints. 'ven' is not a hint.

i'm not going to spiral on the not-boring direction yet. i'm going to:

1. book a flight to guadalajara for thursday.
2. tell roberto i'm taking a personal week, starting friday, possibly more.
3. message daniela because she still has my back even though she's gone.
4. tell camila i need her here when i call mom each day from gdl, which she will already do without being asked.
5. not google neck masses and CT scans. not google. not google. i will google. ok i won't google for the rest of today. tomorrow is a different question.

i keep thinking about her saying 'no quería preocuparte sin saber qué era'. mom always does this. she had her gallbladder out in 2019 and didn't tell me until two days before the surgery. dad does the angina episodes the same way. they're the kind of parents who think 'no preocupar' is a love language. it isn't. but it is also kind of beautiful in a way that hurts. they're trying to spare me. and i'm trying to be ten years old looking at her again going 'why didn't you tell me sooner ma'.

breathing exercise time. brown belt me, where you at. i'm going to go three rounds on the heavy bag tonight at atlas box even though it's not a training night, because the only way i sleep is if i empty the tank physically first. and then i'm going to pack a bag. and then i'm going to call dad again, just to hear his voice once more before tomorrow. and then i'm going to lie in bed and stare at the ceiling and probably not sleep. that part is unavoidable. brown belt me cannot punch through that one. only thursday morning at the gdl gate will reset the nervous system.

booked the flight. aeroméxico, cdmx-gdl, thursday morning, returning the following thursday. one-way would have been smarter in case she needs me longer but the round-trip was 1800 mxn cheaper and i'll change it if i need to. also told roberto in slack that i'd be out next week for family medical reasons. he replied within four minutes: 'of course. take what you need. let me know if there's anything we should re-prioritize.' which was — fine? actually decent? i was bracing for some bullshit and i didn't get any. i should give him that. one decent moment doesn't undo standup norms 2.0 but it's noted.

daniela also responded — i'd messaged her at the same time, just to vent, just because she was always the person at brújula who'd know what to say in a moment like this — and her reply was 'cabrón, mucha fuerza, dile a tu mamá que estoy pensando en ella. y si necesitas algo del trabajo o si roberto se pone raro, me hablas. en serio.' i read that twice. there's something deeply settling about having a former manager who is still your manager in the ways that count.

i also messaged pablo because pablo is the only person who has met my mom in person — they hit it off during her last cdmx visit two years ago, mom fed him three plates of pozole, and they have a private whatsapp thread where pablo sends her photos of his lab work and she sends back nutrition advice. pablo replied within thirty seconds: 'cabrón, qué necesitas, te llevo al aeropuerto'. i said 'no, gracias, voy a uber'. he said 'wey, te llevo al aeropuerto, no estás discutiendo esto conmigo'. so he's driving me thursday morning at 5am. that is a thing i do not deserve and will accept anyway.

camila has been quiet but moving. she went to the pharmacy for melatonin because she knows i won't sleep tonight. she put the dog bed near our bed instead of in the living room because she knows nube being close helps me. she made hibiscus tea at 9pm because she knows the chamomile brand i prefer is out and she doesn't want me to find that out at 11pm when i go looking for it. she pulled my old gdl playlist from spotify and put it on low in the kitchen — songs i listened to in the car driving from cdmx to gdl five years ago when i was still doing that drive monthly. she didn't say 'i'm doing these things for you'. she just did them. that's what 5 years of relationship looks like in real time. that's the love i don't deserve and would die for. tonight, in particular, i'd die for it twice.

mom's voicemail was clipped — i think the carrier cut it off — and she did not call again. that's not like her. she usually calls again the next day to repeat herself. the absence of the followup call was the second signal. the first signal was the 'no me llames' phrasing in the voicemail. i listened to the 14 seconds of voicemail four times before deciding to call her back. tía isabela picked up. that's when the actual conversation happened.

— before i hung up tía isabela said 'sebastián, no le dejes a tu madre sola con esto. ella es fuerte pero ahora le toca dejarse cuidar'. that's the line that put me on the plane. tía isabela is a retired pediatric nurse and she does not waste sentences. when she tells you to fly, you fly.

— also: i looked at my phone log. mom had called twice that morning before the voicemail. both unanswered. i was in standup. i'd silenced her calls without thinking — phone in airplane mode for the meeting. the silenced calls are now their own data point. i had not picked up. that's the part i'm sitting with.

— after the call with tía isabela i sat on the floor of the kitchen for ten minutes and let nube climb on me. she did the kneading thing on my chest. her purr is exactly the frequency that you're supposed to use as a stress-down tool. cats are good for this kind of week. she did not understand what was happening. she understood that i was sad and that the floor was the right place. that was enough.

i googled. of course i googled. it's 11pm and i told myself i wouldn't but here we are. i'm going to write down the spiral so i can look at it tomorrow and feel ridiculous, because the only way out of a webmd spiral is to expose it to daylight.

starting query: 'lump on neck below ear adult woman'. results page is — in this order — three medical-tourism clinics in mexico city, two webmd articles, a cancer.org page, and a reddit thread titled 'i found a lump'. i clicked the cancer.org first because i have some discipline.

cancer.org says: most neck lumps in adults are benign. lipomas, sebaceous cysts, swollen lymph nodes from minor infection, salivary gland stones. ok. fine. but it also says: neck masses in adults over 40 that persist longer than two weeks should be evaluated for malignancy. 'evaluated for malignancy' is a phrase that, once you've read it, you cannot unread. mom is 62. mom said 'last week'. which is technically less than two weeks but who's counting. i'm counting. she's been feeling it for at least seven days, possibly longer. probably two weeks. probably more. she would have downplayed when she found it.

webmd next: same content but more flowery. 'persistent unexplained lump' is the phrase here. there's a list of 'red flag features': hard, fixed (doesn't move under skin), painless, growing, accompanied by night sweats / weight loss / fatigue. mom on the call sounded fine. she sounded tired. she didn't mention sweats or weight loss but she also wouldn't, because 'no preocupar'. i went and looked at the photos i have of her from her last visit to cdmx — six weeks ago. i was looking for whether she'd lost weight. it's hard to tell from a photo. she might have. she might not have. i can't tell. that's the worst kind of evidence — the ambiguous kind that the brain treats as confirmation either way.

reddit thread: a guy thirty years old found a lump, six replies saying 'mine was benign, don't worry', three replies saying 'mine was hodgkin lymphoma, get it checked'. i closed reddit. but not before reading two of the hodgkin replies in detail. one was a 28-year-old who'd had a lump for three months before going to a doctor. mom went to a doctor within a week of finding hers. that's a meaningful difference. mom is a nurse. she knows what 'don't sit on it' means.

back to a less alarming search: 'lipoma of neck'. lipomas are: soft, mobile, slow-growing, painless, almost always benign. the 'mobile' part is important — they move under the skin when you press them. mom said 'una bolita' and 'del tamaño de una canica' and didn't say whether it moves. i can't ask without sounding like i've been googling, which i was specifically not supposed to do.

'cervical lymphadenopathy adult': enlarged lymph nodes. usually reactive — your body fighting an infection, even one you don't notice. usually <2cm, soft, mobile, tender. usually resolves in a few weeks. one of the alternative causes: lymphoma. one of the alternative causes: metastatic head and neck cancer. the metastatic option requires a primary somewhere — usually a smoker, usually has had ENT symptoms — mom doesn't smoke, never has, no ENT symptoms i know about. lymphoma doesn't require a primary. lymphoma can show up out of nowhere. lymphoma is the bad-but-treatable bucket if it has to be a bad bucket.

'lymphoma neck symptoms': painless, persistent, slowly growing lymph nodes; b-symptoms (night sweats, weight loss, fevers). also more common in younger adults. mom is 62. mom does not have b-symptoms that she's mentioned but again. 'no preocupar'. survival rates for adult lymphoma — i don't even want to type the numbers — are actually pretty good for hodgkin if caught early. non-hodgkin is more variable. either way the next step from a CT is usually a biopsy. that's the path. CT first, biopsy second, then the actual diagnosis. there's still 5+ days between today and any diagnosis becoming concrete, regardless of what the CT shows.

then i went down a parallel road: 'CT scan neck mass what to expect'. 25-30 minute scan, with contrast (iodine-based, mom is fine with iodine i'm pretty sure), evaluator looks at: size, density, borders, lymph node involvement on the same side, other masses. the report typically comes back to the ordering doctor within 24-72 hours. dr beltrán said tuesday for results. that aligns. so the CT report is going to a head-and-neck specialist who will then call mom. the head-and-neck specialist part is concerning — that's what you'd call in for malignant or surgical workup — but it's also what you'd call in for any neck mass over a certain size for a definitive read, even benign ones. i couldn't tell from the websites whether dr beltrán is at the level of 'general radiologist' or 'cancer-focused specialist'. i didn't have the energy to dig further. i'll dig in gdl tomorrow. i can ask mom directly about dr beltrán's specialty without raising suspicion — it's a fair question for any son to ask.

at this point my hands are slightly cold. classic anxiety response. i recognize the spiral, i recognize that i'm doing it, and i continue to do it. i am drinking yerba mate at 11pm which is also a bad call. caffeine plus medical anxiety equals 4am thoughts.

camila just walked into the kitchen, looked at the laptop, and said 'no estás googleando, verdad'. i said 'no' and she said 'cierra la laptop, vámonos a la cama'. we went. i stared at the ceiling for a while. i listened to nube's breathing on the floor next to me. i thought about all the times mom took me to the doctor when i was a kid, the way she'd hold my hand in the waiting room and tell me a story about a bird so i wouldn't think about the needle. and i thought about beatriz, camila's mom, and how cancer doesn't ask before it picks who it wants. i thought about the church bell at the funeral in são paulo and how it was one note over and over for what felt like an hour. i didn't sleep until like 2am.

so. tomorrow. last day in cdmx before i fly. work tasks to wrap. a few things to set up so the team isn't blocked. flight is at 7:30am thursday. i'll be in gdl by 9. i'll go straight from the airport to my parents' house in zapopan. dad will pretend to be unfazed. mom will try to feed me. nube is staying with camila. i'll be back next thursday or whenever the results come and we know.

don't google. tomorrow's seb, you are not allowed to google. tonight's seb already burned that quota. and even if today's seb is right that the most likely outcome is benign — the spiral wasn't about getting the most-likely answer right, it was about emotionally pre-living all the worst answers so that if any of them turn out to be true i'll be 12 hours less surprised. it's not a coping strategy. it's an avoidance strategy disguised as a coping strategy. i learned this in the third therapy session that camila and i did with dr muñoz, where she pointed it out about how i deal with work stress. it didn't stop me applying it to my mom's neck tonight. progress is non-monotonic, etcetera.

the other meta-thing — and i can write this here because nobody is reading — is that i've been silently terrified of this exact phone call since beatriz died last spring. like, the funeral in são paulo broke a seal in my head and ever since then every late-afternoon call from a parent has carried this background dread. i hadn't admitted that to myself out loud until tonight. it's a kind of anticipatory grief, maybe? the brain pre-running the bad outcome on a 1% probability budget, every day, for eight months. you don't notice the cost of it because it's distributed. you notice when the call actually comes and your nervous system is already at 80% load and there's no headroom to absorb the shock. that's the place i'm typing from right now.

wrote the handoff doc for the week i'll be gone. andrés can run small fixes solo, the bigger things i flagged for next week when i'm back. wrote 'in case of emergency contact carla via emergency channel' even though she's on parental leave because she'd answer if it was actually critical. roberto reviewed the doc and added a section i didn't ask for called 'communication plan during absence' which is just 'seb will respond to slack within 24 hours during personal week'. i edited it to 'seb may not respond during personal week unless it's critical, please escalate via roberto'. he accepted the edit. i'm tracking small wins.

the handoff itself ended up being three pages, which is more than i wanted, but it covers: (1) current routeopt staging health (green), (2) the memory regression i just fixed and how to verify the fix held, (3) two open tickets andrés can drive (postgis index for region-bounded queries, and a cleanup of the deprecated python endpoints), (4) one ticket nobody touches without me back (the runtime sizing change for burst load — that's a 'don't break this while i'm gone' note), (5) the on-call rotation override (i was up next, andrés taking it instead, page him not me unless it's prod-down). i feel ok about the doc. it's the kind of thing carla would have written cleaner. but it'll do.

other work-things i wrapped before flying: closed all my open PR reviews, archived two slack channels i was the only active member of, set my OOO message to 'out for personal week, please loop andrés on routeopt and roberto on prioritization questions, will check messages once daily but expect 24h+ response time'. that last bit i debated — the 'once daily' part — because i don't want to actually check messages once daily, i want to ignore them entirely. but if i set 'will not check' and then prod blows up i'll regret it. compromise: once-daily, evening check, 30 min budget, no slack notifications enabled.

andrés took the handoff well. he asked two good questions about the runtime sizing ticket — both questions i should have anticipated in the doc — and i added paragraphs to address them before sending the final version. he's going to be fine. he's better than i was at his age. when i was three months in at kueski i was still embarrassed to ask senior engineers anything; he asks freely and it makes him grow faster. roberto, to his small credit, has been encouraging that. i'll log the credit even if i'm tracking the debits more carefully.

later: camila came home and asked 'cómo te fue'. i said 'mom has a lump'. she dropped her bag. she asked 'where' and i said 'cuello izquierdo'. she said 'cancer?' i said 'no sé, doctor doesn't know either, CT friday'. she said 'voy contigo si quieres' and i said 'no, vos quédate, alguien tiene que cuidar a la gata'. that is not why she should stay. she should stay because she has a deadline at her firm and we both know it. but neither of us said the real reason. we said the gata reason. that's couples' shorthand for things you can't quite say.

— before bed i made a 'mom checklist' on my notes app. items: book gdl flight thursday, tell roberto monday-friday off, reschedule kickbox sparring, send camila list of things in the fridge that need to be eaten, pre-fill nube's auto-feeder, charge the airpods (i'll need them on the plane), call insurance to ask if mexicali medical is covered (it should be — premier plan), pack lightly (zapopan november is 22c day 14c night).

— at 11pm i made yerba mate and stood at the kitchen window looking down at the calle orizaba light pollution and thought about probabilities. 1.5cm cervical mass in a 62-year-old non-smoker, no constitutional symptoms. literature says ~70% benign in this profile. lipoma, reactive node, sialolithiasis. ~30% requires further workup. of that 30%, lymphoma is the most probable malignancy at this site. lymphomas in this demographic are highly treatable, especially if early-stage. so the absolute worst case from this differential is something like a 15% probability of a treatable lymphoma. these numbers should be reassuring. they were not. probabilities are the wrong frame for a 62-year-old i love.

camila is being very camila about this, which is the best version of any partner. she's not panicking. she's not minimizing. she's being calm, present, doing the small caring things — packed me a snack bag for the flight, washed my favorite t-shirt without me asking, put a note in my carry-on that just said 'estoy contigo'. i found the note at the airport gate and i had to pretend to look at my phone for a minute. five years and she still knows the exact temperature of caring i can absorb without bursting into tears. she said 'call me when you land. i'm here. nube's here. take the time you need'. i'm a lucky cabrón.

she also did the thing she does when i'm spiraling — she didn't try to talk me out of the spiral, she just sat with me while it ran its course. last night around 1am i was in the kitchen googling, she came in, didn't tell me to stop, just made tea and sat across from me and said 'cuéntame qué encontraste'. and i told her. she listened. then she said 'ok. los números más probables son benignos. la siguiente semana lo vamos a saber. ahorita necesitas dormir'. she wasn't fixing me. she was sequencing me. that's the level of read i can't articulate when sober.

the other thing — and i didn't tell her this but i need to write it somewhere — is that camila losing her mom eight months ago has changed how she handles other people's medical fear. before beatriz, camila would have been the one freaking out. she would have been the one googling at 1am. now she's the one sitting across from the googler making tea. that's grief converted into competence. i don't know if i've articulated this to her. i should. when i'm back from gdl, when this resolves whichever way it resolves, i'm going to tell her that her steadiness this week is what made the week survivable.

she also asked, last night before bed, 'querés que vaya contigo a gdl?' and i said 'no, alguien tiene que estar con nube'. but the offer matters. she would have flown. she would have packed in twenty minutes. there is a version of camila that prioritizes me even over nube and over her foster + partners obligations and over the rent and over everything else, and i caught a glimpse of that version last night and it gave me the same emotional vertigo as that morning of the funeral when she put her hand on my hand on the plane and said 'gracias por venir'. the gratitude is the wrong direction. i should be the one saying gracias. but that's the deal we have, we both keep saying it to each other, and the loop is the thing.

before bed i sent camila two voice notes from the airport — one short ('te amo, en una hora pongo el avión') and one long-and-rambly about how nube was looking at me extra hard from the carrier when camila handed her over and how the carrier was doing the small cat shake the way it does at every airport visit and how i hated leaving her and how i'd be back. camila replied with a heart emoji and a photo of nube in our bedroom curled up on my hoodie.

— while i was packing camila came in and sat on the bed and watched me. she didn't say anything for a while. then she said 'sebastián, vení que sea lo que sea van a estar bien, los tres'. she meant me, mom, dad. she didn't say camila herself in that sentence which i noticed. she said 'los tres'. she's putting herself outside the family unit when describing the support structure i'd need if mom got bad news. that's not a fight; that's just an honest read of where she sits in the geography. i nodded.

— the cab on the way to airport was eerily quiet. the driver — guy named josé luis from xochimilco — asked if i was traveling for work or for family. i said 'family'. he said 'que dios la cuide'. i said 'gracias'. that was the entire conversation. cdmx cab drivers know when to say one thing and stop. it's a skill.

in guadalajara. landed at 9, dad picked me up because of course he did, said 'no era necesario que vinieras, mijo' twice on the way home, which is dad-speak for 'gracias por venir'. mom was in the kitchen making chilaquiles when we got there. she hugged me too long. her hands smelled like cilantro which i can't stand and which i will never tell her again, and she put a plate down and said 'no preguntes nada hasta que comas'. i ate. they had me eat half a plate of chilaquiles before the topic was allowed.

the CT is friday morning. 8am. hospital méxico americano on av. américas. dr velasco — mom's general practitioner for 20 years, who delivered me into the cdmx-to-zapopan move as a kid — referred her to a head-and-neck specialist named dr beltrán who's reading the scan. mom showed me the actual referral form and i held it like it was a tax document trying to bite me. the relevant text:

'paciente femenina 62 años, antecedentes médicos no relevantes, presenta masa palpable cervical izquierda, región submandibular, aproximadamente 1.5cm, móvil, no dolorosa, sin adenopatías cervicales adicionales palpables. solicito tomografía computarizada de cuello con contraste para descartar etiología neoplásica vs. benigna (lipoma, ganglio, quiste).'

— two things i clung to from this: 'móvil', 'no dolorosa'. mobile and painless. 'mobile' in this context is generally a good sign for benign — lipomas and reactive nodes are usually mobile, while malignant lumps tend to be fixed to surrounding tissue. 'no dolorosa' is more ambiguous; both benign and malignant can be painless. but the combination is not a panic signal. and the size — 1.5cm — is on the lower end of 'big enough to scan but not 'oh god' big'. for context, the cancer.org site i was reading at 11pm tuesday said neck masses under 2cm in adults are most often benign or reactive. 1.5cm is comfortably under that. and 'sin adenopatías cervicales adicionales palpables' — no other palpable nodes — is also a reassuring data point because if there were systemic involvement (lymphoma, metastasis) you'd expect more than one node.

— one thing that gave me a tight chest: 'descartar etiología neoplásica'. literally 'rule out neoplastic etiology'. that's the doctor explicitly listing cancer as something they want to rule out. that's standard language for any neck mass in an adult over 40 — you can't NOT rule it out, that's medicine — but seeing the word neoplásica written in reference to my mom did something to me that i'm choosing to lock in a box and revisit on saturday.

— the order matters: the differential is listed as 'neoplásica vs. benigna (lipoma, ganglio, quiste)'. dr velasco is putting neoplásica first. that could mean she ranks it as more likely, or it could just be the standard formal order in mexican medical referrals (you list the more serious thing first to make sure the radiologist takes it seriously). i don't know dr velasco's writing style well enough to interpret. i'm going to ask mom if she's seen dr velasco's other referrals — she has, dozens of them, she was a pediatric nurse for 35 years and they all looked like this — and mom will say 'es la forma estándar' and i will believe her because she would know.

i asked mom about dr beltrán and she said he's 'el especialista de cabeza y cuello más reconocido en gdl, gente le viene desde aguascalientes y monterrey'. so he's good. she said dr velasco specifically referred her to him because he's known for being conservative — 'no opera por opera' — which i parsed as 'doesn't recommend surgery unless he absolutely has to'. that's a good kind of specialist to have on your case if you're hoping the answer is 'benign, leave it alone'.

so. CT friday 8am. results probably tuesday. dr beltrán said he might be able to give a preliminary read same-day if it's clearly benign. mom is being calm in a way that makes me suspect she's been calm for a week and is finally letting herself rest now that i'm in the house. dad keeps making me yerba mate without me asking, which is his version of saying 'i'm scared too'. brother mateo is calling tonight from tijuana, wants to know if he should fly down. i told him to wait until results.

zapopan is the same as it always is. sun-bleached and bougainvillea-heavy. my old room still has my high school posters. i lay on my old bed for an hour and cried a little, which is the most embarrassing thing i could possibly admit but it's what happened. then nube wasn't here and i missed her. then mom called me to dinner. then we ate and watched the news and dad fell asleep in his chair and mom and i sat on the porch in the dark and didn't talk and that was enough.

the weirdest emotional thing about being here is the temporal vertigo. the house operates in a memory register: every room has a thing that happened to me in it as a child. dad in the chair is dad in the chair from when i was eight. mom in the kitchen is mom in the kitchen from when i was eight. but the people are also 25 years older and i'm a 33-year-old engineer here because my mom has a possible cancer scare and the past and the present are stacked on top of each other and i can't put them down. i hugged dad at the door tonight and felt how much smaller he is than he used to be. he used to lift me with one arm. he can't really lift things with one arm anymore. neither of them is going to live forever and that has been true since i was born and i have been not-thinking-about-it for 33 years and it is now thinking about itself, in this house, this week.

which is also probably what every adult child realizes the first time their parents have a real medical scare. i'm not special. but the specific texture of it — the kitchen calendar, the chair, the chilaquiles, the hands that smell like cilantro — is mine, and writing it down makes it less abstract.

other things noted from today, for the record because i don't want to forget: the neighbors across the street still have the same yellow door. the dog two houses down — a labrador named who-knows-now — is on his deck like always. the mailbox at the corner is repainted; mom mentioned the alcaldía came through last spring. the lemon tree dad planted in 2008 is now taller than the house. mom said she hasn't pruned it because dad won't let her. dad said the tree is 'doing its thing'. that's the most dad-physics quote possible. trees do their thing. tumors might not. i'm going to bed.

last log of the day before sleep: i made yerba mate at 11pm, sat on the porch, listened to the cicadas, and called camila. we didn't talk for long. she said 'cómo está'. i said 'bien, está fuerte'. she said 'y tú'. i said 'aquí'. she said 'te quiero'. i said 'te quiero'. five-second pause. she said 'mañana hablamos'. and we hung up. and the porch was dark, and the lemon tree was tall, and zapopan was quiet, and my mom was asleep in her room with my dad next to her, and tomorrow at 8am she gets a CT and we don't know yet what comes after that. i am going to bed and i am going to sleep, somehow, because brown belt me did three rounds on the heavy bag in the cdmx gym 12 hours ago and the body is finally tired enough that the brain might let go for a while.

postscript before sleep: dad just walked in to ask if i wanted yerba mate. i said yes even though i don't want it. he's making it because he wants to make me something. the pour, the bombilla, the temperature — same as he's done since i was a teenager and started drinking it. that's the ritual. friday is the CT. tonight is the yerba.

CT was this morning. mom was nervous in the waiting room and i could tell because she was rearranging her purse for fifteen minutes straight. i held her hand and we did the bird story she used to tell me — about a colibrí who couldn't decide which flower to land on — except this time she told it to me. role reversal, it caught me off guard. the scan itself took 25 minutes. she came out and said 'ya estuvo, no fue tan terrible'. dr beltrán said he'd review and call mom this afternoon if it was clearly benign, otherwise tuesday. we drove home. ate. waiting now. waiting is the hardest part. did not google.

on the way out of the hospital we passed the oncology floor signs and mom flinched almost imperceptibly. i pretended not to see it. dad noticed too — i caught his face in the elevator. neither of us said anything. one of those family micro-moments where everyone clocks the same thing and chooses, in the same instant, to not name it. that's how mexican families operate at maximum emotional load. we let the silence do the work.

logistics of the CT itself, for the record: arrived 7:30am, paperwork until 7:50, called in by a tech named lalo who was friendly and efficient. mom drank the contrast — iodine, cherry-flavored, she made a face — at 7:55. into the scanner at 8:05. the scanner is a siemens somatom, which is a midrange unit but solid; mom recognized the brand from the gdl pediatric hospital where she spent 30 years and said 'es bueno, nada malo aquí'. i appreciated her clinical confidence even in the moment of being the patient. coming out of the scanner, the tech showed her a frame on the screen — i couldn't see it, but mom looked at it and nodded like a peer. dr beltrán himself didn't appear; the radiologist on duty for that hour was someone else, and dr beltrán reviews remotely. that's standard.

the rest of the day in zapopan: mom did laundry, refused help. dad watered the lemon tree and read the paper. i worked on a sketch for hugo's cedar chess board on a yellow legal pad — dimensions, joint detail, where to put the magnetic embedding mateo suggested. ate carnitas at 2pm at a stand mom used to take me to as a kid; the cook is the son of the original guy and they've kept the recipe. by 4pm we were all in the living room pretending to read. by 6pm dr beltrán's office had not called. by 8pm we accepted the call wasn't coming today. ate dinner — sopa de fideo, mom's anxiety food. went to bed by 10. tomorrow is saturday. monday no answers. tuesday dr beltrán's office calls. four more days. the math of the wait is not math, it's just the staring at the calendar with the underlined-twice CT entry that's now done and the underlined-twice 'resultado martes' entry below it. that one i'm going to underline three times in mom's calendar tomorrow morning before she's awake. she'll know who did it. she'll smile.

a thing i forgot to mention: friday morning before the CT, while we were in the waiting room, mom held my hand and said 'gracias por venir, mijo'. she said it in the voice she uses when she doesn't want to make a big deal of something but it's a big deal. i said 'siempre, ma'. dad was on the other side of her, and his hand was on her other hand. we held her hand the whole 25 minutes she was inside.

— thing i won't forget: when mom came out of the CT machine she looked at me first, before dad. she's done that since i was small. it's the kind of thing only a son notices. dad is the husband; i am the firstborn-mom-helper; in moments of medical fear she goes to me first because i'm wired into the eldest-son responsibility script in our family. the fact that it still works at 33 is both moving and a little sad.

— from the waiting room, while mom was inside the CT, i texted camila: 'todo bien por ahora, ma adentro, papá conmigo en sala de espera'. camila replied with 'estoy contigo' and a photo of nube on the bed. right amount of texting. she didn't pile on. just 'estoy contigo'. she's good at this.

still waiting. dr beltrán didn't call today. so tuesday it is. four days. mom went to bed at 9pm with a book she didn't open. dad fell asleep in his chair. i sat on the porch. the cicadas were doing their thing. zapopan-cool wind from the north which means the season is shifting. i made yerba mate. i did not check the routeopt slack. brown belt me sat with the wait. small win.

later: nube voice-memo from camila. she's making 'the small chirp' which is the noise she makes when she misses someone. apparently she's been sleeping on my hoodie. dog brain logic: hoodie smells like seb, sleep on hoodie, seb returns. sometimes dog brain has the right answer. four more days.

ok let me write something not about the CT for a minute because if i don't i'm going to combust. so it's saturday in zapopan and i am with my parents in the house i grew up in and the little weirdnesses of being back here are accumulating in a way i want to remember.

things that are unchanged: the smell of the house. coffee plus the orange tree in the back patio plus whatever cleaning soap mom has used since 1998 plus the faint dog-from-decades-ago smell that has soaked into the couch (we had a dog when i was 7-15, a labrador named rufo, and the couch is still kind of his even though he died 18 years ago). dad's chair, in the same corner of the living room, oriented toward the same tv (a different tv, a much newer one). the kitchen calendar with a single appointment written for this week — 'CT, viernes 8am' — written in mom's careful nurse-handwriting and underlined twice. underlined twice. that's the part that gets me. mom underlines important things twice. milk on the grocery list gets one line. 'CT' gets two.
