things that are changed: dad has gotten smaller. i mean physically. he's always been a tall guy, my whole life he was the tall guy in family photos, and somewhere in the last two years he's lost an inch or two and he hunches a little when he reads now. mom has gotten quieter, which is the more concerning change because she was never a quiet woman. quieter is not necessarily a sickness thing — it could be the lump weighing on her, it could be retirement (she retired three years ago and i think she's still figuring out what to do with herself), it could just be 62. dad's bookshelf has more chess books than i remember; he's been playing more. there's a new espresso machine on the counter (much smaller than my lelit, more of a stovetop moka pot situation, but it's new). the back patio has a hammock that wasn't there last time, which mom uses in the afternoons, which is a new mom-routine i didn't know about. they're living their lives without me here, of course, but it always startles me to discover the specific shape of what their lives look like when i'm not in them.

things i'm noticing about myself here that i don't notice in cdmx: i sleep ten hours a night. ten. like a teenager. it's the air, it's the latitude, it's the quality of darkness in zapopan vs cdmx (cdmx is never fully dark even at 3am because the streetlights are everywhere), it's not having la polar underneath me. but also i think it's the absence of work — i'm physically far enough from brújula and roberto and the routeopt service that my brain has finally exhaled. i did a back-of-the-envelope calc: i sleep five hours in cdmx at the worst, ten here, which is double, which is a sign that i should probably look at my cdmx sleep more seriously. also: i eat slower. i eat less. i drink more water. i don't refresh slack. i haven't checked twitter — i mean mastodon — in four days and i don't miss it. zapopan is a different operating system and my hardware runs cooler on it.

things dad has said this week, just to log them, because dad doesn't say much:
— 'la mate, está bonita la torta', when i sent him a photo of the honda CR-V (he hasn't seen it since i bought it).
— 'tu mamá está bien, mijo. preocúpate menos.' (he doesn't know i can tell when he's reassuring himself out loud).
— 'cómo van las clases de kickbox?' (he hasn't asked about my training in three years. he asked because he wanted to give me a non-medical thing to talk about. i love him for it.)
— 'el roberto ese del trabajo, suena medio mamón' (i mentioned roberto exactly once. he caught it. he's still sharp.)
— 'a tu abuela lupita le hubieras gustado camila' (out of nowhere, while we were watering the plants. dad has not mentioned abuela lupita to me in maybe a year. he's thinking about her this week. of course he is.)
— 'cuándo vas a hacer una tabla de ajedrez para mí también?' (he saw me sketching the cedar dimensions for hugo's chess board. i told him 'after hugo's, dad'. he said 'no apuro' but i could tell he was pleased. i'm putting that on the to-do list.)
— 'el f1 ya no me gusta tanto desde que checo no anda en buen equipo' (we watched the brazil GP highlights together. checo's car was a disaster again. dad has been a quiet checo fan for a decade because i was loud about it as a teenager. he won't admit he watches because of me. it's there.)

things mom has said this week:
— 'comes bien? te ves flaco' (i've gained weight since vegetarianism, she just always says this).
— 'cómo está camila, mijo? cuándo trae a los niños' (the kid thing. always. mom and the kid thing have been a duet for two years now.)
— 'no te preocupes, hijo, el doctor beltrán es muy bueno' (this is for me, not her. she's the one with the lump and she's reassuring me.)
— 'tu abuela lupita estaría muy orgullosa de ti' (out of nowhere, last night, while we were doing dishes. i had to leave the kitchen briefly. i think she was trying to say something she's been trying to say.)
— 'la tomografía me la pidieron por precaución, no por sospecha' (this is the line she's repeating to herself. i don't know if she believes it. i'm going to act like i do.)
— 'cuídame a tu papá si me pasa algo' (saturday morning, while she was making coffee, said it like she was asking me to pass the salt. i said 'ma, no te va a pasar nada' and she said 'ya, ya, lo sé, pero por si acaso'. i couldn't say anything for a while after that.)
— 'tu hermano ya está más maduro, ya no me preocupa tanto' (saturday afternoon, looking at a photo of mateo and sofía. she's letting go of mateo a little. she's not letting go of me. that math is its own thing i'll think about later.)

things i did this week that i don't normally do: read a paper book for 90 uninterrupted minutes (the chiang). played dominos with dad's old friends who came over saturday — they remembered me as a 14-year-old and one of them, don rogelio, kept calling me 'sebastiancito' which i didn't correct because at 76 you have earned the right to use whatever diminutive you want. ate two homemade tortillas at every meal. didn't check linkedin. let mom over-feed me. let dad over-explain a chess opening to me twice. didn't mention work. didn't mention rent. didn't mention roberto.

i'm going to put the laptop down and go sit on the porch with dad and pretend to watch the chivas game even though i don't care about chivas. that's the move. that's how this weekend works. and tonight or tomorrow i'll sit with mom on the porch alone and we'll talk about something not-medical for an hour and that will count too. zapopan time is the time i need this week.

last thing — every time i'm here for more than three days i remember that i used to know how to do nothing. i don't know if you can re-acquire that as an adult once you've trained your nervous system on cdmx urgency for ten years. but a week of zapopan is at least a partial re-acquisition. i'm taking it.

addendum at midnight: dad came out to the porch with two yerba mates and we sat without talking for 40 minutes. didn't talk about the CT. didn't talk about anything. just listened to cicadas. then he said 'mañana hay tortillas frescas a las siete' and i said 'ok pa' and he went to bed. that's the entire conversation. that's also the entire conversation. some things don't need words and dad has known that since i was eight.

mateo flew in from tijuana saturday afternoon despite me telling him to wait. he didn't tell mom he was coming. she opened the door, saw him, and burst into tears. he lifted her off the ground in a hug. dad and i watched from the kitchen and dad said quietly 'qué bueno que vino'. i'm glad he came. mateo is 28 and looks like a younger me except slightly worse at hiding when he's worried. he and i went out for tacos saturday night just the two of us, talked about everything except the thing, and at the end he said 'estás bien tú?' and i said 'estoy bien' and he said 'ok mentiroso' and we ordered another beer.

he asked about hugo's chess board too — i'd told him about it on whatsapp last month — and we ended up sketching dimensions on a napkin while we ate al pastor. mateo is a software designer, so he's good at the geometry side. he had ideas about magnetic embedding for the pieces that were genuinely better than what i'd planned. i'm going to use them. brothers are good for stuff like this. i miss him every time i see him.

specifically what mateo proposed: instead of embedding rare-earth magnets directly under each square (which is what i was going to do, fiddly drilling, expensive), he suggested a single thin steel sheet under the entire board, hidden behind a 1mm cedar veneer, with the magnets only in the chess pieces themselves. the math is way better — instead of 64 perfectly-drilled magnet wells you do one steel sheet and one veneer pass. cost goes from ~1200 mxn in magnets to ~400 mxn in steel + magnets only in pieces. precision goes up because you're no longer fighting drilling alignment per square. and the board feels solid, not hollow, because the steel adds mass. i sketched the cross-section on the napkin: cedar core, steel layer, cedar veneer top, walnut squares laser-cut into the veneer. mateo nodded. he said 'se va a ver chingón'. i agree.

we also talked about him and sofía. they're solid. she's stressed about wedding planning — mateo said 'todo tiene que ser perfecto, mom isabela, mom mia, mom mi suegra, son tres mamás, nadie va a quedar contenta'. i told him to pick the lowest-friction option at every fork and let the moms have the higher-friction stuff. mateo laughed and said 'esa es muy buena estrategia, gracias por venir a guadalajara solamente para darme consejos de boda'. and we both got a little weepy and ordered another beer. brothers.

before we left the taco place i told him about the kid conversation with camila and dr muñoz. i hadn't told him before. he listened, didn't interrupt, and at the end said 'oye, mi hermano, no estás solo en esto. mira que sofía y yo también estamos pensando esa misma cosa'. i didn't know that. they've been talking about it. so the three of us — mateo, sofía, camila, me — are all in the same conversation at slightly different stages and didn't know it. mateo said 'tal vez los próximos primos van a llegar al mismo tiempo'. that landed weird. it landed in a hopeful weird, not a sad weird. ok. ok. one thing at a time. mom first.

other notes from sunday: dad and i went to the panadería for conchas at 4pm — mom's panadería, the one she's gone to since 1994 — and the lady at the counter said 'el ingeniero, qué milagro'. she remembered me. she had not seen me in two years and she remembered me. i bought a kilo of conchas and dad made coffee at home and we sat on the porch and dipped them. mom came out and joined us. dad fell asleep with a concha in his hand. i took a photo. mom said 'no me la mandes a mateo, se va a burlar'. i sent it to mateo. he said 'pa está cansado'. we were all cansados. we had been cansados since wednesday.

— at one point during the porch hours dad said 'mijo, hicimos lo correcto'. i said 'qué cosa, papá'. he said 'venir a vivir aquí. en zapopan. desde la ciudad. en el 95'. he meant the move from cdmx to zapopan when i was 2. he was still validating the decision 31 years later. that's the kind of decision parenting consists of: making it, then re-making it, every quiet sunday for the rest of your life.

sunday. quiet day. mom went to mass. i didn't. dad and i played chess. he won. board lasted 23 moves. respectable loss. we did not talk about tuesday. but he made his queen sacrifice on move 18 and looked at me with the kind of half-smile he used to have when i was a kid and he was about to tell me something. then he just played the move. didn't say anything. some father-son conversations happen in chess notation. notebook entry: g3-d6, sac qe7, move 18.

ok writing this from my parents' kitchen because i need to lay out how this week is going to actually go. i've been here since thursday and i'm going to be here through next thursday at minimum, which is a full week off work, which is the longest stretch i've taken since starting at brújula. seven calendar days. i told roberto monday-friday but i'm working it as a full week.

let me sketch the week so i remember it later:

thursday: flew cdmx → gdl, 7:30am flight, dad picked me up at the airport. went home to zapopan. ate chilaquiles. cried in my old bedroom. went on a walk with dad to the panadería. talked about the CT.

friday: CT scan day, 8am at hospital méxico americano. mom nervous. i held her hand. scan took 25 minutes. dr beltrán said he'd call same-day if benign, otherwise tuesday. did not call. went to dinner at a vegetarian restaurant on av. chapultepec gdl that opened since i moved away. mateo called from tj saying he was coming saturday.

saturday: mateo arrived at 2pm. mom cried. we ate. mateo and i went out for tacos at night. talked about not-the-thing. he asked how i was. i lied. he saw through it.

sunday: mom went to mass at san francisco de paula. i did not. dad and i played chess in the living room. he won, 23 moves, which is a respectable loss against a 66-year-old who's been playing chess since before i was born. we made hugo's chess board mentally — i told him about the cedar plans for camila's dad's 70th. he liked the idea. asked good questions about wood.

monday (today): mateo had to fly back to tj because work. mom and i went grocery shopping at the soriana — felt absurdly normal — and made a big pot of lentejas for the week. i did three hours of routeopt review work in the morning before mom was awake, just in case roberto pinged me, but i deliberately did not start anything new. ran a loop in colomos park. it was 30°c at 9am. cdmx-fit-me cannot do gdl heat. came back drenched.

tuesday (tomorrow): result day. dr beltrán is supposed to call mom by 4pm. i will be sitting next to her when she takes the call. dad will be in his chair. mateo will be on whatsapp video. camila will be on whatsapp video. nube might be too. that's the lineup.

wednesday: depending on result, this is either decompression day or escalation day. if benign: we celebrate with cake from the place mom likes by chapalita. dad will get sentimental. i'll cry maybe. mateo will get drunk on whatsapp from tijuana. if not benign — i'm not going to plan that yet. i'll plan it tuesday after the call. either way i extend my stay.

thursday: original return flight to cdmx. i'll change the ticket if needed. i think i'll fly back regardless because camila is alone with nube and roberto's standup norms 2.0 has probably mutated into something worse in my absence and i want to be there.

friday: cdmx, work. trying to re-engage. depending on result this is either a normal friday or a different kind of friday entirely.

that's the week. one whole week of my life. plus the front-end shock of finding out and the back-end of processing whatever happens. i don't think i've taken a full week off since i moved to cdmx three years ago. for context: when daniela went on vacation last february she took eight days and we all noticed. brújula is not a place that loves vacation. i am taking this anyway.

the weirdest thing about a week off is how slow the days feel. i've been here four days and it's like a month. zapopan time is different from cdmx time. it's like the pace of life is set by mom's chilaquiles routine and dad's chess pace and the rhythm of the bougainvillea outside. i'm not complaining. i'm just noting that i'd forgotten this kind of time exists. cdmx time is dense — every hour has work in it, traffic in it, decisions in it. zapopan time is loose. you can sit on the porch at 4pm and three hours can pass without anything happening and that's not a wasted afternoon, that's a full one. i don't know how i lost the ability to feel that way about an afternoon. some part of it is moving away from your hometown. some part of it is becoming the person who runs a routeopt rust migration and is on the hook for thousands of customer deliveries an hour. the cost of getting good at the urgent stuff is losing the equipment you need for the not-urgent stuff.

the other thing — and i need to think about this when i'm back in cdmx — is that this whole week has been a forced experiment in 'what does seb's life look like with the urgent stuff turned off'. answer: he sleeps. he reads (got 80 pages into the chiang book that's been sitting on my desk for 3 months). he plays chess with his dad. he eats slowly. he goes for runs in the heat. he doesn't refresh slack. he doesn't check twitter. and he is mostly fine. anxious about mom, of course, that's the headline of the week. but underneath the mom anxiety, the steady-state seb of zapopan is more rested and more present than the steady-state seb of cdmx. that has implications for the cdmx situation that i don't want to fully think about until next week.

what are the implications, briefly: maybe the rate-of-urgency in cdmx is not just 'the cost of doing business' but is itself the thing that's wearing me down past the point where i can be a good partner, a good son-in-distance, a good engineer who finishes the rust migration without making three more memory-leak mistakes. maybe roberto is just the most visible spike in a pattern that includes la polar and the rent and the kid-question, and the pattern is 'cdmx wants me on the burner all the time'. maybe i could choose differently. maybe brújula is not the only viable employer. maybe mit could be a real plan if it lands. maybe i could take a sabbatical. maybe — and this is the version of the thought that scares me — maybe gdl is not as far from cdmx in actual life-quality terms as my 33-year-old self likes to pretend. mateo lives in tj happily and works remote and looks ten years younger than me. i'm not moving back to gdl. but i am noting that the script of 'must live in cdmx to be a real engineer' was internalized at 22 and never re-examined, and a week in zapopan has put a small crack in it.

a quick budget exercise i did this morning while dad was watching the news: cost of cdmx life — rent 33k, utilities 4k, food 12k, gym 2.5k, transport 3k, savings 35k, miscellaneous 5k = ~95k mxn/month. cost of equivalent zapopan life — rent 18k, utilities 3k, food 9k, gym 1.5k, transport 2k, savings 35k, misc 4k = ~72k mxn/month. delta: 23k/month, 276k/year, 14% lower cost basis. salary equivalent: i could take a 14% pay cut and live the same lifestyle in zapopan. or keep cdmx salary and double savings rate by moving. or — the version i'm not actually proposing — split: 60% gdl, 40% cdmx remote, the way mateo splits tj/cdmx for sofía's family. that math works on paper. it would not work for camila professionally. so it's a thought experiment, not a plan. but i needed to write the numbers down to know they exist.

órale. tuesday. one more sleep. mom is asleep already. dad is half-asleep in his chair watching the news. i'm going to make yerba mate and read a few pages of chiang. nube would love this porch. i miss her.

amended last note: just realized i've written this whole entry in present tense even though i'm describing a hypothetical week. that's because my brain refuses to imagine tuesday as anything other than a benign result. that is denial as a technical strategy. it's working. i'll keep using it until tuesday.

tuesday morning. mom is up early. the house is too quiet. dad is in his chair already even though it's 7:30am. i made yerba mate twice already. i've changed shirts twice because nervous-sweat, which is a thing i thought i'd left behind in my early 20s. dr beltrán's office said he'd call by 4pm. it's currently 8:14am. there are 7 hours and 46 minutes between now and 'by 4pm'. i'm going to go for a run. i don't care how hot it is. i need to outrun the morning.

garmin is going to log this run as 'unhinged for the heat profile' but garmin doesn't know what's coming this afternoon. i'm doing 8k at sub-5min/km in 28°c just to make my body do something physical instead of letting my brain do something cognitive. cognitive is a worse use of these hours. i'll be physically depleted by 9:30am, which means i'll be too tired to spiral, which means i'll be merely scared instead of catastrophizing. that's the trade. i'll take it.

route plan: through colomos park, the loop along the camellones, around the small pond where the herons live, back to mom's house. 8.4 km by garmin's last measure of this loop in 2019. i ran it as a high schooler in the rain at 14, ran it as a college freshman in 95° heat at 19, ran it pre-cdmx-move at 28. there is a layer of geography here that is also a layer of self-history. running it tuesday morning at 33 with mom waiting on a CT result is going to feel like every previous version of me is also running it.

pre-run check: shoes laced, phone with apple maps and garmin chest strap, water bottle, cap, sunscreen on the back of the neck. mom said 'cuídate del sol, mijo' and 'no te tardes' as i left, which is the same exact two sentences she said when i was 14. some templates don't update.

actually-running update — wrote this in my head for the last 2km, putting it down now: i finished in 39:27, which is slower than my cdmx 8k pace by about 90 seconds per km, which is the heat tax. heart rate average 168, peak 184, which felt fine because the run wasn't about fitness. the camellones smelled like jasmine. the pond herons are still there. i passed two viejitos walking and they nodded. i passed a kid on a bike who looked exactly like me at twelve. i did not cry on the run because if i'd started i wouldn't have stopped. i did sit on a bench at km 6 for ninety seconds and stared at the sky. the sky in zapopan in october at 9am is so blue it looks photoshopped. i remember now why i used to run here. i used to run here to be reminded that the world is bigger than the thing in front of me. that still works. small wins. heading to shower.

— the funny thing about being on the porch with both parents at once is that dad does most of the talking and mom does most of the looking. dad describes things, mom watches them. dad will tell you which neighbor's car broke down last week; mom will look at the neighbor's car and tell you the neighbor is sad. the division of labor in zapopan is consistent and unspoken and beautiful. they've been doing this for 40 years and they're not going to stop.

— after the porch hours we went inside and watched the late news together. there was a story about la polar (cdmx) being investigated for a different licensing matter. dad laughed and said 'tu cantina está en las noticias, mijo'. i did not engage. some battles you don't bring to your parents.

— the porch hours have a thing called 'el silencio de papa' which is a family in-joke about how dad will sit on the porch for hours without saying anything and then suddenly emit a single declarative statement. tonight's: 'el zacate está creciendo, hay que cortarlo'. silence for 23 minutes. then: 'la mate, ¿cuándo te volvieron a aumentar la renta?'. so he was tracking the rent thing this whole time. he hadn't asked because he wanted me to bring it up, and i hadn't, so on the porch in the silence he found a moment to ask without making a thing of it. dad's emotional architecture is mostly silence with single declarative sentences emerging from it.

ran 8k in colomos at 9am, came back, drank water, took a cold shower, helped mom hang laundry. it is now 11:30am. there are still 4.5 hours until 'by 4pm'. mom is in the garden. dad is reading a physics paper i didn't know he still got delivered. i'm going to write code for an hour because if i don't have a screen in front of me i'm going to start cleaning closets. update: roberto pinged me about a 'small clarification' on routeopt. it was not small. i answered anyway. it took my mind off the clock for forty minutes. kind of miraculous. give him this one.

specifically he asked me to validate that the memory fix i shipped to staging last week was 'production-grade'. i said yes, walked him through the test i added, walked him through the load profile, walked him through the abort semantics. he said 'great, thanks, this is exactly the kind of write-up i need from senior engineers'. i would normally roll my eyes at 'exactly the kind of write-up i need' but today, today specifically, the validation hit a different way. i'll take it.

what i actually wrote him, since he asked for production-grade evidence: (1) the unit test i added (paste of the timeout_aborts_sibling_task test), (2) heaptrack output before-and-after with rss curves, (3) the locust output showing flat memory over 90 minutes at production-shape load, (4) a note about the runtime sizing concern that's still open and a recommended sequence for resolving it (load test bumped to 2x prod traffic, observe burst patterns, decide on multi_thread runtime). that was a 1500-word writeup typed in 35 minutes. i don't think i could have produced it that fast last month. zapopan operating system, apparently, is also better at concise technical writing.

mom watered the garden plants while i was typing. dad finished the physics paper, started reading a chess book — 'el final de torre' by something. he said 'mira esta posición' and showed me a king-and-rook endgame on his pocket chess board. white to move, win in 12. i looked at it for a minute, said 'la torre detrás del peón pasado'. he said 'sí, exacto'. proud-dad face. that lasted ten seconds. then he closed the book and stared out the window. dad's stare-out-the-window is its own state — he's not seeing anything, he's running the same loop i'm running, he's waiting for the phone to ring. we are all in the same nervous system right now. the house is one big breath being held.

12:45pm now. five and a quarter hours until 'by 4pm'. i ate two corn tortillas and some refried beans because mom said 'come algo' and at 33 you still listen when your mom says come algo. nube on whatsapp video earlier — camila held her up, she looked confused at the camera, then licked it. that helped. ten minutes of nube licking the screen would help anything. the day is being crossed off in micro-units.

mateo called me at 11pm tijuana time. asked how mom was. asked how dad was. asked how i was. i said 'ok, llorando un poco'. he said 'normal, hermano'. then he said 'sé que el doctor beltrán es bueno. el papá de un amigo mío en la base militar pasó por él el año pasado, mismo tipo de bulto, fue un quiste. quédate con eso esta noche'. i did. i stayed with it. quistic. probabilistically a quiste. i fell asleep around 2am holding that word.

— i logged a checkout note from the day: ate three meals, slept ten hours, ran twice (gentle), did 20 minutes of pull-ups on the door frame, read 40 pages, did not check brújula slack, called camila twice, called nube three times via camila, played chess with dad and lost twice. that's the full ledger. not bad for a sunday.

— at dinner, abuela elsa (mom's older sister, 78) called from puerto vallarta to ask about the CT. she said 'mija, dile a sebastiansito que no se preocupe, que la familia castellanos siempre ha tenido la cabeza dura' (the family has always had a hard head). it was meant to be funny. mom laughed. abuela elsa sounded older than i remember. mom thanked her and they hung up. mom said 'a tu tía abuela también la queremos mucho, pero ya está mayor'. yes. ya está mayor. everyone is.

1pm. nothing. lunch. ate without tasting. mom is calmer than me, which is humbling. she made sopa de fideo because that's what she makes when she's nervous, it's the kind of food that requires you to babysit a pot for thirty minutes which is its own form of meditation. dad ate two bowls. i ate one and pushed the noodles around. mom looked at my plate and didn't say anything. that's how i know she knows. usually she'd comment. today she let me have it.

she also did the thing where she sets out a little plate of avocado on the side because i mentioned three years ago that i missed having avocado with sopa de fideo, and she has remembered every visit since. mexican mothers do that. they collect your offhand preferences and store them indefinitely. it's a love language i am extremely bad at reciprocating. i need to be better.

inventory of avocado-equivalents from my mom's memory bank, things i've mentioned once and she has stored forever:
— i don't like cilantro (soap gene), so she chops it for dad's plate but not mine. for years.
— i prefer corn tortillas to flour. no flour ever appears on my plate. mateo gets flour. parents have parallel plates.
— i drink yerba mate in the morning and coffee in the afternoon. she's stocked yerba in the kitchen since 2019 even though nobody in zapopan drinks yerba except me.
— i like my coffee unsweetened. dad takes two sugars. she makes mine separately. the same little glass next to the same red mug.
— i like a dash of lime in beans, mateo doesn't. she puts a lime quarter on the side of my bowl. she has done this since i was 17.
— i'm vegetarian since 2022. she has mastered three vegetarian meals — chiles rellenos with cheese, sopa de fideo, and a vegetarian albondigas — and rotates them in. she still puts meat on dad's plate.

each of those is a small piece of attention sustained over years. i don't track that for her. i don't even know what avocado-equivalent she has — what offhand preference she once mentioned that i could have stored and reciprocated. let me think. she likes the flowers from the corner stall on av. patria. she takes lemon in her water but not lime. she prefers the gel-tip pens to ballpoints. she likes the bread from the panadería on calle hidalgo over doña marta's (which is a treason i don't tell doña marta about). she watches the news at 7pm but mutes the politics segment. she keeps a small box of postcards she's saved from places mateo and i have traveled. she's never asked us to send them. she just keeps the ones we send.

i'm going to start sending her postcards again. from cdmx. from anywhere. just a postcard a month. write a few sentences. tell her something. she'll keep them. the box will grow. that's what you do for the person who has been remembering avocado for three years.

still 4pm-minus-three-hours. her phone face-up on the kitchen counter. she keeps glancing at it. so does dad. so do i. the phone is winning the staring contest with all three of us. we are very bad at this kind of waiting. mexican families are dramatic in big moments and stoic in waiting moments and we have lots of practice with the first kind and almost none with the second.

— camila's voice notes today were great. she sent a 9-second one with nube purring directly into the phone speaker, no commentary, just nube. that's exactly the right response to a sister-in-law-in-distress. no advice. just cat audio. camila is good at this stuff in ways i am not. i'm going to remember to thank her properly when i'm back.

— mom said something interesting at lunch: 'mijo, el doctor beltrán dijo que si el resultado tarda dos días puede ser por la cola del laboratorio, no por el resultado en sí'. so the lab queue, not the finding, drives the delay. that's the explanation she's holding onto and i'm holding onto it with her.

— camila sent a video of nube watching a moth on the patio. nube was making the chittering noise that means 'hunt mode'. moth survived. 23 seconds of cat content. exactly what was needed.

2:45pm. phone is on the table. we're all in the living room. mom has the phone closer to her, dad in his chair, me on the couch, mateo on whatsapp video on the laptop next to me waving from his apartment in tijuana, camila just joined from cdmx with nube on her lap. nube is licking her face on screen. mom keeps glancing at the phone like it might bite. dad opened a spanish wine an hour ago which is either a celebration plan or a coping plan. i don't know which yet. i'll know in 75 minutes.

mateo brought a beer to the call which is the most mateo thing possible — he set it on his desk like he's at a sports bar waiting for kickoff. camila is in our cdmx kitchen and i can see the calendar on the wall behind her with a blue dot for our barcelona trip in march. i love that she joined the call. she didn't have to. i didn't ask. she just opened the laptop at 2:30 her time and waited.

she's wearing the green sweater i got her last christmas — which she only wears when she's nervous, like it's a weighted blanket disguised as a sweater. she said 'hola, isabela' to mom and mom waved and tried to smile. mateo said 'hola jefa, tranquila' and mom said 'tranquila tú, hijo'. dad raised his wine glass at the camera and mateo raised his beer. the family ritual of waiting through a frightening thing, distributed across four cities (cdmx, gdl, tj, são paulo if hugo joins) and one couch. i told hugo sr he didn't have to join, the three-hour time difference is rough; he texted me 'estoy con ustedes en pensamiento, abrazos' which is hugo for 'i love you all, im here'.

camila brought her chiang book to the camera so i could see it — she's reading the same one i'm reading because she said 'si lo lees lento, lo leo lento contigo'. i didn't know she was doing that. she's been reading at the same pace as me, on her side of the apartment, since i started in september. she's at page 162. i'm at page 184. she just bumped up to my page count this morning by reading on the metro. i didn't know any of this. i'm finding out by glimpsing the bookmark sticking out of her copy on a video call.

mom keeps touching her own neck. not the lump side, the other side. unconscious. dad is holding the wine glass without drinking. i'm watching the phone. mateo is watching the phone through the camera. nube fell asleep on camila's lap. it is 3:01pm. one more hour. fifty-nine minutes. mom said 'cuéntenme algo, distráiganme'. mateo started a story about sofía and the wedding cake tasting. it filled twelve minutes. nobody remembers the details. we just needed the noise.

— at the dinner with the family last night, dad asked me about the rust migration. i was surprised. he doesn't usually ask about work. i tried to explain it in non-technical terms — 'the system that figures out the best route for a delivery truck, but in a faster language' — and he said 'a ver si no la rompes' and i laughed and said 'estoy tratando de no romperla, pa'. he said 'tú eres el que la va a arreglar si la rompes, así que está bien'. that was a kind thing to say.

— at one point during the week i caught myself talking out loud to nube as if she were here. dad walked past and didn't comment. mom walked past and said 'ya, ya, mijo, ya casi vuelves'. she knew exactly what i was doing. she always does.

— last note: i did 60 pushups before bed because the body needed something to do. i was way too jittery from the wait. brown belt me said 'do something physical'. i did. it helped a little. then i couldn't sleep for an hour anyway.

— mom slipped me 500 pesos at breakfast this morning 'para tu camino'. i did not need it. she has been doing this since i was 18. i took it and put it in my wallet. she felt better. i felt 8 years old again. fair trade.

3:20pm. forty minutes. i'm going to put down the laptop because if i keep typing i'll have written a novel by the time the call comes. one thought before i go quiet: i was thinking about abuela lupita earlier and what i regret about her — not visiting her last year, the way i waited too long because work, because cdmx, because the assumption that she'd always be there. mom is 62 and healthy and right here in the kitchen and i am sitting in her living room because i flew here on 24-hour notice and i am not going to make the abuela mistake twice. whatever the call says, i'm here, i was here, i'll be here for whatever comes next. the rest is the rest.

the specific guilt about abuela has been sitting in me for seven years and it doesn't really get smaller, it just changes shape. some weeks it's 'i should have visited more often when she was still mentally there', other weeks it's 'i should have called every sunday for the last year', other weeks it's 'i should have flown back when mom said estable because mom was wrong'. tonight whatever shape it takes is the right shape because tonight it's the engine that got me on the plane on thursday.

abuela lupita is also the person who gave me the bombilla mate cup with my name on it. i drank yerba this morning out of that cup, here in the house, with abuela's name engraved on the rim seen by mom in the kitchen and unmentioned. i'm sure mom saw it. she always sees it. she has never said anything about it because she also has feelings about her mother dying and us not being there and that's its own private channel between us that we don't broadcast on. but the cup is on the kitchen counter, like a small altar, and it has been part of every yerba i've drunk in this house since 2017. mom has never moved it.

other people i'm going to do better with after this week, even if mom is fine: dad. mateo. hugo sr. camila's siblings. pablo's parents who i've met twice and never followed up with. profe esteban. carla — when she's back from leave i'm taking her for coffee. i'm going to send mom a postcard a month. i'm going to call dad once a week instead of once every three weeks. i'm going to fly to gdl twice a year minimum even when there's no medical reason. i'm going to fly to tj for mateo's wedding and stay an extra two days. i'm going to take camila to são paulo to see hugo at least every six months. these are not aspirations. these are the operational consequences of seeing your mom touch her own neck while waiting for a phone call.

ok 38 minutes. stopping here. closing laptop. brb.

— small thing i logged in my notes app: the kitchen calendar's 'CT, viernes 8am' was still there saturday morning even though the appointment was already done. mom hadn't crossed it out. she was waiting until results to cross it out. that's the kind of small psychic detail that shows up in a journal entry six months from now and breaks me.

— small log: dad asked me what brújula stock was worth (we have an option pool). i said 'pa, son options, no son acciones todavía'. he said 'pero si las pueden vender'. i said 'sí, en una salida'. he said 'pero si no hay salida'. i said 'entonces no'. he nodded. dad understands risk. he understood it before i did.

— before bed i checked the brújula slack briefly. roberto had posted a 700-word announcement about 'standup hygiene improvements' which i read but did not engage with. it was the kind of post that wants reactions to validate it. the team had given it three thumbs-ups. that's the maximum i'd give it too. some battles you fight by NOT clicking the heart.

— mom asked me 'cuándo es el próximo viaje a guadalajara, mijo?' i said 'navidad'. she said 'de verdad?' i said 'de verdad, ma'. that's six weeks. she nodded. she is counting weeks. so am i.

phone just rang. it's 3:47pm. mom picking up. i can't breathe. brb. dad froze with the wine glass mid-air. mateo on screen put down his beer. camila put both hands on nube. mom said 'sí, doctor beltrán'. that's all i caught before brain went quiet. typing this with one thumb behind the laptop where mom can't see me being weird. heart in my throat. both hands shaking. apparently brown belt training does not prepare you for this kind of round. brb for real.

ok. dr beltrán was still on the phone with mom for like twelve minutes when i started this. i can hear her saying 'sí doctor', 'sí doctor', 'gracias', and she is nodding, and i don't know what's being said but the nodding is steady, not panicked. dad is gripping the arm of his chair. mateo is silent on video. camila is silent on video. nube is asleep on screen, oblivious. mom just said 'entonces no es...' and then nodded. i don't know what's after the 'no es'. i'm afraid to interpret. i'm not breathing well. brb again.

— ok update mom said 'sin invasión' just now and i had to grab the table to not fall over. 'sin invasión'. that means it isn't spreading. that means it's encapsulated. that means — i can't say what it means yet. she's still on the call. she's still nodding. dad has tears in his eyes already and the call isn't even over. brb.

— second update. she's writing things down on the notepad she keeps by the phone. her hand is steady. that's a tell. mom's hand shakes when she's bracing for bad news. her hand is steady. i'm reading her face which is, for the first time in seven days, not braced. her shoulders are lower. dad has not unfrozen but his eyes are tracking her. mateo is leaning into the camera. camila is biting her thumbnail. nube has rolled over and exposed her belly. nube is the only one in this household with the right energy for this moment.

— third update. she said 'biopsia de aguja fina, viernes próximo, ambulatoria'. that's the standard FNA biopsy procedure for confirmation. fine-needle aspiration. ambulatory means she goes home the same day. that's the workflow for a 'we're 95% sure it's benign, just need histology to close the file' diagnosis. that's not the workflow for cancer. cancer goes to surgical biopsy or excisional biopsy, not FNA. FNA is the chill version. FNA is 'come in tuesday, take 30 minutes, results in a week'. i learned this distinction at 1am tuesday from googling that i told myself i wasn't going to do.

— fourth update. she said 'bueno doctor, muchísimas gracias' which is end-of-call energy. she's wrapping up. she's about to hang up. dad is leaning forward. mateo's hand is hovering over his beer. camila's eyes are full. i'm trying to type and breathe at the same time. she said 'sí doctor, hasta el viernes'. and then she said 'gracias, gracias, hasta luego'. and then she put the phone down. and the room went very quiet. brb to the next message because i need to actually be in the room for what happens next.

— camila and i had our first argument in two weeks last night, and it was about my flight back. i said 'i'll book wednesday'. she said 'don't, stay until friday'. i said 'i need to be home'. she said 'your home will still be here on friday. your mom's house won't always'. i hung up to think. i called back fifteen minutes later and said 'ok, friday'. she said 'ok'. that's how arguments work in this relationship — short fuse, fast reset, real outcome.

— while i was packing for the flight back, mom came in with a tupperware of frijoles de la olla and a bag of conchas. 'para cami'. for camila. she was sending food across the country because that's what she does. i said 'ma, no me dejan pasar comida en el avión'. she said 'sí te dejan, en el equipaje documentado'. i checked. she was right. she's always right about logistics. the conchas made it back to cdmx.

— before sleep mom came to my room and asked if she could lay down on the bed for a minute. she lay down on the bed. she didn't say anything. she closed her eyes. i sat on the edge and didn't say anything either. after twelve minutes she said 'ya, ya estoy bien, gracias mijo'. she got up and went to her room. that was its own kind of mom-conversation.

— last log of the night before bed: my phone battery says 73%. mom is asleep. dad is asleep. nube is purring through camila's whatsapp voice memo. yerba mate is cold. tomorrow is tuesday. i love them all. closing.

she hung up. she put the phone face-down on the table. she said 'el doctor dice que es muy probable un lipoma. necesita una biopsia con aguja para confirmar al cien, pero la imagen se ve consistente con tejido graso, sin nodos sospechosos, sin invasión'. i'm typing this verbatim because i don't trust my translation right now. then she said 'tengo que ir el viernes para la biopsia, pero el doctor no está preocupado'. dad stood up and hugged her. mateo on video burst into tears. camila on video burst into tears. i sat down on the floor because my legs went. nube woke up because of camila's sniffling and started licking her face again on screen. mom is okay. probably. pending biopsy. but okay. dad opened the spanish wine. mateo opened a beer in tijuana on video. camila and i clinked nothing across screens. i'll process all of this properly later. right now the only thing i can write is: she's okay. she's okay. she's okay.

three hours later, after dinner: we ate. mom ate. dad ate three plates because he hadn't eaten properly in five days. mateo stayed on video while we ate, like he was sitting at the table from tijuana. camila waved goodbye around 8pm because she had a foster + partners early call. i extended my flight by two days — flying back saturday instead of thursday — so i can be here through the FNA on friday. roberto okayed it in slack within five minutes. daniela texted 'gracias por avisarme, mucha tranquilidad para tu mamá'. pablo sent a voice note that just said 'cabrón, llóralo, llóralo bien, te abrazo desde acá'.

mom went to bed early. dad and i sat on the porch and didn't talk for an hour. then he said 'hijo, gracias por haber venido' and i said 'no era opción no venir, pa' and he said 'ya sé, pero gracias' and we sat for another twenty minutes and he went to bed. i'm still on the porch. it's almost midnight. the cicadas are loud. the lemon tree dad planted in 2008 is doing its thing. i can see a single light on across the street, somebody else awake at midnight, somebody else's small private moment of relief or terror or just insomnia. zapopan is full of those tonight, like every night.

i'm going to sleep. but before i do — small log for future-me, when this week is a year ago and i've forgotten how it felt: it felt like the floor came back. for seven days the floor was somewhere else, and at 3:47pm on tuesday it came back. friday's biopsy is friday's problem. mom is okay. probably. pending histology. but the floor is back. that's what i needed to write down before sleep tonight.

— last log of the night before sleep: it's 11:30pm zapopan time. mom is asleep. dad is asleep in his chair. nube would be on my chest if she were here. she's not. camila is in cdmx. tomorrow is the result. i don't know what tomorrow looks like yet. i'm closing the laptop and going to bed. yerba mate is warm in the cup beside me. ok.

— last entry. tomorrow i go back to cdmx. roberto will have done something terrible. la polar will have done another saturday at 80db. the rent on the new contract starts december 1. nube will be confused for ten minutes and then act like i never left. camila will have read three more chapters of her detective novel. mom is staying in zapopan with dad. i will call them every other day. the lemon tree dad planted in 2008 will be tall. brown belt me will be back at the gym thursday. ok. closing.

— and: my last memory of being in zapopan as 33-year-old seb in november 2026, before whatever tomorrow's call brings: dad asleep in his chair, mom asleep in her room, lemon tree tall in the dark, cicadas, yerba mate cooling, my old high school posters on the wall above me, camila on whatsapp last-seen 11:24 pm cdmx time, nube probably curled up on her side of the bed, and me, sitting up writing this, and i don't know what tomorrow holds and that's ok. i'm going to sleep now. ok. closing for real.

back is wrecked. tweaked something low — left side, right at the SI joint area — sparring last night with Pablo at Atlas Box. felt a little pop on a low kick I checked badly because mom's lump thing has me distracted, didn't sleep, stupid. now I can't bend to put my socks on. órale. Pablo said I looked grey at the end of the round. I had to drive home in his car because mine felt impossible. twenty-six steps from the parking spot to the apartment door, counted them. ibuprofen 400, ice pack on the lumbar, side-lying with a pillow between my knees like a child. I am 33 and the body is telling me something specific. I will not listen tomorrow. but I am noticing tonight.

Pablo iced me down after sparring, drove me home in La Torta because I couldn't drive. he is good people. eleven years of friendship and the man has never once made me feel small for being injured or sad or stupid. said I looked grey on the way home. probably did. didn't talk much, mostly just put on Maná in the car at low volume because he knows it makes me sleepy and that was the goal — get me home, get me horizontal, get me to stop thinking about mom. left me at the door with a bottle of agua mineral and the words 'descansa, cabrón.' that is the entire vocabulary of love between two Mexican men. enough.

ok ibuprofen kicked in, I can move now but only in slow motion. I think it's muscular not disc — pain doesn't shoot down the leg, it sits, it stays put on the left side of the lumbar like a small angry person who has decided to live there for a few weeks. I've had this once before, back in Wizeline days, when I tried squatting heavy without warming up like an idiot. that one resolved in like 10 days but I was 25 then and the body forgave that kind of stupid in roughly a week. I'm 33 now, and there is a noticeable shift in how it feels — less elastic, more stubborn, the recovery curve flatter at the front. I notice it most in the morning when I first try to sit up and the muscles refuse to fire in the order I expect them to. as a kid I used to bounce off any couch in any house in Guadalajara without thinking about which muscle was about to lift which other muscle. somewhere in my late twenties that stopped being free. now every act of getting vertical is a small calculation, a quick check-in with the lower back, a half-second of negotiation. I do not love the negotiation but I am starting to respect it as the price of admission to my mid-thirties.

I'm going to ice/heat alternating for the rest of the day, take the laptop to bed, work in horizontal with the ThinkPad propped on a pillow on my chest like a hospital patient with a tablet, ignore standup if Roberto pings, dare him to write me up while my mom waits for a CT scan in Guadalajara. that was uncalled for. sorry. I'm just very tired and the lump thing has me catastrophizing. mom called me yesterday — quick voice, IMSS GP told her 'probably a lipoma but we want to be sure' which is the kind of medical sentence you cannot stop rearranging in your own head — and now I'm trying to ship a PostGIS query feature on a screen I cannot really see clearly because tears keep being a thing. I am not crying because of the back. the back is just a license. the back is the thing I am allowed to cry about on a Tuesday afternoon at my desk because if I cried about the lump my body wouldn't let me work, and I have to work, because if I stop working I will spend eight hours googling 'subcutaneous neck mass differential diagnosis' and that path leads nowhere good. I have already opened that tab once today and closed it. I will not open it again. that is the deal. that is the whole deal. type the rust. close the tab.

the plan: book a flight to Guadalajara for tomorrow morning, Volaris, the early one. tell Roberto a family emergency. I'm going. mom doesn't know yet that I'm coming. I'll surprise her at the airport. she'll fight me for thirty seconds about the ticket money and then cry into my shirt for thirty more and then it'll be fine. that's how it'll go. I know my mother. I will pack a small bag tonight while my back is still cooperative. I'll bring the bombilla for the morning mate ritual at her kitchen table — Mama Lupita's bombilla, the one with my name on the rim, mom keeps the box for it on her shelf for exactly these occasions. I'll bring the navy linen shirt because she likes me in that one. I'll bring two books I won't read. I'll bring my laptop because I will work from her kitchen table whether she likes it or not. and I'll bring nothing else, because the plan is to spend a week being her son and letting her be my mother and ignoring the rest of the world for as long as it takes for the CT scan to come back.

trying to push the PostGIS bounding-box query for service zones today, lying down with the ThinkPad on my chest like a hospital patient. the encoder I wrote back in month 7 is still the load-bearing piece for everything spatial in the rust service — every PR touches it, every test depends on it, every metric we track at the spatial layer eventually flows through that little 240-line module of mine. should probably extract it as a crate at some point but every time I look at the code I remember the four nights I lost to the geography vs geometry distinction in PostGIS — geography uses spheroidal math, geometry uses planar, they do not interoperate cleanly across function families, and sqlx's PostgreSQL type system did not, at the time, know how to encode geography directly so I had to write a custom Encode/Decode that round-trips through WKT — and I just close the tab.

Andrés keeps asking how to add a new spatial type and I keep saying 'soon, soon.' I am not great at mentoring while horizontal. for the bbox query specifically the issue today is subtle: when the bbox crosses the dateline, the PostGIS ST_Intersects with geography behaves differently than with geometry, and our test fixtures didn't catch that because all our test routes are within Mexico. shipping this without the dateline test is fine for now but I'm noting it in TODO_FUTURE.md so I don't get a 3am page from a customer who runs a Pacific shipping route someday. the bbox itself is straightforward — ST_MakeEnvelope of four corners, intersected with the geography column on the routes table — the trick is that the geography column needs to be cast to geometry temporarily for the bbox math to be planar and fast, then we re-validate the result with the spheroidal ST_DWithin against the actual route polyline. it's a two-step query: planar prefilter for speed, spheroidal for correctness. classic geo trick. I learned it from a 2017 Boundless Geo blog post that is now a 404 because Boundless got acquired and the blog evaporated, but the trick survives in the test code I wrote month 7.

I'll add the dateline test case when my back lets me sit at a real desk again. I am also flagging in the PR description that the encoder needs a unit test specifically for the geography → WKT round-trip with non-ASCII characters in the location name field, because right now we only test with English and Spanish names, and one of our logistics customers in Tijuana has Mandarin in their stop names and I noticed last week the encoded WKT was double-escaping their UTF-8. small bug. cosmetic. but I will fix it next week because leaving cosmetic bugs around in a load-bearing module breeds a culture of ignoring small things and small things become big things in 18 months.

Roberto pinged at 9:45am asking why I missed standup. I replied 'family emergency, OOO today, will post written update by EOD.' he replied 'sorry to hear, please be sure to document any in-flight work for the team.' as if I would not. as if I am not literally writing this PR description from a horizontal position with a heating pad under my lumbar. the man's instinct in any human moment is to ask me to document. Daniela would have replied 'descansa, mijo, te llamo si necesitas algo.' Daniela left at month 8 and this — the gap between Daniela and Roberto — is the thing I miss most about the team I had a few months ago. it's not technical. it's the texture of the management around me while I am trying to do hard work. I will think more about this when my back is better. right now I just want to ship the bbox PR and get on a plane to my mother.

ok I'm in Guadalajara. flew in this morning, Volaris 7am, the kind of flight where the gate agent calls boarding before the boarding-pass printer has finished printing your boarding pass and then everyone in zone 4 stands up at once and pretends they know what they're doing. I slept maybe an hour. mom picked me up at the airport in the same Pointer she's had since I was in prepa, the AC still doesn't blow on the driver side and she was waving a piece of cardboard at her own face the whole drive home, the kind of folded cardboard you keep in the door pocket for exactly this purpose, that has been folded into the same shape for so long the creases are basically a permanent geometry. she looks small. I don't think I've used that word about her before. she was always taller in my head than the rest of us — pediatric nurse for 35 years at the IMSS hospital in Zapopan, she'd come home and tell stories about kids screaming themselves blue and laugh, and now she's the one with a thing on her neck and a CT scan tomorrow at 10am at Hospital San Javier in Zapopan and a voice that goes up at the end of every sentence like she's asking permission.

dad was at the chess club so I had her to myself for an hour. she made me chilaquiles even though I told her I'd eat air, fine, anything, please don't fuss. the chilaquiles were red, no cilantro because she remembers the soap-gene thing and has remembered it since I was 9 and refused her famous cilantro rice in front of Tía Rosario at a christening and embarrassed her, and she put two extra over-easy eggs on top and said 'come, mijo, you look like you sleep in an office.' which I do, basically. and then while I was eating she finally let me feel the lump. it's left side of the neck, just below the angle of the jaw, soft, mobile. that last word was hers — 'mobile' — she said it the way only a nurse would say it, halfway hopeful, halfway not letting herself be hopeful. she said the GP at the IMSS clinic told her 'probably a lipoma but we want to be sure' and she repeated it three times in a row, 'probably, probably, probably,' like she was pacing herself. she said the GP looked at her with the eyes you give a colleague — mom's been at IMSS since the year I was born, she knows half the staff, she trained one of the nurses in pediatric vein-finding back in 2008 — and that pissed her off, she didn't want the colleague-eyes, she wanted the patient-eyes. she wanted to be told the same thing they tell the woman who came in off the bus from Tonalá with a plastic bag full of medical records.

back is killing me. the airplane seat was a cruelty. I've been moving around the house in the same shuffle Mama Lupita did the year before she died, and I do not want that comparison in my head right now but here we are. I went into the spare room — what mom still calls 'tu cuarto' even though my old desk is now where she stores the Christmas decorations and there's a plastic bin of tinsel where my Atari used to be and a small pile of Reader's Digest from 2003 that nobody has touched since I left for ITESM — and I just lay on the floor on the rug for twenty minutes, staring at the ceiling, doing the back stretch the kinesiologist showed me a few years ago in CDMX. one knee to chest, hold ten breaths, switch. on the third repetition I started crying for absolutely no reason I could name. I don't know if it was the back or the chilaquiles or my mother saying 'mobile' or all of it. probably all of it. probably the airplane seat. probably the bombilla on the kitchen shelf with my name on the rim, which mom has kept in the kitchen since I was 22 — when Mama Lupita gave it to me — even though I took the cup itself with me when I moved to CDMX. she kept the box. she kept the dust cover. she kept the little fabric bag with the embroidered M for Mendoza, Lupita's surname. like she was holding the rest of the kit waiting for me to come back and reassemble.

I sent Camila a voice note from the floor. she said 'respira, vida, una cosa a la vez.' she's good at that. she lost her own mom less than a year ago and she's the one steadying me. eso me parte. I told her I felt like an idiot for the back, on top of everything. she said 'no eres un idiota, sos un kickboxer cansado y triste, y el cuerpo dice cosas.' the way she puts a Spanish sentence with a Brazilian rhythm and an Argentinean voseo all in one sentence kills me every time. I told her about the chilaquiles. she said 'tu mamá te quiere mucho.' I told her about the cardboard fan. she said 'qué linda.' I told her I was going to take a nap on the floor. she said 'usá una almohada.' I used a pillow. I slept for forty minutes on the rug under a flag of Jalisco that mom hung on the wall in 2002 and never took down.

later addendum, written from bed at 11pm: I keep thinking how grief actually works in the body. when Camila's mom died last October I noticed Camila's neck — she carried it tilted slightly to one side for about three weeks. like the weight of her grief was not metaphorical, it was kilos, and she was compensating mechanically. I am, I think, doing some version of that now. the back tweak in sparring isn't only about the kick. I came into Wednesday's class with my shoulders up around my ears because mom had called me on Tuesday with the lump news and I'd lied and told her I was fine. I lied to my mother and then I went and got hurt as if my body was settling the books. Profe Esteban — I haven't told him yet about mom — would say it differently. he would say 'no estabas presente, mijo. te castigaste.' he'd say it kindly. then he'd hand me a foam roller and walk away.

the other thing I want to write down before I sleep is the smell of this house. it has not changed in 33 years. it is the smell of mom's perfume (the one with the mariposa on the bottle, she's worn it since 1989), the smell of dad's pipe tobacco (he doesn't smoke pipe anymore but the curtains still have it), the smell of mole base from the Saturdays mom makes mole, the smell of Pinol cleaner, and underneath everything the smell of clay roof tile baking in the sun on a hot afternoon. I have walked into a thousand houses in my life and only this one smells like this. I close my eyes and I am 8 and 14 and 19 and 33 simultaneously. the house is the same. I am not the same. mom is the one with the lump now. I am the son with the back. dad is the one with the angina and the chess notebooks. it is the same house with different problems and the same smell.

and the Pointer. let me write about the Pointer one more sentence. it is a 1996 Volkswagen Pointer in the color the dealership called 'cobalt' and that everyone else calls 'azul mexicano,' the same color as a thousand camionetas in this state. it has 230,000 km. mom drives it like a careful nurse, never above 70 on the libramiento, both hands at 10 and 2, and she makes that little tongue-click when other drivers pass her aggressively. that little tongue-click is half my childhood. I am 33 and I made the same tongue-click last week when an Uber cut me off in Reforma. you become your mother. you become your mother's small noises. and her small noises become safe to you, and then one day you make them, and you realize that's what 'home' is, that's what the word actually means. it's not a place. it's the catalog of small sounds your parents made that you eventually start making.

let me also write that I noticed something on the way home from the airport that I did not want to notice. her hands on the steering wheel are smaller. or maybe they are the same size they have always been and my hands have grown — my hands are full kickboxer hands now, calloused at the knuckles, slightly broader at the palms than they were at 25 because of ten years of glove work — and her hands look small only in comparison. either way, the ratio between her hand and the wheel is different than it used to be. there is a kind of hand that is the hand of a mother who has retired from her career but has not retired from being a mother. that is the hand at 10 and 2 on the Pointer. it is the hand that has carried thirty-five years of pediatric IV lines and held a small thermometer to a thousand fevered foreheads. it is the hand that pulled my brother Mateo out of the front seat when he tried to climb out of the moving car at age 4. it is the hand that signed for the IMSS pension paperwork in 2024. and it is the hand that, three days ago, found a lump on her own neck. her hand found her own lump. that is what the hand is now. and tomorrow at 10am the radiologist's hand — a stranger's hand — will rest on the same neck and decide what the lump is. the symmetry of those two hands keeps me awake.

ándale. tomorrow we know. tomorrow we know. I'll set my alarm. I'll wear the shirt mom bought me for Christmas 2022 because she'll want to see me in it. I'll hold her bag in radiology. I'll pretend I'm not afraid. I'll be afraid anyway. so will she. so will dad. but we'll go in together. that's all it is. that's all you can do.

dad came home from chess club at almost 10pm. lost two of three to Don Heriberto, didn't seem to mind. he said 'Heriberto está jugando la Caro-Kann otra vez, le da resultados, hay que respetarla.' which is, in dad-speak, 'I have to study harder.' he saw me on the floor on my back doing the knee-to-chest thing and just said 'la espalda, no.' put a heat pack on the small of my back without asking — the one mom keeps in a drawer with the iron. didn't ask about the hospital tomorrow. that's how he does worry. he gets practical. he treats the available problem. a son with back pain is a problem he can heat up. a wife with a CT scan is not a problem he knows how to operate on. so he handles the back. mom watched from the kitchen door and didn't say anything either. a quiet little family of people not asking about the thing.

we ate together at the table — the same wooden table from my childhood, the one with the scratch on the corner where I dropped a pewter horse from the chess set in 1998 — mom had made tortilla de papa with the chorizo on the side because she remembered I'm vegetarian, she set it up so that everyone could serve themselves the chorizo and the eggs without having to argue with anyone about whose plate had what. that is mom. that is the engineer of small dignities at a family table. dad asked me about RouteOpt. I gave him the simple version: rust service, geographic queries, will replace an old Python service over the next year. dad nodded thoughtfully the way he nods when I tell him about software, which is the same nod he gives Don Heriberto when Don Heriberto is explaining a chess opening dad already understands. dad has been a high school physics teacher for 35 years. he understands more about systems than he lets on. he just doesn't speak the same vocabulary, which means I have to translate, and I am tired, and I am not sure he wants the long version anyway. what he wants is for his son to be at the dinner table.

after dinner he went to his chair in the living room and opened his chess notebook on the side table. didn't say anything. didn't watch TV. just opened the notebook, took out a pencil, played both sides of a Caro-Kann line for thirty minutes, made some annotations in his small tilted handwriting, closed the notebook. mom sat next to him knitting. nube was not here — Camila has her in CDMX — but she would have curled up under his chair. nobody talked about the CT scan. nobody talked about my back. the radio was on at low volume — Radio Universidad, classical at this hour, some Brahms quintet. mom poured him a glass of water without asking. he drank it without thanking her. they have been doing this dance for forty years. it is the dance of being married for forty years. it does not require speaking. I went to bed and listened to it from my old room, the radio and the knitting needles and the soft clack of dad putting his pencil down on the side table. I have not slept that well in months. seven hours straight. the body, with the back tweak and the airplane and the lump-news adrenaline finally letting go, just shut down.

dad's BP is fine, btw. I asked. he turned 66 and his cardiologist says he's better than he's been in five years. 'gracias a tu loca dieta,' he calls it, the thing where I stopped eating meat after watching them crack his sternum open in 2022. he laughs every time he says 'tu loca dieta' like the connection between his vegetarian son and his own statin compliance is some kind of family joke and not, you know, the foundational trauma of my late twenties. anyway. he made tortilla de papa for dinner, apologized that there was no chorizo because mom bought him chorizo. asked twice if I had eaten. classic Hugo.

the bypass story is, every year, slightly more compressed in family memory. in 2022 it was the central event of an entire winter — the surgery in October, the recovery in November, the rehab in December, the cardiologist appointments through Christmas. now in 2026 it is the thing dad jokes about over chorizo. that is how families process trauma: by sanding it down with repetition until it has the texture of a household saying. 'tu loca dieta' is one of the household sayings now. so is 'la cosa esa que le pasó a tu papá en el 22.' so is 'cuando tu mamá vino al hospital con el suéter mal abotonado.' all of these are family jokes now. they were not jokes at the time. they became jokes because surviving them earned us the right to make them jokes.

I think tonight, watching him in his chair with his chess notebook, I felt the bypass year compress further. I felt it land as a single sentence rather than a winter. and I felt mom's scare maybe doing the same thing in real time — getting compressed into 'la semana del bulto' even before we know whether the bulto is benign. it is. it has to be. that is the bargain. that is the only bargain I am offering the universe tonight. but I am noticing how families begin to make a joke out of a fear before the fear has even resolved, and I think that is actually adaptive. the joke is a placeholder. the joke says: we are going to survive this, and when we do, this is the shape the surviving will take, and we are going to start fitting ourselves into that shape now. mom asked tonight, while she was washing a plate, 'mañana en el hospital me llevas en La Pointer o tomamos un Uber.' she did not say 'mañana, el día del CT scan.' she said 'mañana en el hospital,' the way you'd say 'mañana en la tienda.' she is already making it a chore. she is already making it a thing-to-be-done. and I love her for it. ándale. mañana en el hospital. en La Pointer. claro.

side reflection because I cannot sleep. the year of the bypass — 2022 — was the year I started kickboxing seriously. those are not coincidences. dad's near-death made me look at my own body for the first time in my late twenties. I had been a software engineer who ran sometimes. the year dad was on the operating table I joined Atlas Box and I started working out four days a week and I have not stopped in 4 years. I have brown belt now because of dad's chest cracking open. that is a sentence I have never said out loud. I write it down here because it is true. children of cardiac patients sometimes get scared into their own discipline. that's me. dad's surgery is the reason I am fit at 33. dad does not know this. I do not think I will ever tell him. it would embarrass us both. but the brown belt I will earn a black belt over in the next eight months — that belt has dad's surgery in its origin story. ándale, papá. te debo más de lo que sabes.

scan day. mom in a paper gown looks like a bird. I held her bag with the rosary in it and the IMSS card and the little compact mirror she has carried since 1991 — I know because she told me once, the year she bought it, the same year I was born. radiology is on the second floor, hospital San Javier in Zapopan. tile floor, fluorescent lights, smell of disinfectant and cheap coffee from the cafeteria one floor down. they took her in at 10:14. tech said results in 24-48h, smiled the practiced smile of someone who delivers that sentence twenty times a day. mom waved. dad held the empty seat. now we wait.

we waited in the cafeteria because I couldn't sit in the radiology lobby a minute longer. mom drank a Sidral Mundet and ate half a cookie and pretended she wasn't anxious by talking about Marina's daughter's quinceañera and which DJ they hired and how that DJ also did Coco Hernández's son's bautizo and ohhhh the music was awful, mijo, awful. she did a full ten-minute tangent on a DJ to avoid thinking about a CT scan. I let her. I held her hand under the table. she squeezed back twice. 'two squeezes for I love you,' she used to do that on bus rides when I was 7.

dad didn't come into the cafeteria. dad walked the parking lot for the better part of an hour, slowly, in his loafers, hands behind his back like a colonel inspecting a regiment. I could see him through the window from our table. he walked twelve circuits of the lot. I counted because counting was something to do. each circuit took about five minutes. on the thirteenth he came inside and sat with us and ordered an espresso and drank it standing. he said one thing the whole time: 'la radióloga es la doctora Pacheco, ¿verdad?' mom said yes. dad said 'la conozco. era amiga de la prima Olga.' that was the entire content of his anxiety management. identify the radiologist. trace her through a third party. confirm she is locatable in the social fabric. relax microscopically.

the cafeteria is the kind of hospital cafeteria with plastic chairs in beige and round tables with a salt shaker, a sugar bowl, and a small vase with a dried flower on each one. the dried flower at our table was a faded pink carnation. mom kept reaching for it without thinking and turning the vase a quarter-turn each time, the way she used to turn the salt shaker on every restaurant table in my entire childhood. that small motion is her version of fidgeting. she does not bite her nails. she does not drum her fingers. she turns the vase. I watched her do it ten times. I did not say anything. I just kept holding her hand under the table and let her turn the vase with the other one. I think I will remember that carnation for a long time.

I texted Mateo from the cafeteria too. my brother, the graphic designer in Tijuana, six years younger, currently working on a series of posters for a punk festival in Ensenada. he replied within three minutes: 'cabrón estoy sentado en mi auto en el chingado tráfico de Tijuana llorando, dile a mi mamá que la amo, dile a mi papá que mañana le hablo, dile a ti que te amo cabrón, ya respira.' that is Mateo. he can write a sentence with three different verbs of love and an obscenity in the same breath. I read it twice and put my phone face-down on the table because I did not want mom to see me do the thing my face was about to do. she was on her third Sidral by then. she had moved from the DJ to the new shopping mall they are building in Zapopan and how the parking lot is going to ruin the neighborhood. let her. let her tangent for as long as she needs to. that's the thing to do for a sister-mother-wife in a hospital cafeteria. you do not redirect. you follow her into the parking lot. you nod about the parking lot. ándale. mañana el scan.

Roberto pinged me three times today on Slack. 'standup?' 'standup?' 'standup??' I told him I'm OOO with a family emergency. he replied 'understood, please post a written update by EOD so the team isn't blocked.' so the team — by which he means him — isn't blocked. unblocked from what, exactly. I am pushing absolutely zero PRs from a hospital cafeteria, manager man. I sent him a one-line: 'PostGIS bbox query merged yesterday, blocked on QA env, no other updates.' that's all you get.

the more I deal with Roberto the more I miss Daniela. Daniela ran the team like she trusted us. her standup was 90 seconds — 'who's blocked?' — and if no one was blocked the meeting ended. she'd send a 'good luck' text on the morning of a hard PR review and a 'descansa' text at 6pm on the day someone shipped something. she did the things managers are supposed to do. Roberto does the things managers do when they don't trust the team. he wants written updates because he is anxious about visibility. he wants standups to take twenty minutes because he is using the meeting to feel managerial. he is not a bad person. he is a person who is bad at this specific job. those are different things. I should remember that they are different things when I am tempted to be cruel.

the thing about Roberto is that he is, structurally, one of the people who got promoted into management because he was a good individual contributor at his last firm and somebody decided 'good IC = good manager' which is one of the most enduring industry mistakes. he is great at debugging a CI pipeline. he is bad at trusting an engineer he does not know yet with a load-bearing system in production. he is bad at the part of the job that is not code. and I am, unfortunately, the engineer he does not know yet, with a load-bearing system in production. so we are in a learning phase together where my job is to be the most legible engineer he has ever managed. legible enough that he doesn't have to manage me. legible enough that he can leave me alone. which means written updates. fine. I'll write them. but I will not let them eat the day I spend with my mother while she waits for a CT result.

I am in a hospital cafeteria looking out the window at my father walking in circles and my manager wants a written update. I will give him the written update. I will write it from the cafeteria with my left thumb on my phone while my mother does a tangent about a DJ at a quinceañera. and then I will close Slack. and then I will not open it for the next 24 hours no matter what he writes. that is the limit. that is the line. mom is in a paper gown. the rust service can wait one day. ándale.

addendum from later that night, in the spare bedroom, after dad fell asleep on the couch in front of a Pumas game: I have been replaying the Slack thread with Roberto in my head and I want to write down the thing I have been avoiding. I think Roberto is going to be my manager for at least the next 12 months and possibly the next 24, and I have been treating him as a temporary obstacle. that is wishful thinking. he is not temporary. he was hired to replace Daniela permanently. he is not going to leave and Daniela is not coming back. so I have to either learn how to work with him or leave Brújula. those are the two options. there is no third option where Roberto becomes Daniela. and yet I have been operating, internally, as if option three exists. that is a category error. I am writing it down here so that future me has it in writing: option three does not exist. it is option one or option two. I am, for now, choosing option one. I will revisit in six months. ándale. mom is sleeping. dad is on the couch. the Pumas lost. la noche es larga. mañana el CT scan.

Profe Esteban texted me. word travels in Atlas Box — I think Pablo told him I tweaked my back. he wrote 'mijo, escúchame: el miércoles te quedas sentado. nada de patadas, nada de plyo. te quiero ver, pero te quiero ver entero.' I'm honestly relieved. when Profe orders you to sit out, you sit out, that's how the man works. he was a pro fighter for fifteen years, fought on Mexican TV in the late 90s, has a torn rotator cuff he never repaired and two ACL surgeries on the same knee. he knows what an injury looks like in the first 72 hours and what it looks like in the next 72 weeks if you don't shut up about it. so Wednesday I'm benched. I'll go to the gym, watch class, take notes, drink agua de jamaica from the cooler, and not throw a single round kick. it's going to itch.

the rule with Profe is that you do not negotiate with him about injuries because if you negotiate he benches you for an extra week as a tax. he calls it the 'impuesto de necedad,' the stubbornness tax. last year a yellow belt named Rafa argued with him about training on a sprained ankle and Rafa got sat out for three weeks instead of one. nobody argues with Profe anymore. you nod, you sit, you serve your time, you come back better. the entire gym culture is built around the unspoken understanding that Profe knows the body better than you know yours, and that the small humiliation of being benched is the price of admission to a long career.

I am also going to use Wednesday's bench-time productively. I'll bring a notebook. I will watch Pablo's left low kick because his left low kick has gotten heavier in the last month and I want to know what he is doing differently. I'll watch the new yellow belts and try to remember what it felt like to be at that level — that thing where you're trying to put your hip into the kick and the hip is doing five other things wrong at the same time. there is something useful about being a senior student who has to sit out and watch. it makes me a better senior student when I come back. it makes me less arrogant. I will write it all down so that Iván the PT and Profe and future-me can all see I took this seriously instead of moping. moping is what 25-year-old me would have done. moping is the impuesto de necedad in slow motion. I am going to do the work.
