let me also note something Profe said in a previous bench-time conversation that I am holding onto. it was June 2024, I had a sprained left wrist from a botched parry, I was benched for ten days. on day three I was sitting on the bleachers watching class with a Tecate Light in a paper cup that I was pretending was an electrolyte drink, and Profe sat down next to me. he said, in Spanish, in the slow careful way he says important things, 'mijo, the body teaches you in three ways. the first is when it works. the second is when it breaks. the third is when you watch other bodies. right now you are in the third school. it is the slowest. it is also the deepest. do not waste it.' I have not wasted it since. that is the spirit I am bringing to Wednesday. it is the spirit I will bring to every Wednesday I am benched for the rest of my training career. ándale, profe. te lo prometo.

the irony of getting hurt the week mom finds a lump. body decides solidarity is its love language. injure thy spine in mom's honor. amen. the universe has a clumsy sense of narrative — it likes to underline its lessons with physical objects, lump on the neck, twinge in the lumbar, both of them at the same time, just in case I missed the connection. ok universe. I see it. I am paying attention. I am noticing. I will stretch. I will breathe. I will call my mother. I will not throw a round kick for two weeks. I am ready for the next chapter. you can stop underlining now.

mom and I walked to the panadería this evening. slow walk. she wanted a concha. she was a pediatric nurse for 35 years, she's seen a thousand neck CTs come back lipoma — she said this twice tonight, once on the walk, once at the table — 'son los más comunes, mijo, los más comunes.' she's saying it to herself, not to me. the panadería still has the same señor at the till from when I was 14. he didn't recognize me. he asked mom if I was her nephew. she laughed too loud. then she introduced me, with her hand on the back of my neck the way she has always introduced me to acquaintances since I was 6. 'mi hijo Sebastián, vive en CDMX, ingeniero, vino a verme.' the señor — turns out his name is Don Beto, has been at the panadería since 1989, mom has been buying conchas from him for 37 years — said 'qué bueno mijo, qué bueno que viniste a ver a tu mamá.' that hit me harder than I expected. some days a stranger commenting on the obvious is the thing that makes you want to cry.

we walked back the long way through the small park near the house, the one with the dry fountain and the bench where mom used to wait for the bus to IMSS in the years before they bought the Pointer. she pointed at the bench and said 'ahí me pasé media vida.' she meant literally — half her career, the early years before they could afford a second car, was spent at that bench at 5:30am. she said it without bitterness. she said it like a person noticing a small piece of furniture that had been hers, briefly, in another life. then we walked another block and she pointed at a tree and said 'aquí te caíste cuando tenías cinco.' she remembers the trees. she remembers the corners where I fell off bikes. she remembers the streetlight that flickered in the months I was a colicky baby. her brain is a topographical map of her own children's small disasters and small triumphs and I am walking through it tonight at the speed of a 62-year-old woman who is afraid of her own CT scan but who has decided to talk about a panadería instead.

side note for the record. when we got home she put the conchas in the same wicker basket she has used for 25 years — the one with the broken handle that she has rewired with kitchen twine three different times — and she said 'tu papá no debe comer esto, le subió el azúcar.' so she ate one, I ate one, and we left the third in the basket on the counter as a kind of edible monument. dad came in from the patio later, saw the concha, looked at mom, said nothing, ate half of it, walked back out. mom shook her head and muttered 'cabrón.' that is their love language. it has been their love language for thirty-eight years. she keeps a list, in her head, of foods he should not eat. he eats them anyway. she says 'cabrón.' he laughs. they continue. I have grown up watching this exact dance and I have come to understand it is one of the four or five things keeping them married. it is also, I now suspect, the foundation of my own approach to boundaries with people I love — half-enforce, half-acknowledge the violation, never actually fight, never let it ruin the meal. I learned that from these two. I should thank them for it. I have not. someday I will. ándale.

I keep thinking about Mama Lupita. I'm sitting here in my old room with the bombilla she gave me — the one with my name on the rim, 'Seb,' her writing curled at the bottom of the cup — and I keep thinking how I didn't visit her in 2019 the way I should have. she was in Atotonilco, two hours by bus from Guadalajara, she was already small, and I was 25 and I told myself 'next month.' and then she got pneumonia and then she didn't get out of pneumonia and that was the year. and I'm here right now, in October seven years later, because mom found a lump and I caught the first flight, I dropped everything, I made Roberto angry, I lost a week of rust velocity. and that's the right call. that's the obvious right call. but what it really is, if I'm being honest, is correction. I'm correcting for something Mama Lupita didn't get from me. I don't know if she'd care. she was a pragmatic woman, she'd probably tell me 'no seas sentimental, mijo, vivime, no me llores.' but I am sentimental. that's the engraved name on the cup, that's the candle I light every March 14, that's me at 33 in a paper-thin ranchera-print bedspread on a twin mattress because I will not screw this up the way I screwed up the last one.

the thing I want to say to anyone listening — and by anyone I mean me, the person re-reading this when I'm 40 — is that grief teaches a thing about reaction time. before Lupita I was a 'next month' kind of grandson. I am no longer a 'next month' kind of son. that's the lesson. that's what 2019 cost me to learn. it's a stupid expensive lesson but it's the one I have. and there is a darker version of that lesson — the version where someone tells you 'don't be sentimental about this, you were a kid in 2019, you didn't have money, you couldn't fly to Atotonilco every weekend, you were working eight hours away in a Wizeline cubicle in CDMX learning how to talk to American banks about retail wire transfers' — but I notice when I tell myself that version of the story I get less. I get less from it. it doesn't change my behavior. the version that changes my behavior is the one where I just say: I missed her. I missed her last year. I had the time and I had the money and I had a working car — a 2013 Sentra at the time, before La Torta — and I told myself a lie about how busy I was and Mama Lupita died not knowing that I had been about to come, because I had not been about to come, I had been about to 'come next month,' which is a kind of intent that does not arrive.

let me write about the cup itself for a minute. because I want to remember what it looks like in this room, on this nightstand, at 11:47pm on a Tuesday in October 2026. it is a wooden mate cup, calabaza-shaped, dark from years of yerba, with a leather wrap around the bottom that has gone a little slick in the spots my fingers go. the silver rim has the 'Seb' engraved in Lupita's hand, the same hand that wrote my name on every birthday card for twenty-two years. the bombilla itself is alpaca silver, slightly bent at the tip from when I dropped it on a tile floor in 2020, drinking distance is now slightly off-axis, which I like, it's a fingerprint of my own. inside, the gourd is dark with the patina of ten thousand cups of Cruz de Malta. when I drink from it now I am drinking from a small museum of myself. that's not metaphor. it's literally what the gourd is. each cup of mate I drink lays down another microscopic film. by the time I am 60 the patina will be thick enough to count as the inside of the cup. by the time I am 80 it will be older than I am. by the time I die the cup will be the most patient artifact in my apartment.

so here I am with my back a wreck, in a twin bed in my mother's spare room, in a city I left at 22 and have only come back to for weddings and funerals, holding a bombilla cup that an old woman engraved with my name eight years before she died, drinking yerba mate at 11:47pm by the light of a single bedside lamp, listening to my dad snore in the room next door, and I am here. I am here. that's the difference. there isn't a tumor in my mother yet — please god — but there is a lump and there is a CT scan and I am here. that is correction. that is the body of a 33-year-old man making up for the impatience of a 25-year-old man. and if mom calls me from this room in ten years asking me to come because something else has gone wrong with her, I will be here. I will be on the next flight. I will not say 'next month.' I will say 'in three hours, mom, hold on.'

and I want to say one more thing because the mate is hot and the room is quiet and I am not going to sleep tonight. I want to say that the bombilla itself — this little metal cup with the leather wrap around the bottom and my name engraved on the rim in Mama Lupita's curling script — has come to mean something I did not understand when she gave it to me. I was 22. I had just started at Kueski. I was newly in CDMX. she gave me the cup and a packet of Cruz de Malta yerba and said 'para que pienses despacio,' for slow thinking. I thought she meant 'don't rush at work.' I thought she was advising me about my career. it took me eight years to understand she meant: don't rush at people. don't rush at love. don't rush at the people who are going to die before you do. drink slowly. think slowly. show up slowly. she didn't have a degree. she had this. and I drink mate four to six times a day from this cup and I am only now, here, on this bed, hearing what she said to me when she handed it over. ándale, abuela. perdóname. te juro que aprendí. tarde, pero aprendí.

one more thing and then I'll try to sleep. she taught me a small thing about candles, too, which I want to write down because I have not written it down anywhere. on her birthday in March I light a candle for her. that's the public ritual. but the private ritual — the one she taught me when I was twelve, sitting in her kitchen in Atotonilco watching her light candles for her own dead mother — is that the candle has to be lit while you say the dead person's name out loud in a kitchen. not a church. not a bedroom. a kitchen. she said 'la cocina es donde la abuela quiere que la recuerdes, no la iglesia, en la iglesia ya no la sirves nada.' the kitchen is where the grandmother wants to be remembered, not the church, in the church you can't serve her anything. so every March 14 I stand in the kitchen of the Roma Norte apartment with the candle on the counter and I say 'María Guadalupe Mendoza, te recuerdo,' out loud, and Camila knows to stay out of the kitchen for those 30 seconds, and the candle burns through its little hour, and I make myself a coffee, and that's the ritual. it is not religious in any sense the church would recognize. it is religious in a sense Lupita would recognize. it works. I have done it seven times now. I will do it seven more, and seven more, and however many. the kitchen knows. that's the point. ándale.

let me also write down a thing about her hands. she had the hands of a woman who had kneaded bread three times a week for sixty years. the back of her hand was the texture of a paper bag that has been folded too many times. the palms were smooth. the knuckles were wide. she wore a thin gold band on the ring finger, no other rings, ever. when she engraved 'Seb' on the rim of the cup she did it freehand with a small hand-tool from a leather workshop on the main square in Atotonilco where the engraver was a man she had known since the 1970s, a man named Don Próspero who is also dead now, who engraved cups for everyone in town and who, she told me once, did not charge her for the engraving on my cup because he had known her since they were both 18 and he had not forgiven himself for not marrying her in 1962. I did not know any of that until I was 30 and asked her where the cup came from. these are the stories that are buried in the small objects in your mother's spare room. you have to ask. nobody volunteers them. you have to ask.

enough. the mate is finished. the second cup is in the gourd ready to pour but I am too tired to drink it. I will leave the bombilla in the gourd overnight, which Lupita would have scolded me for — 'mijo, sécalo, se va a oxidar' — but tonight I am going to leave it. tonight I am going to be a man with a bombilla in a gourd in a spare room in his mother's house in Guadalajara at 12:14am with the lamp off and the mate going slowly cold and the rain just starting to fall on the clay tiles outside. tomorrow we know. ándale.

no results yet. radiology says tomorrow morning. mom is sleeping — she finally let herself, passed out on the couch with the news muted on the TV and the rosary still in her hand. dad is in his chair, asleep with the chess board open, three pieces still on the squares from a position he was studying. nobody has eaten dinner properly. there is a half-empty tortilla de papa in the fridge, which mom will reheat tomorrow without comment. I'm going to bed. setting the alarm for 7am so I'm fully dressed and coffee'd before the call.

BENIGN. it's a lipoma. radiologist confirmed at 9:42am — Dra. Pacheco, the one dad recognized as a friend of cousin Olga's, called mom's mobile, mom answered on speaker, kitchen in full daylight, dad's hand on the coffee pot, my piece of bolillo halfway to my mouth. she said 'señora Castellanos, los hallazgos son consistentes con un lipoma subcutáneo, no se observa adenopatía, sugerimos seguimiento clínico en seis meses.' mom is on the kitchen floor crying laughing. dad is making coffee like nothing happened, which is how dad does relief. I'm shaking. ándale. ándale.

I want to write this down before the feeling evaporates because the feeling will evaporate, that's how relief works, it leaves and you stop being able to remember the shape of the dread that was there an hour ago. I have learned this from previous relief — the relief after dad's bypass came off the operating table in 2022, the relief that the pose-est project would actually run on a phone after months of TF debugging in 2025, the relief on the night I knew Camila would not move back to São Paulo after her mom's funeral. relief erases its own footprint. you have to take notes inside it.

the call came at 9:42am. mom answered on speaker because she wanted me and dad in the room. the radiologist's voice was professional and a little bored, which is exactly what you want — bored is good, bored means routine, routine means nobody is gathering themselves for bad news. she said 'señora Castellanos, los hallazgos son consistentes con un lipoma subcutáneo, no se observa adenopatía, sugerimos seguimiento clínico en seis meses,' and mom said 'gracias doctora' in a voice that didn't sound like her voice and the call ended and there was about three seconds of complete silence in the kitchen. dad's hand was on the coffee pot. I had a piece of bolillo halfway to my mouth. a flock of grackles outside, the AC unit clicking. I think I was holding my breath because when the silence ended I made a sound I have never made before, a sort of laugh-yelp, and mom slid down with her back against the cupboard and just sat on the floor and then I was on the floor with her and dad was patting my shoulder over and over like he was burping a baby and saying 'ya, ya, ya' which is the most expressive Hugo Río has ever been in my adult life. my dad — physics teacher, 66, retired, half-deaf in the left ear because of a chemistry accident in 1979 — saying 'ya, ya, ya' while patting my shoulder. you tell that man you're proud of him and he goes silent for a week. you tell him his wife is fine and he becomes a hand on your shoulder.

I called Camila. she was in a site meeting in Polanco — the project where they're retrofitting that 1960s tower behind the Sheraton, the one with the famous concrete fins. she stepped outside. when she heard 'lipoma' she said 'gracias a Dios' and then said nothing for a long time, and I realized she was crying because of her own mother — because of how recently she got the other phone call, the one that went the other way. the one where the doctor in São Paulo said 'señorita, lo siento mucho,' and Camila slid down a wall in an Uber Black at 4pm in the rain and her mother was already gone before they could fly out. I felt awful for being the one with the good news. she said 'no, no, no seas tonto, agradece, agradece, te quiero, ya respira, ya respira.' five years and I still don't deserve her some days. I told her I'd be back in CDMX by Sunday and she said 'no apures, quedate con tu mamá, hace una semana entera, hace lo que tengas que hacer, yo cuido a la nena,' meaning nube. nube is 'la nena' to her. they have a whole language. I told her dad had patted my shoulder. she said 'qué hermoso, lo voy a recordar siempre.' she means it. she remembers things people say in moments like that. she will reference dad patting my shoulder five years from now in a Christmas dinner toast. that's how she works.

I texted Pablo. he replied 'no manches qué bueno cabrón, te invito a una fría cuando regreses' which is Pablo for 'I love you.' he then sent a photo of the Atlas Box mat with a single red sparring helmet sitting in the middle, a private joke we have about how I can't spar for two more weeks but the mat is waiting. classic Pablo. understated, perfect. he is the man I would call if I had to dispose of a body. I will probably never have to dispose of a body. but if I did. it would be Pablo. that's the friendship.

I texted Mateo. he replied 'avísale a Sofía, ella pregunta cada día.' I had not realized Sofía — my brother's fiancée, dental hygienist in Tijuana, met-her-twice-this-year Sofía — was praying for my mother. that hit me sideways. the family has been knitting itself together around mom's neck this week in ways I didn't know were happening. Marina, the cousin who runs the panadería — she lit a candle. Tía Rosario in Mexicali — she lit two and called the priest. apparently a chain. apparently a literal phone chain of women in three states sending mom intentions, which is the word my Tía Rosario uses, 'mandándole intenciones.' nobody told me about this network. it just was. it activated when the lump appeared and now it deactivates and goes back to its other business, lighting candles for somebody else's mom somewhere else.

dad is now insisting we go to the panadería. all three of us. mom is changing into her good blouse for some reason, she keeps saying 'no es nada, no es nada,' and putting on lipstick. relief makes my mother vain in a way that is genuinely beautiful. she wants to walk out into the world with her pretty face on and prove she is fine. let her. I'll buy every concha in the case if she asks.

later, after panadería, after we walked back the long way through the park because dad wanted to: I'm sitting on the back patio with mom while dad naps in the chair inside. she's looking at the planters where her gardenias were before they died last summer and she said something I want to remember. she said 'mijo, no me había dado cuenta de lo cansada que estaba de tener miedo, hasta que dejé de tenerlo.' she said she didn't realize how tired she was of being afraid until she stopped being afraid. and I thought about the entire week, the seven nights of mom not sleeping, the seven mornings of mom getting up at 6am and putting the coffee on like normal and pretending nothing was different, the way she kept touching the lump in the kitchen when she thought I wasn't looking, the way she kept washing the same plate three times. the body of a 62-year-old woman carrying a CT scan around inside her chest for a week. and now putting it down. and discovering only by putting it down how heavy it had been.

I hugged her. she smelled like the mariposa-in-the-jar perfume she's worn since I was a child. I told her I was going to call her every Sunday from now on at 11am, no exceptions. she laughed and said 'mijo, ya me llamabas cada dos semanas, sé realista.' fair. I told her every other Sunday. she said 'eso sí.' deal.

last thing for tonight. when we got back from the panadería there was a voicemail on the landline from Tía Rosario in Mexicali. she had heard the news. her message was three minutes long. she said the rosary on tape, into the answering machine, the entire rosary, and at the end she said 'gracias, Diosito, por mi hermana, gracias, gracias, gracias.' three minutes of an old woman saying the rosary into a 1990s answering machine in Guadalajara. mom played it twice and then deleted it on the second play because she said she couldn't keep listening to it without falling apart. dad listened in the doorway with his hands in his pockets. nobody said anything afterwards. that's how it goes. somebody prays for somebody else into a tape. the tape gets deleted. the prayer remains in the air, somehow. lapsed Catholic that I am, I cannot disprove this. I will not try.

one more thing — and this is the thing I most want to remember. tonight, before bed, mom stood in the kitchen drying a plate and said, almost to herself, 'mijo, qué cosa tan rara, sentirme tan agradecida y tan triste al mismo tiempo.' she did not elaborate. she did not need to. she meant: she was happy the lump was nothing AND she was sad about all the years she has spent being afraid of small lumps in small ways, and about all the women in her life who got the other phone call, and about her own mother's death in 1998, and about the fact that this is what 62 looks like, that the relief and the grief and the gratitude all stack on top of each other and you don't get to feel them clean. she said it and then she put the plate down and went to bed. I sat at the kitchen table with the radio on at low volume and listened to a man on the local AM station read the weather for tomorrow and then I went to bed too. tomorrow is supposed to be sunny. tomorrow I will buy mom flowers. tomorrow I will be the kind of son who buys his mother flowers on the day after the call comes back benign. ándale. la cosa esa que le pasó. la cosa esa que no le pasó.

mom's first move post-relief was to cook. of course. she made enchiladas verdes for the neighbors as a kind of gratitude offering. nobody asked for them. the neighbors got pans of enchiladas they didn't know they were getting. doña Concha next door cried a little. doña Olivia from across the street accepted the pan with a polite face that did not match the welt of feeling that was clearly happening behind her eyes — Olivia has lost two sisters to cancer in the last decade, and the news of mom's clean scan visibly landed on her like a long-delayed parcel. mom told everyone 'me dijeron que no es nada' like she was sharing weather. I ate three. dad ate four. there were two bottles of Coca-Cola from the corner tienda. it was like being 12 again, except I'm vegetarian now and mom carefully made my pan with mushrooms, which she called a 'pendejada vegetariana' to my face, with enormous love.

after the enchilada-distribution we sat at the table — me, mom, dad, an empty plate for nobody — and ate the leftovers in something close to silence. mom kept getting up to wipe a counter that did not need wiping. dad kept slicing pieces of bolillo and not eating them. these are the small displacement activities of people who have just released a week of dread and don't know what to do with the empty room inside themselves yet. there is a phenomenon where after relief the body keeps producing the chemicals it was producing during fear, and you have to wait for them to clear, and during the wait you wipe counters and slice bolillo. that was tonight.

I called Camila on the way back to the spare room. she answered on speaker — she was cooking. risotto with mushrooms, our Friday-night ritual, even though I was not there. she had still made it. she said 'es ritual, no es ritual contigo, es ritual.' the woman is 30 years old and already operates with the dignity of an 80-year-old contadora who knows every line item of her own life. I love her so much I had to sit on the bed for a minute. then I called her back and we talked about nothing for forty minutes. tomorrow I fly home Sunday. tonight is the second-to-last in this house. I am going to enjoy it.

additional detail I want to record from the day. when the radiologist's report came in via the patient portal at 11:14am, mom was at the stove. she opened the email on her phone with the spatula in her right hand and oil already heating in the comal. she read it. she put the phone down on the counter, face up. she stirred the onions in the comal. she stirred them for about forty-five seconds, until they were soft. then she turned the comal off. then she sat down on the kitchen chair and did not say anything for two full minutes. dad and I stood there. dad eventually said '¿qué dice, mi vida?' and mom said 'es benigno' and burst into tears. dad sat down next to her. they held each other. I made the call to my brother Mateo who was waiting in Tijuana. Mateo cried. I cried. it was 11:16am on a Friday in October and the comal was off and the onions were sitting in the pan getting cold and that is the moment our family learned that mom was not dying. I will remember the cold onions. I will remember the spatula on the counter. ándale.

flying back to CDMX tomorrow morning. one more night here. I bought my dad a new chess clock at the mercado on Av. Chapultepec — the digital one with the buttons he likes, the DGT 1001, hard to find in Guadalajara, I had to ask three vendors. couldn't bend down to pick it up off the bottom shelf so the vendor handed it over without comment. back is still wrecked but I'm walking. dad opened it tonight at the kitchen table, set it up, played both sides of a Caro-Kann line for thirty minutes against himself with the new clock running. mom and I watched him from the couch. he didn't say thank you. he kept playing. that means he loved it.

the DGT 1001 is the same clock used at most amateur tournaments in Mexico. dad has been using a 1990s analog clock with a sticking minute hand for the last eight years. Don Heriberto and the other guys at the club have all upgraded to digitals at various points and dad has stubbornly held on to the analog because — I think — he was raised in a generation where you do not buy yourself things, you wait until somebody gives them to you. that's how Hugo works. so he was waiting for one of his sons to notice and one of them did, finally, at 33. mom whispered to me on the couch 'gracias mijo' in the way she whispers things she would rather dad not hear, even though dad is half-deaf in the left ear and was facing away from us anyway. it was the conspiracy whisper. I miss the conspiracy whisper. there is no whisper in the world like the whisper of a Mexican mother telling you, in three syllables, that you have done a small good thing for your father.

tomorrow morning I fly. mom will pack me a tupperware of leftover enchiladas verdes for Camila even though I will tell her not to and she will do it anyway. dad will drive me to the airport in the Pointer because he insists, even though it would be cheaper and easier in an Uber. we will not talk much in the car. he will hug me at the curb in the particular way Hugo Río hugs — short, tight, one pat on the back, then a slight push away as if to say 'go, go, you have a flight, do not be late.' that's also conspiracy. that's also love. ándale, papá. con cuidado en el regreso.

I want to write down a small specific thing that happened tonight that I want to remember. dad set up the new clock, ran the test mode through twice to make sure both buttons clicked clean, then took out a small velvet pouch from the buffet drawer. it was the pouch he keeps his tournament chess pieces in — wooden Stauntons, weighted, from a tournament he won in Tepic in 1989, his most prized chess possession. I have seen him use these pieces maybe a dozen times in my life. he uses the cheap plastic set for daily play. tonight he set up the wooden ones. he played a Caro-Kann against himself with the wooden pieces and the new clock. he didn't comment on doing this. I didn't comment either. mom looked at me on the couch and her eyes filled and she said nothing. dad was christening the clock with the good pieces. that was the thank you. ándale. ese fue el gracias.

back in Roma Norte. apartment smells like Camila's coffee. Doña Marta on the 6th was already kneading dough at 4:50am and woke me up before the alarm because the floors in this 1948 building transmit every motion of every woman over 60 in a flour-dusted apron, but I was so happy to be home I didn't care. nube didn't recognize me at first then attacked me with kisses. La Polar downstairs is queueing up Saturday lunch — the trompeta player is testing scales through the wall. CDMX. we're back.

Camila and I had the kind of conversation tonight that happens after you almost lose a parent and you are partnered with someone who lost a parent eleven months ago. on the couch, lights off, nube wedged between us. she said 'imagine if it had been the other way' and didn't elaborate, didn't need to. she meant: imagine if this week had ended the way her week with Beatriz ended last October. we sat with that. she cried, the soft kind she doesn't fight. then we talked about Dr. Muñoz — about going back to therapy. we did three sessions back in month 7 around the kid-timing thing and paused. I think we paused too early, I think we used 'we're better' as a way to not have to keep going. she said yes she'd go back if I'd go back. I said yes. easiest yes I've said all year.

the kid-timing thing is, in summary: she wants kids in roughly two years, I am at maybe three or four. we are not far apart but we are not the same. and the difference is compounded by the fact that she lost her mother last year and is, fairly, holding the biological clock more tightly than she would have if Beatriz were still alive to be a future grandmother. I have not always handled the gap well. I have sometimes treated 'three to four' as 'we have time' when the better attitude is 'we have less time than you think.' Dr. Muñoz called this out cleanly in our second session. then we paused. and I think the pause was, on my part, partly avoidance. I did not want to go back into a room where someone with a kind voice was going to gently tell me I was being a coward about a timeline.

tonight, after the lump-week, after the relief, after seeing my mother on the kitchen floor crying laughing, I think I want kids closer than I did a month ago. I do not know if that is the relief talking, or the fear talking, or both, or just an honest update of my own preferences. that is a thing I want to bring to therapy. I told Camila that. she kissed me on the temple and said 'eso es bueno, vida, lo hablamos con la doctora.' she did not push. she rarely pushes. I admire her for not pushing. and yet. and yet I notice tonight that I have been benefiting for a year from the fact that she does not push. I have been getting away with a slowness that is not entirely fair. that's also a thing for Dr. Muñoz. I will book the appointment tomorrow. I will not let this become another pause.

I want to also write down something Camila said later, after we'd been silent for twenty minutes on the couch and I thought she had drifted off. she said, into the darkness — 'sebas, mi mamá no va a conocer a nuestros hijos.' and then she cried again, harder. I held her. I had nothing to say. there is nothing to say to that sentence. Beatriz was 64, she was robust, she was supposed to be alive for another twenty years, she was supposed to teach our kids how to make pão de queijo and how to dance the samba and how to call out their grandfather for buying the wrong brand of coffee. she is not going to do any of that. neither is my abuela Lupita who died when I was 19. the future grandmother bench is, on Camila's side, empty. and on my side, only mom — who almost wasn't there this week. that is the math of the moment we are in. and it is part of why two-years feels different to her than three-years to me. it is not just biology. it is the absence she is parenting against. I have been slow to see that. I see it tonight. I am writing it down. I will bring it to Dr. Muñoz. ándale.

ok decision made. I'm pausing the pose-estimation side project. the kickboxing form analyzer. seven months of weekends, pivoted from TensorFlow to MediaPipe back in month 5, got it running at ~30fps on a phone video, had Pablo and three other guys at Atlas Box test it, logged something like 12 sparring sessions of data — it works. it gives reasonable feedback on stance width, hip rotation angle, kick chamber height. it's not nothing. it's also the most coherent personal project I've completed since I left Wizeline. and I'm pausing it. and I want to write down why, in detail, because future me is going to come back to this in two months and feel guilty and try to revive it without knowing why I stopped, and I want past-me to leave a note past-me will trust.

reasons in order of weight:

1. RouteOpt rust is the bigger fight and it's hitting a memory regression I do not yet understand at the level of a fix. I cannot win two technical fights at once. one of them needs my brain at full power and that one is the one that pays my salary and is being watched by Roberto. the side project has no deadline. RouteOpt has a deadline — month 16 staging, prod a few months after that. losing momentum on rust is going to cost me materially in performance review territory and probably in my equity refresh next year.

2. I tweaked my back. I am not currently the test subject of my own kickboxing form analyzer. no Wednesday class for me, not for at least three weeks, and Profe Esteban said something about wanting an MRI if it's not better in 10 days. I am the lab. the lab is closed. it would feel weird to keep iterating on the analyzer while I am physically banned from the activity it analyzes. I'd be tuning a piano I can't play. additionally, the test users — Pablo and three other guys — are all Atlas Box regulars and are not interested in being videoed at the gym while I am literally on the bench watching them. the social dynamics of asking 'hey can I record your sparring while I cannot myself spar' are uncomfortable.

3. mom thing. obvious. I lost a week to it physically and another week to it mentally. I do not have the spare cognitive change for a side project right now. and I want to use the recovered emotional bandwidth on Camila and the relationship we just talked about reviving therapy on, not on debugging Streamlit deployment quirks at 11pm.

4. honestly — and this is the one I'd rather not write — the multi-camera angle problem in month 9-10 broke me a little. I never solved video alignment between two phone cameras shooting a sparring round from different sides. every fix introduced drift. without hardware sync (which would cost $500+ and require fighters to wear sync triggers, which is dumb in a gym), I'd need a frame-level optical flow approach that works across angles, which is a research problem, not an engineering problem, and I am an engineer with a day job, not a researcher. I wasted three weekends on it. when I think about restarting the project a small part of my brain pre-emptively flinches. that flinch tells me I need a real break, not a half-break. I should respect the flinch.

5. Camila has been doing 100% of nube walks for two weeks. that's not fair. she lost her mother eleven months ago and her partner has been working on a side project on weekends instead of taking the dog to the park.

6. the side project is also, secretly, a kind of avoidance. it lets me say I have a 'personal project,' which is a thing senior engineers are supposed to have, while not having to risk a real one. I have been thinking lately — and I don't know if this is fair to me — that the pose-est project is a placeholder for something braver I don't yet want to commit to. like applying to MIT. like trying to publish a paper. like writing a real product. pose-est is a hobby that performs as ambition. and it is fine to have hobbies. but I should call it that. naming the thing reduces its grip.

7. (last one) I want to make a chess board for Hugo Sr's 70th. that's six months out. I want to learn woodworking. I cannot learn woodworking on top of the rust fire and the body recovery and the kickboxing form analyzer. so the analyzer goes on the shelf and the chess board comes off it. swap.

so: code is sitting on my private GitHub repo named 'kickform-mp,' README updated to say 'paused, not abandoned, see notes/2026-10-pause.md for full reasoning,' Streamlit demo URL killed because the AWS bill was annoying ($14/mo, but $14 is $14, and I refuse to pay AWS for a tab I'm not opening), MediaPipe model weights archived in S3 Glacier deep archive (literally cents per month). the data — 12 sparring sessions of MP4s and pose JSON — is on an external drive in the desk, labeled 'kickform_data_2025-2026' and backed up to a second drive in Camila's filing cabinet. pulled the ThinkPad's old ssh keys out of the deploy pipeline, revoked the AWS IAM user, archived the github project to my 'paused' folder.

I'll come back to it. or I won't and it'll become an MIT app talking point — 'I prototyped this thing and learned X' — which is also fine. side projects don't owe you completion. they owe you the lessons they give while they are alive. and if I'm honest, it has already given me the lessons. another six months of weekend tuning was not going to give me more lessons. it was going to give me marginal improvements to a tool that will only ever be used by twelve guys in Colonia Juárez. that is not the right ratio of effort to impact.

lessons it gave me, brief, for the record:

- MediaPipe Pose is shockingly capable on CPU. 30fps on a 2021 phone with no GPU. Google should write more about this stack than they do. it is genuinely better than the consumer narrative around it.
- Streamlit is a great prototype-and-throw-away tool. it is not a productionizable framework. do not be fooled by a clean prototype into thinking the production version is one refactor away. it is six refactors away. I almost made this mistake in month 6 and pulled back.
- video frame alignment without a hardware sync is hard. I underestimated this by, like, an order of magnitude. the correct prior is 'hardware sync or it doesn't work,' not 'I'll figure out the software fix.' lesson: when a problem domain has standard hardware solutions costing hundreds of dollars, the software-only solution does not exist for hobbyists.
- fighters do not want feedback in real time, they want feedback after the round, ideally with a chai. UX lesson: latency is not a feature here. don't optimize for it. I optimized for it. wasted weekends.
- and you cannot iterate on a body project while the body is broken.

the last lesson is the one I want to keep. write a project that depends on your own body being functional and the moment your body breaks — when, not if — the project is ungrounded. for the next side project, pick a domain where my participation is cognitive, not physical. that's a real constraint. that's what the chess board project gets right and what pose-est gets wrong. the chess board does not require me to throw a round kick.

one more thing. I want to leave a note for revival, just in case. if I revive this in six months or twelve months, the way to do it is: NOT to keep iterating on the multi-camera problem. the way is to make it single-camera-only, lean hard into the limitations, and ship a v1.0 that is honest about what it can and cannot see. one phone, fixed angle, 90 seconds of sparring footage, reasonable feedback on chamber height, hip rotation, weight transfer. no claims about what it can't see. no two-camera. no real-time. no hardware. no Streamlit production. write a simple Python CLI that takes a video file and outputs a JSON report. that's the v1.0 if I come back. that's the contract with future-me. ship something honest. don't try to ship something perfect. perfect was the trap that killed v0.x. ándale, kickform-mp, regresarás algún día. or you won't. either way. respect.

let me also write down what I am going to put in the kickform-mp/notes/2026-10-pause.md file because I want a public-facing version of these notes that lives inside the repo itself, separate from this private journal. the public version will say: paused October 2026 due to author injury and competing priority on day-job rust service. data and weights archived. revival path: single-camera v1.0 with a Python CLI. dependencies: MediaPipe Pose (pinned), opencv-python (pinned), numpy. test fixtures in tests/fixtures are stable, do not regenerate. avoid temptation to upgrade MediaPipe major versions on revival without re-recording fixtures. the fixtures were recorded at Atlas Box on August 9, 2026, with Pablo as the model. the fixtures are part of the project's ground truth and the project will not be the same project if you regenerate them with a different fighter or in a different gym. that is the entire revival doc. that is what will live in the README of the paused project. ándale. paused. dignified. respected. next.

running heaptrack on the staging instance to confirm theory 1. heaptrack is the best memory profiler I have ever used in any language, let alone rust — KDevelop's gift to humanity, free, accurate to the byte, beautiful flame graphs. I have been using heaptrack since 2020 when I worked on a C++ feature at Wizeline that had a similar leak shape, and I have brought it with me to every job since. the fact that it just works on rust binaries with the right flags is one of the small kindnesses of modern systems tooling.

dump excerpt: 1.42GB across 14.2M allocs leaked in alloc::alloc::alloc → Vec::clone → routeopt::analytics::send_event::{{closure}} → tokio::runtime::task::core::CoreStage. 76% of all leaks. so. theory 1 confirmed, brutally. the Clone is from the payload struct deriving Clone and getting cloned into the move closure. textbook 'I want to send analytics but also return the response' shape.

implementing the semaphore + timeout fix and shrinking the AnalyticsEvent struct from 45KB to 32 bytes. running again in 6h. fingers crossed.

I am also adding two unit tests to the analytics module: one that asserts the semaphore rejects when full, and one that asserts the timeout fires when the downstream is slow. I want both of these as regression tests in CI so that future PRs cannot accidentally remove the bounds. unit tests for concurrency primitives are annoying to write but they catch the kind of mistake that hides for six months until production. and I am writing comments on the new module that explain WHY the bounds are there — not just what they are. comments that explain why are the only comments worth having. comments that explain what the code does are worse than no comments because they go stale and lie.

Andrés is going to PR-review this and I want him to learn from it. that's also part of the win — turning a leak into a teaching moment for the junior on the team. he was the one who first asked 'why don't we use mpsc with a bounded channel?' which is also a valid solution and possibly cleaner. I am sticking with semaphore for now because the diff is smaller and I do not want to introduce a structural change in a hotfix branch. but the mpsc refactor is the v0.15 conversation and I will write up the trade-offs in the team wiki this week so Andrés can take that on as his next stretch task. that's how you grow a junior. that's how Daniela grew me. ándale.

for the postmortem write-up I want to remember the timeline cleanly. tuesday I noticed RSS climbing on the staging metric dashboard — slow, like 50MB an hour, the kind of slope you only see if you happen to glance at the graph during a 36h soak test. wednesday I confirmed it wasn't a one-off. thursday I instrumented with tokio-console and got nothing useful (tokio-console is great for tasks but bad for leaks of allocations not tied to live tasks). friday I switched to heaptrack and got the full picture in 90 minutes. so the lesson is: don't waste a day on tokio-console for a leak shape; go straight to heaptrack. I am putting that in the team runbook. the other lesson is the dashboard slope detection — Andrés set that alert up two months ago and I dismissed it as noisy at the time. it was not noisy. it caught a real regression a week before it would have hit production. note to self: trust Andrés's alerts more. note to self: thank Andrés for the alert in the postmortem. give him credit publicly. he is going to be a senior engineer in three years and the way you make that happen is by giving him the credit he earns when he earns it. ándale.

load test result after 6h: RSS plateau at 412MB, stays there. CPU normal. dropped analytics counter <0.5% (we drop on semaphore overflow, that's expected, that's the design). memory leak fixed. that was very satisfying. boring numbers staying boring. going to write a runbook entry tomorrow, also a public post-mortem for the rust gotchas wiki page so the next person on the team who spawns a task without bounds gets caught in the linter rule I am about to add. ándale. v0.14.3 ready to cut.

first PT appointment today. Clínica Querétaro on calle Querétaro, three blocks from the apartment, lucky. therapist is a guy named Iván Beltrán, late 30s, used to work with Pumas players, very chill. he has the build of a former amateur boxer himself — turns out he was a 65kg amateur in his early 20s, then tore his ACL, then PT school, then Pumas, then private practice. he speaks fighter. that is going to matter.

he did an assessment for an hour. diagnosis: lumbar facet sprain on the left, mild SI joint irritation, hip flexor tightness on the right (from kickboxing — duh). he laughed when I described the kick that did it. he has heard this exact story a hundred times. he said 'tu derecha está acostumbrada a generar fuerza, tu izquierda está acostumbrada a estabilizar' — that line stayed with me for the rest of the day. he palpated my QL and made me wince like a child. he watched me bend forward and immediately said 'no estás articulando, estás colapsando.' he watched me do a single-leg balance and laughed not unkindly when my left wobbled. 'no eres chido para el equilibrio, sebastián, es ok, lo vamos a trabajar.'

he said 'no kickboxing for two more weeks minimum, we re-evaluate.' I made a face. he laughed and said 'todos hacen esa cara.' giving me a routine of stretches and some mobility work. twice a week sessions for the next month. Tuesday and Friday at 11am. I scheduled them out for the next eight weeks already. paid up front for the first month — the receipt is on the fridge as a kind of contractual reminder to me that I cannot weasel out.

the clinic itself is small — three treatment rooms, a small open-plan rehab gym at the back with a TRX rig, two physio tables, a reformer pilates machine, a shelf of foam rollers and bands, and a corner with a mat for floor work. clean, well-lit, smells faintly of eucalyptus oil. Iván shares the place with a woman named Marisol who does pelvic floor specialty work, and they have a part-time receptionist who knew my name on arrival because Iván had typed it into his calendar with a small note: 'kickboxer, back, 33, ITESM grad' — Iván also went to ITESM, which is how the receptionist later mentioned it, ITESM solidarity is a small reliable network in this country. it makes me trust him another notch. small affinities matter when you are about to spend two months horizontal on a stranger's table being told to breathe.

ándale, Iván. te tengo confianza. the receipt is on the fridge. the calendar invites are on my phone. Camila has been told the times so she does not schedule anything over them. and I have already told Roberto I will be 30 minutes late on Tuesdays and Fridays for the next eight weeks because of medical appointments, framed as 'medical' in the calendar because I do not want him to know what specifically until I have to. it is nobody's business that the rust engineer is rehabbing his lumbar. ándale. semana uno arranca el martes. el primer ejercicio es no fingir que no me duele. el segundo es decirle a Iván cuando me duele. el tercero es escuchar lo que me responde.

ok logging the PT routine for myself because Iván emailed me a PDF and I'll lose it. printing it out and taping it to the bathroom mirror. doing this twice a day, morning and evening, plus the in-clinic sessions Tuesday and Friday at 11am.

MORNING ROUTINE (~15 min, before any work, before yerba mate, ideally before checking phone but realistically with the phone face-down on the floor):

1. cat-cow on hands and knees. 10 reps slow. focus on segmental movement, not just flopping the spine. Iván demonstrated this and made me do 4 reps in front of him; said I was 'collapsing the lumbar instead of articulating it.' I have a homework note: lead with the tailbone on the rounding part. lead with the chest on the arching part. don't let the head flop. breathe out on the rounding, in on the arching. if pain shows up at end-range, stop short of end-range, do half-range.

2. dead bug. supine, both arms straight up, knees stacked over hips at 90°. extend right arm + left leg, hold 2s, return to start. 8 reps each side. brace the abs the whole time as if you're about to be punched in the stomach (Iván used this exact analogy, knew it would land with me, smart guy). the lumbar should not arch off the floor. if it does, smaller leg extensions. visual check by sliding a hand under the lower back — there should be just enough room for fingertips and no more.

3. bird dog. quadruped position. extend opposite arm/leg, hold 5s. 8 reps each side. don't let the hip drop on the supporting side. don't twist. think 'long line from fingertip to toe.' this one is brutal for me — my left side is much weaker than the right, which Iván identified within 15 seconds of watching me. he said 'tu derecha está acostumbrada a generar fuerza, tu izquierda está acostumbrada a estabilizar, hay que invertir un poco esa lógica.' translation: my right is the kicking side, generates force, my left is the planting side, stabilizes, both sides need to know how to do both things.

4. glute bridge. supine, feet flat on the floor 6 inches from butt, knees bent. drive through heels, squeeze glutes. 12 reps, hold 3s at the top. add a pause at the top to make it harder. progress to single-leg in 2 weeks if pain allows. should feel the glutes burning before the hamstrings, before the lower back. if it's lower back first, position is wrong — feet too far forward usually.

5. side-lying clamshell. left side first (sore side). band around the knees just above the kneecaps. hips stacked. open the top knee like a clamshell, keep heels together. 15 reps each side. burn should be in the lateral glute, not the lower back. if the lower back is involved, smaller range. progress to longer hold (3s at top) in week 2.

6. couch stretch for hip flexors. one knee on a folded towel, that foot up against the wall behind, other foot planted in front in a lunge. 60s each side. this one hurts in a good way. for me the right side is way tighter — that's the kicking-side hip flexor on a kickboxer with a chronic right round kick. duh. should feel a deep stretch in the front of the hip and quad of the back leg, not in the lower back.

EVENING ROUTINE (~10 min, before bed, after teeth):

1. supine knee-to-chest. one knee at a time, gently pull toward chest with hands, hold 30s. then both knees together. lower back should feel a slow lengthening, not a stretch. 'no debe doler, debe respirar' Iván said. breathing slowly while held — five long breaths per knee.

2. supine pelvic tilt. focus on engaging the lower abs to flatten the lumbar against the floor. no holding, just rocking the pelvis. 10 reps slow. this one reminds me how disconnected I have been from my own pelvis for, possibly, years. Iván said 90% of his lower-back patients cannot do a controlled pelvic tilt on the first session. I was in the 90%.

3. piriformis stretch — figure four. supine, ankle of one leg crossed over opposite knee, pull the bottom leg toward chest. 60s each side. should feel deep in the back of the hip / butt cheek of the crossed-over side. if it feels like a knee stretch, position is wrong.

4. thoracic rotation, side-lying. 'open book.' lying on side, knees stacked and bent at 90°, arms straight in front. rotate the top arm up and over, opening the chest toward the ceiling, eyes follow the hand. 8 each side. this one I love, my mid-back has been locked for years and this feels like a gift. if I had to pick one stretch to keep doing for the rest of my life, it would be this one.

5. child's pose with side reach. on knees, butt back on heels, arms reached forward. then walk both hands to the right for a side stretch, then to the left. 30s left, 30s right. the side reach lengthens the QL — quadratus lumborum, the muscle group that has been a central villain in my back saga.

AVOID list (Iván made me read this back to him, twice, because he said most patients ignore it):
- deadlifts (any variant) for 6 weeks. no exceptions. yes, even Romanian. yes, even single-leg. 'todos los pacientes me preguntan si los rumanos cuentan, los rumanos cuentan, todo cuenta.'
- round kicks, side kicks, push kicks. anything with hip rotation under load. teep is borderline-ok in 2 weeks because it's straight-line.
- prolonged sitting >45 min without standing up. set a timer if I have to, and I will have to, because deep work makes me forget my own body for hours. there's a free Mac app called Stretchly that pings you. Iván recommended it. installed.
- Camila's 'fun' yoga class on Sundays — Iván said NO when I described what they do. it's a hot vinyasa class with a lot of forced backbends that the instructor calls 'opening.' he literally said 'eso no es yoga, eso es CrossFit con incienso.' I am framing that quote. Camila found it less funny than I did. she might be right.
- heavy backpack. Iván specifically called this out — my work backpack with the laptop is too heavy and I sling it on one shoulder, classic kickboxer asymmetry. he wants me using both straps and ideally getting a smaller bag. I am ordering a smaller bag.
- carrying nube. she's 18kg, I have been picking her up to put her on the kitchen counter when she's anxious. no more for a while. she's going to be sad. she'll have to be brave on the floor.

DO list:
- walk every day, 30 min minimum, no incline. flat ground. measured: from the apartment to Cardinal café and back is 28 minutes, perfect. or to the park and twice around the central path is 35.
- core work daily (the morning routine covers it).
- ice if it flares acutely. heat in the morning to loosen things up. I have an ice pack in the freezer and a heating pad on the bookshelf.
- swim if I can find a pool — 'a la perfección,' he said. I do not have a pool. there is one at the gym but it's a kid pool. I could try the IMSS pool at Parque España — it's open Tuesdays and Thursdays mornings to non-affiliated members for a small fee. will check.
- sleep on the side with a pillow between the knees. yes, like a teenager with a heartbreak. Camila found this funny and has helpfully named the pillow 'Rodolfo.' Rodolfo is between my knees right now. thanks, Rodolfo.

WEEK-BY-WEEK milestones (Iván sketched this on a paper towel and I photographed it):
- week 1-2: pain reduction, full ROM in flexion and extension, no kickboxing. complete the morning + evening routine without skipping. attend in-clinic Tuesday + Friday.
- week 3: light bag work allowed, no kicks. teeps allowed if pain-free. shadow boxing only, no impact.
- week 4: re-evaluate. if SI joint stable, low kicks allowed at 50%. round kicks held back further.
- week 5-6: progressive return to round kicks at 70% intensity. no sparring. return to padwork with Pablo at controlled pace.
- week 7-8: full return to sparring if everything stable. final clinic check-in, discharge if cleared.

this is two months out. that's brown belt + one and a half belts. I have to mentally plan around that. I'm going to use the time. it's tempting to feel sorry for myself but the truth is in ten years of training I have not had two months off, ever, and my body is asking for it. the smart fighter listens. Profe Esteban says it constantly: 'el cuerpo siempre tiene razón.' the body always has the reason. I'm taking it seriously. the alternative is being the guy who can't do round kicks in his 40s and that is not the guy I am willing to be.

Iván closed the first session by saying 'mira, sebastián, tienes mucho potencial de seguir kickboxeando hasta los 50 años, pero solo si las próximas seis semanas las haces como un monje.' six weeks of monk discipline. ok. monk it is. I will be the most boring kickboxer in Mexico City for six weeks. then I will be the kicking kickboxer again. ándale.

for the record — and to be reread by future me when I am tempted to skip a session — the cost of not doing this routine is: another tweak, another two months off, possibly an MRI, possibly a referral to a spine specialist, possibly disc damage. the cost of doing this routine is: 25 minutes a day for six weeks. those are not comparable costs. I will not skip. I will write it here so future me can be ashamed if he skips. ándale, monk-Sebastián. al ataque.

Wednesday — first benched class. went in, sat on the bench by the heavy bags, took notes, drank agua de jamaica from the cooler, watched Pablo do four rounds of pad work with Profe and feel things I have not felt before, namely envy and admiration in approximately equal parts. Pablo's left low kick has gotten heavy in the last month. like really heavy. I was watching the bag swing and thinking 'huh, when I get back, that's the thing I want.' Profe came over after class, sweaty, pointed at my back and said 'cabeza, mijo, cabeza.' meaning: smart head saved your back. I'll take it.

the gym at Wednesday 7pm is a particular ecosystem. Profe at the front in his old red shorts and the Tijuana FC towel around his neck. Pablo hitting pads on the left side of the room. three yellow belts in the back trying to learn how to put their hip into a round kick. one new student named Beto who is six-foot-four and built like an upright fridge and cannot figure out where his feet are supposed to be — Profe has been working with him for two weeks and you can see, just barely, the change in Beto's stance from last Wednesday. fragments of progress. that's how this gym works. nobody arrives elegant. everybody is, at some point in their first six months, a six-foot-four upright fridge.

I took two pages of notes. Pablo's hip rotation on the left low: he is staying connected to the floor longer than I do, which means more torque transfers from his planted leg through the kicking hip into the bag. he is doing what I keep failing to do: not jumping off the planted foot at the moment of impact. Profe corrects me on this every six months and I always backslide. when I come back from injury that is the first thing I am going to drill. plant foot stays planted. plant foot stays planted. I will write that on the inside cover of my training notebook.

I also noticed something I had not seen in five years of training: there is a small ritual the senior students do, all of them without coordinating, when class ends. they walk to Profe one at a time, bow slightly, say 'gracias profe' in low voices, and Profe nods, sometimes adds a small correction or a small encouragement, sometimes says nothing. you only see this if you sit on the bench. you do not see it if you are part of the line going through it because you are in your own moment. tonight I saw it from outside. there were eleven students, all eleven walked through the line. Pablo went second to last and Profe held his shoulder for an extra second and said something I could not hear. last in line was Beto, the upright fridge, who did not know about the ritual because he is two weeks in, and Profe gestured him forward and he came forward and bowed awkwardly and Profe said 'mejor cada día, mijo, mejor cada día.' Beto walked out three inches taller than he walked in. that is the gym.

I sat on the bench afterwards for ten more minutes. I did not want to go home yet. ándale, Atlas Box. seis semanas. te veo de regreso.

I also want to record what the gym smells like at 8:30pm on a Wednesday after eleven students have done two hours of pad work and bag work. it smells like sweat — but a particular sweat, not the gym-rat sweat of an LA Fitness, more the sweat of bodies that have been working in coordinated effort, which has a different note, and which is mixed with the particular smell of agua de jamaica and the eucalyptus liniment Profe sells in 200ml bottles at the front desk for 80 pesos. it smells like a place. it smells like my place. you cannot replicate that smell. you cannot get it in another gym. you can get other smells, in other gyms, that may also become 'your place' over time. but not this one. this one is gone the day I leave Atlas Box. and I am not leaving Atlas Box. that is one of the unstated reasons I will say no to Bogotá in two weeks even though I have not yet been formally offered Bogotá. I just know it is coming. I have a strong feeling it is coming. and I also know that the smell is one of the data points in the no. you cannot put a smell on a spreadsheet. but you can write down that you cannot put a smell on a spreadsheet, and that the smell is part of the decision, and that ten years of repetition produced the smell and ten years of repetition is what cannot be ported. ándale.

Camila just got home and dropped news. Foster + Partners' CDMX office is putting her up for the Pritzker Prize student-mentor recognition. I'm not even kidding — the firm itself is endorsing her as a candidate, which means it goes into a real shortlist process this year. she's been mentoring the architecture students from UNAM through some firm program for three years, she's been the lead on it for the last 18 months, and they want to formally recognize that work.

she didn't tell me right when she came in. she put her keys down. she took off her coat. she hugged nube. she opened the fridge. she stared at it. she closed it. she said 'tengo algo' in this small voice and then she said it. and I yelled. I yelled in the apartment, the cantina-noise floor, neighbor-pissing-off level yelled. I picked her up. picked her UP, with my busted back, do not tell Iván. I spun her once in the kitchen. she was laughing the way she laughs when she's actually surprised by joy, which is rare, because she's a contained person, my Camila. her eyes go round and her mouth opens and she does this little inhale where she sounds like she's trying not to laugh in a meeting. that face. that's the face I marry someday.

I know what this means in the architecture world. Pritzker is the top of the pile, the architecture Nobel. student-mentor recognition is a different track from the main prize but it carries enormous weight, especially at her stage — 30 years old, getting nominated by a firm of that calibre, that is the kind of thing that follows your name on every project page for the rest of your career. she's going to be 'Camila Ferreira, Pritzker student-mentor nominee' on every byline, every conference badge, every project credit. she earned every microgram of this. she has put more thought into how those UNAM kids learn than anyone I've ever watched do mentorship. she calls them by name, all of them, without checking. she remembers the project they were on six months ago. she sends them feedback at 11pm with apologies and bullet points and screenshots, the kind of feedback that takes 90 minutes to write and reads like 4. she knows their families. she knows the one whose mom is sick. she knows the one whose dad is in the army. she knows which of them is on scholarship and which is paying their own way, and the one who is paying her own way was the one Patricia Quintana would later, at the dinner, name first.

the firm is hyped. her boss in the CDMX office, Patricia Quintana, has been sitting on this for two months while the formal process happened, apparently. when it finally landed Patricia called Camila into her office and said 'tengo algo que decirte que tiene que ser confidencial por una semana.' and Camila spent that week not telling me. she is incredibly disciplined about firm confidentiality. I am routinely amazed by the things she keeps inside her own head for clients. she has known about projects worth tens of millions of dollars before they were public, and I have never gotten a hint of any of it from her, ever, and she has not had to actively dodge — she has simply held the knowledge as a separate compartment in her brain. it is one of the things I admire most about her. anyway. the embargo lifted today. she came home. she put down her keys. and now we know.

the weirdest part — and this is the part I want to remember — is what she said next, after the spinning, after the laughing. she sat down on the kitchen floor (we keep doing kitchen floor moments lately, what is that, why do all our big news moments happen with us seated on a tile that needs grouting), she pulled nube to her, she said 'mi mamá hubiera estado tan orgullosa' and she cried. and I sat with her on the kitchen floor and we cried for Beatriz, who would have called every relative in São Paulo and Buenos Aires and probably some in Lisbon, who would have made sure the whole continent knew, who would have framed the email, who would have ordered a cake from the bakery on rua Augusta that she ordered every important cake from, who would have been the loudest person at any celebration. and Camila gets to celebrate this without her mother. and that is the cost of the year we've had. that is the bill. you do not get to compartmentalize joy from grief. they ride together. they always ride together. nobody has ever been completely happy without simultaneously remembering who they would have wanted to share it with. that is a feature of being human. that is a feature of having loved someone and lost them.

I'm so proud of her I could break. she is — and I mean this — the most quietly brilliant person I have ever lived with. she is also the most quietly disciplined. she is also the most quietly funny. she sits with her morning coffee at the kitchen table and reads the news in three languages — Folha de São Paulo, El País, the Guardian — and underlines things in pencil and puts them in a notebook and three weeks later one of those underlines becomes a turn of phrase in a presentation she gives to a partner from London who calls her 'incisive' and she comes home and tells me the partner used the word 'incisive' and we both know it is because she reads three newspapers and writes things down. that is how she works. that is how she became Pritzker-nominee Camila Ferreira at 30. nobody is going to read that email and know about the underlining. but I know. and I get to live with her. ándale.

last thing. when we finally got off the kitchen floor she said 'sebas, prometeme algo.' I said sí. she said 'no me dejes hacer lo que mi mamá me hubiera dicho que no haga.' which is, roughly: don't let me become the kind of person my mother would have warned me against. she meant: don't let me become a Pritzker-nominee asshole. don't let me lose the thing that made me good at this. don't let success make me different. I told her I promised. I will. that is not a hard promise to keep with Camila. she has been the same person for five years. she will be the same person at 60. but she wanted me to say it out loud, and I said it, and now it's said. there. promise registered. tomorrow we go to her favorite vinyl shop, by the way, because I owe her a celebration and Discos Tlamatini on Tabasco is two blocks from us and I have been meaning to go for a month.

for the record — because future me is going to want to remember this — when Camila said 'no me dejes hacer lo que mi mamá me hubiera dicho que no haga,' she was wearing a navy linen shirt with a small coffee stain on the cuff that she had been wearing for two days, no makeup, hair in a clip, glasses still on her head from the metro, and she was sitting on the kitchen floor on the tile that has been there since the apartment was built in 1948 with a bowl of cherries from the mercado at her side that she had bought yesterday and forgotten about. that is what the second-most-important sentence she has ever said to me looked like. the first-most-important is private and I am not going to write it down. but the second-most was in our kitchen with a clip in her hair and a forgotten bowl of cherries. ándale. I love this woman. I am not letting her become anyone different. promise registered. delivered. signed. countersigned. dated.

let me also note for future me: when news like this lands in your apartment, the most important thing is not the celebration. the celebration is easy. the most important thing is to remember to celebrate the partner's career as if it were your own — because in a real partnership, it is. her win is mine. mine is hers. nobody owes anybody this math but a marriage works because both people decide, freely, to do this math. tonight Camila's win is the household win. the apartment is happy. nube knows something is happening because we have been weeping and laughing in roughly equal measure for two hours and dogs read the room. she has been a furry barometer all evening. ándale, Camila Ferreira, Pritzker-nominee. you are going to be insufferable for about a week and I am going to enjoy every second of it.

Camila's firm sent flowers to the apartment — a gigantic arrangement, white roses and lilies and something green and architectural that I do not know the name of, with a card from Patricia Quintana that says 'congratulations, you earned this, don't be modest about it for at least 24 hours.' her colleague Sebas (the other Sebas, hilarious, we call him Sebas-2) brought champagne to the office. she sent me a photo of her holding a Veuve Clicquot bottle in the office kitchen looking embarrassed and pleased. that face is my favorite face.

ok so to celebrate Camila's Pritzker thing we went to my favorite vinyl shop in Roma — Discos Tlamatini, on Tabasco, two blocks from the apartment — and I told her she could pick anything for herself and I'd buy us one for the apartment and one for me, my treat, my month, my back is benched and I have to spend money on something that isn't ramen. she picked Stan Getz/Astrud Gilberto for the apartment, the obvious choice, the one we've been meaning to own forever, the one we've played from Spotify so many nights cooking dinner that we could probably perform the second side a capella. I'd been low-key hoping she'd pick it but I let her arrive there alone. she did. for me — and this is where the story gets good — I found 'Beny Moré con la Banda Gigante.' the original RCA Victor Mexico pressing. orange label. 1958. mono. I have been hunting this record for two years.

I need to explain why this matters because it doesn't translate well outside of Latin jazz nerds and I want it to translate. Beny Moré is the most important Cuban singer of the 20th century, full stop. 'Bárbaro del Ritmo,' nobody before or since has had that voice, that phrasing, that ability to turn a son montuno into a small orchestra crisis and resolve it on the next downbeat. la Banda Gigante was the band he formed in 1953 in Cuba and ran for the rest of his short life — he died at 43, alcoholism, in 1963 — and the recordings they made together are among the highest-quality dance band sides ever pressed in the Caribbean. RCA Victor Mexico did the Mexican licensing for these because Beny had a fan base here as big as the one he had in Havana. the orange-label pressings are a specific run from late 1957 through about 1959. they are what we call 'nadar contra la corriente' rare in CDMX — the Cuban pressings are easier to find here for embargo reasons (long story). the orange-label Mexican one is the one for fans because it has slightly different track sequencing and includes a track called 'Castellano que Bueno Bailas Tú' that is missing from later compilations. I have known about this pressing since I was 25. I have been looking for one in playable condition since I was 31.

the shop owner — Don Tlama, real name Tlamatini Cruz, weird beautiful man with a gray ponytail and a gold tooth and a Pioneer SX-1980 that is worth more than my car — saw me pull it from the rack and made a noise. 'eh, mira, mira, ya por fin lo encontraste.' he knew I'd been looking. he had been holding it back for me without telling me, he confessed, since an estate sale of an old señor in Coyoacán three months ago. the estate had 600 records and Don Tlama had been at the auction at 7am with a coffee and a flashlight and bought 80 of them, and this one he had pulled aside for me when he saw it, and he had not told me because — his words — 'quería verte pasar por aquí buscando otra cosa, que es la única manera real de encontrar un disco así, sin querer.' he wanted me to find it accidentally. that is the way of vinyl. I almost cried right there in the bin.

the cover is in incredible condition for its age, slight ringwear, the sleeve is the original with the Cuban national bandera in the corner and the RCA dog logo in the upper right, the inner sleeve has someone's handwriting in fountain pen — 'para Lalo, el día que conocimos a Beny en El Patio, agosto 1958.' someone gave this record to someone in 1958 with that note. Lalo got to meet Beny. someone gifted Lalo a record commemorating the night they met him. Lalo's record is now mine. I am now responsible for what happened in El Patio in agosto 1958. cuídate, Lalo, wherever you are. I bet El Patio was hot that night. I bet the band played for three hours. I bet Beny took a break around midnight and stood in the lobby smoking a cigarette and somebody named Lalo went up to him and said something nervous, and Beny said something gracious, and Lalo went back to his date and bought her another drink and could not stop talking about it on the bus home, and the next day his friend who couldn't go bought him this record as a teasing gift. that is one possible Lalo. there are others. either way, Lalo is dead now, and his record is mine, and I am 33 in 2026 and I will play this record on Saturdays for the next forty years if I am lucky.

Don Tlama charged me 1800 pesos which is, frankly, too cheap. the going rate for an orange-label Beny Moré in this condition on Discogs is closer to 4500 MXN. I argued with him to take more and he said 'no mames, te he visto rebuscando dos años, te lo doy a precio justo, no te aproveches.' he refused. then he made me a coffee from his stovetop pot in the back of the shop, the one he has had since 1991, the one he says has only ever made coffee, never water, never tea, only coffee, and the patina on the inside is something a chemist could write a paper on. we drank coffee. Camila joined us — she had been browsing the Brazilian section — and Don Tlama immediately asked her about a Tom Jobim Spanish-language pressing from 1965 that he had been hunting for four years. she had heard of it but not seen one. they exchanged what I can only describe as grail-list intel for ten minutes. she has won him over, Tlama is now her ally too, this is excellent for our future hunting.

we listened to the first track in the shop, on his beautiful old Pioneer turntable, 'Bonito y Sabroso,' that horn line, that Beny voice — there's a moment about 47 seconds in where Beny does this little laugh-into-the-vocal that I have heard a thousand times on the digital remaster but on this orange-label original on a tube system in a Roma vinyl shop on a Saturday afternoon next to the woman I am going to marry someday I felt something happen in my chest that was not pulmonary. it was the opposite of the lump scare from two weeks ago. it was relief turned all the way up. it was the sound of being alive in a city that has always known how to be alive on Saturdays. it was the sound of a 1958 horn section rendered through a stylus in 2026 and being heard by a man who has just learned his mother is fine. all those time signatures stacking on top of each other. I stood very still in the shop. Camila watched me. Don Tlama watched me watching the turntable. nobody said anything for the length of one Beny Moré song. that is the highest compliment a vinyl shop can pay a customer. they let you finish the track.

Camila bought a Caetano Veloso record for herself, 'Transa,' she's been wanting it. we walked back through Plaza Río de Janeiro with two LPs each and stopped at the cart for esquites with extra chile. nube was waiting at the door of the apartment doing her full body shake of welcome. record number 151 in the collection. and the best one. and I will play it for my dad next time he's in CDMX. and I will play it for nube. and I will play it on the day my MIT app gets accepted, or rejected, either way. some music is for both. some music is bigger than the result. that's why you keep it on vinyl. that's why you let Don Tlama save it for you for three months without telling you. that's why.

one thing more. when we got home, I cleaned the record before playing it — Pro-Ject anti-static brush, then a 1:9 distilled water and isopropyl wipe with a lint-free pad, then dry brush, then back into a fresh poly-lined inner sleeve I had on the shelf for exactly this kind of moment. ritual matters. I put it on the turntable. nube watched me from the rug. Camila watched me from the kitchen with a glass of wine. I dropped the needle. the room filled. nube tilted her head the way she does when she hears a sound she's never heard before. that's the moment. that's the apartment. that's the woman. that's the dog. that's the record. that's the year. that's everything I am trying to protect against the lump and the sparring injury and Roberto's politics and the rust leak and the kid timing and the imaginary future. that. that exact moment. that is what I am defending. ándale, Beny. te tardaste pero llegaste.

let me also note for the catalog (I keep a notebook of every vinyl I own with provenance and condition and where I bought it, started it in 2019, am at 151 entries now): catalog #151, Beny Moré con la Banda Gigante, RCA Victor Mexico orange-label, 1958, mono, condition near-mint sleeve VG+ vinyl, purchased 1800 MXN at Discos Tlamatini Roma Norte CDMX from Don Tlamatini Cruz on a Saturday in October 2026, the afternoon Camila Ferreira's Pritzker student-mentor nomination became internal news at Foster + Partners CDMX. provenance: estate sale Coyoacán 3 months prior, original owner unknown, gifted in August 1958 to a person named Lalo per ink inscription on inner sleeve. I will write all of this in pencil tonight in the catalog notebook. the pencil is a Faber-Castell 9000 HB which is the only pencil I trust for the catalog. future me will be glad I did this. future me always is. ándale.
