wrote up the runbook entry for the analytics task leak. 'POST-MORTEM: analytics fire-and-forget memory regression in RouteOpt rust v0.14.2.' four sections: detection, root cause, fix, prevention. published to Confluence. linked from the team wiki under 'rust gotchas.' Andrés read it within an hour and asked great questions about why we don't use mpsc with a bounded channel instead of semaphore + spawn. honestly? semaphore was just simpler. mpsc would also work. that's a refactor for v0.15 if we want.

the post-mortem template I'm using is one I started building back at Wizeline in 2018 and have refined every job since. five sections: timeline (timestamps, what was observed, what was done), evidence (graphs, heaptrack snippets, log lines, anything reproducible), root cause analysis (the actual mechanism, not 'we shipped a bug'), remediation (what we changed in code, in process, in metrics), and prevention (what would prevent this class of bug in the future). I think the prevention section is the most important. teams that write good prevention sections end up with codebases that are quietly hardened against entire classes of bug.

for this one the prevention section reads: any tokio::task::spawn whose handle is not awaited must (a) be acquired from a bounded semaphore, (b) include a tokio::time::timeout, (c) carry a payload that is intentionally minimal — extracted fields, not the full upstream type. these three rules will be added to the team's rust playbook and to the PR review checklist. I will also propose a clippy lint that warns on bare task::spawn without a corresponding semaphore acquire — not sure if that's feasible but worth investigating with Andrés as a Friday afternoon project. it would make the rule self-enforcing. self-enforcing rules are the only rules that survive in a growing team.

the post-mortem also includes a small graph that I am proud of: prometheus screenshot of the RSS gauge over the 6h soak before-and-after, with a horizontal annotation line at the 412MB plateau and a vertical annotation line at the deploy time. you can see the slope go from positive to flat. the visual is the kind of thing a junior engineer shows in their interview portfolio for the next ten years. ándale. it is also going on the wall behind my desk.

Roberto read the post-mortem and replied 'thanks for the detail. let's discuss in 1:1 if there are process changes we need at the team level.' that is Roberto-speak for 'I want to put my fingerprints on this somehow before it ships as a team artifact.' I am going to let him put his fingerprints on the process section. I do not care. the fingerprints will not change the substance and they will give him a sense of ownership which will reduce his anxiety in the next sprint. that is good for me. that is the politics of writing post-mortems with a manager who needs to be visible. let him be visible on this one. I will stay invisible on the technical content, which is where the value actually is. Daniela also taught me this — let your manager take the process credit, you take the technical credit, both of you walk out of the meeting happy. it is a renewable resource. ándale.

I want to add one structural reflection here for future-me. this is the third post-mortem I have led at Brújula in 18 months and the second this quarter. each of these costs me about half a day to write properly, and another half a day to socialize and get the team's input on. it adds up to about a week per quarter of pure-write time. that is non-trivial. but I have noticed that the teams who skip post-mortems on small leaks (because they're 'small') are the same teams that ship the catastrophic leak six months later. small post-mortems are the immune system of a codebase. they are also the ladder a junior engineer climbs to senior — Andrés will write his first post-mortem in the next two months and I am going to make sure he gets the experience of writing it under the same template I am using. that is part of the unspoken succession plan in this team. ándale.

Camila's Pritzker thing changes the kid-timing math in a weird way I haven't said out loud. she's getting nominated at 30. if she has the kid in 2 years, she's 32, which is fine biologically but means the year after the nomination is the year she's pregnant in. and pregnancy doesn't mix with the post-nomination touring/talking circuit she'll be on. so either we move kid timing closer (less likely with the year we just had) or we move it further out and let the Pritzker year breathe. I haven't said this. she hasn't said it. we're both thinking it. I want to bring it up at therapy when we go back. not before.

the math is, more specifically: the Pritzker mentor recognition formal selection happens over the next 6-9 months. shortlist around month 3, decision around month 8. during that period there are interviews, panels, articles, possibly a small ceremony in Tokyo or wherever the host city is. that is, structurally, a year of high-visibility career time for her. you do not want to be 14 weeks pregnant on the day Forbes interviews you about your mentorship philosophy. you want to be 14 weeks pregnant on a year that has lower-visibility career events.

I am also noticing — and this is the harder thing to write down — that we have been, for two years, treating 'kid timing' as a thing where I am the brake and she is the accelerator. and that frame is starting to feel wrong. the Pritzker thing actually tilts the math in a direction where neither of us would benefit from acceleration right now. she has reasons of her own to want to wait. those reasons might be more important than mine. and that means the brake-and-accelerator frame, which I have used in private to explain my own slowness to myself, is actually a way of ducking the harder conversation, which is: what are we both actually choosing for, separately and together. Dr. Muñoz will love this. Dr. Muñoz is going to take one look at this and smile and say 'cuéntame más,' the way she does, and we will be there for an hour. and that's good. that's why we go back. ándale.

I also want to write down a thing about Camila and her own ambition that I have been noticing. for five years she has talked about her career almost entirely in third person — 'el equipo está haciendo,' 'la firma está intentando,' 'el proyecto avanzó.' she rarely says 'I.' she has a deeply Brazilian-Argentine humility about it that sometimes I worry crosses into self-erasure. but in the last 48 hours, since the Pritzker news, she has, twice, said something in first person about her own work. yesterday at dinner she said 'pensé en una manera distinta de hacerlo y funcionó.' tonight she said 'soy buena con los chamacos, eso lo sé.' those are the first two first-person ambition sentences I have heard from her in five years. I am going to remember the dates. October 2026, week 2 after the nomination. those sentences are the seedlings of a different Camila. I want her to keep saying 'I.' I want her to claim her own work in her own grammar. I will tell Dr. Muñoz this too. Dr. Muñoz will love it more than the kid-timing math, frankly.

one more thing I want to put on paper. I have been worried — quietly, since about month 6 of our relationship — that Camila's pattern of erasing the 'I' was something she would carry into a co-parenting decision, and that I would end up making decisions for both of us by default because she would defer to me and call it love. I have not wanted that. I do not want to be the husband who decides because his wife is too kind to. and so the seedlings of the 'I' from this week are not just career data. they are also relationship data. they are evidence that the woman I am going to raise children with is becoming, slowly, more capable of saying 'I want.' that matters. that is, possibly, the single most important development in our relationship this year, and it is hidden inside a Pritzker week and easy to miss. I want future me to remember that the Pritzker news's deepest gift was not the recognition. it was the first-person grammar. ándale.

ok so. the in-laws photo thing. context: I've been thinking about the chess board project I want to start for Camila's dad's 70th. it isn't until month 17, that's six months out, but I want to start planning. and tonight while I was scrolling through the family WhatsApp — the one with my parents and Mateo — I came across a photo my mother sent during the lump week, when I was in Guadalajara, that I hadn't really looked at properly. it's a photo of my dad at the chess club, sitting at the table with Don Heriberto. a wide shot, taken by some friend at the club, candid. dad is leaning over the board with his glasses on the end of his nose, the same wire-rim glasses he's had since 2018, the ones with the tiny scratch on the left lens that he refuses to fix because 'apenas se nota,' and there's a notebook beside him — and I just zoomed in, in the dim light of the apartment, and the notebook has a hand-drawn chess board in it. squares numbered. arrows. annotations in dad's tiny tilted handwriting. he is preparing openings the way he prepared physics problems for his students for 35 years. of course he is. why did I never know this.

the drawing has the level of patience and precision that I associate with my dad in the best possible way. you can see he ruled the lines with a ruler — straight, parallel, clean — then drew the squares freehand with a fineliner, alternating dark/light by shading with hatching not solid fill, which is somehow more elegant. the hatching is at a consistent 45° angle across all the dark squares. the pieces aren't drawn — he writes the algebraic notation in each square where a piece should sit. e2-e4 e7-e5, etc., in a tree structure of alternative replies, with arrows showing the move. there's a note in the corner: 'Heriberto siempre juega Caro-Kann. preparar B-c4 línea principal y la trampa de Karpov 1972.' so he is preparing for a specific opponent. he has a notebook where he prepares for Don Heriberto. it's a study notebook. for a 66-year-old man at a chess club in Guadalajara playing for honor against retired men named Don Heriberto.

I sat with this for a while tonight. I texted mom: '¿papá tiene un cuaderno de ajedrez?' she replied immediately: 'mijo desde antes de que nacieras. siempre. cuántos cuadernos llevará, cinco, seis. los guarda en la repisa con los libros de física.' since before I was born. five or six notebooks. on the shelf with the physics books. I have been in that house for 33 years, I have walked past that shelf maybe a hundred thousand times, and I never knew. nobody told me. nobody hid it from me. it was just my dad's quiet thing, the thing he did that didn't need an audience.

and here is the thing — I am going to make Camila's father a chess board for his 70th. and now I want, in some small way, to honor my own dad in that build, because it's chess and it's wood and it's him too. nothing visible. nothing weird. but maybe the joinery has something I learned from him. or I'll burn a tiny date on the underside, or I'll hide a peso coin from his birth year — 1960 — under the felt of one of the pieces. some private quiet honoring. he doesn't have to know. the universe will know. or, more honestly, I will know, and that's enough.

also: I want to start sketching the board. like, with a pencil. like dad does. patient and freehand and a little crooked. cedar planks for the body, walnut for the dark squares. magnetic chess pieces with 3D-printed bases — that part I'm stealing from a Reddit post I saw last year. dimensions: 20-inch playing surface, 2-inch border, beeswax finish. drawer at the bottom for the pieces. I'm going to drive to Maderería El Roble in Toluca to pick the cedar in a few weeks — it's the place a woodworker friend of Pablo's recommended, said the foreman lets you sight down the planks before you commit. and I'm going to take a picture of dad's chess sketch and tape it inside my workshop notebook so I look at it every time I sit down to plan. there. that's the project. that's the why.

let me describe the photograph more carefully because I want to remember it. dad is at the corner table by the window — the one with the wobbly leg they've all stopped caring about, that they keep level with a folded piece of cardboard from a pack of cigarettes in the leg slot. he is wearing the gray long-sleeve shirt with the small embroidered ITESM logo on the chest pocket, the one I gave him in 2014 when I graduated, the one he has worn so many times the embroidery is slightly faded but still legible. he is holding a black knight in his right hand. his left hand is on the notebook, finger on a line of notation. he is leaning slightly forward, the way he leans when he is in a position he is not sure of, and his glasses have slid down his nose. Don Heriberto across from him is out of focus but you can tell he is smiling slightly because Don Heriberto knows dad well enough to know what that lean means. there is a little espresso cup on dad's side, empty. it is afternoon light, the windows behind them have the half-shutters drawn, sun coming in at an angle that catches the cigar smoke from another table — there is always one cigar smoker at the chess club, and tonight it appears to be a man in a cream guayabera at the next table — and the smoke is making a curl in the upper left of the frame. it is one of the most beautiful candid photos of my father I have ever seen. and my mother sent it to me in a WhatsApp at 4:30pm on a Thursday during a week when she was waiting for a CT scan. she sent it to me because she likes the photo. she did not annotate it. she did not say 'mira a tu padre.' she just sent it. that is also her language.

one more thing. dad is 66. his angina has been quiet this past year, statin compliance is good, walking 30 min a day, BP under 130. Hugo Ferreira sr — Camila's dad — is 70 next year, healthy, retired civil engineer, plays chess himself and mentioned a few months ago that he and dad have spoken once on a video call, awkward, in a mix of three languages, but they each independently told their respective children that the other man 'parecía un buen hombre.' it would be a small thing to host them both at our table sometime. let them play a game. with my chess board. that's the dream of the whole project, if I'm honest. a board where Hugo and dad sit across from each other, and dad uses one of his notebook openings, and Hugo says something in a mix of Portuguese and Spanish, and Camila and I cook in the kitchen and listen. that's the project. nothing has to happen for the project to be real. but if it happens — if those two old men sit down across from each other on a Sunday afternoon in CDMX or São Paulo or Guadalajara — I will have made the right thing.

I want to write down also that I just realized, sitting here, that the reason I want to make this chess board is partly because I cannot make my own father young again. I cannot give him back his 40s. I cannot un-bypass his sternum from 2022. I cannot un-die Mama Lupita, and I cannot un-widow Hugo Ferreira sr from his Beatriz, and I cannot make my mother's neck never have had a lump in it. but I can make a small wooden object with my hands, something that will sit on a table for the next thirty years, that says: I noticed. I noticed all of it. I noticed who you are. I noticed what you needed. I am acting on it. that is what woodworking is for. that is what it has always been for. ándale. cedar. walnut. beeswax. drawer at the bottom. coin from 1960 underneath. let me sketch it tonight.

addendum, sketched at 11pm: did the first sketch on graph paper. board is 20-inch playing surface, 2.5-inch border, drawer 2 inches deep underneath, total external footprint 25x25x4 inches. cedar will be 0.75 inch thick for the squares, glued to a 0.5 inch plywood substrate for stability — solid wood across that span will warp, the plywood prevents it. dark squares will be walnut, 0.25 inch veneer overlaid on the cedar, end-grain joinery at the corners. drawer slides on hand-cut runners — Pablo's friend says do not buy industrial slides for a chess board, the silence of hand-cut runners is part of the experience. ok. hand-cut runners. I will learn. I will probably fail and re-cut. that is also part of the experience. I taped the sketch to the wall above my desk where the pose-est project plan used to be. one project ends, another begins. the wall is the same. the project changes. ándale.

two weeks since the lipoma news and I'm still riding the relief. it's strange — I expected to bounce back to baseline anxiety in a few days, but instead my baseline has shifted slightly downward. I worry less. I sleep slightly better. I am genuinely happier. it's almost embarrassing how much energy my brain was using to be afraid of the lump being something else. that fear had a footprint. now there's space. I'm using some of the space on PT, some on rust debugging, some on Camila's Pritzker thing, some on remembering my dad has a chess study notebook. it's not bad.

the more interesting thing is that I am also less reactive at work. when Roberto sent me a 'gentle reminder' about the standup format yesterday I did not feel my chest tighten. I felt a small distant amusement, the kind you feel when you watch a child have a tantrum at a birthday party — you remember being that child, you are not that child anymore, you can wait it out. I think the lump-week did this to me. spending a week with my mother in a paper gown reset my calibration on what is actually scary. Roberto's emails are not scary. an angry Slack thread is not scary. a missed sprint is not scary. these are work events, in a work context, with people who go home at night and worry about their own mothers in their own paper gowns. I am, briefly, a person who has access to that perspective. I want to preserve it.

I also notice — and I want to write this down so future me notices — that I have been more affectionate with Camila this week. more present. more interested in what her day actually contained. I think the lump week opened a small window of attention and the Pritzker news kept it open. I should not need a near-miss with my mother's life to be this attentive a partner. but if a near-miss is what gives me access to a different register, I will use the access. I will try to remember the register when the access fades. that is the plan. that is the only plan. ándale.

and one more thing for the record: I think the new baseline I am noticing is partly a function of being benched from kickboxing. when you cannot hit a bag for an hour to discharge the day, you have to find other places to put what is in your body. some of what I have been putting into kickboxing for ten years was anger. I knew that abstractly. I did not know it in my body until I had two weeks of not being able to throw a kick. now I know it. and I am putting that energy elsewhere — into the chess board sketches, into talking to Camila for longer, into reading Chiang stories at 11pm with nube on my chest. these are not bad places to put energy. these are, arguably, better places to put energy than a heavy bag. and yet. and yet. I miss the heavy bag. I will go back to the heavy bag. but I will go back changed.

I also want to record a small private observation about what relief tastes like. it tastes like nothing. it has no taste. that is the surprise. I expected relief to feel like joy or pleasure or some specific positive emotion. it does not. it is the absence of a thing that was there. it is the room being quieter than it was. it is the dish being lighter than it was when you picked it up. it has no flavor of its own. I think this is why people who have been sick for years and then get well report that they have to learn to feel joy again — because relief does not automatically convert to joy. relief just removes the bad thing. you have to do the work of replacing it with a positive thing. I am, slowly, starting to do that work. the chess board sketches. the longer conversations with Camila. the Chiang stories. the slow PT routine. these are positive things I am putting into the room that mom's tumor was occupying for two weeks. ándale. so this is what relief looks like. it looks like a slow refurnishing.

Andrés found another small leak — Redis client creating a new connection on each retry instead of reusing the pool. fixed in 30 lines, single-PR cleanup. he wrote the PR, I reviewed, merged. boy is good, he caught it from reading the heaptrack output on his own initiative without me asking, that is the ground-truth signal that he is going to be a senior engineer in three years. proud manager moment, weirdly. Daniela would have told me to mark it on his perf doc. I am marking it on his perf doc. ándale, Andrés.

second week of PT. Iván says the SI joint is calmer, facet still inflamed. he did some manual mobilization on me today, the kind where you lie on your side and they crank your hip and something internal pops, and afterward I felt 30% better. not magic — he laughed when I said magic, said 'no, biomecánica.' going to keep at it. the boring exercises are doing more than I'd like to admit.

the dead bugs and bird dogs are starting to feel different. in week 1 they felt like punishment — small, embarrassing, slow movements that I had to do in a private corner of the apartment because I was too proud to do them in front of nube. in week 2 they feel like a small daily craft. I am paying attention to the order in which my muscles fire. I am noticing that on week 1 my left glute did not fire at all on a single-leg bridge — the hamstring took the entire load — and now in week 2 the glute fires first, as it should. one tiny, slow motor pattern reorganizing itself in two weeks. that is the whole game. that is, in miniature, what kickboxing is. I have just been doing it on the floor instead of on the bag.

Iván is also a chatty guy during the manual work. today he told me about a bantamweight boxer he treated three years ago who came back from a herniated disc and went on to have his best year. story was meant to encourage me. it did. he also told me he has treated three former Pumas players in the last month and that the way they describe pain is structurally different from how civilians describe pain — civilians say 'it hurts,' athletes say 'it limits.' that's the lens shift I want to keep. I will train myself to describe pain by what it limits, not by how it feels. that is the smart fighter's vocabulary.

after the manual work he had me do a lateral lunge with a band around my hips, which is the new addition to the routine for week 2. it forces the lateral glute to fire while the supporting leg stabilizes, which is the exact pattern that fails in my round kick when the planted-leg glute disengages. four sets of eight on each side. I am garbage at it. my left side wobbles like a drunk man on a metro platform. Iván laughs. I laugh. we will do it twice a week for the next six weeks. by week 8 I should be able to do it without the wobble. that is the bet. that is the contract. ándale, Iván. I am taking notes.

I want to also document the rehab routine in full so future me can copy-paste it for the next injury (and there will be a next injury). morning, 15 minutes: 90/90 hip stretch each side 60 seconds, dead bug 3x10 with 4-second negatives, bird dog 3x10 alternating, glute bridge 3x12, supine spinal rotation each side 8 reps slow. evening, 20 minutes: foam roller upper traps and lats 90 seconds each, hip flexor stretch lunge 60 seconds each, side plank 3x30 seconds, banded lateral lunge 3x8, wall slides for thoracic mobility 3x12. weekly, twice: PT visit. weekly, daily: 30 min walk in Parque México for general circulation, no kicking, no plyo, no rotation loaded. that is the program. it is unromantic. it is going to bring me back to training in 4-6 weeks if I follow it. ándale.

Camila's nomination got a write-up in Architectural Record online. small piece, a paragraph and a photo from the office, headline reads 'Foster + Partners CDMX backs Ferreira for Pritzker mentor recognition.' she sent it to me from the metro, no comment, just the link. she's bashful about it. I am not bashful about it. I'm telling everyone — Pablo, my mom, my brother, Profe, Doña Marta upstairs, the guy at the corner tienda. I am insufferable. I am proud. let me be insufferable for one week. ándale.

FaceTime with mom tonight. she's fine. wearing her lipoma like a small triumph — she keeps touching it absently and going 'no es nada, no es nada.' she also told me she's signed up for a 6-week aquafit class at the IMSS pool. retired nurses doing aquafit. she said three women in her class also had benign neck lumps in the last year. lipoma club. I love her. I told her dad's chess sketches had blown my mind and she said 'mijo, lleva 40 años haciendo eso, ¿cómo no lo sabías?' I have no answer.

she walked me around the kitchen with the phone, showed me the new Tupperware organization scheme she invented this week — color-coded by lid type, blue lids for rice, red lids for soup, yellow for everything else, mom-engineering at its purest. she is happier than she has been in months. relief is doing what relief does, which is to make a 62-year-old woman reorganize her Tupperware. she also told me, in passing, that doña Concha next door is finally talking to her again — they had a small feud about a delivery man six weeks ago that nobody can quite remember the cause of, but doña Concha showed up at the door yesterday with a tray of cookies and a shoulder-shrug that meant 'paz' and mom accepted the cookies and the peace and they drank coffee in the kitchen for an hour. these are the things that happen when a 62-year-old woman gets her CT scan back clean. she repairs the small civic things. she makes peace. she organizes her Tupperware.

she also asked me about Camila — about the Pritzker, about how Camila was. I told her Camila was fine but quiet, that she was holding the win the way she holds wins, which is small and inward. mom said 'esa muchacha. tan trabajadora. tan callada. dile que estoy orgullosa.' I told Camila later. Camila cried small tears for ten seconds and then said 'qué bonita es tu mamá, sebas.' she did not say it about her own mother. she did not need to.

side detail. mom got very quiet when I told her about the chess board project for Camila's dad. she said 'qué bonito mijo, qué bonito.' she meant it. she also meant something else — she is wistful about her own father, my abuelo Joaquín, who died at 58 of a stroke and never met me. she has never said this. but I have come to recognize the silence she makes when she has been reminded of his absence. tonight she made the silence and then said 'qué bonito.' I heard both layers. ándale, mamá. te oigo en los dos planos.

the call ended with mom asking me, in passing, if I had been doing my back exercises. I lied for a quarter-second and said 'sí mamá, todos los días' before catching myself and clarifying that I had skipped the morning ones twice this week and was going to do better. she said 'mijo, una madre se da cuenta cuando le mientes, hasta cuando le mientes en chiquito' and then she laughed her nurse laugh, the one that ends in a small cough. I love that laugh. I have heard it at every important moment of my life. ándale, mamá. te quiero. domingo a las 11 como prometí.

additional note from the call. mom mentioned that the IMSS pool aquafit teacher is a woman named Carmen who used to work the night shift on her ward in 1995. Carmen recognized mom and they spent fifteen minutes catching up before class. mom said 'mijo, en este país nunca se acaban los chismes, todos sabemos de todos.' she said it with affection. that is the nurse-network of mom's generation — it spans every hospital, every shift, every retirement age, every aquafit class. I have grown up inside this network without ever being a node in it. it is one of the things about Mexico I will only ever be able to observe and appreciate from the outside, because I did not become a nurse, because I do not have 35 years of shift work in IMSS to draw on. ándale. there is more than one Mexico. there are dozens. mom lives in one of them. I live in another. they overlap on Sunday afternoons. that is enough.

Roberto in 1:1 today asked me to 'tighten up' my standup updates. I asked what he meant. he said 'longer is better — show your work.' I said the standup is supposed to be three bullets, that's the format we agreed. he said 'that was Daniela's format.' that line. that line was not subtle. he's going to make me write essays at 9:30am every day. I had to physically stop my face from doing a thing. anyway, will give him three bullets and a sentence of context per bullet. that's the compromise. that's what we'll do until I run out of patience.

the meta-pattern with Roberto is that he doesn't trust the team because he doesn't know the team yet, and his solution is to make the team produce more legible artifacts so he can audit them. that is not how senior engineers should be managed. senior engineers should be trusted by default and audited by exception. but I get it — from his side he just inherited a team of strangers, and he wants to know what is happening. so I will give him what he needs in the cheapest possible form. three bullets and a sentence of context. takes me three minutes a day. costs nothing. produces enough surface for him to relax. that's the deal I will make with this particular phase of management. it is not a sustainable deal forever. but it is the right deal for the next 90 days.

I will also start setting up an asynchronous weekly written update that contains the full context — decisions, trade-offs, blockers, dates. that way Roberto has something to read every Friday and does not feel a need to chase me during the week. front-load the legibility. it's a Daniela trick — she taught me this when I was reporting to her and Daniela's manager was anxious. you build a weekly artifact and then you point at it whenever someone asks 'what's going on with X.' the artifact does the explaining. you keep working. it's also good for me — it forces me to articulate what I think is important each week, which is a useful exercise in its own right.

the format I am going to use, more specifically: a Notion page titled 'Sebastián — weekly,' refreshed every Friday at 5pm, with five sections — shipped this week, in-flight, blockers, decisions taken, decisions pending. each section has bullets, each bullet has a date, each bullet has a one-liner. takes me 30 minutes to write. Roberto can read it in 5. it covers everything he could possibly want to ask in a 1:1. and it is a public artifact, so he cannot pretend to forget what was discussed. that is also a Daniela trick. write down everything in a format that is non-deniable. ándale. Roberto, I'll see you Friday. and the Notion page will see you every Friday after that.

I want to also note something I learned from this Roberto-week that I think is general. when a new manager arrives and changes the operating rhythm, the senior engineers on the team have a choice — push back on every change or pick the changes to push back on. younger me would have pushed back on every change. I would have argued the standup format on principle. I would have made it about Daniela. I would have lost the argument anyway and become known as the 'difficult senior.' current me is going to give him the standup format he wants, give him the weekly Notion page he doesn't yet know he wants, and save my pushback chips for the things that actually matter — the rust ship, the headcount, the on-call rotation, the hiring loop. those are the chips I will spend. I will not spend chips on standup. ándale. el ajedrez del trabajo. small pieces traded, big pieces kept.

thinking about kickboxing and bodies and time. brown belt at 33, three months ago. ten years of training behind me. the test for brown belt was the hardest physical thing I've done — six belt grades in one day, you have to fight every grade up to your level, including a 3-minute round with Profe at the end. I survived. I drove home with my hands shaking on the wheel and slept fourteen hours that night. now I'm here, lying on a foam roller in the apartment, banned from kicking, doing dead bugs, watching a YouTube video about hip mobility while nube sleeps on my left shoulder. and the truth I cannot escape is: every athlete I've ever respected has had a season like this one. injuries are not a deviation from the path of training, they are the path. they are a permanent part of the curriculum. the problem with injuries is that the curriculum doesn't tell you which lesson they are. you have to figure that out from the inside.

Profe Esteban has had two ACL surgeries, a broken nose three times, a torn rotator cuff that he never had repaired and that just lives in his right shoulder forever. he is 61 and still moves like a man who knows where his feet are. he learned that, in part, by being injured. by being the man on the bench. by watching. by deciding he was a fighter even on the days he could not throw a kick. he told me once — I think after the brown belt test, late, sitting on the mats wiping his forehead with a Tijuana FC towel — 'mira, mijo, el día que pares, paras. el cuerpo te dice. y entonces le pones atención al cuerpo. el cuerpo siempre tiene razón.' the body always has the reason. I'm trying to listen. I have not always listened. that is part of why I am where I am right now.

the tricky part of listening is that the body says different things in different registers. there's the loud register — the day of the injury, the screaming muscle, the icepack and the ibuprofen and the 'no kicks for two weeks.' that one is easy. you listen because you have no choice. then there's the quiet register — the way you stand at the bus stop, the side you put your bag on, the foot you push off when you climb stairs. the quiet register has been telling me things for ten years that I have ignored. my right hip flexor is locked because I throw round kicks with my right side and I never stretch the right side enough. my left QL is overactive because I compensate. my upper traps are tight because I clench when I work and never breathe. all of these are things the loud register couldn't get me to fix. it took an injury to get me into a PT clinic where Iván could palpate my back and read me out loud. that's the cost of ignoring the quiet register: the body eventually has to scream to get heard.

two more weeks of PT before I can re-evaluate kicking. probably four before I'm sparring again. probably six before I'm in the rotation for serious rounds. I'm 33. I have, optimistically, ten more years of competitive intensity left in me, before kickboxing turns into 'work the bag, teach a class, demonstrate the technique without throwing the kick.' I want every one of those ten years. I am not going to spend them by being stupid in week one of an injury. I have already been stupid in week one of every previous minor injury and that is part of how I got here.

black belt is the next milestone. probably 6-8 months out if everything goes well. that's the test where Profe hands you the belt personally on the mat. I have been imagining that moment for ten years. mom and dad in the audience. Pablo recording on his phone. Camila in the back not understanding what is happening but understanding that it matters. Profe walking up with the black belt in both hands — that hand-folding way he does, four turns and a tuck — and presenting it like a sword. I will be there. but I will be there because I did the dead bugs, not because I rushed the dead bugs.

the deeper thing — and this is the thing I want to write down — is that I think kickboxing is the thing in my life that has taught me the most about how to be a senior engineer, how to be a partner, how to be a son. not because of the violence or the discipline, although both of those help. because of the time. you cannot get to brown belt in three years. you cannot get to brown belt in five. it took me ten. it will take me eleven or twelve to get to black. and once you've internalized that — that the thing you want is on the other side of a decade of consistency — every other domain in your life starts to look the same way. the rust migration is a 12-month effort. the chess board is a 6-month effort. the relationship with Camila is a 5-year effort and counting. mom's relationship with me is a 33-year effort. every important thing in my life has time as its medium. kickboxing taught me that. injuries are part of how it teaches you. ándale.

there is a related thing I want to write down because it is connected. I have noticed in the last month that I am much less reactive at work. when Roberto says something dismissive, I no longer feel my chest tighten. when a junior on another team pushes back on my code review, I no longer feel defensive. I think this is a kickboxing thing too. the gym has trained me, slowly, to not flinch. to absorb a strike and continue moving. to keep my hands up and my chin tucked and my breath steady when somebody is trying to land a left hook on me. and these are physical lessons that have come back as social ones. when Roberto says 'that was Daniela's format,' my old self would have flushed and stammered. my current self goes 'mhm, ok,' and writes the three bullets with a sentence of context per bullet. the gym taught me to do that. the gym is teaching me to be 33 instead of 25. injuries are part of the gym. so even the injuries are teaching me. ándale.

side note before I go to sleep on this foam roller: I want to re-watch the video Pablo took of my brown belt test. I have not watched it. I have been afraid that I'd see something cringy. but I think now is the time. I think I'd see it differently. I'll watch it tomorrow. I'll let you know what I see.

last thing. there is a small private moment I want to record from the brown belt test that nobody knows about and that I have not told anyone. between the fifth fight and the test round with Profe, I had a 90-second water break. I sat on the bench. I drank water. I closed my eyes. and I said, in my head, 'Mama Lupita, ayúdame.' I asked my dead grandmother for help in a sweat-stinking gym in Colonia Juárez before fighting my coach. I am a lapsed Catholic and I do not pray to dead people in any normal week. but on that bench, on that day, with my hands shaking and my legs feeling like they had betrayed me, I asked. and then I went out and fought Profe and I did not win the round but I survived it and at the end he handed me the brown belt. that was August. that was four months ago. I want to write down that I asked. I want future me to know I asked. ándale, abuela. te llevo conmigo. I bring you with me into the ring. always.

addendum, written 20 minutes later because I cannot sleep: there is one more thing about the brown belt day that I want to record because it has been in the back of my head for four months. when Profe handed me the belt, he leaned in and said something very quietly into my ear that I could not quite hear over the gym noise but I think was 'tu abuela estaría orgullosa.' I never asked Profe how he knew about Mama Lupita. I have never told Profe about her. but he might have heard it from Pablo, who knows. or he might have just said something generic about a grandmother, which is also possible — Mexican coaches often invoke grandmothers at moments like that, it is part of the cultural architecture. I will probably never ask. asking would unwrite the moment. some moments are better left ambiguous. I prefer to believe Profe somehow knew, somehow saw, somehow did the right thing on instinct. that is my Profe. that is the man who is going to hand me a black belt in 6-8 months if I do the dead bugs. and I will do the dead bugs. ándale. ya, a dormir.

Doña Marta gave me a loaf this morning. she does this maybe twice a year, always unannounced, always while I'm leaving for work. 'pan de muerto, todavía hay,' she said, and shoved it at me. it's a Tuesday. there is no calendar reason, Día de Muertos was last week, this is leftover. she just does it sometimes. she also slipped in a thumbnail-sized piece of marzipan from her own kitchen, which is the kind of extra she does for tenants she likes. blessed woman. tomorrow morning I will leave her a vinyl I have a duplicate of — Pedro Infante, the cheap stuff she likes. trade.

Camila's dad — Hugo — flew in from São Paulo on Friday for a long weekend. he comes monthly since Beatriz died, but this trip he flew in specifically to celebrate the Pritzker nom. brought a bottle of Brazilian wine, some terrible Argentinian medialunas from the deli at his airport (he is Brazilian by location only, his loyalty to Argentinian baked goods is a gentle joke). hugged me twice. said 'Beatriz nos está viendo, nos está viendo.' I think he might have meant his daughter. I think he might have meant my mom. either way.

Hugo is 69, will be 70 next year, which is the chess board project. retired civil engineer, tall, slow, quiet, the opposite of his late wife who was loud and warm and ran every room she walked into. since Beatriz died Hugo has gotten quieter. it is not depression — Camila has been watchful — it is something more like a lower volume setting. he speaks in shorter sentences than he used to. he listens longer. he has started reading more, particularly history, particularly the history of Brazilian infrastructure projects of the 1960s and 70s, which was his professional era. he brought a book this trip — 'Brasília: a construção da capital,' which I have heard him talk about for two years — and read 30 pages on Saturday morning at the kitchen table in the slow careful way of a 69-year-old engineer reading a book about engineering by younger engineers.

he and I had coffee together on Saturday afternoon while Camila went to the office to meet a partner. just us. he asked me about RouteOpt. I gave him the simple version. he asked thoughtful follow-ups about service zones and routing — civil engineering and logistics overlap more than people realize, he had run a couple of bus-route studies for the city of São Paulo in the 90s. we talked about that for an hour. then he said 'Sebastián, sos un buen muchacho. cuidame a Camila.' I said yes. that's all. the conversation ended. I have been chosen by the man whose chess board I am about to make. it is, structurally, the closest thing my generation has to a blessing. ándale, suegro. te voy a hacer un tablero.

side detail I want to record about Hugo Sr. since I'm going to be making him a chess board and want every observation I can get. he holds his coffee cup with both hands. always. since Beatriz died — Camila says he didn't used to. he uses an old Olivetti fountain pen for crosswords; the same pen for thirty years apparently, refilled weekly. he hums when he reads, very quietly, the way some old men do when they think nobody is listening — Saturday morning at the kitchen table I caught him humming what I'm pretty sure was 'Aquarela do Brasil,' Ary Barroso, the version Beatriz used to play on Sunday mornings according to Camila. he didn't notice he was doing it. Camila noticed and her eyes filled but she said nothing. he wears the same sweater Beatriz knit him in 1987 — navy with a small geometric pattern on the cuffs — every trip. I am going to incorporate, somehow, an Ary Barroso reference into the chess board. maybe a small inlaid initial 'AB' on the underside. nobody but me will know what it means. Hugo will not need to know either. but it will be there. that's the kind of small care I can put into a piece of wood. ándale.

ok the Pritzker nomination dinner. Foster + Partners CDMX threw a small thing at the office on the rooftop terrace, view of the Reforma towers all lit up, the Estela de Luz blinking in the middle distance. maybe forty people — colleagues, two visiting partners from London, the mentees from UNAM, one of the founding partners on a video call from the London office because of a time-zone thing. Camila in a black jumpsuit, bare shoulders, that gold pendant her mother gave her at 16, hair loose. I wore my one good shirt — the navy linen Camila bought me for my 32nd birthday, the one I have spilled red wine on twice and lemon juice on three times, somehow still navy. nube was at home with a chew toy and the trazodone the vet prescribed in case of fireworks (it is October, fireworks happen, she is mortally afraid of them, that's a whole separate issue we are dealing with).

the principal architect at the CDMX office — Patricia Quintana, formidable woman, runs the place like a cathedral, has a gallery of architecture awards in her office that she calls 'la pared del ego' to her face but defends fiercely if anyone else jokes about it — gave a short toast that was so well-constructed it didn't sound rehearsed and absolutely was. she walked through Camila's contribution to the mentorship program over three years, named six of the UNAM students by name and described one project of each that 'showed Camila's fingerprints, in the best way.' she said 'Camila does not teach you to design like Foster + Partners. Camila teaches you to design like yourself, and she clarifies the difference.' that line. that line. I almost dropped my wine glass when she said it. that is exactly what Camila does. that is exactly what I have watched her do for three years. I had not known anyone else had seen it.

one of the UNAM kids — a girl named Luciana, project on rainwater capture for low-income housing in Iztapalapa, real one, scholarship student, lives with her grandmother and two cousins in Tláhuac, takes the metro 90 minutes each way to studio and back — made an unannounced toast back. she stood up with her glass and said 'arquitecta Camila, le quiero decir que cuando quería renunciar al programa, me dijo dos cosas: que mi proyecto importaba aún si nadie lo construía nunca, y que renunciar no era una traición a mis padres sino a mí misma. me quedé. estoy aquí. gracias.' Camila — who is composed, who is the most composed person I know in any room — wept. real tears, not movie tears, real ones, the kind that come from somewhere the person didn't know they had. she had to hand the wine glass to Patricia because her hand was shaking. I wept. I weep at engineering all-hands when someone announces they're leaving and I weep at architecture toasts apparently. Hugo wept with the unselfconsciousness of an old Brazilian man who has nothing left to prove. the visiting London partners were a little stunned, in the British way — one of them, a tall woman named Frances something, kept dabbing her eye and pretending it was the wind, although we were on a rooftop with no wind. it was a moment. a real one. those are rare in any context but particularly rare in corporate ones, where the architecture of the event itself usually prevents real moments. Patricia had built a small space — twenty minutes, no PowerPoint, an unscripted toast invitation — and the moment found its way in.

after the speeches there was a thing where the mentees each put a small pebble — the firm did this, it's a thing they apparently do for these — into a bowl on the table, and Patricia said 'each of these is a project we'll watch.' very Foster + Partners, slightly precious, but also kind of beautiful. each pebble was river-smooth, dark gray, sourced from a quarry in Hidalgo apparently (Patricia mentioned this; she would). Camila was given the bowl to keep. it's now on our kitchen counter. nube tried to eat one of the pebbles when we got home, we caught her in time, she got a stern talking to and then a treat to soften the blow.

the mentees came up to Camila one by one afterwards. Luciana cried again and Camila held her by the shoulders and said something I couldn't hear. another kid, named Esteban, told her about a competition he was entering and showed her sketches on his phone — Camila was already squinting at them and giving him feedback before her wine was finished. that's the kind of mentor she is. there's no off button. there's no 'this is my night' button. she is incapable of receiving an honor without immediately spending the moment of attention on someone else. it is not a strategy. it is who she is. it is one of the four or five things about her that I sometimes look at and think 'I do not know how to be that.' I am working on it. I will work on it for the next forty years.

late, on the cab ride home through the empty Reforma, the city blurry through the window, Camila — half asleep on my shoulder — said 'tengo miedo' and I said 'de qué' and she said 'de que la vida sea tan buena que se voltee' and I held her and we drove in silence the rest of the way through Reforma into Roma. that's the thing about the year we've had. we've learned that joy and grief don't take turns. they come at the same time. you have to carry both. and tonight she carried both. and so did I. and Hugo, in the back seat asleep with his head against the window like an old man on a long bus, was carrying three — joy for his daughter, grief for his wife, fatigue for the long Sunday ahead — and his face in sleep was the face of a man who had survived something terrible and arrived at something good. that's what 70 looks like. that's the threshold he is approaching. I want to make him a chess board worthy of it. I want our kitchen counter to keep its bowl of pebbles. I want our living room to fill, slowly, over the next decade, with these accumulated objects of attention paid: pebbles, vinyl records, cedar boards, a black-belt patch eventually. that's the apartment I want to grow old in. with this woman. and this dog. that's the project. that's the actual long-term project, the one all the others are subordinate to.

one thing I want to record because it might disappear if I don't. when Patricia finished her toast and Camila stood up to respond, Camila did not respond by talking about herself. she did not list her achievements. she did not thank the firm. she stood there for a moment, took a breath, looked at the UNAM students sitting at the front table, and said 'lo que tengan ustedes que decir esta noche, lo escucho. lo escucho yo y lo escucha mi mamá donde sea que esté.' she opened the floor. she gave the moment to them. then she sat down. that is when Luciana stood up. that is when the toast happened that made everyone cry. Camila engineered that. she did not say to herself 'I am the recipient of this honor and I should be at the center of this moment.' she said to herself 'the people I have been mentoring are at the center of this moment, and I should yield to them.' and she did. and the moment happened. and afterwards in the cab, when she said 'tengo miedo de que la vida sea tan buena que se voltee,' she meant: I am afraid of being a person who deserves this. she meant: I am afraid of believing I have earned the joy. she has earned the joy. she will not believe it for a while. that's also part of who she is. ándale. amen.

let me also note for the record what we did when we got home. nube had thrown up a small pile of trazodone-orange foam on the rug, which we cleaned up while still in our jumpsuit-and-linen-shirt outfits because nube does not care about your Pritzker-nominee status, nube cares about your ability to clean a rug at 11:30pm. Hugo took the guest room. Camila and I sat on the kitchen floor — again the kitchen floor, the locus of all our important moments — and ate cold leftover pasta directly from the tupperware with two forks. neither of us had eaten dinner. she leaned her head on my shoulder. nube curled at our feet. the bowl of pebbles sat on the counter above us. that was the end of the day. that was 11:47pm on a Friday in October 2026. I will remember the bowl of pebbles. I will remember the cold pasta. I will remember Camila's hair smelling of rooftop wind. I will remember nube's snoring. ándale. esto fue. esto pasó.

month 11 wrap. mom's lump benign. back PT working. memory leak fixed in rust. Camila's career hit a ceiling-shaking moment. pose-est paused gracefully. Camila's dad in town for a weekend. me lighter than I have been in months. I'm going to take a quiet weekend. listen to Beny Moré. read Chiang. drink mate. do dead bugs. that's the whole agenda.

if I look at this month from the outside it is one of the most consequential months of my life — both a near-disaster averted and a real piece of forward progress on almost every axis I care about. and yet what I want to do this weekend is read a Ted Chiang short story collection slowly with mate at hand and not think about any of it. that is also a sign of growth, I think. there was a younger version of me that would have used a weekend like this to plan the next thing — the MIT app, the chess board purchase trip to Toluca, the rust v0.15 design doc. and there is some part of me that still wants to plan all of those. but the wiser part of me — the part that did the dead bugs every morning for two weeks straight — knows that the right move after a high-amplitude month is rest, not more amplitude. so I am going to rest. I am going to drink mate. I am going to listen to records. I am going to take nube to the park twice a day. I am going to make Camila pancakes on Saturday morning the way she likes them, with banana inside the batter, with a little vanilla. that is the entire agenda. it is sufficient. it is more than sufficient. ándale.

let me also write down — because months tend to blur if you don't pin them — the specific shape of the things this month gave me. mom's CT scan came back benign, which is the dominant fact of the month, and which I have not yet fully metabolized. I think it will keep arriving in waves over the next year. some Tuesday in February I will be at my desk and I will think 'mom is alive' as a separate fact, freshly, again. back PT — week 4 of 6 — is unambiguously working; I can sit through a workday now without the right-side spasm I had been hiding for two months. memory leak in the rust v0.14 candidate is fixed; the actual root cause was a tokio JoinHandle leak in the retry layer, which I should have caught in v0.13 and which I am writing this down to remember to put into the post-mortem doc. Camila's Pritzker nomination — that one I will think about for the rest of my life. Hugo Sr.'s visit was the gentlest weekend of the year. pose-est paused gracefully — Itzel and Mariana have moved to NeRF stuff and we three are still going to grab dinner monthly even without the Discord, which matters more to me than I expected. the side project closing without rancor is the kind of thing I will quietly be proud of in five years.

I want to write one more thing about month 11 because it pertains to the kind of year I have had overall. this is the third month in a row where I have ended the month with a higher proportion of 'I rested' than 'I crammed.' that is a step-change in the way I do months. younger me would have closed month 11 with a sprint into month 12. current me is going to close month 11 by reading Chiang on Sunday afternoon with mate. that's the difference. ándale. me lo gané. nube too. she also rested. she is currently asleep on my left foot.

Roberto pulled me into a one-on-one this afternoon and pitched me on a transfer. Brújula opened a Bogotá office last quarter — they want a tech lead for the infra team there. someone who already knows our stack, has shipped a rust service, can stand up the team from scratch. they want me. eighteen months in Bogotá, full relocation, 25% salary bump, equity refresh, quarterly flights back to CDMX, the whole thing. they'd ship me and Camila and nube. January start. report directly to the CTO. I told Roberto I'd think about it. he said 'two weeks.' I went to the bathroom on the third floor afterwards and stood at the mirror and said 'no manches' out loud. I am writing this from the apartment. Camila is in São Paulo with her dad this weekend. nube is on my chest. apartment too quiet. have to call Camila. don't know what I want her to say. don't know what I want me to say.

let me also note the meta-context. Bogotá is, structurally, where Brújula is going to build out its Andean-region presence over the next 24 months — the Bogotá office is supposed to anchor Colombia, Peru, Ecuador for the logistics product. that's a real growth bet. tech lead there is not a sideways move. it is a step up. I would manage an infra team of 4-8, which I have never done — I have lead-engineered teams informally, but I have not had hiring authority or perf-review authority. that is the thing I would actually be learning over the 18 months. it is, on paper, the right next step for someone on a track to senior staff in five years. I am the right age for the offer. I am the right tenure for the offer. the offer is well-formed. and yet. ándale. una cosa a la vez. una cosa a la vez. mañana a Camila.

let me also note what Roberto specifically said, in his exact phrasing, because I don't trust myself to remember the wording in two days. he said 'Sebastián, te tengo una propuesta importante. el CTO me preguntó por nombres para Bogotá y dije el tuyo. es tu nombre el que está en la mesa.' he said 'la mesa' as in 'the table' — like the decision is happening at a table I'm not at. classic Roberto. he then said 'pero tú tienes la última palabra.' which is true, formally, and also slightly threatening in the way it was said — like, 'you have the last word, but the rest of the table has already used theirs.' I noted the framing without flinching. he then walked me through the package on his laptop. it was actually a solid package. he wasn't lowballing me. the equity refresh number was real — I haven't done the math but it's probably 15-20k USD over the four-year vest. I left the room and went to get water from the kitchenette and when I came back to my desk I had three Slack DMs from Camila about her flight to São Paulo and one from mom about a leftover-tamale recipe. life kept going. that's the thing about big offers. they happen on a Tuesday afternoon. and then your mom asks about tamales and you have to answer that too. ándale.

honest sub-thought: when Roberto said 'Bogotá' my first feeling was a flash of validation. ego is a real input. I noticed it before doing anything else with it. I have been a senior at Brújula for 2.5 years without a title move. tech lead would be the rung above. that is real. ego flash was about the rung. I am not going to take Bogotá to satisfy the rung. but I am writing it down so future me knows the rung was a real input I subordinated. ándale, jefe. te respondo en una semana.

told Camila tonight when she got back from São Paulo. she dropped her bag at the door, kissed nube, kissed me, sat on the couch. I told her the whole pitch — Bogotá, 18 months, 25% bump, full reloc, January start, report to CTO, headcount budget, the works. she put her book down before I'd even started the second sentence. she did not say anything for what felt like a minute after I finished. then she said 'sebas, qué te late.' which is 'what's your gut.' she didn't say her gut. she asked mine.

I told her my gut, in this order, slowly, because I was figuring it out as I said it:

1. it would be a real career step — first international leadership role, ground-up team building, exactly the kind of resume line that opens senior staff doors at any company in five years. that's not nothing. that's the kind of opportunity that doesn't come twice when you're a senior engineer in Mexico without a US passport.

2. 25% bump is not nothing. our rent went up 18% three months ago. savings would balloon. another 35-40k USD/year. that's not nothing either. that's a down payment in three years. that's MIT tuition possibly.

3. Roberto wants me out of his hair, I think a small portion of this is political. he probably wants someone less senior in the CDMX role for a few years, someone he can manage more directly. that part smells. but it's not necessarily a no — political offers can also be real opportunities, the political layer doesn't disqualify the substance underneath.

4. she just got Pritzker-nominated. her firm is here. her career is here. her dad flies in here. asking her to relocate now is asking her to put her career in storage for 18 months while mine accelerates. F+P has a Bogotá office but the Pritzker mentor program is here. Patricia is here. the UNAM kids are here. the bowl of pebbles on the kitchen counter is here.

5. nube would hate the move. she is anxious already. cargo flight, new climate, new sounds, new park system. it would set her back a year on the thunder thing.

6. mom is in Guadalajara. CDMX-Guadalajara is a 1.5h flight, 50 minutes if you're lucky. CDMX-Bogotá is 5+. if mom calls about another lump, I want to be 90 minutes away, not eight hours.

7. dad has angina. he's stable but he's 66. same logic.

8. Profe Esteban is irreplaceable. there are great gyms in Bogotá but my coach is in Colonia Juárez. and I have a black belt test in 6-8 months. I need him.

9. PT with Iván. mid-rehab. moving cities mid-rehab is a setback I do not want.

10. but. but. when do these offers come. it's hard to say no to the first international leadership offer of your life when you're 33 and ambitious and have just survived a year that included a manager change, a promotion, a near-miss with my mother's life, a side project closure, and a girlfriend's career-defining nomination. I have been telling myself for years that I'd take a leadership role abroad if it came. it has come. the form of it is wrong. the timing is wrong. but the offer is real.

she listened. she did not interrupt. when I was done she said something I am going to remember forever. she said 'puedo seguirte si lo decides. tengo opciones en Bogotá, F+P tiene oficina ahí, podría hacer una rotación. no quiero pero podría.' she did not flinch. she was offering to come with me. she meant it. and that — the fact that she meant it without hesitation, while also saying 'no quiero,' which she did not need to add but added because she is incapable of being dishonest in her own kitchen — was actually the moment I knew. because if she'd hesitated I'd have been able to lean on the hesitation. I'd have been able to say 'see, even she isn't sure, this isn't right.' her not hesitating put it back on me. it's mine to decide. she's not going to be the one who killed it. she's not going to martyr her career to give me an out. she is too generous for that and too honest for that. she would come. she would come without complaint. she would come and make it work and she would lose 18 months of momentum on the Pritzker year and she would do it and she would not blame me. but I would know what it cost her. and that would change us.

I told her I needed a few days. I'd build a spreadsheet. I'd talk to Daniela — my old manager Daniela Otero, the one who left for the Series A startup back in month 8, the one who knows me and knows Brújula's politics. I'd talk to Pablo. I'd talk to her dad — actually no, I won't talk to her dad about it, that's not fair to him. I'd talk to my mom. I'd talk to therapy if we could get a slot before the deadline. she said 'lo que necesites.' she meant that too. then she put her glasses back on, which she had taken off when I started talking, and went back to her book and nube curled tighter against her side and I went and made tea and stared out at the rooftops and at the planters Doña Marta tends on her balcony two floors up, full of geraniums she's had for thirty years, and I thought: this is the apartment. this is the city. this is the rooftop. this is the woman. this is the dog. this is the man two floors up's geraniums. these are the coordinates of my life right now and they are good coordinates. you don't trade good coordinates for a career line. or you do but you have to be honest about what you're trading.

I am 80% sure already. I am writing it here so future me can hold me to it. I am 80% sure I'm going to say no. but I'm going to do the spreadsheet and the call to Daniela first, because saying no without the diligence would be cowardly, and saying no after the diligence would be informed. I owe Roberto an informed answer. I owe me an informed answer. I owe Camila an informed answer. but my gut already knows. and my gut tonight is sitting on the couch with this woman and this dog and the geraniums two floors up are catching the late light and I am not leaving.

one more thing for the record. when Camila said 'no quiero pero podría,' it was the first time in five years I have heard her admit, openly, that she has a strong preference about her own life. she has always — always — submerged her preferences into mine. when we moved in together it was 'where do you want to live' and I said Roma Norte and she said 'me parece bien.' when we got nube it was 'do you want a dog' and I said yes and she said 'me parece bien.' when I started kickboxing she said 'me parece bien.' I have rarely heard her say 'I want X' as a primary preference. she is so accommodating it has been, over the years, a worry of mine — am I getting what I want at the expense of what she wants? and tonight she said 'no quiero.' two words. and they meant: I have a preference, sebastián. my preference is to stay in CDMX. I am not going to make this decision for you, but I am going to tell you my preference, and I am going to trust you with it. that is the first time. I am writing it here so that future me does not forget it. she said 'no quiero' and I heard it. and I think — I think — that's the end of the discussion, even though I will go through the spreadsheet motions for two more days for ego's sake. she said 'no quiero' and so we are not going. ándale, mi amor. respeté tu voz.

addendum, after Camila went to bed: I went out to the balcony with nube on a leash. she did her thing on the corner of the planter box (Camila will yell about this in the morning, but the planter is mostly dirt and a sad spider plant, nube has chosen it as her territory and we have lost that battle). I stood at the railing with my hands on the iron and looked across at the rooftops. there is a particular quality of light in Roma Norte at 11pm in October — the streetlights are old sodium-yellow, not the LED ones the city has been replacing them with elsewhere, so the light is warm and a little dim and it makes the cantera-stone facades look like something from a 1955 movie. you cannot get that light in Bogotá. you cannot get that light in Houston. you cannot get it in Buenos Aires or in Medellín. it is here. it is specific to this five-block radius. and I love it. and I love the fact that I have learned to love it. two and a half years ago I was a kid from Tijuana who had just moved to CDMX for a job and thought of this neighborhood as 'the one with the cafés.' now it is 'the one with the corner where Doña Marta's daughter sells flowers on Sundays' and 'the one with the tiles in the entryway of our building that Camila photographed for a project' and 'the one where nube has a friend, the gray cat from across the street, that she stalks every morning.' specificity is what love is. love does not happen at the level of the city. it happens at the level of the corner. it happens at the level of the tile. you cannot port love. you can only re-grow it elsewhere from a smaller starting point. I am not done growing this one. I do not want to start over.

I went back inside and Camila was already asleep, book open on her chest, glasses still on. I took the glasses off and put them on the nightstand and put the book on the floor. I lay down next to her. nube curled at the foot of the bed. and I lay there for ten or fifteen minutes thinking about the offer one more time and the answer was the same as it had been at 8pm on the couch. it is no. it has been no since the moment Roberto said the word 'Bogotá.' the next 48 hours are formality. ándale.

ok pasting the spreadsheet. I built it tonight in my notebook in pencil before I made it digital because I wanted the slow act of writing each line by hand. then I typed it up. here it is, full version. I am going to leave this here and stare at it for two days before I decide.

BOGOTÁ TRANSFER — DECISION SHEET v1
last updated: tonight, late

=== FINANCIAL ===
  + 25% salary bump (+24k USD/yr on top of 95k → ~119k USD)
  + equity refresh (worth ≈ 15-20k USD over vesting; need to confirm)
  + relocation package (movers + temp housing 60d + flights one-way for me, Camila, nube)
  + quarterly flights CDMX-BOG return (4 trips/yr)
  + dog stipend (≈ 1500 USD per international move per the company HR sheet)
  + Bogotá cost of living slightly lower than CDMX in some categories (rent comparable; food cheaper; transport similar)
  - CDMX rent contract — 6 months left, can break for ~1 month penalty
  - CDMX storage of belongings (vinyl 151 records, espresso machine Lelit, woodworking tools, my dad's old bookcase, Camila's drafting table, Hugo's pebble bowl) ~2k USD/yr OR ship at company expense (vinyl is the worry — temperature/humidity in cargo is bad for records)
  - Camila salary risk if no F+P rotation = up to 70k USD/yr lost
  - tax complexity: dual-country income year, would need accountant in both BOG and CDMX
  net financial: clearly positive ASSUMING Camila gets F+P rotation. ASSUMING she doesn't, neutral-to-negative because we lose her income

=== CAREER (SEB) ===
  + first international leadership role
  + ground-up team build, 4-8 hires, full hiring authority
  + executive visibility — direct line to CTO
  + rust expertise leveraged at regional scale
  + reset dynamics with Roberto (he wouldn't be my mgr — I'd report to CTO)
  + resume line for next 10 years: 'led infra team for regional expansion'
  + opportunity to set engineering culture from day 1 — hiring practices, on-call rotations, code review norms
  - 18mo away from CDMX office network
  - return path uncertain — what if there's no role open back in CDMX in 18mo?
  - MIT app pending — if accepted, BOG transfer becomes irrelevant or messy. could even create a contractual problem with Brújula about 18mo commitment
  - rust service prod rollout: do I do it from Bogotá or hand it off?
  - BOG market for senior backend talent is unproven — could struggle to hire 4-8 people I'd actually want
  net career: probably positive but with re-entry risk

=== CAREER (CAMILA) ===
  +/- F+P Bogotá rotation possible but would interrupt Pritzker mentor program here. cannot mentor UNAM kids from Colombia.
  - Pritzker nomination just announced, presence in CDMX matters for next 12mo (firm partners, press, alumni events, the visiting partners want her on London projects, etc.)
  - UNAM mentorship relationships disrupted. some of these kids she's been with for 3 years.
  - Spanish in Colombia is fine, but her PT/network is here. her therapist Dr. Muñoz. her best friend Andrea. her dance class. the cantina she likes in Condesa.
  - if she doesn't get an F+P rotation she would be on extended unpaid leave or look for another firm in BOG.
  - she just said 'no quiero' last night about the move. that is data.
  net career (her): negative for the next 12-18 months. she said 'puedo seguirte' but the cost is real.

=== FAMILY/PERSONAL ===
  - mom Isabela in Guadalajara — 1.5h flight from CDMX, 5h from BOG (one direct daily, currently)
  - mom just had cancer scare (benign but recent). probability of another health event in next 18mo is non-negligible. nurses see them more often.
  - dad Hugo — 66, mild angina history, would be further. statins help but the cardiologist visits are quarterly. emergencies happen at 3am.
  - in-laws (Hugo Sr.) — São Paulo, BOG actually closer than CDMX to São Paulo (shorter flight). small positive for Camila's family time.
  - Mateo wedding (?) probably 2027, accessible from either, slight edge to BOG actually for Tijuana
  - Pablo, Profe, Atlas Box gym — all CDMX. Pablo has been in my life for 11 years. Profe is irreplaceable.
  - therapy with Dr. Muñoz (we're starting again) — would have to switch.
  net family: tilts negative but not catastrophic. small positives for Camila's side.

=== LIFESTYLE ===
  - Roma Norte neighborhood: best apartment we'll ever have at this rent. Doña Marta upstairs. La Polar below. Cardinal café for remote days. Discos Tlamatini for vinyl. Nudo for ramen. Atlas Box for kickboxing. Clínica Querétaro for PT.
  +/- Bogotá: high altitude (2,640m vs CDMX 2,240m — 400m higher, will affect kickboxing recovery initially, takes 4-6 weeks to acclimatize), good food scene, beautiful neighborhoods (Chapinero, Usaquén), excellent jazz scene (apparently), but cooler/wetter year-round
  - kickboxing gym network would need rebuild — Profe Esteban is irreplaceable. there are good Muay Thai gyms in Chapinero apparently but it's not the same.
  - Spanish dialect adjustment (minimal but real, Mexican Spanish vs Colombian Spanish is closer than I sometimes think but the slang differs)
  - vinyl shop scene in BOG: actually good per friends, Don Tlama there is a guy named El Maestro Cifuentes apparently, good network
  - PT routine with Iván — would have to find new clinic, and I'm mid-rehab. setback of probably 4-8 weeks while finding a new physio.
  net lifestyle: tilts negative, worse for body recovery year. would also be a real adjustment for Camila who has nested in CDMX.

=== DOG (NUBE) ===
  - cargo flight stress (could mitigate with vet sedation, $$$, but trauma)
  - new climate (cooler, fine), new sounds, new park system, separation anxiety would flare
  - thunder anxiety: Bogotá has LESS thunder than CDMX actually so that's a wash or positive
  - she just settled in. she's 18 months old. moving her now is a setback to her training.
  net dog: neutral with bad transition phase

=== WOODWORKING / CHESS BOARD ===
  - I just committed to building Hugo Sr's chess board. cedar planks. Toluca trip planned. Festool router I need to buy. workshop space (currently the apartment balcony with a tarp).
  - moving disrupts this entirely. would have to start over in BOG with a new wood supplier and a new workshop arrangement.
  - Hugo Sr's 70th is in 6 months. timing-critical project. moving in January means losing 3 months of build time.
  net woodworking: clearly negative. project might miss deadline.

=== INTUITION ===
  - my gut wants to say no.
  - my ego wants to say yes.
  - the body wants to stay where the PT clinic is.
  - the relationship wants to stay where the Pritzker bowl of pebbles is.
  - the parent in me wants to be 90min from mom.
  - the engineer in me wants the CV line.
  - the woodworker in me wants the Toluca cedar.
  - the boyfriend in me wants Camila's year to be hers, not mine.

the scoreboard reads: stay. but the engineer says go. that tension is the actual decision. I'm going to sleep on it. seven nights. then I'll call Daniela. then I'll talk to Camila again. then I'll decide. but I am writing down here, in this private little log, that my gut said stay tonight, before I built the spreadsheet, and I want future me to remember the gut said stay first. the spreadsheet was an exercise in respecting the engineer in me. it was not an exercise in deciding. the deciding was the kitchen-counter geraniums-light moment. the spreadsheet just confirmed it.

I will say one thing for the record though. if Camila had said 'no, sebas, no quiero ir,' the decision would have been easy and trivial. she didn't say that. she said 'puedo seguirte.' and that means I have to be the one to choose. and choosing this — choosing to say no, choosing CDMX, choosing the apartment, choosing the chess board, choosing my mother being 90 minutes away, choosing Profe Esteban as my coach for the next decade, choosing to bet on the MIT app — choosing all of that is a decision, not a default. it is a decision I will own. I will not look back at age 50 and say 'I should have gone to Bogotá.' I will look back at age 50 and say 'I chose CDMX in 2026 and I lived the life that grew out of that choice and it was a good life.' that's the frame. that's what I'm choosing into.

there's a sub-frame I want to also write down. I am going to spend my career, if I do this right, making a series of decisions like this one. not to leave Mexico. not to chase the bigger title. not to trade good coordinates for a career line. some of those decisions will be wrong, in the sense that they cost me money or visibility or speed. but they will be the decisions of a person who knows where his ground is. and the ground is here. it's been here for two and a half years and it has only gotten more here. CDMX is mine now. Roma Norte is mine now. Atlas Box is mine now. Camila is mine and I am hers. nube is ours. mom and dad are accessible. PT is happening. the chess board is starting. the rust ship is happening. the MIT app is going. all of this is here. I am here. saying no to Bogotá is just saying yes to here.

or I'm 80% sure. let me sleep on it for two more days.

last addendum, 1am: I just looked up the historical climate for Bogotá. average daily high in January is 19°C. average daily low is 8°C. it rains 105 days a year. CDMX averages 22°C in January and 100mm of rain in the entire year. so Bogotá is colder and much wetter. nube hates cold rain. that doesn't disqualify it but it adds a little. ándale. tonight my decision is no. tomorrow we'll see. but it's no. it's no.

second addendum, 1:35am, cannot sleep: I keep coming back to one thing. the Pritzker nomination is the single most consequential moment of Camila's career so far, and it landed less than three weeks ago, and the next 12 months are going to shape what kind of architect she is for the next decade. asking her to put that in storage so that I can put a leadership-line on my CV is, structurally, asking her to subordinate the most important year of her career to mine. I cannot do that. I will not do that. even if she said 'puedo seguirte.' especially because she said 'puedo seguirte.' the only way to honor that sentence is to not make her cash it in. ándale. that's the answer. that's been the answer. I am going to sleep now. for real.

called Daniela. former manager Daniela, the one who left Brújula for the Series A startup back in month 8. she answered, glass of wine in hand, in her new home office. I told her about Bogotá. she laughed first. then she said 'Roberto is moving you, mijo.' I asked what she meant. she said 'they don't put their best rust engineer 5,000km away from the rust service in production unless someone wants the local credit.' she said it without rancor — Daniela doesn't do rancor. she said 'go if you want the line on your CV. don't go if you can't sleep with leaving Camila in the firm year. you decide.' she also said she'd write me an MIT rec letter if I asked, no questions. I almost cried. I told her thank you. I'll ask. soon.

Daniela also gave me a few practical things. she said 'if you do go, negotiate hard. double the relocation. ask for moving insurance specifically for the vinyl. ask for a guaranteed return role at CDMX in 18 months in writing, not as an aspirational phrase. ask for a housing stipend in the first 90 days, not just temp housing. ask for two return flights a quarter, not one. these are all available, they just need to be asked for. don't let them lowball you on the move because you're flattered by the offer.' that is the kind of advice nobody else in my life would give me. that is Daniela. she was my best manager and she is now my best ex-manager. I will never have a manager that good again.
