I told her so. she laughed and said 'you'll have one again, you'll just have to find them, sometimes you have to leave to find them.' that line stuck with me. she meant something specific by it. I am still chewing on what. ándale, Daniela. te debo una. más de una.

I also want to record what Daniela's voice sounded like during the call because the Daniela I worked with day-to-day was someone I knew physically — her presence in a room, the way she'd lean against a doorframe with a coffee mug — and the Daniela I now know via video call is a slightly different person, lighter, less guarded. she has lost about five kilos since leaving Brújula. her hair is longer. she is wearing a T-shirt with a startup logo I don't recognize. she said her new commute is 30 minutes of bicycle into Coyoacán and she has been swimming twice a week. she said 'the company is hard but it is hard in the way I want hard to be — the work is the hard thing, not the politics.' she said it casually but I heard it. she was telling me something about my own situation. she was saying 'the politics at Brújula are not the work, and that is data.' that is data. that is data I have been ignoring. I have been telling myself for six months that Roberto is just a manager, just a transition, just a phase. but maybe Roberto is the work now. maybe the work has become the politics for me. that is a depressing thought to write down at 11pm but it is, I think, true. and Bogotá would not be a fix for that. Bogotá would be a geographic distraction from a problem that is portable. so the answer is still no, but I'm noting that the question 'how long can I do this Brújula gig with Roberto as my manager' is now active. it has joined the queue. I will think about it next month. for now: Daniela said no with grace, and I will say no with grace too. ándale.

two days into thinking and I already know. I know I know. I'm going to say no. the spreadsheet was a ritual to give my ego a fair hearing. the gut won. the gut is right. Camila's year is here. mom's flight is from here. PT clinic is here. nube's anxiety, the vinyl shop, Doña Marta's bread, the Roma Norte rooftop, the bowl of pebbles, my mat at Atlas Box that Profe Esteban will hand me a black belt on someday. the answer is no. I'll tell Roberto next week. I'll wait two more days for the noise in my head to die down so I tell him cleanly, no, with a smile, with thank you for thinking of me, with respect, but with no. then I'll write to Daniela about the rec letter for MIT. one door closes. one door opens. ándale.

I want to also note for myself, here at the close of month 11, that this is the first big career offer I have actively turned down in my life. there was a smaller one back at Wizeline in 2020 — a transfer to the Houston office — that I declined without much agonizing because Camila and I had just started dating and the timing was obviously wrong. this one is different. this one is bigger, the timing is ambiguous, the stakes are real. and I am still saying no. and I am noticing that saying no, here, makes me feel more grounded rather than less. it is the opposite of the FOMO I expected. apparently when you turn down something that is wrong for you for clear reasons that you have written down, you feel more like yourself afterwards, not less. that is data I want to remember for the next time. the next time will come. probably also from Brújula, probably in 18 months when the Bogotá role has opened a regional pipeline and somebody else is leading it and they want a senior engineer to anchor a major component. I will face it then with a year and a half of additional context. I will probably say yes or no based on the same kinds of considerations I weighed tonight. ándale. December. me, Camila, nube, the apartment, the chess board sketch on the wall. that's the whole agenda. that is more than enough.

one final addendum that I want to record before this month ends and the noise settles. I am noticing that the act of writing down 'I am saying no' has, itself, made the no feel more permanent. there is something about the journal that converts ambivalence into commitment. I have known this abstractly for years. tonight I am watching it happen in real time. by the time I close this entry the no will be structural. I will not relitigate it. that is one of the small powers of writing. ándale. cerrado el mes. al doce.

one true sub-thought before I sleep. there is a version of this decision where I pick yes — Bogotá in January, lead an infra team, push my career forward two years in eighteen months — and that version of me is also valid. that version of Sebastián lives in a glass apartment in Chapinero, walks his dog through Parque El Virrey at 5pm, has an office on the eighth floor of a Brújula tower, hires four engineers in Q1 and four more in Q2, ships a regional version of RouteOpt by month 12, comes home for Christmas with a salary 25% higher and a wife who has either rotated to F+P Bogotá or has spent eighteen months on extended unpaid leave. that version of Sebastián is a real person. I can see him. I am, today, choosing not to be him. and I want to write that down as a respectful no, not a dismissive no. the version of me that goes to Bogotá deserves my respect. he is not a coward, not greedy, not imprudent. he is just the man who picked the other path. tonight I am picking this. Roberto gets his answer tomorrow. ándale.

real last note. I am sitting on the kitchen floor at 1:18am. Camila asleep. nube curled at my hip. pebble bowl on the counter. cedar planks against the wall. rust v0.14.3 green on the dashboard. mom alive. dad alive. Pablo five blocks away. Doña Marta two floors up. the answer is no. el corazón ya sabía. la cabeza tardó dos días. ándale.

ok so I'm trying to draft the decline letter for the Bogotá thing and it's harder than it should be. Roberto wants the formal email by EOD Friday, copying HR and the Bogotá office director Alejandra Téllez, and I've been staring at the cursor in Cardinal café for an hour with two empty espressos and a half-finished yerba in front of me. Help me work through this — I'll paste what I've got and you tell me if it sounds like a real adult wrote it or if it sounds like I'm apologizing for existing.

Draft v3:

---
Roberto,

After careful consideration, and following several conversations with Camila and a long look at our circumstances here in CDMX, I have decided to decline the offer to relocate to Bogotá to lead the infrastructure team. I want to thank you, and through you the leadership team, for the confidence and for what would have been a meaningful step in my career. The scope of the role — building the platform layer from scratch in a new market, with hiring authority and real architectural ownership — is exactly the kind of opportunity I would have jumped on three years ago, and I do not take it lightly to say no now.

The decision comes down to three things and I want to be transparent about all of them so there is no ambiguity going forward.

First, Camila's career. As you may have seen on the architecture press cycle this month, her firm has put her forward for the Pritzker student-mentor recognition. Foster + Partners has been very clear that the next two years are when her trajectory either compounds or stalls, and uprooting her from the CDMX office at this exact moment would be financially and professionally damaging in a way I am not willing to ask of her. We had this conversation at length over the last two weeks and we both arrived independently at the same answer.

Second, the dog. I know that sounds frivolous in a corporate email so I am going to leave it to one line: Nube has separation anxiety and a pending behavioral plan with the vet, and the Bogotá relocation timeline does not accommodate the import paperwork or the transition window in any humane way.

Third, the work I am leading here. RouteOpt is six months into the rust migration. We just resolved the memory regression from the Tokio task spawning issue, Andrés is finally productive on the codebase, and we are eight to ten weeks from a staging-quality cut. Handing the tech lead role off in the middle of that — to anyone, however senior — would extend the timeline by at least a quarter and risk the metrics I committed to in my Q3 plan. If the goal is for Brújula's infra to be world-class, the highest expected value of my next twelve months is finishing this migration, not greenfielding in a new market.

I realize the offer was not contingent on me staying as RouteOpt tech lead, and I want to be careful not to overstate the conflict. But the truth is that combining a relocation with a service-leadership transition with a partner-career disruption with a dog issue is four heavy things at once, and I do not see a version where I do that well.

I would like to remain on the platform team in CDMX, continue leading RouteOpt to production rollout, and revisit international mobility in twelve to eighteen months if the option is still on the table. I am genuinely flattered by the consideration and I hope this does not close doors.

Thank you,
Sebastián
---

feedback please. specifically:

1. is paragraph one too apologetic. the 'three years ago' line — does that read as ambitious-then or as 'I have peaked'. I keep going back and forth on it. on one hand it signals 'I am not the kind of person who turns down opportunities lightly'. on the other hand it sets up a frame where the past version of me was hungrier and the present version is, what, complacent. I do not want to plant that seed. but if I cut the line entirely the paragraph reads as 'thank you for the offer, no'. cold. wrong tone. corporate emails live and die on tonal calibration and I am bad at this calibration when the email is about me.

2. is the dog line too flippant. Roberto is the kind of guy who will quote it back at me in a 1:1 in six months. I can already hear it: 'Sebastián, you said you couldn't go to Bogotá because of the dog'. and then a strategic pause where I am supposed to feel small. but if I bury the dog reason in legalese it would be worse. the dog is a real reason. Nube is mid-treatment for separation anxiety and the relocation timeline they offered — six weeks to be on the ground in Bogotá — would have meant either flying her commercial under sedation (no), or boarding her in CDMX for two months while we set up a place there (cruel), or asking my mom in Guadalajara to take her (Mom would say yes and then I would owe her for the rest of her life). none of these were going to happen. but I cannot put two paragraphs about a dog in a corporate email.

3. paragraph four — am I overexplaining the migration. he runs the org, he knows. but if I leave it out it looks like the only reasons are personal, which makes the decline read as 'I prioritize my domestic life over my career', which is true but not how Roberto reads decisions. I need at least one professional reason in there or he will write me off as not leadership material. also the metric '8 to 10 weeks from a staging cut' is a commitment I am putting in writing and now I have to hit it. that's actually fine. that's how I work — write the commitment in front of the manager and then beat it.

4. last paragraph — 'twelve to eighteen months' — am I leaving a door open I don't actually want open. because honestly between Camila's trajectory and the Roma Norte rent situation and the back PT and everything else, I don't see us moving in any twelve-month window. but if I close that door now in writing, I am also closing the door on any future discretionary budget for the CDMX team, because Roberto's mental model of 'people who get headcount' is 'people who play ball with corporate strategy'. so the door has to stay cracked. but how cracked. cracked enough to read as compliant, not so cracked that I have to write this same letter again in eight months.

the meta-question: when you decline a relocation, is the convention to give reasons or to keep it vague. I have read both takes online. some people say give one bulletproof reason and stop. others say give the full context because vagueness reads as 'maybe I am quietly looking elsewhere'. I am not looking elsewhere. but I also don't want Roberto to think this is a negotiating move. it isn't. the answer is no. I am not going to be moved by an extra 15% relocation bonus or a director title or anything else. but I cannot say that in writing because it shuts down the relationship completely. so I have to land somewhere in the middle where the no is firm but the relationship survives. I have spent fifteen years cultivating my professional reputation at this stage and I am not going to torch it because Roberto is a worse manager than Daniela was. and Daniela left, which is part of why this whole conversation is even happening — the only reason I am even being offered Bogotá is that the leadership reshuffled when Daniela left for that Series A startup five months ago, and the new map of who-trusts-whom inside Brújula is still being drawn.

órale, also — separately — Camila wants me to add a line thanking the Bogotá director by name for the call we had two weeks ago. her instinct on these things is usually right but I keep hesitating because the call felt like a soft sales pitch and I don't want to look like I'm performing gratitude I don't feel. on the call, Alejandra spent forty minutes telling me about the Bogotá tech scene, the salary parity adjustments, the apartment market in Chapinero, the relative coffee quality of the Bogotá office's espresso machine vs Mexico's. she was good at her job. she was also clearly briefed on every concern I might have. I left the call feeling like I'd been gently herded toward yes. that's not her fault — that's what she's paid to do — but I am not going to pretend the call was a friendship moment. on the other hand, ignoring her in the email would be a slight, because in the political grammar of this kind of decline, the named-and-thanked person remembers you. so probably I add the line. one sentence. 'I am also grateful to Alejandra Téllez for the time she took on our call last month walking me through the Bogotá office's plans.' boring. polite. functional. fine.

I think I am going to send the letter tonight after Camila reads it once. she is the better editor of professional comms in this household by a wide margin and she has read every important email I have sent for the past four years. I have not sent it without her eyes on it since the postgres outage post-mortem. if she okays it, I trust it.

last nervous question: should I CC anyone besides who Roberto told me to. specifically — should I bcc Daniela. she's no longer at Brújula but she's the person who originally surfaced the Bogotá possibility to me back when she was my manager, before she left in month 8. she would want to know I declined. but bccing her on a corporate email feels weird. probably I just text her separately afterward. yeah. text her separately. don't get cute with the email distribution. Daniela still texts me on Sundays sometimes asking how Nube is. that relationship is intact. I do not need to recruit it into a political email.

órale, ok — give me your read. and tell me if there's any line that reads as 'this guy is on the way out' because I cannot afford to sound like that to Roberto right now. Roberto has been here four months and he and I have built about 30% of the trust we need to have functional 1:1s and I am not going to spend that trust on a sentence I worded poorly.

actually one more thing. read the letter and tell me what it says about me, the writer, that I am not aware of. you are good at that. the letter as a x-ray of the person who wrote it. tell me what you see. tell me what is hiding in the rhetoric. is the person who wrote this email the same person who, four months ago, would have written 'thrilled to take the leap'. or is he a different person now. if he is a different person, what changed.

letter sent. clicked the button at 9:14pm and immediately lost three pounds of stress. Roberto won't read until tomorrow morning, replied to my own thread to BCC Camila, then walked away from the laptop. Camila said over dinner that the version I sent was the right version and that the dog line stayed funny in a good way. five years and she still beats me to my own decisions. I feel the door close. relieved. weirdly empty.kept refreshing the inbox like an idiot for ten minutes after sending. nothing back, of course Roberto's asleep. set my phone face-down on the nightstand. drank a glass of water. lay there listening to Camila breathing next to me and Nube sighing on the rug. Doña Marta's bread oven won't kick on for another 4 hours. La Polar is finally quiet. tomorrow's a normal day. that's the whole point. closing the email loop. closing the eyes.

spent most of yesterday in Cardinal trying to write a proper post-mortem for myself on the Bogotá decline and ended up just listing what the apartment sounds like, which is its own kind of post-mortem. Doña Marta upstairs starts her oven around 5:10 — closer to 5:20 if it's cold — and you can hear the metal racks going in. La Polar opens at 11 and gets loud around 9pm Friday, peaks like 11pm Saturday with reggaetón which is fine because by then I'm either at Atlas or asleep with earplugs. The pipes in the kitchen tap when the boiler kicks. Nube pads from the bedroom to the kitchen six times a night, I've counted — I know which bedroom-to-kitchen run is 'water' and which is 'dream'. The 5th-floor walk-up means I notice my own breathing on the third flight, which is humbling for a brown-belt kickboxer. La Torta on the street, parked exactly where I left her last Tuesday because we've been walking everywhere this month. Rent went 28k to 33k MXN four months ago and that 18% bump made me angry for like 3 weeks and then it became background — the same way a 5am bread smell becomes background. I think the Bogotá thing was a stress test on whether this background had become love or had become inertia. I don't know yet which it is. I do know the shape of it has my name on it. Camila said 'sabía que ibas a decir que no' yesterday morning and she didn't say it as a victory, she said it as a fact, which is somehow worse and better at once. The therapy thing — we paused after session 3 with Dr. Muñoz back in month 7 when the kid-timing conversation went sideways, and the unspoken plan was 'we'll restart when we have something specific to work on'. The Bogotá decline is a specific thing. Maybe we re-book Dr. Muñoz for next month. Maybe not. The decline letter felt like therapy in a way — sitting with the discomfort of saying no to money and a title because the math didn't add up if you priced in dog, partner, body, and a migration that's mine. I'm calling that an okay use of a Tuesday morning. Cardinal's barista refilled my mate hot water without asking which means I am here too often. I will accept that diagnosis.

actual list, in no order: 1) Doña Marta's bread oven kicking on at 5:14 like a metronome, 2) La Polar's Friday cumbia bleeding through the floor, 3) the elevator that takes 90 seconds because someone always props it on the 3rd, 4) the specific creak of the kitchen pantry door that Camila identifies me by when I sneak out at 1am, 5) Nube's nails on the parquet when she trots from the bedroom to the kitchen for water, 6) the church bells from San Juan Bautista in Coyoacán that you can faintly hear on still mornings if the wind is east. that's six things I would lose in Bogotá. Chapinero has its own six things and I'm sure they're great, but they would not be these six things, and at 33 I'm starting to understand that the cost of swapping six known things for six unknown things is much higher than I was pricing it at 30. Roberto would never read this list. he would file it under 'soft thinking' which in his vocabulary is a polite word for 'unserious'. but if you asked the version of me at the office at 2pm on a Tuesday what he loves about his life, that man would answer with a list very close to this one. so the soft list IS the list. that's actually the only post-mortem I needed.

órale. closing. mate is cold. going home to walk Nube before the storm watch starts at 4. she's been wearing the harness more lately, the soft chest one, and she leans into the leash now in a way that wasn't there a month ago. she's getting confidence back. that's also a kind of recovery the email could not have measured.

ok. Mateo called me at 2:14am Tijuana time which is 4:14am here in CDMX, and woke me up out of a bad rust-migration dream where Andrés was reading me PostGIS error logs in Italian for some reason. I see the brother icon on the screen, 'Mateo Río' with the photo from 2018 of him in the Ducati jacket, and my first thought is that someone has died, because no one in my family calls at 4am for any other reason. I sit up in the dark, knock my elbow on the bedside lamp, Camila stirs, Nube lifts her head from the foot of the bed.

I pick up. He's drunk. He's drunk in that very specific Mateo way where he gets very precise — over-enunciates, uses my full name, deploys formal Spanish like a courtroom defendant. 'Sebastián Río Castellanos. Tengo una noticia. Me voy a casar.'

I literally sat up further and said 'qué' three times in a row like a malfunctioning Alexa. He goes 'Sofía. Le pedí. Hace dos horas. Dijo que sí. Estoy en una taquería en Zona Río con mis amigos y aún no le digo a nadie de la familia, eres el primero.' I am 28 and a half years older than this kid emotionally even though we're only 5 years apart in calendar age. I am hugely happy and also irritated that he's drunk-calling at 4am which is the older-brother emotion for you in compressed form.

Sofía. Sofía Aguirre. They've been together what — 2 years and change, met when she was a dental hygienist in the office two doors down from his design studio, kept finding excuses to be in the hallway when he was, Mateo eventually asked if she ever ate lunch and that was that. She's lovely. She's 27, originally from Mexicali, very organized in a way Mateo desperately needs to be married to. She handles money. She remembers birthdays. She told me at the last family Christmas that she keeps a shared Google Sheet of household repairs by the front door, organized by tab, and Mateo has not once added a row to it but he reads it every Sunday. That's marriage. He proposed at the Tijuana lookout point above Playas, the one near the bullring, with her favorite tacos from El Rincón Tarasco in his backpack as the post-yes celebration. Of course he did. Of course it was 11pm on a Tuesday and not, like, planned weeks in advance with sunset photographer.

He asked dad's blessing two weeks ago apparently — drove up to Guadalajara, took dad out for menudo at El Sope on Sunday morning, and asked. Dad apparently wept. Dad. The man whose entire emotional vocabulary is 'qué pasó hijo' and one extra-long handshake when something good happens, wept. Mateo says mom Isabela cried too but in the controlled retired-nurse way where she pretended she had something in her eye and then kept pretending for forty minutes. This is 4 months after mom's lipoma scare in month 10 turned out to be benign, so the whole family is operating on a kind of post-relief sugar high anyway, every good news lands harder than it should because we recently tasted what it felt like to expect bad news.

They're thinking summer next year — she wants Tijuana, he's flexible, mom is already lobbying for 'somewhere reasonable for the abuelas', meaning Guadalajara, but mom's lobbying is strategic theater because she knows it's not her call. Dad will play chess on the patio of wherever it is for whoever in the bridal party will sit with him during the reception, that's a guarantee, his travel kit fits in the inside pocket of his guayabera.

I'm best man, obviously. He didn't even ask. He just said 'tú vas a ser mi padrino' with the assumption built in. I told him on the call, half-conscious, 'sí pendejo, claro que sí'. He laughed and got teary which made me teary which made Camila reach over in the dark and squeeze my arm because she'd been awake the whole time pretending to be asleep. Hung up around 4:40. Couldn't sleep. Lay in bed listening to Nube breathe at the foot of the bed. Got up at 5:30 and made coffee.

Camila woke up at 6 and I told her over the espresso, she actually squealed which is rare for her — Argentine reserve breaks for very specific things and this was apparently one. She said immediately 'tenemos que ir a Tijuana' which is true, we have to go meet Sofía properly and meet the parents, and we should plan it before the engagement glow goes corporate. We're talking about going in maybe 5 weeks. Drive. Take Nube. Stop in Hermosillo. Maybe push to Loreto for a long weekend after. I have to think about the migration calendar but I can probably take a long weekend after the next ship.

Also — and I won't tell anyone this except this chat — the kid-timing thing is throbbing slightly in the background of all of this. Camila looked at me over coffee when I said 'Mateo's getting married' and there was a half-second where she was thinking about us, 5 years, no ring, kid conversation paused since couples-therapy session 3 in month 7. She didn't say anything. I didn't either. We'll talk eventually. We have a couples-therapy session-tentative on the calendar for next month. Maybe that's where it surfaces. Maybe over coffee in Tijuana. Maybe on the drive back through Sinaloa with the windows down. We don't have to script it. We just have to actually let it happen.

For now: brother is getting married. I'm best man. I'm pulling out the bombilla cup that abuela Lupita gave me in 2017 — the one with 'Seb' engraved on the rim, the one I almost lost in a taxi in month 4 and recovered — and pouring myself a properly absurd 6am yerba mate to celebrate before Mateo even wakes up with his hangover. Let him sleep. He earned it. Let mom not call me before 8 because I know she's already called him eleven times. Let Camila have her coffee in peace. Let Nube go back to sleep on her side, the tan side with the white sock. Let CDMX wake up around me at its own pace this morning. I am going to sit at the kitchen table and feel this. My little brother is getting married. Abuela Lupita, who would've made the salsa, is not going to be there. That is also true. Both things at once.

update after a coffee — I'm putting the wedding date in my calendar with a tentative flag because Mateo said either July or August and the venue is the bottleneck. Sofía's family wants Tijuana, Sofía wants somewhere coastal but accessible to her grandmother who can't fly, Mateo wants whatever Sofía wants. that's the right answer for him. he's not going to win a venue debate against the bride and her abuela, and he knows it. my job is going to be best man, which Mateo asked me at 4:39am between sniffles and which I said yes to before he finished the sentence. probably I should not have said yes that fast — what if he had been about to ask someone else first as a courtesy and I jumped the line — but no, he's my brother, he was always going to ask me, the speed was correct.

the Tijuana trip is now a real plan. Camila and I drive La Torta up in 5 weeks, stop in Guadalajara to see mom and dad, then continue up the Pacific coast. I'm calling Pablo today to see if he can babysit Nube — he's done it before and she likes him because he wrestles with her in a way Camila will not. if Pablo's out, mom will fly down for the week which she would love and which is going to require a 4-day briefing on Nube's trazodone schedule and the thunder protocol. mom is a competent former pediatric nurse, she will be fine, but I will still write the briefing.

órale, also, abuela — Mama Lupita — would have hosted Sofía at her dining table the first weekend Mateo brought her up to Guadalajara from Tijuana, made her tinga and that ridiculous green salsa Mateo can't stop talking about, and patted Sofía's hand in that specific old-Jalisco-grandmother way that made you feel like you'd been weighed and approved. she's not here. she would have liked Sofía, I'm pretty sure of it. Mateo says Sofía is funny in a dry-Tijuana way, which is exactly the kind of funny abuela respected. five years gone now and I still catch myself reaching for her phone number when there's family news. okay. mate is empty. I'm going to walk Nube now and then call Pablo about the dog-sit. one phone call at a time today.

Mateo also wants to know if I'll give a speech at the engagement dinner in Tijuana, separate from the wedding speech 6-9 months later. I said sure. I have not written a speech in my life. the closest I've come is the post-mortem I wrote for the postgres outage at Brújula in month 5, which Daniela said was 'unusually moving for an incident review' which is the kind of compliment that means 'you wrote feelings into a doc that should not have had feelings, we noticed, it was fine'. so probably I am wired for this. I will need to talk about Sofía. I have met Sofía exactly twice — once over FaceTime when Mateo introduced her last year, once in person when she came down to Guadalajara for Día de Muertos in October. I do not know her. I will need to ask Mateo for stories. I will need to make those stories sound like I knew them already. I will need to do this in Spanish at a Tijuana restaurant in front of her parents who I have also never met. ok. five weeks to write a draft. Camila will edit. Camila edits everything I write that anyone is going to hear. that's our system.

last thought before I close the phone: I am 33, my brother is 28, our parents are alive and healthy, abuela Lupita is dead, and Mateo is starting a new family. life events are stacking like dominoes lately and there's a strange grief inside the joy. that's adulthood I guess. abrazos to Mateo. text sent. moving on.

actually one more — when Mateo and Sofía have kids someday I am going to be the cool tío who teaches them chess on the cedar board I'm building right now for Hugo. that loop closing is something I haven't talked about with Camila yet. her dad's board today, her brother's kid's board tomorrow if Mateo and Sofía have a kid, our own kid's board in 5 years if Camila and I get there. one cedar board, three generations of play. Don Lupe would approve. that is also a way I can quietly explain to myself why this woodworking project matters more than 'I needed a hobby for my back' — it's a practice run. I am not going to send Mateo this paragraph, ever, but I am going to remember it. closing the phone for real. dawn in 4 hours. mate cup empty. Nube under the table. Camila asleep on the couch with the laptop on her chest. fine. moving.

told mom on the phone at 7am her time, she already knew, Mateo had called her at 5am Tijuana which is even more unhinged than calling me. she'd been crying for about ninety minutes when I rang. asked if I was good, asked if Camila was good, asked if Nube was good, in that order. typical mom — checks the entire household before letting herself feel anything. then she said 'tu papá no ha dormido, está organizando un brindis para mañana' which means dad has already mentally hosted a dinner. she wanted to know what color Sofía's family wears so she could coordinate. typical mom.

she asked about Nube — my mom — five seconds into the call, before any wedding talk. 'cómo está la perrita?' I said better, trazodone working. she said good, send a video, then pivoted to wedding logistics.

Andrés finally has a reproducer for the memory regression. It's the spawn-per-request pattern in the dispatch handler. We were doing tokio::spawn for every incoming request to fan out the route-optimization sub-tasks, and the JoinHandles were never being awaited because we treat the sub-tasks as fire-and-forget logging. Each unawaited JoinHandle holds the task's allocation alive in the runtime's task list. Under sustained load this is a slow leak — like 2MB per minute on the staging box, which masquerades as 'memory drift over the day' and then suddenly OOMs at 11pm when the day's traffic has accumulated past the threshold. He showed me the pprof output and the heap is dominated by tokio::runtime::task::Task — like 70% of the resident set. Fix is straightforward: either await the JoinHandles in a background reaper, or drop the spawn entirely and use a tokio::sync::mpsc + a single background consumer. I prefer the mpsc pattern because it gives us backpressure, and we're going to need backpressure anyway when the prod load is real. Andrés is going to draft a small benchmark harness using criterion to validate the fix doesn't regress p95 latency. The PostGIS custom encoder is fine, untouched, bless that thing for not adding to my problems for once. Pairing on this Thursday afternoon. Filing a small RFC in our internal Notion before code changes — Carla flagged in the team slack that she wants to read the design doc before we land 400 lines of refactor. fair. that's how we work now. I miss the days when I could just push the fix and ask forgiveness, but those days were also the days where my code shipped half-baked, so. tradeoff acknowledged. RFC tonight, fix tomorrow, soak test Friday, ship Monday if everything's green.

specifically the issue: tokio::spawn allocates a task on the runtime, and we were doing it inside the request handler with no upper bound. for low traffic, fine. for the burst pattern we hit during the 4pm batch wave (the daily route-replanning dispatch from the city ops dashboard), we were spawning 10-15k concurrent tasks. each task held a small per-task allocation plus a future stack, which adds up. the regression appeared as RSS climbing slowly over a 4-day window — barely visible if you weren't looking at the graph at the right zoom. Carla had flagged it back in week 28 but we didn't have a reproducer until Andrés found one this Tuesday by replaying production traffic at 3x speed in the staging cluster.

fix is to use a bounded mpsc channel. dispatch handler pushes a job onto the channel, a single background consumer task drains it. Capacity 4096 means we get backpressure (which is the right behavior — if downstream is slow, the api should slow down too) instead of unbounded growth. RFC tonight. expecting Roberto to want a full incident-style writeup even though we never actually had a customer-facing incident, just a memory graph that was technically wrong. Roberto is a 'process' guy. fine. I'll write the doc. I'll even write the FAQ section he likes. politics is overhead but RouteOpt staging cut is in 8 weeks and I'm not going to slow it for a 4-page doc.

una pregunta: are you familiar with the tokio runtime starvation issue under burst load? we haven't hit it yet but I'm reading about it because once we fix this mpsc thing I want to know what the next failure mode looks like.

ok the Tijuana trip is properly planned now and I want to write it out before I forget the logistics, because we've spent 3 evenings on this and Camila's already started a Notion doc which means it's real. She titled the doc 'Tijuana Run — primer borrador' and it has tabs for 'rutas', 'hospedaje', 'mascota', 'gastos', 'gente que conocer'. She is the better project manager in this household by some margin and that is fine because I do this all day at work and I do not want to do it on weekends.

Plan: drive La Torta. CDMX to Tijuana is roughly 2,800 km. We split it across 4 days each way.

Day 1: CDMX → Guadalajara, 5 hours via 15D. Sleep at mom and dad's house in Providencia. This is non-negotiable, mom would actually skin me if I drove past Guadalajara without stopping. Mom's already planning a vegetarian-friendly mole verde because she's been refining her vegetarian repertoire ever since I went veggie 4 years ago after dad's bypass — she's got this almond-based mole now that's actually staggering. She told me on the phone yesterday that she has 'tres opciones' for me and she 'wants my honest opinion'. That's the trip's first and last warning that I am about to be evaluated. Dad will have a Rioja open by 4pm and will start asking about chess move 14 of some Caruana-Carlsen game from June and I will pretend I remember it. Camila will play the part of 'enchanted future-something', and she does it well — she actually loves my parents in a way that has surprised me for five years now. She asks mom about her childhood in Lagos de Moreno and means it. We sleep there a night.

Day 2: Guadalajara → Mazatlán, 7 hours along the 15D, stop in Tepic for gas and street corn (no cilantro for me obviously, I have the soap gene, dad still teases me about it like it's a moral failing). Sleep in Mazatlán at this hotel near the old town that Camila found, Casa Lucila, which has dog-friendly rooms with a back patio. Nube comes with us. This is critical and non-negotiable. Her separation anxiety means a 10-day boarding stint is out of the question, plus my back is going to need her on the bed for the post-driving stretches anyway because I sleep better with her weight against my legs and the PT at Clínica Querétaro has me at 50% which is not enough to spend 2 weeks on hotel mattresses without help. Nube does fine in La Torta — I trained her on short trips around Roma Norte and she sleeps in the back, on a folded yoga mat, with the window cracked.

Day 3: Mazatlán → Hermosillo, 9 hours along the 15D, longest leg. We do this one early — out by 6am with yerba mate in the thermos. Stop in Los Mochis or Ciudad Obregón depending on energy. Camila drives the second half because by then my hands will have ached themselves into uselessness on the steering wheel. We have a spotify playlist for this stretch that is mostly Caetano Veloso, Liniers (yes Liniers does music apparently), Beny Moré, and one block of Bad Bunny that Camila refuses to admit she likes.

Day 4: Hermosillo → Tijuana, 8 hours along the 2 and the 8 federal up through Mexicali and across the Sierra. Mateo's apartment is in Zona Río, near the river park, third floor walk-up, dog-friendly because his last roommate had two corgis. Sofía's parents are coming over for dinner that night which means I'm meeting them. Mateo says don't worry, they're 'gente buena onda' from Mexicali, Sofía's dad is a retired truck dispatcher named Ramón and her mom Lupe owns a small bakery called El Volcán. We stay in Tijuana 5 nights — 2 with Mateo and Sofía, 3 in an Airbnb in Colonia Cacho that takes Nube. We have one big family dinner the second night where Mateo will give some half-prepared toast and Camila will cry quietly and I will pretend not to notice. We meet Sofía's parents formally with the gift Camila is preparing (artisanal CDMX coffee beans, pan dulce, a Foster + Partners notebook with Sofía's mom's bakery name embossed which is an extreme move and very Camila). We see Mateo's design studio. We eat fish tacos on the beach at Rosarito on day three. We don't drink too much because I refuse to be hungover meeting in-laws.

Then back: Tijuana → Hermosillo → Culiacán → Guadalajara (sleep one more night with mom and dad) → CDMX. Total trip: 14 days. I'm taking the Friday before and the Friday after off, plus the week in between which is 5 PTO. Roberto signed off, surprisingly fast, didn't even push back. Maybe declining Bogotá bought me some good will, who knows. I'm doing the migration prep before I leave — getting Andrés' fix landed and pinning the staging deploy so nothing ships while I'm in Mexicali eating tacos. Camila is taking the same week off — she's between Pritzker-portfolio sprints so she has 9 days clear and her partner-mentor Begoña has signed off.

The kid-timing thing is going to come up on this trip somewhere, I can feel it in the air. Mom will ask. Dad will not ask but will look at us. Sofía's mom will ask in that direct Mexicali way. Camila will deflect gracefully. We'll probably talk about it on the drive back, somewhere around Culiacán with the windows down and Beny Moré on low and 4 hours of asphalt left in the day. Booking gas stops, packing list, vet certificate for Nube to cross states — actually Mexico doesn't require an interstate vet cert but the hotel in Mazatlán wants one. Got that on Wednesday from Dr. Acosta. La Torta needs an oil change before we leave, brake pads probably last another 5k easy, tires are good, alignment was done in month 11 after the lower-back tweak made me notice she was pulling left. I'm spending Saturday at the mechanic. Cardinal already feels like it's preparing to miss me — the barista asked yesterday when I was leaving.

logistics breakdown for my own sanity, and so future-Seb can audit the planning depth:

Day 1 (Friday): drive CDMX to Guadalajara, 6 hours via 15D, leave 7am to beat traffic out of Toluca, lunch in Querétaro at the carnitas place dad likes (vegetarian for me — they do the salsa with poblanos, fine). arrive Guadalajara 4pm, dinner with mom and dad. mom wants to make her ridiculous mole verde. fine. Camila brings the wine.

Camila wants to walk with mom in centro. dad wants to challenge me to chess on the wooden set he carved himself in the 1990s — I will lose, I always lose, that is the family dynamic. evening: dinner at the family restaurant La Chata downtown, Camila's first time, vegetarian options confirmed.

Day 3 (Sunday): drive Guadalajara to Tepic, 3 hours. coastal pivot. lunch in Tepic, then continue to San Blas for an overnight at a small inn near the playa. that's a vacation segment for Camila and me. I want to read on a hammock. she wants to sketch.

Day 4: Tepic / San Blas to Mazatlán, 4 hours. 2 nights in Mazatlán. malecón, seafood (vegetarian for me, I survive), Camila wants to draw the lighthouse.

Day 6: Mazatlán to Los Mochis, 6 hours. one night, transit only, sleep in a Holiday Inn-tier place near the bus station because we want an early start for the Sonora segment.

Day 7: Los Mochis to Guaymas, 4 hours. one night beach. Day 8: Guaymas to Hermosillo, 2 hours. one night, dinner with my cousin Roberto-not-the-manager who lives there.

Day 9: Hermosillo to Tijuana, 9 hours. that's the long day. early morning start, pack snacks, fill La Torta the night before, top off coolant. arrive Tijuana evening. meet Sofía and her family. dinner with Mateo's future in-laws.

Days 10-11: Tijuana, full days with Mateo and Sofía. Camila wants to see the architecture of CECUT. Mateo wants to take me to the new craft brewery he's into. fine, I'll drink a beer for him.

Day 12: Tijuana to Hermosillo, 9 hours back.

Day 13: Hermosillo to Mazatlán, then Mazatlán to Tepic next day.

Day 14: Tepic to Guadalajara, 3 hours. final night with mom and dad. Day 15: Guadalajara to CDMX, 6 hours. home Friday night.

that's 15 days.
