thinking about how I'm going to feel pulling into Guadalajara after 3 years of mostly flying. The drive into Providencia from the south is the part that hits — Avenida Patria, the trees, the Glorieta Chapalita. The smell of the carnitas place on Niños Heroes that my dad will swear I have to stop at even though I'm vegetarian. I'm going to drive past abuela Lupita's old apartment on Av. de la Paz on the way to mom and dad's. I always do. She died in 2019 and I still half-expect to see the laundry on her balcony. Mateo getting married is making me think about her a lot — she'd have been deep in the wedding planning by phone, telling Sofía how to fold the napkins, sending Mateo black-bean recipes she swore he had to learn before he 'becomes a husband'. She'd have been on call to Sofía's mom Lupe, two abuelas swapping pan dulce intel, exchanging WhatsApps in capital letters at all hours. I'd give a lot to have her on the phone for one of those calls. She knew my brother better than I did in some ways, he'd visit her every Sunday when he was in college and they'd play cards and listen to Beny Moré on the radio. I went on Saturdays for one summer when I was 22 and then drifted off because Saturday was when Camila and I started dating, in 2021 actually after we met at the Oaxaca wedding. Mateo never drifted. He was loyal in the way younger brothers are when there's no competition for grandma's attention. Mateo's never going to have her at his wedding. That's just true. I'll mention her in my best-man toast. I'll figure out how. Probably something about how she'd have made the salsa. Probably something dumb that makes everyone cry. Mom will glare at me from the head table for making her cry in public and then hug me later for the same reason. That's our family rhythm. I miss that woman.

the Glorieta de la Minerva specifically — it's the rotary near the country club where as a teenager I'd sit on the fountain at 2am after parties because that's where everyone congregated. you went there because everyone went there. probably 200 of those nights between ages 16 and 21. I haven't sat on that fountain since 2014. I'm sure they've redone the lighting. I'm sure the vendor on the corner is someone different. I'm sure my friends from then mostly don't go there anymore either. it'll be an artifact of who I used to be, and I'll either feel a tug of nostalgia or I'll feel nothing and that emptiness will be its own diagnosis. either way I want to drive past it. dad keeps saying he wants me to come up more often. I keep saying I will. I haven't. that's a thing that's wearing on him and on me both, I think, but in different ways. the trip will fix it for at least one weekend. better than nothing. mom said she's making my abuela Lupita's mole — the recipe, not a substitute. that means she's been thawing the dried chiles in the freezer for a week, which means she planned this 7 days ago when I told her we were stopping in Guadalajara, which means this trip is already real to her in a way it isn't to me yet. that's the difference between living somewhere and visiting. mom lives in this trip already. for me it's still a Notion doc.

mom emailed me a 14-line draft of 'cosas que decirle a Sofía cuando la conozcas' organized into Categories. Bullet 3 is 'no le digas que es delgada, suena raro'. Bullet 7 is 'pregúntale por su trabajo, no de su familia primero'. Bullet 12 is 'no menciones la política federal, sus tíos votan diferente'. it's like a state-department briefing. I love her. I'm reading it at Cardinal and snort-laughed and the barista looked at me. responded 'gracias mamá, voy a estudiarlo' with three heart emojis to soften the sarcasm. she'll be quoting it back at me in Tijuana when I forget bullet 12 and accidentally bring up the senate.

she added at the bottom 'abuela Lupita habría querido conocerla'. that line made me put my coffee down.

Andrés' mpsc fix is in. Backpressure is a thing of beauty when you're moving from a leaky fan-out to a bounded channel. Capacity 4096 on the channel, single background consumer that batches up to 64 items every 50ms or whenever the channel half-fills, whichever comes first. Memory profile flatlines on the staging box now — we ran a 6-hour soak test, RSS started at 240MB and ended at 247MB which is essentially noise. Compare to the old pattern where 6 hours of synthetic load took us from 240MB to 1.8GB before the OOMkiller came in and we had to restart at midnight. p95 latency went from 38ms (where we were after the multi-thread runtime fix) to 41ms with the bounded channel, which is a tradeoff I'll happily take — 3ms of p95 to remove a memory leak is the easiest swap in the world. Carla's reviewing the PR now — she's been back from parental leave 3 weeks and she's already faster than me on rust idioms because she spent her last 2 weeks of leave reading the tokio book apparently. Who reads the tokio book on parental leave. Carla. She also pointed out a subtle issue with channel close semantics during graceful shutdown which I'd missed — if the consumer task is mid-batch when the sender drops, the batch could be partially flushed and panic on a returned sqlx connection. She wants a select! between channel.recv and a shutdown_token.cancelled to handle that cleanly. Filing the merge for tomorrow. Andrés is humming. I'm going to update the RFC in Notion with the actual numbers tonight after Camila is asleep. that small dopamine hit of writing real production numbers into a doc that 50 engineers will see — nothing else like it in the work.

specifically: dispatch handler now drops a job onto a tokio::sync::mpsc channel with capacity 4096. consumer task is a single tokio::spawn at startup that loops forever, awaits batches of up to 64 items via tokio::time::timeout(50ms), then processes the batch in parallel with a join_set. backpressure: when the channel is full, the dispatch handler returns 503 with a Retry-After header — that's the right error semantics for our upstream caller (the city ops dashboard), they handle 503 already in their retry policy.

benchmarked against a reproducer: pre-fix, RSS climbed from 220MB to 480MB over 4 days under burst traffic. post-fix, RSS held at 240MB flat for 96 hours, no climb. p95 latency unchanged at 41ms. p99 latency improved by 12ms, probably because we're no longer paying allocation cost per spawn. Andrés did the load test and made the graph. Carla peer-reviewed the diff this morning and added one suggestion about graceful shutdown — when we drop the consumer task, in-flight items should drain before the channel closes. fair, valid, easy patch, going in tomorrow. then we'll have an actual ship-quality fix.

shipping to staging on Monday. Roberto wants the writeup before EOD Friday so the platform leadership can read it over the weekend. Roberto is going to red-line the writeup. that is what he does. I will make the changes. that is what I do. but the actual fix lands and that is the thing that matters. closing my laptop.

Nube had a thunder panic attack tonight and I'm shaken. Have to write this out. CDMX rainy season started early this year, we'd had 3 days of evening rain but no real thunder. Tonight around 9:40pm, I was in the kitchen making lentils for tomorrow's lunch, Camila was on the couch reading something for the Mixcoac project she's working up for the Pritzker portfolio, Nube was on the rug between us doing her usual pre-bedtime ritual which is 'find a spot, sigh, restart, find a slightly different spot, sigh again'.

The first lightning strike hit close. I mean close — the windows flashed white-blue, and the thunder came maybe 1.5 seconds after, which means the strike was somewhere in maybe Cuauhtémoc or Roma Sur, basically next door. The whole apartment shook. The hanging spice rack rattled. Doña Marta upstairs probably also yelped — I heard a scrape that might have been her chair scooting back. And Nube — I have never seen her like this. She was on her side one second and the next second she was on her feet, every muscle locked, ears flat, tail tucked up against her belly so tight it looked like she'd reverse-pickled herself. She started panting so hard her ribs were visible through her coat. Then she scuttled — actual scuttle, head down, claws skittering on tile — across the floor to behind the couch. Crawled herself into the gap between the couch and the wall, which is maybe 8 inches wide, and she's a 17-kilo blue heeler, and she got stuck.

Camila said 'qué le pasa' in Portuguese, which she does when she's panicked because Portuguese is her panic language even though her dominant language is now Spanish after five years here. I dropped a wooden spoon onto the tile with a clatter that didn't help, set down the lentils, and went to the dog. She was trembling like a wet kitten. I could feel her heartbeat through her ribs, like literally feel it through my hand on her chest, and it was so fast I couldn't count it — maybe 200 bpm, maybe more, the kind of pulse you can't audibly distinguish into individual beats. The next thunderclap came maybe 90 seconds later, slightly farther but still close, and she actually whimpered — Nube doesn't whimper, she's a working breed, she barks, she growls, she bites if she's confused. She whimpered like a puppy on day one of adoption.

I lay down on the floor next to her with my body between her and the window, blocking the flashes. Camila brought a blanket and laid it over both of us. We stayed like that for 40 minutes. She drooled on my arm. I have a Nube-shaped wet patch on my elbow as I'm typing this. After the storm rolled past — maybe 10:20pm, the thunder fading toward Iztapalapa to the east — she came out from behind the couch but stayed glued to my leg for another 90 minutes. Wouldn't drink water. Wouldn't eat the freeze-dried liver thing she lives for. We tried offering it three times. She turned her head away each time, which she has never done in 8 months of living with us.

Camila said 'esto es nuevo' and she's right — she's never done this. She was born Feb 2024, she's been with us since month 6 of the arc when we adopted her from Hocicos Felices, and we've had storms before but apparently never one this close. Tonight she was not the dog I know. She was the same body but a different brain. I keep thinking about her bone-deep terror. The dog who chases motorcycles down the sidewalk along Insurgentes, the dog who barks the delivery guys into a Pavlovian flinch, the dog who shoulder-checks the freezer door when she wants ice — that dog reduced to a crawling whimper because the air pressure dropped and the sky lit up.

We have the trip to Tijuana in 5 weeks and the rainy season is going to be in full swing through the drive — I can't have her like this for 8 hours in La Torta during a storm in Mazatlán. Camila just looked it up and apparently this is a thing — adult-onset thunder phobia in working breeds, often triggered by a single very-close strike that 'sets' the fear. Once it's set, untreated, it can compound — every subsequent storm reinforces the association.

We have to call the vet tomorrow. Probably Dr. Acosta over in Condesa, the one Camila's coworker Begoña recommended. Maybe she'll suggest something. A thunder shirt, drugs, behavioral. Right now Nube is asleep on the bed, between us, snoring with her chin on Camila's hip, and I'm at the kitchen table writing this on my phone because I don't want to disturb her. I keep listening for the next rumble. I'm more anxious than the dog now, which I recognize as the human-with-distressed-creature pathology — your nervous system catches theirs.

I should sleep. I won't sleep. Tomorrow I'm going to Nudo for ramen at lunch alone, no Pablo, no Camila, just me and the bowl, because I need to think and I think best when I am eating noodles too hot for civilized behavior. I will book Dr. Acosta from the noodle bar. The barista at Cardinal will not see me tomorrow because tomorrow is a noodle day, not a coffee day. Doña Marta's bread will start in three hours. The trazodone era of my life with this dog is about to begin and I don't know it yet on this side of the timestamp.

timing: 9:41pm flash, 9:43pm thunder, 9:43:04pm Nube went rigid in the kitchen, 9:43:30pm she tried to climb on top of the kitchen island and slid off, 9:44pm she ran for the bathroom, 9:44:30pm she was wedged between the toilet and the bath wall and shaking violently. I sat on the bathroom floor with my back against the tub and held the door closed with my foot so it didn't bang, Camila brought me a towel for Nube, I draped it over her, she shook through it like the towel wasn't there. I was talking to her in Spanish in the lowest voice I have. she didn't look at me. her eyes were that wild dilated look I've never seen on her. drool starting to track down from one corner of her mouth.

the thunder kept coming for 40 minutes. CDMX gets these tropical-band convective storms that just sit on top of the city and crack thunder every 90 seconds for 30-60 minutes. she shook through all of it. I measured her shaking by the towel — the towel was vibrating against my arm. I stopped trying to talk her out of it after about 10 minutes because it wasn't working and I was making myself frantic too.

at one point Camila knelt down outside the bathroom and started reading aloud — I don't even remember from what, some architecture book — just for the rhythm of a calm voice. I think it helped me more than Nube. I was crying a bit. I don't think I noticed until Camila pointed out my face was wet. I don't cry easily. seeing her like that broke something. she's a year and a half old, born in February 2024, she's our first dog as a couple, we adopted her at month 6 of arc from Hocicos Felices and the rescue lady warned us blue heelers can be high-strung but I think we both filed that under 'we'll deal with it' the way you do when you're falling in love with a puppy.

rain stopped at 10:25pm, last big thunder at 10:18. she stayed in the bathroom for another 20 minutes after that, then crept out, then went back to the bathroom because she wasn't ready, then crept out again. at 11:05pm she finally walked to the kitchen and drank water for a long time. we both watched her like we were watching a candle. she ate a piece of chicken at 11:15. she's now in the bed with us, glued to my hip, every time the wind shifts she lifts her head.

I am calling Dr. Acosta the moment they open in 8 hours. I have her number from the puppy-shots visit in month 6. I want a behavioral plan, not a referral, not a 'see how it goes', not a 'try a thunder shirt' from the internet. an actual plan with named steps and a medication option I can keep on hand for the next storm. there are storms forecast for next week, week after, week after. CDMX rainy season is just starting and June and July are when the big ones roll. if I don't get ahead of this it's going to be every storm for 4 months. I cannot watch her shake like that 30 more times. nor will Camila. nor will Nube survive it without something snapping in her.

things I'm noticing in retrospect, sitting on the bed at midnight while Nube sleeps with her chin on my thigh: I have not felt this kind of helpless-watching-someone-suffer feeling since Camila's mom Beatriz was dying of pancreatic cancer in São Paulo back in month 0. different scale, obviously — Beatriz was a person, not a dog, and she was dying, not panicking. but the feel-shape is the same: I am here, I am watching, I cannot stop the thing that is causing this suffering, I can only be present for it. Camila put her hand on my shoulder during the worst of the thunder tonight and didn't say anything, and I think she felt the same shape. I think she and I have an unspoken agreement now, after the funeral and the year of grief and the dog adoption and tonight, that being-present-during-suffering is one of the things we are good at as a couple. that is a strange thing to know about your relationship. it is also a useful thing to know.

the other thing I'm noticing: Nube did not look at me during the worst of it. eye contact is how she and I have related since day 1 of adoption. she looks at me to check what to do, where to go, whether the thing on the floor is dangerous. tonight her eyes were not for me, they were locked somewhere middle-distance, dilated, gone. that is what I want to fix. that is the metric. when storm-2 happens — and it will, probably this week — I want her to look at me at least once. that's the success criterion. not zero shaking, not zero hiding. one moment of eye contact during the storm, where she remembers I'm here. if I get that, the protocol is working. if I don't get that for 4 storms in a row, I escalate to Acosta on a different med.

órale, fine. plan: call Acosta at 8am, book first available, ask specifically about a noise-phobia med and a 3-layer protocol — meds + thunder shirt + safe-room training. Camila is researching thunder shirts on her phone right now next to me. I'm going to book Dr. Acosta from the noodle bar. The barista at Cardinal will not see me tomorrow because tomorrow is a noodle day, not a coffee day. Doña Marta's bread will start in three hours. The trazodone era of my life with this dog is about to begin and I don't know it yet on this side of the timestamp.

she's still glued to me. it's 6:15am, Doña Marta's bread oven just kicked on upstairs, and Nube is wedged between my feet under the kitchen table while I make coffee. she usually goes to the door when she hears me up, expecting her morning walk. today she's still hangover-ing the storm. I dropped a fork on the tile a minute ago and she flinched like I'd fired a gun, full-body twitch, then settled back against my shin. I keep replaying the moment last night when I felt her heartbeat through her ribs. there's a particular kind of helpless feeling when a creature you've made yourself responsible for is in pain you can't reason her out of. it's not like a pulled muscle where you can ice it, or a stomach thing where you can withhold food. it's a brain thing in a brain that doesn't have words. and the only tool I have is to be physically near her, which I am, and to keep the apartment as boring as possible until the meteorological situation calms down. Camila just woke up and walked into the kitchen and Nube did not run to greet her, which is the first time she hasn't done that since the day after we adopted her. I said 'good morning' to Camila and Camila said 'cómo está la perrita' and Nube whined faintly from under the table, in that small register that's basically a question — and I realized: she's asking. she's asking if it's safe yet. I told her sí, perrita, ya pasó, and she came out and lay across both our feet like a little fuzzy speed bump. coffee tastes weird this morning. it's me, not the coffee. I'm calling Dr. Acosta the second they open at 8 and I'm going to wear the dog harness with the soft chest pad for the walk to the clinic if we get an appointment today, because she shouldn't be tugging on a flat collar in her current state.

she ate maybe a third of her food this morning, which is not great but not catastrophic. yesterday she would have done the whole bowl in 90 seconds. I'll watch the next two meals. if she's still off her food at dinner I'll soft-boil an egg into rice for her — Acosta has said before that for stress days, bland bulk is better than nothing. Camila is up too, drinking mate with me on the kitchen step. neither of us is going to the office today. Camila messaged her studio lead, I messaged Roberto. Roberto's response was 'take care of the dog, see you Wednesday' which is the most human thing he's said to me since he started. updating my Roberto-as-okay model, marginal increment.

second storm at 3am. she didn't make it behind the couch this time, just shoved herself into my armpit on the bed and shook. Camila held her too. we slept maybe 90 minutes total. I have a 9am standup. I am not a person who can do a 9am standup right now. messaging Roberto at 8 to say I'm working from home today, dog emergency, vet at 11. Camila is calling out of her morning too, says she can shift the Mixcoac model review to Wednesday.

closing the laptop. heating water for mate. Camila is in the shower already. Nube is shoving her face into my knee under the kitchen table. she has not let go of contact since 9:41pm last night. that is also telling me something I should listen to. I'm listening. trazodone in my pocket if needed today.

Dr. Acosta's clinic is in Condesa, Av. Mazatlán, third floor, no parking, walked Nube there because La Torta on the seat would have meant her shaking through the drive, and I refused to add motion sickness to an already bad week. She walked weird the whole way — close to my leg, ears half-flat, kept looking up at the sky and around at every truck that backfired. it's overcast today which probably isn't helping; her brain is treating clouds as warning signs now and I cannot blame her. Camila came too which I'm grateful for because I would have started crying in the waiting room without her. Waiting room was full of a fat tabby cat in a carrier hissing at nothing in particular and a chihuahua with a cone, and Nube did not engage with either, which is unprecedented. she's usually a chihuahua-investigator, ears up, full body lean. today she lay between Camila's feet and put her chin on my shoe. Dr. Acosta saw us 25 minutes late which is normal for vets. she's maybe 38, kind, very calm-voiced, gentle hands, immediately got on the floor when Nube came in which got a tail thump out of her — first tail thump in 36 hours. asked the right questions: which storm, how close, how long had she been hiding, what's her crate situation, what's our schedule like, does she have any other recent stressors, has she been eating normally, has she had any recent vaccinations or new foods. Camila answered most of them — I was running on no sleep and let her pilot, and she's better at vet questions than I am because she's read more on the dog psychology side. Acosta examined Nube — heart, ears, eyes, lymph, the works — said physically she's perfect, classic working-breed body, slightly elevated pulse but nothing crazy, no fever, lungs clear. then she said the words: 'creo que es noise phobia, probablemente desencadenado por la tormenta cercana. esto es muy común y muy tratable.' I let out a breath I didn't know I was holding. Camila reached for my hand without looking.

Dr. Acosta's clinic is in Condesa, Av. Mazatlán, third floor, no parking, walked Nube there because La Torta on the seat would have meant her shaking through the drive, and I refused to add motion sickness to her storm trauma. it was a 22-minute walk from the apartment, Nube stopped twice to look at me as if to confirm we were going somewhere safe. I kept saying 'al doctor, al doctor, vas a estar bien' in the lowest voice I had. Camila walked with us. Acosta took us 15 minutes early because she'd seen my morning email and reorganized her schedule. Acosta is in her late 40s, trained in Querétaro, has practiced in Condesa for 14 years, has a tortoiseshell cat named Meche that lives in the clinic and walks across the exam table during consultations.

she spent 8 minutes just sitting on the floor with Nube before touching her. let Nube approach. Nube did, eventually, with that one-paw-at-a-time approach blue heelers do when they're rebuilding trust. then Acosta examined her gently, slightly elevated pulse but nothing crazy, no fever, lungs clear. then she said the words: 'creo que es noise phobia, probablemente desencadenado por la tormenta cercana. esto es muy común y muy tratable.' I let out a breath I didn't know I was holding. Camila reached for my hand without looking. I think we both nearly cried. there is a specific texture to having a problem named by a competent professional with a treatment plan in their head that you can't replicate any other way. a problem with a name is half a problem.

ok long version of the vet visit because I want to remember it, and because I want to be able to send this to Mateo and to mom in compressed form if they ask what we're doing about the dog. Dr. Acosta laid out the plan in three layers, which is why I like her — she's a systems-thinker vet, not just a dispenser. She drew a little three-tier diagram on the back of a printout while she explained, and Camila has it in her bag now because she's like that.

Layer 1: behavioral modification. We do counter-conditioning starting now. She gave us a Spotify-style 'thunder track' app called Storm Desensitizer which has actual recorded thunder at gradually increasing volumes. We play it 2x a day at low levels — start at 20% volume which is roughly 'distant truck' for a dog's ear — while feeding Nube her favorite treats and praising her, building positive association. Then over weeks we ramp the volume up to actual storm decibels while keeping the treats coming. The goal is to flip her brain's association with the sound from 'apocalypse' to 'snack time'. She estimated 6-8 weeks for meaningful effect. She said don't skip days, don't rush the volume curve, and if at any point Nube shows distress at the playback we drop the volume back two levels and stay there for a week. The schedule is in a Notion doc Camila started before we left the clinic.

Layer 2: environmental management. We set up a 'safe room' — pick the most sound-insulated spot in the apartment, in our case the bathroom (no exterior windows, tile dampens echo, small footprint, walls thicker than the rest of the unit because of the plumbing chase), put her bed in there with a blanket and a stuffed toy with my smell on it, and during storms we lead her to it preemptively if we know one is coming, or let her go there voluntarily. Don't lock her in — she goes by choice. We also got a recommendation for a thunder shirt — the constant gentle pressure helps some dogs by activating something like deep-touch proprioception, doesn't help others, ~50% effective in published studies, worth a try at 700 MXN. Order one tomorrow from the Mexican distributor, ETA 4 days.

Layer 3: pharmaceutical. This is the part I had to sit with. Acosta said for acute storm events — like the one two nights ago, where the strike is close and the dog is in active terror — behavioral and environmental interventions are not going to be enough, especially given she's just been sensitized and is in the post-event hyper-vigilance window. She recommends trazodone, off-label for dogs, situational use only — give it 60-90 minutes before a forecasted storm, dose calibrated to her weight (17kg → 100mg starter dose, can go up to 150mg if 100 is insufficient). It's an SSRI-adjacent compound, FDA-approved for humans, widely used in veterinary anxiety contexts, side effects mild — drowsiness, occasional GI upset, very rarely paradoxical agitation in maybe 5% of dogs which means the first dose has to be done as a 'test' on a non-storm day to make sure she doesn't go in the wrong direction. She wrote the prescription. We picked it up at the pharmacy on Av. Tamaulipas after, blister pack of 20 tablets, 600 MXN. Camila held the prescription on the walk home and looked at me weird. I said 'qué' and she said 'I just thought we'd have these conversations about kids before we had them about Nube' and we both laughed in that exhausted post-vet way and almost cried in the same breath.

Acosta also flagged the Tijuana trip — when I mentioned we'd be driving 4 days each way in 5 weeks she said 'absolutely use the trazodone preemptively for any forecasted storm during the drive, and pre-medicate the night before any leg you suspect will have weather'. She wrote me a separate scrip for travel-dose covering 8 days, total 16 tablets, sealed pack. She also told me where to look up Mexican pharmacies along the 15D in case I run out — Hermosillo, Guaymas, Mexicali all have well-stocked 24-hour Farmacias Guadalajara that carry it. We're going to do the test dose tomorrow afternoon — pick a quiet evening, no thunder forecast, give her 100mg around 4pm, monitor till bedtime. If she stays normal, we're cleared for storm use. If she gets paradoxical we go back to plan B which is gabapentin, which is older school but also reliable for this exact use case.

Acosta also reminded me — and this hit harder than I expected — that this is not weakness in the dog. She said working breeds especially are wired for hyper-vigilance, that's their job, scanning the herd, scanning the horizon, and a single close strike can permanently rewire that vigilance into a phobia in a way that doesn't reflect on the dog's character. I needed to hear that. I'd been quietly worrying that I'd somehow failed her — that her separation anxiety since adoption had compounded into this, that I'd been a bad enough handler that this was inevitable. Acosta said no. 'It's neurology, not love.' She said it twice. The second time she put her hand on my shoulder briefly. Camila wiped her eyes and didn't try to hide it.

We bought Nube a pup-cup at the gelato place on Amsterdam on the walk home, vanilla soft-serve in a paper cup, she ate half of it tentatively and then with growing enthusiasm, fell asleep on the rug at 4pm and is still asleep now at 7. We're driving to the pharmacy tomorrow for the maintenance pack, picking up a thunder shirt online tonight, downloading the desensitization app, and rearranging the bathroom to put her bed in the corner by the toilet which feels undignified for her but she's going to find it cozy because the tile is cool against her belly and she likes cool tile.

Also: I'm sleeping tonight. The vet visit cleared something. The way it cleared when mom's lipoma came back benign back in month 11 — there's a specific physical sensation of having a problem be named and a plan be on paper, three layers, written down, with phone numbers attached. I'd forgotten that sensation. I think the body just lets go a notch when the mind has a checklist. Sleep.

the full 3-layer plan, for the record:

Layer 1 — pharmacological: trazodone 100mg PO, give 90 minutes before forecasted storm onset, repeat dose 8 hours later if storm continues. trazodone is a serotonin modulator commonly used in dogs for situational anxiety. side effects mild — sedation, occasional GI upset. dose calibrated to Nube's 18kg body weight. start at 100mg, can go up to 150mg if needed but Acosta wants me to start low and observe. she gave me a printed script and we filled it at the farmacia veterinaria on Av. Insurgentes on the way home.

Layer 2 — behavioral: thunder shirt (snug compression vest), introduced gradually so it's not associated with storms. wear it for 10 minutes a day on calm days for a week, building positive association with treats. then start using it 30 min before forecasted storms.

Layer 3 — environmental: build a 'safe room' — somewhere small, dark, quiet, that Nube can self-select into during a storm. Acosta recommended the bathroom if Nube already gravitates there (yes, she does), with a thick rug, a piece of clothing that smells like Camila or me, a covered crate or improvised cave (towel over a chair). teach Nube to enter the safe room on a cue word ('refugio') with treats, on calm days, so she has positive association. then during storms she's allowed to go to the safe room voluntarily.

plus a noise-desensitization app — Acosta sent me the link to a Spanish-language app that plays graduated thunder sounds over weeks, starting at 30% volume during meals to associate distant thunder with food.

she said most dogs respond to the 3-layer approach within 4-8 weeks. some are 'fixed' (her air quotes, she warned the word doesn't really apply, anxiety can recur) within a season. some need lifelong management. some get better and worse. Nube might be any of these and we won't know until we run the protocol for a season. I asked about adverse reactions to trazodone. she said vomiting in some dogs in first 48 hours, do a test dose on a calm day before the next storm to see how Nube tolerates. fair. doing that tomorrow at 4pm with a piece of cheese as a chaser.

we walked home with the script in my pocket and a 5-page printout on the protocol. Nube walked between Camila and me with the leash slack. she's not fixed but she has a plan. and just like the morning back in month 11 when mom's lipoma came back benign — there's a specific physical sensation of having a problem be named and a plan be on paper, three layers, written down, with phone numbers attached. I'd forgotten that sensation. I think the body just lets go a notch when the mind has a checklist. Sleep.

called Acosta's office back at 5pm to ask one follow-up question I'd thought of on the walk home: what happens if the storm wakes her at 3am and she missed the pre-dose window. answer: emergency dose, 50mg, give as soon as you wake, do not exceed 150mg in 24 hours, and do not give if she's already lethargic from a previous dose. wrote that on a 3x5 card and clipped it to the bottle. Acosta also said: keep a log, because she wants to see the pattern over 4 storms, see what's working. log columns: storm date, forecast lead-time, dose given, time of dose, shake duration, post-storm food intake, sleep recovery. fine. I'm a backend engineer. I make telemetry. building this little spreadsheet is the easiest thing I'll do this month.

órale. one more thing. I'm putting Acosta's protocol PDF on the fridge under the magnet that holds the takeout menus. that's the level of importance it has now in this household. higher than the takeout menus, lower than Nube's vaccination card. Camila approved the placement. Nube did not but she also does not get a vote on fridge magnetics.

test dose went fine. gave her 100mg at 4pm with a piece of cheese (queso panela because that's what was open in the fridge), watched her like a hawk for the first 90 minutes. by 5pm she was visibly relaxed — not loopy, not staggering, just the kind of slack-jaw heavy-paw cuddle posture she does on a Sunday afternoon on the rug after a long walk. ate dinner at 6 with normal enthusiasm, walked normal at 7 around the block (slowly, but she walks slowly when she's chill), fell asleep at 9 about an hour earlier than usual, slept till 6am without the usual 3am water-bowl shuffle. no GI issues. no agitation. she's cleared. I texted Acosta a thumbs up and she replied with a heart emoji and a line — 'perfecto. úsala con confianza durante tormentas. avísame si algo cambia'. 600 MXN scrip is going on the spending tracker under 'medical — Nube' which is a category I now have. I'm putting the blister pack in the bathroom drawer next to my floss because I think of bathroom drawer as 'health stuff', and the storm bag for La Torta has 4 tablets sealed in a small ziploc with the date written on the bag in Sharpie. Camila approved the system after I walked her through it. she also said 'soy una persona ahora con un kit de tormenta para mi perra' and laughed, she's right, we are. the desensitization app is downloaded, we did our first 2-minute session at 30% volume during her dinner, she didn't react to the recorded thunder at all because at 30% it sounds like a distant truck rumbling four blocks away, she just kept eating with her usual head-low posture. baby steps. starting tomorrow we go 35%. if she eats through it without flinching we'll hold at 35% for 3 days then push to 40%. there's a curve. we're on the curve.

the noise-desensitization app schedule for the week — for my own records: Mon 30%, Tues 30%, Wed 30%, Thurs 35%, Fri 35%, Sat 35%, Sun 40%. only during meals. only 5 minutes max per session. Acosta was firm on the cap because too long pushes the dog into a sensitization curve, which is the opposite of what we want. I'm setting iPhone reminders. I am the kind of person who sets reminders for the dog's noise therapy. I'm at peace with this. I am also the kind of person who used to be 28 and snorted at people who had iPhone reminders for their dogs. life comes at you fast.

thunder shirt arrived from the Mexican distributor. it's basically a snug velcro vest, neoprene-feel, makes Nube look like a tiny EMT. she tolerated putting it on, looked at me like 'qué chingados es esto', then settled into a yoga-flop on the rug. mild help during the 11pm storm tonight — she still went to the bathroom safe-room but didn't shake as much, maybe 60% of last week's intensity. trazodone on board too. layered defense. she slept on the bath mat with the vest still on. I took it off in the morning and she yawned dramatically at me as if I had made her sleep in a tuxedo.

plan layered: trazodone, shirt (mild help), safe-room training, noise app. this storm test felt like a system, not a panic.

third storm hit at 8:40pm. forecast was unclear so we hadn't pre-medicated, kicked myself for that, gave her 100mg at the first low rumble at 8:25 — within window but a hair late by Acosta's protocol. put the thunder shirt on her at 8:30, walked her to the bathroom at 8:35, set up the speaker with the desensitization app at 35% volume to overlay actual thunder sound which is a counterintuitive trick but Acosta swears by it — the brain has trouble distinguishing the categories when one is layered over the other. she went into the bathroom voluntarily this time. that's huge — she chose the safe room. lay down on her bed in the corner under the towel rack. shook for the first 20 minutes hard, then mediumly for 10, then her shaking dropped off as the trazodone hit and the storm peak passed off toward Iztapalapa. By 9:20 she was loose, eyes half-open, head on the bath rug, breathing slow. By 10pm she was up and asking for her freeze-dried liver in the kitchen, and we gave her two pieces because we are not made of stone. Camila and I high-fived in the hallway. it's the smallest thing and it felt like a championship — like watching the rust mpsc fix flatten the memory graph in production. systems working. she still woke me at 4am to come back to the bed but that's normal Nube, baseline normal Nube, not storm-Nube. she's in here on the bed. I have her. she has me. the apartment is quiet and the rain is just rain again.

breakdown of how it went vs the first storm: total shake-time was about 12 minutes, vs 40 minutes on storm 1. she went into the bathroom voluntarily on the first thunder roll, vs hiding behind the toilet on storm 1. she came out within 8 minutes after the storm passed, vs 50 minutes on storm 1. she ate a piece of cheese 20 minutes after the storm, vs not eating until next morning on storm 1. she did not drool. she did not pant in that ragged way. her eyes stayed mostly normal — wider than usual but not the wild dilated look from storm 1. measurable improvement on every metric.

Camila watching this happen in front of her — Camila was watching the rust mpsc fix flatten the memory graph in production. systems working. she still woke me at 4am to come back to the bed but that's normal Nube, baseline normal Nube, not storm-Nube. she's in here on the bed. I have her. she has me. the apartment is quiet and the rain is just rain again.

1:37am, can't sleep, Nube is on Camila's pillow snoring with her chin off the edge, Camila is on her side breathing the slow even breaths of someone who actually deserves the sleep she's getting, La Polar downstairs is finally quiet — the last reggaetón burst was 12:40, which is actually civilized for a Friday — and Doña Marta's oven won't kick for another 3 and a half hours. I'm at the kitchen table writing this on my phone because the laptop screen is too bright and I will not be able to sleep again if I open the laptop and start typing into Notion.

just want to dump where I am because it feels like the year just shifted under me in 30 days and if I don't write it out my brain will keep recombining the elements until 5am.

Bogotá decline went out, the formal letter went to Roberto on Friday at 9:14pm, copied HR and Alejandra Téllez, response from Roberto at 8:02am Saturday saying 'understood, let's grab a coffee Tuesday', which is corporate for 'noted with respect'. I haven't actually felt the absence of the choice yet — like, normally a big no should produce a bigger psychological echo, but maybe my echo system is full from everything else. Three nights of dog-storm vigil will do that.

Mateo getting married out of nowhere, that's the loudest thing in the room mentally even though it doesn't structurally change my life. He called me at 4am drunk three weeks ago and now there's a wedding to go to and a sister-in-law incoming and a new family-of-Sofía to meet and a Tijuana road trip to plan around all of that.

Mom benign at month 11 still has its own afterglow that hasn't fully faded — every time I talk to her I have a half-second 'is she okay' before her voice connects and she's complaining about the gardener and I relax. That afterglow is going to stay for a year easy.

Pritzker nomination for Camila — I'm proud of her, I'm slightly intimidated, I notice myself comparing résumés which is not who I want to be. Foster + Partners is putting her on the Mixcoac school project as a portfolio anchor, which means she's at the office until 9pm three nights a week now. We had spaghetti at 10:30 last Tuesday and she fell asleep on the couch with her plate on her chest and Nube ate the spaghetti off the plate without waking her up. We've been laughing about that for a week.

The rust migration is at the 'we found and fixed the leak' stage which is the only stage that feels good in a backend project — it's the brief window between 'oh god we're broken' and 'oh god we have to ship'. Carla being back is enormous. Andrés is leveling up faster than I expected — he authored the pprof analysis that exposed the Tokio task spawning leak, basically by himself, after I gave him a 30-minute pointer session and walked away. Roberto is being slightly less micromanagy because the metrics are good and people who micromanage tend to back off when the numbers cover them. The promo bump from month 2 has been mostly absorbed by the rent increase from month 9 — net I'm maybe 4% ahead of where I was. The pose-est project is on ice since month 11 and I haven't opened the GitHub repo, I check the dashboard once and there's a small ache, the small ache of a parked thing that wanted to live longer than I gave it.

Body: back PT at Clínica Querétaro is plateaued at maybe 50%. I'm sleeping 6 hours on a good night, 4 on a bad one, and tonight is going to be a 4. The 3am wake-up cycle has been reliable enough that I should probably name it like a feature.

The kid-timing thing is alive in the background of every conversation with Camila but we haven't formally re-opened it since therapy paused after session 3 in month 7 with Dr. Muñoz. The fact that Mateo is now getting married has not helped with that — every Río family member from now until April is going to ask Camila if she wants kids and she's going to deflect and I'm going to feel her tense up and I'm going to be the one to say 'amor, vamos a hablar de eso solos' on the drive back from Mateo's wedding eventually, except there's no formal wedding yet, just the engagement and the Tijuana trip in 5 weeks.

Nube — Nube is the surprise structural event of the month. Three storms, three escalations of fear, then Acosta, then the trazodone, then the shirt, then the safe room. We have a system now. The system feels load-bearing. I had a moment last night where I realized I was tracking weather forecasts more obsessively than I track Roberto's standup attendance, which says a lot about my priority stack. The thunder shirt is in the same drawer as her harness. The trazodone is in the bathroom. The desensitization app is on both our phones. We are dog parents who have a kit, which is a sentence I would have laughed at in 2021 and now is just the truth of my Friday.

The MIT thing is also somewhere in this brain — I haven't said it out loud yet but I keep coming back to a Media Lab project page I bookmarked last week. Affective Computing group, Picard. The pose-est work I parked back in month 11 is exactly the shape of what they do. There's a fall application cycle. I'd be 33 and a half by start. Camila would have to want to be in Cambridge for two years which is a different conversation than 'Bogotá for 9 months'. Saying it on this little chat is the first time it's left my head. It's the lowest-stakes possible version of saying it. Even putting it in a chat window feels like flinching.

The Pritzker nomination on her side and a possible Media Lab on my side and Mateo's wedding and Nube on trazodone and a brother fully grown enough to commit to a life and a back that needs another 6 months of PT and a Camila I love so much I can feel my own pulse when she breathes wrong in her sleep — yeah, this is a 1am dump. okay. closing the phone. Doña Marta in 3 hours. La Polar will reopen in 13. I will exist for both of those. Sleep, Seb. Try.

the 1am thoughts in no order: am I going to regret declining Bogotá in 5 years. is the rust migration going to ship clean. is Roberto going to ever fully trust me or are we going to be 30%-trust forever. is Camila ever going to bring up kids again or has the topic gone underground. did I really just spend 3500 MXN on a Festool I haven't bought yet because I keep talking myself into it for the chess board project. is mom's lipoma actually a lipoma or did the radiologist miss something — that thought ambushed me last week and I had to actively close it. is dad's angina going to crack open at his 70th and is the chess board going to arrive in time. why is my back not 100% yet, the PT is at 50% improvement which sounds fine on paper but on the mat it's the difference between sparring 5 rounds and sparring 3.

russia of the mind. I know enough about insomnia to recognize this is the rumination flavor and the only treatment is to label it and step out. so I'm typing it instead of letting it run on loop. La Polar closed at 1am, it's 1:37am now, the building is finally as quiet as it gets in CDMX. Doña Marta's oven kicks on at 5:14 like clockwork.

the back PT — I started at Clínica Querétaro back in month 11 after the sparring tweak. it's working. it just isn't working as fast as I expected, and the rate of improvement has plateaued for the last 3 weeks. PT is talking about adding glute activation work because the imbalance shows up there. fine. add it. but I want to be honest with myself: I'm 33 next year, the days when I could spar 5 rounds and be fine the next morning are over, and the days when I could spar 3 rounds without taping my back are also numbered. I love kickboxing. I'm 7 weeks from the brown-to-black grading test which is the hardest grading in the federation. I want to take the test. I do not want to take the test injured.

I'm also worried about the chess board. 12 weeks from now Hugo turns 70. I'm a first-time woodworker who hasn't bought a single tool yet. I keep researching routers and tracksaws and chisels and reading r/woodworking and watching Festool YouTube and what I have not done is bought a single piece of cedar and made a single test cut. that's the gap between research and project. I know this gap. I built the pose-est project across this gap, I've built every project I've ever shipped across this gap. you cross it by buying the tool and making the bad first cut. I keep procrastinating on the tool order because the Festool is 25k MXN and the order requires committing and the commitment requires admitting that I am someone who starts a woodworking project at 33, which is admitting that I'm shifting my hobby portfolio in a way that I haven't fully reconciled. okay. tomorrow I order the Festool. closing the loop on that mental thread.

yeah, also: I haven't called Mateo since the engagement call 12 days ago. that's the longest gap between brother-calls since I moved to CDMX. that's data. it means I am avoiding something — probably the fact that he's getting married and starting a family on a normal timeline and I'm declining international moves and worrying about the dog and ordering Festool tracks instead of buying engagement rings. I am proud of him. I am also a little jealous of how unconflicted his trajectory looks from the outside. probably his trajectory is also conflicted from the inside. probably no one's life is actually as linear as it appears. but at 1:48am the comparison is unhelpful and it's the comparison brain that's running anyway. tomorrow I call Mateo. Bogotá decline is the right call. RouteOpt rust is going to ship to staging, I trust the team to commit to a life and a back that needs another 6 months of PT and a Camila I love so much I can feel my own pulse when she breathes wrong in her sleep — yeah, this is a 1am dump. okay. closing the phone. Doña Marta in 3 hours. La Polar will reopen in 13. I will exist for both of those. Sleep, Seb. Try.

Carla shipped the channel-close-graceful-shutdown patch this morning and we're stable. she found that during shutdown, if the consumer task is mid-batch and the channel sender is dropped, the batch in flight could be partially flushed and then we'd panic on a sqlx connection that was already returned to the pool. she added a select! between the channel.recv and a shutdown_token.cancelled, plus a final flush on the consumer side that drains whatever's left in the channel after shutdown is signaled, with a 5-second timeout to avoid hanging shutdown forever. clean. PR was 60 lines. she wrote tests. she's faster than me at this, I keep saying it, and Andrés noticed too because he asked me yesterday in our pair if Carla mentored at a previous job (yes, three years at Wizeline before us, I forgot). Andrés is running a 24-hour soak with both her shutdown patch and his mpsc fix on staging. p95 holds at 41ms, RSS holds flat at 248MB. the PostGIS encoder remains a thing of beauty in the codebase, never causes anyone trouble. Roberto stopped by the Slack channel to say 'great work team' with a clap emoji which is his version of fireworks. moving on to the next blocker which I suspect is going to be how we handle Tokio runtime starvation under burst load — that's still on our radar but not screaming yet. closing my laptop now. dog walk before the next forecasted storm. she's wearing the harness. trazodone in my pocket if needed.

the shutdown patch is small but elegant. on receipt of SIGTERM, the supervisor task closes the channel sender, then the consumer drains all remaining items in the channel before exiting. each in-flight job has a 5-second deadline applied via tokio::time::timeout — if it doesn't finish in 5s during shutdown, we log a warning and move on, accepting the job loss. that's the right tradeoff for shutdown semantics: at-most-once is acceptable for this dispatch path because the upstream service retries automatically. Andrés peer-reviewed and only flagged a doc comment that was wrong. tiny. landed by 11am.

it's done. moving on to the next blocker which I suspect is going to be how we handle Tokio runtime starvation under burst load — that's still on our radar but not screaming yet. closing my laptop now. dog walk before the next forecasted storm. she's wearing the harness. trazodone in my pocket if needed.

fourth storm last night. she went to the bathroom by herself this time, before we even got the shirt out of the drawer. she just walked over, glanced at us, and entered. that is unbelievably cute and unbelievably load-bearing. like the system worked. trazodone on board, shirt on, safe room chosen voluntarily, app playing thunder at her chosen low volume. she shook for maybe 7 minutes total. progress is not linear but it is here, measurable, on the curve.

week-over-week metrics: shake-time 7min vs storm-1 40min vs storm-2 12min. drool none. food intake post-storm: full bowl. she's on a curve and the curve is going down.

Roberto called me into a 1:1 today which he hasn't done since month 9 when he started, that meeting was about 'getting aligned'. today's meeting was 30 minutes long, on his calendar, walking-meeting style — we did laps around the block by the office, La Condesa side. he opened with 'thanks for being clear about Bogotá', which I read as 'we appreciate the clean no, no negotiation games, no slow-roll'. then he pivoted to 'how do you see your next 18 months at Brújula' which is an HR-coached question and I gave an HR-coached answer about wanting to ship rust to prod, mentor Andrés through senior, and be on a stronger career-ladder path generally with meaningful technical scope. He nodded. Then he said something that surprised me — he said the Bogotá role had been positioned partly as a development opportunity and that he wanted to make sure we found other development opportunities for me here so I didn't feel like I was missing out. that's actually thoughtful. I told him, honestly, that the rust migration plus tech-leading the team plus mentoring Andrés is plenty of development for now, and that if there's a stretch architecture-track opportunity at the org level (think tech council, cross-team RFC reviews, etc.) I'd be open. He said 'fair, I'll think about that'. We talked about Mateo's wedding briefly because he asked about the trip and I mentioned the engagement, and he was warm in a way I don't usually associate with him. he asked when. I said summer. he said 'congrats to your brother'. ended the meeting better than I came in. I think Roberto is more okay than I'd been giving him credit for. updating my mental model. Daniela was my favorite manager I've ever had, but maybe Roberto is going to be a fine one if I let him.

the walking-meeting was in Parque España because Roberto's kid had a soccer game in Polanco and he was killing 30 min between meetings. fine. we walked. he asked specifically about the migration and I gave him the honest answer — staging cut in 8 weeks if nothing else breaks, 10 weeks with margin. he nodded. then he asked about the headcount question — Andrés is going to be senior next cycle, do we backfill with a new junior. I said yes, we should backfill, ideally before Carla's parental leave fully closes out. he said he'd raise it at platform leadership. I asked when. he said summer. he said 'congrats to your brother'. ended the meeting better than I came in. I think Roberto is more okay than I'd been giving him credit for. updating my mental model. Daniela was my favorite manager I've ever had, but maybe Roberto is going to be a fine one if I let him.

took an actual half-day off today, drove La Torta to Bosque de Chapultepec, walked the second section for 90 minutes — Lago Mayor, then up the path past the modern art museum, then around the big lake again — sat at the lake on the bench by the duck colony and did an audit of where I am.

33 years old, going to 33 and a half soon. CDMX, Roma Norte, fifth-floor walk-up with Camila for 5 years and counting and one dog. Brother getting married. Mom alive and benign and still calling me every Sunday at 11am her time. Dad alive and chess-playing and post-bypass and slowly statin-stable. Abuela Lupita gone 7 years next month. I lit a candle on her birthday last week, the lapsed Catholic act, the way she'd want me to even though I haven't been to mass since 2009 except for funerals and weddings and Mateo's confirmation when I was already a software engineer.

Carrying her bombilla cup with me to the lake today, drank 3 mate refills sitting on the bench. The thing engraved 'Seb' on the rim, gift from her in 2017, almost lost it month 4 in a taxi and recovered it through a small miracle of a taxi app driver who answered three calls in a row. The mate goes hot all afternoon out of the thermos. The cup feels like a phone line back to her sometimes.

Vegetarian 4 years, since dad's bypass in 2022 — I watched the surgery debrief video the cardiac surgeon sent and decided that night, no animal flesh on this body. Held. Mom still does fish for me at family dinners which is a compromise we have not formally negotiated but both honor.

Kickboxing brown belt as of month 8, 6 months in, hungry for the black belt test which I've been told to plan for around month 15. Money: 95k USD eq salary post-promo from month 2, 45k USD saved across CETES and USD index, 8k USD remaining on ITESM loan (down from 14k at arc start), rent eats meaningful chunk after the 18% bump in month 9. Vinyl collection at 150-ish, latest acquisition was Beny Moré con la Banda Gigante in month 11 from a record store in Coyoacán that smelled like old wood and dust. Hot sauce experiments on hold because I haven't had headspace, last batch was a habanero-mango thing in month 9 that Camila called 'too political'. I miss the kitchen experiments. they'll come back.

Pose-est project paused in month 11, I check the GitHub stars sometimes in a private guilty way, it's at 23 stars from when I posted the demo on Mastodon. The Media Lab daydream that won't shut up keeps pointing at the parked repo like 'so what are we doing'.

Career: tech lead on a rust migration that's about to ship to staging, no obvious next step except keep shipping. Could MIT? Could MIT really? I keep coming back to it.

Personal: I love Camila in a way I didn't know was possible at 30, and I sometimes look at her and think she's already grieving the version of us where we have kids before we even have them. We paused therapy at session 3 back in month 7 and we haven't restarted. Mateo's engagement is making the kid-timing question louder, the way thunder makes Nube louder. Dad will ask me on the drive into Guadalajara. Mom won't ask, she'll observe and then deploy. Sofía's mom will ask in Tijuana over pan dulce because that is what families do. I'll deflect. We'll talk on the drive back. Probably somewhere between Mazatlán and Tepic on the 15D. Probably with the windows down. Probably with Beny Moré on the speakers.

Audit conclusion: I'm where I want to be, more or less, with one large unaddressed structural conversation (kids), one unfinished deliverable (RouteOpt to prod), one growth question (MIT or not), one body-rebuild project (back), one parent-of-creature project (Nube and storms), one approaching family-of-origin event (brother's wedding), one financial habit holding (savings curve), one creative restart pending (vinyl, hot sauce, woodworking maybe), and one quiet resolve (do not become dad's emotionally-closed version of a successful man). That's a list. That's manageable. The bench is wet from a sprinkler. I'm closing the audit. The mate is cold. La Torta wants to go home. The duck colony is judging me. I get up.

did the audit out loud, sort of, sitting on a bench by the lake. the questions: what's working, what's not, what do I want for the next 12 months, what do I fear, what would I regret in 5 years. answers, in order:

working: routeopt is 8 weeks from staging, the team trusts me, the migration metrics are going to be career-defining. Camila and I are stable. Nube has a plan. mom is cancer-free for now. brother is happy. mate cup is whole. parents are alive. café Cardinal still has my mate ratio dialed.

not working: back is 50% PT-recovered and stalling. insomnia is at maybe 5 hours a night average, which is enough to function and not enough to thrive. relationship with Roberto is at 30% trust. relationship with my own ambition is wobbly — I declined Bogotá and I'm fine with it but the back of my head still keeps rerunning the decision. I haven't called my brother in 5 days, ever since the engagement call, because I'm bad at calling family.

want for next 12 months: ship rust migration to prod. take the brown-to-black grading test. give Hugo a chess board he cries at. write something that gets me into MIT or somewhere like it. learn to sleep again. teach Nube that thunder is just water hitting concrete.

fear: dad's angina cracks open. mom's lump comes back as something other than benign. Camila bumps the kid timeline and I'm not ready. the rust migration ships and someone else takes credit at promo time. I become my dad — successful, closed off, emotionally on rails.

would regret in 5 years: not visiting abuela one more time before she died (already done, can't fix), not telling Camila enough that I'm grateful she's the editor of every important thing I write, not learning Portuguese well enough to be funny in it (B1 is enough to be polite, not enough to be charming, and Camila's family deserves charming).

that's a list. that's manageable. so the list is: one big career project, one big athletic project, one big personal project (chess board), one big intellectual project (MIT app), one big bodily project (sleep + back), one big relational project (Camila + family), one new hobby (hot sauce, woodworking maybe), and one quiet resolve (do not become dad's emotionally-closed version of a successful man). That's a list. That's manageable. The bench is wet from a sprinkler.

additional question I asked myself, sitting there with my mate going cold: what kind of 40-year-old do I want to be. I haven't asked myself that since I was probably 23. answer: I want to be the kind of 40-year-old who has shipped one really good thing every year, who can still kick at hip height (cleanly, without an icepack ritual after), who has read more than he's posted, who has cooked more than he's ordered, who has built something every year with his hands, who has called his parents on Sunday more than half of the Sundays in the year, who has not lost his ability to be surprised. some of that is sustainable. some of it requires me to start now.

I'm also realizing — sitting in Chapultepec, watching a couple in their late 60s feed the ducks together — that 'becoming the kind of person who builds with his hands' is a story I've been telling myself for at least 10 years and I am only just now actually buying the tools and making the cuts. that delay is data. it took mother-in-law dying, a thunder phobic dog, a Bogotá decline, a sparring back tweak, and a 70th birthday on the horizon to push me into the workshop. I am wired to need a forcing function. I should design more forcing functions for myself, deliberately, before life supplies them in the form of crises.

I'm closing the audit. The mate is cold. La Torta wants to go home. The duck colony is judging me. I get up.

had a pre-trip dinner with Camila at Páramo on Yucatán to lock in the Tijuana plan. she ordered a tlayuda for the table and I ate the half without queso, the half with extra avocado, no cilantro because I have the soap gene, and they actually accommodated when she asked nicely. she had a mezcalita Tobalá, I had agua de jamaica because tomorrow is Atlas Box day and I don't spar after mezcal, learned that the hard way 3 years ago when I sparred the morning after a mezcal night and Pablo dropped me with a low kick I should have seen from across the room. we made the final packing list for the drive. Camila is bringing her sketchbook because she's planning to draw the Sea of Cortez stretch from Hermosillo to Mexicali, she said the desert light there does something specific. I'm bringing the laptop in case the migration needs an emergency hand and 4 paperbacks because I refuse to work on a 14-day vacation. we went over Sofía's parents' names again — Don Ramón (retired truck dispatcher, big laugh, likes cumbia) and Doña Lupe (a different Lupe than abuela, this one is alive and runs the bakery El Volcán) — and Camila reminded me that Doña Lupe makes a famous pan dulce we should probably bring a small gift for. I suggested a bag of Café Cardinal beans. Camila approved. talked about whether we tell Mateo we're considering re-opening the kid conversation while we're up there. decided no. it's our conversation, not a family conference, and Mateo deserves an undiluted engagement glow. paid the bill, walked home along the median of Yucatán with our hands in each other's back pockets, the way we did our first month together back in 2021 in Oaxaca after the wedding. she leaned her head on my shoulder for the last block. Doña Marta's bread was in our hallway when we got home, slid under our door wrapped in wax paper, the way she does when we've been gone a while. I love her so much it hurts.

the dinner: tlayuda for the table, half-and-half on the queso, mezcal espadín for me (Vago, single shot, neat), Camila on a glass of natural wine. waiter knew us — we've been to Páramo enough that he asks about Nube. we don't bring her in but everyone knows about her. we walked back to Roma Norte through Parque México, took the back way past the pet store where we picked up the harness in month 7. mata-paloma fountains were on. the lights in the trees. CDMX is at its best on a Tuesday night. then the Doña Marta bread under the door. domestic sweetness. I love her so much it hurts.

summary turn for the month. closing month 12 mostly intact and starting month 13 with everything labeled in my head, which is rare for me at 1am the night before a road trip.

Bogotá: declined. Reasons: Camila's career here, Nube, rust mid-migration. Sent letter on Tuesday morning month 12. Roberto good about it, called me into a 1:1, I updated my Roberto-as-okay model further.

Mateo: engaged to Sofía. Tijuana trip planned for 5 weeks out, drive 4 days each way in La Torta, stop in Guadalajara both directions, Nube comes with the trazodone kit. mom is sending me bullet-pointed instructions about how to behave in front of Sofía.

Mom benign relief still on rolling discount, she's the most stable variable in my life right now and I'm grateful.

Camila's Pritzker mentor-nomination is producing portfolio sprints at her firm, she's working until 9pm three nights a week, eating spaghetti at 10:30pm, falling asleep with plates on her chest. she is full-shine right now and watching her in this is one of the great pleasures of being 33.

Nube: thunder phobia diagnosed by Dr. Acosta, system in place — trazodone, thunder shirt, bathroom safe-room, desensitization app. 4 storms in 12 days, system holding, fourth storm she went to the bathroom voluntarily before we even got the shirt out, which is the engineering equivalent of a service self-healing.

Rust migration: Tokio task-spawn leak found and fixed (mpsc bounded channel + bg consumer), Carla shipped the graceful-shutdown patch, p95 at 41ms, RSS flat over 24-hour soak. Roberto called me into a 1:1, was thoughtful, recalibrated my view of him.

body: back PT plateau at 50%, sleep 4-6 hours, insomnia coming. saving for Tijuana now — added 8k MXN line item to the spending tracker for the trip.

closing month 12. month 13 starts with the road trip. month 14 starts with the chess board project for Camila's dad's 70th, which I've already started sketching in my head — cedar, walnut squares, magnetic pieces, beeswax finish. that's a project for after Tijuana. for now: shoes off, Nube at my feet, Camila in the shower humming, the apartment quiet, the rain over for tonight.

last notes for the month — since I'm calling this turn the closer for month 12 and 13:

month 12: Bogotá declined, Camila Pritzker nominated, RouteOpt mpsc fix shipped (Andrés' work, Carla's review), back at 50% PT, mom stable, dad stable.

month 13 starting: Mateo engaged to Sofía, Tijuana trip planned in detail, Nube has thunder protocol with trazodone + thunder shirt + safe room + desensitization app, La Polar Friday noise still terrible but I've stopped writing letters about it, Doña Marta still bread-baking at 5:14am, Cardinal still my second living room.

tomorrow morning Tijuana drive prep — La Torta into the mechanic for service, brake check, alignment, fluid top-off. Wednesday Pablo confirms dog-sit. Thursday last-day-in-office before we leave Friday at dawn.

in the back of my mind: the chess board project for Camila's dad's 70th, which I've already started sketching in my head — cedar, walnut squares, magnetic pieces, beeswax finish. that's a project for after Tijuana. for now: shoes off, Nube at my feet, Camila in the shower humming, the apartment quiet, the rain over for tonight.

ok so I'm doing it. cedar chess board for Hugo's 70th birthday. Hugo Ferreira sr — Camila's dad — turns 70 in roughly 12 weeks. I've been thinking about a gift since the engagement got me thinking about family logistics, and the Tijuana trip last week locked it in because Mateo asked dad about chess pieces over post-dinner mezcal in Providencia and dad lit up the way he only does about chess and I realized, sitting at the table watching dad explain a Marshall Defense variation to Mateo who does not play chess, that Camila's dad also plays. Not at my dad's level — dad has played correspondence chess since the 80s, has a USCF rating archived from a tournament in '94, plays daily on lichess at a 1700 ELO — but Hugo plays. He learned at 14 from his civil-engineering uncle in São Paulo. He taught Camila when she was 8 and she remembers it the way some kids remember learning to ride a bike. The whole Ferreira side has chess history. Since Beatriz died at month 0 of this arc Hugo has been visiting us monthly from São Paulo, and every visit I've watched him sort of drift. He doesn't have the social rituals he had with her, the Tuesday-bridge group dissolved, his calendar is empty between bridge nights and Sunday lunches that no longer happen. Camila says he's been playing chess online a lot, against a Brazilian engineer friend who's also a widower. The board he uses is a beat-up plastic travel set with a magnetic snap closure. I want to make him a real board. cedar. walnut squares. magnetic pieces — magnetic because his hands shake slightly now and pieces tipping over makes him angry, he told me this in month 7 when he visited and I caught him cursing at the travel set. 20-inch, full-size tournament dimensions. beeswax finish, no varnish, because varnish is for furniture not for things you touch. I've never woodworked. I have 12 weeks. tomorrow I research routers. or saws. or both.

specs draft, before I forget the constraints: cedar base, 20-inch square (full-size tournament dimension is 20.5 inches but I'm sticking with 20 because Hugo has a smaller travel chess case and I want it to fit in the same protective sleeve). 22 x 22 inch outer dimension including the 1-inch border. 1-inch thickness for the slab — heavy enough to not slide on a table, thin enough to not be ridiculous. cedar for the base because Hugo grew up in a Brazilian region where cedro vermelho is the local woodworking wood and the smell will hit him in the gut.

walnut for the dark squares because walnut/cedar is a contrast palette I've seen on classic Brazilian boards and the walnut is dense enough to take a beeswax finish without splitting. inlay technique: route 6mm pockets into the cedar in a 8x8 grid using a Bosch trim router with a 1/4-inch shank straight bit, then drop walnut squares into each pocket and glue with PVA, then sand flush. I need a jig to keep the pockets identical — I'm going to build a 2x2 MDF jig with an internal guide that the router rides against.

magnets for the chess pieces because Hugo's hand tremor — his low-grade essential tremor that he's had since the 1990s — knocks pieces off boards he plays. I'm 3D-printing the piece bases with embedded N52 neodymium magnets, polarity-aligned, so the pieces snap to the board surface. I don't have a 3D printer; Pablo has one in his shop in Coyoacán and has agreed to print 32 piece bases for me at cost. the pieces themselves I'm buying from a chess supply shop, classic Staunton design, hollow bottoms that I'll fit the magnetic bases into.

beeswax finish, no varnish, food-safe, hand-rubbed. heat the wax with mineral oil 1:3 ratio, apply with cotton, buff with soft cloth, repeat 3 times. takes a week with cure times.

timeline: 12 weeks. week 1 wood acquisition. week 2 acclimation. weeks 3-4 milling and edge-glue base. weeks 5-6 walnut inlay routing and gluing. weeks 7-8 sanding and squaring. week 9 piece printing and assembly. weeks 10-11 finishing. week 12 box / packaging / pre-flight.

tomorrow I research routers. or saws. or both.

drove to Toluca yesterday for cedar planks. need to write this out because it was one of those days where the universe decides to be specific and you walk away with a person in your life you didn't have at breakfast.

pre-context: I've spent the last 8 days reading r/woodworking, watching Paul Sellers videos at 1.25x speed, reading 'The Anarchist's Workbench' by Christopher Schwarz on the recommendation of a Mastodon friend, and assembling a list of supplies. The wood is the foundation. I want cedar because (a) it smells like Hugo's home in São Paulo — his old neighborhood is full of peroba which has a similar aromatic quality, close enough to connect for someone with that olfactory memory, (b) it's stable enough for a chess board if you let it acclimate properly, and (c) it's sourceable in Mexico, specifically from the central highlands.

spent 3 evenings researching Mexico City wood suppliers. most of the CDMX shops carry pine, oak, mahogany, but the cedar selection is thin and the prices are absurd because they're catering to high-end carpinteros in San Ángel building dining tables for clients who do not flinch. Someone on the Mastodon woodworking thread, a Chilango carpenter who goes by @Tornillo, told me to drive to Toluca, specifically to Maderería El Roble in Lerma which is the Toluca metro area, and ask for Don Lupe. He said 'dile que vas de parte de El Tornillo, te va a dar precio justo'. I texted Pablo to come with me — he's a mechanical engineer, he loves a good road trip, also he knows wood from his motorcycle restoration projects where he's rebuilt a couple of café racers from the carb up.

picked Pablo up at 6:30am Saturday in Polanco. La Torta's tank full, thermos of mate on the dashboard, Spotify playlist that I had to lengthen because the drive is 75 minutes each way and the playlist had been built for a 35-minute commute. Mexico City to Toluca via the 15D — climbs 1000 meters in elevation, passes through pine forest, drops down into the Valle de Toluca with the volcanoes visible to the south on a clear morning. Cold. like 8°C cold at 7am. we get to Lerma at 7:50, Maderería El Roble is exactly where Google Maps says it is, off the carretera, in a sort of compound with high walls and a barn-style metal gate. There's a sign that says 'Especialidades en Madera Aromática' which is promising. It's open, against my expectations for 8am Saturday.

inside is a cathedral of wood. I'm not exaggerating. high ceilings, racks of planks 6 meters tall, cedar smell hitting you the moment you walk in like the church incense at midnight mass at Christmas. There's a man in his 60s in a flat cap and overalls drinking coffee out of a peltre cup, and he's clearly Don Lupe. I introduce myself in Spanish and say I'm a friend of @Tornillo's and I'm looking for cedar planks for a 20-inch chess board. he looks at me for about 8 seconds without saying anything, takes a sip of coffee, and says 'qué tipo de cedro'.

I am not prepared for this question. I say 'el normal'. Pablo audibly snorts. Don Lupe smiles slightly and says there are 4 species of cedro available — cedro rojo (Mexican red cedar, Cedrela odorata), cedro blanco (Spanish cedar, but the Mexican variety from Chihuahua), cedro de Oregón (Western Red, imported, expensive), and a Mediterranean cedar he occasionally gets that costs 4x. for a chess board he recommends cedro rojo because it's stable, it cuts cleanly, it has a beautiful warm reddish-brown grain, and importantly he says 'el cliente lo huele desde la sala', the recipient will smell it from across the room. that's the whole point. 'sí, cedro rojo' I say.

then Don Lupe asks me what grain orientation I want for the board — quartersawn, riftsawn, or flatsawn. I say I don't know. he sighs gently — not condescending, kind, like an elementary school teacher — and explains the difference. quartersawn is the most stable, doesn't cup or warp with humidity changes, gives the cleanest grain pattern (vertical lines through the thickness of the board), costs 30% more. riftsawn is a compromise, lines roughly 30-60 degrees through the thickness. flatsawn is cheap but warps in humid environments because the growth rings run roughly parallel to the face. I'm building this for São Paulo eventually (Hugo will travel back with the gift) and CDMX has 70% humidity in rainy season — he says quartersawn, no question. for a tournament-size board you want 2 planks of 22 x 4 x 1 inches, a little extra in case of mistakes.

he goes to the rack, pulls out two planks, plays them like dominoes on the central table — knock-knock, listening for soundness — and says 'estas dos'. they're perfect. tight grain, the dark heartwood lines almost parallel, no knots, no checks. then he adds a third plank as 'por si la riegas' which means 'in case you screw up' and I love him immediately. He also recommended a smaller block of walnut for the chess squares, because you typically inlay walnut squares onto the cedar base for the dark squares rather than try to do it all in cedar — the contrast is the whole point. picked walnut from his rack — Pablo helped me eyeball the grain match, holding the walnut block against the cedar planks under the skylight. total cost: 2,800 MXN. I expected 5,000+ in CDMX. paid in cash. Don Lupe gave us each a wedge of his wife's pan dulce 'para el camino' and shook my hand and said 'good luck with the gift, traélo cuando esté listo a que lo vea'. it was the kind of handshake where the older man holds slightly longer than you'd expect and you get the message: come back.

Pablo helped me wedge the planks into La Torta — they fit diagonally across the back seat with the seats folded. drove back. stopped at a roadside taco truck near Ocoyoacac for breakfast tacos (I had veggie nopal, Pablo had cochinita), got back to Roma Norte at 11:15. unloaded the planks into the apartment hallway. they have to acclimate to CDMX humidity for 2 weeks before I can mill them. Camila came home from yoga, walked into the hallway, smelled the cedar, and started crying because it smells like Hugo's apartment. she didn't expect it. she said 'lo va a romper, papá'. she's right. it's going to break him. I'm in. fully in. saving Don Lupe's number under 'Lupe Maderería' for the maple I'm going to need in 4 weeks for the chess pieces, return trip mandatory.

Don Lupe's full name is Guadalupe Cortez and he runs Maderería El Roble out of a corrugated-tin building 8km outside Toluca centro on the road to Metepec. I drove La Torta out at 7am, took the 15D toll, rolled in at 10:15. found him in the back lot of cedar planks stacked under a tin roof, wearing a denim apron over a flannel shirt and reading a battered copy of 'Cien Años de Soledad' on a folding chair next to a pile of lumber. I introduced myself. he asked what I was building. I said a chess board. he closed the book.

he asked who it was for. I said my girlfriend's father, turning 70, Brazilian, his wife died last year. Don Lupe stood up, walked into the cedar pile, ran his hand over a 12-foot quartersawn cedro rojo plank, said 'éste es el que va a llorar primero'. that's what he said. that exact phrase. 'this is the one that will cry first.' I didn't ask what he meant. he didn't explain. he pulled out 4 planks, all matched, all from the same tree which he confirmed by showing me the matching grain on the end-grain. asked the moisture content (he had a meter, used it, 9.2%, perfect for an indoor finished project after 2-3 weeks of acclimation in CDMX).
