The verbal forces you to reason about why the discipline exists, where it came from, what it means to take on the responsibility of becoming a teacher even informally. He doesn't say 'teacher' explicitly during the test but the questions are designed to surface whether you've thought about this stuff or just accumulated reps without reflection. I've thought about it. Pablo's thought about it more than I have. We've had hours of conversations about lineage and discipline and what we owe to the students younger than us, sitting at Cardinal café over cortados while Mariana the barista pretends not to listen but absolutely listens. The verbal will be the easiest section if I can stay calm. Calm under interrogation has never been my natural state. We'll see.

Profe also said something subtle that I want to capture: he doesn't post the test sequence ahead of time except for the testees themselves. The other students don't know the format until they see it executed. The reason, he says, is that the unknown sequence is part of why the test is hard — you can't pre-rehearse the energy distribution across the five sections if you don't know how they're ordered. Pablo asked once if he tweaks the order between cohorts. Profe smiled. That smile means yes, he does, and it's calibrated to each cohort's specific weaknesses. So our cohort will get a different sequence than the cohort before us. Probably ordered to test our specific tendencies. Mine: gas out under back-to-back sparring. Pablo's: rush combos. Iván's: lose composure under verbal pressure. The test will be tailored. Profe has watched us for years.

Camila pushed me to download a CBT-I app — Cognitive Behavioral Therapy for Insomnia, the structured program version. There are like four major ones in this space and I tried two over the last three weeks. Here's the honest review because someone is going to ask me about this in three months and I want a record.

App 1: CBT-i Coach (free, made by the US VA — the Veterans Affairs department, which I learned by reading the about page). Two weeks of use.

Clinical and dry. The UI looks like it was designed in 2015 and never updated, which I respect in a 'this is a tool, not a product' way. Lots of forms — daily sleep diary (asked at wake time), beliefs questionnaires (taken once at intake), pre-sleep routine checklists, mood ratings. The core technique is sleep restriction therapy, which is the thing where they tell you to deliberately spend LESS time in bed (like, 6 hours total) so you build sleep pressure across the day, then gradually increase the window as efficiency improves. The logic is sound — sleep is partly homeostatic, you build pressure during waking hours, more pressure means deeper and more consolidated sleep, deeper sleep should reduce mid-night wake events.

The execution destroyed me. The app calculated my sleep efficiency from the diary entries (time in bed vs time actually asleep, as estimated by my report) and decided my permitted sleep window was 11:30pm to 4:30am. Five hours total. For five days minimum. I felt like a zombie. Did I sleep more solidly during that 5-hour window? Yes, actually — fewer wake-ups, deeper REM by the diary's measure, the wakes I did have were shorter. Did I function during the day? Absolutely not. I almost rear-ended someone on Insurgentes Sur at 9am because my reaction time was shot. I made a regex error in a code review at work that Andrés caught and was too polite to mock me for. I was useless from 2pm onwards. After day five I bailed.

App 2: Sleepio (paid, ~$400/year, actually went through my employer's wellness benefit which I didn't know I had until Camila pushed me to look it up — Brújula's HR portal has a health benefits page I'd never opened). One week of use.

Prettier, gamified, has a virtual sleep coach character ('the Prof' — yes really, an animated white guy in a cardigan, slightly creepy mostly because his lips don't quite sync with the audio). Sessions are weekly, structured, more like a Coursera class than an app — you do a session, then practice for a week, then come back for the next session. The content overlaps heavily with CBT-i Coach but the framing is friendlier and the data viz is actually good. They show you sleep efficiency trends over time, correlate with your daily ratings, identify patterns. If I were a UX person I'd respect the design work.

Same restriction technique though, dressed up in nicer clothes. Same problem — I couldn't sustain the time-in-bed restriction with my actual life. I have kickboxing four nights a week, demanding job with on-call rotations, a girlfriend who I want to spend evenings with, a dog who needs morning walks regardless of how I slept. Telling me to be in bed by 12 and out at 5 sharp doesn't survive contact with reality. I lasted one week before I started lying to the app about my actual bedtime to avoid the restriction calculation pulling tighter.

The stimulus control bit is more useful in theory than in practice — get out of bed if you can't sleep within 20 minutes, do something boring in dim light, return to bed only when sleepy. The idea is to break the bed-as-anxiety-spot conditioning. Bed should mean sleep, not 'lying awake worrying about a load test from yesterday.' But at 3am when the wake hits, the LAST thing my body wants is to leave the warm bed and stand in the cold kitchen reading a paper book under a small lamp. So I lie there, breaking the rule, and then I'm adding guilt about breaking the rule on top of the original insomnia. Net negative on stress.

What actually has helped, modestly: yerba mate cutoff at 2pm (down from 5pm), no kickboxing later than 7pm (had to renegotiate with Profe to skip the late Tuesday class), hot shower right before bed, dimming the apartment lights from 9pm via the smart bulbs Camila installed last year. Boring stuff. The CBT-I apps probably work great for someone with more lifestyle flexibility than I have right now. For someone who can spend two weeks reorganizing their entire schedule around sleep restriction without other obligations colliding. For me, in this season, with a black belt test and a rust migration and a chess board and a girlfriend and a dog — didn't stick. Both apps deleted off my phone last week. Camila looked relieved when I told her, which is not the response I expected — I thought she'd be disappointed that I quit. She said 'I'm glad you tried, but you weren't actually going to stick with it.' She knows me better than I know myself.

I know you can't prescribe me anything. Just venting. The plan is now: traditional good sleep hygiene without the structured-program overhead, and revisit if it's still bad in two months. Post-test. Post-rollout. When the calendar opens up.

One specific thing I want to record about the Sleepio sessions because it might come up if I describe this to Dr. Tovar at the appointment Camila wants me to book. Session 3 of Sleepio focused on cognitive restructuring around sleep beliefs. The Prof character (cardigan, lip-sync issues) walks you through a list of common dysfunctional sleep beliefs — 'I need 8 hours or I can't function,' 'if I don't sleep tonight, tomorrow is ruined,' 'I'm a poor sleeper, this is my identity now' — and asks you to identify which ones you hold and rate their conviction on a 0-100 scale. I rated 'I need 7+ hours or my decision-making degrades meaningfully' at 75. The Prof's response was that this belief is not actually well-supported by the evidence, that decision-making is robust to a wider range of sleep durations than we think, and that the belief itself is doing harm by amplifying the consequences of a bad night.

I disagree with the Prof on this specific point. I have specific anecdotal evidence — the regex error after 5 hours of sleep, the near-miss on Insurgentes Sur, the times I've shipped buggy code on bad-sleep weeks and fine code on good-sleep weeks — that points to a real performance degradation correlated with sleep quantity below 6 hours. The evidence in my life doesn't support the Prof's claim that the belief is unfounded. He's giving me a generic CBT script that may not apply to high-cognitive-load workdays. Or maybe my belief is partially true but the catastrophizing version of it is what's harmful — i.e., needing 7 hours is reasonable, but treating 5 hours as a 'ruined day' instead of a 'suboptimal day with workarounds' is the actually-harmful framing. That's a softer version of the cognitive restructuring that I could agree with. The app didn't offer that nuance. It went straight from 'belief identified' to 'belief is wrong, here's the rebuttal.' Felt like being lectured by an algorithm.

Other thing I want to remember: the sleep diaries themselves were useful even when the apps weren't. Logging time-in-bed, time-asleep-by-estimate, wake count, mood-on-wake — that data is independently useful regardless of which app collected it. I now know that my actual time-asleep is consistently 75-80% of time-in-bed, which is below the CBT-I 'good' threshold of 85% and explains the persistent low-grade fatigue even on weeks where I'm in bed for 8 hours. The numbers were the value, not the algorithmic prescriptions on top. I'll keep a manual sleep log going forward — Camila bought me a small notebook for it that lives on the nightstand, paper and pencil version, no screen, with a column for hours, wake count, dream content if any, and a 1-5 mood rating in the morning. Lower-tech than the apps. Actually sustainable. Maybe that's the real conclusion: the apps were over-engineered for a problem that wanted a paper notebook and a doctor.

The other CBT-I claim I want to register a disagreement with: the idea that lying awake in bed is bad for sleep conditioning, and you should get up if you can't sleep within 20 minutes. The logic is that the bed should only be associated with sleep, not with frustration or wakefulness. So if you're not sleeping, you should leave the bed and do something boring elsewhere until you're sleepy. I get the logic. I disagree with the practical application for a couple of reasons. First, getting up at 3am to sit in a cold dark kitchen reading a paper book under a small lamp is, for me, not boring — it's stimulating, because the cold and the silence and the unusual hour create a distinct experience that my brain wakes up further to engage with. The conditioning that's supposed to weaken (bed = wakefulness) gets replaced by a different conditioning (3am = adventure), which I'd argue is worse. Second, the 20-minute timer is a hard threshold that doesn't fit how my body actually works. Some nights I lie awake for 25 minutes and then drift back to sleep effortlessly. The CBT-I rule would have me get up at minute 21 and miss the natural drift. Third, the implicit framing is that a wakeful 30 minutes in bed is wasted time. I disagree. I do some of my best mental synthesis lying still in the dark, half-asleep, where the brain runs in a different mode than fully awake. Some of my best ideas have come at 3am while ostensibly trying to sleep, including the architecture sketch for the rust migration which I worked out in my head one night in month 3 of prep before writing it down at 5am. Productive insomnia is real. I'm not going to optimize it away just because a generic CBT-I script says I should.

Carla pushed her synthetic spawn_blocking saturation test today and we found a real thing — at 600+ concurrent CPU jobs, the spawn_blocking pool starts reusing threads, and the OS context switching overhead becomes meaningful enough to show up in latency. Latency degrades sub-linearly but it does degrade — about 8% p99 increase per additional 100 concurrent blocking tasks beyond 600. Realistic load is nowhere near that — we'd need 600 simultaneous geometry decodes and our peak is 200ish — but Carla wants us to add a hard cap and a metric so if we ever cross it we know.

Done deal. Capped at 256 explicitly via `tokio::runtime::Builder::new_multi_thread().max_blocking_threads(256)` in our custom runtime construction. Metric exposed via tokio-metrics as `runtime.blocking.threads.active`. Alert wired up in our prometheus rules to fire if we cross 200 sustained for 5 minutes — that gives us early warning before we'd actually hit the cap, so we have time to investigate why our blocking workload doubled before it becomes a saturation problem.

Andrés watched her debug it. She had me share my screen, walked him through her diagnostic flow — start with metrics dashboards, narrow with logs, confirm with strace on a running pod, validate with re-run after the fix. Five steps, twenty minutes from 'I think there's something here' to 'here's the patch.' Andrés said afterward 'I feel like I just watched a magic trick.' Same. Carla is, in her quiet way, the best rust engineer I've ever worked with — she's not flashy, she doesn't post on Mastodon about it, she just consistently produces correct work and finds bugs other people miss. Andrés is going to learn more from her in three months than he learned from me in the previous nine. That's fine. I'm here for it. Senior leveling up junior is the most valuable thing on the team right now and I'm glad it's not all on me anymore.

Specifically the diagnostic flow Carla walked Andrés through, because I want to remember this for when I'm coaching juniors more directly: (1) form a hypothesis from metrics — what panel showed weird? what does the shape of the weird suggest? (2) narrow with logs — find the events corresponding to the weird, look at structure, is it bursty, sustained, periodic? (3) confirm the hypothesis by reading code along the path — does the code path match the hypothesis or contradict it? (4) reproduce the issue under controlled conditions — synthetic test that triggers the metric pattern on demand, (5) ship a patch with a measurement so you can verify the fix and detect regressions. Five steps. Standard discipline. Most engineers skip 1 and 4 and get burned by partial diagnoses or untested fixes. Carla never skips. She'd been awake six hours, just back from four months off, and she ran the full sequence cold without reference notes. Andrés was sitting next to me watching it unfold and I could see in his face the moment when he realized that Carla had been operating at this level for years. The look was a mix of admiration and 'I have so much to learn.' Both warranted. I felt the same way the first time I saw her debug something three years ago at her old company before she joined us, when I was interviewing her and she fixed a bug in our take-home assignment in real time during the conversation.

Carla also pulled an old story out from her last job, where she worked on a similar service that hit a similar runtime starvation pattern — except they discovered it in production at 3am during a Black Friday surge, with paged engineers awake all night. She said our discovery via pre-prod load testing is the version of this experience that doesn't ruin anyone's life. The 3am-prod version teaches you the same lesson with much higher cost. I've been there before too — the postgres outage was my version. The memory regression we caught earlier in this migration was another version, smaller scale. You'd think we'd learn faster. We do learn but the universe keeps providing new failure modes faster than we can learn the old ones. Anyway. The catch this time was cheap.

3:08am. Brain on rust. Going to write code instead of staring at the ceiling because at least if I'm coding the wakefulness is productive. There's a follow-up ticket I've been meaning to clear from the rollout backlog. Camila is asleep. Nube is asleep on my feet, snoring lightly. The apartment is quiet except for La Polar's last drunk customer arguing with a taxi driver outside. Roma Norte at 3am has a specific atmosphere I have come to know intimately.

Since I'm awake at 3am again — 3am thoughts, take them with the appropriate amount of salt and the assumption that anything I say tonight I will partly disagree with by 9am tomorrow when the sun is up and the brain has reset to its more reasonable defaults.

I keep wondering if the insomnia is a symptom or a cause. Like, am I sleeping badly because I'm stressed about the rust migration, the black belt test, the chess board, the MIT essay, all of it? Or is it that I've optimized so much of my waking life that my body has nowhere to put the tension except into sleep? Camila's friend who's a psychiatrist said something at a dinner months ago that stuck — that for high-functioning people, anxiety often presents as somatic stuff, not as obvious worry. Sleep, gut, jaw, headaches, weird tingly extremities. My jaw is fine — Camila checked, she's been a teeth-grinder herself for years and knows the signs. My gut is fine — no IBS, no reflux, no weird food sensitivities. Sleep is the canary. The body has chosen sleep as the place to surface whatever it is.

What's interesting is what I was sleeping fine THROUGH. I was sleeping fine through the postgres outage last year — 4-hour prod incident, I was on call, wrote the post-mortem, and I slept seven hours that Sunday night after it resolved. I was sleeping fine through Beatriz's funeral in São Paulo. Through dad's bypass surgery in 2022 when we genuinely thought he might not make it. Through the worst weeks of those events. So it's not stress in any obvious correlated-with-bad-events way. The insomnia started right around when the rust migration entered the home stretch and the black belt prep ramped to four days a week. Both things I want, intensely. Things I've been working toward for over a year. Not stress in the bad sense — anticipation? Excitement that doesn't have anywhere to land in the daytime so it shows up at night?

I also notice I'm not bored. Pre-arc, I had bored evenings. I'd put on a record, lie on the couch, watch the ceiling fan, let my brain rest in idle. Now there's always a thing — chess board sanding, code review for Andrés, sparring rounds, an MIT essay forming in my head that I haven't started writing, a podcast about distributed systems in the background while I clean the kitchen, vinyl shopping research for the next purchase. The 'never bored' might be the problem. Idle time is when the body recovers from the neurological accumulation of the day. I've crowded out the idle time in pursuit of being maximally engaged with the things I care about, and the body is presenting me with the bill at 3am.

The most useful framing I've heard is from a podcast (of course I heard it on a podcast, that's where ideas come from now) — insomnia is your body saying 'I'm not done with the day.' Whatever I haven't processed before bed, my brain comes back for at 3am to finish processing because it's the first quiet space available with no incoming signal. The CBT-I people would call this rumination but I don't know if that's quite right. It's more like — open tabs in a browser. Tasks that started during the day and never got closed out. So the brain comes back to them at 3am because there's quiet space and nothing else competing for cycles, and it grinds through the queue of unfinished mental processes.

If that framing is right, the fix isn't sleep technique — it's day-time technique. Close more tabs during the day. Have actual decompression rituals between work and home. Process emotional stuff out loud rather than in my head, which means more conversations with Camila about non-logistical things, which means dinners that aren't us both on our laptops at the table. The CBT-I apps might be solving the wrong problem for me, which would explain why they didn't stick — they assume the problem is at bedtime and try to engineer bedtime, when actually the problem is upstream during the daytime and bedtime is just the symptom site.

Alternatively, the framing is wrong, and I'm just a 33-year-old man with a chemical imbalance who needs to talk to a doctor about it instead of writing 3am philosophical posts and pretending self-analysis is the same as treatment. That's also possible. Probably both, in different proportions. Going to drink water and try to close some tabs. Not the browser tabs. The mental ones. The browser ones I'll keep open because right now they include three rust forum threads about runtime behavior that are genuinely useful for tomorrow's work, and I'm not closing those for any psychiatrist or any podcast. Priorities.

Other thing I keep noticing at 3am: I have stopped reading for pleasure. Not in a 'I have no time' way — I have time, I just don't pick up books anymore. The last novel I finished cover-to-cover was almost a year ago, when Camila and I went to Tulum for a weekend and I read a Bolaño on the beach because there was nothing to do and no wifi. Before that, I read constantly, half a book a week minimum. Now my reading is almost entirely technical — rust forum posts, papers on distributed systems, woodworking blog entries about grain orientation, kickboxing technique articles. All instrumental. All pointed at things I'm trying to do better. None of it for the pleasure of reading itself.

That might be the same shape as the not-being-bored thing. I've optimized away the slack in my mental life, and one of the casualties is fiction, which exists outside instrumentality. Fiction doesn't make you better at anything specific. It does something more diffuse — gives the brain a different kind of input than the optimization-shaped inputs of the rest of the day, lets it process narrative and emotion at a leisurely pace, lets characters exist in your head without you needing to extract value from them. I've cut that out. I cut it out without noticing.

Camila reads constantly. She always has. She has a book in her bag at all times, on the nightstand, on the kitchen counter, in the car. She reads in transit on the metrobus to her firm. She reads at restaurants while waiting for me to arrive when I'm late, which I often am. She reads Clarice Lispector and Jorge Luis Borges and contemporary Mexican writers I should be reading more of like Fernanda Melchor and Valeria Luiselli. I've been quietly noticing for months that her mind feels more spacious than mine these days. Less optimized, more permeable, more open to surprise. I think the reading is part of why. It's not the only reason — Camila is a different kind of person from me, fundamentally — but the reading is at least adjacent to the difference.

I'm going to start reading fiction again. Not as another optimization target — that would be missing the entire point. Just as a thing I do, for thirty minutes a day, before bed if I can. Camila has a stack of books she's recommended to me over the past year that I have not opened. The MIT essay can wait. The rust forum threads can wait. I'm going to start with the Lispector that's been on my bedside table for four months unread. If the insomnia is the body saying 'we have unfinished tabs,' maybe the answer is partly 'open a different kind of tab.' One that's allowed to stay open for a while. One that doesn't need to close out tonight.

Anyway. 4:11am now. The horizon is gray-pink at the bottom edge of the window. Nube is sleeping under the bed where she goes when I'm restless because she has decided that the under-bed darkness is more peaceful than the bedroom. Camila is sleeping with one arm out from under the blanket which is her tell that she's deeply asleep and warm enough. I'm going to put the phone down. I'm going to lie still for fifteen minutes without trying to sleep, just lying. If I sleep, I sleep. If I don't, I get up at 5 and have a slow morning. Either is fine.

I'm also going to register, for honesty's sake, that the 3am philosophical posts are themselves a coping mechanism that I'm not sure is healthy. Writing through the wakefulness gives the brain something to do other than spiral. It produces text that looks like productive synthesis but might just be the rumination wearing a more articulate costume. Camila wakes up at 6:30 and finds me having written 1500 words at 3:42am and she's torn between being impressed (the writing is sometimes good) and being concerned (I should be sleeping). I'm torn the same way. Tonight I'm going to write less and lie still more. I'm setting a 200-word limit on this entry and I'm going to honor it.

Counting now: this entry is already past 200 words including the meta-commentary about the 200-word limit, which is itself the 3am brain doing the thing where it converts every constraint into a meta-puzzle to be cleverly handled instead of obeyed. Camila would call this 'why you don't sleep, mi amor.' She'd be right. Phone going down. Eyes closing. Going to count breaths. 4 in, 4 hold, 4 out, 4 hold. We'll see.

The other 3am realization, less philosophical and more practical: I've been trying to optimize sleep with the same intensity I optimize everything else, and the optimization itself might be making the problem worse. Sleep is one of the few human functions that resists optimization because it requires letting go, and letting go is the opposite of trying. The harder I try to fix the sleep, the more I'm in the wrong mode for actually sleeping. Camila has been saying this for weeks and I've been receiving it as a soft suggestion rather than as the actual diagnosis. She's right. Going to read fiction tonight, drink chamomile, no melatonin, no app, no diary entry, no plan. Just lie down and see what happens. Engineering brain off.

Pablo and I started doing the verbal prep on Sunday afternoons over coffee at Cardinal. Going through the history of muay thai, the import to American kickboxing in the 70s and 80s, the Dutch styles that emerged from Mejiro Gym in Amsterdam in the 80s and 90s, the modern hybrid systems that mix all three lineages. Profe gave us a reading list which is basically four photocopied pages stapled together (the original copies are yellowing — Profe's been using the same handout for 20 years) and a Bruce Lee quote book his abuelo gave him in 1972 that he keeps in a plastic sleeve in his office at the gym.

We're memorizing the lineage of Atlas Box itself — Profe trained under a guy named Benjamín 'Benji' Solís in Tijuana in the late 80s, Benji trained under one of the Sityodtong fighters who came to LA in the early 80s and split off to form a small school in Otay Mesa, that fighter trained under the original Sityodtong line in Bangkok which traces back to Kru Yodtong Senanan in the mid-twentieth century. There will be a question about the lineage. There's always a question about the lineage. Profe likes to remind us that knowing where you come from is half of knowing where you're going, and at black belt we should be able to recite the names like family.

Pablo made flashcards. Of course Pablo made flashcards. He's a mechanical engineer, he's incapable of approaching any learning problem without a structured method. He's also made me a little study guide with bullet points organized by topic and historical period, which is both helpful and slightly insulting because he laid it out like I'm in fourth grade. I told him so. He said 'pero está funcionando, no?' He's right. It's working. I now know names of Dutch kickboxers from the 80s I'd never heard of two weeks ago — Rob Kaman, Ramon Dekkers, Ivan Hippolyte. Cardinal's barista Mariana started recognizing us as 'the kickboxing flashcard guys' which is a label I'm fine with.

The deeper thing the verbal prep is teaching me, which Profe probably planned, is that knowing the lineage forces you to take seriously the responsibility of being part of it. Saying 'I trained under Profe' is one thing. Saying 'I'm in the lineage that goes Sityodtong-Bangkok → LA Otay Mesa → Benji Solís Tijuana → Profe Hernández CDMX → Sebastián 2026' is a different thing entirely. The first is a fact about your training. The second is a position in a multi-generational continuity, with obligations attached. You're now responsible for the generation that comes after you to the same degree that the generations before you were responsible for you. Pablo and I have talked about this — he gets it instinctively, maybe more than I do. He says 'we're going to be the link to the next group of brown belts' and that's true and it changes the responsibility of showing up at the gym. We're not just training for ourselves anymore. We're training to be the people the next group of testees looks at when they're worried about their own test, the way Pablo and I looked at the black belts who tested before us five years ago and used their continued showing-up as evidence that you could do it and survive.

Mariana the barista at Cardinal made us a special drink last weekend that she called 'el pre-cinturón' — espresso with a shot of mezcal added, which is technically not on the menu but Cardinal's vibe is loose. She's been watching us drill flashcards for weeks and finally asked what it was for. Pablo gave her the dramatic explanation. She said 'wow' and made the drink. We tipped her well. The drink was actually good — the mezcal's smoke against the espresso's bitter — and Pablo decided he wants this to be the post-test drink, not the pre-test one. The post-test version we'll order regardless of how the test goes. Symbolic completion of the prep arc.

We rolled the multi-thread fix to staging tonight. Watching the metrics dashboard while eating leftover pasta Camila made on Sunday with her grandmother's recipe — she's been digging into Beatriz's old recipe cards lately, an unexpected way of staying connected. p95 is sitting beautifully at 38ms, p99 at 180ms even under the synthetic burst we injected this evening to verify the deployment. Tail latency is the cleanest it's been in months on this service. Carla said in slack 'this is the first time I've seen this dashboard not lie to me' and Andrés sent the laughing emoji eight times in succession, which is his version of whooping.

One small thing I want to remember: the rollout itself was completely uneventful. Helm chart updated with the new image tag, kubectl apply, watch the rolling restart, three pods cycle through over 90 seconds, traffic stays clean throughout because we have proper readiness probes that wait for the runtime to fully come up before accepting traffic. The boring rollout is the good rollout. Compared to the postgres outage of month 5 — which went sideways at every step, with rollback failing, then the rollback rollback failing, then a manual database surgery at 11pm — this was textbook. We've gotten better at this, slowly, deliberately, and I'm proud of the team for it. Andrés has been writing better deployment manifests than I did at his level. That's the way it should work.

Now tomorrow we plan the prod rollout phase 1. Which is going to be less boring because production traffic has shapes that staging can't reproduce. But for tonight — staging is green, the dashboard is gorgeous, Camila is on the couch reading a book by Clarice Lispector that her dad mailed her, and I'm going to pour myself one mezcal (Vago Espadín, of course) and call it a night. Tomorrow's problems can wait until tomorrow.

Andrés asked me today, while we were watching the dashboard together, when I knew it was going to work. I said 'when the trace logs showed the offending span name in the diagnostic test.' Three weeks ago. Once I had the offender named — `geometry::simplify::douglas_peucker` — and could see it correlate with the latency spikes, the rest was just engineering: pull the work out of the async path, wrap it in spawn_blocking, validate, ship. The 'aha' moment was the diagnosis, not the fix. He nodded and said 'I want to be at the level where I can do the diagnosis.' I told him 'you will be. you're already further than you were six months ago, and the only way to get further is more reps.' He said 'reps in what?' and I said 'reps in being wrong. you can't develop diagnostic intuition without being wrong a lot of times first and noticing the pattern.' He looked thoughtful. I think he got it. He'll be wrong a lot in the next year and he'll learn from it, which is the only path.

Carla gave Andrés the closing lesson at the end of the deploy: 'when the rollout is boring, that's when you've done the engineering correctly.' Andrés nodded and wrote it down. He's been keeping a notebook of Carla-isms over the past two weeks. I caught him doing it during a planning meeting. The notebook is a small leather one that he says his girlfriend gave him for his birthday. He's filling it with engineering wisdom from Carla. The next senior engineer at Brújula is going to be Andrés in a couple of years and the notebook is part of how he's getting there. I told him to also record what he disagrees with as he encounters it, not just what he agrees with — disagreements that you've thought through are also wisdom. He nodded, wrote that down, then immediately disagreed with it. Self-aware disagreement is actually the start. He's getting there.

Nube and I went for a 6:30am run in Parque México because I was up anyway, might as well make use of the wakefulness and get the dog her exercise. Mistake. Halfway through, around the second loop of the park's outer path, my hands started feeling weird. Numb is the wrong word — they felt like I was running through gel. Like the air was thicker around my fingers. Like the connection between my brain and my fingers was running on a delay. The Garmin showed heart rate at 168 which is high for me at conversational pace — should be 145 at this effort, 168 is threshold-zone for me. I stopped at a bench, drank water from the bottle in my running belt, sat for five minutes. Hands came back to normal. Heart rate dropped into the 110s. I continued the run at a much easier pace and it didn't recur.

I know exactly what this is. It's exhaustion. 4 hours of sleep last night, kickboxing yesterday evening (Wednesday class, light technique work, but still volume), the back already grumpy from the Saturday class earlier in the week, plus probably mild dehydration because I'd forgotten the bombilla cup at home and only had half a glass of water before heading out. Body sending a 'no más' signal through the extremities because they're the cheapest thing for the autonomic system to dial down — keep the core organs and brain prioritized, dial down peripheral perfusion to the hands and feet. That's the body talking when the deficit gets meaningful.

But still. New entry in my ongoing list of weird things my body is doing. The list now includes: 3am wake (consistent), persistent low-grade tension headache by Friday afternoon (most weeks now), weird tingly forearm thing during long compile cycles (probably positional, not neurological, but I should check), back that's at 50% and might never fully fully go away to baseline, occasional eye twitch in the left lower lid, and now hand-feels-numb during running. I'm 33. I'm supposed to be at peak. This is not peak. This is a body that's running on an over-leveraged budget.

Pablo would tell me to deload. Profe would tell me 'pero te falta solo un mes para el examen.' Camila would tell me to sleep, in that order of repetition. They're all right. Going to take Wednesday off the gym this week and use that evening to nap then sleep early. The one thing I'm not willing to deload is the rust work because we're at the finish line on staging and the prod rollout is starting next week, and the chess board because Hugo's birthday isn't moving and the inlay work is the most time-consuming phase still ahead.

I told Camila about the hands-numb thing tonight at dinner and she went quiet for a second before saying 'Seb, just go to the doctor.' Not 'see the PT' or 'rest more' — she escalated past the soft suggestions she usually makes and went straight to the medical-professional recommendation. That registered. She doesn't usually escalate. She trusts me to manage my own body within reason. The fact that she went to medical-professional means I've been pushing past her tolerance threshold and she's been quietly worried about more than just the sleep. I asked her what specifically she's worried about and she said 'about the cumulative load. I think you're stacking systems and one of them is going to fault.' She used the word fault, like a structural engineer. The architect in her is leaking through into how she thinks about my body. She's right. I am stacking systems — work, training, hobby project, family obligations, sleep deprivation, recovering injury — and the thing about stacking systems is that the failure mode isn't usually the system you'd predict. It's a low-probability tail event that hits one of the smaller systems first, and then the cascade brings down the rest. I am putting the appointment with Dr. Tovar on the calendar for next Friday. Camila is going to be at the appointment with me, which she suggested and which I didn't fight, because I know if I go alone I'll downplay the symptoms by 30%.

Profe pulled me aside after Wednesday class. He doesn't usually do this — he saves the individual feedback for during drills, in front of the group, where the lesson lands harder. He looked me in the eye, said three words: 'estás listo. solo no te lesiones.' Then he walked away to fix something on the heavy bag chain that he claimed was loose but was probably fine. That was the whole conversation. I'm going to carry that with me. He doesn't say things he doesn't mean.

5 hours last night. Best night in three weeks. Reclassifying as 'good night' because the bar is on the floor and I'm here for it. Camila said I look less hollow this morning. Nube didn't try to wake me up at 5am for once — I think she senses the household sleep economy and decided to grant me a pardon today, only one of us in the apartment can be perpetually awake at a time and she had her turn yesterday. Going to ride this micro-win all morning. Coffee tasted better. Standup was easier. The team noticed I was less zombified and Andrés made a joke about it that I caught instead of missing.

Putting together the staging-to-prod rollout plan with Carla and Andrés. We landed on a four-week phased rollout with shadow traffic for week one. Here's the plan I'm going to circulate to Roberto and the broader engineering org tomorrow morning, after one more pass with Carla.

Week 1 (shadow): all prod traffic mirrored to the new rust service via a shadow proxy that we built last quarter for exactly this kind of thing. New service responses are discarded — clients never see them, the proxy returns the Python service's response to the client in the foreground. We compare metrics between Python (live, in the foreground) and Rust (shadow, in the background) on identical input. We compare result correctness on a 1% sample by having an offline worker re-execute both Python and Rust paths against the same input later and diff the outputs. Acceptance criteria: <0.5% diff on dispatch decisions (some non-determinism is expected from random tie-breakers, see point 1 below), all latency SLOs met or beaten by the rust path, no error rate increase vs baseline. We'll publish a daily report with these numbers and the team will review at 11am every day.

Week 2 (1% real traffic): traffic split 99% Python / 1% Rust. The 1% is sticky-routed by customer ID so we don't have any single customer flapping between paths and getting confused. Manual canary review with Roberto and Carla daily — we run a 30-minute review meeting at 11am every day reviewing dashboards together. Rollback in <60s via traffic split flip — we have a feature flag wired up to do exactly that, validated in staging via simulated rollback. Acceptance: error rate matches Python within 0.1%, p95 within 5ms, no customer support tickets attributable to dispatch behavior.

Week 3 (50/50): even split. Customer-facing impact monitored carefully, both via metrics and via the customer success team's incoming ticket queue. The LATAM expansion launch is week 5 so we want to be confident before week 4 ramp. We'll watch the Mexican, Colombian, and Peruvian customers especially since the LATAM expansion will load them up and we want the rust path stable before that traffic increase hits. The Chilean retail chain in particular runs the most complex routes (22+ stops on a typical morning) and would be the first to notice any dispatch quality regression.

Week 4 (100% Rust): Python service kept warm but no traffic. After 7 days of clean run, Python decommissioned and only available as emergency rollback target. Then 30 days later we delete the Python repo and have a small ceremony with cake. (Carla says this is unprofessional. I'm doing it anyway.)

Three things I'm still chewing on:

1. The shadow comparison worker is non-trivial. We'd need to replay both pipelines deterministically against the same input. The dispatch heuristic has a randomized tie-breaker — non-deterministic by design, because we want load distribution across drivers when costs are equal so no single driver gets overloaded with the same kind of route every day. So we need to seed it for the comparison run, but not in prod (where we want the randomness to actually distribute load). The cleanest solution I've come up with: pass a `comparison_seed` header from the shadow proxy that both pipelines respect. Production traffic doesn't set the header, so the existing random behavior is preserved. The shadow path sets it (computed from a deterministic hash of the request ID), both Python and Rust use it to seed their RNGs. Same seed → same random tie-break → comparable output.

2. The 1% canary's error budget. Standard SLO is 99.9% success on this service. 1% of prod traffic is ~6k requests/min during peak hours. At 99.9% that's 6 errors/min as 'normal noise floor.' To detect a regression we need significantly more sensitivity than aggregate error rate. Carla suggests pulling per-customer error rates rather than aggregate, since the LATAM customers are the ones who'd notice first if dispatch decisions degrade — they have the most complex routes and the tightest delivery windows. Per-customer dashboards already exist; we just need to wire them into the canary review meeting and set per-customer alerting thresholds.

3. Rollback semantics on the runtime swap. If we rollback to Python after week 2 or week 3 of the rollout, we lose all the rust-only optimizations including the multi-thread runtime fix. The Python path was designed for lower throughput — Python prod was peaking at 600 RPS with 340ms p95 latency, and we've grown 40% in traffic since then. Need to confirm the Python path can handle current peak load, not just the load it handled when we ran it as primary. We haven't load-tested Python in months because we assumed it was a dead path.

Carla wants us to do a one-day load test of the Python path next week as insurance, even though the plan is to never actually use it again. Cheap insurance. I agree. If Python can't handle current load, then 'rollback to Python' isn't actually a viable option — and we should know that BEFORE we start the rollout, not during a 3am incident in week 2 when we discover the rollback target is also broken.

Thoughts on any of these? Especially the rollback load-test bit. Worth burning a half-day to validate? I think yes, but I want a sanity check from someone who's done these rollouts before. The good news: I trust Carla on this. Andrés is keeping up, asking good questions, taking ownership of the canary dashboards. And Roberto is being uncharacteristically hands-off, which I'll take as a sign that he trusts us to execute. Or that he's saving his political capital for the next quarter's planning fight. Either way, fine.

One more thing I want to capture about the rollout plan philosophy, because Andrés has been asking and I think writing it out helps me too. The four-week rollout is deliberately slow. We could go faster. We could go to 50% in week 2 and 100% in week 3 and save a week of calendar time. The reason we don't is that the cost of a slow rollout is a week of engineering time, which is cheap. The cost of a fast rollout that goes wrong is customer impact, which is expensive — both in revenue and in trust. The asymmetry between those costs justifies the slow pace by orders of magnitude. Even a 1% chance of a serious issue at 50% traffic in week 2 would, in expectation, cost more than the certain extra week of monitoring at lower traffic levels. Risk-adjusted cost calculation. I learned to think about rollouts this way after the postgres outage in month 5 — that incident burned ~$80k in customer credits and probably 2-3% of customer trust as measured by NPS the next quarter. After that I stopped trusting any rollout that hadn't been at least 30% as long as we could afford to make it. 

The other thing I want to keep in mind: a slow rollout buys you optionality. At week 2 you can decide to slow down further if you see something unusual, or you can speed up if the data is clean. The slow plan is the maximum-optionality plan. The fast plan is the bet-on-confidence plan, and confidence is worth less than optionality during a migration. We have one shot to do this rollout right. We don't get a redo if we burn the customer relationship by being too aggressive. So we go slow, we watch the dashboards, we let the data tell us when to ramp, and we let Roberto's increasingly impatient pings about 'when can we declare done' bounce off us until the data says we're ready. Roberto is not a fan of the slow approach. He's been ping me twice a week asking if we can compress weeks 3 and 4. I'm holding the line. Carla agrees with the slow approach which means I have political cover even if Roberto pushes harder. And Roberto, to his credit, has accepted the answer each time even when he doesn't love it. He may not love the speed but he respects the discipline.

Andrés asked an interesting question during today's planning meeting that I want to record: 'what's the success criterion for declaring the rollout done?' We hadn't formally written it down. We knew it implicitly — clean metrics for 7 days at 100% — but we hadn't made it explicit. Carla and I sat with it for a few minutes and came up with a more rigorous version: 'four consecutive weekdays at 100% Rust traffic with all the following true: error rate within 0.05% of Python baseline, p95 latency at or below Python baseline, p99 latency at or below 1.5x Python baseline (some tail variance is expected during a new system's first weeks), no customer-reported issues attributable to dispatch behavior, and no unexpected memory or CPU growth on the Rust pods over 96 hours of observation.' Putting that in the rollout doc tomorrow. Andrés has been better than I expected at asking the questions that surface the implicit assumptions. He's got a future as a senior. He's still got two or three years of work to get there but the trajectory is clear from where I'm sitting. Andrés asked tonight whether we should pre-write a rollback runbook before the rollout starts. Yes, obviously. We have one in draft. We're going to test the runbook in staging this week — actually execute the rollback on staging, time it, write down every step, identify which steps are slow or error-prone. The runbook tested cold is worth orders of magnitude more than a runbook drafted but never executed. Untested runbooks lie. Tested ones tell the truth.

Sparring tonight against a guy two weight classes above me. Profe likes putting the lighter fighters against bigger ones in prep — 'el peso real es el del miedo.' He's not entirely wrong, though he's not entirely right either; weight does matter when you're getting hit. Got tagged in the second round, clean cross to the temple, saw stars for half a second. Recovered, kept moving, finished the round without going down or getting tagged again. Pablo on the bench making a face like I should have ducked. Yes Pablo, I should have ducked. I will buy you a torta later for not yelling at me from the bench.

After class my ear is ringing slightly and I can feel a small lump on the side of my jaw where the cross landed. I held an ice pack to it on the drive home. Three weeks to the test. I need to not get knocked out before then. Profe says 'el cinturón se pierde si te lesionas, no si te pegan.' Belt is lost on injury, not on getting hit. Fair distinction — a blow you absorb is part of the test, a blow that takes you out of training for two weeks is what costs you the date. Going home now. Ice on the jaw for thirty more minutes. Camila will give me the 'I told you to wear headgear' look that I am going to receive with appropriate humility. Sleep early. No screens after 9 tonight, even though I want to read the latest tokio runtime PR thread.

Camila when I got home immediately spotted the side of my jaw and made me sit down on the couch while she got an ice pack from the freezer. She didn't yell. She didn't lecture. She just held the ice on the swollen spot for fifteen minutes and made me drink water, asked when I'd last eaten, told me to go take a hot shower and then a cold shower and then come back to the couch. Then she said 'three weeks. Don't get knocked out before three weeks. After three weeks, do whatever you want.' I told her that's the same advice I'm giving myself. She didn't smile because she was scared, but she didn't make a big thing of it either. Five years of dating a person who does an aggressive sport has calibrated her to not panic over the visible-but-recoverable bruise, while still letting me know with one well-placed sentence that she's pulled tight to the limit of her tolerance. The maturity of that response is something I notice and don't take for granted. A different partner would have escalated. She held the line.

Texted Profe before bed to let him know about the headblow and he sent back 'cabeza primero, ego después. mañana fácil.' Head first, ego second, easy day tomorrow. So tomorrow's training is going to be light. I'll do bag work at low intensity, no sparring. Form drills only. Profe doesn't actually want me to skip — he wants me to train smarter. Skipping a day at this stage of prep would be worse than light training, because the rhythm of showing up matters as much as the intensity of what you do when you're there. Same logic as the chess board work. Show up. Even if the work is small. The accumulation does the heavy lifting.

Pablo texted me later from the bench — he'd been watching, of course — saying 'cabrón, le agarras al peso, no al ego.' Take the weight, not the ego. Same thing Profe said but Pablo's version. They've been giving me the same advice in different words for weeks and I keep half-listening, which is why I got tagged today. Heard them more clearly tonight. Going to listen better next sparring. Three weeks is short. I cannot afford another one of these. The body math doesn't work.

Carla pushed back on the rollout plan today — wisely, as always. She wants to add a chaos engineering pass in week 1 alongside the shadow traffic. Specifically: kill one rust pod mid-burst and verify graceful failover. We have liveness probes but we've never actually killed a pod under load in staging, let alone in shadow against prod. Easy thing to skip, classic 'works on day one then bites you in production at 3am six months later when an actual node fails' pattern. She's right. Adding it to the plan. We'll do the chaos pass on day 4 of week 1 once we have a reliable shadow signal — that way if the chaos test surfaces a bug, we've already got 3 days of clean shadow data to compare against, and we know whether any new errors came from the chaos injection or the underlying behavior.

The specific scenarios she wants to test: (1) kill a single pod mid-request — verify the load balancer drains in-flight requests via SIGTERM grace period, ideally with no error returned to the client, (2) network partition between rust pods and Postgres for 5 seconds — verify the connection pool reconnects cleanly without crashing, sqlx should handle this but we've never verified, (3) Redis unavailable for 30 seconds — verify we degrade gracefully (cached lookups fail, fall back to Postgres, latency increases but errors stay near zero). These are good tests. We should have been doing this all along across our infra.

Brújula's chaos engineering practice has been... aspirational for two years. There's a Confluence page from 2024 outlining 'our chaos engineering plan' that nobody has executed on. This will be the first real exercise. Carla's making me write it up in a wiki post afterward so other teams can see what we did and copy the format. Fine. I'll write it. The post is going to be called 'How we broke our service on purpose and what we learned.'

I told Carla over coffee that the chaos engineering effort is overdue across the org, not just on RouteOpt. She agreed. She also said 'pick our battles, Seb' — meaning, we're not going to single-handedly fix the org's chaos engineering practice while also rolling out a critical migration. We do it for our service, document it well enough that other teams can copy the format, and trust the platform team or the SRE team to take it on across the org over the next few quarters. That's the right scope. Trying to do too much at once is how the rust migration goes sideways. I've been reading 'How to Be a Senior Engineer' content in my evenings over the past few months and the recurring theme is that the difference between senior and staff isn't technical depth, it's scope discipline — knowing what to take on and what to leave for someone else even when you'd be happy to take it all on. Carla operates at staff scope discipline naturally. I'm still learning. Watching her draw the line on the chaos work — 'we do this much, no more' — is part of how I learn it. Senior managers I've had in past jobs would have either let scope creep happen or pushed back too hard and damaged trust. Carla finds the middle. Documents what we did. Leaves the broader effort for someone else to pick up. That's the move.

Roberto reluctantly approved the chaos addition to the rollout plan after I framed it as risk reduction rather than scope expansion. The framing matters in management conversations. 'We're adding work' triggers the resource-protection reflex. 'We're reducing risk' triggers the responsibility-protection reflex. Same intervention, opposite reactions, depending on the framing. Carla taught me this years ago and it's been one of the most useful pieces of political-engineering wisdom I've absorbed. Andrés watched the conversation play out and took notes after.

Two weeks out from the test. I'm trying to sit with what this means, because if I don't sit with it now I'll be doing it during the test and that's a recipe for losing focus.

When I started kickboxing at Atlas Box I was 27 and had just moved to CDMX from Guadalajara, knew almost nobody in the city, and wanted a place to put the restless energy that comes with relocating to a city of 22 million people where you have no family within a 6-hour drive. The first week I was here I walked past Atlas Box on the way to Cardinal café — different errand, different reason — and saw guys hitting the heavy bag through the window and thought 'I can do that.' I couldn't, obviously, but I went in anyway. Profe took one look at me, told me my stance was a wreck, and put me through a beginner class so brutal I threw up in the parking lot afterward. I came back the next day, hung over from the soreness, and did it again. He told me later that's why he kept coaching me — most people who throw up don't come back. Most people fold the white belt and leave the gym and never return, and then six months later send a friend instead. I came back. He noticed.

White belt to yellow took 8 months. Yellow to green another year. Green to blue the same year and a half. Blue to brown was 18 months and that's when it stopped being something I 'did' and started being something I was. The kickboxing changed how I held my body. How I sat in chairs. How I walked into rooms — there's a forward-leaning awareness that the training builds, like you're constantly aware of the space around you and the angles into it. I didn't have it before, and now I do, and Camila has said — multiple times, in multiple contexts — that she can tell when I haven't trained for a week because my walk changes. My posture goes back to its pre-training default of slightly slouched, slightly disengaged, and she clocks it before I do.

Brown to black is supposed to be the longest gap and the most consequential. If I pass this test, the belt comes with a name on it from Atlas Box's lineage record, and the lineage record gets sent to Profe's old school in Tijuana, and from there to the Sityodtong line. There's a paper trail that goes back to Bangkok in the mid-twentieth century. My name will be on it. Which feels absurd to even type, like I'm overdramatizing my hobby for emotional effect, except that I'm not — that's literally how it works. Profe explained it to me once over a beer after a brown belt promotion ceremony, with the genuine pride of someone who knows his small school in Roma Norte is part of a longer historical thread.

What I keep telling myself: I'm not testing for the belt. I'm testing for the moment Profe hands it to me on the mat. That's the thing I want. The piece of cloth is just paper and embroidery and synthetic fiber. The moment is where the value is. The moment is where my mom and dad are in the chairs after flying in from Guadalajara, where Camila is standing at the back of the gym next to where she puts her bag for yoga on Sundays, where Pablo is holding his phone up to record because he insisted he'd record mine and I'd record his, and we'd have the videos for the rest of our lives to remember exactly what each other looked like at this specific moment of becoming.

Pablo's in the same boat — same cohort, same prep, same test date. He's been doing this longer than me — he started in Querétaro before he moved to CDMX five years ago. Brown belt happened for him three years ago. He's been at brown since because life kept getting in the way of the test — surgery on a torn ACL, then the pandemic delays, then a job change. Now we're testing together. He asked me last week if I'm scared. I said yeah. He said 'good. me too. me a lot.'

My mom is flying in. My dad. My brother Mateo and Sofía from Tijuana. Camila is organizing something at the apartment after — she keeps calling it 'a small thing' but I see her texting her friends about it and I see her writing a list on the fridge that includes a number followed by 'people' and the number is bigger than 'small.' I have no idea what she's planning and I love her for not telling me. The not-telling is itself part of the gift.

If I fail the test, I retest in three months. That's the rule at Atlas Box — Profe doesn't let you re-test the next month, you have to wait, you have to use the gap. The retest is calibrated to your weak points, so it's not actually the same test, it's a tailored version. So worst case, I fail and try again. But I don't want my family to have flown in for a fail. So I'm going to be obnoxiously prepared. Hence the four-day-a-week schedule. Hence the Saturday cruelty. Hence Pablo's flashcards. Hence the not-getting-knocked-out priority during sparring at any cost, even if it means dropping rounds I could have won by being more aggressive.

Anyway. Sentimental rambling on a Tuesday morning. Back to PRs. Carla is waiting on a review of the chaos test scenarios, and Andrés is going to Slack me at 9 sharp asking when the meeting is. I will pretend I have not been thinking about kickboxing for an hour.

There's one specific memory from the early years that I keep returning to this week and that I'll write down here because the writing-down is part of how I metabolize it. The summer of 2019 — second year at Atlas Box, blue belt, struggling with low kicks. Profe pulled me aside after a class where I'd been getting tagged on the lead leg by a smaller fighter and he wasn't able to point out the technical issue in real time, and he said 'son las caderas, no la pierna.' It's the hips, not the leg. He had me drill a hip-pivot drill for forty minutes, alone, on the heavy bag, while he watched from across the room without speaking. By the end my hips were bruised in the iliac crests from the pivot motion and my low kicks had a different snap to them than they'd ever had. He came over at the end and said 'ahora.' Now. That's what training with Profe is. He doesn't explain. He drills you until your body finds the answer, and then he gives you a one-word acknowledgment that the answer is found. That class was the moment I knew I'd stay at Atlas Box for as long as he was teaching there.

What's interesting is that I've had the equivalent experiences in software with senior engineers I've worked under. The architects in my early career who'd let me chase a wrong solution for three days, then ask one question that revealed the right solution, then say 'now you see it.' Same pattern. Same teaching technique. Same trust that the student will learn faster from finding the answer than from being told the answer. Profe and the best architects I've worked under share this pattern, which is interesting because they couldn't be more different on paper — Profe is a fifty-five-year-old kickboxing teacher in Roma Norte; the architects are mostly software engineers in their forties scattered across remote teams. But the teaching philosophy is identical. There's something in adult expertise transmission that's universal across domains, and I keep noticing it now.

If I become a black belt, I'll have absorbed two decades of Profe's teaching plus another decade of similar architect-teaching. I'll be carrying a synthesis of two adult-expertise lineages that I didn't ask for but received anyway. The least I can do with that synthesis is pass it forward — to Andrés at work, to Iván and the brown belts at the gym, to whoever asks for my time. Profe's speech the other day about the third-dan students who keep showing up — that's the model. You earn the belt. Then you spend the rest of your training career being the model for the next generation. The belt is the entry to that responsibility, not the conclusion of any individual journey. I think I'm starting to understand what he meant in a way I hadn't quite understood until I sat with it for a few days.

Final thing I want to write down today, while it's fresh: I asked Camila last night if she'd come with me to the gym for the test. I'd been afraid to ask because I didn't want her to feel obligated. She looked at me like I was an idiot and said 'of course I'm coming. Where else would I be?' I should have known. She's been at every brown belt promotion ceremony, every blue belt promotion. She knows what these moments mean to me. The asking was unnecessary. The asking was me processing my own anxiety about whether I deserved her presence. She doesn't operate on deserve-logic. She just shows up. Five years and I still get surprised by her steady presence in moments where I'm preparing myself for it to be conditional. It's not conditional. It hasn't been conditional. I should stop being surprised. But I'm still surprised, every time, and maybe the surprise is itself part of the love — recognizing the gift each time it's offered, instead of taking it for granted.

Three nights in a row of 6+ hours. The body is finally giving in. The wake-up shifted to 4:30am for two of those nights, and last night it was a full uninterrupted block from midnight to 6:15am. I'll take it as evidence the system can recover, even if I'm not sure what specifically helped. Probably the no-screens-after-10 thing combined with the post-stress relief from rust shipping to staging finally landing in the body.

Shadow rollout begins Monday. Week 1 of the four-week plan kicks off at 9am sharp and we'll watch the dashboards together as a team. Carla and I are pairing on the comparison worker today, working through the deterministic-replay problem we identified last week. Our solution: tag every prod request with a `comparison_seed` value in a header, propagate it through both Python and Rust paths, and have both paths use it to seed the dispatch tie-breaker RNG. The seed is computed by the shadow proxy from a deterministic hash of the request ID using SHA-256 truncated to 64 bits. Adds 8 bytes to the request payload. Nobody will notice in the wire-level metrics. Diff worker gets clean inputs that are reproducible after the fact if we ever need to investigate a specific comparison.

The header is set by the shadow proxy from a deterministic hash of the request ID, so we can reproduce any specific shadow comparison after the fact if we need to. Production traffic doesn't see the header — only shadow traffic does. The Python and Rust services check for the header on entry and seed their RNGs accordingly. If the header is absent (production traffic), they use the default seed-from-time behavior.

Done deal in code. Roberto signed off on the plan this morning, with one note ('please overcommunicate during week 1') that I'm choosing to read as supportive rather than suspicious. The chaos test scenarios are scheduled for Thursday of week 1, after we've collected three days of shadow data. Carla and Andrés are running point on chaos. I'm running point on the canary metrics. Nice clean ownership split — three engineers, three areas, no ambiguity about who gets paged for what. Going to a long lunch at Nudo on Álvaro Obregón because I haven't had ramen in two weeks and the body is asking for it specifically. They have a vegetarian shoyu that I order every time. The owner Yuko knows my order before I sit down.

The team chemistry is good. That's the thing that matters most heading into a migration, and it's the thing that's hardest to engineer for. Carla has always had the technical depth. Andrés has the eagerness and the willingness to take ownership. I have the historical context on the codebase and the relationships with stakeholders. The combination has been working better than the sum of the parts for the last month. Roberto noticed. He sent me a one-line slack today that said 'whatever you're doing with the team dynamic, keep doing it' which is the closest Roberto comes to praise. I'll take it. The team is going to be fine. The rollout is going to be fine. The remaining unknown is the prod traffic shape itself, which we'll discover Monday — and that's the only unknown we couldn't get rid of in advance, by definition. Some things only show up under real load. Monday is when we find out which ones.

After lunch I came back and the team was already gathered around Andrés's desk because something interesting had shown up in the Python baseline metrics — a 3% regression that Andrés had spotted while pulling baseline numbers. It turned out to be a benign deploy of an unrelated service that had increased Postgres replication lag slightly, which we'd otherwise have missed. The fact that Andrés found it independently while doing baseline data collection is exactly the kind of incidental win you get from a slow deliberate rollout. Going fast you'd never have noticed.

Last Saturday class before the test. Profe ran us through the full sequence as a dress rehearsal — forms, target practice, sparring, board breaks, verbal questions. Took two hours. I am wrecked in a way I haven't been since the brown belt prep a year and a half ago, the kind of wreckage where lifting your arm to drink water is a planning exercise.

The forms went best. I've drilled them so many times now that my body does the sequence before my mind catches up to the fact that the sequence has started. There's a moment in the third form where you transition from a side kick to a spinning back fist and the timing is tight — Profe used to call it the 'momentito de mierda' because that's where most students lose the rhythm; the spin is supposed to chain off the side kick's recoil but if you're a beat off it becomes two separate movements instead of one continuous flow, and you can see it clearly from outside even if it feels fine from inside. I hit it cleanly today. He nodded once. The forms are about muscle memory more than anything else, and muscle memory is just reps. I have done these reps. Hundreds of reps in this gym, then hundreds more in my apartment when I couldn't sleep at 5am. The body knows. The body knows even when the mind is foggy.

The target practice was middling. Eight combos on the heavy bag, called by Profe in random order. I rushed the third combo — left jab, right cross, left hook, right low kick — and threw the low kick before resetting my weight, which is the same mistake I always make on this combo and have been making for two years. Profe made me restart the whole sequence, which is what he'll do during the test, because the test is about composure under failure as much as it is about technical correctness. I restarted from combo 1. Did all eight, slower this time, with a deliberate pause after each combo before the next call. All clean. Profe said 'mejor, pero todavía rapido en algunos. piensa en el peso.' Better, but still rushing on some. Think about the weight. He means: think about your bodyweight, where it is, where it needs to be. Noted. Two weeks to drill this specific issue.

The sparring round — I went against Profe himself for the dress rehearsal, which is unusual; he usually saves his round for the actual test. He doesn't go full speed during prep but he doesn't go slow either. He read every kick I started to throw before it left the chamber. Counter-timing. Three minutes is a long time when you're chasing a guy who's already where you're going to be. I landed two clean strikes in three minutes. He told me afterward, 'two clean is more than most of the brown belts manage. Don't think about landing on me. Think about not getting hit by me. Different optimization function.' He's right. The black belt sparring round isn't about offense — it's about staying composed under pressure from someone better than you. Survive the round. Show you can think in there, can adjust, can take a hit and keep moving. Don't try to win. The win is the survival.

The board breaks were the surprise of the rehearsal. Three boards, three strikes. Elbow break worked first try — full power, board cracked clean down the score line, no resistance. Knee break — I bounced. The board absorbed the strike and didn't crack. For a horrible moment I thought I'd failed it and my mind started spiraling about the actual test. Profe had me reset and told me 'pegale al banco, no a la madera. la madera es transparente.' Translation: hit the bench underneath, not the board itself. The board is transparent. The trick is to aim through the board, not at it — your strike should be planning to end at a point on the bench below, with the board incidental to the trajectory. I did, on the second attempt, and the board snapped with a satisfying crack. Side kick break also first try, no problem — side kick is my strongest technique, has been for a year. So 2/3 first-try, 3/3 overall. I'll take it.

The verbal — Profe asked me three questions and I answered two of them well. The first was 'who taught your teacher.' I knew this cold — Benjamín 'Benji' Solís in Tijuana, late 80s, who trained under one of the original Sityodtong line via the LA branch in Otay Mesa. The second was 'what does the brown belt mean to you.' I said 'preparation,' Profe asked me to elaborate, I said 'preparation for becoming a teacher, even if you don't want to be one' — which is something he had said in a class six months ago about the brown belt being the moment when you start being responsible for other students even if you don't realize it, and apparently he liked that I'd remembered the framing and put it in my own words. The third was about the difference in stance between Dutch and traditional muay thai and I babbled. I knew the answer. I'd reviewed it with Pablo on Sunday using his flashcards. But under the pressure of the dress rehearsal, with the gym watching, I lost the structure of the answer and gave a meandering version that touched the right points but didn't organize them. Profe just said 'piensa en eso esta semana.' Think about that this week. I will. The answer is: Dutch stance is wider, hands lower, more forward pressure, evolved for dealing with low kicks and the low-kick-heavy Dutch competition rules; traditional muay thai stance is narrower, hands higher, more upright, evolved from the Thai context where hands shape clinch attacks differently. Three sentences, structured. I have the structure now. I just have to deliver it under pressure.

After the dress rehearsal, the seven of us testing sat in a circle in the gym and Profe did this little speech I'd never seen him do before in eight years of training there. He told us the test isn't a graduation — it's an entry. White belt to black is one journey, and black belt forward is a longer one. He said the people he respects most in the gym aren't the new black belts. They're the third-dan and fourth-dan students who keep showing up even though there's nothing left to prove and nothing forcing them to come. The ones who train because the training itself has become the point, not because they're trying to reach somewhere or earn something. The ones who teach by being there, not by being asked to.

He looked at each of us in the circle as he said this. When his eyes met mine he held the look for a second longer than the others, which I noticed and which I don't know what to make of. Maybe nothing. Maybe everything. Maybe he was just blinking slow. I'll let it sit and see what it means in retrospect.

I got home and Camila asked how it went. I told her, 'I think I'm ready.' First time I've said that out loud, to her or anyone else, in eighteen months of working toward this. She kissed my forehead and told me to take a hot shower and stop talking for thirty minutes. Two weeks. Then we'll see what the body and the mind can do under the real stakes.

The other thing I want to remember about today, while it's fresh: Pablo did not have a perfect rehearsal. He stumbled on his second form in the same place I would normally stumble — the side-kick-to-spinning-back-fist transition, the 'momentito de mierda' — and he had a worse target practice round than mine, missed a board on the elbow break and broke the knee board on the second try, and his sparring round against Profe was a one-strike-landed round (he'd told me before he was hoping for two). His verbal answers were stronger than mine, especially the lineage question which he answered with details I didn't even know — apparently Benji Solís had a younger brother also in the LA-Sityodtong line who later moved to Phoenix, which neither I nor Pablo had known until Pablo turned it up in research yesterday. He lit up when he got to share that detail.

I tell you this not because I'm comparing myself to Pablo but because it makes the test feel real to know that he's in the same vulnerable place I am. We're both showing up tired. We're both going to be imperfect. The test isn't about being perfect — it's about being prepared enough that imperfection doesn't unmake you. The black belt isn't given to fighters who don't make mistakes. It's given to fighters who make mistakes and recover from them within the same round. I think we both will. I think we both might not. That's the actual truth of it. Two weeks. Pablo and I are going to drill the third-form transition together every Sunday for the next two Sundays at the gym before classes start, because we both have the same gap. Solidarity in the gap is its own kind of preparation.

Pablo and I got coffee at Cardinal afterward, both still in our gym shorts, Mariana made us cortados without asking what we wanted because she has internalized our test-prep order. We sat in the back corner and didn't talk much for ten minutes. Then Pablo said 'la pasamos.' We made it through. He didn't say 'we'll pass the test' because that was tempting fate, he said 'we got through the dress rehearsal' which is the smaller, more honest claim. I said 'we got through.' That was the whole conversation. We finished the cortados, paid, and walked out. The wind was up on Álvaro Obregón. The jacarandas were starting to drop their last petals of the season. CDMX in late spring has a particular quality of light that I find hard to describe — softer than summer, more directed than winter, the city softer in outline somehow. We walked to where the cars were parked and went home.

Test is in 6 days. Mom's flight from GDL is booked for Friday morning. Dad is packing his bag two days early — peak Hugo behavior, he has done this for every family trip since I was a child. Mateo and Sofía drive down from Tijuana on Friday afternoon, will arrive late Friday night, and have promised to bring the salsa de chile de árbol from their favorite place in Tecate that Camila has been wanting for three months. La Torta will be parked in the underground spot for the whole weekend. The apartment is going to be loud. I am here for it.

Pre-test nerves manifesting as insomnia, predictably. 3am sharp again last night, like clockwork, like the body wanted me to know it remembered the schedule and was holding it for me even when I'd hoped the recent good nights meant the pattern had broken. Brain ran the third form sequence in a loop until 5am, debugging the spinning back fist transition I've drilled a thousand times and could probably do under anesthesia. Five days. Then either celebration or retest in three months. Probably celebration.

Pablo came over last night with a six-pack of agua mineral and his flashcards and we drilled the verbal questions for two hours straight at the kitchen table while Camila worked from the couch. By the end I could recite the Atlas Box lineage backwards starting from Profe and going back to Bangkok, name three Dutch kickboxers from the 80s who influenced the modern stance (Rob Kaman, Ramon Dekkers, Ivan Hippolyte — Pablo had me memorize their fight records too which I think is gratuitous but okay), and quote Bruce Lee's 'be water' bit in three different translations including the spanish one Profe prefers. He left around 11pm, gave me a hug at the door that lasted a beat longer than usual.

I lay on the couch for a while afterward thinking about how nobody outside the gym understands why we're doing this. To them it's just martial arts, just a hobby, just something Sebastián does on weeknights, just exercise with violence. To us — it's the room where we became different people over the course of years. The room where I went from someone who couldn't hold a stance for sixty seconds to someone who could spar three rounds with a guy two weight classes above and finish the rounds upright. That transformation didn't happen in any other room. Not at work, not at home, not at any of the schools I attended. The transformation is part of why we keep going back even when we're exhausted.

I tried to text Pablo to say something to that effect and instead I sent 'no te dejes pegar Pablo.' He sent back 'tampoco tu.' Same thing in different words. We don't say the sentimental version because we'd have to look each other in the eye after, and that's too much for either of us, both of us being middle children who learned to express affection sideways. The 'don't get hit' version carries the weight without the cost of articulating the actual feeling. He understands. I understand. That's the friendship.

The friendship with Pablo is one of the things I'd lose if I moved to Boston. I keep not saying that out loud because once it's said it becomes a thing the decision has to weigh.
