Pablo and I see each other three to four times a week between training and weekends and the occasional Sunday dinner with Camila and his girlfriend Renata. The frequency matters. Long-distance friendships are a different category of friendship — fine, sustaining, but not the same as the friend you spar with every Wednesday. If I go to Boston, the friendship survives in the long-distance form, but the every-Wednesday version dies. That's a real loss. I don't know how to weigh it against the MIT-shaped possibility yet. I'm going to write the essay first and see if the writing helps me figure out what I actually want, and if writing it makes me certain I want to apply, then I weigh the costs. Tonight isn't the night for that. Tonight is the night for the test in two days.

Sat on the couch a long time after Pablo left. Camila came over and put her head on my shoulder and asked what I was thinking about. I said 'the cohort.' She said 'tell me about Pablo' which is a thing she does when she wants me to talk about a person rather than a problem. So I told her about Pablo and Renata and how Renata called Camila this week to ask about post-test plans because she wanted to coordinate the apartment celebration without involving us. The two women have been quietly running a parallel logistics operation while Pablo and I drilled flashcards. Of course they have. They're better at the people-coordination than we are.

Mom and Dad arrived. Picked them up at AICM, drove La Torta back to Roma Norte through Friday rush traffic that took 75 minutes for what should be a 30-minute drive — classic CDMX, especially on a Friday afternoon when half of Polanco is also commuting south. Mom immediately started rearranging things in our kitchen — 'mi hijo, ¿cuándo fue la última vez que limpiaste detrás del refrigerador?' I haven't, mom. The answer is never. The refrigerator has lived against that wall the whole two and a half years and I have not moved it once in that time, and frankly I'm not sure the kitchen layout supports moving it without disconnecting the gas line. She decided this was her project for the weekend. By Saturday morning the area behind the fridge will be cleaner than the rest of the apartment.

Dad walked straight to my vinyl shelf and pulled out the Beny Moré con la Banda Gigante record I bought back in month 11 right after mom's lipoma scare resolved, and he looked at it like it was treasure. He said dad-of-mine things — 'esta sí es una buena prensa, ¿original?' He flipped it over to check the catalog number on the deadwax. He's owned probably eight different copies of this record over his life, in three different formats. Vinyl is the language he speaks fluently. We listened to side A together while mom continued the kitchen reorganization in the background, occasionally yelling that we should help. We did not help. The record played, dad's eyes closed at the third track, and for a few minutes the apartment had a different texture than usual.

They're both nervous about the test in the way only parents can be — pretending not to be while asking 50 oblique questions about Profe and the mat and 'is the floor padded?' Mom asked if I'd be wearing a helmet during the sparring round. I told her no. She did not love that. She started telling me about a nephew of a nurse colleague who got concussed playing basketball and I had to gently redirect to dinner plans. Mateo and Sofía arrive tomorrow night from Tijuana. The apartment is full and I'm not entirely mad about it.

Mom asked at dinner if Camila and I have talked about getting married. I gave her the practiced answer — 'we're moving in that direction, no specific timeline, we both want to.' Which is true and which I've said before to her at least four times in the past two years. She nodded and said 'no presiona, mijo, solo curiosa.' Not pressuring, just curious. Then dad changed the subject to the rust migration which he claims to understand the gist of even though he's a retired physics professor with no software background. He asks me about it the way he used to ask me about my school assignments — interested in the structure of the problem, not the technical details. He always says 'it sounds like an organizational problem disguised as a technical one' which is half true and which I appreciate him saying because he's right at a level that most software people miss. Mom was already half-asleep on the couch with Nube next to her by 10pm. CDMX altitude plus the flight plus the post-fight excitement of seeing me had wiped her out. I'm grateful they're here. I haven't seen them this much in person in two years.

Mom said before bed 'are you sleeping enough, Sebastián.' I said 'no, but it's getting better.' She said 'I'll make you eggs every morning while we're here.' She made me eggs every morning while she was here. By Sunday morning I'd had more eggs in three days than in the previous three weeks. The kitchen smelled like home. She made huevos a la mexicana the way she's made them since I was a kid. Camila watched her cook and asked for the technique. Mom showed her. Two generations of women feeding the man who can't sleep. I felt simultaneously infantilized and deeply loved. Both are valid. I ate the eggs.

Mateo and Sofía in. He hugged me too long at the door, the kind of hug where you can feel he's nervous about something he won't articulate. Sofía was carrying the Tecate salsa in a glass jar wrapped in a kitchen towel like a sacred relic. She handed it to Camila with both hands. Mateo wouldn't say it but he's nervous for me. Older brother in name only — emotionally he's the more sentimental one, always has been, and I'm not entirely sure he didn't cry on the drive down.

Test day morning. Woke up at 5:50am — which is to say, never really slept, just dozed in waves between 1am and 5:30 with brief stretches of unconscious that didn't add up to real sleep. Made yerba mate in the bombilla cup, the one Mama Lupita gave me back in 2017 with my name engraved on the rim. Drinking from it on test mornings is a ritual I started before the brown belt and I've kept it for every promotion since. Four cups of mate before 7am, all from the same cup. The cup gets a refill, never a swap. Continuity.

Camila was already up making me eggs even though I told her three times I couldn't eat anything. I forced down half a piece of pan dulce and a banana. She watched me eat the banana with the specific attention of someone trying not to say anything maternal but visibly wanting to. Drove to Atlas Box at 8 with Pablo riding shotgun in La Torta — he came over at 7:30 because he didn't want to drive himself, said he didn't trust his hands on the wheel that morning. We didn't talk much on the drive. Listened to a recording of Profe's brown belt ceremony speech from a year ago that Pablo had on his phone, more for the cadence of Profe's voice than the content.

Profe was already there sweeping the mat himself when we arrived, which is a thing he does on test days and that nobody else is allowed to help with. He looked up when we came in, saw us, gave one nod, kept sweeping. Pablo and I changed in the locker room without speaking. Sat on the bench together, wrapping our hands. Watched the rest of the gym arrive — the other five testees, the other students who'd come to spectate, parents and friends in the folding chairs. Mom and Dad came in at 8:45 — they sat in the third row of folding chairs near the side wall. Camila came in with Mateo and Sofía at 8:55. Iván arrived at 8:57, last of the testees, looking like he hadn't slept. Test starts at 9. Heart rate is 110 sitting still.

Pablo and I did our pre-test ritual at 8:50 — the one we'd developed during brown belt prep without ever discussing it formally. He puts his right hand on my shoulder, I put mine on his, we don't say anything for thirty seconds, then he says 'no te dejes pegar' and I say 'tampoco tu,' and that's the whole thing. Profe knows we do this. He pretends not to notice but I know he watches because I've caught him glance over once or twice from across the gym during similar rituals before brown belt tests. Probably his guys had similar rituals when he was testing decades ago. The unbroken thread of small private rituals that get inherited unconsciously. Ours is a copy of someone else's that we don't know we copied.

Iván came over after our ritual to wish us luck — first time he's been warm with us in weeks, the prep phase had made everyone competitive. Test-day brings out the cohort solidarity. We're not competing for limited belts. Profe will pass everyone he thinks is ready. He doesn't ration. Test-day is collective. Iván saying 'suerte, cabrones' means something different on test day than 'suerte' on a regular Tuesday. The cohort is together now. We rise or fall as one cohort, even though the test is individual. Profe nodded at all three of us together when Iván left to wrap his hands. Profe sees everything. Profe approves of cohort solidarity. The look held a beat longer than usual.

Profe walked over to where Pablo and I were sitting with our hands wrapped, looked at us for a beat, said 'están listos. solo no se lesionen.' You're ready. Just don't get hurt. Same thing he's said before every test for both of us. We nodded. He went back to sweeping. The familiar opening to a familiar ritual. Not familiar enough to be comfortable. Familiar enough to be navigable.

OK. I want to write this down before I forget any of it, while it's still fresh and the body still remembers the specific shape of the day. I'm sitting on the couch, Camila is in the shower washing the day off, Nube is asleep on my feet snoring lightly, the apartment smells like the empanadas mom brought from a place near AICM and the chiles toreados Sofía made from the Tecate salsa, and I have a black belt with my name embroidered on it folded on the coffee table next to my abuela's bombilla cup. Two objects from two different lineages, both heavy in different ways. Mama Lupita's kindness from 2017 in Guadalajara, before she died in 2019. Profe's eight-year coaching arc culminating today. Both objects sit on the entryway shelf usually. Tonight they're together on the table.

The test ran four hours. Seven of us went up. The order was set by Profe with no warning — he calls your name and you come up, no buffer, no second to compose yourself, no chance to shake out the nerves. I went third, after a guy named Iván who's been at brown for two years and was retesting from a previous attempt that hadn't gone his way, and before Pablo. Going third meant I had to watch two tests before mine, which Profe knows is harder than going first because anxiety has time to compound while you watch.

The forms section, I did them clean. The Mateo I know would have laughed at how mechanical I looked, like a robot programmed to do the sequence on rails, but mechanical was what I needed in that moment. Three forms in sequence. The third form has the spinning back fist transition I'd been dreaming about and rehearsing in my head at 3am for weeks, and I hit it on the count without thinking, body memory taking over before nerves could intervene and screw it up. Done. Profe nodded once. The nod is everything. Move on, sit down, let the next person go.

Target practice — eight combos on the heavy bag, full power, called in random order by Profe walking around the bag while I waited. I rushed combo five again, like in the dress rehearsal — same combo, same rush, same throwing the low kick before my weight had reset properly. The mistake I always make on that one combo. Profe didn't make me restart the whole sequence this time. He just said 'otra vez' for that one combo. I did it again, slower, deliberately resetting my weight, landed it cleanly. Combo six through eight clean. The bag took the abuse. My shins took the abuse. Profe nodded again. Move on.

Sparring was the hardest section. Three rounds. Three minutes each. The first guy was my size — Andrés Mejía, a quick fighter from the noon class, lefty stance which I always find awkward because the angles all flip. He tagged me twice early in the first round, both clean jabs to the cheekbone, the second one stinging more than the first because I'd seen it coming and not gotten my hands up in time. I adjusted in the second minute, started reading his timing, finished the round even on the unofficial scoring. The second guy was a bigger student from the noon class I don't usually see — a guy named Tomás, heavier hands than I expected, came forward aggressively from the bell. I caught one to the ribs that I'm going to feel tomorrow when I wake up, the kind that wraps around your ribcage and stays. I kept moving, threw the low kicks I'd been drilling for weeks, landed a couple of teep kicks (push kicks, lead leg, used to keep distance) that surprised him because he wasn't expecting that range from a smaller fighter. Both rounds I survived. No knockdowns either way.

Third round was Profe. Three minutes with Profe is the longest three minutes of my life, and I have done many long three-minute things in this gym. He didn't go full speed — never does, at his age and with his role he can't risk hurting students. He went the speed where he was always one step ahead of where I was. Every kick I started to throw, his guard was already adjusted. Every time I tried to set up a combo, he'd cut the angle off before I could finish the setup. I landed three clean — my pre-fight goal had been two — and didn't get knocked down, didn't get tagged hard, didn't lose my composure. When the bell rang he tapped me on the shoulder and said 'bien.' One word. I would do that round again every Saturday for the rest of my life for that word. Profe doesn't say bien lightly. Profe says bien when he means it.

Board breaks. Three boards on the floor, scored side up. Elbow first try — full power, board cracked clean down the score line on the first strike. Knee — bounced once, and for a horrible moment I thought I'd failed it, my mind started spiraling with the same fear I'd had in the dress rehearsal, but Profe nodded for a reset, told me 'pegale al banco, la madera es transparente' — hit the bench, the wood is transparent — and I aimed through the bench like he'd taught me, and the board snapped on the second attempt with a satisfying crack. Side kick first try, easiest of the three because side kick is my best technique and has been for a year. Three boards on the floor in three pieces. Profe nodded again.

Verbal. He asked me four questions, not three like in the rehearsal. The lineage of Atlas Box — knew it cold, recited it with the names and dates Pablo and I had drilled, going from Profe back to Benji Solís in Tijuana, back to the Otay Mesa Sityodtong branch, back to Bangkok and Kru Yodtong Senanan. The difference between Dutch and traditional muay thai stance — this time I had it, structured in three sentences exactly: wider base in Dutch, hands lower, more forward pressure, evolved for the low-kick-heavy Dutch competition rules; traditional muay thai is narrower, hands higher, more upright, evolved from the Thai clinch context. Profe nodded. What does the brown belt mean to you — I said 'preparation,' Profe said 'go on,' I said 'preparation for becoming a teacher, even if you don't want to be one,' and he nodded again because he liked that answer in the rehearsal too. And the surprise question — 'why do you train?' I almost said something glib. I almost said something about exercise and stress relief and the social aspect, the stuff people say when they don't want to say the actual thing. I didn't. I paused for what felt like a minute and was probably four seconds and I said because I needed a place where the parts of me that don't fit in offices or apartments or the rest of my life have somewhere to go, and because Profe is the first man other than my dad who has ever been straight with me about what I'm capable of and what I'm not. I almost cried saying it. The mat went very quiet. Mom was crying in the chairs already, I could tell from the way the silence had a shape. Profe just looked at me for a long moment and said 'siéntate.' Sit down.

After all seven finished, Profe had us line up at the front of the mat. He walked down the line slowly, unhurried, the way he walks during a class when he's deciding whose technique to correct first. Two passed brown to black today: me and Pablo. The other five he gave specific feedback to with retest dates — three months for some, six months for one, one year for another. Each kid took the news with a nod and 'gracias Profe.' Iván, who'd retested today after a previous fail, was passed up again — he'll go for it a third time in three months. He shook Profe's hand, said gracias, and stood at the end of the line with the composure of someone who had done this before and knew the drill.

Then Profe turned to Pablo and me. He walked back to the cabinet at the side of the gym, the one nobody but him is allowed to open, the one he keeps the test belts in, and he brought out two folded belts. Black, embroidered, our names along the tail in white thread.

He came to me first. Took the belt out of the fold deliberately. Said in front of everyone — Mom, Dad, Mateo, Sofía, Camila, the gym, the other students who'd come to watch, the parents and friends in the folding chairs — 'Sebastián Río, primer dan.' Then he wrapped the belt around my waist himself and tied it with the formal knot. He's done that for every black belt he's ever awarded in his career at Atlas Box. Twenty-something students over almost three decades. He hands them and ties them personally. He doesn't let you tie your own. The tying is the moment. I felt my hands shake at my sides while he tied, my body finally allowed to feel everything it had been holding back for the four hours of the test. His hands didn't shake at all. He's tied a lot of belts in his life.

Then he did Pablo. Pablo's voice broke when he said 'gracias Profe.' Profe pretended not to notice, but I saw his jaw tighten the way it does when he's holding something in. The whole gym applauded. Mom was crying audibly now, that specific Isabela cry that's mostly silent except for the occasional sniff. Dad was holding her hand and his face was doing that thing where he was trying to look stoic and was failing entirely. Mateo had his phone out filming the whole time, hand shaking just enough to make the video bumpy in a way that's going to make it impossible to edit cleanly but real in a way that no cleaner footage would be. Sofía was crying too — she barely knows me and she was crying. Camila just kept looking at me like she was memorizing the moment, neither smiling nor crying, just looking.

Pablo had had his phone propped up on the bench since the test started, recording the whole thing on a wide angle from a stable position. He'd set it up before the test like we'd discussed — his phone for my test, my phone for his, each of us recording the other's whole sequence so we'd have the videos forever. He showed me the recording later in the locker room — the tying of the belt, the look on my face, my mom's face in the background, Profe's hands working the knot, the whole gym applauding behind us. He said he's going to send it to Mateo for the family WhatsApp group and to my abuelos on dad's side who are too old to fly but who I know watched at home and would want to see it tonight. I haven't watched it through yet. I will tonight, after everyone leaves the apartment. I want to watch it alone, in the dark, with no distractions.

We took photos for an hour. The whole gym wanted in. People I'd trained with for years, some I'd just nodded at across the mat, all wanting a photo with the new black belts. Iván — who's retesting in three months — was the one who handed me water afterwards and said 'next time, hermano, contigo en el círculo.' Profe came over and shook my hand last, after everyone else had cleared and the mat was empty. He said 'tomorrow you train just like Monday. The belt doesn't change the work. It only marks the moment when the work begins seriously.' I nodded. He smiled — Profe smiled, which is its own rare event, you can count on one hand the number of times I've seen it across eight years — and walked away to start sweeping the mat again. The mat doesn't care about belts. The mat needs to be swept regardless.

The drive home I sat in the back of La Torta because I couldn't drive. Camila drove. Mom in the passenger seat, dad in the middle row with Mateo. Sofía followed in their rental car. We came home, Camila pulled out food she'd prepped the day before — vegetarian tamales because she remembered my dietary preferences six years ago and has never forgotten them, plus the empanadas mom had brought from the place near the airport, plus Sofía's chiles toreados — and friends started showing up around 4pm. Pablo. Andrés from work who I'd invited last week half-jokingly and who showed up early with a bottle of wine. Two of Camila's architect friends who'd never met me before but had heard the saga and wanted to meet the person it had happened to. Tono, the gym owner, came by for an hour and stayed two. Music — Beny Moré on the turntable because dad insisted, then Caetano Veloso, then Astor Piazzolla. The vinyl collection got a workout. Empanadas. Tamales. People. Nube was thrilled and confused, accepting pets from every stranger who knelt down to greet her, occasionally retreating to her bed under the kitchen table when overstimulated.

I'm sitting here now and the noise of the party has dissolved into Camila singing in the shower (Vinicius de Moraes, 'Insensatez') and the city outside the apartment doing its 11pm thing — La Polar's last call drift, a motorbike on Álvaro Obregón, somewhere a dog answering Nube's phantom bark. Black belt. Primer dan. My name on the belt in white thread. Profe's hands tying it in front of my mom. Pablo's phone in the corner of the room recording the whole thing for posterity.

I told Camila in the kitchen earlier, when she came back from saying goodbye to the last guests, 'thank you for the party.' She said 'it was Pablo's idea, mostly. I just executed.' I'll write something to Pablo tomorrow when I'm not exhausted. Right now I'm just going to let this sit. I think Mama Lupita would have liked this. I think she'd have made me a tamal and said 'mira, mi nieto.' She didn't get to see this. Dad got to see it. That counts for something. That counts for a lot.

Watched Pablo's video three times this morning before getting out of bed. Profe's hands tying the belt look like he's done it ten thousand times because he has, and there's a confidence to the gesture that the wide-angle camera somehow caught even from across the mat. The video is 47 minutes long unedited — Pablo had set it up before the test started — but the ten seconds of the belt-tying are what I keep replaying. Sent it to my abuelos in Guadalajara. To Tía Marta in Puebla. Already three voice notes back from the family group.

Took mom and dad to the Frida Kahlo museum yesterday. Dad insisted on paying for everyone's tickets — 'es un día especial, hijo' — even though it's a 90 peso ticket and the day was special for me, not for them, but you don't argue with Hugo about who pays for things. He's been buying tickets for everyone in the family his whole life and he's not stopping now. Mom kept stopping in front of every exhibit and tearing up which made me realize the tearing up wasn't about Frida specifically. It was about being in CDMX with her son who was now a black belt, in a museum with her husband who'd recovered from cardiac surgery four years ago and was here, walking, breathing, holding her hand and pointing at paintings.

We had lunch at El Bajío in Polanco after the museum. Mom ordered for me without asking, like always — sopa de tortilla without the chicken because I'm vegetarian and she remembered, enchiladas verdes with the queso fresco substitute, agua de jamaica. Dad and I split a sopa azteca and let mom carry the conversation the way she does when she's processing emotion sideways through small talk. He told me, somewhere between bites of soup, 'cuando te ataste el cinturón ayer, pensé en mi padre.' I asked why. He said 'because he never saw me become any of the things I became, he died in 1984 before I'd finished my master's, and yesterday I got to see you become this one.' I didn't know what to say. I think I touched his arm. We finished the soup in silence for a few minutes, and that was the right thing. He doesn't say sentimental things often. When he does, you let them sit, you don't try to respond in kind because you'll mess up the moment with words that don't match. The silence was the response.

After lunch we walked through Parque Lincoln and Hugo took my arm — physically took my arm, which he never does — and slowed our walking pace to half of mom and Sofía's. He wanted a private minute. He told me Camila is special and that I should not assume the relationship will keep working without effort just because it's been working without obvious effort for five years. He said relationships have a half-life that you have to actively counter. He said 'do you understand?' I said yes. He said 'I'm telling you this not because I think you're failing but because nobody told me when I was your age and your mother and I had a hard year in 2003 that I almost didn't recover from.' I had no idea about 2003. He said it had nothing to do with anyone else, just the inattention of a married couple in their early forties getting comfortable. They worked through it. He thinks they're stronger for it. But he wishes someone had warned him earlier. He's warning me now because he sees me doing the thing he was doing — letting work be the primary sender of meaning into the marriage and the relationship be the residual recipient. He doesn't have to use those words for me to understand them. I will think about this. I'll think about it tomorrow. Today I'm grateful he said it. Some advice you get only from the parent who's lived through the same mistake on a delay.

I've been carrying the conversation with Hugo around with me for three days. He's right and I've been doing the thing he warned me about. Work has been the primary sender of meaning into the relationship for months and Camila has been receiving it as the primary residual recipient. I'm going to do something about it. The Saturday no-obligations is one piece. There's a deeper piece about being more present at dinners, putting the phone face down, asking her about her day before she asks about mine. Easy to articulate. Hard to actually do. I'm going to try.

Pablo edited the test video into a 4-minute cut and sent it in the family WhatsApp group last night around 10pm. He cut my forms tight to a Beny Moré track because he knows me — specifically 'Bonito y Sabroso' which is one of dad's favorites, and which Pablo would have only known if he asked Camila, who he definitely asked because Pablo plans things. The whole family group is responding with crying emojis and clapping emojis and the praying-hands emoji that older relatives use when they don't know what else to send.

Tía Marisol who I haven't talked to in two years (since the family argument about whether her son should move to the US for university) sent a paragraph that began with 'mi hijo querido' which she has never called me before in my life, and which on any other occasion would have triggered a complicated response from me but tonight just made me feel grateful. My cousins from Veracruz sent voice notes — two minutes from Memo, three from Vero, both essentially saying 'we always knew you would do this' which is a lie because they did not always know but it's the kind of lie I will accept on a celebration day. Even my F1-obsessed cousin Carlos sent a message comparing me to Checo Pérez which is the highest praise in his vocabulary.

Mom forwarded the video to her old nursing buddies, women I've met maybe three times at quinceañeras and weddings throughout my childhood. They're sending heart emojis like I'm their own son. The video has done more PR for me in 12 hours than I did for myself in all of 2023. I'm sitting here a little embarrassed and a lot grateful. I texted Pablo 'hermano, te debo la vida' and he sent back '$200 USD por el video, pago en pesos.' He'll never let me actually pay him. The fee is a joke we'll keep using for the rest of our lives, the joke being one of those long-running jokes that gets funnier each time it's invoked because both parties know exactly what it means.

The thing nobody tells you about big life events is how much of the celebration happens in WhatsApp group chats afterward, asynchronously, with people you haven't seen in years suddenly showing up to celebrate. The pre-internet generation did this through letters and phone calls. We do it through emoji reactions and forwarded videos. Same energy, different channel. Mom is from a generation that wrote handwritten letters to congratulate births and graduations; my generation forwards Pablo's video and adds three crying emojis. The bandwidth of the reaction is lower per-message but the reach is bigger and the response time is faster. Different trade-offs. I'm not sure either is better — handwritten letters were more durable and more thoughtful, but they didn't reach Tía Marisol in the same six-hour window. The asynchronous reach of the WhatsApp version is what made the warmth feel widely distributed instead of locally concentrated. Both modalities have their place. Mom would still prefer letters.

Even Mateo's wedding-planning chaos — which has been a slow-motion train wreck of texts about seating charts and DJ negotiations — paused for the test. He sent the family group a 'no wedding talk this weekend, just Sebastián talk' and Sofía co-signed it. They've been in deep wedding logistics for three months. Pausing it for two days was not nothing. I appreciated it more than I told them. Family members who can pause their own thing to honor someone else's is a sign of the family being healthier than I sometimes give us credit for.

Mateo and Sofía stayed an extra two days after the test, which I hadn't planned for but am very glad about in retrospect. Mateo and I haven't spent this much continuous time together since I moved to CDMX two years ago, and most of our communication since then has been WhatsApp messages and the occasional phone call when something major happens. Two full days of being in the same physical space is a different mode entirely.

We took La Torta out to Tepoztlán on Saturday for a day trip — Sofía wanted to see the pyramid, mom and dad joined, Camila stayed home with Nube and a deadline at her firm she'd been pushing all week. The four of us drove out — Mateo riding shotgun because he claimed shotgun before mom could (she let him, knowing she'd lose), mom and Sofía in the back row chatting about wedding planning the whole way. Did the climb up the Tepozteco trail (mom and dad waited at the base because dad's heart and the altitude don't agree, and dad was good-humored about it, made a joke about being the basecamp manager). Ate quesadillas at the market afterward, took photos with the cliff in the background that mom is going to print and frame because that's what she does with photos.

Mateo and I ended up sitting on a stone wall behind the market while Sofía and mom and dad were in a tianguis stall buying handicrafts that mom would later try to give me as housewarming gifts even though we've lived in this apartment for two years. The wall faced the valley. The wind came up from below carrying the sound of the market and a ranchera from a speaker at one of the food stalls.

He asked me directly if I'm thinking about the engagement. Camila and I, the engagement, the next step. I said yes, sometimes. More than I admit out loud, even to Camila, even in the couples therapy sessions we did briefly back in month 7 that paused after three. He asked if Camila wants to. I said she's mentioned it more times than I've responded to her mentions, which is itself a kind of answer that we both know counts as a non-answer. He nodded.

Then he said something I didn't expect and didn't want to hear and needed to hear — 'no te apures por mi boda, hermano.' That if Camila and I were going to do something, we should do it because we wanted to, not because Mateo's October wedding in Tijuana was creating an artificial deadline that I had been treating as an unspoken pressure for months. He's two years younger than me and he was telling me, his older brother, to take my own time about the most personal thing in my life. The reversal felt right somehow. He's grown up faster than I've noticed.

We sat there for a while without talking. The wind came up the slope from the valley. A bird I couldn't name called from somewhere in the trees behind us, three notes descending. Then he said 'pero igual, sí casarte primero estaría chido.' Older-brother prerogative restored, which is also Mateo making sure he hasn't tilted the dynamic too far toward sentiment. We laughed.

Sofía is good for him. I've watched them this weekend more carefully than I usually do. She's calm in the way Mateo isn't — he's high-energy, talks fast, jumps from topic to topic mid-sentence, makes connections that are interesting but sometimes leave the listener three topics behind — and she takes his energy and gives it shape, slows it down without dulling it. She finishes his sentences when he loses the thread, in a way that doesn't feel like correction but like translation. They're getting married in October in Tijuana, three months from now. I'm best man — I haven't actually been formally asked but he keeps saying things like 'cuando hagas tu speech como padrino' and I'm not going to correct him. He'll get to it eventually. The fact that he hasn't formally asked me yet is a Mateo thing where he's nervous I'll say no, even though we both know I'll say yes immediately.

We drove back to CDMX in silence for the last hour, mom asleep in the back seat with her head against the window, dad reading on his phone, Sofía and Mateo holding hands across the middle row armrest. Mateo and I have always been quiet at the end of long days. It's a thing we got from dad, who was quiet at the end of his teaching days when we were kids and who taught us by example that some kinds of tiredness are best honored with silence rather than speech. The quiet between us isn't tense — it's the comfortable kind, where you've said enough already and the rest doesn't need to be said. La Torta hummed along the highway. The sun went down behind us as we came into the valley. We hit traffic at Cuernavaca like always. Mom woke up briefly, looked out the window, said 'qué bonito atardecer,' and went back to sleep.

That night when we got home and dad was settled with mom watching TV in the guest room, I went out on the apartment's small balcony with a glass of mezcal and the bombilla cup full of yerba mate (mate first, then mezcal — I don't mix them obviously). The balcony faces an interior courtyard, no view, just other apartment windows and a couple of jacaranda trees in the courtyard below that Mama Lupita would have liked. I sat there for maybe an hour thinking about Mateo's question about the engagement. Camila and I have been together for five years. We've talked about marriage in low-stakes ways — passing comments, jokes, what kind of wedding we'd want. We've never had the actual conversation about timing or commitment. The not-having-the-conversation has itself become a thing.

Mateo's right that I shouldn't make the decision based on his wedding. But Mateo's wrong if he thinks his October wedding hasn't been quietly working on me for months. Watching him plan it. Watching him commit to it. Watching him become a person who makes that kind of commitment. There's a thing where younger siblings make decisions and the older sibling has to either follow or feel slightly behind, and that thing is operating on me whether I want it to or not. I don't think it's bad. I think it's a useful pressure that's surfacing my actual readiness rather than constructing an artificial one. If Mateo's wedding is making me ready, that means I'm ready, just with Mateo as the catalyst. The catalyst doesn't have to be the cause. It can just be the trigger that surfaces something already there.

I texted Camila from the balcony — she was asleep on the couch — asking if she wanted to walk Nube together at 6:30am tomorrow before the family wakes up. She didn't see it until morning. She said yes. We walked. We didn't talk about engagement. But we were walking together with the dog at a private hour, and I had the thought again that walking the dog together at 6:30am in a quiet city is one of those small recurring moments that makes a relationship into a partnership. We've done that walk maybe four hundred times together. Each one is a small repeat. Each one is its own evidence. The pattern of small evidences is more meaningful than any single conversation. The conversation will happen. Probably soon. Soon enough that Mateo will see it before October if my mom hasn't already strangled both of us by then with her not-pressuring questions. The decision is forming. The articulation is the last step. I'm going to stop forcing it and let it happen.

The day after Tepoztlán Mateo and Sofía took mom and dad out to a brunch place near Coyoacán while Camila and I had a few hours to ourselves at the apartment, the first quiet hours we'd had since the family arrived three days earlier. We didn't do anything big. We made coffee, sat on the couch, talked about her firm's project deadline and about my prod rollout next week and about whether the Saturday no-obligations thing was going to be one Saturday or every Saturday for a few weeks. She voted for several Saturdays. I agreed. We're going to renegotiate the home-life baseline now that the test is over and the rollout is winding into its slow phase. Less hyper-scheduling, more spaciousness. She's been carrying more of the household logistics for the past two months than is fair. I'm going to take back some of that load — grocery runs, dog vet, the building maintenance issue with the elevator that's been pending for weeks. Domestic equilibrium reset. We don't formalize these things often, but when we do, we always feel better afterward. The conversation lasted maybe twenty minutes and changed the texture of the rest of the week.

Mateo and Sofía left Sunday afternoon to drive back to Tijuana — they were going to stop in Querétaro overnight to visit Sofía's aunt — and the goodbye at the apartment had the weight that family goodbyes have when you don't see each other often. Mom and dad stayed another two days. Mom continued the kitchen reorganization to its bitter end and is now back in Guadalajara having achieved her actual goal of the trip, which was apparently to clean my apartment. The apartment hasn't looked this good since before we moved in. Camila is mildly horrified and mildly grateful — she's been trying to clean it on her own schedule for months and mom did in 48 hours what Camila and I together hadn't accomplished in a year.

Few days out from the test now and I'm trying to figure out what changed. The belt is in a glass display case Camila bought before the test — she'd ordered it online at week 5 of prep, not telling me, on the assumption I'd pass. She said later she wanted to be ready either way, but she ordered the case sized specifically for the black belt, not the brown belt I was wearing at the time. Vote of confidence I didn't know she was placing. The case sits on the entryway shelf next to my abuela's bombilla cup. Two objects from two different lineages — Mama Lupita's kindness from 2017 in Guadalajara before she died, and Profe's eight-year coaching arc culminating in primer dan. Both heavy in different ways. I find myself glancing at the shelf when I come home from work and when I leave the apartment in the morning. I never used to glance at that shelf. Now it's a small ritual. The ritual is forming on its own without my deciding it should.

I showed up to the gym on Monday like Profe said. Trained like a Monday. He didn't treat me different and I was glad — anything else would have felt off. Iván, who's retesting in three months, was already back at it, drilling forms in the corner before class started. He asked me to drill the third form sequence with him after class because he wants my spinning back fist transition. He'd watched it land clean during my test and wanted to know what I was doing differently from his version. I'd never been the person another student asked for technical help. Now I am, apparently. That's the only thing that feels different inside the gym, and it surprised me — Profe had warned us in the speech about becoming teachers whether we wanted to or not, and apparently it starts immediately, on Monday, before I've had time to think of myself as someone who teaches.

The video. Pablo's phone video. I've watched it eleven times now in three days, which is more than I've watched any video except maybe the Senna documentary in 2010. It's not vanity — or maybe it is, partially, and I should be okay with that — but the thing I keep watching is Profe's hands. He ties the belt and his hands don't shake at all and there's this weight to the gesture that the camera somehow caught even at a wide angle from the bench across the mat. Twenty-something black belts in his career and his hands tie each one the same way, with the same authority, the same care for the knot. There's something in that I don't quite have words for. I don't know if it'll matter to anyone but me ten years from now, when the video has aged poorly and the audio has compression artifacts and the image quality looks dated. But it'll matter to me, and that's enough. I'll watch it on my fortieth birthday probably. Maybe on my fiftieth. The video will mean different things at different ages.

The mood in the apartment has been weird since the family left on Sunday. Camila is happy for me but she's also tired — she had to do a lot of the party logistics with my family in town and her own architect deadlines stacking up at the firm at the same time, and the asymmetry of who-was-celebrated wasn't lost on either of us. I owe her a clean weekend with no obligations. She mentioned wanting to do nothing this Saturday. We will do nothing this Saturday. I am going to make pancakes (her preferred kind, with the lemon zest, not my preferred kind with cornmeal) and we'll watch one of the trashy reality shows she likes that I pretend to dislike but actually enjoy because watching her laugh at the contestants' bad decisions is one of my favorite things in the world. Nube will sleep between us. I will not check Slack. The phone will be face-down. Whole-day commitment.

Work has been weirdly normal. The rust shadow rollout begins Monday. I came back to a clean inbox because Carla and Andrés handled everything during the days I was off. Andrés left a note in my Slack DMs that said 'enjoy the belt, jefe' which is the first time he's called me jefe and I'm pretending I didn't notice while also definitely noticing. Carla just sent the chaos test scenarios for review, no commentary on the belt because Carla is not a commentary-on-personal-events kind of person. Pure Carla. Roberto sent me a one-line congrats — 'felicitaciones por el cinturón Sebastián, listo para el lunes?' — that was so on-brand for him I laughed out loud at my desk and Andrés looked over.

The big thing nobody warned me about: the day after a goal you've worked toward for years, you're a little disoriented in a way that's hard to articulate to people who haven't done it. The fixed point in the future has dissolved. There's no Saturday cruel-classes anymore. There's no testing on the calendar. The thing I was sprinting toward is behind me, not ahead. I have to find the next thing or I'll feel hollow, which is a kind of hollow I haven't felt before because I've always had the next training cycle to point at. Some part of me knows the chess board is the next thing — Hugo's birthday is six weeks out, the inlay phase is up next, Camila's dad cries easy and I want to make him cry on his birthday in the good way. Some part of me knows MIT is the bigger next thing — the application I haven't started writing the personal statement for is sitting in my mind like a heavy object I keep walking past in the apartment, knowing I'll trip over it eventually if I don't move it.

But neither of those is the same shape as the test. The test had a date. The test had a binary outcome — pass or retest. The test was witnessed by people who flew in for it. The chess board is private, an offering between me and Hugo. The MIT app is uncertain — submission is months from result, result is months from any actual change. Different fuel sources entirely. I'm going to have to learn to run on those different fuels, because the test fuel — the calendar event with witnesses — is rare and I can't construct one for every project. Maybe Profe was talking about exactly this in his speech when he mentioned the third-dan and fourth-dan students who keep showing up. The fuel switches at some point from external to internal, and you either learn to run on internal fuel or you stop showing up.

Profe told me on the way out Monday — 'el primer dan no es la cumbre. Es la base.' First dan isn't the peak. It's the base. Five more dans above me potentially. I have years of training ahead. The belt is just a marker that the real work has now started, in the same way that getting hired at your first real job isn't the achievement, it's the entry to the actual work. I think I knew that going in. I think I needed Profe to say it out loud anyway, the way some things only become real when someone you respect names them.

Anyway. Sentimental rambling on a Wednesday morning before standup. Going to bed at a reasonable hour tonight. Insomnia has been better since the test — the body finally relaxed, the cortisol surge that was hitting at 3am has been lighter. I forgot what 7 hours of sleep felt like. Not bad. Going to keep this up, at least until the next thing arrives.

One specific thing I want to write down before it fades: I noticed during Monday's training that I was less anxious about getting hit. Not less attentive — I'm not stupid — but less anxious. Some of the latent test-related tension had bled out of my sparring. I was looser. My defense was more economical. My counter-strikes happened earlier and with less wind-up. Profe noticed and pulled me aside after class to say 'estás más relajado, sigue así.' You're more relaxed, keep it up. The relaxation is the dividend of the test being behind me. The body had been holding tension for months without my noticing the daily cost. The cost was visible in retrospect now that it's gone. I wonder how much better my training will be over the next year now that the body isn't running on test-anticipation for the first time in eighteen months. The next test (segundo dan) is at minimum two years away under Atlas Box rules. So I have a long stretch of training without an external goal pressing on me, and I get to discover what my training looks like in that mode. Profe says it's where the real learning happens — when there's no test in front of you and you're training because the training is the thing. We'll see if I'm built for that mode or if I need a goal in front of me to stay sharp.

Camila ordered a small framed print of one of the photos from the test — the one Pablo took right after Profe handed me the belt, where I'm looking at Profe rather than at the camera and Profe's hand is still on my shoulder. She had it framed at a place near our apartment in Roma Norte, small black wooden frame, simple matting. It sits on the entryway shelf next to the display case for the belt. When you walk into the apartment now, the first thing you see is that shelf. The bombilla cup, the belt, the framed photo of the moment of receiving the belt. Three objects in a row. I keep thinking about how those three objects map onto the three lineages I'm part of — Mama Lupita's family lineage from Guadalajara through to Mexico City, Profe's eight-year coaching arc through Atlas Box, and the moment itself which is the present-tense version of both of those threads converging in a public ceremony. The shelf is a small altar of sorts, though I don't say that out loud because I don't want to dramatize it. Camila understands without me saying it. She sees me touching the shelf as I leave for work and she doesn't comment. The not-commenting is a kind of understanding. Camila looked at the framed photo this morning and said 'qué guapo cuando ganas.' How handsome you look when you win. I told her I look the same when I lose. She said 'no, but I love you for not knowing the difference.' That's going on the t-shirt next to Profe's other lines.

Got back to the chess board today. Hadn't touched it in two weeks because of test prep and family in town. The cedar has settled in the apartment well — no movement that I can see, no visible warping, no joint creep on the squares I'd already cut and laminated. Small mercy. I was half-expecting to come back to a disaster after two weeks of CDMX humidity swings (the building's old radiators do weird things to ambient moisture in the apartment), and instead I came back to wood that had behaved itself. Wood is a more stable material than I'd given it credit for, when you set it up correctly. The hard part is the setup.

Started on the walnut inlay today, which is the part I've been quietly dreading since I started this project because there's no margin for error on the inlay. The walnut is more expensive per square inch than the cedar, and the visual appeal of the whole board depends on the contrast of the dark walnut squares against the warm cedar substrate. One bad cut on a walnut piece and I'm driving back to El Roble in Toluca for more, two hours each way through Friday traffic, and Camila will not be amused at another solo-Sebastián vinyl-listening road trip in La Torta this month, especially not so soon after promising her the no-obligations Saturday.

Spent two hours just measuring and re-measuring before making a single cut, drawing on the walnut with a marking knife along a steel ruler so the lines are sharp and consistent rather than a soft pencil mark that compresses under the chisel. The Veritas chisel I sharpened over the weekend on the diamond stones is taking nice clean shavings, the kind that curl off the edge in continuous ribbons that stay together rather than splintering or breaking. The shop floor at the Roma Norte maker space is now cedar shavings and walnut chips and one stray Nube hair from when she came to visit last week and tried to eat a cedar shaving before I caught her. Hugo's birthday is in 6 weeks. I think I can finish. I have to. The deadline is non-negotiable in a way work deadlines aren't.

The maker space was emptier than usual today. The other regulars were on their own projects in the corner — one guy doing a turned-bowl on the lathe, another doing some carving work with a chisel set I didn't recognize, a couple working together on a small cabinet — and the room had the focused-quiet that good shops have when everyone is in their own task. I'd missed the room for two weeks. The room is part of the work. The other woodworkers don't talk to me about my project but they nod when I come in and nod when I leave, and that's the right level of community for what I want from the room. Not friends. Not strangers. Co-occupants of a shared space who recognize that we're all there for the same reason — to make something that will outlast the day's labor. Hugo's birthday in 6 weeks. I left at 9pm with the first walnut piece roughed out and clamped under cauls to settle overnight. Tomorrow morning I'll cut the second piece. The pace is established.

Tomorrow I'm doing the second walnut piece. Maker space at 7pm after dinner, three hours of cutting and shaping with no music in the headphones because the work needs the sound of the tools — the saw, the chisel, the occasional curse — to focus. Hugo's birthday in 6 weeks. The pacing is fine. The pacing is also non-negotiable. The board has to be done.

Three days in a row of 6+ hours of sleep. Body is finally catching up to the absence of test pressure. The 3am wake hasn't completely stopped — last night I woke at 3:50am and lay there for fifteen minutes — but I rolled over and was back asleep within those fifteen minutes, which has been impossible for months. The wake is shorter and shallower now, more like a brief surfacing than a full wake. Whatever was driving it isn't driving as hard now that the test is behind me.

Back is at maybe 60% — best it's been since month 10 when I tweaked it sparring during the Tijuana-belt-incident week. PT exercises continuing but I've cut down to twice a week from three because Dra. Reyes thinks the active load from kickboxing post-test will be lower for a couple of months and we can use that window to consolidate gains rather than just maintain. She says we can probably wean off PT entirely in another month if I keep up the home routine — single-leg bridges, dead bugs, the resistance band exercise I still hate, the cat-cow stretches. The home routine takes 12 minutes morning and 12 minutes evening and I've gotten consistent enough about it that the body now expects it.

Camila says I look less haunted in the morning. I had not realized I looked haunted in the morning. The body remembers stress even when the mind has moved on, apparently — for weeks the mind was telling me 'we're fine, the test is going well' and meanwhile my face was telling Camila something else entirely at 6am over coffee. She didn't say anything at the time because she didn't want to add to the stress by pointing it out. Now that the stress is receding she can mention it as a past-tense observation. I'll take her observation as data. The body knows. The face knows. The closest observer knows.

What I want to track, methodically: the sleep is improving but the underlying causes I identified at 3am — the chronic-stress framing, the never-bored thing, the optimization of slack out of my mental life — those haven't gone away. The test pressure released and the symptom subsided, which is consistent with the chronic-stress hypothesis but doesn't prove it. The next stress event will be a test of whether I've changed anything structural or just had a temporary reprieve. The MIT essay is still ahead. The chess board deadline is closing in. The rust prod rollout is in a critical week. Camila's deadline at her firm is in two weeks. So I have a stress portfolio of medium-grade items lining up for the next month. If the sleep stays good through that, I'll consider it real recovery. If it goes back to 3am wakes when the next deadline gets close, then the test was just the surface symptom and the underlying pattern is still there. Going to keep the sleep notebook. Will know in three weeks.

The thing I'm watching most carefully is whether I can hold this sleep recovery while the rust prod rollout is in its critical window. The rollout starts Monday. If I can sleep through the rollout's most stressful days, that's actual data about whether my body has reset. If the 3am wake comes back the night before week 2 of the rollout when we're moving from 1% to 50% traffic, then I'll know the test was just the surface and the underlying stress response is unchanged. Empirical experiment running. Will know within three weeks.

Shadow rollout day 1. Watching the dashboard with the kind of attention reserved for childbirth. Carla is on a video call from her desk at home (her partner has the baby this morning), Andrés is in the office bullpen with two monitors set up specifically for the rollout, I'm in the corner office with two screens — one for Grafana with the latency and error rate panels pinned, one for the diff worker output streaming the per-request comparison results in a tail -f window. Roberto is in his office across the hall pretending to do other work and refreshing the rollout page every two minutes. He came over once already at 11am to ask 'is it working?' and I said yes and he said 'good' and walked back. Twice more before lunch. By 2pm he'd settled and stopped coming over. Healthy detachment achieved.

Summary so far, four hours in:

- 100% of prod traffic mirrored successfully via the shadow proxy. No origin server impact (the shadow proxy buffers and fires async with a bounded queue, exactly as designed). Zero customer-visible latency increase. The mirror itself is invisible to clients.
- Rust pod CPU steady at 28% under our normal Tuesday afternoon load. Memory flat over four hours, no growth visible in the heap RSS metric, no growth in the active-tasks gauge from tokio-metrics. The multi-thread runtime is humming without complaint.
- p50 / p95 / p99 on rust path matching staging numbers within 2ms across the board. Beautiful. The metrics chart is so clean it looks fake — flat lines where production usually has texture, because the rust path is consistently faster than any tail load on Python.
- Diff worker is reporting 99.7% match rate on dispatch decisions. The 0.3% delta is exclusively in the multi-stop optimization where Python and Rust break ties differently. Carla's now wondering if our seed-based determinism has a bug. We're investigating in parallel with the rollout, not blocking on it.
- Healthz under shadow load is returning in <1ms consistently, exactly like staging — which is itself a meaningful signal because healthz behavior under real prod load shape was the failure mode we'd discovered a month ago and it has stayed clean throughout four hours of real traffic.

One genuinely interesting catch: at the 90-minute mark we saw a single rust pod's task count climb to 4x baseline for about 8 seconds — went from ~120 active tasks to ~500. No latency impact (stayed within p99 SLO), no errors, no memory spike. Just a visible spike on the tokio-metrics task gauge that I noticed because I happened to be looking at that panel.

Carla dug in immediately, the way only Carla does — pulled the corresponding logs by timestamp, joined them against the request log, identified the customer ID. Turned out to be one of our enterprise customers (a Mexican retail chain whose name I won't say in chat for confidentiality) that fired 2k requests in a single batch at us, which is well within their rate limit but unusual at that concentration. Their batch processor must have flushed all at once, probably a deployment or a scheduled job kicking off. Rust handled it elegantly. Multi-thread runtime ate the burst, scheduled the work across all 4 worker threads, and went back to baseline in under 10 seconds. Healthz never blipped. The same burst on the old current_thread setup would have starved the runtime — exactly the failure mode we hit in load test back in month 14. So we already have a real-world receipt for the runtime fix on day one of shadow, with real customer traffic. Carla and I high-fived over slack with the high-five emoji which is the closest we get to celebration during work hours.

The diff-worker investigation: I'm pretty sure the 0.3% mismatch is real, not a determinism bug in our seed propagation. The Python tie-breaker uses Python's `random.choice` with a seeded `random.Random` instance. The Rust side uses `rand::rngs::SmallRng::seed_from_u64`. Both seeded with the comparison_seed header that the shadow proxy sets. But the underlying PRNGs are different algorithms — Python uses Mersenne Twister (MT19937), SmallRng uses xoshiro128++. Same seed, different output. So even with identical seeds, the random number streams diverge after the first call, and any time the dispatch heuristic hits a tie that needs a coin flip, the two implementations may flip differently.

Embarrassing that we didn't catch this in staging — we caught it on day one of shadow because the diff worker ran against thousands of real requests with realistic tie distributions, instead of the synthetic test cases we'd built for staging which had less ambiguous inputs and rarely triggered tie-breaks.

We can't make these match without either (a) reimplementing Mersenne Twister in rust on the dispatch path, which is a non-trivial cost both in implementation effort and in runtime overhead since MT is slower than xoshiro, or (b) accepting the 0.3% delta as 'expected non-determinism, both decisions equally valid by the dispatch heuristic.' We're going with (b) and documenting it in the rollout runbook. Andrés is going to write the explainer for the broader engineering team so nobody panics when they see the diff number in the daily shadow report and assumes there's a bug.

Carla also flagged something subtle that I would have missed: the diff distribution within the 0.3% mismatch is uniform across customers and request shapes, which is what you'd expect from a tie-break difference — random distribution across customers. If it were a logic bug we'd expect it to cluster around specific request shapes or customer IDs (like 'all customers with >20 stops' or 'all requests with backhaul flag set'). So the uniformity is itself evidence that the 0.3% is genuinely just RNG drift, not a real divergence in dispatch quality. I'm going to add this observation to the runbook explainer because it's the kind of subtle reasoning that other teams can borrow when they do similar comparisons.

Going to keep watching the dashboard for another two hours then break for the 7pm sparring session at Atlas Box. First training session as a black belt. I keep forgetting and then remembering and grinning at the screen like an idiot. Carla noticed the third time and asked what I was grinning at. I told her. She said 'felicidades, ahora vuelve al trabajo.' Congratulations, now back to work. Same thing Profe would have said in the same circumstance. I love working with adults who can compress an emotion and a redirect into one sentence.

End of day stats, for the daily report I have to send Roberto and the engineering org tomorrow morning:

- Total mirrored requests: 8.4M over 9 hours.
- Diff worker comparisons completed: 84,000 (1% sample as planned).
- Match rate on dispatch decisions: 99.7% (consistent with the early-day number).
- p50 / p95 / p99 on rust path: 22ms / 71ms / 142ms.
- p50 / p95 / p99 on python path (live, baseline): 28ms / 119ms / 287ms.
- Error rate on rust path: 0.011% (one bad request from a malformed customer payload that we'll handle in the runbook).
- Error rate on python path (live): 0.034%.
- Memory footprint per rust pod: stable at 380MB across all three pods.
- CPU utilization per rust pod: 24-31% across the day, peaking at 31% during the 2pm secondary dispatch window.

The numbers are better than I expected. Significantly better on the rust path across every percentile. We knew rust would be faster, but the absolute deltas — 6ms on p50, 48ms on p95, 145ms on p99 — are bigger than the staging numbers predicted. Production traffic shape must be more pathological than our staging synthetic load. Possibly the geometry decode CPU work, which was the thing that triggered runtime starvation in the first place, has more variance in real customer data than we were modeling. The rust path is handling that variance much more gracefully than Python ever did. That gracefully is going to translate into lower P99 outliers for customers, which is going to show up in their internal dashboards as 'dispatch is faster' which is the kind of thing that gets noticed even when nobody's looking specifically for it.

Closing the laptop now. Going to Atlas Box. First night training as a black belt. Profe will treat me like a regular Monday because he's already told me he will, and that's right. The belt is on the wall at home in Camila's display case. The work continues. The rollout continues tomorrow. Every metric pointing the right direction. Cautious optimism. The kind of cautious optimism that earned a high-five emoji from Carla, which is the highest possible cautious-optimism celebration in our team. I'll take it.

Carla mentioned in our evening call that she was proud of how the team executed today. Carla doesn't say things like 'proud' easily. The compliment landed. I told her 'we built this together over months and the rollout is the test of whether we built it right' and she said 'yeah, we built it right, and the rollout is showing it.' Andrés saw the slack thread later and added a fire emoji which is his version of clapping. Roberto saw the same thread and replied 'felicitaciones, mañana otro día.' Congrats, tomorrow another day. He's right. The rollout has 27 more days to run. Today was day 1. We can't celebrate yet because the work isn't done. But we can register that day 1 went well and use that as fuel for the days that come. Tomorrow's plan: validate the day-one numbers continue to hold, run the chaos pod-kill at noon, watch healthz behavior under a kill, write the chaos test up for the wiki Carla wants. Going home now. Camila is making pasta. The dog needs a walk. Day one done.

OK, two days post-test and the dust is finally settling enough to write something coherent. Belt ceremony was Saturday at Atlas Box CDMX, the gym in Colonia Juárez, and against everything I told myself I would be cool about — I was not cool about it. Profe Esteban tied the black belt himself. He's been my coach for eleven and a half years, and I have wanted this thing for so long that the actual moment of receiving it was disorienting, like the script in my head and the experience in the room weren't lining up. Big news first: my whole family flew in for it. Like, my whole family. Mom flew in from Guadalajara on Friday afternoon, and I picked her up at AICM in the rain — she got off the plane in her good shoes carrying a small duffel and a box of conchas from the panadería near her clinic, because she cannot show up empty-handed even when I told her three times the gym would feed everyone afterward. Dad flew in Saturday morning at 7am with an extra suitcase that I correctly suspected was full of physics books for me to take back to the apartment, even though I have not asked him for any physics books in approximately a decade. Mateo and Sofía drove down from Tijuana, which is twenty-eight hours of highway with a 26-week-pregnant Sofía in the front seat — Mateo says she insisted, says she was not going to miss it for anything, says the doctor cleared her for road travel as long as they stopped every two hours, which is the only reason I let it happen without arguing. They got in Friday night and slept at our apartment in Roma Norte, and Sofía and Camila spent two hours catching up at the kitchen table while Nube did her best blue-heeler impression of being personally responsible for both of them. The ceremony itself: forty-three people in the gym, mats cleared, all the colored belts arranged behind Profe in order. I was at the front. My mom sat in the chair Profe brought out specifically for her — she had told him at some point that she has a back thing — and Camila and dad and Mateo and Sofía and Pablo and Pablo's wife Renata stood behind her. Profe said three sentences about the test the previous Saturday, then said one sentence about the eleven years, then said one more sentence about the kind of student that doesn't quit when the season is hard. He tied the belt. The whole thing took maybe four minutes. The clapping was loud enough that I heard it from inside my own head, like through a thick wall, because my ears were doing the closing-up thing they do when something is too much. Then Profe did the part I had not expected. He walked over to my mom's chair, leaned down so his face was at her level, and said in his low voice in Spanish — which my mom doesn't fully share with him, since he's from Veracruz and she's tapatía, but they made it work — that he wanted to thank her personally for the eleven years of letting me drive an hour each way three times a week to the gym, even when I was a teenager and probably had homework I should have been doing. He said the kid he met at age twenty-one had been raised right. My mom started crying immediately. My dad did the dad thing where he put his hand on her shoulder and just kept it there, like a stabilizer. I did not look at any of this directly because if I had I would have lost it. Afterwards we went to the place on Álvaro Obregón that does the pasta, the one Camila and I take everyone to. Mom sat next to Sofía and they spent half the dinner talking about names and pediatric stuff that I half-listened to. Mateo and dad got into a long argument about something in physics that I did not follow. Pablo gave a small speech about how he met me on day one of jiu-jitsu eleven years ago and never thought I'd outlast him at it. Renata cried. Camila held my hand under the table the whole way through. I had two glasses of wine and stopped because I wanted to actually remember the night, and then I had a third because Mateo poured it for me and I couldn't say no. We got home at almost midnight. Nube met everyone at the door for the second time that day. My mom slept on the pull-out couch. My dad slept on an air mattress in the office because he refuses to take a real bed when offered. Mateo and Sofía got the guest room. Camila and I got our actual bed. The apartment had seven people in it for one night and it felt like a small wedding. The next morning my mom made huevos divorciados for everyone in our small kitchen, ordering me around like I was twelve, and dad and Mateo took Nube out for the long walk to Parque México while Camila and Sofía and I prepped coffee. They flew out separately on Sunday afternoon — mom and dad together back to Guadalajara, Mateo and Sofía driving back to Tijuana with strict instructions to call when they got past Hermosillo. The apartment got very quiet again. Camila and I sat on the couch and didn't say much for a while. The belt is still in the gym bag, which is in the corner of the bedroom, where it goes. I keep almost going to look at it and then deciding I don't need to right now. It's there. It's there. Pablo texted asking if I want to climb at La Cueva on Saturday morning. I said yes. I always say yes to climbing because I always need it more than I think I do. Nube is curled up on the rug behind me and every fifteen minutes she does the dramatic-sigh thing that blue heelers do when they think you should be paying attention to them. I made yerba mate at midnight and Camila made fun of me for it, said it would keep me up. She was right. It always keeps me up. I drink it anyway. Profe Esteban texted me a video of one of the new white belts trying to do a half guard. Took me back. Took me back so far I had to put the phone down for a second. Camila played a Spinetta record on the new turntable while we cooked, and for a moment the apartment felt like it was set in 1976 Buenos Aires. Dad has been re-reading Feynman over the holidays, and he sent me a long voice note about the spinning plate observation as if I hadn't heard it from him five times before. I listened anyway. We watched twenty minutes of a documentary about Argentine asados and Camila got homesick in real time, then we ordered takeout from the place on Álvaro Obregón to compensate. Mom called from Guadalajara, she's still working at the pediatric clinic three days a week, and she asked if I'm sleeping. I lied. I always lie about that with her. Camila got home around eight, kicked her shoes off at the door, and asked what I'd eaten — I had to admit it was just a banana and coffee, and she laughed at me but in the way that means she'll cook. Grandma Lupita's birthday would have been Monday — five years gone now — and I made her champurrado recipe from memory because mom is too sad to do it lately. Profe stopped me after class to ask how the family visit went, and I told him truthfully — best three days I've had in a year. Pablo and I went to the rooftop tacos place on Saturday and ate too much al pastor, then walked it off doing laps around the Parque México. Camila's been deep in a project at Foster — a museum extension in Querétaro — and she came home tonight buzzing about a structural detail she finally cracked. Took Nube out at 6am for the long walk to the park and back, and the air in Roma Norte was that specific cold-blue that only happens in dry-season mornings. Mateo called from Tijuana — Sofía's pregnancy is going well, twenty-six weeks, they're going to find out the gender next week and I told him whatever it is I'm already buying tiny boxing gloves. Mama Lupita would have been ninety-three. I think about what she'd say about the chess board. She'd probably tell me I'm overthinking it. Which I am. Camila played a Spinetta record on the new turntable while we cooked, and for a moment the apartment felt like it was set in 1976 Buenos Aires. I want the finished board to feel heavy in Hugo's hands, weighty enough that he knows it took time, that someone made the decision to make it for him specifically. I'm going to laminate the body in two stages with hide glue because the open time is forgiving and the joint is reversible if I screw up. Profe says you can tell a serious student by what they do on the days nobody is watching. I've been holding onto that one since the test. The Grafana board has fifteen panels now and I keep meaning to clean it up, but every time I try I find a reason to keep one more. I don't know if I'm going to apply to MIT — I haven't told anyone about it yet, not even Camila — but the fact that I'm even considering it means something has shifted. Mama Lupita would have been ninety-three. I think about what she'd say about the chess board. She'd probably tell me I'm overthinking it. Which I am. Hugo Ferreira's birthday is closer than it feels, and the chess board has roughly twelve discrete tasks left, and I am behind. We watched twenty minutes of a documentary about Argentine asados and Camila got homesick in real time, then we ordered takeout from the place on Álvaro Obregón to compensate. I keep wanting to refactor the config loader but I keep telling myself: not while the rollout is in flight. After. After. Code review on Andrés's PR took longer than it should have — I left twelve comments and only three were actually important, the rest were preference. I went back and deleted nine of them. The maker space tools are nicer than what I have at home but the commute eats forty minutes round-trip, so I do prep work at home and run heavy ops there. The deployment pipeline still uses the old GitLab runner, which I want to migrate to GitHub Actions but not this quarter. Adding it to the technical-debt list. I think I'm finally allowing myself to want the harder thing, which is something my younger self never had the bandwidth to admit out loud. The slow accumulation of competence is the only kind that lasts. The rest is fashion. Roberto wants a one-pager for leadership on the rollout, which I'll draft tomorrow. Bullets, not paragraphs. He doesn't read paragraphs.

Body finally getting the message. Two days post-belt and post-family-visit, and the deep tiredness is showing up — the kind that lands in the legs and lower back like a weight you didn't know was suspended. Slept 8.5 hours last night. No 3am wake. Woke up to Nube standing on my chest at 7:40 because Camila had already left for the office and the dog had decided I was on duty. Did some easy mobility work and a short walk. Did not look at any work. Want to keep this floating-feeling for a couple days before the rollout window opens up again. I think I'm finally allowing myself to want the harder thing, which is something my younger self never had the bandwidth to admit out loud.

Back at work today and the rust rewrite is the thing eating most of the headspace. We had the planning meeting this morning at 10am. Andrés led the staging readiness checklist, Carla covered the chaos test plan, I covered the metrics and rollout gates. Roberto sat in the back and asked one question, which is roughly his pattern. The question was about the rollback time. He wanted a number in seconds. I said ninety. He nodded. He left at 10:25. Right now the rust route engine has been building in a feature branch for almost three months. The shape of the migration is: shadow rollout at 1% for a week, then 5%, then 25%, then 50% with full chaos testing turned on, then 100%. We did the shadow rollout last sprint and the numbers were healthy. Today we are five days from the staging cutover, which is the first time real internal traffic — driver dispatch sandbox, ops dashboard pre-prod — touches the rust path in a non-shadow context. I spent most of the afternoon on the metrics infrastructure. The way I'm setting it up: every dispatch decision in the rust path emits a trace span with attributes for input distance, vehicle class, time bucket, and the chosen route's expected duration vs the duration the old Python service would have predicted. Carla wrote a clever Loki query that surfaces the cases where the two predictions diverge by more than ten percent, which is going to be our anomaly hunting tool. We batched up about 400 such cases from the shadow rollout and walked through them in a Wednesday session — 380 of them turned out to be cases where rust was right and Python was sloppy, which is the outcome I had hoped for but had not let myself bet on. The remaining 20 cases were genuinely interesting. Two of them were edge cases involving the pre-1996 vehicle data that has slightly different road-network metadata in our DB, and the rust code was making an assumption that worked for everything in 1996+ but produced strange routes for the older fleet. We patched it. The other 18 were spread between rounding differences (which we accepted as wins for rust because rust was using a more accurate distance calculation), one weird race condition in the trace emitter that does not affect the actual route output, and a handful of cases where the Python side was relying on cached state from a previous request, which the rust path doesn't have. We patched the cache-leak case in the rust path so that we don't introduce a regression in a workload that depends on it. Andrés has been quietly excellent through all of this. He's at the eight-month mark on the team now and is fully owning the metrics pipeline plus the chaos test scaffolding. I told him in his last 1:1 that he's writing better code than I was at his career stage, and he made the face he makes when I compliment him, which is to say he made no face at all and just nodded. He is the most poker-faced engineer I've ever worked with. He also got pulled into a meeting on Monday that he didn't need to be in, and he sent me a Slack DM afterward asking how to politely decline future invitations of that flavor — I told him 'just say no' and he typed back 'lol' and I'm reasonably sure that was his first ever lol. Carla on the other hand is fine but in a different way. She had her baby Camilo six months ago and is still adjusting to the new sleep budget. We're flexible on her hours — she does 7am to 1pm in the office and then takes calls or async work in the late afternoon depending on what Camilo is doing. The chaos test framework she's building is genuinely solid. We agreed today that the staging cutover should not happen on a Monday or Friday — Monday because everyone is groggy from the weekend and Friday because nobody wants to debug something at 4pm when the bar is calling.
