We picked Wednesday next week. Roberto signed off on the timeline but added that he wants a written go/no-go memo from me by Tuesday at noon. Which is fine. He's a political manager, not a technical one, but he's predictable and I've learned how to write the memo he needs. Three sentences for the recommendation, three bullets for the risks, three bullets for the mitigations, one paragraph at the bottom on what success looks like and the success criteria for backing out. He reads it in three minutes and gives me one of two answers, and I need to make sure both answers are acceptable to me. The rust path has never once shown a memory leak in any of the soak tests we've run, and that fact alone has done more for my confidence than anything else. The Python service had a slow leak that nobody had ever fully diagnosed and that we'd worked around with monthly restarts. The rust path's RSS plateaus within forty minutes of startup and stays there. We had it running in the soak harness for ten days last month and the memory profile was a flat line. Beautiful. I don't know what I expected from rust but apparently the rumors are true. Alright. Going to go take Nube on her walk. The rollout starts in five days and I want to be sleeping eight hours a night when it does. Spent forty minutes on Loki trying to track down a single span that looked weird in the trace. Turned out to be a logging artifact, not a real problem. Code review on Andrés's PR took longer than it should have — I left twelve comments and only three were actually important, the rest were preference. I went back and deleted nine of them. I keep wanting to refactor the config loader but I keep telling myself: not while the rollout is in flight. After. After. Latency histograms look healthier than I expected — the long tail past p99 is now compressed against the body of the distribution, which is the whole point of the rewrite. Andrés has been quietly cleaning up the old Python code paths now that traffic is gone — we'll archive that module next quarter once we're sure nothing else imports from it. The deployment pipeline still uses the old GitLab runner, which I want to migrate to GitHub Actions but not this quarter. Adding it to the technical-debt list. Disk I/O on the read-replica side is the next thing I want to look at — there's a slow query that wakes up every five minutes and we've been ignoring it for too long. I'm logging everything in the runbook in Notion as I go, so the next person who has to debug this isn't doing it from scratch at 2am. The shadow rollout taught us that the old service was actually doing a thing nobody documented — a retry-with-backoff that the new one had to learn to imitate. We had one transient blip at 04:17 that turned out to be a network hiccup on the Mexico City side, not anything we did. PagerDuty fired and resolved itself in 90 seconds. Carla pointed out that we never wrote the chaos test for the new path, so I'm going to do that next week before we forget how the rewrite works. I added a second alert on tail latency specifically because the first one only fires on average and I want to catch the moment a single shard goes weird. The Grafana board has fifteen panels now and I keep meaning to clean it up, but every time I try I find a reason to keep one more. Carla pinged me on Slack to ask if we should add a circuit breaker around the new path; I said yes but probably in the next sprint, not this one. Quiet Tuesday at home. Camila is at the Foster studio late, working on the Querétaro museum extension structural detail she's been chasing. Made arroz con leche from my mom's recipe — she sent the voice note version on Sunday before flying out — and Nube has been doing the polite-but-aggressive sit-stare next to my chair. The apartment in Roma Norte is finally quiet again after the family weekend. Going to read for thirty minutes and then go to sleep. Mateo called from Tijuana — Sofía's pregnancy is going well, twenty-six weeks, they're going to find out the gender next week and I told him whatever it is I'm already buying tiny boxing gloves.

Mid-morning work update. Andrés and I paired for an hour on the metrics emitter, walked through the shadow-rollout dataset and refined two of the queries Carla wrote. We reorganized the dashboard so that the latency panels are in the top row and the error/saturation panels are in the bottom row, which is how I want the on-call engineer to read it during the cutover next week. The change took about forty minutes and required me to re-pin a half-dozen dashboards in our other Grafana instances, but the final version is much easier to scan during an incident. We also caught one bug during the pairing — the trace emitter was double-counting one specific route class because of how the span attributes were being merged. Andrés found it. I would have caught it on the cutover day instead, when the impact would have been a confusing dashboard rather than a quiet bug. Saved us a lot of stress next week. He noticed because the count of one class was visibly higher than the same class's count in production telemetry, and the numbers should match within about five percent in shadow mode. We patched it, redeployed the shadow service, and the count is now back in alignment. Roberto stopped by my desk at 2pm to ask how Tuesday's go/no-go memo was coming. I had drafted it in the morning. Sent him the link to the doc and he said 'looks good, will read tonight.' Which is Roberto for 'I will skim this and probably approve it.' Which is fine. The memo is solid. Three sentences for recommendation. Three bullets for risks: shadow only covers business-hours traffic, rollback procedure has not been chaos-tested, and Andrés is the only one besides me who deeply understands the new path. Three bullets for mitigations: extended shadow window for off-hours traffic this weekend, scheduled chaos-test of the rollback on Thursday evening, and Carla shadowing me during the cutover and writing the postmortem-runbook in real time so we can share knowledge faster. Carla finished the chaos test framework today. We tested it against the staging environment and killed two pods mid-request. The new path handled it like the old path — load balancer notices, sheds traffic, healthy pods absorb the difference, no stranded requests. Beautiful. We are five days from staging cutover and I am cautiously optimistic, which for me is approximately as optimistic as I get during a major rollout. The fact that I've been sleeping is also helping. Chess board status: the body is laminated, the squares are glued up, and I'm into the inlay work on the border. The cedar is behaving better than it did three weeks ago, when I kept getting tear-out on the softer rays — I think I'm finally getting a feel for which way to push the chisel and how much pressure the tool wants. The walnut inlay strips are cut to 1.5mm and they're seating properly into the cedar. I'm using hide glue on the inlay because it's reversible, in case I or Hugo ever needs to repair it twenty years from now. Spent four hours on Sunday at the Roma Norte maker space dialing in the router setup for the inlay channels. The Festool TS 55 REQ has a router attachment that I had not used before, and learning it under deadline pressure is exactly what Profe would have advised against, which is why I gave myself the whole morning to do test cuts on offcuts before touching the actual board. Wound up doing eleven test cuts before I was satisfied. The twelfth one I did on the actual board, holding my breath. It came out clean. The drawer for the magnetic pieces is the part I'm now stuck on. I wanted to do a hand-cut dovetail joint, both because dovetails are pretty and because Hugo will appreciate a hand-cut joint — he taught Camila to make pão de queijo from scratch when she was six because 'machines hide what your hands learned' is something he says — but I am not yet competent enough at hand-cut dovetails to risk it on this board. So the compromise: I'm going to use box joints, which I can cut precisely on the table saw with a jig, and the visual difference is small enough that I think Hugo will not notice unless he is looking for it. Camila said 'he will absolutely notice and he will absolutely love them anyway.' Probably true. Two more weekends of work and I should have the board glue-up done. Then finishing — three coats of beeswax-and-mineral-oil, applied by hand, with sanding between coats. Then the magnetic chess pieces, which I've been 3D-printing in batches at Pablo's friend's lab in Polanco. The pieces are walnut-PLA and have a small neodymium magnet embedded in the base. They click. They click in the way a good piece should click. Spent forty minutes on Loki trying to track down a single span that looked weird in the trace. Turned out to be a logging artifact, not a real problem. Code review on Andrés's PR took longer than it should have — I left twelve comments and only three were actually important, the rest were preference. I went back and deleted nine of them. The deployment pipeline still uses the old GitLab runner, which I want to migrate to GitHub Actions but not this quarter. Adding it to the technical-debt list. The shadow rollout taught us that the old service was actually doing a thing nobody documented — a retry-with-backoff that the new one had to learn to imitate. I keep wanting to refactor the config loader but I keep telling myself: not while the rollout is in flight. After. After. Carla pinged me on Slack to ask if we should add a circuit breaker around the new path; I said yes but probably in the next sprint, not this one. We had one transient blip at 04:17 that turned out to be a network hiccup on the Mexico City side, not anything we did. PagerDuty fired and resolved itself in 90 seconds. Latency histograms look healthier than I expected — the long tail past p99 is now compressed against the body of the distribution, which is the whole point of the rewrite.

Climbed at La Cueva this morning with Pablo. He's been chasing a V5 for three weeks and finally sent it. I cheered. I tried it twice, peeled off the second move both times, decided to work on something at my actual level. The gym was packed — the early-bird crew of finance guys plus a youth team coach setting projects for kids. We grabbed pan dulce afterward at the corner panadería and walked back. Good morning. Camila played a Spinetta record on the new turntable while we cooked, and for a moment the apartment felt like it was set in 1976 Buenos Aires. Profe stopped me after class to ask how the family visit went, and I told him truthfully — best three days I've had in a year.

Long Sunday afternoon. Camila is at the studio finishing a model. Nube and I have the apartment, and I've been thinking — really thinking — about what comes next, professionally, after this rust rewrite ships. I have not written this thought down anywhere yet because I have not worked out the shape of it. Putting it here so I can come back to it. I've been at Brújula Logistics for almost four years. The role started as senior backend on a six-engineer team and has become something more like staff engineer for the whole platform, although the title has not officially changed because Roberto's political instincts say titles attract attention and we don't want attention on the salary band right now. I am owning the route engine, the dispatch optimizer, the legacy migration, and most recently the rust rewrite. The work is the most technically interesting work I have ever done and the team is the best team I have ever worked with. I am being paid well. The apartment is comfortable. Camila and I have a life we like. By every external metric, the move would be: stay. And yet. I have been twitching toward something. I cannot fully name it. The closest I can get is: the work is interesting but it is interesting in a vector I have already explored a lot of. Distributed systems. Performance. Latency. Reliability. The rust rewrite has stretched me in some directions but the underlying domain is still 'making a backend run faster and not crash.' I have been doing variations of that for ten years. I am good at it. I will get better at it. But the marginal joy of getting better at it is decreasing for me, not increasing. What has been increasing is my interest in how systems interact with people. Specifically: how do you build software that adapts to the emotional state of the user, that responds to attention and frustration and confidence, that treats the human as a sensor rather than as a generator of clicks? The dispatch system at Brújula has, in a tiny way, hinted at this — drivers' schedules and well-being feed into the optimizer, and we've had good conversations internally about how to do that ethically. But the field is much bigger than what we touch. Affective computing. Attention systems. Wearable bio-signal pipelines. Stuff that lives at the intersection of ML, HCI, and neuroscience. I keep finding myself, on weekends, reading papers from a particular research group at MIT. The group studies how to model affective state from physiological signals — heart rate variability, electrodermal activity, head pose, voice tone — and how to use those models to build interfaces that respond to the human rather than expecting the human to respond to the interface. The group's PI is Rosalind Picard, who founded the field. I have read Affective Computing twice in two years, the second time with notes. I have downloaded fifteen years of papers and read most of them. This is brewing. I haven't told anyone. Not Camila, not my dad, not Pablo, not Profe. Not yet. Because I do not yet know what I want to do about it. The obvious move would be: apply for a PhD. The obstacles to that obvious move are large. I am thirty-three. I have never been in a research environment. I do not have a research advisor. I do not have publications. I would be moving from a senior IC role on a healthy team to a PhD stipend in Cambridge, Massachusetts, which is a 70%+ pay cut and a multi-year time commitment. Camila's job is in CDMX. The dog. The chess board project. The black belt I just earned. And yet the idea will not leave me alone. It came back today during a long shower while Nube sat outside the bathroom door — I caught myself doing the thing my dad does when he's working out a problem in his head, which is rub the back of his neck and stare at the ceiling. I had been staring at the ceiling for ten minutes. Nube was patient. I came out and wrote nothing down because I am not yet ready to write it down anywhere with my real name on it. My dad would understand it the most easily of anyone in my life, but my dad is sixty-six and wants me to stay close to home, in the way that fathers want that. My mom would worry about Camila. Camila would understand it but her career is in Mexico City and the practical question of two careers in two cities for several years is a real one. Pablo would be supportive but his day-to-day is so different from research that the conversation might not land. Profe would say 'do what makes you a more serious person.' I think that is what he would say. OK. I'm going to keep this in the back of my head for now and let the rollout finish at work. After the rollout, when there's some breathing room, I'll write down the actual thinking and start a real conversation with Camila. Not now. Now is for shipping and finishing the chess board and being present at home. The rest can wait six weeks. The slow accumulation of competence is the only kind that lasts. The rest is fashion. Camila's dad Hugo is still grieving Beatriz in his quiet way, and I think the chess board project is partly for him and partly for me, which is the honest version of why I'm doing it. I'm thirty-three and the people I love are getting older and I'm noticing it in small moments — my mom's voice on a phone call, the way my dad takes a beat before answering a complicated question. Five years ago this version of my life would have looked impossibly stable. The amount of work that compounded silently to get here is more than I want to count. I don't know if I'm going to apply to MIT — I haven't told anyone about it yet, not even Camila — but the fact that I'm even considering it means something has shifted. There's a difference between being scared and being unprepared, and the test taught me to feel that difference in my body before my brain catches up. I think I'm finally allowing myself to want the harder thing, which is something my younger self never had the bandwidth to admit out loud. There's a thing my dad used to say — that the work expands to fill the panic budget you give it — and I keep coming back to it on quiet weekends. Profe says you can tell a serious student by what they do on the days nobody is watching. I've been holding onto that one since the test. I keep noticing that the things I find scariest in advance turn out to be the most boring in execution. The rewrite went well because we designed it to go well. The mythical 'launch day adrenaline' was just sitting at a screen looking at numbers go down. The black belt has changed almost nothing about my actual jiu-jitsu, but it has changed a lot about how I walk into rooms in the rest of my life. Mama Lupita would have been ninety-three. I think about what she'd say about the chess board. She'd probably tell me I'm overthinking it. Which I am. When I tell Camila about a problem at work she doesn't try to solve it; she just asks one question that reframes the whole thing. That's a superpower and I should tell her that more often. Pablo texted asking if I want to climb at La Cueva on Saturday morning. I said yes. I always say yes to climbing because I always need it more than I think I do. The shadow rollout taught us that the old service was actually doing a thing nobody documented — a retry-with-backoff that the new one had to learn to imitate. Mateo called from Tijuana — Sofía's pregnancy is going well, twenty-six weeks, they're going to find out the gender next week and I told him whatever it is I'm already buying tiny boxing gloves. Latency histograms look healthier than I expected — the long tail past p99 is now compressed against the body of the distribution, which is the whole point of the rewrite. Nube is curled up on the rug behind me and every fifteen minutes she does the dramatic-sigh thing that blue heelers do when they think you should be paying attention to them. We had one transient blip at 04:17 that turned out to be a network hiccup on the Mexico City side, not anything we did. PagerDuty fired and resolved itself in 90 seconds.

Thursday night. Camila got home at 8pm with the model still in her head — she'd been working on a structural detail for the Querétaro museum, a tension cable system for the cantilevered overhang, and she walked in and immediately drew it on a napkin to show me. She was tired but in the good tired, the kind where the work has been productive and the body just needs to catch up. We made a quick pasta — orecchiette with chile-toasted breadcrumbs and pancetta — and ate at the kitchen table with Nube doing her best 'i am also here, i should also have orecchiette' routine. Camila's been at Foster + Partners for almost three years now in their CDMX studio. She moved here from São Paulo for the job. Her dad Hugo is in São Paulo still, alone in the apartment, and Camila Skypes with him every Sunday. The Sunday call is sacred. She blocks it on her calendar. Hugo is in the slow-moving phase of grief — Beatriz has been gone for almost three years, but he still has trouble with afternoons, that's what Camila says. The mornings he's fine. The evenings he reads. The afternoons are the hard time. She told me tonight that Hugo asked about me on the call last Sunday. Specifically he asked how the chess board was coming along. I have not said anything to Hugo about the chess board — Camila told him weeks ago and now he asks about it almost every call. So now I am working on a present for someone who knows it is coming and who is asking about it, which has converted the project from a surprise into something more like a shared expectation. I am not sure if this changes how I should think about the project or not. Camila says it does not. She says Hugo is at the age where asking about a thing is its own pleasure, separate from receiving the thing. I think she is probably right. Dad has been re-reading Feynman over the holidays, and he sent me a long voice note about the spinning plate observation as if I hadn't heard it from him five times before. I listened anyway. Camila got home around eight, kicked her shoes off at the door, and asked what I'd eaten — I had to admit it was just a banana and coffee, and she laughed at me but in the way that means she'll cook. Nube is curled up on the rug behind me and every fifteen minutes she does the dramatic-sigh thing that blue heelers do when they think you should be paying attention to them. Profe Esteban texted me a video of one of the new white belts trying to do a half guard. Took me back. Took me back so far I had to put the phone down for a second. Took Nube out at 6am for the long walk to the park and back, and the air in Roma Norte was that specific cold-blue that only happens in dry-season mornings. Mom called from Guadalajara, she's still working at the pediatric clinic three days a week, and she asked if I'm sleeping. I lied. I always lie about that with her. Mateo called from Tijuana — Sofía's pregnancy is going well, twenty-six weeks, they're going to find out the gender next week and I told him whatever it is I'm already buying tiny boxing gloves. Profe stopped me after class to ask how the family visit went, and I told him truthfully — best three days I've had in a year. Pablo and I went to the rooftop tacos place on Saturday and ate too much al pastor, then walked it off doing laps around the Parque México.

Camila threw me a small party on Saturday night, and I am still slightly hungover and very grateful. She had been planning it for weeks without telling me — I learned about it Saturday afternoon when she said 'don't make plans tonight' and gave me her conspiratorial face. I should have known. I did not know. Anyway. Twelve people in the apartment. Pablo and Renata, Profe Esteban and his wife Adriana (who I had only met twice before), Andrés (who came alone because he is not currently dating and is uncomfortable being asked about it), Carla and her partner Joaquín (Camilo the baby was with Carla's mother for the night), Camila's coworker Mariana, and a sister-of-Mariana visiting from Querétaro who turned out to be a chess player and who spent most of the night talking with Profe about strategy in a way that I'm fairly sure baffled Pablo and delighted Profe. Twelve people plus Nube, who treated the party as her personal social event and did fifteen minutes of meet-and-greet at every door knock. Camila had set the apartment up beautifully. She'd hung small white lights on the kitchen pass-through. She had pulled out the good plates that we never use. She had bought three bottles of mezcal Don Mateo del Valle and one bottle of cachaça (for the Brazilian touch she likes to slip in), and she'd had Mariana help her prep about a dozen small plates — guacamole, cured tuna with lime, slices of plantain with crema, the panela cheese and mango salsa thing she does, plus a vat of pozole that simmered all afternoon and made the apartment smell incredible. The pozole was the killer move. Pozole on a chilly CDMX night with twelve people in your apartment is a kind of love language. Profe arrived first at 7:30pm with Adriana, who I had only briefly met at a gym anniversary years ago and who turned out to be wonderful — a children's book illustrator, bilingual in Spanish and Portuguese (her mother is from Belém), with strong opinions about Mexican mezcal. She and Camila bonded almost instantly. Within thirty minutes they were in the kitchen comparing notes on Brazilian and Mexican Christmas traditions and laughing at something I couldn't quite hear. Pablo and Renata came at 8pm with a bottle of red and a small wrapped gift, which turned out to be a black-and-white framed photo from our first jiu-jitsu tournament eleven years ago — I had completely forgotten this photo existed. We are both in white belts. I am 22 years old in the photo, twenty pounds heavier than I am now, with a haircut that should not have been allowed. Pablo is grinning. He had won that day. I had lost in the second round to a guy from Veracruz who I would later spend three years trying to beat and finally beating in 2019. The photo is now on the bookshelf next to the espresso machine and I keep almost looking at it and almost not believing that the kid in it is me. Andrés arrived at 8:15pm with a small bag of chocolates from a place on Reforma that I had never heard of but apparently is the place for chocolates. He took one look at the room, said 'oh,' and immediately tried to position himself near a wall where he would be slightly less visible. Camila had me steer him toward Profe, who took one look at Andrés and said 'come on, come over here' in the tone Profe uses with new white belts, and within ten minutes Andrés was telling Profe about a chess opening he'd been working through. Andrés plays chess online at around the 1900 ELO mark, which I had not known. They talked for forty minutes. Andrés left the corner. He laughed twice. Carla, when she arrived at 8:30, looked at Andrés in the middle of the room and said 'is that Andrés' and gave me the look that meant 'how did you accomplish this.' Carla brought Joaquín, who is calm in the way that you become calm when you have a six-month-old at home. Joaquín works in marketing for a hotel group and is very different from Carla in personality but somehow the match works. He talked to Pablo about climbing for an hour. Pablo gave him the same speech he gives me roughly every six months, which is that climbing is the last honest sport because the wall does not care who you are or what you do for a living. Joaquín nodded politely. I do not think he is going to start climbing. At 9:45 Camila tapped a glass and said she wanted to say something. I tried to leave the room. She physically grabbed my arm and pulled me back. The speech was short. She said: eleven years. She said: the first time she met me, she met me at a dinner party at Mariana's apartment and I had spent half the night talking about the differences between gi and no-gi training, and she had thought 'this man is exhausting.' She said: she was wrong. She said: the thing she has learned about me in five years is that the depth and consistency are real, and that the belt is not a trophy, it's a marker. She said: the marker is for showing what kind of long-haul person I am. I lost it. I lost it in front of twelve people in my own kitchen. Camila pretended not to notice. Profe pretended not to notice. Pablo, kind man, made a loud joke about something else to give me thirty seconds to recover. Adriana came over to me afterwards and put her hand on my arm and said in Portuguese 'querido, é bom chorar.' I'd later ask Camila to translate. It means 'dear, it is good to cry.' They left between 11 and midnight. Andrés got an Uber home. Profe and Adriana stayed last, helping us clean up despite our protests. Profe insisted on washing the pozole pot while Adriana dried. He did not say much during the washing but at the door, before leaving, he held my shoulder for an extra beat and said 'estás bien, mijo.' Which is Profe for 'you are doing okay.' Which from Profe is a lot. Camila and I sat on the couch at 12:30 with the apartment looking like a small wedding had happened. Nube was asleep under the coffee table, exhausted from being a host. We did not talk much. We just sat there with the white lights still on, watching the candles burn down, smelling the leftover pozole. I held her hand. I'm holding it now, in a manner of speaking, by writing this down. The party was the best thing. She is the best thing. End of entry. Mom called from Guadalajara, she's still working at the pediatric clinic three days a week, and she asked if I'm sleeping. I lied. I always lie about that with her. Grandma Lupita's birthday would have been Monday — five years gone now — and I made her champurrado recipe from memory because mom is too sad to do it lately. We watched twenty minutes of a documentary about Argentine asados and Camila got homesick in real time, then we ordered takeout from the place on Álvaro Obregón to compensate. Roberto wants a one-pager for leadership on the rollout, which I'll draft tomorrow. Bullets, not paragraphs. He doesn't read paragraphs. Sunday morning recovery. Apartment still smells faintly of pozole. Drank two espressos and walked Nube the long route to Parque México and back. Watched a documentary about the Mexico City metro in the afternoon while Camila napped on the couch with her head on my leg. Did not look at work email once. The chess board is sitting on the bench in the spare room waiting for me. I will get to it tomorrow. Dad has been re-reading Feynman over the holidays, and he sent me a long voice note about the spinning plate observation as if I hadn't heard it from him five times before. I listened anyway. I made yerba mate at midnight and Camila made fun of me for it, said it would keep me up. She was right. It always keeps me up. I drink it anyway.

Monday standup at 9:30. Roberto sat in for the first time in three weeks. He likes to drop into standups before any major rollout, partly to be visible and partly to flag any political concern he wants on the team's radar. Today he flagged that the COO had asked about the rollout timing because of the quarterly board meeting on Thursday. Translated from Roberto-speak: do not ship anything that might cause a P0 on Thursday morning. We had already planned the staging cutover for Wednesday at 11am, deliberately slotted to give us 36 hours of buffer before the board meeting, so this is fine. Andrés walked through the readiness checklist. Out of 47 items, 44 are green, 2 are yellow, 1 is red. The red is the chaos test of the rollback procedure — we have not yet practiced backing out. Carla and I are pairing on this Wednesday morning before the cutover. The two yellows are: extended off-hours shadow data (Carla has a 96-hour collection window running and the data so far looks good but is not yet complete), and the runbook's section on rollback decision-making (which I'm cleaning up tonight). After standup I spent two hours reviewing Andrés's last PR in the rust path — a refactor of the trace emitter that simplifies the code by about 40 lines and makes the merging logic less likely to bug out the way it did last week. I left three comments. One was a real concern, two were nits. He addressed the real concern within an hour and pushed the merge by lunch. We are in good shape. Lunch with Camila at the place on Álvaro Obregón. She had a sandwich and I had the mushroom soup. We did not talk about work. We talked about a book she's reading on Niemeyer's late period. Came back to the office, wrote the rollback runbook section, left at 6:30pm. Quiet, productive day. Tomorrow more memo work and a final rehearsal of the cutover steps. Wednesday is the big day. The deployment pipeline still uses the old GitLab runner, which I want to migrate to GitHub Actions but not this quarter. Adding it to the technical-debt list. Carla pointed out that we never wrote the chaos test for the new path, so I'm going to do that next week before we forget how the rewrite works. I'm logging everything in the runbook in Notion as I go, so the next person who has to debug this isn't doing it from scratch at 2am. Disk I/O on the read-replica side is the next thing I want to look at — there's a slow query that wakes up every five minutes and we've been ignoring it for too long. Roberto wants a one-pager for leadership on the rollout, which I'll draft tomorrow. Bullets, not paragraphs. He doesn't read paragraphs. The shadow rollout taught us that the old service was actually doing a thing nobody documented — a retry-with-backoff that the new one had to learn to imitate. I added a second alert on tail latency specifically because the first one only fires on average and I want to catch the moment a single shard goes weird. We had one transient blip at 04:17 that turned out to be a network hiccup on the Mexico City side, not anything we did. PagerDuty fired and resolved itself in 90 seconds. Carla pinged me on Slack to ask if we should add a circuit breaker around the new path; I said yes but probably in the next sprint, not this one. Andrés has been quietly cleaning up the old Python code paths now that traffic is gone — we'll archive that module next quarter once we're sure nothing else imports from it.

Roberto signed off on the go/no-go memo this morning at 9:14am. He left two comments. The first asked if I had run the rollback chaos test yet — I told him no, that's tomorrow morning. He said 'do it before the cutover or move the cutover,' which is exactly the answer I expected and exactly what the plan already was. The second comment was about external comms: he wants a Slack post in the engineering channel right after cutover saying 'rust path is now the default' in plain language so non-rust-aware engineers know what to expect if they're debugging. I had not thought of this. Good catch on his part. I'll write it. Spent the rest of the morning rehearsing the cutover steps. We have 23 distinct steps in the runbook, each timed and each with an assigned owner. The steps fall into four phases: pre-flight checks, traffic shift from 50% to 100%, monitoring window, and post-cutover validation. I walked through it twice on paper and then once in the staging environment with the actual tooling. The whole sequence takes about 90 minutes if everything is healthy. In the afternoon I had a 1:1 with Andrés. He's at the eight-month mark and we did our quarterly career conversation. He wants to lead a project of his own next quarter. I think he's ready. We sketched out what 'his' project might look like — most likely the migration of the legacy reporting pipeline to a more modern setup, which is a bounded scope but technically meaty. He said yes. I'll talk to Roberto about it after this rollout. Got home at 6:45pm. Camila was already there. We watched an episode of a Brazilian crime show with subtitles for me, ate leftover pozole, walked Nube to the corner and back. Light night. Code review on Andrés's PR took longer than it should have — I left twelve comments and only three were actually important, the rest were preference. I went back and deleted nine of them. Latency histograms look healthier than I expected — the long tail past p99 is now compressed against the body of the distribution, which is the whole point of the rewrite. Carla pinged me on Slack to ask if we should add a circuit breaker around the new path; I said yes but probably in the next sprint, not this one. Andrés has been quietly cleaning up the old Python code paths now that traffic is gone — we'll archive that module next quarter once we're sure nothing else imports from it. I added a second alert on tail latency specifically because the first one only fires on average and I want to catch the moment a single shard goes weird. Spent forty minutes on Loki trying to track down a single span that looked weird in the trace. Turned out to be a logging artifact, not a real problem. The Grafana board has fifteen panels now and I keep meaning to clean it up, but every time I try I find a reason to keep one more. The deployment pipeline still uses the old GitLab runner, which I want to migrate to GitHub Actions but not this quarter. Adding it to the technical-debt list. Carla pointed out that we never wrote the chaos test for the new path, so I'm going to do that next week before we forget how the rewrite works. I'm logging everything in the runbook in Notion as I go, so the next person who has to debug this isn't doing it from scratch at 2am. I keep wanting to refactor the config loader but I keep telling myself: not while the rollout is in flight. After. After.

Wednesday morning. Got into the office at 7am for the chaos test of the rollback procedure. Carla was already there with two coffees, one of which she handed to me without asking. We did the test against staging at 7:15. The procedure: simulate a P0 in the new rust path, run the rollback runbook step-by-step, measure end-to-end time from decision to recovery. The test went fine. Better than fine. End-to-end time from 'declare incident' to 'rollback complete and traffic on Python path' was 71 seconds. The bottleneck was a manual confirmation step that we kept in the procedure for safety — without it the time would have been about 45 seconds. We left the manual step in. Some friction is good when you're rolling back. Carla had built a small Slack bot for the chaos test that posts updates to a private channel with the timing data, which made the test much easier to review afterwards. We ran the test twice to confirm reproducibility. Both runs landed within five seconds of each other. Logged the results. Updated the runbook. Done. At 11am we did the actual cutover. Forty-four people in the engineering Slack channel. Roberto in the war room (also Slack). I did the steps. Andrés watched the metrics. Carla watched the error budget. The traffic shift took six minutes — we ramped from 50% to 100% in three increments, watching for anomalies between each. No anomalies appeared. We hit 100% at 11:14am. The next forty-five minutes I just sat at my screen watching numbers. Latencies dropped, as expected. Error rates flat. Memory profile flat. CPU lower than the Python service was using, by a noticeable margin. We held at 100% with full eyes-on for two hours. Nothing happened. Nothing was supposed to happen. The cutover was clean. Posted to the engineering channel at 1:30pm: 'rust path is now the default. dispatch decisions now go through the rust route engine. legacy python path is still warm and can be recovered in 90 seconds if needed.' Thirty replies of various flavors. Roberto sent a thumbs up. Andrés went and got us all sandwiches from the place on Insurgentes. Carla called her mom to check on Camilo. Quiet success. Disk I/O on the read-replica side is the next thing I want to look at — there's a slow query that wakes up every five minutes and we've been ignoring it for too long. Code review on Andrés's PR took longer than it should have — I left twelve comments and only three were actually important, the rest were preference. I went back and deleted nine of them. I'm logging everything in the runbook in Notion as I go, so the next person who has to debug this isn't doing it from scratch at 2am. Carla pointed out that we never wrote the chaos test for the new path, so I'm going to do that next week before we forget how the rewrite works. I keep wanting to refactor the config loader but I keep telling myself: not while the rollout is in flight. After. After. I added a second alert on tail latency specifically because the first one only fires on average and I want to catch the moment a single shard goes weird. Spent forty minutes on Loki trying to track down a single span that looked weird in the trace. Turned out to be a logging artifact, not a real problem. Andrés has been quietly cleaning up the old Python code paths now that traffic is gone — we'll archive that module next quarter once we're sure nothing else imports from it.

Wednesday afternoon, 3pm. Three hours since the cutover at 100%. The dashboards are all green. P50 latency is at 44ms, which is below where Python was sitting at 78ms. P99 is at 189ms vs Python's 412ms. Tail behavior is dramatically better. Memory is flat. CPU is at 23% per pod vs Python's 41%. Error rate is unchanged and very low. This is the best post-cutover hour I have ever experienced. I keep waiting for something to go wrong. I have my muscle memory primed for the rollback. Nothing is happening. Andrés keeps refreshing the dashboard and saying 'still flat.' Carla finally said at 2:45pm 'sebas, it's done. it's working. go for a walk.' I went for a walk. Walked around the building twice. Came back. Still flat. The afternoon stretched into the evening with no incident. We agreed at 5pm that we'd keep a low-key on-call rotation for the next 72 hours but that we did not need to maintain the war room. Roberto sent a Slack DM that said 'great work, will mention to leadership' which from him is a parade. Andrés left at 5:30pm, Carla at 5:45pm, and I left at 6pm with a quiet feeling I have not had in months. Came home and Camila could see it on my face when I walked in. She had cooked — pasta with a Brazilian-style sauce her dad makes with cashews and tomato. We ate at the table. Nube did her thing. I told Camila 'we did the rollout, it went perfectly, I think the next two days I will mostly just stare at dashboards and do nothing.' She laughed. We watched a movie. I went to bed at 10:30. Slept well. I'm logging everything in the runbook in Notion as I go, so the next person who has to debug this isn't doing it from scratch at 2am. Disk I/O on the read-replica side is the next thing I want to look at — there's a slow query that wakes up every five minutes and we've been ignoring it for too long. Latency histograms look healthier than I expected — the long tail past p99 is now compressed against the body of the distribution, which is the whole point of the rewrite. Carla pointed out that we never wrote the chaos test for the new path, so I'm going to do that next week before we forget how the rewrite works. I added a second alert on tail latency specifically because the first one only fires on average and I want to catch the moment a single shard goes weird.

Thursday, post-cutover day one. I went into the office at 9am, looked at the dashboards for ten minutes, and confirmed that nothing had moved overnight except the small overnight traffic spike from the 6am pre-rush hour deliveries, which the rust path absorbed cleanly. Sent a status update to Roberto. He replied 'great.' That is now two compliments from Roberto in two days, which is unprecedented. Spent the morning catching up on PR reviews I had been ignoring during the rollout — six of them in queue from various team members across the org, none of them blocking but all of them deserving of attention. Worked through them. One refactor I had to push back on (it would have introduced a circular dependency between two services that we have been very careful to keep decoupled). The other five I approved with minor comments. At noon I had a PT appointment at Clínica Querétaro — back has been at the steady-state 70% for several weeks now, which is the best it's been since the back tweak last year. Dra. Reyes had me do the new resistance band exercise plus the single-leg bridges and a new one with a foam roller. She said the back has 'consolidated,' which is her way of saying I have hit a plateau and we should re-evaluate the program. We agreed I'll keep doing the home program for two months and then we'll reassess whether to taper or to add new exercises. Came back to the office at 2pm. Andrés had spotted a small issue — not a bug, more of a logging optimization — and we paired on it for an hour to clean it up. Left the office at 5:15. The board meeting at the COO level happened at 4pm and apparently went well, with the rollout being mentioned as an example of successful execution. Roberto told me this on the way out. He looked pleased. Home by 6pm. Quiet evening. Read for an hour. Camila came home at 8pm. We made dinner together, talked about her week. Slept by 11. I keep noticing that the things I find scariest in advance turn out to be the most boring in execution. The rewrite went well because we designed it to go well. The mythical 'launch day adrenaline' was just sitting at a screen looking at numbers go down. I'm thirty-three and the people I love are getting older and I'm noticing it in small moments — my mom's voice on a phone call, the way my dad takes a beat before answering a complicated question. Camila's dad Hugo is still grieving Beatriz in his quiet way, and I think the chess board project is partly for him and partly for me, which is the honest version of why I'm doing it. There's a difference between being scared and being unprepared, and the test taught me to feel that difference in my body before my brain catches up. Profe says you can tell a serious student by what they do on the days nobody is watching. I've been holding onto that one since the test. The black belt has changed almost nothing about my actual jiu-jitsu, but it has changed a lot about how I walk into rooms in the rest of my life. Mama Lupita would have been ninety-three. I think about what she'd say about the chess board. She'd probably tell me I'm overthinking it. Which I am. Five years ago this version of my life would have looked impossibly stable. The amount of work that compounded silently to get here is more than I want to count. When I tell Camila about a problem at work she doesn't try to solve it; she just asks one question that reframes the whole thing. That's a superpower and I should tell her that more often.

Friday afternoon at the maker space, finally back on the chess board after the rust rewrite consumed every spare hour for two weeks. Got there at 1pm and stayed until 6pm, which is the longest single session I've had on this project. Today's task: cut the box joints for the storage drawer. I made a jig for the box joints last weekend and tested it on offcuts of poplar. The jig worked. The box joints came out tight. Today I cut them in walnut, which is what the actual drawer is built from. Walnut behaves differently from poplar — denser, harder, less forgiving on the chip-out. I had to slow the saw blade and feed more carefully. The first joint came out clean. The second joint came out with a small bit of tear-out on the back side, which is fixable but annoying. The third and fourth joints came out clean. I dry-fit the drawer at 4pm. The fit is tight enough that I needed a mallet to get the corners home. Slightly too tight, actually — I'll need to take a couple of thou off one face with a hand plane before I glue up. Better too tight than too loose, in any case. Spent the last hour on the magnetic chess pieces. Pablo's friend at the lab in Polanco printed another batch on Wednesday, and I picked them up Friday morning. The print quality is better on this batch — Pablo's friend tweaked the slicing parameters to reduce the layer lines, and the result is noticeably smoother. I'm sanding each piece by hand on a soft pad before doing the final wax finish. The sanding takes about four minutes per piece. There are 32 pieces. I have three of them done. So that's a lot of evenings at the kitchen table ahead of me. Hugo's birthday is in five weeks. I have the body of the board done, the inlay done, the drawer cut and ready to glue, and 3 of 32 pieces sanded. The finish work — three coats of beeswax and mineral oil with cure time between coats — needs at least ten days of calendar. I am behind. I will catch up on weekends. Spent forty minutes on Loki trying to track down a single span that looked weird in the trace. Turned out to be a logging artifact, not a real problem. Roberto wants a one-pager for leadership on the rollout, which I'll draft tomorrow. Bullets, not paragraphs. He doesn't read paragraphs. Carla pinged me on Slack to ask if we should add a circuit breaker around the new path; I said yes but probably in the next sprint, not this one. The deployment pipeline still uses the old GitLab runner, which I want to migrate to GitHub Actions but not this quarter. Adding it to the technical-debt list. I keep wanting to refactor the config loader but I keep telling myself: not while the rollout is in flight. After. After. The shadow rollout taught us that the old service was actually doing a thing nobody documented — a retry-with-backoff that the new one had to learn to imitate. Disk I/O on the read-replica side is the next thing I want to look at — there's a slow query that wakes up every five minutes and we've been ignoring it for too long. Code review on Andrés's PR took longer than it should have — I left twelve comments and only three were actually important, the rest were preference. I went back and deleted nine of them. The Grafana board has fifteen panels now and I keep meaning to clean it up, but every time I try I find a reason to keep one more. I'm logging everything in the runbook in Notion as I go, so the next person who has to debug this isn't doing it from scratch at 2am.

Lazy Saturday morning. Camila woke up before me and made pão de queijo from scratch — her mom's recipe — and the apartment smells incredible. Nube is doing the dramatic-stare thing because she knows pão de queijo and is hoping for a corner of one. We're going to walk to the market on Cuauhtémoc later and pick up vegetables for a slow lunch. Quiet day. Only chess board work scheduled. No work. Camila played a Spinetta record on the new turntable while we cooked, and for a moment the apartment felt like it was set in 1976 Buenos Aires. Pablo texted asking if I want to climb at La Cueva on Saturday morning. I said yes. I always say yes to climbing because I always need it more than I think I do.

Sunday call with mom. She caught me up on the family — Tía Marisol in the hospital briefly for blood pressure (she's home now and fine), Mateo and Sofía's baby news (still 26 weeks, healthy), and dad has been on a Feynman re-read kick which I already knew about. Mom asked again about the chess board. She always asks about projects. She told me Mama Lupita would have approved of the project and I had to put the phone down for a second. Hung up at 4pm. Profe Esteban texted me a video of one of the new white belts trying to do a half guard. Took me back. Took me back so far I had to put the phone down for a second.

Sunday afternoon at the maker space. Glued up the drawer. Used Old Brown Glue (hide glue), which has open time of about 15 minutes at room temperature, which is forgiving for a four-corner glue-up if you've got your clamps prepped. I had my clamps prepped. I had eight clamps. I wound up using six. Glue-up went smoothly. The corners came together square. I checked diagonals with a tape measure in both directions and got 0.5mm difference, which is well within tolerance for a hand-built drawer. I left it in clamps for two hours, which is overkill for hide glue but the maker space wasn't busy and I had nowhere else to be. While the glue cured I sanded another six chess pieces. Six down, twenty-six to go. I'm getting faster — three minutes per piece now instead of four — but it's still a lot of evening hours. I bought an audiobook on the history of the Mexican railway specifically to listen to during sanding. Weirdly compelling. After the drawer came out of the clamps I dry-fit it into the storage compartment of the chess board body. It seats well — slides in and out smoothly, no binding, no slop. The drawer has a small recess at the front that I'll cut a finger pull into next weekend. I had thought about adding a brass ring pull but decided against it; Hugo's aesthetic is restrained and a brass ring would feel decorative in the wrong way. The finger recess is more in keeping with what he likes. Camila's been at the studio late this weekend. She's racing a deadline on the Querétaro museum extension. I made dinner solo on Saturday night — black bean chili and rice, which is my Sunday-prep dish — and saved her a portion for tonight. She'll be home by nine. We'll eat together. Three weeks until Hugo's birthday. The board body is done. The drawer is done. The chess pieces are 6 of 32 sanded. The finish work has not started. I need to start the finish work next weekend at the latest if it's going to cure properly before mailing. The mailing itself is a whole other thing — DHL to São Paulo, customs declarations, insurance — that I haven't even started yet. Adding it to the list. The deployment pipeline still uses the old GitLab runner, which I want to migrate to GitHub Actions but not this quarter. Adding it to the technical-debt list. We had one transient blip at 04:17 that turned out to be a network hiccup on the Mexico City side, not anything we did. PagerDuty fired and resolved itself in 90 seconds. The shadow rollout taught us that the old service was actually doing a thing nobody documented — a retry-with-backoff that the new one had to learn to imitate. Carla pinged me on Slack to ask if we should add a circuit breaker around the new path; I said yes but probably in the next sprint, not this one. Disk I/O on the read-replica side is the next thing I want to look at — there's a slow query that wakes up every five minutes and we've been ignoring it for too long. Carla pointed out that we never wrote the chaos test for the new path, so I'm going to do that next week before we forget how the rewrite works. Code review on Andrés's PR took longer than it should have — I left twelve comments and only three were actually important, the rest were preference. I went back and deleted nine of them. I added a second alert on tail latency specifically because the first one only fires on average and I want to catch the moment a single shard goes weird.

Tuesday morning standup. Five days post-cutover and the rust path is humming. I gave an update on the metrics at the team's larger weekly sync at 11am — about thirty engineers in the room. P50 down from 78ms to 44ms. P99 down from 412ms to 189ms. Memory flat. CPU down 44%. Pod count effectively unchanged because we sized the rust pods based on Python's footprint and we've been running them lightly utilized since cutover. Roberto has already mentioned this in two leadership meetings. I floated the idea internally of writing the rollout up as an external blog post once we have a month of data. The team agreed. Andrés volunteered to write the first draft, which I encouraged — he has writer's block-prone tendencies and I want him to push through that. Carla agreed to review and edit. I'll do a final pass and Roberto will get political review. Spent the afternoon on a different problem entirely: the legacy reporting pipeline. This is the project Andrés wants to lead next quarter. I started sketching what the migration would look like — moving from a custom Python ETL with hand-rolled scheduling to a more modern Airflow setup with better monitoring. The sketch is still rough but the shape is coming. We'll do a proper design doc in a few weeks. Got home at 7. Camila was already there with takeout from the al pastor place we both like. Easy night. Watched something on TV. Slept by eleven. I added a second alert on tail latency specifically because the first one only fires on average and I want to catch the moment a single shard goes weird. Need to write this down because I've been avoiding writing it down. The insomnia is back. Worse than the test prep version. I'm down to four to five hours a night, on a more or less regular pattern, and it's been like this for nearly two weeks now — it started about three days after the cutover, when the work pressure should have been releasing. Instead the wake time crept earlier and the back-to-sleep got harder. Pattern: I fall asleep fine. Usually around 11pm. I sleep until somewhere between 2:45 and 3:30am. I wake up. I am instantly fully awake — not groggy, not half-conscious. Wired. Heart rate elevated. Mind doing the racing thing. I lie still for thirty minutes, sometimes an hour, and either I drift back off into a light unrestful sleep until 6:30 or I don't, and on the don't-nights I get up at 4am and read in the kitchen with the lamp on so I don't wake Camila. This is different from the test-prep insomnia in two ways. First, the test-prep insomnia made sense — there was a thing I was anxious about, the test was on the calendar, the worry was tracked to a real event. This one has no such target. The cutover went well. The post-cutover monitoring went well. By every external metric the work pressure is at its lowest in months. And yet the body has decided, without my permission, that it's anxious about something. Second difference: the test-prep version, when I lay there at 3am, my mind would replay test scenarios. Specific drills. Specific opponents. Specific weaknesses. This one — and this is the part that has been bothering me — my mind goes to the future. Not the immediate future. The medium-term future. What am I doing in five years? Should I still be at Brújula? Why have I been reading those affective computing papers every weekend? Why does the idea of a research environment make my chest tight in the way wanting something makes a chest tight, and not in the way fearing something makes a chest tight? Those are the thoughts. Round and round. Camila has noticed. She would have to be unobservant to not notice. She has been unbelievably gentle about it. She has not pressed me. She has only said 'you'll tell me when you're ready' in the kind of way that makes me want to tell her now. I have not yet because I don't yet have a coherent thing to tell. I have a vague pull and a worsening sleep schedule and a pattern of weekend paper-reading that I have not previously discussed with anyone. That is not a coherent thing. That's a symptom. The back-pain history is making this harder to ignore than I'd like. I learned during PT last year that for me, when something is wrong emotionally, the back goes first. The sleep going bad is a second-order signal that something has been wrong for a while and I haven't addressed it. The first-order signal would have been the emotional thing itself, which I should have seen earlier. I have an option here. I can talk to Camila this weekend. I can tell her what I have been quietly reading. I can lay out the scary obvious move — applying for a PhD — and let her tell me what she actually thinks. Not what she thinks I want to hear. What she thinks. Camila is the most direct person I know about her own preferences once she knows you want the unfiltered version, and I have spent five years learning to ask for the unfiltered version. I am going to do that this weekend. Putting it on paper here so I commit to it. Saturday night, after dinner, when we're on the couch, I will say: hey, can I tell you what's been keeping me up? She will say of course. I will tell her. We will see what happens. I am not asking permission. I am asking what she sees, what she thinks, what concerns her, what excites her. Then we'll figure out what we do with whatever the conversation produces. In the meantime: tonight I am going to take a melatonin (1mg, my old test-prep dose), drink chamomile, get into bed by 10:15pm, and if I wake at 3am I will get up immediately rather than fight it. Reading in the kitchen at 3:30am has been less destructive to my sleep architecture than lying in bed forcing it. Pablo would say 'go to the gym instead and burn it off' but the back will not appreciate jiu-jitsu at 4am, and I am old enough now to know that. Notes for whoever reads this later: the body knows things before the mind does. The body has been saying something for two weeks. The mind has been refusing to translate. The translation is happening this weekend whether I want it or not. There's a thing my dad used to say — that the work expands to fill the panic budget you give it — and I keep coming back to it on quiet weekends. The slow accumulation of competence is the only kind that lasts. The rest is fashion. Five years ago this version of my life would have looked impossibly stable. The amount of work that compounded silently to get here is more than I want to count. I'm thirty-three and the people I love are getting older and I'm noticing it in small moments — my mom's voice on a phone call, the way my dad takes a beat before answering a complicated question. There's a difference between being scared and being unprepared, and the test taught me to feel that difference in my body before my brain catches up. When I tell Camila about a problem at work she doesn't try to solve it; she just asks one question that reframes the whole thing. That's a superpower and I should tell her that more often. Camila's dad Hugo is still grieving Beatriz in his quiet way, and I think the chess board project is partly for him and partly for me, which is the honest version of why I'm doing it. The black belt has changed almost nothing about my actual jiu-jitsu, but it has changed a lot about how I walk into rooms in the rest of my life. I don't know if I'm going to apply to MIT — I haven't told anyone about it yet, not even Camila — but the fact that I'm even considering it means something has shifted. I keep noticing that the things I find scariest in advance turn out to be the most boring in execution. The rewrite went well because we designed it to go well. The mythical 'launch day adrenaline' was just sitting at a screen looking at numbers go down. Profe says you can tell a serious student by what they do on the days nobody is watching. I've been holding onto that one since the test. Mama Lupita would have been ninety-three. I think about what she'd say about the chess board. She'd probably tell me I'm overthinking it. Which I am. I think I'm finally allowing myself to want the harder thing, which is something my younger self never had the bandwidth to admit out loud. Mom called from Guadalajara, she's still working at the pediatric clinic three days a week, and she asked if I'm sleeping. I lied. I always lie about that with her. Hugo Ferreira's birthday is closer than it feels, and the chess board has roughly twelve discrete tasks left, and I am behind. Grandma Lupita's birthday would have been Monday — five years gone now — and I made her champurrado recipe from memory because mom is too sad to do it lately. I don't know if I'm going to apply to MIT — I haven't told anyone about it yet, not even Camila — but the fact that I'm even considering it means something has shifted. Big update. RouteOpt — the broader name we use internally for the rust route engine plus the optimizer pipeline — officially shipped to staging at month 16 of the project. Today is the day. We threw a small pizza thing at 5pm in the office. About twenty engineers showed up plus Roberto plus the COO came down for fifteen minutes which is the kind of thing he does only when something has gone right. I gave a five-minute talk that I had not prepared, which is the right way to do five-minute talks. The headline metric: the p95 latency on dispatch decisions has dropped from 340 milliseconds on the old Python service to 38 milliseconds on the rust path. From 340 to 38. That is roughly a 9x improvement on p95. Memory consumption has dropped 60%. CPU is down 44%. Error rate is unchanged at our already-low baseline, which means we have not introduced any new failure modes in exchange for the speed. This is the metric Roberto wanted to lead with in the leadership readout. Three figures. Two of them are reductions, one of them is unchanged. Roberto loves this kind of slide. He likes 'we made this faster, smaller, and didn't break it.' He doesn't like ambiguity. We gave him no ambiguity. How we got here: it's been 16 months from the first 'should we rewrite this in rust' conversation to the staging cutover. The first six months were mostly skepticism — Andrés and I built a tiny prototype and convinced ourselves it was worth trying, then convinced Carla, then convinced Roberto, then convinced his boss. The middle six months were design and implementation, which involved a lot of rewriting, a lot of throwing away of approaches that turned out to be bad, and one major architectural pivot at month 8 when we realized we'd been wrongly modeling the route problem as graph traversal when it was actually a constrained optimization problem. The pivot cost us six weeks of calendar but saved us about three months downstream. The last four months have been hardening, shadow rollout, chaos testing, and the cutover. Looking back at the design doc I wrote in month 1 — and I just dug it up — it's interesting to see what I got right and what I got wrong. I correctly predicted that the rust path would be 5-10x faster on p95. I incorrectly predicted that memory would be 'about the same.' Memory turned out to be much better than that, partly because rust gives you tighter control over allocation patterns and partly because we redesigned the data structures to be more compact during the rewrite. I correctly predicted that we'd hit a major architectural realization midway through (I called it the 'month 6 surprise' and it turned out to be the month 8 surprise, which is close enough). I incorrectly predicted the rollout would take six weeks; it took twelve. The team is in great shape. Andrés is confident and clearly ready for his own project next quarter — the legacy reporting pipeline migration is the candidate. Carla is back at full capacity after her parental leave and has been writing some of the best chaos test code I've ever read; she's been talking about wanting to move toward an SRE-leaning role and I am thinking through how to structure that. Roberto has been politically supportive in exactly the way I needed him to be, which is mostly by getting out of the way and translating leadership concerns into bullet points I could address. What's next at work, project-wise: we have to harden the staging cutover into a full production cutover, which means about 4-6 more weeks of monitoring and adjustment. After that the rust path replaces the Python path entirely and we can begin removing the Python service from the architecture, which is a project of its own (the Python service has tendrils into about seven other internal services and unwinding those tendrils will take care). I do not yet know whether I want to lead that unwinding or hand it to Andrés. We will see. The COO came down to the pizza party at 5:15pm and stayed for about fifteen minutes. He is not someone I have spent much time with — he's two layers up from me — and he was warmer than I expected. He said 'thank you' twice, which from him is roughly equivalent to a parade. He asked Andrés a question about the architecture and Andrés answered it well, which made me happy. He left and we ate pizza for another forty minutes and Carla showed me a picture of Camilo in a tiny costume from a recent photoshoot. Came home at 7:30pm. Camila could see I was lit up. We made a small celebratory dinner — she opened a bottle of red — and I told her every detail of the day. She listened the way she always listens. She asked at one point what was next for me, in the bigger sense. I told her: I want to talk about it on Saturday. She said okay. She did not press. We watched something light, went to bed at eleven. Slept 5 hours. Better than four. Carla pointed out that we never wrote the chaos test for the new path, so I'm going to do that next week before we forget how the rewrite works. There's a difference between being scared and being unprepared, and the test taught me to feel that difference in my body before my brain catches up. Andrés has been quietly cleaning up the old Python code paths now that traffic is gone — we'll archive that module next quarter once we're sure nothing else imports from it. The walnut inlay is harder than I planned — I keep underestimating how the grain wants to chip when I crosscut, and I keep oversetting the depth. Camila got home around eight, kicked her shoes off at the door, and asked what I'd eaten — I had to admit it was just a banana and coffee, and she laughed at me but in the way that means she'll cook. Profe says you can tell a serious student by what they do on the days nobody is watching. I've been holding onto that one since the test. The slow accumulation of competence is the only kind that lasts. The rest is fashion. I'm going to laminate the body in two stages with hide glue because the open time is forgiving and the joint is reversible if I screw up. Latency histograms look healthier than I expected — the long tail past p99 is now compressed against the body of the distribution, which is the whole point of the rewrite. Profe stopped me after class to ask how the family visit went, and I told him truthfully — best three days I've had in a year. I want the finished board to feel heavy in Hugo's hands, weighty enough that he knows it took time, that someone made the decision to make it for him specifically. Spent forty minutes on Loki trying to track down a single span that looked weird in the trace. Turned out to be a logging artifact, not a real problem. Slept four and a half hours last night. Got up at 3:45am, sat in the kitchen with chamomile and a book, went back to bed at 5am, slept until 7. The 'get up immediately' approach is at least kinder than the 'lie in bed forcing it' approach. Going to make it through Wednesday on coffee and yerba mate and try again tonight. Saturday talk with Camila is on the calendar. Until then, just managing. I think I'm finally allowing myself to want the harder thing, which is something my younger self never had the bandwidth to admit out loud. Wednesday at the office. Andrés led the standup for the first time, which I had asked him to do as a small step toward his Q1 project. He did it well — clear, concise, actually finished in 12 minutes. The rust path has been steady-state for nine days. No incidents. We are coasting in the best possible way. I left at 6 and walked home through Roma. The walk helped. Carla pinged me on Slack to ask if we should add a circuit breaker around the new path; I said yes but probably in the next sprint, not this one. The Grafana board has fifteen panels now and I keep meaning to clean it up, but every time I try I find a reason to keep one more.

Wednesday evening at the maker space. Cut the finger pull into the front of the drawer. This was a delicate cut — the drawer face is the most visible part of the storage compartment, and a finger pull is small enough that you can see every flaw. I used a small Forstner bit (12mm) on the drill press for the rough excavation, and then a curved gouge to clean up the inside surface. The first attempt I went too deep with the Forstner and had to compensate with the gouge, which made the inside feel a little uneven. The second attempt — which I did on the actual drawer because there was no good way to test on offcuts — came out clean. I am making peace with the fact that small imperfections will exist on this thing and that they are part of what makes it hand-built. Sanded another four chess pieces while listening to the Mexican railway audiobook. Ten down, twenty-two to go. The audiobook is genuinely interesting; I had not realized how much of CDMX's nineteenth-century development was driven by which neighborhoods got the rail lines first. Hugo's birthday is now four weeks out. I will start the finishing process on the board body this weekend. Three coats of beeswax-and-mineral-oil with two days of cure between coats. Total finishing time: about ten days. Plus mailing time to São Paulo: another five to seven days for DHL. I'm not actually behind. I'm tight, but I'm not behind. Got home at 9:30. Camila was on a video call with her dad — Sunday call moved up because Hugo had a doctor's appointment Sunday morning his time. I waved at the camera. Hugo waved back. I stood in the kitchen for a minute and watched her talk to him in Portuguese, smiling at something he said. He looks well. He looks like a man whose afternoons are still hard but whose mornings are improving. Carla pointed out that we never wrote the chaos test for the new path, so I'm going to do that next week before we forget how the rewrite works. The deployment pipeline still uses the old GitLab runner, which I want to migrate to GitHub Actions but not this quarter. Adding it to the technical-debt list. Andrés has been quietly cleaning up the old Python code paths now that traffic is gone — we'll archive that module next quarter once we're sure nothing else imports from it. I added a second alert on tail latency specifically because the first one only fires on average and I want to catch the moment a single shard goes weird. The shadow rollout taught us that the old service was actually doing a thing nobody documented — a retry-with-backoff that the new one had to learn to imitate. Code review on Andrés's PR took longer than it should have — I left twelve comments and only three were actually important, the rest were preference. I went back and deleted nine of them. We had one transient blip at 04:17 that turned out to be a network hiccup on the Mexico City side, not anything we did. PagerDuty fired and resolved itself in 90 seconds. Disk I/O on the read-replica side is the next thing I want to look at — there's a slow query that wakes up every five minutes and we've been ignoring it for too long. Roberto wants a one-pager for leadership on the rollout, which I'll draft tomorrow. Bullets, not paragraphs. He doesn't read paragraphs.
