The other thing I added at the last minute was the bit about onboarding Andrés. I went back and forth on whether 'onboarded the junior engineer' is a lever-y management story or a flex. The decision logic was: Daniela has historically valued mentorship as a signal more than IC accomplishment. She kept telling me in 1:1s that 'the test of a senior engineer is the third year, not the first year, when you're being graded on whether your team's juniors can fly without you.' That sentence has lived in my head for two years. It's the reason the Andrés bullet is in the brief. I hope she reads it and recognizes her own framework coming back at her. I think she'll smile.

 Reading the brief back tonight I noticed something I had not consciously written but which reads, to me, as load-bearing. The whole brief is structured as evidence of consistency under conditions that would have justified inconsistency. The migration shipped despite the manager change. The migration shipped despite Carla's leave. The migration shipped despite my back injury. The migration shipped despite my mom's medical scare. The migration shipped despite the postgres outage retrospective work. I had not noticed I was building a 'guy who delivers under load' frame, but reading it back, that's the frame. It's also accurate. The arc has been a long stress test of whether I can deliver while the world tries to break me. I have, mostly. The brief is an honest record. The other thing I noticed is the closing paragraph about why I want the masters — the bit about Picard's group and the pose-est project — reads as a quiet pivot from 'I delivered for Brújula' to 'I want to learn to deliver something different.' Daniela will catch this. The admissions reader might not, but Daniela will. She is going to translate it for them. That's why she's the heaviest hitter on the rec list. Translation work between Mexican Series B engineering and MIT admissions speak is the actual labor of writing a rec letter, and Daniela is uniquely positioned to do it because she has sat on both sides — she got her own masters in the US a decade ago, and she's now back in Mexico running engineering teams. She is the right human. I am sending the brief tonight after one more read. I will then send a thank-you bottle of mezcal to her new office, which she will pretend to be annoyed about and then put on her desk where everyone can see it. Daniela has a thing for Convite Pechuga. I know where to get it. I will not tell her in advance.  Camila just walked past and read over my shoulder and said the brief reads like me, which is what I wanted. Honest, slightly informal, with the structural bones of a serious document. She said the closing paragraph about Picard makes the whole thing feel like a real career narrative rather than a CV summary. Daniela will edit ruthlessly anyway. That's the point. I write loose. She tightens. The collaboration is intentional. We have done this dance before — the post-mortem doc was edited 14 times and the final version was 60% her phrasing, 40% mine. Same model. The output will be better than what I would write alone. That's why I asked her.

 One last note before I send. The brief is going to read more honest than what most senior engineers would send their former managers when asking for an MIT rec. That's intentional. Daniela has zero patience for the polished CV register. If I send her a polished CV she will write a polished generic letter, and the polished generic letter will lose to specific letters from other applicants. The unpolished honest brief gives her the raw material to write a specific letter. The specific letter beats the generic letter. The honesty is the wedge. I am sending the wedge tonight.

Hit a paperwork snag — MIT wants my undergraduate transcript translated and notarized. ITESM only has it in Spanish. I emailed the registrar at ITESM Guadalajara and they have a process for this but it takes 10 business days and costs 800 MXN. Filed the request today. Hopefully it lands before December 1. Also realized I need to get my IELTS or equivalent — actually wait, I think I'm exempt because I went to a school where instruction was in English for a portion. Need to double-check the small print. Putting it on tomorrow's list. There is always one more piece of paperwork. I've started keeping a sticky note on the fridge that just lists outstanding application items and I cross them off as they land. Camila walked past it this morning and added 'sleep' at the bottom in red sharpie. I have not yet crossed that one off. The current list reads: 1) transcript translation/notarization (filed, awaiting), 2) IELTS exemption confirmation (need to call admissions), 3) personal statement final draft (in progress), 4) rec letter from Pablo (awaiting his draft), 5) sleep (per Camila). I have a fantasy where on December 1 I cross all five off in one motion and then go for a long bike ride. The fantasy is not realistic because the bike ride is on December 2 at the earliest. December 1 is for nervous laptop refreshing and ritual yerba mate consumption.

 The other paperwork I keep forgetting about: the IELTS exemption letter requires a separate form from MIT admissions, which I have to fill out, sign, scan, and email back. Took me 25 minutes tonight. I had to dig out my Wizeline employment letter from 2018 to prove that my professional working language has been English for the past 9 years, which the admissions office accepts as evidence in lieu of the actual test. Pablo helped by reminding me of which folder in Google Drive has my old employment letters, because I had forgotten that I'd ever needed them and certainly forgotten where they lived. The 2018 Wizeline letter is hilariously formal — 'we hereby certify that Sebastián Río Castellanos is an employee in good standing whose primary working language is English' — signed by an HR director I never met. The letter has now been deployed for the first time in 8 years and is justifying its filing-cabinet existence. There is a small lesson here about why I keep paper trails. There is also a small lesson about why my Google Drive looks the way it looks, which is to say chaotic, but apparently functional in emergencies. I am going to do a Drive cleanup over Christmas. I have said this every year for four years. This year, maybe.  I crossed off transcript translation today which felt like a mini-victory. Three items remain on the fridge sticky note. The pen is right there next to the sticky note. The pen is going to get a workout in the next two weeks.

first mate of the day on the balcony. cool morning. bombilla cup in hand. just thinking. 10 minutes of nothing before i open laptop. the cup has 'Seb' engraved on the rim, abuela lupita gave it to me in 2017, two years before she died. mama lupita's cup. i hold it on mornings like this and just sit with it for a minute. Sky is pale orange over the Roma Norte rooftops. Doña Marta's bread smell coming up through the courtyard. A neighbor's dog barked once and stopped. The sound of the city waking up — a moto two blocks away, somebody calling 'aguacates' down the street, the soft rumble of the metro under our feet that you get used to and stop hearing. The cup is warm in my hands. The thinking is slow. I'm not thinking about the application or the chess board or anything specific. I'm just sitting. This is the most precious 10 minutes of my day. Nube knows it and stays inside. Camila knows it and lets me have it. The bombilla scratches on the

Draft 6 of the personal statement done. New opening landed better than I expected. The kick-with-eyes-closed paragraph works, then I move into the broader question, then one paragraph on the pose-est project as evidence of follow-through, then the research questions, then a one-paragraph close on why MIT specifically and why now. Word count: 1023 — they asked for under 1000 so I'll trim. Camila read it in 4 minutes flat and said 'this is the one' and then qualified with 'maybe one more pass.' She means: this is the one but you'll fight it for another draft because that's what you do. She's not wrong. Pablo read it on his phone during a metro ride and said 'finally sounds like you' which is the highest compliment he gives. I'm letting it sit for 24 hours and then doing the trim pass tomorrow night. Word count target after trim: 990-995. Locked frame, locked content, just polish. The trim pass is the part I actually enjoy — surgical word-cutting, fixing a transition, replacing one weak verb with a stronger one. I have done five trim passes on the post-mortem doc at Brújula and I have an embarrassing amount of fun doing them. Camila says trim-pass-Sebastián is the best version of me to live with because I'm cheerful and focused and not panicking. She is correct. The drafts where the frame is moving are the bad ones to live with. The trim pass is a good apartment week. We have agreed I will do exactly two trim passes — draft 7 and one final read — and then submit. No more. She has the laptop password as a hostage if I try to do a draft 8.

 The other thing I'm thinking about with the personal statement is the small risk of it being too cute. The kick-with-eyes-closed opening could land as profound or as performative — the difference is in execution, not in concept. I am going to read it cold tomorrow morning, not having looked at it for 10 hours, and try to feel whether it's profound or performative. The cold-read is the only honest read. If the cold-read fails, I switch to a different opening. I have two backup openings on standby, both starting from a sensorimotor moment but framed differently — one starts from a coach saying 'feel your hip,' the other starts from a rep counter showing me a number. Either could work. I just need to not be precious about which one I keep. Camila has been asking me 'are you holding any sentence too tightly' and the honest answer is yes, I have probably been holding the kick-with-eyes-closed sentence too tightly for two days. Tomorrow morning I will know.  Camila has been making sure I eat actual meals during this drafting phase and not just snacks-at-the-laptop. I had not noticed I was about to go feral on grilled-cheese-only week. She fixed it. The grilled cheese stays. The other meals are added. Married eating.

It's 3:14am. Doña Marta won't start the oven for another hour and forty minutes. Camila is asleep. Nube is asleep. I am awake and have been since 1:47am, which is when my brain decided to start playing the personal-statement reel on loop, except not even the actual sentences, just a kind of meta-commentary on whether I have any business writing them at all. Going to vent here because if I open Twitter at this hour I will go feral.

The question rattling around. Am I a researcher trapped in a senior IC body, or am I a senior IC who has romanticized research because his current job is run by a politically maneuvering manager and his real talent is just being good at writing CRUD APIs really fast?

Let me list the evidence on each side. Researcher in IC body:
- I have been writing — actually writing, not just shipping — for three years. The Mastodon threads, the post-mortems, the design docs. People reach out about them. The PostGIS crate has 87 stars and a tiny community of users I correspond with.
- I read papers. Not enough, but I read them. I read the Picard 2024 paper four times. I read the MediaPipe original paper three times. I have started reading Bertinetto et al. on whatever-the-hell-it-was on a flight last month, which is not a thing senior backend engineers normally do voluntarily.
- The pose-est side project. I built it from a research question, not from a feature request. I tested hypotheses, ran sessions, plotted graphs. That's research-shaped behavior even if no academic touched it.
- I get bored quickly when problems are solved. Once RouteOpt's rust migration hit 38ms p95 I felt an immediate hollow. Researchers get hollow when problems are solved. ICs celebrate.
- I correspond with at least four academics on email — none about Brújula work, all about side topics. That's not normal IC behavior.
- The reading I do for fun overlaps with the reading I would do as a researcher. Borges, Chiang, Picard, the occasional cog-sci paper. The reading I do for industry — staff-engineer leadership books, distributed systems blog posts — feels like work even when I'm doing it on a Sunday.

IC who romanticized research:
- I'm 33. The youngest first-year MIT PhD in my cohort would be 24. (Ok masters not PhD, masters cohort more varied, but still.) I'm betting against compounding ICs and 35-year-old senior staff comp.
- I've never published. Not even a workshop paper. I have zero academic CV. Not zero — but zero peer-reviewed.
- I am addicted to the legibility of shipping. Every two weeks at Brújula I get to point at something and say 'I made this faster' and it lands in prod. Research is the opposite of legible. The dopamine cycle is months not days.
- The MIT thing might be partly about Roberto. The political-management thing has been getting under my skin since month 8. Am I trying to escape a manager I dislike, dressed up as a career pivot?
- I almost took the Wizeline job in 2017 for the money over a smaller research-y opportunity at a Mexican AI lab. I do this. I have a track record of choosing legibility over depth. Why is this time different?
- The financial calculation, sober: 95k USD eq trajectory in industry, vs. masters then either back to industry at maybe a wash, or PhD which is a 5-7 year delay against compounding. The expected-value math, narrowly defined, favors industry.
- I have not actually worked in a research environment. I have read about research environments. There is a real chance my mental model of research is romanticized.

Counter to the counter. Is it actually different? Yes — Camila has supported it openly which she would not have done if I were doing it for the wrong reasons (she has a sharp ear for that), Daniela said yes to the rec letter and Daniela is allergic to bullshit, Pablo said this morning when I asked for the wildcard letter that he'd 'been waiting for me to do this for years' which I didn't expect at all, my actual research question — nonverbal feedback in physical training — is a thing I have a verifiable kink for, and the project I keep coming back to in unstructured time is not the rust migration, it's the pose-est code.

Also: the financial expected-value math is an illusion of precision. Nobody knows what 'industry compensation' looks like in a 5-year window. Nobody knows what the academic-to-frontier-lab pipeline pays in a 5-year window. The math feels rigorous but it's stacking guesses. The qualitative question — what do you want to do with your hours — is the actually rigorous one because the answer is observable.

Another counter. The Wizeline-was-for-money pattern-match isn't quite right. Wizeline was a 22-year-old's choice with limited information, made under the gravitational field of a parent who'd just had a near-death experience. The MIT pivot is a 33-year-old's choice with full information and a 5-year track record of building things outside of work. The pattern-match is shallow. The deep pattern is different — the Wizeline-Seb wanted external validation, the Brújula-MIT-Seb wants internal alignment. Different driver. Different decision.

I think — and I'm writing this so I can read it back to myself when I doubt — I think the answer is: maybe both things are true. Maybe I am an IC who romanticized research AND a researcher trapped in IC clothing, and the way to find out is to apply, hope to get in, do the masters, and find out which one breaks first. That's the test. The test is the doing. There is no thought experiment that resolves it. There is only the doing.

The other thing — and this is the one that always quiets the spiral — is Mama Lupita. I did not visit her in her last year. She died in 2019 in Guadalajara and I had not been home in eight months because I was 'building career capital' at Wizeline, which translates to: I was working on a USA bank project that I do not now remember the name of. I traded eight months of my grandmother's last year for a project I cannot name. The exchange was not worth it. The exchange will never be worth it. Every time I doubt the MIT pivot I think about the Wizeline project I cannot name and I remember that the legible career path has a way of becoming a thing you trade your grandmother for. I would rather pivot at 33 than trade another grandmother. There are no more grandmothers to trade. I have one grandfather left. I see him at Christmas. I am going more often this year.

It's 3:42 now. I'm going to lie back down. I might be up at 4 with the bread oven. Whatever. Tomorrow I either trim the personal statement to 1000 words or I cry. Probably both. The melatonin didn't take the edge off tonight — sometimes it works, sometimes it doesn't. The CBT-I app I tried for a week in month 15 is sitting unopened on my home screen and I keep meaning to delete it because the icon stares at me accusingly. Maybe I delete it tomorrow. Maybe I open it again. Inconsistency is its own kind of insomnia. Going to bed.

 One last thing before I lie back down. The thing about Roberto. The micromanagement. The political maneuvering. The fact that he has been quietly trying to take credit for the rust migration in standups even though he didn't write a line of code or sit through a single design review. The way he has been pinging me on Slack at 9pm asking 'quick question' and then unloading 800-word strategy memos into the DM. The way he has been scheduling 1:1s and then turning them into status checks. I have been managing him for ten months. I am tired. The MIT application is partly an answer to Roberto. Partly. I want to be honest about this. The version of me that is being honest at 3am admits that one of the variables in the 'should I apply to grad school' decision was 'do I want to keep working under this manager.' The answer was no. The application is partly a reaction to that no. But the application is also more than that. The application is the positive reason — Picard, the research questions, the pose-est project. The Roberto reason is the negative reason. Both are true. I am not going to pretend the negative reason isn't there. I am also not going to let it be the dominant reason. If MIT says no, I will not be re-stuck under Roberto for the next 5 years — I will leave Brújula for a different role somewhere else, regardless. The Roberto-shaped problem is independent of the MIT outcome. I should solve it independently. The MIT path is the positive path. The 'leave Roberto' path is the parallel obligation that exists regardless. I am writing this down so 3am-Sebastián stops conflating them. They are separate questions with separate answers. OK. Going to lie back down. Doña Marta starts the oven in 32 minutes. I might smell the bread before I sleep. That is OK.  One more 3am rumination before I shut up. The thing I keep noticing is that my body is not in revolt. I am sleep-deprived but I am not unwell. The lower back is at 70 percent. The kickboxing is going. The yerba mate works. The cardio is fine. The body is holding the application like a small load that the body can carry. The mind is what is generating the noise. The mind always generates the noise. The body is the steadier instrument. I should listen to the body more than the mind. The body says: this is fine. The mind says: imagine these 47 catastrophes. The body wins. Going to lie back down. Body wins.

 Last absolute last thing. Mama Lupita's bombilla on the kitchen counter. I am going to use it tomorrow morning. Eight years since she gave it to me. Eight years of mornings. The cup has held maybe 3000 cups of mate. The metal at the rim is darker now than it was when she handed it to me. The darkening is the record of every morning I sat with my own thinking. The cup keeps the count. I am going to make her proud tomorrow. I am going to make her proud the next day too. I am going to keep going.

Pablo and I sparred light tonight at Atlas Box and afterwards he asked when his rec letter draft is due. I said anytime in the next two weeks but the sooner the better. He laughed and said he's going to write it on his phone in the metro because that's where he writes everything important. I asked if he wanted me to give him the brief I gave Daniela. He said no — he said his angle is going to be 'I am a peer and I have watched this guy turn an obsession into a project and I think he should be allowed to do it as a job for a while.' Which honestly? That might be the best framing of any of the three letters. He doesn't have credentials but he has 12 sparring sessions worth of being a test user and three years of friendship. The wildcard rec is supposed to be wildcard. I'm going to let him write what he wants. I told him that and he saluted me and then made me run an extra round of pad work as 'tribute.' Fair tribute. After class Profe pulled me aside and asked, in his slow Spanish, whether I was ready to start teaching the white belts when the new beginner cohort starts in November. I said yes if it's just the warmup and basic combinations. He said yes that's what he meant. I have not been a teaching assistant since I tutored linear algebra at ITESM in 2013 and I am, mildly, terrified. Pablo thinks it's hilarious. He said 'el sensei chiquito' which is rude. The lower back is at 70%. I can teach. I cannot spar hard. White belts cannot sense this distinction. They will throw soft punches at me and I will demonstrate the basic stances and we will all leave class on time.

 The teaching assignment Profe described is the basic-combinations class, Tuesdays and Thursdays at 6pm, beginner cohort that runs for 8 weeks. He wants me to handle the first 20 minutes — warmup, footwork, jab-cross. Then he takes over for the technical work. It's a tutorial-assistant model, basically. I asked him whether I needed any kind of certification. He laughed and said 'you have a black belt, that is the certification.' Mexican kickboxing gym pedagogy is delightfully informal. Pablo, when I told him about the new gig, immediately offered to come to the first class as a 'plant' to laugh at me. I have asked Profe to ban him. Profe said he'd think about it, which I believe means he won't ban him. The first class is November 4. Three weeks. Plenty of time to write a warmup playlist. I am taking this very seriously and I will probably overprepare the way I always overprepare for new things. Camila has already laughed at me for opening a Notion doc called 'whitebelt_class_plans/'. I refuse to be embarrassed. White belts deserve good warmups.  Profe asked at the end of class whether I plan to compete now that I have the black belt. I said no. He nodded gravely. He said competition is a path some take and some don't and either is fine. I love that he said this. Some Profes pressure their black belts into competition. He doesn't.

Pre-submission jitters in full force. The application is sitting in the MIT portal in 'ready to submit' state. Personal statement is at draft 7 and locked at 994 words. Recs are queued — Daniela's letter went in this afternoon, Pablo says his is a few days out, Lozano is the wild card timing-wise but she said she'd send by Wednesday. I keep refreshing the portal even though nothing changes when I refresh it. Camila told me to step away from the laptop. I am not stepping away from the laptop. Tomorrow I submit. Probably. Maybe. We'll see how I feel tomorrow morning. I might submit tonight. I might submit at 2am. I might wait until Friday. Who knows. Brain is mush. The cursor is hovering over the submit button. I have hovered over it for approximately three hours of cumulative time today. Camila has made dinner without me twice this week. I owe her at least one nice dinner where I am physically and mentally present. Realized at 11pm tonight that I should also write a brief acknowledgments-and-thanks email to Daniela, Pablo, and Lozano regardless of outcome. The submission of an MIT application is a not-quite-public event in their lives too — they've put their reputation behind my name. I should mark it. I drafted three versions of the email. None of them are good. All of them say some variation of 'thank you, this is in MIT's hands now, and I will tell you when I know.' I will send them tomorrow regardless of whether I have submitted by then. The thank-you email goes first. The submit-button comes second.

 I keep thinking about the people who'd be reading the application packet in February or March. The admissions readers at MIT Media Lab. Picard's group reviewing fit. The faculty making cohort decisions. Strangers in Cambridge whose hands my future runs through. I have an irrational urge to thank them too — to send a one-line note via the portal that just says 'thank you for reading.' I will not do this. It would read as ingratiating and it would not help. But the urge is real and it's interesting that the urge is there. The application is a one-way communication. They evaluate. I wait. The asymmetry of attention is part of what makes the wait so hard. They have my whole written self in front of them. I have nothing of theirs. I will learn their decision through a one-line email in February. That's the relationship. I find it strangely intimate and strangely cold simultaneously.  I keep thinking about how each rec letter is now a small artifact of the relationship that produced it. Daniela's letter is shaped like five years of management. Lozano's letter is shaped like 12 years of low-frequency academic correspondence. Pablo's letter is shaped like 6 years of friendship and 12 sparring sessions. The packet is, accidentally, an autobiography in three voices.

submitted. green checkmark. confirmation email landed. hands shaking slightly. mate going cold next to me. nube on my foot. camila in the kitchen. 6:47pm october 24 2026. it's done. it's in mit's hands. i am going to walk into the kitchen and tell her now.

 The portal page now says 'application complete — under review.' That's it. The button is gone. The thing I have been wrestling with for six weeks is now a record in a database somewhere in Cambridge. Camila is going to come out of the kitchen any second and see my face and she will know. I want to remember the texture of this exact 30 seconds. The hum of the refrigerator. Nube on my foot. The mate gone cold. The portal screen still glowing. Going. Going to her now.  Walking to the kitchen now. She is humming something. Going.

OK so what just happened. I refreshed the page one more time. The 'Submit Application' button was glowing at me. I closed my eyes — like literally closed my eyes — and clicked it. Got the green checkmark. Got the email confirmation. Walked into the kitchen where Camila was making tea and said 'I did it.' She put the kettle down and hugged me without saying anything for a long time. Now I'm sitting on the couch and my hands are kind of shaking and I don't know if it's adrenaline or the four mates I had today. Probably both. Nube can sense something is up and is sitting on my foot looking concerned. I keep checking my email like another confirmation might come through. It will not. The application is now in a queue with thousands of other applications. The decision drops in February. The next 90 days are going to be an exercise in not refreshing the portal. I am going to fail at this exercise. I texted the three people who'd carried me through this — Pablo, Daniela, Lozano — and said 'submitted, thank you for everything.' Pablo replied with three exclamation points. Daniela replied 'orgullosa.' Lozano replied with a paragraph in formal Spanish that I am going to have to read three times to take in. Camila and I are going to Nudo for celebration ramen. I am putting the phone in airplane mode for two hours. The portal cannot tempt me from inside an airplane mode.

 I keep thinking about how it felt physically to click the button. The click itself was nothing — it was a button, it was a normal button, the kind I click 1000 times a day. But my finger took maybe 90 seconds to land on it. I positioned the cursor. I held the mouse. I closed my eyes. I clicked. The click sounded like every other click. The page reloaded. The green checkmark appeared. The email landed in my inbox 14 seconds later. The whole thing took maybe 30 seconds end to end and the entirety of it is now unforgettable. Camila has just come back into the living room with two cups of chamomile and is making me put my phone away. She is right. The phone goes in the other room. The application is gone. The next 90 days are mine.  Camila and I are going to walk to Nudo and back. The cold air is going to feel good on my face. I am going to put my phone in my coat pocket and not check it. The application is in. The waiting starts tomorrow. Tonight is for ramen.

MIT portal updated — Daniela's recommendation letter has been received. Got the little 'Letter received' tag next to her name in the dashboard. She emailed me separately to say 'sent. you got this. don't refresh the portal.' I am of course going to refresh the portal. The fact that her letter is in is genuinely the biggest weight off my chest because she was the heaviest hitter on my list — former eng manager, has actual context on RouteOpt and the rust migration metrics. I asked her if she'd share the letter with me afterward and she said maybe in five years when I either am or am not a Picard student. Fair. She also said her new gig is going well, that she's already two months in and feels like she made the right call leaving Brújula. Then she said 'tell Roberto I said hi' which is a joke, I think, because I am pretty sure Daniela does not like Roberto and Roberto definitely does not like Daniela. I am not going to tell Roberto anything. Things stay where they belong. She added at the bottom of the email that she'd written one paragraph in the rec letter specifically about how I'd handled the manager transition between her and Roberto — said it was 'one of the things she'd most respected' from her last six months at Brújula. I had not realized she'd noticed. Apparently she had noticed. Apparently she'd told her new boss about it. Mortifying and gratifying at the same time. The rec letter is going to land in front of an admissions reader who has no context on Mexican Series B startup politics, and is going to read 'managed transition' in a generic way, but the thing Daniela was actually praising is real and I'm going to take the praise even if no one else hears it the way she meant it.

 I want to log something specific about Daniela's letter that I'm trying not to dwell on too much but is sitting with me. She mentioned in her email that she had asked Carla, behind my back, for a 'sanity check' on whether the paragraph she'd written about me handling the manager transition was accurate. Carla had apparently said 'oh that's understated, the actual story is worse than that' and Daniela had taken note. I had no idea this was happening. Daniela was triangulating with my current colleague behind my back to make sure her rec letter was honest. That level of care from a former manager — six months out from working together, with no incentive to do extra work — is something I do not want to forget. I will reciprocate when she eventually wants something from me. Could be in five years, could be in ten. The favor bank with Daniela is one I want to keep deep.  I keep wondering whether Daniela's letter mentions the Mastodon thread on Tokio runtime starvation. She read it when I posted it and complimented me on the technical writing in our last 1:1. The letter probably references it. I will never know. The letter is sealed.

And Lozano's letter is in. Dr. Patricia Lozano, my ITESM thesis advisor from 2014. She emailed me a heads-up before submitting. Twelve years since I sat in her office. She said in the email that she'd had to think hard about what to write because I'd been a 'good student but not the most academically ambitious one she'd advised that year.' (Brutal. Accurate.) She said the letter focuses on intellectual curiosity sustained over a decade — the fact that I've kept emailing her once or twice a year about random research papers and asking her opinion. I had not realized I'd been doing that. Apparently I have been doing that. She thinks the consistency itself is the evidence. Honestly I love that framing. Three rec letters in. Just Pablo's left. She also said — and I'm going to quote because it's making me a little emotional — 'you have always been a question-asker. The masters is the right venue for question-askers.' I am not crying I am just sitting with that for a while. She also asked, almost in passing, whether I'd be open to an interview or a follow-up call with anyone at MIT who wanted to triangulate on her letter. I said yes obviously. Twelve years of academic distance just collapsed into one email. She closed with 'estoy contenta de saber que sigues siendo curioso, Sebastián. Cuídate.' I had to read that line about four times before I could move on with my evening.

 I want to remember something about the email exchange with Lozano. We've kept in light touch since 2014 — once or twice a year, like she said — but I had not realized she was reading the random papers I was forwarding her with attention. She said something in the email like 'your three notes about the 2023 Bertinetto paper actually changed how I'm framing my own seminar this semester.' I had no idea. I had thought I was just shooting random papers into a void out of vague intellectual loneliness. Apparently the void was reading. She also said she'd been waiting for me to either write something serious or apply somewhere serious, and was glad it was the second. The 12-year academic distance was not as distant as I'd thought. There's a small lesson in here about long, low-frequency, low-pressure correspondence with people who matter. The slow drip is its own form of relationship maintenance. I will keep emailing her papers. I will probably do it more often.  I am going to email Lozano back tomorrow and ask if she'd be willing to meet for coffee in Guadalajara when I'm up at Christmas. We have not been in the same room since 2014. I want to close the distance. She will say yes. I am pretty sure of it.

the waiting is going to be its own kind of torture, isn't it. four months until the decision. a hundred and twenty mornings of opening the portal and seeing 'under review' and closing it again. four months of pretending to be normal at brújula stand-ups. four months. i can feel the insomnia warming up already. The decision drops in February — they said early February, the form letter said 'we typically notify by mid-February' — so call it February 12 plus or minus a week. That's about 110 days from now. 110 mornings of opening the portal. 110 nights of going to sleep with the portal closed. The MIT decision dropdown is going to become a daily ritual whether I want it to or not. Camila has already said she's going to put a child-lock on the laptop, which would be funny except she's somewhat serious. I have agreed to refresh the portal at most twice a day. Morning with mate, evening before bed. That is the rule. We will see if I can keep it. Honestly, knowing my brain,

Camila asked me tonight what the personal statement journey was actually like — not the content, the process. I told her: seven drafts. SEVEN. Let me retrospect on each because I want to remember the shape of this for the next time I have to write something hard.

Draft 1. Written month 17, week 1. Two evenings, both with mezcal. 1600 words, way over. Opened with the black belt moment — Profe handing the belt, Pablo recording on his phone, the family flying in, the way Profe held my shoulders for three seconds before tying the belt. Spent 400 words on that scene. Camila read it and said 'this is a great essay but it's not for MIT.' She was right. It was for me. I'd been wanting to write the black-belt scene since the night it happened and the personal statement was just the first excuse. Saved that draft as 'belt_essay_dont_throw_away.md' and started over. I'm going to come back to that essay one day. Maybe as a Mastodon post. Maybe as something for a blog I don't have yet. The scene deserves its own home.

Draft 2. Different week. Written sober, on my ThinkPad at café Cardinal. 1100 words. Way too academic. Two paragraphs of literature review. Citations to three Picard papers. No personal voice. Sounded like a grad-school applicant who had read 'how to write a personal statement' Substacks. Pablo read this one and said 'who is this' and I knew it was over. The funny thing is I was so proud of draft 2 when I finished it. It felt like the kind of thing a serious applicant would write. That feeling is the warning signal. If a draft feels like the-kind-of-thing-someone-would-write, it's not the kind of thing you would write, and admissions readers can sniff it out.

Draft 3. Third week. Tried to compromise between 1 and 2. 1300 words. Half-personal, half-academic. The seam between the halves was visible. Camila called it 'the centaur draft.' Front legs of a horse, back legs of a person, awkward. She wasn't being cruel. She was being precise. The metaphor stuck.

Draft 4. Fourth week. Threw out drafts 1-3 and started from a single sentence I'd written at 2am: 'The body is a black box from the inside.' Built outward from there. 950 words. Tighter, but Pablo said the opening line sounded 'philosophy-grad-student-y.' He was right. I sat on it for two days. I was tempted to defend it because I liked the sentence. I had to remind myself that liking your own sentences is the second-warning signal of personal-statement writing. The good sentences in your statement should be the ones the readers like, not the ones you like. Different judges.

Draft 5. Same skeleton as 4, different opening. Tried 'When I was a blue belt I asked my coach if my hip rotation was right and he said sure, sebas, it's right' as the hook. Pablo said 'this one's better.' Camila said it was undermining itself in the second paragraph — said I was hedging on the research questions in a way that read as insecure. 1010 words. Submitted to a friend-of-a-friend who's an MIT PhD for a quick read — she said the research questions were strong but the personal scaffolding was 'overcorrecting from autobiographical to performative-humble.' That note was useful. She also said something I have not been able to forget: 'the strongest personal statements are the ones where you can hear the writer's voice arguing with themselves on the page.' I had been editing out my own arguments, trying to sound certain. The certainty was the problem.

Draft 6. Took the friend's note. Revised the personal scaffolding. Allowed the arguing-with-myself to come back in. New opening — 'I closed my eyes during a kick combination and tried to feel my hip angle. I couldn't.' Then pivoted faster into the broader question. 1023 words. Camila said 'this is the one.' I didn't trust her because she's said that about three other things this year — including a Picard paper title I tried out as a working subtitle, which she'd liked and then I'd cut anyway because Pablo had been confused by it.

Draft 7. Trim pass. Cut 29 words. Tightened two transitions. Re-read it three times in three different settings (laptop, phone, printed on paper) to catch tone issues that don't show up in just one medium. Locked at 994 words. Submitted with the application this evening. The final draft has the kick-with-eyes-closed opening, one paragraph on the pose-est project as proof of follow-through, three paragraphs on research questions about nonverbal sensorimotor feedback, one paragraph on why MIT and why Picard's group, and a final 80 words on what I'd want to do after the masters.

What I learned. The thing I'll tell anyone who asks me about this is: the first draft is for you. You have to write it before you can write the real one. I needed to get the black-belt-moment essay out of my system before I could write a personal statement that wasn't secretly about kickboxing. I'd been carrying that essay around since month 15 and it was getting in the way. Once I wrote it and shelved it, I could think about MIT clearly.

The other thing I learned. Pablo and Camila are different readers. Pablo flags voice problems — when I sound like I'm trying too hard, or when I sound like someone other than me. Camila flags structural problems — when the seams show, when the argument doesn't hang together. I needed both. If I'd had only Pablo I would've sent draft 5. If I'd had only Camila I would've sent draft 4. Together they got me to draft 7. Owe them both.

Third thing. Word counts are tyrants but they're useful tyrants. Every cut from 1300 to 950 made the draft better. Every cut from 1010 to 994 also made the draft better. The worst draft I wrote was the 1600-word one because no constraint was forcing me to choose. The best draft I wrote was the one that hit the hard ceiling exactly. Constraints sharpen.

Fourth thing. Read it in three media. Laptop reading misses tone problems that the phone catches. Phone reading misses structural problems that printed paper catches. Printed paper misses run-on sentences that the laptop catches in the line wrap. I caught two genuine errors in the printed-paper read of draft 7 that all the laptop reads had missed. I will do this for everything important from now on. I had not done this before. I will do it now.

Fifth thing — and this one I want to remember most. The seven drafts took six weeks and felt like six months. The decision to count them as iterations and not as failures was psychologically load-bearing. Every time a draft came back wrong I had a 30-minute window where my brain wanted to catastrophize and conclude I was an impostor. Camila or Pablo would say 'cool, what did you learn from that one,' and the framing of 'what did you learn' would convert the catastrophe into a data point. I would learn something. I would write the next draft. The thing that kept me writing was the framing, not the content. Carry this lesson forward into anything hard.

Sixth thing. The black-belt-essay artifact, saved as 'belt_essay_dont_throw_away.md', is the most valuable byproduct of this process. It exists because it had to. It needed to be written for me to write the real thing. I have learned that some essays are precursor essays whose only job is to free the next one. I will probably write more precursor essays in my life. I will not be embarrassed by them. They are the cost of the real essay.

What's done is done. The portal says 'submitted.' Now I wait. Camila just brought me chamomile tea because I will not stop talking about the application. I am going to drink the tea and shut up about it for one whole evening. Probably.

 One more thing about the seven-draft journey. The act of writing draft 1 — the wrong draft, the autobiographical-belt-essay draft — was paradoxically the most important act in the whole sequence. If I had not written draft 1 the way I wrote it, with its 400 words about Profe handing me the belt and Pablo recording on his phone, I would not have been able to write the disciplined draft 7. Draft 1 was the catharsis. Draft 7 was the discipline. The catharsis had to come first. The discipline could not have come first. I would have been editing a corpse instead of editing a living thing. I want to remember this for any other hard writing project I take on. Write the wrong draft first. Write it loose, write it long, write it from your gut, do not show it to anyone, save it as 'draft_1_dont_throw_away.md', and then start over. The draft 1 will live as a private artifact. The draft 7 will be the public artifact. The world will see the draft 7 and assume you wrote it directly. They will be wrong. They will not know about the draft 1. The draft 1 was the parent of the draft 7. The parent is invisible. The parent has to be invisible for the draft 7 to look professional. But the parent is real. I have file 'belt_essay_dont_throw_away.md' as evidence. I am keeping it. I will probably never publish it. I will read it in five years and remember why the personal statement exists. The personal statement exists because the belt essay was written first. Submit the personal statement. Save the belt essay. Tell the next person who has to write something hard: write the wrong draft first.  One more meta-observation about the whole drafting arc. The seven drafts took me 47 days. The first draft I have logged in git is October 1. Draft 7 was committed on October 23. Submission was October 24. So 23 days from first draft to submitted final, with the bulk of the drafts produced in week 3 and week 4. The first two weeks were structural exploration. The last two weeks were execution. The pattern is identical to how I write technical design docs at Brújula — long exploration, then a sudden week where the doc converges. I had not noticed I was using the same writing pattern across both contexts. Useful self-knowledge.

 Last meta-thought. The friend-of-a-friend MIT PhD note about 'arguing with yourself on the page' has stuck with me beyond this application. I think it generalizes. Arguments-with-yourself are the texture of any honest writing. Documents that don't argue with themselves are advertisements. The personal statement that I submitted has, I hope, two visible arguments — the autobiographical-vs-academic tension, and the rehabilitation-vs-sports-science framing. Both arguments are visible. I left them visible because I trust the reader to follow them. If the reader can't follow them I can't help them. The argument is the work.

Pablo's letter just hit the portal. Last of the three. He texted me 'hecho' and a picture of him in the metro looking smug. He also forwarded me the letter, which he should not have done, but Pablo is Pablo. I read it. It's three paragraphs. The first is about the pose-est project — about how I built it because I wanted an answer to a question, not because anyone asked me to, and how he was a test user and watched me iterate on it for seven months. The second is about — and I'm paraphrasing so I don't quote it directly — how I'm the kind of friend who reads papers on the bus and texts him about them at 1am. He calls this 'pathological intellectual generosity' which is the warmest insult I've ever received. The third is one sentence: 'Sebastián should be a researcher and you should let him.' That's it. Three paragraphs. The wildcard letter is exactly what a wildcard letter should be. I have no notes. Pablo also signed off the email he sent me with 'admissions can stop reading at the third paragraph imo' which is exactly what he'd say. All three letters in. Application complete. Now I genuinely just wait. The portal status field went from 'awaiting recommenders' to 'application complete — under review.' I screenshotted the status change. Camila will hate that I screenshotted it. I am going to delete the screenshot tomorrow morning, after I have looked at it twelve more times tonight. Maybe thirteen.

 I'm trying to decide whether to thank Pablo with something — beers don't feel like enough, but he'd refuse anything bigger. The wildcard letter from a peer is a particular kind of vulnerable on his end. He wrote three paragraphs about me to a stranger at MIT. He took time on the metro to draft it on his phone. He attached his name to my application packet. The asymmetry of debt is a thing I keep wrestling with — Daniela had a manager-shaped reason, Lozano had an academic-advisor-shaped reason, but Pablo just had a friendship-shaped reason. There's no professional capital in writing a peer rec letter. He just did it because he believes I should be doing this. I am going to make him a small cutting board for his birthday in March. Not as repayment — there is no repayment — but as a hand-made acknowledgment of the kind of thing the rec letter was. Camila has agreed this is the right move. The cutting board project starts in December as a parallel to the second chess board for my dad.

Camila and I had dinner at Nudo tonight to celebrate the submission. She had the spicy miso. I had the shio. She raised her glass and said 'to my partner the future MIT student.' I said 'don't jinx it.' She said 'it's not a jinx, it's a wish.' She told me she's been quietly proud watching this whole thing because — and she said this verbatim — 'you've been the one supporting my career moves for five years and this is the first one that's been really yours.' Her Pritzker mentor nomination, my Brújula promo, the Bogotá decline — those were collaborative life-decisions. The MIT app was mine. She said 'I wanted you to have something that was just yours.' I had to look at the menu for a while because I didn't have a response that wasn't going to be embarrassing in public. After dinner we walked back through Roma Norte and the streetlights had that warm October sodium-yellow that makes everything look like a memory in advance. We didn't talk much on the walk. We didn't need to. When we got home she went to brush her teeth and I sat on the couch with Nube and watched the apartment for a while. Hugo's chess board is still on the dining table. Hugo is back in São Paulo by now. The board has the Italian opening still on it from the night of the party. I haven't moved a piece. Camila walked past it on the way to bed and said 'we should leave it like this for a while.' I agreed. Sometimes a board mid-game is the most accurate state for an apartment to be in.

 The other thing she said tonight that's been turning over in my head: 'whatever happens with MIT, you're not the same person you were a year ago.' She said it in a particular tone — not cliche, not romantic, more like an observation a mentor would make. I asked her what she meant. She said 'you're more honest about what you want. You used to dress up wants as obligations. You stopped doing that this year.' That landed. I had not noticed I was doing the dressing-up. Apparently I was. She named it before I did. There's a kind of intimacy in being named by someone better at noticing than you are. I'd been thinking the MIT pivot was a logistics question. She'd been seeing it as an honesty question. She is, of course, right. The pivot was always about getting honest.  On the walk home she asked me, in a way that was both casual and load-bearing, whether I was happy. I said yes. She said good. She said she had been wanting to ask for weeks and had been waiting for the right moment. The walk was the right moment. The streetlights were the right light. The submission was the right pretext. Sometimes happiness is asked about correctly only after the prerequisites are in place.

walking nube. light rain. she's nervous but holding it together. trazodone helping. small win. 21 months in and we are slowly fixing the thunder thing. nube is looking up at me every fifteen seconds checking that the rain is a controlled rain and not the bad rain. controlled rain. small good moments add up.

 The streetlights are reflecting off the wet pavement and Nube keeps stopping at every reflection like it might be a portal. Some other dog walker just passed us with a bigger dog and Nube did her usual move where she pretends to ignore the bigger dog while flexing one paw forward in case there's eye contact. There was no eye contact. The bigger dog ignored Nube ignoring it. Mutual respect. Roma Norte is quiet at this hour, just the late-shift workers and the dog walkers and a guy in a hoodie smoking outside La Polar. Going home now. Drying her off. Treats. Bed.  Bathroom break for Nube. Treats. Towel-drying. Bed.

OK. People keep asking what the personal statement actually said and I'm tired of summarizing it badly. Here is the final draft, draft 7, 994 words, submitted to MIT Media Lab Affective Computing group on October 24, 2026. Pasting in full because some of you have asked. I will probably read it back in five years and cringe. But this is the version of me that submitted it.

---

I closed my eyes during a kick combination and tried to feel my hip angle. I couldn't.

My coach at Atlas Box CDMX, Esteban Domínguez, had been telling me for two years that my left hook chambered late and my hip rotation lagged on the second kick of a combination. I would nod, throw the combination again, and feel — to me — exactly the same as before. The disconnect between what my coach saw and what I felt was the most persistent technical problem in my training, and the most persistent technical problem of human motor learning more broadly. The body is a black box from the inside, especially when you're learning. The body doesn't tell you what it's doing. It tells you what it intended to do.

I am a senior backend engineer. I work at Brújula Logistics, a Series B last-mile delivery startup in Mexico City, where I lead a small team migrating our route-optimization service from Python to Rust. The work is about making large systems behave under load. I am good at it. But the question that has consumed my unstructured hours for the past 18 months is the question I just described — how to give athletes, and patients, and learners of any physical skill, an honest answer to the question 'what is my body actually doing?' that doesn't depend on a coach being in the room.

In pursuit of that question I built a side project: a pose-estimation system for kickboxing form analysis. I started with TensorFlow, found it too slow on commodity hardware, and rebuilt on top of MediaPipe Pose. I instrumented twelve sparring sessions of my own and three friends'. I plotted hip-rotation angle against kick-chamber timing. I generated frame-by-frame visualizations of the moments where motion deviated from intent. The system told me what my coach had been telling me for two years: my second-kick chamber timing was inconsistent. But unlike my coach's verbal feedback, which I had heard a thousand times and could not internalize, the visualization made the deviation legible. I could see the gap between intention and motion. Within four sessions of using my own tool, the gap had closed by an order of magnitude. I earned my black belt — first dan — at Atlas Box four months ago.

This is not a personal statement about kickboxing. The kickboxing is illustrative. The point is the broader question of nonverbal sensorimotor feedback, and the proposition that machine learning systems are uniquely positioned to close the proprioceptive gap that human teachers cannot. A coach can describe the body. They cannot show you the body to itself in real time, framed in coordinates the body's owner can act on.

The research questions I want to study at the Media Lab are these. First: what classes of motor-learning problem are best suited to ML-mediated nonverbal feedback, and what classes are not? My intuition, formed by my pose-est work, is that high-frequency, fast-feedback skills — combat sports, dance, instrumental music — benefit most. Slow, embodied skills — yoga, weightlifting form — may benefit less. I want to test this systematically. Second: what is the right modality of feedback for the body's loop? Visual? Haptic? Auditory? My pose-est work used visual feedback. Haptic feedback, delivered post-rep, may work better for sports where eye contact with the opponent is required. I want to study modality choice as a research question, not as a UX afterthought. Third: how do we evaluate this work? The ML community has good metrics for pose estimation and for emotion detection. We do not have good metrics for whether a learner is actually closing the gap between intention and motion. I want to contribute on the evaluation side.

These questions sit at the intersection of affective computing and motor learning, and I believe Rosalind Picard's group at the Media Lab is the place to study them. I have read and re-read Professor Picard's work on extending affective computing beyond emotion to broader bodily self-knowledge — the threads in her later writing that argue for affective systems as augmentations of human introspection rather than substitutes for it. That framing is exactly the framing I have been groping toward, alone, for the past 18 months. I want to study with the person who has been articulating it.

I come to this without an academic publication record. My evidence of capability is the work I have done at Brújula — shipped systems, post-mortems, technical writing — and the side project that birthed the question I want to study. The masters program is, for me, a deliberate pivot. I have nine years of industry behind me, a clear research interest, and the time and willingness to start over in a research context. I am 33. I am not the youngest applicant in my cohort. I do not believe I am the least serious.

In five years I want to be the person who has built systems that close proprioceptive gaps for athletes recovering from injury, for stroke patients relearning motion, for surgical residents shortening their training time. I want to do this in a research context first, and then in industry, where the question of whether the work meets people will be settled.

The body is a black box from the inside. I want to learn how to crack it open, gently, with tools that respect the bodies they're attached to. I would like to study at the Media Lab.

— Sebastián Río Castellanos

---

That's the thing. 994 words. I will never read it again voluntarily. It is now MIT's problem. Anyone who's asked me to summarize it in two sentences over the last week — sorry, here's all of it. The two-sentence version always failed. The full version is the only honest version. Read it or don't. Tell me if it lands. Or don't. I am bracing for opinions and also for the absence of opinions, and I am unsure which will be worse for my brain. Camila has set a rule that nobody is allowed to discuss the personal statement in the apartment after 9pm because she correctly identified that I will spiral. The rule starts tonight. It is currently 9:04pm. This post counts as me publishing the statement before the curfew kicks in. Loophole observed. The rule is now in effect. Going to put the laptop away.

 Reading it back now, four days after submission, the thing that strikes me is how compressed it is. 994 words to summarize a research interest, a project, a career pivot, an autobiographical hook, three research questions, an advisor preference, and a reason for switching domains at 33. Most of the content is implied rather than stated. The kickboxing paragraph implies five years of training. The Brújula paragraph implies a senior IC role with nontrivial deliverables. The 'I am 33' line implies the whole career-pivot conversation that I'd been having with Camila for six months. The reader has to fill in the gaps. The gaps are the work. A bad personal statement makes the reader fill in the wrong gaps. A good personal statement makes the reader fill in approximately the right gaps. I think this one does the second thing. I will not know for sure until February. The other thing I notice reading it back is that the voice is mine — I can hear myself in the sentences, the way I'd talk if I were telling Pablo about the project over beers, except cleaned up by 30%. Pablo's note in draft 5 — 'finally sounds like you' — was the right note. The voice is doing more work than the content in admissions packets. Picard reads thousands of these. The ones she remembers are the ones with voice. I think this one has voice. The risk is that the voice is too informal — that 'the body is a black box from the inside' reads as performative and not as plain. I have decided not to worry about it. The line is the line. If they don't like the voice they don't admit me and I work on the voice for the next round. The voice is not infinitely tunable. I'm not going to write in someone else's voice to win an admissions decision.  The thing I keep wanting to add to the personal statement, even retroactively now that it's submitted, is one sentence about Hugo and the chess board. Some line like 'this past month I made a chess board for my father-in-law's 70th birthday and learned how a hand-made object can carry meaning that purchased objects cannot.' But it would have been gratuitous in the personal statement. The personal statement was about ML and motor learning. The chess board was about a different question. I cut the line. The line lives here in this log. Some sentences belong to the personal statement and some belong to the journal. I am learning the difference.

 Last thought before bed. I sent the cold email to Picard at 4pm. She has not replied. She probably will not reply. The email is now sitting in her inbox where it might or might not get read. The lottery ticket is purchased. The expected return is whatever it is. The act of having purchased it is now a fact in the world that cannot be unmade. I am going to forget about it for three months. The portal is closed. Camila is asleep. Nube is on the rug. The night is quiet.

Day after submission. Slept 7 hours last night which is the most consecutive sleep I've had in months. Apparently the cortisol of having an unsubmitted application sitting in a portal was non-trivial, and removing it has measurable effects on my sleep architecture. I woke up at 7am, made yerba mate, walked Nube, and spent two hours doing literally nothing on the couch. Camila said it's the first time in three months she's seen me without a laptop on my legs. Going to give myself a week of nothing before I start thinking about the next thing. Or, well, that's the plan. We'll see if the brain cooperates. I noticed a small thing this morning — I instinctively reached for my laptop after breakfast and there was nothing on the immediate horizon to work on. The reach was a habit. The void on the other side of the reach was new. I'm trying to sit with the void instead of filling it. Lasted about 40 minutes before I opened a Picard paper anyway. Camila laughed when she walked past and saw me with the paper. She said 'sitting with the void, are we?' I said 'shut up.' She said 'OK enjoy your void.' She kissed me on the head and went to do actual work. The Picard paper is a 2019 one I hadn't read carefully — about extending stress-detection wearables into bodily-state-awareness feedback. There are two paragraphs in section 4 that read like she has been waiting for me to finish my masters and join her group. I underlined them. I will not write to her about them yet. The cold-email-to-Picard idea I had at 3am still feels overconfident. Let it sit.

 Mid-afternoon I sat down and started drafting the cold-email to Picard. Three sentences. Took me 90 minutes to land on the right three sentences. The first sentence is just 'Dear Professor Picard, I am writing to introduce myself as an applicant to your group's masters program for the 2027 cohort.' Plain. The second sentence: 'I came to your work via your 2024 paper on extending affective computing into bodily self-knowledge, which articulated a thread I had been groping toward in my own side-project research on ML-mediated proprioceptive feedback in physical training.' Specific. Third sentence: 'I am not asking for anything — I am simply noting that one of your papers was load-bearing for me, and I look forward to the possibility of contributing to your group.' Closed. Sent. The whole email is 110 words. I drafted seven versions. The version I sent is the one that doesn't ask for anything. That's the version that respects her time. Camila proofread. She approved. I clicked send before I could second-guess. Now whatever happens, the email exists. The asymmetric upside lottery ticket is purchased.  Camila brought me a bowl of soup for lunch and laughed at me for having read seven pages of the Picard paper since breakfast. She said 'so the void is full of Picard now' and walked away. The void is, in fact, full of Picard now. The void compromised. I compromised back. Mutual.

Back at Atlas Box tonight for the first time in two weeks. Profe noticed immediately. He said 'estás otra vez,' you're back, and made me do a long warmup before letting me onto the bag. Light rounds only. The lower back is at maybe 70% — PT has been getting me there slowly, the Clínica Querétaro physio is a saint — but I could throw clean combinations again without that pinching sensation. The black-belt patch on my gym bag still surprises me when I see it. It's been two months. You'd think I'd be used to it. Pablo asked if I'm planning to compete now. I said no. He said 'pendejo' affectionately. Then he hit me with a body shot. Profe pulled me aside at the end of class and asked, in his slow careful Spanish, whether the MIT thing was 'real real or just real.' I said real real. He nodded. Said 'be back to teach the white belts when you have time.' I will. The Fairtex shin guards are still holding up after two and a half years of daily use, faded red now where they used to be brick red. The Twins Special gloves smell like something between sweat and copper and there's not enough Lysol in the world to fix that. I do not want to fix that. They smell like home. I went home, took a long shower, and ate cold leftover poblano pasta straight out of the tupperware standing at the kitchen counter. Camila walked in, watched me for a second, and said 'you look happier than you've looked in three months.' I think she's right. Sparring fixed something the application had not.

 On the walk home from Atlas Box Pablo asked, almost in passing, whether I thought I'd miss the gym in Boston. I told him: yes, deeply, and that's part of the math. There's a kickboxing gym he'd already googled in Cambridge — he sent me the link last week, unsolicited — that has decent reviews and a good Mexican-Brazilian-Cuban representation. I'm going to walk in there if I get accepted. I am not going to start over from white belt though. I am going to walk in with my black-belt patch on my bag and let the gym tell me what they want to do with me. Maybe I just train. Maybe I help teach. The body knows what it knows and a different gym can take or leave that knowledge. Pablo says I should not train at the new gym for the first month — should just observe — and I think he's right. The body needs to read the room before throwing a kick.  Profe also asked me, before I left, whether I've been sleeping. I told him sometimes. He said 'sleep more.' He says this every two months. He is right every two months. I have not yet figured out the trick to sleeping more on demand. He thinks the trick is just to do it. He may be right.

tired in a clean way tonight. body tired, not brain tired. forgot what this felt like. there's a difference between the bone-tired you get after a hard workout and the static-tired you get after a long mental project, and i had been living in the second one for so long i'd forgotten what the first one was. body tired is friendly tired. Camila came in, sat next to me, looked at me, and said 'mi amor, this is your post-application body, this is the body that has put a thing down.' She rubbed my shoulder. I made one bad joke about cortisol curves. She tolerated it. We sat there for a few minutes. Nube came over and put her chin on my knee. The apartment was quiet in a different quiet than usual — not the strained quiet of a working evening, not the social quiet of a party morning, just the genuine quiet of a Tuesday with nothing left to deliver. I had forgotten that texture. I want to remember it. Tomorrow is just

OK so this has been brewing for weeks and I want to lay it out properly because I'm going to need to come back and read this when I'm doubting myself. The career-pivot question. The rumination.

The path I'm on without changing anything: Senior Backend Engineer at Brújula, currently 95k USD eq. Roberto as manager. Likely promotion to Staff in 12-18 months given how RouteOpt landed. Brújula goes Series C in 2027 maybe, equity probably worth something, maybe a lot, maybe nothing. I stay 3-5 more years. I become a Staff Engineer at a Series C/D company, comp scales to maybe 130-150k USD eq, I keep shipping, I get good at the politics, I learn to manage Roberto-types from a position of seniority. This is the legible path. Most of my ITESM cohort is on some version of this path. It is not bad. I am not unhappy on it.

The path I'm trying to open: MIT Media Lab masters. Two years, MIT. Probably ~80k tuition + living, partially funded if I'm lucky, fully self-funded plus loans if I'm not. Career break of 2 years. Then either a PhD (5-7 more years), or an industry research job (Anthropic, OpenAI research, FAIR, MSR, DeepMind), or back to industry IC at a research-flavored team (Adept-style, Glean-style). Or, the weird option, a full faculty pivot which I almost certainly will not pursue at 35-37.

The rumination is: which path is correct, given who I actually am, not who I want to be on Sundays.

What I know about myself, from the data of the past 18 months:
- I get bored when problems are solved. RouteOpt rust shipped to staging at 38ms p95 and I felt hollow within a week. Researchers report this hollowness as a feature, ICs report it as a bug.
- I read papers in my unstructured time. I have read more papers in 2026 than I read in all of 2014 in school.
- I do not actually love the IC craft for its own sake. I love the questions. I love the questions and I tolerate the craft as the price of getting the questions answered. This is the inverse of how my Brújula peers describe their relationship to engineering.
- I am bad at the politics. Not catastrophically — I survive Roberto — but I find it genuinely draining in a way my peers don't. Two engineers I know find office politics fun. They are senior engineers because they find it fun. I find it tedious.
- I have done two career pivots already (Kueski → Wizeline → Brújula) and each one felt vertiginous in the moment and obvious in retrospect. Pattern-match suggests this one is the same.
- I have ~45k USD saved. Plus 8k USD in student loans, manageable. I can afford one expensive year of MIT before I'd need to take loans. Two years is harder. Camila has stable income at Foster + Partners.
- The work that gives me genuine joy on a Tuesday afternoon is reading a paper, drafting a Mastodon thread, looking at the PostGIS crate I open-sourced and seeing it has 87 stars. Not the rust commit log. Not the prod metric dashboard. The thinking work.

What I don't know:
- Whether I will get in. Acceptance rate at the Media Lab masters is brutal.
- Whether I will love research once I am inside it. There is a non-zero chance I get to MIT, do my first semester, and discover I miss shipping. The IC romanticization theory.
- Whether I will hate being a student again at 33-34, surrounded by people younger than me. The status step-down.
- Whether Camila's career trajectory holds in Boston. Foster + Partners has a Boston office but no architect-of-her-level position open right now.
- Whether my parents will quietly worry. (They will. That's known. I can't optimize for it.)
- Whether I'm romanticizing 'research' the way I once romanticized 'consulting' before Wizeline disabused me of that romance in about six months.

The least-bad path forward, as far as I can tell tonight: assume I get in. If I do, take it. The downside of taking it and discovering I'm an IC at heart is two years and some money and a great story; the downside of not taking it is wondering for the next 30 years. Asymmetric regret. If I don't get in, apply again next cycle, refine the angle. If I don't get in twice, take the data seriously and reroute — maybe industry research at a frontier lab via the public-paper-and-cold-email route, or an internal research position at a larger company via networking.

The other-other-other consideration: what would I tell a friend in this exact situation? If Pablo came to me with this exact stack of facts and pros and cons, I'd tell him to go. I'd tell him the regret-asymmetry argument. I'd tell him the career-pivot pattern-match argument. I'd tell him to stop interrogating his own motives and let the application play out. I'd tell him you can always come back to industry but you cannot easily go back to 33 with a clean two-year window in front of you.

That's the answer. The advice I'd give a friend is the advice I should take. The interrogation of motives is a procrastination ritual. I have submitted the application. The only thing left to do is wait, and to live a normal life in the waiting.

The thing that helps me sleep tonight is: Camila and Pablo and Daniela all, separately, when I asked them about this, said 'you're going to do this.' Three independent reads. None of them are flatterers. None of them have anything to gain. If three people who know me well all see the same trajectory, the trajectory is probably real. I should probably stop interrogating my own motives and let the application play out.

The other thing that helps me sleep — the smaller thing — is the memory of Mama Lupita, who died in 2019, and how I never visited her in her last year because I was too busy building career capital at Wizeline. Career capital that I have now traded for the chance to do something I actually want. The trade was uneven on her end. I owe it to her to make it less uneven on mine.

The last thing — and this is the thing I keep coming back to when I imagine the rejection scenario — is that even if MIT says no, I will have done the work of the application, which has clarified my own thinking in a way nothing else has. The personal statement forced me to articulate what I actually want to study. That articulation will not disappear if MIT rejects me. It will inform the next application, the next side project, the next mid-life pivot in 2030. The application is itself a tool, separate from its outcome. Whatever February brings, I am 18 months smarter about my own work than I was in October 2024. That's a real win that the binary outcome cannot take from me.

I'll close the laptop and go to bed and try not to refresh the MIT portal one more time. I will fail at the second one.

 The career-pivot rumination is also being shaped by the texture of recent days. The post-application float, the chess board moment, Hugo at the party, the way Camila and I had the long talk last night. The future feels less abstract this week than it has in months. I can almost see two specific paths — the Boston version and the CDMX version — with enough texture that I'm picking up on the daily-life rhythms of each. Boston version: Cambridge winter, walking to MIT in a parka, Camila working on East Coast institutional projects, weekend trips to Cape Cod, learning to live without yerba mate access on every corner, Nube in snow boots (do they make those?). CDMX version: continuing in Brújula or moving to a different role, finally buying a small apartment, Sunday brunches at Doña Marta's, weekend bike rides to Xochimilco, kids in 2027-28, watching my parents age in 2-hour-flight reach. Both versions are full lives. Both versions include the same names — Camila, Nube, Mateo, my parents, Hugo, Pablo, Daniela, Profe. The cast is the constant. The setting is the variable. The setting matters but the cast matters more. If MIT says yes, the cast moves to a different stage. If MIT says no, the cast stays put. The cast doesn't change. The work of the next 40 years is the cast, not the stage. I keep coming back to this. I think it is the answer that lets me sleep tonight. The cast is the answer. The MIT decision is just about which stage. Either stage is a stage I can build a 40-year life on. Either stage will be a good stage with this cast. Going to bed.  The other variable I keep forgetting to factor: time-of-life regret asymmetry. At 33 with one year of clean two-year window in front of me, taking the masters has the lowest opportunity cost it will ever have. Five years from now I will likely have a child, more obligations, less mobility. Ten years from now the masters becomes essentially impossible. The optimal time to take the masters is now. Even if I get rejected this cycle, the optimal time to apply the second time is also now-ish. The window narrows. The pivot has to happen in this 24-month band or it doesn't happen. That's a real constraint and I should treat it as one.

 The other thing I want to record — and this is important for me to remember in case the rejection comes — is that the people who told me 'you are going to do this' all agreed on the trajectory but disagreed on the timeline. Camila thinks first try. Pablo thinks first try. Daniela thinks 'first or second try.' My dad thinks 'three tries was fine for me, will be fine for you.' My mom thinks 'whenever, mijo.' Lozano said 'the right cohort, eventually.' The signal is unanimous on direction and noisy on timing. The right move is to take the direction seriously and not over-fit to the timing.

Roberto pinged me on Slack today asking for a 'rough timeline for the prod rollout.' He's been asking variants of this question for three weeks. The honest answer is: Carla and I are baking observability into the rust service before we flip the traffic, Andrés is writing the migration runbook, and we'll be ready in 2-3 weeks. I gave him exactly that. He replied with 'can we tighten that to 1-2 weeks?' which is Roberto-speak for 'I want to tell my boss a smaller number.' I said 'we can tighten it to 1-2 weeks if we cut the canary phase, which I would not recommend.' He replied with a thumbs-up. That's how the conversation ended. This is the dance. This is what I am potentially trading for two years of grad school. There is some clarity in stating it that way. Carla messaged me right after, on a side channel, with the eye-roll emoji. She and I have a quiet alliance. Andrés is too junior to weigh in. I appreciate that she's been around long enough to recognize the dance for what it is. After Carla's eye-roll she asked if I want to grab a coffee Friday and 'talk about the next thing.' She doesn't know about the MIT app. I haven't told anyone at Brújula except Daniela for obvious reasons. I told Carla yes to the coffee but I haven't decided whether to tell her about MIT. She is the kind of person who would be supportive and discreet. She is also the kind of person who deserves the heads-up that the team's tech lead might be gone in 12 months. I'll think about it. Probably tell her over the coffee. Probably.

 The 2-3 week timeline is a real number. We need to bake in canary observability — the rust service is going to take 1% of traffic for 48 hours, then 10% for 48 hours, then 50% for 72 hours, then 100% rollover. Each step has a rollback runbook. Carla and I wrote the canary plan together over two long sessions in month 16. Andrés has been running the staging-load tests this week and the metrics look clean — 38ms p95 holding under sustained burst, no memory regression, no Tokio runtime starvation. The data is there for a confident rollout. Roberto wants the smaller number because his quarterly review is coming up and a rust-migration-completed bullet would make his slides nicer. I sympathize with his political incentives but I am not going to compromise the rollout for them. Carla agrees. Andrés is too junior to weigh in but I have explicitly told him that we ship on the canary plan timeline regardless of leadership pressure. He nodded gravely. He is becoming a senior engineer in front of my eyes.  I am going to draft a one-pager for Carla outlining the canary plan in writing.
