Even though we wrote it together, I want her to have a clean version she can hand off if I have to step away for any reason. Defensive engineering applied to my own relationship to my own job.

Camila and I are going to do a single check-in session with Dr. Muñoz next week. We paused therapy at 3 sessions back in month 8. We're not in crisis — actually the opposite, we want to do a check-in while things are good, before MIT decision news either way changes the whole landscape. The kid-timing conversation has been quietly humming under everything. Camila said she wants to talk about it with someone in the room who isn't us. I agreed. I think that's healthy. I think the version of me from a year ago would've avoided the session. The version of me now booked it. Dr. Muñoz responded to the email within 90 minutes which is faster than usual — I think she remembers us and was glad we reached out. We have a 6pm slot Tuesday next week. Camila put it in our shared calendar with a heart emoji. The first session was 11 months ago. Lifetime ago. Different couple. We're going to walk in there with the framework we've already roughed out — MIT outcome determines geographic next 2 years, kid timing follows from there with a 1-year buffer post-stability — and we're going to ask Dr. Muñoz to stress-test it. That's a different therapy use case than crisis-triage. I think it's the right one. I'm actually a little excited for the session, which is not a sentence I expected to ever type about therapy. Growth observable in real time.

 The other thing the Dr. Muñoz check-in is going to surface, I suspect, is the question of how Camila and I want to handle the 4 months of waiting. She has her own anxiety patterns around big-decision uncertainty — she does not spiral the way I do, she goes quiet and gets a little tense in the shoulders for a few days at a time, and then she calls her sister in São Paulo and the two of them go for a long walk on opposite continents and somehow this fixes her. My pattern is the 3am rumination. We are different patterns. The session might help us articulate to each other how to support the other one's pattern without trying to convert it. Dr. Muñoz is good at that exact kind of articulation. The first session 11 months ago helped us articulate the kid-timing fight in a way that turned it from a recurring crisis into a recurring conversation. I want to see her translate the 4-month-wait into a similar shape. It probably will not be a long session. We have homework, basically.  Camila's shoulders have been less tight this week. I can tell because she stopped doing the small head-tilt-to-the-right she does when her trapezius is locked up. Body language as a stress diagnostic. She does not know I read her like this. She would tease me if she knew.

going to bed. brain feels lighter than it has in months. weird sensation. enjoying it. the post-application float is a real thing. nube on the rug. camila reading. lights low. october 25 ends here. tomorrow is just sunday. that is allowed.

 The way the lamp on Camila's side throws shadow on the wall is suddenly something I'm noticing for the first time in months. The book she's reading is a Lispector — same one she gave me but in the original — and she's reading slowly with her finger under the line, mouthing the Portuguese. She caught me looking and smiled without saying anything. The fan is on low. Nube is doing the slow contented breathing that is the most reassuring sound I know. October 25, ten o'clock at night. Submitted yesterday. Survived today. Going to sleep tonight. The arc continues but the shape is gentler now. I will take it.  Phone in the other room. The rule holds tonight. Going.

Called my parents tonight. Mom answered, put me on speaker. Dad's angina has been quiet for a few weeks (he qualified this with 'so far,' which is his usual). Mom is fine, no more lump scares. I told them the application is in. Mom said 'mijo, qué orgullo' and her voice did the thing it does. Dad said 'remember it took me three tries to get into the masters in physics. Don't take rejection personally if it comes.' That was unexpected. He'd never told me that. He said it like it was nothing. We talked for 20 minutes about the chess board and Hugo's reaction at the party last week — they keep retelling it to each other apparently. I didn't realize how much it mattered to them until they kept circling back to it. Dad said again, in passing, 'don't forget mine in four years.' I told him I won't. I won't. Mom asked when we're coming up for Christmas. I said December 21 to December 28 — it's a longer trip than usual because we're trying to do Christmas with my family and then New Year's in CDMX with Hugo since he's flying back over. She said good, she's going to make tamales, and asked Camila what kind of pan dulce she wants. Camila yelled an answer from the other room. The call ended at 9:42. I sat with the phone for a minute before putting it down. Dad's three-tries-for-the-masters is a thing he has never mentioned in 33 years. I do not know what to do with the fact that he had to tell me about MIT for it to come up. I am going to call him tomorrow. Just to call him. Not for any reason.

 After we hung up I sat on the couch and stared at the wall for ten minutes. Dad's three-tries-for-the-masters had landed harder than anything else in the conversation. My dad got his masters in physics from UNAM in 1992 — I had always known this — and I had assumed he'd gotten in on the first try because the version of dad I grew up with always seemed to do things efficiently. Apparently not. Apparently he tried in 1989, was rejected. Tried in 1990, was rejected. Tried in 1991, accepted. He never told me. He told me tonight because his son was on the verge of his own masters application and dad wanted to make sure I had a shock absorber if rejection hit. Three tries. The man kept applying. The man also worked at a high-school physics-teacher salary the whole time, in a way I was not until tonight calibrating, and he applied three times. I do not deserve the version of this man that I am. I am going to call him tomorrow. Just to call him. Maybe I tell him I'm proud of him for trying three times. Maybe I just talk about Nube. Whatever lets the conversation be a conversation. I will call.  Mateo texted me 10 minutes after the call ended saying 'mom told me about dad's grad school three-tries thing.' He had not known either. We both have been somewhat re-meeting our father at age 33 and 28 respectively. There is more to him than we realized. There always is.

It's 3:11am. Awake again. Different flavor of insomnia tonight — not the pre-submission grind anymore, this is the post-submission spiral. The brain has nothing to grind against, and instead of resting it has decided to invent imaginary rejection emails. I have composed approximately seven of them in my head between 1:40 and now.

Let me just write them out so I can stop.

First imagined email: 'Dear Sebastián, thank you for your application to the MIT Media Lab. We received a record number of applications this cycle and unfortunately must inform you...' Standard form rejection. The kind of email that deletes the future. I imagine reading it on my phone at the Brújula office and having to maintain a poker face during stand-up. I imagine Camila's face when I tell her. I imagine the second-cycle apply, the iteration, the better-positioned-next-time speech.

Second imagined email: a more personal rejection. 'We were impressed by your application but felt the fit with our current research priorities was not strong.' This one is more painful because it implies they read it carefully. I imagine sending the email to Daniela and Pablo and Lozano and saying 'thanks for trying.'

Third imagined email: a waitlist. The torture variant. 'You have been placed on our waitlist...' Months of low-grade hope.

Fourth: an interview invitation. This one I let myself imagine for about 90 seconds before talking myself down because if I get attached to it I will be devastated when it doesn't come.

Fifth: an acceptance. I won't even type it because I'm superstitious.

Sixth: an acceptance with funding. I refuse to even hold the thought.

Seventh: a 'we'd like to invite you to apply to a different program' redirect. This one I genuinely have not been able to predict the emotional response to. It's Schrödinger's email.

The absurd part is the application was submitted four days ago. Decisions don't come for months. I am pre-grieving outcomes that aren't due until February. This is not a productive use of my brain at 3am.

What I know about my own pattern from the past 18 months: when I'm under-stimulated my brain invents stimulation. Insomnia peaks correlate not with stress, but with the absence of a clear task. Last year's insomnia peak was month 16 — the rust migration was about to ship and there was nothing left to do but wait. Same pattern now. Brain hates the void. Brain fills the void with imagined catastrophes.

Actionable thoughts at 3:14am:
- Trazodone if it gets worse. Vet uses it for Nube in storms. (Joke. Sort of.)
- The melatonin works for getting to sleep but not for staying asleep, which is the actual problem. Maybe ask Dr. Muñoz next week if she can refer me to a sleep specialist.
- The CBT-I app didn't stick because I am not a person who will use a phone app to fix my brain. Maybe try the actual workbook.
- Stop drinking yerba mate after 4pm. I keep saying this. I keep failing at it.
- Build a project. The brain needs a thing. The chess board took up 3 months of unstructured time and the pose-est before that. Without a project, the brain is feral. What's the next project? I don't know yet. Maybe something small. Maybe finally finish the hot-sauce recipe book I've been talking about for 3 years. Something that won't compete with potential MIT mental load if I get in.
- Write an actual email to Picard. Not asking for anything. Just acknowledging I'm in the applicant pool, briefly stating my background, and noting one specific paper of hers that influenced my thinking. Cold but warm. The kind of email you can send without expectation. I have been resisting this because it feels presumptuous. But the worst case is she doesn't read it; the best case is she reads it and remembers my name when she sees the application packet. Asymmetric upside. I'll draft it tomorrow when I'm not 3am-brained.

One more thing. The reason I'm awake. It's not actually the imagined rejections. It's that for the first time in eighteen months I do not have a thing to deliver. The arc that ran from October 2024 to October 2026 was a constant series of deliveries — funeral logistics, promo packet, post-mortem, rust migration milestones, the kid-timing conversation, the chess board, the personal statement. Always one more thing on Tuesday. Tuesday now is just Tuesday. The hollow that follows the delivery is what's keeping me up. The hollow is not bad. The hollow is just unfamiliar. I have to learn to live in it for a few months.

Another thing. The cortisol of the application was carrying real load. Now that the load is gone the body is asking 'what now' and not getting a satisfying answer. The body wants an answer. The body wants something to clench against. There is nothing for it to clench against. So the body is inventing things to clench against, which is what the imagined rejection emails are. They are the body's request for a load to bear. The healthy response is to give the body something else to bear — the gym, the hot sauce zine, the cutting board for Pablo, the cold email to Picard. Anything that's a real load instead of a phantom one. I'm going to make a list tomorrow. Cold-email Picard is on the list. The hot-sauce zine is on the list. Maybe even open the Lispector book Camila gave me and try to read three pages without using the dictionary. The list is the medicine. Without the list I will keep inventing rejection emails for the next 90 days and Camila will leave me. (She will not leave me. She has the patience of a saint. But the principle stands.)

Doña Marta starts the oven in 49 minutes. The smell of hot bread will be a cruel taunt at 4am. I'm going to lie back down. If I'm awake at 4am writing this from my phone again I'll know the spiral is winning. If I'm asleep at 4am I'll know the hollow is teaching me something. Either way the bread will be there at 5.

 The other thing I want to write down before I close this. The night feeling. The 3am light. The fact that I am alone with my brain and a phone and a glass of water and the dog snoring downstairs. There is a particular kind of solitude in 3am insomnia that does not exist at any other hour. It is not the solitude of a quiet morning. It is not the solitude of a long bus ride. It is the solitude of being unable to sleep while the people you love are asleep three meters away. They are right there and they are unreachable. The phone is in the other room. The brain is the only company. The brain is bad company at 3am. The brain at 3am is not the same brain as the 11am brain. The 3am brain has a different relationship to risk, to memory, to identity. The 3am brain is a different person in a meaningful way. I have to remember this when I read these notes back tomorrow. The 3am Sebastián who wrote about imagined rejection emails is not the 11am Sebastián who is going to read those notes. The 3am Sebastián is closer to a 17-year-old version of me than to the version of me that ships rust services. The 3am Sebastián thinks in catastrophes. The 11am Sebastián thinks in plans. They are different people. I should not give the 3am Sebastián decision-making authority over things the 11am Sebastián is responsible for. The notes from 3am are a diary of the 3am person, not a directive for the 11am person. I am going to carry this distinction with me. The thing the 11am person should do tomorrow is read the notes, make a list of the actionable items, and then archive the rest. The catastrophes go in the archive. The actions go on the list. The 11am person executes the list. The 3am person takes the night off.

 Doña Marta starts in 28 minutes. The bread will smell like reassurance. The light through the kitchen window will turn soft amber. Camila will wake at 7 and find me on the couch with notes-app open and will not say anything except to bring me coffee. This is the post-3am-rant ritual. We have done it many times. The morning forgives the night. Going to lie back down and try one more time. If it works, great. If it doesn't, I have notes for the 11am Sebastián to read.  The phone is buzzing with a notification from a Mastodon thread I started two days ago about the migration. Some Tokio core dev replied with a useful pointer about the multi-thread runtime tuning. I am not going to read it tonight. I am going to read it at 11am tomorrow when 11am-Sebastián is back in command. The 3am Sebastián is not allowed to read technical content. Bad ratio of catastrophe-fuel to insight at this hour.

Slept maybe 5 hours after last night's spiral. Up early, walked Nube, made coffee, told Camila what my brain was doing at 3am and she said 'mi amor, you've been doing this for months, can we make a rule that you write me a note instead of writing your brain a five-page essay?' I said yes. The rule is: when I wake at 3am, I tap her on the shoulder, she says 'go back to sleep,' I go back to sleep. Or try to. We'll see if it works. The 'go back to sleep' instruction from someone you love at 3am has a different weight than the same instruction from your own brain. We'll see if my body recognizes the difference. I'm willing to try the experiment. We tested it tonight at midnight as a dry run — I was about to start spiraling on whether I should have written a different second paragraph in the personal statement, I tapped her shoulder, she said 'go back to sleep' without opening her eyes, and somehow it worked. The brain just turned off the loop. Five years of sleeping next to someone has trained my nervous system to recognize her voice as a parasympathetic signal. I had not known this. Now I know. Going to deploy more aggressively.

 The other thing I want to write down — Camila's been quietly doing this for me for years and I'd never named it. She has a way of providing low-key parasympathetic regulation through her presence alone. Five years of sleeping next to her, eating across from her, walking next to her have apparently trained my nervous system to drop into a calmer state when she's near. I had not understood this as a real physiological thing. Tonight's experiment confirmed it: her voice at midnight, half-asleep, saying 'go back to sleep,' did something my own internal narration could never do. I think this is part of what long-term partnership is for. Not just companionship. Co-regulation. We are each other's nervous-system anchors. I am going to thank her tomorrow morning. She will brush it off. She will brush it off in a way that confirms the thing.

 We tested it tonight at midnight as a dry run. I was about to start spiraling. I tapped her shoulder. She said the four words. I went back to sleep. Five years of conditioning is a real biological asset. Going to deploy it more. Camila has agreed to be the parasympathetic intervention without being grumpy about it. She did say I owe her one extra long Sunday morning of laptop-free coffee per shoulder-tap. I have agreed. The exchange rate is fair.  I want to remember that the rule we tested tonight worked partly because I had explicit consent to use her presence as a regulator. The consent makes it different from just nudging her awake. The consent makes it a tool we both own. We will calibrate. The rule is provisional. Real partnership.

Camila and I had the long talk tonight. The one about the next 5 years, conditional on the various MIT outcomes. We've been circling it for weeks. I'd been avoiding it because I didn't want to make plans on top of an unsubmitted application; she'd been respecting the avoidance. After the submission and the party for her dad and a quiet Sunday it just kind of arrived naturally — we were on the couch, Nube was asleep on the rug, it was 8pm, and Camila said 'OK, can we talk?'

The scenarios.

Scenario A: I get into MIT. We move to Boston for two years (her firm has a Boston office, she'd lateral over, possibly with a small comp drop, possibly not, depends on what's open in Q1). She said tonight she's checked informally with two of her colleagues already and there's at least one open spot at the right level. We rent in Cambridge. Nube comes. Apartment will be smaller, climate worse, food worse, but the cohort and the city would be a real adventure. We'd be there from 2027 to 2029. She wants to be open about the Boston option not being a sacrifice — she said tonight, with some emphasis, that she's been wanting an excuse to work the Boston office because the firm's American institutional projects are stronger there. I didn't know that. I'd been carrying around guilt about asking her to move and apparently the guilt was misplaced. She wants the move. That changed something for me. I had been bracing for a conversation that started 'OK what would I be giving up' and instead it started 'OK what would I be getting.' Different shape. Different posture. I have to recalibrate my own internal narrative because for six months I had been quietly running the ledger of how much I'd be costing her, and the ledger turns out to be wrong in significant ways.

Scenario B: I don't get in. We stay in CDMX. I either re-apply next cycle or pivot to industry research via a different door. We either rent or buy in CDMX in the next 18 months. She mentioned, gently, that her firm has been talking about her potentially leading a new internal program in 2027 that would anchor her here for 3-4 years. So Scenario B is actually attractive too. Not a downgrade. Just a different shape of life. I noticed, sitting there, that for the first time in two years I had two genuinely good scenarios in front of me, not one good and one consolation. Both versions of the next five years are appealing. That's a luxury I don't think I've ever had before.

The kid timing. We talked about it. Honestly, we talked about it more clearly tonight than we have in any of the three couples-therapy sessions or any of the dozen private conversations since month 7. She said: in Scenario A, kid timing is post-MIT, so 2029-2030, when I'm 36-37 and she's 33-34. Late but not unreasonable. In Scenario B, the timing is more flexible, and she'd want to start trying in 2027. Both windows are workable for her. Both windows scare me different amounts, and I told her that, and she said that's fair. We agreed: we don't lock the kid timing tonight. We lock the framework. The framework is: MIT outcome determines geographic next 2 years; kid timing follows from there with a 1-year buffer post-stability.

The other thing — and this is where I got emotional — she said something about her dad and the chess board last week, about how watching him receive the gift made her think harder about what we want our 70th to look like. She said: 'I want to build the kind of life where, at 70, you're crying over something someone made you with their hands.' I don't know if I'm getting that quote exactly right. But it landed. We're not on a sprint. We're on a 40-year project. She also said, almost as an aside, that the chess board moment had clarified something for her about what kind of family she wants — a family where the children grow up making things for the grandparents. A family where the value gets transmitted in objects you can touch. I had not heard her say something like that before. We did not directly talk about kids again after she said it. But it sat there.

The Bogotá decline came up briefly. She thanked me again for it. I told her I was actually glad I'd declined because if I'd gone to Bogotá I wouldn't have had the bandwidth to apply to MIT, and the Bogotá comp would've been a golden handcuff. She said 'I told you so' which she had not, in fact, told me. I let it pass.

Money came up too — briefly, calmly, more like an inventory than an argument. Scenario A: my 45k saved + her continued salary + partial loans gets us through Boston without catastrophic damage. Scenario B: we'd be in a position to buy a small place in CDMX within 18 months, possibly with a down payment we can pull together by mid-2027. We've been together five years and we have never sat down to do a money inventory like this. It took fifteen minutes. The number was less scary than I expected. We have more runway than my anxiety has been telling me we have.

Dr. Muñoz check-in next week now has a different tenor. We're not bringing in a list of fights to triage. We're bringing in a framework to test. That's a healthier use of the session. It might even be a short session. The pause we took at 3 sessions back in month 8 was because we'd run out of fights to triage and didn't know what to do with the time. Now we have something to do with the time — stress-test the framework. Different therapy use case. Probably the right one.

Going to bed feeling like I have a plan instead of a fog. The fog has been part of the insomnia. I think tonight I sleep through. We left the conversation at around 10pm, brushed our teeth, got into bed, and Camila put her head on my chest and said 'this is going to be a good five years either way.' I believed her. I believe her. The thing I will remember about tonight is not the scenarios. It's the way she said it. It's the certainty. It's that she has done the math and concluded the math is good either way, and she shared the conclusion with me, and now I get to carry that conclusion around with me into whatever February brings.

One more thing. As we were drifting off she said quietly 'whatever happens, thank you for trying.' I said thank you for trying what. She said 'just thank you for trying. Most people don't apply. You applied.' I lay there in the dark thinking about that for a long time. There is a non-zero moral weight to having done the thing. Even if MIT says no, the application itself is a moral act — a public statement that I take this seriously enough to bet on it. Camila sees that. I think Hugo sees that. Pablo definitely sees it. The version of me that did not apply does not deserve the version of me that did. I am glad to be the version that did.

 One last thing about tonight that I want to log before sleep. I had been carrying around, for about six months, a quiet image of how this conversation might go badly. The bad version was: Camila reluctantly agrees to consider Boston as a sacrifice, the kid timing becomes a tense subtopic, the money inventory exposes more anxiety than I want to admit, and we both go to bed with that particular post-fight numbness. I had been bracing for the bad version for so long that I had not let myself imagine what the good version would feel like. The actual conversation tonight — the slow, calm, genuinely-curious conversation about both scenarios — was a third thing I had not prepared for. It was not the bad version. It was also not the bright-eyed-let's-do-it good version. It was the careful-shared-life version. We had data. We had been thinking about this independently for months. We brought our independent thinking to the couch and we crossed the data with care. The crossing produced a framework. The framework will get tested in February when the MIT decision comes. The framework will get tested again when we eventually decide on kid timing. The framework will get tested in 5 years when I finish either the masters or whatever Plan B becomes. The framework is robust because it was built carefully, and it was built carefully because we have been together five years and we have learned to build frameworks before crises rather than during them. This is a thing married couples (or married-equivalent couples) get good at if they're paying attention. We are paying attention. We have learned. We are different people from the people we were when we started couples therapy at month 7. Dr. Muñoz will see this difference next week and will, I think, smile. The growth is real. The framework is real. The next five years will be good either way. I am going to sleep now. Camila is already asleep. The apartment is quiet. The chess board is on the dining table with the Italian opening still on it. The arc that started in São Paulo with a funeral 18 months ago is closing tonight in Roma Norte with a calm shared plan. There is a kind of symmetry in that. Funerals start things. Plans end things. The 18 months had a shape after all.  The conversation also touched on something we had not directly named in five years. Camila said tonight that she had quietly been measuring whether I would ever choose myself over what is expedient, and that the MIT application was the first observation she had where the answer was clearly yes. She said she had been waiting to see this without telling me she was waiting. That landed hard. I did not know I was being watched on this axis. Apparently I was. Apparently I had passed. The pass was not announced. The pass was just absorbed into the framework. The framework now assumes I am the kind of partner who chooses the harder version of myself. That's a heavy assumption to live up to. I am going to live up to it.

 The thing I want my future self to know about tonight is that the conversation made me happier than the submission made me. The submission was a closing of a chapter. The conversation was the opening of a different one. The next 5 years are now a shared plan, not a private hope. That changes the texture of the days. I had not realized how lonely the private-hope version had been until the shared-plan version replaced it. Loneliness ended tonight in a small specific way. Going to sleep with that.

checo qualified p7 today. the long-suffering pérez fan in me will take it. race tomorrow morning. nube knows the f1 theme song now and gets up when it plays. i did not train her to do this. she trained herself. blue heelers are deeply unsettling sometimes.

 Camila is asleep already. Nube has migrated from the rug to the foot of the bed, where she has decided she is now allowed to be on Saturdays after sunset. I did not negotiate this. The blue heeler decided. Tomorrow's race is at 6am CDMX time which means I'll watch the start over coffee on the couch with the dog watching the screen with me. She is not, to be clear, watching the cars. She is watching the screen because the screen is on. Same energy as me sometimes. Going to bed. Tomorrow Pérez and the Honda crew. Vamos Checo.  Setting the alarm. 5:50 for the start. Coffee at 5:55. Race at 6.

Walked Nube to Cardinal this morning, sat on the patio with a cortado, watched her watch pigeons for 40 minutes. She is 21 months old now. Fully grown. Her face has gotten older, less puppy. The separation anxiety from the early days is mostly gone. The thunder panic only comes during bad storms now and the trazodone tames it. She has become the dog Camila and I imagined when we adopted her. I keep thinking about the version of me eighteen months ago at the rescue, trying not to fall in love with three different dogs in 90 minutes, ending up with this one because she made eye contact and didn't break it. Best decision of the arc. Easily. She will be 5 years old when (if) I finish the masters. She's going to ride out to Boston with us if it happens. I imagine her in a Cambridge winter, paws on a snowy sidewalk, looking at me like I personally arranged the weather. I have already mentally apologized to her for the snow. The barista at Cardinal, Mario, knows her now and brings her a small dish of water with a piece of ice in it the way she likes. Nube has built her own social network in Roma Norte without my involvement. The ice cube is a personal thing between her and Mario. I am not invited.

 The walk home I let Nube off-leash for a block — Camila would kill me but the side street was empty and Nube kept perfect heel for the whole block, so technically nothing happened. We came back through the courtyard and Doña Marta was sweeping outside the bakery and Nube went over to say hi and Doña Marta said 'qué linda perrita' for the eighty-thousandth time and gave Nube a tiny corner of pan. This is the morning ritual now. Mario at Cardinal, Doña Marta in the courtyard, the pigeons in the small park on Calle Tabasco that Nube watches but never chases. She has built a routine inside my routine that I am peripheral to. I am the bipedal accessory in her social calendar. I have made peace with this. The morning is hers more than it is mine. I am writing this on the couch with her snoring against my thigh because she earned the nap.  Nube has a particular thing she does at Cardinal where she takes the ice cube out of the water dish, places it carefully on the patio tile, and stares at it until it half-melts. I do not know why. I have asked Mario. Mario does not know. The ice-cube ritual remains a mystery between her and the patio.

The 'what's next' question has been sitting with me all week. Now that the application is in and there's a real waiting period, I want to think clearly about the next 2 months — not the post-MIT decision big-question stuff, but the smaller question of what I do with my unstructured hours between November and the February decision drop.

The trap I'm watching for: my brain hates the void. The 18 months of arc just behind me have been packed. RouteOpt from month 6 to 16, the rust learning curve, the pose-est from 4 to 11, the chess board from 14 to 17, the MIT app from 17 to 18. There has not been a void in 18 months. There has barely been a weekend. And I've already documented in my own 3am rant that under-stimulation is what kicks off the worst of the insomnia. So the question is — what occupies the unstructured space between now and February without competing with potential MIT mental load if the answer comes back yes?

Options I've been mulling:

1. Resume pose-est. Pull it off the shelf. The codebase is in decent shape. I could add the haptic-feedback experiment I've been mulling for a year. Build maybe 4 more sessions of test data with Pablo and the gym crew. The risk: this is exactly the project that fed the personal statement, and if I burn it out before MIT it stops being useful as a 'I will continue this work in your program' artifact. So maybe restraint here. Maybe just clean the codebase up, write a proper README, add a few small features that don't require hypothesis-testing.

2. Hot-sauce recipe book. I've been threatening this for 3 years. I have probably 30 working recipes documented in a chaotic Notion. The book would be: photograph each, write the story of where it came from, set them in a small printed zine. 50-page project. Two months feels right. Camila would shoot the photos. This is purely fun. No career implication. Brain rest. Mom would lose her mind in a good way. Mateo would demand 50 copies.

3. Second chess board. For my dad this time. Smaller, simpler, tighter execution given what I learned the first time. The Festool's already paid for. Cedar reorder from El Roble. Maybe walnut for a different visual. The risk is I make Hugo's mistake all over again — start ambitious, get warped, learn humility. The benefit is I now know what I don't know, which is the rare position I'm allowed to be in. Pablo wants a cutting board. Maybe both — give my dad the board for his birthday in February and finally make Pablo's cutting board as a side-quest.

4. Read more Picard. Not for app posturing — for fun. Read her old papers, read Affective Computing the book end-to-end, read the side-citations. This one's free, requires no setup, and prepares me intellectually if I do get in.

5. Long bike rides on weekends. Back is at 70%, PT therapist says cycling is fine. Get out of the apartment. Get sun. Don't think about MIT for 4 hours at a time. There's a CDMX cycling group that does weekend rides out to Xochimilco that I've been meaning to try.

6. Write the black-belt essay properly. The one I cut from the personal statement. Let it live as its own thing — maybe a Mastodon thread or a blog post. The scene deserves its own home and it's been bouncing around in my head for two months waiting for a venue.

7. Volunteer-teach white belts at Atlas Box. Profe asked. I've been resisting because I'm bad at teaching beginners. But a 4-month commitment of one Saturday a month could be exactly the kind of structured-but-not-stressful obligation that fills the void without competing with anything else.

8. Cold-email Picard. Not pleading, not asking for anything, just acknowledging I'm in the applicant pool and naming one of her papers that influenced me. Asymmetric upside. The worst case is she doesn't read it. The best case is she remembers my name when my packet hits her desk in February. Three sentences. Should take 20 minutes to draft. I keep talking myself out of this. I am going to do it this week.

9. Read the Lispector book Camila gave me. In Portuguese. Slow. Three pages a day. The point is the slowness, not the throughput.

10. Sleep more. Not a project. A condition. Listing it because the other nine items will not work if this one doesn't.

The answer is probably some combination — read Picard daily, hot-sauce zine on Sundays with Camila, pose-est restraint, second chess board started in December as a slow-burn for February gift, weekend bike rides for cardio + sun, one Saturday a month at Atlas Box teaching the kids who can't yet throw a jab, cold-email to Picard this week, three pages of Lispector daily. That covers brain, hands, body, obligation, and intellectual nutrition. The shape I want.

And if the rejection comes — because it might — the projects already in motion are a soft landing. No abrupt empty-quarter. Just keep going. The hot-sauce zine doesn't care if I got into MIT. The cutting board for Pablo doesn't care. My dad's chess board doesn't care. The bike rides don't care. The white belts at Atlas Box won't even know. That's actually the gift of having things to do that aren't tied to a single binary outcome — they survive the outcome either way.

The other thing I want to remember about this list. None of the items are RouteOpt. None of them are Brújula. The work itself continues — the prod rollout in two weeks, the Andrés mentoring, the Roberto-managing — but it does not need to occupy the unstructured space too. It already has its full 40 hours. The unstructured space is where the not-work goes. I think I have been confusing 'extra work' with 'meaningful unstructured time' for years and the chess board was the first project that broke me out of that pattern. The chess board was hands-on, slow, useless for my career, and it was the most meaningful project of the year. The list above is built in the chess board's image. More handwork. More slowness. More uselessness in the career sense. Less Slack. Less laptop in the evening.

Going to write this list down properly tomorrow. For now it's a notes-app draft. I'm going to commit to two of the ten items by Friday and start moving on them next week. Probably the hot-sauce zine and the bike rides. Those two are the lowest-friction, highest-immediate-payoff items, and they'll establish a rhythm that the harder items can slot into.

 The other thing I want to flag for myself in this list — I have been chronically bad at downtime in my career, and the next 90 days are going to be the most concentrated test of whether I can fix that. The pattern from past 'completed-a-thing' moments: post-thesis 2014, post-Wizeline-leaving 2021, post-Brújula-promo 2024 month 2. Each time I had a finished-thing, I responded with a frantic search for the next thing within 7-10 days, before the system had time to recalibrate. The recalibration is the part I keep skipping. The recalibration is the part where the body gets to remember that it doesn't always have to be doing something. The recalibration is what produces good thinking later. The recalibration is the soil for the next thing. The next thing grows from the recalibration. If I skip the recalibration the next thing is malnourished. The chess board grew from a 6-month recalibration period after the post-promo phase ended in month 4. That recalibration period looked like 'I am building nothing right now' and felt like 'I am wasting my career.' It was actually 'I am preparing the ground for the chess board.' I did not know that at the time. I know it now. The next 90 days have to be a similar recalibration period. The 10-item list above is not a denial of recalibration — it is a structured way to give the body small tasks while the deep recalibration happens underneath. The hot-sauce zine is recalibration-shaped. The bike rides are recalibration-shaped. The slow Lispector is recalibration-shaped. Even the cold-email to Picard is small enough to be recalibration-shaped. The list is built to look like work without being work. That is the technique. I am explicitly using it on myself. Camila says she's noticed me doing this and approves. She has been waiting for me to learn how to recalibrate properly for years. She is going to enjoy watching the next 90 days. Maybe she'll join the bike rides. Maybe Mario at Cardinal will start to recognize me as a Sunday-morning regular instead of a Wednesday-laptop-grinder. Small shape changes. Real ones.  One more thing about the project list. The hot-sauce zine and the Pablo cutting board can both be Christmas-shippable in some loose sense — zine in early December for family stocking-stuffers, cutting board in March for Pablo's birthday. Both have natural deadlines that aren't MIT-shaped. That's the trick. Projects with non-MIT deadlines absorb the brain in non-MIT ways. The brain cannot ruminate on MIT if the brain is also panicking about a Christmas-shippable hot-sauce zine. Distraction is medicine when applied with care. I am applying it with care.

 One additional consideration — the obligation to not over-fill the void. The 10-item list is generous because the brain wants something. But the actual project of the next 90 days is to under-fill, not over-fill. The void is medicine. The list is permission to take some of the medicine in small doses. I do not need to do all 10 items. Three would be enough. Six would be too many. The selection process matters more than the list.

first mate of the day. abuela lupita's bombilla. it's october 26 — she would've been 87 last week. lit a candle. saying her name. lupita maría castellanos jiménez. 1939-2019. 80 years on this planet, eight of them spent listening to her grandson complain about ITESM and then about Wizeline and then about life in CDMX. patient woman. miss her every morning at this exact moment. The candle is on the windowsill in the small ceramic dish she gave me when I left for ITESM in 2010 — the dish that says 'paciencia' in faded blue glaze. I keep it for exactly this reason. Once a year, on her birthday, I light a candle in it and say her full name and remember the way she'd hold the bombilla with both hands like it was hot when it wasn't. She wasn't a religious woman, but she taught me to keep small rituals because the rituals are how the dead keep visiting. The candle will burn until I leave for work. The mate will be cold by

Finished Exhalation tonight. Took me eight months which is embarrassing for a story collection. The title piece I'd already read but hit different the second time around — the universe-as-a-system-running-down framing, the breath as currency, the entropy clock. Chiang and Borges share that thing where the philosophical premise is so clean you forget you're reading fiction. After this I'm going to start something less heavy — Camila gave me a Brazilian short-story collection from her dad's shelf, in Portuguese, which is my B1-level reading challenge for the next 3 months. We'll see if I can get through it without giving up. The first story is a Clarice Lispector and I am already lost in the third paragraph and looking up words on my phone every twenty seconds. This is going to be slow. I am going to enjoy it being slow. The slowness is the point. There is no faster I can read in Portuguese. I have to live with the speed I have. Camila walked past tonight while I was reading and looked at me with exactly five Portuguese words on the page underlined. She said 'mi amor your phone has been on the dictionary tab for forty minutes.' I said 'I know.' She said 'good, that's the point.' She kept walking. The Lispector story is about a woman who watches a chicken in her kitchen and has an existential crisis about the chicken's interiority. It is the most Lispector thing imaginable. I am going to read it three more times before moving on to the next story. Slow Portuguese. Slow chicken. Slow October.

 The other thing about the Lispector story — there's a sentence in the second paragraph that translates roughly to 'the chicken did not understand its own eggs' and I have been turning it over for a week. Lispector is doing her thing where the small domestic moment becomes existential without warning. The chicken laying eggs that the chicken cannot conceptualize as 'mine' is suddenly the entire story of motherhood and creation. I read that sentence to Camila in Portuguese and she paused, looked at me, and said 'careful, you're starting to sound like you can read Portuguese.' I cannot read Portuguese. I can decode Portuguese very slowly with help from a dictionary. But the sentence got through. There is a difference between vocabulary and meaning that small dictionaries cannot bridge. Some sentences arrive without permission, in any language. The chicken sentence is one of those.  The Lispector book has a faded yellow cover. The pages smell like Hugo's São Paulo apartment because the book sat there for 30 years before it traveled to me. The smell is half of the book's gift. The Portuguese is the other half. The chicken sentence is the bonus.

Beers with Pablo after Friday class. Light beers, just two each, neither of us drinks much these days. We talked about his rec letter — he asked if it landed OK. I told him it was the best letter of the three and he laughed and said 'pendejo, of course it was, I know you better than your manager.' We talked about his upcoming engineering certification thing, his trip to see his sister in Querétaro, normal life stuff. He asked, near the end, what I'll do if I don't get in. I told him: the same thing I'm doing right now, with a slightly different shape. He nodded, drained his beer, said 'good answer.' Then he said he was going to make sure he sees me at gym at least twice a week regardless of MIT, because he doesn't want me getting 'sad and indoor.' His exact phrase. Sad and indoor. I love this guy. We talked about his certification — it's an industrial engineer credential he's pursuing partly because his current employer is nudging him toward it and partly because he's been low-grade restless about his own career for a year. He asked what I thought. I said I thought it was a good move, especially because the credential travels and his employer pays for it. He said 'so we are both pivoting at 33 just in different directions.' That sentence has been sitting in my head ever since. We hadn't really framed it that way before. We are both pivoting. Different directions, similar moment. I think I'm going to remember that conversation as a small landmark. Two friends, both trying to become slightly different versions of themselves at the same age, in different cities of CDMX, at the same gym. I am very lucky to have him.

 Pablo and I closed out the night talking about our parents — both of his are in Querétaro, retired now, both my parents in Guadalajara. We agreed that we are at the age where the parental phone calls are getting more frequent and shorter and more important. He said his mom had a cardiac scare in September that he hadn't told me about because he didn't want to 'make it real by saying it out loud.' She's fine now. Stress test came back clean. Cardiologist said it was indigestion. But Pablo said the week of waiting for the test results had been the longest week of his life. I had not heard about any of this. I felt awful for not knowing. He said I shouldn't, he had purposefully not told me. We sat with that for a while. I told him: please tell me next time. He nodded. He said: okay. He meant it.  I am going to invite Pablo to come help me work on the second chess board over December weekends. He won't be useful. He will hold pieces of cedar and ask questions. The point is not the cedar. The point is the holding.

bedtime. nube on the rug. camila reading. quiet apartment. october 26 ends here. tomorrow is monday and the application is submitted and the chess board is gifted and the back is at 70% and the world has, briefly, no sharp edges. logging this so future me remembers what an even keel feels like.

 Going to read a chapter of the Lispector before sleep. Slow Portuguese. Slow chicken. The Lispector chicken is going to outlive us both at this rate. Lights low. Camila is on her side. Nube has moved to the foot of the bed in her usual rotation. Phone in the other room. The application is in. The portal is closed. Tomorrow Camila is up early for a client call, I'm at Brújula. The arc continues but the night is quiet. October 26 logged. Sleeping now.  Lispector first. Then sleep. Both slow. Both correct.

Tomorrow is a normal Brújula Monday. Stand-up at 9. Sprint planning at 11. RouteOpt rollout call at 2. The application is submitted, the decision is months away, and the actual structure of my day-to-day life is unchanged. I find this reassuring. Whatever the outcome in February, the next 90 days are: ship rust to prod, support Andrés, work with Carla on the next phase, continue PT, see Profe three times a week, walk Nube, drink yerba mate, see my parents over Christmas. The arc was big. The arc is also over. The dailies are back. There's a kind of quiet relief in that. I had forgotten how it feels to have a Monday that isn't load-bearing. Tomorrow is just Monday. I'm going to enjoy it being just Monday. Carla's coffee is Friday. I've decided to tell her about MIT. She deserves the heads-up and she will keep it private. The Logistics Platform team has been my professional home for three years and I owe at least one person in it the honesty of 'I might be moving on in 12 months.' Carla is the right one. Roberto is not. Andrés is too junior to carry the information. Carla, on Friday, with coffee. That's the move. The rest of the team finds out later, or not, depending on outcome.

 I want to log one more thing because tomorrow it might feel less true. The arc that just ended — the 18 months from October 2024 to October 2026 — felt while I was inside it like a continuous high-cortisol stretch. The funeral, the promo, the pose-est, the postgres outage, the rust migration, the manager change, the rent shock, mom's lump, the back tweak, the chess board, the application. Every Tuesday a new thing on fire. From inside it I could not see the shape. From outside it now, on the eve of a normal Brújula Monday, the shape is clear: it was the shape of a person figuring out what they actually want to do with their next 40 years. The fires were not the point. The fires were the heat under which the question about the next 40 years was annealed into a clear answer. I have the answer now. The answer is: research, family, hands-on work, slow accumulation of objects and people. The MIT application is the test of one specific version of that answer. The other versions will exist regardless. Tomorrow is just Monday. Sleep.  Carla's coffee is on Friday at 8am at Cardinal. I have already mentally rehearsed the four sentences I am going to use to tell her. Sentence one: there's something I want to share with you. Sentence two: I applied to MIT for next fall. Sentence three: the decision drops in February. Sentence four: I wanted you to know first because the team's shape might change.

It's the last night of October. Camila's in São Paulo for a long weekend with her dad — she flew Friday, comes back Tuesday. The apartment is quiet. Nube is on the couch with her chin on my thigh. I've got a glass of wine I'm not really drinking. I want to do an end-of-arc reflection because eighteen months is a long stretch of life and I want to mark it before the next thing starts. This is going to be long. I'm going to ramble. Bear with me.

October 2024 is when this all kicked off — though I didn't know it was kicking off, of course, you never do. Camila got the call from her brother that her mom Beatriz had gone into hospice in São Paulo. We flew out the next day. The funeral was a blur of Portuguese I half-understood and Brazilian churrasco I couldn't eat and Hugo Ferreira sr at 68 suddenly the head of a smaller family. Camila was on bereavement leave for weeks. I took a lot of bad walks in São Paulo before we came home. That was the start.

Then the year compressed in a way I still don't understand. The promo cycle landed in month 2 — Daniela put me up for Senior, the bump came through, 18% raise, awkward to be excited while my partner was grieving. I bought a Lelit Mara X espresso machine in month 3 like a guilt apology to nobody in particular. Started the pose-estimation side project in month 4 — kickboxing form analyzer, TensorFlow first, then pivoted to MediaPipe when the CPU couldn't keep up. Month 4 was also when my dad had a small angina flare and I thought about Mama Lupita and how I never visited her in her last year before she died in 2019, the regret that sits in me forever and probably always will.

Month 5 was the postgres outage. Four hours of prod down. I wrote the post-mortem that became the case for the rust migration. Month 6 we kicked off RouteOpt rust — Axum, Tokio, sqlx, with PostGIS that tokio-postgres didn't natively handle so I wrote a custom encoder. Same week, Camila and I went to Hocicos Felices and adopted Nube, this insane blue heeler puppy with too much energy and the cleverest eyes I'd ever seen on a dog.

Month 7 was the rough patch. Camila brought up kid-timing — wanted to start trying within 2 years — and I had a quiet panic about it that lasted weeks. We started couples therapy with Dr. Muñoz. Three sessions, then we paused. The therapy didn't fix anything but it taught us the vocabulary, which turns out to have been the actual point.

Month 8 — brown belt. Big test at Atlas Box, 6-belt-grade exam, exhausting day, Profe Esteban handed me the patch. Same month Daniela announced she was leaving Brújula for a Series A. Roberto Sánchez took over. Birthday — turned 33. Quiet dinner with Camila.

Month 9 was the rent shock — 28k to 33k, 18% increase, the absurdity of CDMX in 2026. Argued with the landlord about hot water for two weeks. Roberto started his manager regime. La Polar cantina downstairs broke records on Friday volume. Roma Norte life.

Month 10 was the worst week of the arc. Mom called about a lump on her neck, CT scheduled. I flew to Guadalajara on no notice, took a week off, sat with her at the doctor's office. Same week tweaked my lower back at Atlas Box and Profe made me sit out a Wednesday class. Body breaking down, mother possibly dying. I wrote almost nothing in those days.

Month 11. Mom's lump was a lipoma. Benign. Profound, knee-buckling relief. Paused the pose-est project. Started PT at Clínica Querétaro for the back. Camila got nominated for the Pritzker Prize student-mentor recognition through Foster + Partners — industry buzz, a small career rocket — and I was so happy I forgot about Roberto for two days.

Month 12 Brújula offered me a transfer to Bogotá. Lead infrastructure, comp bump, new city. I declined. Camila's career was here. Nube was here. The CDMX life had bones.

Month 13 Mateo announced his engagement to Sofía. The Tijuana wedding plans started. Nube had her first thunderstorm panic that month — cried, hid, the trazodone got prescribed, the thunder shirt arrived. We kept her on the bed during storms after that. Camila called it the dog parenting upgrade.

Month 14 I started the chess board for Hugo's 70th. Drove to Maderería El Roble in Toluca for cedar planks. Bought the Festool TS 55 REQ plunge router — embarrassing how much I love that tool — and a set of Veritas chisels. Cut the wrong grain orientation on the first attempt and had to re-plane the whole frame. Beginner's-mind tax.

Month 15 was black belt. Months of extra Saturday classes paid off. Profe handed the belt personally on the mat. Pablo recorded it on his phone and the video is on my hard drive in three places because I'm terrified of losing it. My parents flew in from Guadalajara, Mateo and Sofía from Tijuana. Camila threw a small party at the apartment. The peak personal moment of the arc.

Month 16 — RouteOpt shipped to staging. 340ms p95 down to 38ms. The technical peak. Same month the chess board warped in one corner from CDMX humidity and I had to re-glue and clamp. Insomnia hit its worst point — 4-5 hours of sleep nightly. Started thinking about MIT Media Lab.

Month 17. Chess board complete. Beeswax-finished cedar with magnetic walnut pieces, 3500 MXN of materials, three months of hands. Hugo's 70th birthday party in CDMX. Hugo cried when he opened it. My dad put his hand on Hugo's shoulder. Camila on my arm afterwards in the kitchen. The most tender moment of the arc.

Month 18 — that's now. October 2026. MIT Media Lab masters application submitted. Personal statement on its 7th draft, locked at 994 words, theme: ML for nonverbal feedback in physical training. Recommendation letters from Daniela Otero (former mgr), Dr. Patricia Lozano (ITESM thesis advisor), Pablo Mendoza (peer wildcard). GRE waiver granted. Now I wait.

What I learned in 18 months. Just listing. This is the part I want my future self to read.

- Grief is logistics. The first three weeks after Beatriz died were almost entirely about flights and visas and what to do with bread that doesn't get eaten. The grief itself came later. Months later. It comes in shapes you don't expect — Camila on a balcony at 6am, Hugo at the airport in a shirt his wife bought him, my own dad's hand on Hugo's shoulder during a chess board unveiling. Grief is not a feeling. Grief is a long shape made of ordinary moments.
- Career wins land badly when your partner is grieving. Time them right when you can. Apologize when you can't.
- Rust is worth the pain if the workload demands it. PostGIS will fight you. Tokio's runtime will fight you. The 9x p95 improvement will not.
- A junior engineer onboarded onto a critical migration is more management work than the migration itself. Andrés is great. Onboarding him was 30% of my year.
- Roberto-types exist everywhere and they will be your manager sometimes. Survive politely.
- Get a dog if you can. Nube has lowered my baseline cortisol in ways I can't measure but can feel.
- Therapy doesn't fix. It vocabulary-builds. That alone is worth the price.
- A black belt is mostly a record of consistency. The body doesn't care if you have talent. The body only cares if you keep showing up.
- Woodworking will humble you. The grain has opinions you don't yet have.
- The personal statement is best written after you've already written the personal statement that's actually for yourself. Do that one first. Then do the real one.
- A 70-year-old grandfather can be made to cry by a piece of cedar. Hands-made gifts have a different weight than money-bought gifts. I will remember this until I die.
- 3am insomnia means the brain is unstimulated, not overstimulated. Counterintuitive. Build a project.
- Camila and I are stronger after this 18 months than we were at the start. The kid-timing thing is unresolved but the framework is solid. We are on the same 40-year project. This is the most important sentence in this entire log.
- I am, possibly, a researcher trapped in a senior IC body. The MIT application is a test of that hypothesis. The result comes in February. Either way I'll know more about myself than I did in October 2024.
- My parents are aging. Mom's lipoma was benign but the next call might not be. Dad's angina is quiet but quiet isn't gone. I'm going to visit Guadalajara more.
- Hugo Ferreira sr is a small giant. I want my own dad to live to 70 and beyond and I want Hugo to live to 80 and beyond and I want Camila and me to be at every birthday in between.
- The big project of my 30s is not RouteOpt and not the chess board and not the MIT masters. The big project is the relationships. Camila. Nube. Mom and dad. Mateo. Pablo. Hugo. Profe. Daniela. Lozano. Andrés. Carla. The names that I'll remember as the cast of this 18-month arc are the names that I'll add to in the next 18 months and the 18 months after that. The work projects come and go. The names persist. I do not think I knew this at the start of the arc. I think I know it now.
- Everything ends. The arc ended. The chess board ended. The RouteOpt migration is ending. Beatriz ended. Mama Lupita ended. The MIT decision will be one outcome or another and that will end too. The only thing that doesn't end is the next arc starting. There is always a next arc. The trick is to be ready for it without rushing it.
- The five categories of insight from this arc — work, body, family, partner, self — are also the five categories I'll need to keep in balance for the next 40 years. Any one of them gets too much weight, the whole thing wobbles. Brújula tried to take all the weight in months 5-7 and the wobble showed up as the rough patch with Camila. The black belt training in months 13-15 tried to take too much, and the back gave out. Balance is not a vibe. Balance is an active scheduling problem. Schedule it.
- The chess board is the model. Slow, hand-made, useful, beautiful, gifted. If I can build my next 40 years to look like the chess board project — slow research, hands-on work, useful artifacts, beautiful in some specific way, given to people I love — then the 40 years will have been good. That is what I want. That is what I am working toward.
- One more. Hugo said 'made by my son' in Portuguese to his old engineer friends at the party. I have been turning that line over in my head for a week. It is a small thing. It is also the gift of the gift. Some objects you make change the names other people use for you. The chess board changed Hugo's name for me. I do not know what other objects I will make in my life that will do this. I want to make more of them.

That's it. That's the arc. The next arc is — who knows. February. We'll see what MIT says. We'll see what life says. The yerba mate is cold. Nube is snoring. The candle for Mama Lupita has guttered out. October 2026 ends here, and tomorrow is just Monday.  One absolute final thing. I want to log the names that the next arc starts with — because the names are the through-line and I want to remember who I am bringing forward. Camila Ferreira. Hugo Ferreira sr. My parents Isabela and Hugo. Mateo and Sofía. Pablo Mendoza. Daniela Otero. Profe Esteban Domínguez. Andrés Ruiz. Carla Vela. Dr. Patricia Lozano. Dr. Muñoz. Doña Marta. Mario at Cardinal. The Nudo guys. Don Federico. Don Ricardo. Nube. The cast for the next arc, whichever stage it plays out on. I am very lucky. I will not forget that. The arc ends now. Tomorrow is just Monday. Going to sleep.

 Almost done. The candle for Mama Lupita has gone out. The wine in the glass is half-drunk. Nube is now fully asleep with her tongue out a little, which is how she sleeps when she's deeply relaxed. The window is open and the late October air is cool. The Roma Norte streetlights are doing their amber thing. Tomorrow is November 1, all-saints, Día de Muertos eve. I am going to set out the small ofrenda Camila and I have been doing the past three years — Mama Lupita, Beatriz, my abuelo paterno who died in 2011, our friend Lila who passed in 2023. The ofrenda is small. Photos. Marigolds. A piece of bread. A sip of mezcal in a tiny glass. The dead visit. The arc closes. November 1 begins. The next arc starts tomorrow.
